Issue Title: Certain Denylisted customers performed prohibited BTC transactions
Issue Description:
Denylisted customers performed prohibited BTC transactions
 

 Control Description:
 Denylisted customers are prohibited from executing BTC on-chain and LTN deposit and withdrawal transactions.  
 

 Testing Results:
 Out of 11,494 attempted Bitcoin transactions (including on-chain withdrawals/deposits and Lightning send/receive) by denylisted customers in the review period, 195 were identified as successful (1.69%). Of these, 91 transactions occurred more than six hours after the denylist timestamp (to account for time zone differences) and were associated with 21 unique customer tokens.
 

 GCMT identified the following causes for the prohibited transactions transacting successfully:
 1) A bug in the system’s flag logic resulted in the Denylist requirement not being applied as expected, allowing BTC on-chain and LTN deposit and withdrawal transactions from denylisted customers to process successfully.
 

 2) The system inappropriately prioritizes business eligibility checks over of denylist status. The following business eligibility categories were given precedence over denylist-related transaction blocking:
 - BUSINESS_REMEDIATION
 - PERSONAL_USE_VIOLATION
 - BUSINESS_USE_VIOLATION 
 - KYC_ADVERSE_ACTION
 - SOURCE_OF_FUNDS_ERROR

Issue Title: CTR Reporting Exception - Southwest Border GTO
Issue Description:
On April 14, 2025, Cash App failed to file timely CTRs for three paper money deposits between $230 and $300 that occurred at Walmart locations within the scope of FinCEN’s Southwest Border GTO. These transactions were not detected by the Exception Monitoring Report, which was designed to flag deposits of $200 or more for reporting.
 

 The issue was first identified on September 12, 2025, when Compliance Analytics reviewed the Exception Monitoring Report and discovered the missed exceptions. Further investigation revealed two main contributing factors. First, a subset of nine-digit zip codes provided by InComm was not properly recognized by Cash App’s $190 deposit limit control, allowing transactions above the limit at certain Walmart locations. Second, the Exception Monitoring Report contained a timezone conversion bug. Instead of converting the correct transaction timestamp field, the report used a truncated date field, which caused the transactions to appear outside of the GTO’s effective period.
 

 As a result of these combined errors, the exceptions were missed, and the related CTRs were filed late on September 15, 2025. Follow-up review confirmed that no additional missed exceptions were identified beyond the three transactions at issue.
RCA Description:
The primary root cause of this issue was a technology failure in the Exception Monitoring Report logic, compounded by data handling limitations in the deposit control process. Specifically:
 

 The Exception Monitoring Report incorrectly used the movement_date (DATE) field rather than the movement_at (DATETIME) field during timezone conversion. This caused deposit timestamps to be shifted back one day for Mountain and Central time zone locations, making three April 14, 2025 deposits appear to fall outside the GTO period.
 

 InComm-provided data contained nine-digit zip codes that were not recognized by Cash App’s $190 deposit limit control. As a result, certain Walmart locations were not subject to the intended restriction between midnight and 6PM ET on April 14, 2025.
 

 Participating Walmart stores did not implement InComm’s $190 deposit limit, increasing reliance on Cash App’s internal controls.
 

 These combined defects directly led to the failure to flag three qualifying deposits, resulting in late CTR filings.
Remediation Strategy:
-Ensure all CTR-eligible transactions are detected and reported in compliance with GTO requirements.
 -Correct the flawed Exception Monitoring Report logic to ensure proper timezone conversion.
 -File late CTRs with FinCEN.
 -Conduct retrospective review for additional missed exceptions.
 

 Success Criteria:
 -Zero missed CTR exceptions attributable to system or data-handling errors.
 -Successful validation of Exception Monitoring Report logic against historical and test data.
 -Documented partner alignment on deposit limit enforcement requirements.
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: The issue resulted in late CTR filings, creating regulatory exposure, but was contained to three transactions and corrected. No systemic, ongoing reporting failures were identified beyond this occurrence.
Risk Domain: Compliance | Financial Crime | Anti-Money Laundering & Terrorist Financing (1000126)
Issue Action Plans: Southwest Border GTO – CTR Reporting Exception Remediation and Validation (1004620)
Closure Request Justification: Remediation has been completed. Requesting closure. Thanks!
Issue Internal Validation Justification:
This MAP is has been addressed by completing the following:

 Engineering fix controls (Sept 15, 2025)
 Comprehensive review to ensure no other exceptions (completed Sept 15, 2025)
 Conversation with InComm concerning GTO implementation ensuring compliance with rule (Sept 25, 2025).

Issue Title: Dual eIDV and dIDV requirements for BTC eligibility not enforced in some instances
Issue Description:
Control Description:
 Customers must pass two identity checks (electronic and documentary verification) before they can use any Bitcoin services. The system automatically blocks all Bitcoin transactions until both checks are successfully completed. Once both checks pass, Bitcoin access is automatically granted to the customer.
 

 Testing Results:
 Analysis was performed across BTC withdrawal transactions for the review population, with deposit reversals removed from the exception counts for 4/9/2025 - 9/3/2025.
 - Before April 9, 2025: 19,709 of 1,647,834 transactions failed the dual-IDV control (1.20% exception rate).
 - On/After April 9, 2025 (through September 3, 2025): 4,284 of 1,527,139 transactions failed the dual-IDV control (0.28% exception rate).
 

 Root Cause
 Per review from GCMT and Engineering, the potential root cause appears to stem from data sourcing errors in how DIDV/EIDV status is referenced during Bitcoin eligibility checks:
 - In confirmed breach cases, customers had a DIDV record but lacked a corresponding EIDV record.
 - The control logic might have defaulted null DIDV values to “verified”, incorrectly granting eligibility.
 

 This indicates a potential data sourcing error, where the system feeding Bitcoin access decisions does not consistently utilize or reconcile DIDV/EIDV records from the correct source.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description:
Root Cause
 Per review from GCMT and Engineering, the potential root cause appears to stem from data sourcing errors in how DIDV/EIDV status is referenced during Bitcoin eligibility checks:
 - In confirmed breach cases, customers had a DIDV record but lacked a corresponding EIDV record.
 - The control logic might have defaulted null DIDV values to “verified”, incorrectly granting eligibility.
 

 This indicates a potential data sourcing error, where the system feeding Bitcoin access decisions does not consistently utilize or reconcile DIDV/EIDV records from the correct source.
Remediation Strategy: Risk Acceptance
Risk Acceptance Justification:
Risk accepted. 
 Product Compliance (PCM) agrees with the business that the risk posed by the residual exception rate of 0.28% is deemed acceptable without further investigation or corrective action. The affected population is small enough, and there is a known challenge that customers who currently use dIDV as a fallback IDV method have sometimes experienced these challenges. All customers who interact with BTC withdrawals at least have dIDV. There is no concern that KYC is insufficient for these customers. As such, the risk is deemed acceptable.
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Minimal, localized consequences with negligible impact on overall business operations or reputation. Escalation is unlikely due to effective containment or the isolated nature of the issue.

Issue Title: The Lower $ limits for new accounts and Limit on # of withdrawals for all accounts fails in some instances
Issue Description:
Control Description:
 1. Lower BTC withdrawals $ limits to $1K/week for new accounts under 90 days old
 2. Limit # of BTC withdrawals to 20 per week
 

 Testing Results: 
 For the adjusted time period of 4/9/2025 - 6/1/2025, the following failure rate was observed: 
 [Restrict BTC Withdrawals] Limit on # of withdrawals for all accounts : 0.1% failure rate
 [Restrict BTC Withdrawals] Lower $ limits for new accounts: 2.48% failure rate
 

 Notably, the majority of breaches clustered close to the $1,020 threshold (typically in the $1,030–$1,100 range). A small “fuzzy” buffer of $20 above the $1,000 limit was applied in the testing results.This buffer was introduced to minimize borderline false positives driven by FX rounding, fee treatment, timestamp variances between source tables, or minor currency normalization differences, and was applied in agreement with control owners.
 

 Root Cause
 Further manual review and investigation confirmed that the sampled cases represented true breaches of the control thresholds. Potential root causes include:
 - Misaligned anchoring timestamps across transaction types
 - Latency in SSN connection data
 - Variances introduced by retrospective reconciliation of BTC exchange rates and fees
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description:
Root Cause
 Further manual review and investigation confirmed that the sampled cases represented true breaches of the control thresholds. Potential root causes include:
 - Misaligned anchoring timestamps across transaction types
 - Latency in SSN connection data
 - Variances introduced by retrospective reconciliation of BTC exchange rates and fees
Remediation Strategy: Risk Acceptance
Risk Acceptance Justification:
Risk accepted. 
 Product Compliance (PCM) aligns with the business that the risk posed by the residual exception rate of 2.48% is deemed acceptable without further investigation or corrective action. The impacted population is small, the limit on number of withdrawals and lower $ limit were not implemented as permanent solutions. The plan was always to evaluate the effectiveness of these controls to determine a longer term implementation. As such, the risk is deemed acceptable.
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Minimal, localized consequences with negligible impact on overall business operations or reputation. Escalation is unlikely due to effective containment or the isolated nature of the issue.

Issue Title: General Availability percentage was not at 100% as reported by Compliance
Issue Description:
Background
 Compliance relies on accurate GA (General Availability) rollout documentation to determine when controls are fully operational and when performance testing can meaningfully begin. GA 100% dates are referenced in Compliance trackers, PRDs, and may also be relied upon by regulators or banking partners.
 

 Issue
 For all six controls subject to this review, Compliance trackers and applicable PRDs recorded GA 100% rollout as occurring in Q4 2024. Product and Engineering teams later clarified that:
 

 - 5% Holdouts (5 controls — e.g., limit on number of withdrawals, lower $ limits for new accounts, liveness verification, compliance exclusion list, and other Bitcoin eligibility controls): A deliberate 5% holdout population was maintained until April 8, 2025 to enable impact measurement. Per Product, this approach was communicated to and approved by Product and Product Compliance leadership through meeting updates and Slack communications.
 

 - 50% Rollout (1 control — eIDV + dIDV as a BTC access requirement): The eIDV + dIDV control remained at 50% rollout from Dec 2024 through April 2025, not by design but due to a human error. This reduced coverage was not communicated to Compliance, who had documented December 2024 as the GA date.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description:
The governance process for control rollout needs to be enhanced to confirm exact GA launch timeline for key controls: 
 Update Existing Documentation: Ensure the actual GA timeline is updated consistently across all existing sources of record — product/engineering trackers, compliance trackers, management reporting, and PRDs.
 

 Integrate GA Documentation into LRD / CRD Workflow: As part of the new process, ensure that GA 100% rollout dates are explicitly documented and linked to each control requirement. GA dates should be included as required fields in the LRD, alongside compliance approvals.
 

 Attestation at Post-Launch Review: Require Compliance to attest to GA 100% dates during the post-launch review phase, using evidence from Engineering/Product rollout data (e.g., Amplitude experiments, holdout closure). This serves as the reconciliation step to confirm implementation matches the LRD and provides a governance checkpoint.
Remediation Strategy: All teams with relevant documents will update them accordingly with the accurate launch dates. PCM will align with compliance team to implement launch date confirmation procedures for post launch review.
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Minimal, localized consequences with negligible impact on overall business operations or reputation. Escalation is unlikely due to effective containment or the isolated nature of the issue.
Issue Action Plans:
Correct documentation for GA timeline (1001980)
 Correct documentation for GA timeline (1001984)

Issue Title: Security Risk: Lack of Link Sanitization and Handling Protocols for Customer-Provided External Links
Issue Description: A critical security vulnerability was identified within our Customer Success (CS) operations, specifically concerning external links embedded by customers in either their In-App communications or within documents uploaded through SendSafely (following CS requested supporting documentation). The issue was discovered internally during review of case #189243245, where suspicious links prompted the first known escalation to scaled operations. The vulnerability stems from two key gaps: the absence of automated security measures to sanitize or deactivate potentially harmful embedded links in uploaded documents, and the lack of proper guidance in Advocate Knowledge Articles (KAs) for handling external links received from customers. This security gap has resulted in decreased operational efficiency, extended case resolution times, inconsistent customer experiences, and reduced advocate confidence in decision-making, highlighting the need for immediate system-level security improvements and clear operational procedures.
RCA Description: Advocates received a url through Cash App (in app) messaging from the customer and were able to click on a potentially harmful link (concern escalated by the Advocate). The link received was live (not stripped) so the system was allowing this risk to be there. The intent was missed when building out the in-app messaging technology, where it should strip live links out automatically, so Advocates cannot accidentally expose the customer to security risk. Root cause: Inadequately executed Technology Solution.
Remediation Strategy: Develop a systemic guardrail to prevent links from being live or preventing customers from being able to send them through in-app messaging. If there are any changes needed to Customer Success Knowledge Articles (KA's), ensure they are updated.
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure exposing the company to security risks/vulnerabilities
Issue Action Plans: Security Risk: Lack of Link Sanitization and Handling Protocols for Customer-Provided External Links (1002642)

Issue Title: Android Claims Evidence Upload Failure Due to Invalid URL Formatting (INC-5859)
Issue Description:
On March 16, 2025, SEV-5859 was identified running concurrently with SEV-5845 (referenced in INTAKEFORM-141), both of which involved invalid link usage in the claims processing system. While related in nature, SEV-5859 specifically impacted customers who encountered issues with the evidence upload flow expiration and invalid link usage. The primary impact manifested in a subset of Android customers who were unable to upload evidence for their claims due to improper URL formatting in the system.
 

 

 The incident required coordinated remediation efforts between both events due to their overlapping nature. The Engineering team implemented a technical fix on July 2, 2025, which resolved the URL formatting issues and eliminated further customer impact. The resolution addressed both the immediate URL formatting concerns and the underlying link validation problems that had been affecting the Android platform from March 16, 2025, to July 2, 2025.
RCA Description: A recent deployment to the Postmaster service introduced an issue affecting the in-app evidence upload functionality. When customers receive push notifications on Android devices containing the upload link, they are unable to initiate the upload flow due to incorrect URL decoding by Postmaster. The service, which is responsible for sending these notifications, is now malforming the URLs during the notification delivery process, causing errors when customers attempt to access the upload feature.
Remediation Strategy:
Action #1: Review any new claims that were added to the impacted list after the latest analysis was completed. (Juan Torrente Corbett) - Complete
 

 Action #2: Obtain Necessary Approvals for Communication Distribution: Partnerships, Counsel and/or Compliance, where applicable. (Juan Torrente Corbett) - 12/01/2025
 

 Action #3: Risk Operations to review the impacted population and determine/perform remediation. This includes: Comms Distribution, Financial reimbursement (via the app for open accounts and via check for closed accounts, where applicable). (Juan Torrente Corbett) - 12/01/2025
 

 Action #4: Closure of SEV-5859 (related to this issue): Visual confirmation that the issue is closed, and confirmation that the fix is working as designed (Kiran Sudhir) - 12/12/2025
 

 Action #5: Risk Business Partner to work in tandem with the Issue Owner to create/enhance Controls, if applicable. (Wendy Yan) - 01/30/2026
 

 Action #6: Issue Management Validation (Corey Griggs) - 02/13/2026
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: Operational Gap or Process Failure resulting in an impact to regulatory obligations.
Issue Progress Updates: Remediation completed. Working on validation and will prep for closure 2/9/26.
Issue Action Plans: Android Claims Evidence Upload Failure Due to Invalid URL Formatting (INC-5859) (1002645)
Closure Request Justification: Issue reviewed and closed
Issue Internal Validation Justification: Approved based on completed validation with Issue Management and no outstanding action plans.

Issue Title: Lack of process to accommodate persons with disabilities while verifying identity.
Issue Description:
An accessibility-related complaint has helped identify a gap in how we support customers with disabilities. A customer, citing a disability, expressed frustration on LinkedIn about their inability to resolve a service problem. A Cash Complaints Operations Team (CCOT) Group Lead found related cases tied to the customer - each with accessibility-related concerns. 
 The customer was trying to authenticate themselves but their disability prevented them from being able to go through Cash App’s normal process, which is taking a selfie with your ID. The customer is unable to lift their arm to take selfies. 
 This specific issue can be solved with more inclusive options for verifying identity. Additionally, as part of resolving this issue, Block’s obligations to the ADA need to be identified and a risk assessment needs to take place to determine if there are additional gaps that need to be addressed in a separate issue(s).
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: Block's Executive/Press Complaints ETS Extension
Issue Description:
An Extension of an April 2024 Exception Request
 

 The organization lacks a centralized complaint intake pathway and clear definitions for what constitutes an executive level complaint resulting in inconsistent handling, reporting, and oversight across teams. Complaints are currently received through multiple uncoordinated sources, many of which are not integrated with the various CRM systems. This fragmentation leads to operational inefficiencies, unclear ownership, and the over-processing of non-complaint inquiries. The core issues include the absence of a standardized intake process, undefined complaint categories, and limited communication on where and how complaints should be submitted. Addressing this issue will require establishing a single CRM-connected complaint funnel, standardizing definitions, and improving communication to ensure consistent complaint management and regulatory alignment across all business units.
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: Inability to Remove Former Authorized Persons from Square Accounts
Issue Description:
Due to system limitations, Square is currently unable to remove the details of former directors or authorized persons from company accounts. This limitation constitutes a breach of ASIC Regulatory Guide 271 (RG 271), which requires firms to maintain effective complaint resolution processes. In practice, when the merchant complained about removal of a former director, and Square responded that its systems cannot do it, AFCA determined that to be a breach of RG 271.
 

 Square is now exposed to compliance, reputational, and financial risk. In the cited case, Square was ordered to compensate the complainant AUD 1,000 (USD 660) for stress and inconvenience caused by our inability to remove a director’s details. If financial loss had occurred, compensation would have been higher.
RCA Description: Square’s TOA limitations root cause has been identified as KYC/KYB gaps
Remediation Strategy:
Square’s TOA limitations, along with broader KYC/KYB gaps, are being addressed through the KYC Refresh initiative, a major cross-functional program scheduled for delivery in H2 2026. The initiative includes:
 Periodic merchant re-verification (annual, 3-year, 5-year), based on risk.
 Enhanced beneficial ownership change tracking, aligned with regulatory expectations.
 New self-service ownership transfer capability, classified as a “should-have” for the 2026 launch.
 Dedicated support from Compliance, Legal, Engineering, Design, and contractor resources.
Not an Issue Justification:
This potential Issue was not identified by Complaints and is being remediated under the KYC/KYB Refresher. 
 Square’s TOA limitations, along with broader KYC/KYB gaps, are being addressed through the KYC Refresh initiative, a major cross-functional program scheduled for delivery in H2 2026. The initiative includes:

Issue Title: Inability to authenticate/support out of network individuals
Issue Description:
Block currently lacks consistent mechanisms to authenticate or support non-customers and out-of-network individuals who cannot successfully complete our authentication processes. Different parts of the business have recognized this issue in their own domains, and they have attempted to remediate it in silos, leading to inconsistent outcomes for our customer and non-customer base. This is fundamentally a consumer protection, regulatory, and reputational risk that cuts across products and intake channels.
 Examples of where this problem has already manifested include:
 Identity Theft Reports (Tax Documents): Non-customers reporting identity theft after receiving tax forms generated by Cash App.
 Identity Theft Reports (Cash App Card): Individuals issued Cash App cards who report they never opened an account.
 Customer Support Voice Intake: Callers who cannot pass PII verification and are therefore unable to receive assistance.
 Pools: Out-of-network contributors who fail PII verification and cannot be supported.
 To address this risk, we need a unified, consistent framework—revisiting prior remediation efforts and ensuring all future solutions align—so that anyone who engages with Block receives the same treatment and support.
RCA Description:
While non-customers primarily contact us through the Voice IVR channel (as Chat is unavailable to them), our current systems and processes cannot reliably differentiate between true non-customer contacts and customer contacts where Voice PII authentication failed. This limitation impacts Cash App’s ability to
 Track and analyze non-customer contact themes to proactively identify and address emerging issues
 Monitor potential fraud and scam patterns reported to Cash directly by non-customers
 Enhance reporting used by the CFPB working groups
Remediation Strategy: There is already a cross-functional project team actively working on remediation that will address the concerns listed in this issue and more.
Not an Issue Justification: After we created the draft issue we found out there is already a cross-functional project team actively working on remediation. A review of the project documentations and conversation with the project manager ensured remediation will cover the areas this issue called out. Currently members of the CP team attends the project meetings. Additionally several members of CP are listed on the project documentation for approval.

Issue Title: Lack of process to accommodate persons with disabilities while verifying identity
Issue Description:
An accessibility-related complaint has helped identify a gap in how we support customers with disabilities. A customer, citing a disability, expressed frustration on LinkedIn about their inability to resolve a service problem. A Cash Complaints Operations Team (CCOT) Group Lead found related cases tied to the customer - each with accessibility-related concerns. 
 The customer was trying to authenticate themselves but their disability prevented them from being able to go through Cash App’s normal process, which is taking a selfie with your ID. The customer is unable to lift their arm to take selfies. 
 This specific issue can be solved with more inclusive options for verifying identity. Additionally, as part of resolving this issue, Block’s obligations to the ADA need to be identified and a risk assessment needs to take place to determine if there are additional gaps that need to be addressed in a separate issue(s).
RCA Description:
Inaccessible ID Verification for Customers with Disabilities 
 

 Contributing Factor 1: People-based shortcomings 
 Cause 1: Insufficient training of ADA procedures for frontline and escalation teams 
 Cause 2: Unclear ADA related responsibilities 
 

 Contributing Factor 2: Process design weaknesses 
 Cause 1: Lack of standard escalation paths for accessibility concerns
 

 Contributing Factor 3: Product Design
 Cause 1: The App requires physical movement to verify identity
 Cause 2: The authentication process offers no accessible alternatives to selfie ID verification
 

 Contributing Factor 3: Technology limitations 
 Cause 1: Inadequate app accessibility
 Cause 2: weak case search infrastructure 
 

 These factors collectively contribute to control breakdowns, unresolved complaints, and heightened operational and reputational risk.
Remediation Strategy:
-Develop and implement alternative accessible verification methods
 -Train front line staff how to handle such cases
 -Conduct an ADA Risk Assessment
 -Establish ADA Policies and Procedures
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: The impact was isolated to one person. We ultimately able to assist the person after they reached out to a Block employee on LinkedIn
Issue Action Plans:
Create an alternate process for ID Verification for Customers (1032504)
 Create an ADA Policy (1032506)

Issue Title: Cash App Taxes Alignment GCO ETS Extension
Issue Description:
An Extension of a April 2024 Exception Request
 

 The current complaints logging and processing forms in Cash App Taxes' Salesforce instance are not aligned with the standards set by the Global Complaints Program or the structure used in CF-1. This misalignment creates inconsistencies in data capture, limits visibility across programs, and hinders our ability to report and manage complaints effectively at an enterprise level. Updating these forms is essential to ensure standardization, improve data integrity, and enable more efficient escalation and resolution workflows.
 

 To ensure accuracy, efficiency, alignment with updated process expectations, and reduce advocate ambiguity, we need to revise the complaints forms to streamline inputs, improve field clarity, and incorporate logic-driven guidance where possible.
 

 An Exception to the Standard was requested in April 2024 as the above does not meet GCO’s Standards and they are working to meet the Standard. 
 

 The required solution to the Exception was not complete in the original allotted time and an extension was requested and granted.
Explanation for Delay in Issue Creation: Issue owner was offsite so it took an extra week to compete the final details of the Issue Intake and RCA
RCA Description:
Technology and Process 
 

 When the Global Complaints Program launched in August 2023, the business decided that, due to bandwidth constraints and technical limitations, certain teams would be temporarily excluded from transitioning to the new process. 
 

 Cash App Taxes (CAT) was among those exempted because it operates on a separate Salesforce instance with restricted data-sharing capabilities.
 The exemption was intended to last no more than 6–12 months, but ongoing system dependencies and resource constraints have required CAT to repeatedly request extensions beyond the original timeframe. Although exempt, the team has continuously worked to align complaint-handling processes as closely as possible with the Global Complaint Standard (GCS) while remaining in a state of iterative refinement.
 

 During the most recent Business Requirements Document (BRD) submission for new tooling, it was discovered that CAT’s exemption had inadvertently lapsed in March 2025, leaving the team technically out of compliance until integration and tooling updates can be completed.
 

 The Action Plan will address the root cause of ongoing system dependencies and resource constraints in order for Cash App Taxes to be in compliance with the Global Compliance Standard.
Remediation Strategy:
1. Strategic Alignment & Risk Management
 

 Establish formal CAT complaint policy aligned with GCP
 Enhance regulatory compliance framework
 Improve enterprise-wide visibility
 Strengthen risk assessment capabilities
 

 2. Process & Technology Enhancement
 

 Implement two-tiered resolution system
 Early Resolution (5-day SLA)
 Formal Resolution (15-day SLA)
 Modernize complaint classification
 Enhance data quality and reporting
 

 3. Operational Excellence
 

 Streamline complaint handling processes
 Improve tracking and monitoring capabilities
 Enhance quality assurance framework
Impacted Business Area: CCO Cash App Tax Group Lead
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: While the Cash App Taxes complaint forms are not yet aligned with the Global Complaints Standard, the issue is localized, governed through an approved exception, and does not cause operational or regulatory disruption. Because complaint processing continues effectively and the deviation is temporary and well-contained, this issue can be reasonably classified as Low Severity.
Issue Action Plans: Cash App Taxes Complaint Management Framework Alignment (1006606)

Issue Title: Cash App Reopened Complaints GCO ETS Extension
Issue Description:
An Extension of a April 2024 Exception Request
 

 Cash App’s complaint management system lacks standardized processes and sufficient technical capability to effectively manage reopened and duplicate complaints. The system does not automatically identify, flag, route, or link repeat complaints, nor can it merge related cases. Built primarily for initial intake and first-time resolutions, it has no workflow triggers, escalation paths, or defined procedures for reopened issues. These limitations prevent visibility into a customer’s complaint history and hinder recognition of unresolved or recurring concerns.
 Manual handling further compounds the problem — complaint handlers have limited tools and capacity to identify or consolidate duplicate cases, often leading to overlapping investigations. Resource shortages, including insufficient specialist staffing and manual review capacity, exacerbate inefficiencies. Consequently, customers may receive conflicting or inconsistent responses, resulting in confusion, frustration, and diminished confidence in the complaint process.
 

 An Exception to the Standard was requested in April 2024 as the above does not meet GCO’s Standards and they are working to meet the Standard. 
 

 The required solution to the Exception was not complete in the original allotted time and an extension was requested and granted.
RCA Description:
Technology and People
 

 Technology: The difficulty in operationalizing reopened complaints stems from the creation of duplicate complaint records within Cash App’s CRM system (CF1). The current system design does not support the ability to merge, link, or attach duplicate and reopened complaints, resulting in fragmented case management. The core issue is a functional limitation within the CRM tool itself.
 

 People: Advocates are not currently trained or equipped to holistically resolve customer issues or to identify when a customer has submitted a prior complaint within a defined timeframe. This gap makes it difficult to operationalize the process for reopening complaints. Although a 2024 exception routes reopened complaints to a specialized team, this serves only as a temporary workaround. The existing process relies on customers explicitly requesting to “reopen” a complaint, rather than enabling.
Remediation Strategy:
To address these challenges, a comprehensive Reopened and Duplicate Complaint Management framework is proposed:
 Automated detection and flagging of both reopened and duplicate complaints within case management systems.
 Routing protocols that direct reopened or duplicate cases to specialized teams or escalation paths for appropriate oversight.
 Enhanced investigation procedures to ensure repeated issues receive deeper review and higher quality resolutions.
 Assignment of complex cases to designated teams with relevant expertise.
 Centralized tracking and reporting on reopened and duplicate complaint metrics to monitor trends and drive improvements.
 Merging duplicate complaints so customers receive a unified, thorough response to their concern—streamlining communication and reducing confusion.
 

 Implementing these enhancements will standardize complaint resolution processes, reduce customer frustration, and create a clearer, more efficient experience for Cash App users.
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: This issue represents a contained, process-level inefficiency rather than a compliance, financial, or reputational risk. While remediation is necessary to improve complaint handling and customer experience, the impact is minimal and localized, justifying a Low Severity classification.

Issue Title: SEV-6022: Duplicate Cash-In Caused Unexpected Instant Transfer Fees & Potential Linked Bank Overdraft Fees
Issue Description: Between 2/1/25 and 7/30/25 some iOS customers who intended to do a singular cash-in from their linked bank into their Cash App balance, had two cash-ins processed off of this singular transaction request. Some customers experienced undetected duplicate cash-ins, potentially triggering overdraft fees at their linked banks (activity that Block cannot directly monitor). In response, some customers attempted to reverse the duplicate cash-in by initiating instant transfers back to their accounts, which resulted in additional, unintended instant transfer fees. The business team has identified affected customers and refunded their instant transfer fees, marking these refunds with the note "instant transfer fee refund." However, no customer communications had been issued about this Block incident, including details about when the issue began and when it was resolved. As a result, customers remained uninformed about their ability to request refunds for any related overdraft fees incurred at their linked banks. The customer’s singular cash-in should have resulted in only one cash-in (not two). Discovered internally on 6/2/25 (4 months after the incorrect code was released on 2/1/25) while testing out another feature. After connecting with the Insights Team, it was found that ~18K customers had complained about dupe cash-ins during the impact period.
RCA Description: A Cash App system problem may have caused customers to be harmed financially (via overdraft fees) when their cash-in transaction was duplicated. Between 2/1/25 and 7/29/25, we occasionally processed two cash-ins (pulling from a customer's linked external bank account), when only one was requested. Transfers were initiated twice on the same flow id, but with different external ids (different idempotency keys), and as a result were treated as separate cash-in attempt. While the FaceID hurdle was happening, a customer could tap the transfer CTA again (should not be possible), triggering a second cash-in attempt. The issue was linked to the roll-out of the new More Ways to Add Money UX. Technology / Inadequately executed Technology solution.
Remediation Strategy:
- Action #1: Provide evidence of systemic containment/fix (what mitigated risk of further impacts). This solution automatically detects duplication and prevents processing / Owner: Trevor Luong / Target Date: 7/29/25 
  - Action #2*: Provide evidence on those likely impacted by an instant transfer fee where we proactively refunded/credited back the one instant transfer fee amount / Owner: Trevor Luong / Target Date: 8/4/25 
  - Action #3a: Implement Server Monitoring alert for unintended double cash-ins / Owner: Candice McCollough / Target Date: 8/5/25 
  - Action #3b: Implement Client Monitoring for unintended double cash-ins (both iOS and Android users). This entails:monitor parameters outside 99% range, Alerting #transfers-mobile-alerts and Response strategy / Owner: Kevin Veloso / Target Date: 7/31/25 
  - Action #4: Align with Legal and Compliance on the communication strategy / Owner: Trevor Luong / Target Date: 8/25/25 
  - Action #5: Pull & provide final impacted population (including Customer Token ID, dupe cash-in amount & where applicable instant transfer fee amount refunded), breaking into the following cohorts: Cohort A: Complained and Cohort B: Has Not Complained / Owner: Trevor Luong / Target Date: 10/30/25 
  - Action #6: Align with Legal & Compliance on: Email Comms content for Cohort A, Regulator comment content for both Cohorts (A&B), Knowledge Article Content (ChaunCharae' Burch owning this sub-action) / Owner: Trevor Luong / Target Date: 10/31/25
  - Action #7: Submit Content Team request to: Have SendSafely Drop Zone & Link created and set up via Quicktext (can take up to 8 weeks to set up) , Publish related KA (to include reference to the Sendsafely link) / Owner: ChaunCharae' Burch / Target Date: 11/26/25 
  - Action #8: Have bulk Account Level Comment applied via Regulator for advocate readiness (Dependent on completion of Action #7) / Owner: Trevor Luong / Target Date: 11/26/25 
 - Action #9: Send batch email communications to Cohort A Customers via Regulator (Dependent on completion of Action #7) / Owner: Trevor Luong / Target Date: 1/9/26
  - Action #10: IM Analyst to perform closure activities (collect all supporting evidence, perform email comm validation and have internal QA performed). / Owner: Georgia Desper / Target Date: 1/30/26
Issue Type: Gap – a needed process or control is missing entirely.
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure resulting in an Impact to regulatory obligations
Issue Progress Updates: Pre-closure validation completed after Action Plan Owner (Trevor Luong) provided all necessary supporting evidence which has been loaded as an attachment (Issue Summary Pre-Closure Package) and has been reviewed/approved by Issue Manager, Josh King.
Issue Action Plans: SEV-6022: Duplicate Cash-In Caused Unexpected Instant Transfer Fees & Potential Linked Bank Overdraft Fees (1003962)
Closure Request Justification: See closure attachment - all action plan requirements have been completed.
Issue Internal Validation Justification: Issue completed in line with remediation put forward

Issue Title: Lack of documented processes and procedures related to Square's Terms of Service (TOS)
Issue Description: GCMT assessed the adequacy of internal controls to avoid UDAP concerns, noting management has not documented processes related to Square's Terms of Service (TOS) disclosures as it relates to their creation, updates, review and approval, as well as their circulation to merchants. Management has not formalized procedure standards specifying roles and responsibilities of key functions (such as Legal, Compliance, Product, etc.) for the creation of new or modifications to Terms of Service disclosures, or requirements for retaining evidence of TOS review and approval. GCMT reviewed Square's General Terms of Service, and Terms related to Payments, Bill Pay, and Point of Sale noting the reviewers and approvers of the disclosures were not documented and evidence of their review and approval was not retained.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Lack of documented processes for the processes being conducted.
Remediation Strategy: Current processes related to creation, updates, review, approval and circulation of Terms of Services should be documented to avoid future issues.
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: Failure to document the processes in effect may result in non adherence to regulatory expectations (i.e items that are not necessarily stated explicitly in law or regulatory guidance, more open to interpretation)
Issue Action Plans: Formalized SOP for Terms of Service (TOS) (1005950)

Issue Title: The TOS disclosures contain incomplete sentences, vague language and non-working website links
Issue Description:
GCMT reviewed Square's General Terms of Service (TOS), and Terms related to Payments, Bill Pay and Point of Sale, noting that certain disclosures are not aligned with existing practices, or the disclosures contain incomplete sentences, vague language and non-working website links that may create confusion for customers. Examples include:
 

 1) Practices do not align with Terms of Service: 
 i. Square's General Terms of Service states that merchant payouts are scheduled to automatically initiate at 8:00 PM Eastern Time (ET) / 5:00 PM Pacific Time (PT), with exceptions only for merchants who have requested custom settlement times. However, transaction testing revealed that 16 of 20 payments were initiated after this time, indicating an inconsistency between the Terms of Service and the operational practices.
 ii. Management informed GCMT that the merchant’s use of a service 30 days after the notification of a new or revised term is deemed as an acceptance of the terms; however, this is not clearly stated in the General Terms of Service. Currently, the General TOS states "Your continued use of the Services after the posting of a Revised Version constitutes your acceptance of such Revised Version" but does not specify the 30 day timeline.
 

 2) Non Working, Incorrect or Missing Links: 
 i. Payment Terms of Service: Includes a reference to a non-working link which is supposed to provide details on pricing prior to 12/6/23. 
 ii.Point of Sale Terms of Service: Does not reference the General Terms of Services in "Other Sections" or "Other Terms". GCMT noted, the General TOS is referenced in the Terms of Service for Payments, and Invoices. It is important to reference the General Terms of Service to ensure that the merchant is aware of all TOS binding requirements.
 iii.Point of Sale Terms of Service: Includes non-working hyperlinks in the following sections; Gift Card Load Fees (section 2 - Gift Cards), Email Marketing Guidelines (section 7 - Email Marketing Service), and Use of Sendgrid (section 7 - Email Marketing Service).
 

 3) Vague Language:
 i. Point of Sale Terms of Service: Informs the customer that the auto-renew feature for the subscription can be turned off, but fails to inform the customer how to cancel the subscription auto-renewal. 
 ii. Point of Sale Terms of Service: Informs the customer that they may call the help center for cancellation requests; however, a telephone number is not provided. 
 iii. Point of Sale Terms of Service: States "We also reserve the right to limit or cease phone or chat support to you if you consume a disproportionate amount of such customer services or otherwise negatively affect our ability to provide support to other customers (as determined in our sole discretion)", but does not provide examples of activities that may negatively affect the ability of Square to provide support to the merchant. 
 iv. Square General Terms of Service: The term 'days' is used multiple times across the Terms of Services reviewed. In certain instances, the disclosures fail to explain how “days” are defined. For example, the General Terms Of Service (section 10 - Paid Services) states "We reserve the right to change our Subscription Fee upon thirty (30) days’ advance notice", but does not specify if the term 'days' represents business days, calendar days or working days. The Payment Terms Of Service (section 11 - Payout Schedule) states "Square will initiate a payout of Proceeds received on non-business days before the next business day", but does not specify if business days and non-business days applies to the merchant's days of operation or Square's. 
 

 4) Incomplete Sentences:
 i. Typos including incomplete sentences (Square General TOS) and asterisk symbols (Bill Pay TOS) without further clarification were observed. For e.g. The Bill Pay TOS states that “You agree that you are responsible for any Tax information reporting requirements with respect to payments made to your Vendors and you agree for any such Tax information reporting requirements that we are not acting as your broker, payor, middleman, or similar intermediary, and we expressly disclaim any responsibility for such Tax information reporting except to the extent required by applicable law.**”, however no additional details associated to the asterisks have not been provided
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Certain disclosures are not aligned with existing practices, or the disclosures contain incomplete sentences, vague language and non-working website links that may create confusion for customers.
Remediation Strategy: GCMT recommends Legal to update the applicable Terms of Services sections as stated in the finding.
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: Inaccurate TOS may result in non adherence to regulatory expectations (i.e items that are not necessarily stated explicitly in law or regulatory guidance, more open to interpretation)
Issue Action Plans: Updates to Terms of Services (TOS) (1005952)

Business Unit: CCO Afterpay Customer Service - Group Lead
Issue Title: Customer Success and Complaint Management Agent adherence Standard Operating Procedures for complaint acknowledgement (service level agreement), complaint tiering and complaint reporting fields can be improved
Issue Description:
Background;
 GCMT conducted testing on a sample of 100 complaint tickets across Tiers 1, 2 and 3 to verify that all required processes documented within the Complaint Standard Operating Procedures were followed by the Customer Success and/or Complaint Management Agent as part of the complaint handling and resolution. 
 

 GCMT identified three key complaint areas where the effectiveness rate across all tiers was under 85%. 
 

 Complaint Area #2 – Complaint Acknowledgement 
 Procedure Requirement: The "IDR Complaint Response Timeframes" Standard Operating Procedure requires Clearpay to acknowledge all complaints within 1 business day or 24 hours. There are two categories of acknowledgement 1 - Automatic (This can be verified in the events of the ticket) and 2 - Manual - This must be done by the team member when responding to the customer.
 

 Complaint Area #2 Issue:
 GCMT identified an 85% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 15% exception rate where a complaint acknowledgement was not sent or sent outside the required SLA. These results are broken down below by complaint tier. 
 

 Tier 3: GCMT tested a sample of 40 Tier 3 complaints and identified 9 exceptions (22%) where the customer was not sent a complaint acknowledgement, or the complaint acknowledgement was sent outside the required timeframes. This consisted of the following breakdown: 
 GCMT identified 2 exceptions where the customer was not sent a complaint acknowledgement at all. Of these 2 complaints, 1 was received through the Complaint Form, however no Event was noted in Zendesk, and the other was due to the Customer Success agent not sending a manual acknowledgement. Per investigation by Complaint Management and Service Excellence this was due to a GAP in the system trigger for auto acknowledgements, where there was a mismatch between the complaint form and region. A fix was implemented on the 18th of September. Further investigation to determine impacted customers is currently underway. 
 GCMT identified 7 exceptions where a complaint acknowledgement was sent by the Customer Success agent however this was not completed within the 1 business day/24-hour SLA. 
 3 exceptions where the Complaint Acknowledgement was sent outside the SLA by 1 day 
 3 exceptions where the Complaint Acknowledgement was sent outside the SLA by 2 days 
 1 exception where the Complaint Acknowledgement was sent outside the SLA by 4 days
 

 Tier 2:
 GCMT tested a sample of 30 Tier 2 complaints identified 3 exceptions (10%) where the complaint acknowledgement was sent outside the required timeframes. This consisted of the following breakdown: 
 GCMT identified 3 exceptions where a complaint acknowledgement was sent by the Customer Success agent however this was not completed within the 1 business day/24-hour SLA. 
 1 exception where the Complaint Acknowledgement was sent outside the SLA by 1 day 
 2 exceptions where the Complaint Acknowledgement was sent outside the SLA by 2 days 
 

 Tier 1:
 GCMT tested a sample of 30 Tier 1 complaints and identified 3 exceptions (10%) where a complaint acknowledgement was sent by the Customer Success agent however this was not completed within the 1 business day/24-hour SLA. 
 3 exceptions where the Complaint Acknowledgement was sent outside the SLA by 1 day
 

 Complaint Area #3 – Complaint Tiering
 Procedure Requirement (Tier 2): The "How to Update Complaint Fields" Standard Operating Procedure and "Escalation Pathways" guide defines a Tier 2 complaint where the complaint is a reopened complaint, the customer requests a supervisor/manager or escalation or where the customer has contacted Clearpay multiple times and there is escalating frustration, all SOPS have been exhausted by the agent and where the customer has tried all available troubleshooting however the issue remains unresolved. 
 

 Procedure Requirement (Tier 1): The "How to Update Complaint Fields" Standard Operating Procedure and "Escalation Pathways" guide defines a Tier 1 complaint where the complaint is the most severe type of complaint. These complaints could mean possible legal implications for Afterpay | Clearpay.
 

 The "How to Update Complaint Fields" Standard Operating Procedure requires the application of the "Unfair Treatment" Legal tag where the following is met "Unfair, Deceptive, Abusive Acts or Practices occurs where the customer or complainant believes that our practices, policies or servicing does not offer consumer protection or violates your country’s consumer protection laws. Discrimination falls under this category and is the unfair or prejudicial treatment of individuals or groups based on any of the following: race, religion, national origin, gender, family status, sexual orientation, age.
 

 Complaint Area # 3 Issue:
 GCMT identified an 82% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 18% exception rate where the incorrect tier was recorded by the customer success/complaint management agent. This consisted of the following breakdown:
 

 Tier 2: GCMT tested a sample of 30 Tier 2 complaints and identified 3 exceptions (10%) did not meet the definition of a Tier 2 complaint and should have been reported as a Tier 3. 
 

 Tier 1: GCMT tested a sample of 30 Tier 1 complaints and identified 15 exceptions (50%) did not meet the definition of a Tier 1 complaint and/or the Unfair Treatment tag should not have been applied. 
 For 7 of the 15 complaints which were incorrectly classified as Tier 1 per the definition included in the Complaint Reporting Fields SOP and the Escalations pathway SOP, these were due to the agent incorrectly interpreting the classification of unfair treatment 
 A common theme is that customers will note "unfair" in their complaint response, and this is then marked by CSAs as unfair treatment despite no other indicators present per the SOP i.e. discrimination or consumer protection breaches. Of these 7 complaints, 5 complaints had the "unfair treatment" tag applied.
 For 8 of the 15 complaints there were no triggers present or indications about why the complaint would have been classified as Tier 1
 

 Complaint Area #6 – Complaint Reporting Fields 
 Procedure Requirement: The "How to Update Complaint Fields" Standard Operating Procedure requires agents to complete the mandatory fields in the Zendesk Ticket prior to the closure of a ticket.
 

 Complaint Area #6 Issue:
 GCMT identified an 75% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 25% exception rate where the complaint fields entered by the agent were incorrect based on the reviewed complaint. These results are broken down below by complaint tier.
 

 Tier 3: GCMT tested a sample of 40 Tier 3 complaints to verify that prior to the closure of a complaint on Zendesk, that the mandatory complaint fields were being updated by the agent, with the selection accurate based on the nature of the complaint and the provided resolution. 
 

 GCMT identified 13 exceptions where the complaint fields entered by the agent were incorrect based on the reviewed complaint. This consisted of the following breakdown: 
 4 exceptions where the root cause was incorrect 
 5 exceptions where the secondary complaint field was not updated or was incorrect 
 3 exceptions where the primary issue was incorrect 
 1 exception where the resulting action was incorrect
 

 Tier 2: GCMT tested a sample of 30 Tier 2 complaints to verify that prior to the closure of a complaint on Zendesk, that the mandatory complaint fields were being updated by the agent, with the selection accurate based on the nature of the complaint and the provided resolution. 
 GCMT identified 7 exceptions where the complaint fields entered by the agent was incorrect based on the reviewed complaint. This consisted of the following breakdown: 
 1 exception where the root cause was incorrect 
 2 exceptions where the secondary complaint field was not updated or was incorrect 
 3 exceptions where the primary issue was incorrect 
 1 exception where the resulting action was incorrect
 

 Tier 1: GCMT tested a sample of 30 Tier 1 complaints to verify that prior to the closure of a complaint on Zendesk, that the mandatory complaint fields were being updated by the agent, with the selection accurate based on the nature of the complaint and the provided resolution. 
 GCMT identified 14 exceptions where the complaint fields entered by the agent was incorrect based on the reviewed complaint. This consisted of the following breakdown: 
 2 exceptions where the root cause was incorrect 
 3 exceptions where the primary issue was incorrect 
 5 exceptions where the resulting action was incorrect

Issue Title: Clawback reversals on chargebacks cancellations: Ongoing customer remediation
Issue Description:
In 2024, an issue was identified related to Cash Apps handling of chargebacks. As part of Cash Apps chargeback handling, Cash App attempts to claw back funds from customers both when a new chargeback is opened and again if the chargeback is ultimately lost. However, sometimes a “lost” chargeback isn’t always final, customers or their bank may cancel the chargeback outside of the chargebacks handling timeline. When this happens after a successful clawback, Cash App ends up with more money than it should: both the clawed-back amount and the refunded chargeback.
 

 In these cases, Cash App programmatically reverses the original clawback to ensure funds are returned appropriately and avoid over-collection.
 

 Up until August 18, 2025, this was only addressed manually when a customer contacts Cash App. Prior analysis shows most customers didn’t reach out, or their cases weren’t correctly routed, resulting in poor user experiences. An automated process was established, and essential to improving customer trust and ensuring fair outcomes. 
 

 Historically,Cash Apps lack of established processes resulted in ~$8.4m in over collected funds since 2018. Please note, these numbers are approximate based on available data.
 

 An original issue was lodged relating to the project plan to implement an automated solution (see: #1223147). This issue has now been closed on August 18, 2025 as the project has rolled out to 100%. A subsequent issue will be raised on the same day related to the customer remediation plan.
RCA Description: Issue record was incorrectly opened, remediation details have been included in the original issue record (ID # 1223147).
Remediation Strategy: Issue record was incorrectly opened, remediation details have been included in the original issue record (ID # 1223147).
Not an Issue Justification: Issue record was incorrectly opened, remediation details have been included in the original issue record (ID # 1223147).

Issue Title: CCOT Internal Complaints Backlog SLA Breach
Issue Description: The CCOT Internal Complaints team is currently managing a significant backlog of approximately 25,000 complaints, with a substantial volume of complaints exceeding our 15-business-day SLA ( ~14,000), with a subset of that volume also surpassing our extended 45-business-day SLA ( ~11,000). CCOT - Internal Complaints has seen a substantial increase in incoming volume, from roughly 2,500 complaints per month in January to over 14,000 complaints per month by July. CCOT was aware of changes that would increase the internal complaint volume and forecasted staffing around the forecasted complaint volume increase. Compliant volume increased more than the forecasted staffing number.
RCA Description:
People, Process, and Technology
 

 CCOT’s Internal Complaints has seen a substantial increase in incoming volume, from roughly 2,500 complaints per month in January to over 14,000 complaints per month by July. This led to the incoming complaint volume outpacing the capacity of the team.
 This increase is primarily attributed to:
 Policy Changes: Updates to UDAAP policies expanded the criteria for allegations that qualify as Tier 1 complaints.
 Increased Complaint Identification Rate: Targeted Operational and Product-Led efforts to increase Complaint ID rates by +~30% in 2025.
 Tooling Enhancements: The rollout of ML-powered complaint alerts in our voice channel has led to a higher identification rate of complaints.
 This has created a backlog that must be resolved to restore service levels. This document outlines the comprehensive strategy we are executing to mitigate this risk.
Remediation Strategy:
Immediate Backlog Reduction: making an immediate and significant impact on our aged, out-of-SLA backlog: 
 Building Long-Term Efficiency Gains: future-proofing our operation by creating more sustainable workflows for Downgraded Complaints and adopting an Abbreviated Handling process for Low-Risk Complaints to reduce manual effort
 Unlocking Capacity: creating capacity within our existing operation by optimizing high-volume manual processes, including UDAAP Triage and Manual ML ID Rate Reviews
 Scaling for the Future: addresses the root cause of the backlog—incoming volume outpacing our capacity. Through a strategic and aggressive BPO hiring plan, we will scale to meet current and future demand.
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: The majority(29,228) of the complaints related to this issue are classified as Tier 1, which represents the highest level of complaint severity. While it is significant, the impact is contained within the Cash App environment and does not extend to other parts of the organization. There is already a robust, data-driven remediation plan in place to address the root causes. This means the team is actively working on solutions and that the path to resolution is well-defined. Because of this structured approach, the severity can be reasonably set to moderate, as the issue is on a clear path to being managed and mitigated.

Issue Title: Cross Brand False Positive Denylisting
Issue Description:
Account denylisting and deactivation are top complaint drivers across Block brands (Cash App, Square, Afterpay). Common pain points include unclear notifications, false positives, inconsistent communication, and limited appeal visibility.
 

 Cash App: Customers report unexpected account restrictions impacting direct deposits and recurring payments. Review of 200 complaints (100 Risk, 100 Compliance) showed false positives and communication gaps. “Big Sweep Phase 2” contributed to most overturned Risk decisions; ~50% of Compliance complaints involved false positives.
 

 Square: Multiple teams (Risk, Compliance Ops, Sanctions, Transaction Monitoring) can deactivate accounts, leading to false positives and operational disruption. $4.1M GTV was frozen, with an average 27.7-day interruption. High-value clients ($100K+) accounted for 79.5% of the financial impact. 96% of cases reviewed were reinstated.
 

 Afterpay: Lower volume, but similar false positive trends. Inconsistent reinstatement and communication practices across regions affect customer credit access and experience.
 

 Themes Across Brands:
 

 Communication: Generic or unclear responses; limited support visibility.
 

 Appeals: Fragmented, slow, and inconsistent processes across teams and brands.
 

 Impact: Financial and operational disruption for both individuals and businesses, damaging trust and brand reputation.
 

 

 There is an opportunity to standardize communication, appeal handling, and decision transparency across brands to improve customer experience and ensure consistency in denylisting practices.
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: Systemic Quality Oversight Gaps in Risk Operations Post-Jan 2024 Headcount Reductions
Issue Description:
The Quality Management function for Block Customer Operations identified systemic oversight gaps in independent quality checks across multiple high-priority operational areas following January 2024 headcount reductions. The issue was discovered through a comprehensive leadership review conducted by Quality Management leadership under Mario Castillo's direction, in collaboration with operations leaders and external vendor management teams.
 

 Specific Deficiencies Identified:
 - Risk & Disputes Operations: Multiple dispute types and loss-document workflows exhibit limited internal quality checks; "audit-the-auditor" spot checks reduced from ~10 to 6-8 per reviewer
 - Banking Operations: Transaction monitoring for partner banks, ACH/wire recalls, check deposit returns, and risk reviews have limited oversight; team leads responsible for excessive domain coverage
 - Scams & Account Integrity: Coverage gaps in estates, deceased customer handling, power of attorney, escheatment, and scam-related queues
 - Quality Management Systems: Automated quality checks, contact reason analysis, and summarization tools underutilized due to lack of dedicated ownership
Explanation for Delay in Issue Creation: This was originally under issue 1297067. However, QM for Risk Operations recently transitioned to the Risk org. We are opening a separate issue as it relates to the Risk Ops portion of 1297067.
RCA Description:
Significant headcount reductions were implemented in 2024 that impacted the Quality Management (QM) team, without a corresponding reduction in workload or workflow complexity. As customer volume and the number of operational workflows continued to expand, the remaining QM team was unable to maintain sufficient coverage or consistency in independent quality assurance reviews. Multiple regulated and high-risk workflows (e.g., Disputes, Banking Operations, Scams, Account Integrity, and Escheatment) were left with insufficient QA oversight. This created a gap that increases the likelihood of undetected operational errors, inaccurate customer outcomes, and potential non-compliance with regulatory obligations (e.g., EFTA/Reg E, partner bank requirements, and unclaimed property laws).
 

 The headcount reduction also indirectly caused several secondary issues:
 - Expansion of work allocated to external review partners without dedicated internal oversight to ensure alignment of standards and prompt issue resolution
 - Fewer "audit-the-auditor" checks and calibrations, resulting in reduced scoring consistency and diminished feedback loops
 - A planned statistical sampling program has not yet been fully implemented; current manual sampling methods are inadequate for reliable results in certain areas
 - An insufficient number of team leads covering an excessive number of domains, leading to bottlenecks
 

 Supporting Evidence:
 - Leadership review conducted post-headcount reduction (January 2024) identified entire operational areas with no active QA coverage
 - Documentation from QM leadership and operations partners highlighted suspended reviews, reduced calibration frequencies, etc.
 - Staffing and coverage data indicated a reduction in QA headcount while supported workflows increased, confirming the mismatch between resourcing and workload growth
 - Internal review reports and vendor management updates confirmed inconsistent scoring outcomes and delayed resolution of quality findings, indicating weakened oversight
Remediation Strategy: Overall, remediation will be achieved through the upskilling of current QM agents to cover existing gaps in QM evaluation in relevant business areas. This will start with the assessment of business areas that are equipped for QM evaluations, and the strategy for freeing up bandwidth of existing agents who can step into those new spaces. QM will use risk-based prioritization to address identified deficiencies and expansion of adequate coverage. Once these steps are validated, a roadmap for agent upskilling will be developed and then carried out by QM leadership and business area SMEs. The end goal of the action plan will be to evidence risks the business is willing to accept in order to increase the bandwidth of existing QM agents, as well as the completion of the necessary upskilling in new coverage areas and enhanced evaluation strategy moving forward.

 CCO QA - Team Lead
 CCO QM - Group Lead
 CCO QM - Program Manager
 CCO Quality Control - Analyst
 CCO Quality Control - Team Lead

 Risk Ops - Team Lead

The moderate rating appropriately reflects the operational risk and need for timely remediation without indicating organization-wide critical consequences.

Issue Title: AU Customer breached the $120 Plus Late Fee Cap
Issue Description: Compliance Monitoring for September 2025 identified one customer that breached the $120 Plus Late Fee Cap in Australia
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description:
The code which was originally built with regard to the complexity of the product and volume of transactions, did not support the significant change in transaction volume which was encountered due to expected anticipated business growth.
 The increase volume caused the code which appropriately operated under previous volumes to lock in the system to come after the fee calculation, where multiple fees were able to be calculated, and charged within milliseconds of another which did not capture both (almost concurrent) calculations, resulting in two fees to be applied based of the sale cumulative late fee calculation which did not factor in both (almost concurrent) fee calculations, breaching the fee cap. Where the code applied correctly under previous volumes, the lock in system to prevent concurrent fees to be applied prior to the calculation being conducted, preventing the historical fee cap structure to be charged twice.
 This issue was not identified in recurring testing due to the testing not being able to reproduce the significant volume which applied, and was considered an unintended consequence of ongoing business growth.
Remediation Strategy:
The code fix was deployed to production as a part of ap-core-release-2541 on 17th Oct 2025, preventing any further overcharging of late fees.
 

 All impacted customers have had the excess fees refunded.
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Rated in accordance with issue management program
Issue Action Plans: Determine root cause and deploy fix (1005288)
Closure Request Justification: MAP complete - DRI confirmed no additional issues emerged from monitoring, from the same root cause - No additional fix was required
Issue Internal Validation Justification: MAP confirmed as completed

Issue Title: Potential gap in Section 311 List Screening coverage for certain international transactions
Issue Description:
It has been identified that current compliance screening processes may not fully address Section 311 (31 CFR §1010.651) screening requirements across all relevant transaction types. Remittances are screened through our bank partner, Dandelion, and Chainalysis provides real-time Section 311 screening and blocking for all digital asset, blockchain transactions. A potential compliance gap exists for International ACH transactions managed through partner banks, such as Sutton, as their specific screening protocols remain unconfirmed.
 

 Initial discussions with the FinCrimes Legal team indicate that although 311 screening obligations formally apply to U.S. legal entities, the requirements are unclear for U.S.-based Money Services Businesses (MSBs) with global operations. Further legal analysis is required to determine what potential controls may be required to comply completely with FinCEN 311 including identification of instances wherein Block has a direct responsibility and instances wherein Block may rely on a Partner or Vendor to execute.
RCA Description: Screening control coverage for Section 311-listed entities was designed primarily around OFAC and other sanctions lists. While Block third-party partners (e.g., Dandelion, Chainalysis) maintain appropriate 311 controls for their domains, enterprise-level coverage was not formally assessed or extended to all Block payment modalities (notably International ACH). Relying on an assumption that, as a US based MSB processing primarily U.S.-domiciled transactions, Block may not be subject to 311 requirements, and that bank partners’ screening control would be sufficient.
Remediation Strategy: Remediation will include an assessment (“research MAP”) to determine current screening coverage, evaluate applicability of 311 requirements across transaction types, and design an implementation plan for meeting 311 screening requirements. Additionally, Compliance will coordinate with FinCrimes Legal for regulatory interpretation and further align with partner banks and service providers on control expectations. Finally, implementation of required updates to screening scope and procedures will be completed.
Issue Type: Gap – a needed process or control is missing entirely.
Issue Rating Justification: Potential regulatory compliance gap with Section 311 obligations; currently mitigated by partner controls in key channels but unconfirmed coverage for International ACH. Moderate rating reflects limited customer impact but material compliance exposure.

Issue Title: Historical Coverage Assessment Remediation
Issue Description:
This MAP consolidates remediation items from historical Transaction Monitoring (TM) Coverage Assessments across various business units and regions. Its purpose is to remediate pending items, provide visibility into current remediation progress, and define next steps for unresolved findings.
 

 During the Coverage Assessments, a number of transaction monitoring gaps and findings were identified that require remediation. These include missing signals, incomplete rule implementations, and blocked items due to operational readiness or dependencies on external inputs (e.g., legal/vendor processes); however, the items were blocked or remain unresolved due conflicting priorities. The findings were identified through Coverage Assessments conducted in 2024, which encompassed activity for 2023.
 

 During 2025, the Financial Crimes Global BSA/AML Program Office gained ownership of the Transaction Monitoring Coverage Assessment and conducted a historical review to reconcile previous findings. This review analyzes whether previously reported remediation items were deprioritized, remain unaddressed, or require immediate action.
Hyperlinks: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Co_wVAXI2JDxTE5b0AWGbmBPNJCxTCmnajr110BmmUU/edit?tab=t.ulflna1g43jg|Issue Management Templates - Historical Coverage Assessment Remediation
RCA Description:
The primary driver of unresolved historical Coverage Assessment findings is the lack of a structured remediation governance framework. An absence of clearly defined ownership, timelines, and prioritization hindered remediation efforts, allowing items to go unaddressed across multiple business units and regions.
 

 A completed analysis using the 5 Whys revealed the following contributing factors: the work was scheduled to be completed in 2025+, some items were deprioritized due to higher-priority initiatives, high-risk items required extended remediation time, organizational changes caused unclear ownership, and some low-risk items lacked defined remediation deadlines.
 

 The lack of remediation governance framework resulted in multiple operational impacts: blocked items remained dependent on operational readiness, legal/vendor approvals, or other priorities; historical reliance on informal processes led to inconsistent tracking and monitoring of remediation progress; and overall, the absence of a formal framework caused gaps in accountability, timelines, and prioritization for historical Coverage Assessment findings.
Remediation Strategy:
To address the unresolved historical Coverage Assessment findings and prevent recurrence, the Financial Crimes Global BSA/AML Program Office will implement a structured remediation framework. This framework will ensure clear ownership, standardized timelines, consistent prioritization, and continuous tracking of all TM Coverage Assessment items across business units and regions.
 

 Key Objectives:
 Complete remediation of all historical Coverage Assessment findings.
 Standardize governance framework including: ownership, deadlines, and escalation processes.
 Maintain transparent progress tracking via a centralized MAP tracker and assessment matrix. All Coverage Assessment findings under this MAP will be individually tracked within the centralized tracker to ensure visibility of ownership, remediation progress, and closure status.
 Prevent recurrence through structured governance, workflows and documented procedures.
 

 Success Criteria:
 100% of historical findings remediated or have been formally risk accepted.
 Clear ownership, timelines, and status tracked in a centralized MAP tracker.
 Low and Medium-risk items completed within the scheduled timeline (2025–2026).
 Governance framework established to prevent recurrence.
 Demonstrated readiness for future TM Coverage Assessments and regulatory review.
Impacted Countries:
Australia
 Canada
 New Zealand
 United Kingdom
 United States

Issue Rating Justification: Medium impact across multiple business units and regions; gaps may affect regulatory readiness but no active customer impact.
