Issue Title: CF1: Confirmed FCRA/FACTA Impact - Default Email Lock Prevented ID Theft Response Delivery
Issue Description:
Default email addresses set during case creation (within Salesforce/CF1) cannot be updated systemically (no built-in mechanism to update this default setting), requiring manual corrections for all subsequent communications. When CS Voice advocates manually correct email addresses (due to mishearing, typos, or caller preferences), CF1 ignores these updates and continues to use the original email address entered during case creation as the default email address. When cases transfer between teams (from CS Voice to ID Theft, Disputes etc.), the system's use of the original incorrect email address persists, as downstream teams may be unaware that manual corrections are needed for each communication. To compound this breakdown, there are three systemic or workflow weaknesses that increase email delivery failure risk: 
 CF1 lacks front-end validation to prevent malformed email addresses with invalid domains (e.g. .com/.org/.net)
 Some team Knowledge Articles (KAs) or work instructions do not include email verification protocols (such as reading addresses back during calls)
 The workflow requirement of immediate case closure after sending communications prevents advocates from detecting bounce-back notifications for invalid addresses. 
 There has been one confirmed FCRA/FACTA regulatory impact where an ID Theft resolution response failed to reach a customer within the mandatory 30-day window (due to an incorrect default email address being used), demonstrating tangible regulatory risk. This issue was identified through customer reports of missing communications and QA/QC team observations of email address discrepancies in responses. No automated detection method exists to identify the full scope of impact.
Explanation for Delay in Issue Creation: The Quality Control Team initially identified the issue on May 5, 2025, discovering a potential breakdown or gap in processes. Subsequent investigation of several related cases revealed a systemic process weakness that had existed since the initial launch of the Customer Success (CS) support tooling in CF1. Although Customer Success Advocates were aware of this weakness, they had attempted to mitigate it by implementing a manual workaround. This workaround involved trying to manually communicate updated email information when cases were transferred between departments. However, this manual intervention proved to be an ineffective solution to the underlying problem.
RCA Description:
Customers are failing to receive critical communications after the CS Advocate has 1. Corrected the customer's email address during the support call, and 2. Transferred the case to another queue (i.e. Disputes, ID Theft, etc.) for additional servicing. This has resulted in customer service failures and exposing the company to regulatory compliance risks (with 1 confirmed ID FCRA/FACTA Impact on a failure to send an ID Theft resolution response within 30 days). These are the three key systemic gaps/problems:
  - CF1 does not allow updates to the default email address after initial case creation
  - CF1 does not prevent saving an email address containing an invalid domain (i.e. ".com", ".org", ".net", etc.)
  - The communications sending queue is not alerted when a communication bounces back for invalid email address
 Secondarily, there are opportunities to strengthen Advocate Knowledge Articles to increase (having the advocate repeat the email address back to the customer before saving) the likelihood of an accurate email address being captured from the start, however it is recognized that there will always be potential for human error in servicing.
 

 From a System Control Perspective: CF1 does not have a built-in mechanism to update the default setting for the email address capture. This is especially important for scenarios where the Advocate input what the customer had provided, yet the customer then later requested modificaiton of an email address because they had given an inaccurate one earlier in the conversation, changed their mind, OR the customer realized that they gave an invalid email address by mistake.
 Secondarily, from a Human Servicing Perspective: There is a content/workflow instructions gap where they do not detail the steps an advocate should take to ensure the email associated with the customers account is accurate by reading it back to the customer. At the time of building out the Salesforce/CF1 case management process/tooling and work instructions, some important intent was missed.
Remediation Strategy:
- Systemic solutions in CF1/Salesforce (allowing updates to the default email address + preventing an email address from being saved with an invalid domain +implementing alerts to the queue sending a communication if it bounces back undelivered) 
 

  - Knowledge Article (KA) Content modifications (clarifying to repeat back the email address to ensure accuracy before saving)
Impacted Business Area: Cash - Afterpay - Customer Operations - Customer Service
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure resulting in an Impact to regulatory obligations
Issue Action Plans: CF1: Confirmed FCRA/FACTA Impact - Default Email Lock Prevented ID Theft Response Delivery (1013861)

Issue Title: New York Department of Financial Services (NYDFS) Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Compliance Gap: Square Regulated Products (Bill Pay, Checking, Payroll)
Issue Description:
There is a compliance gap in the implementation of the NYDFS Cybersecurity Regulation (23 NYCRR 500) effective November 1, 2025, which requires that Square enforce MFA ahead of NY individuals accessing an “information system” of a “Covered Entity,” as defined under the Regulation. This gap impacts three regulated Square products: Bill Pay, Checking, and Payroll, which will not meet the compliance deadline. MFA is currently enabled for all accounts but not always required, leaving usage at the discretion of the user. Currently 90% of Square’s customers using these three products have opted in to using MFA.
 

 Current Status & Remediation as of 10/29/25:
 

 - MFA is deployed and optional for these products
 - Remediation efforts are already underway with dedicated engineering resources
 - Implementation work is actively progressing for all three regulated products
 - Bill Pay and Checking are on track for completion by end of year 2025
 - Payroll is targeting January 9th completion; while the team plans to complete the work within 2025, deployment will be deferred to mitigate the risk of end-of-year SEVs, which can significantly impact Payroll operations.
 - Other Block products have either achieved compliance (Cash App, Afterpay) or will have achieved compliance by the deadline (Neighborhoods).
RCA Description:
This issue had two primary root causes: (1) A miscommunication among the Counsel team that led to a factual misunderstanding of work that needed to be completed to reach compliance with the Rule; (2) Challenges for the Product team in prioritization of the work against pre-existing priorities and concerns about poor customer experience and risk of errors. 
 

 (1) After determining the products that were regulated by NYDFS and subject to the Regulation, Privacy Counsel conducted an initial review in partnership with Product Counsel of the MFA features for those products. At the time, it was believed that these products required MFA (step-up authentication). When Counsel then prepared final documentation of this review in early August, vetting this documentation revealed that there were certain edge cases where customers do not always use MFA. As a result of this misunderstanding, Counsel only informed the business of the need for production changes in August, ahead of a November 1 deadline, which is a shorter timeframe than is usually provided to product teams to complete compliance implementations. 
 

 (2) On this shorter timeline, the business determined that the tradeoffs, including potentially deprioritizing already planned business initiatives and the possibility of poor customer experiences (see tradeoffs discussion here), did not justify expediting the work to achieve compliance by November 1.
 

 Due to the combination of these two factors, work was not completed in time to meet the compliance deadline.
Remediation Strategy: We will remediate this issue on a go-forward basis by requiring MFA for “covered” products. Key milestones will include implementing a step up authentication flow that requires MFA enrollment for 1) Bill Pay, 2) Checking, and 3) Payroll.
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory impacts.
Issue Action Plans:
Correction of MFA Compliance gap (Bill Pay) (1007921)
 Correction of MFA Compliance gap (Checking) (1007923)
 Correction of MFA Compliance gap (Payroll) (1007927)

Issue Title: Gaps in Dispute Handling and Refund Capabilities for Internal Merchant ATO via Cash App Pay
Issue Description:
Customers are experiencing unauthorized Cash App Pay transactions due to internal merchant account takeovers (e.g., DoorDash, Shein). Fraudsters gain access to the merchant account and leverage saved Cash App Pay credentials to complete unauthorized purchases.
 

 Because Cash App systems only display the customer’s device as “in possession” and lack fraud signals from the merchant side, these disputes are frequently denied as “authorized,” even when customers are legitimate victims.
 

 Additionally, Cash App Pay refunds cannot currently be processed directly in Regulator. Refunds must be handled through reimbursements, which creates:
 -Risk of duplicate payouts (refund + reimbursement)
 -Incomplete visibility into customer remediation
 -Regulatory risk under Regulation E (denial of legitimate claims)
Explanation for Delay in Issue Creation: Additional time was required to identify Issue Owner and Issue Approver
RCA Description: Cash App’s fraud detection and dispute resolution systems lack sufficient merchant side fraud risk signals for Cash App Pay on-file transactions. During on-file transactions, the consent authorization flow where risk indicators and behavioral patterns are typically captured is bypassed. As a result, Cash App cannot accurately determine whether a disputed transaction originated from a legitimate customer or a compromised merchant account.
Remediation Strategy: Overall remediation will be achieved through working with key merchants to allow sharing of fraud risk signals when CAP on file transactions are authorized for enhanced internal Cash App Pay dispute investigation and resolution. Major milestones will include agreements renegotiation with key merchants and process/workflow updates that will account for the new information received from merchants. The end goal of the action plan will be to evidence Cash App can effectively determine when CAP on file disputes are legitimate or not.
Issue Type: Gap – a needed process or control is missing entirely.
Issue Rating Justification: Due to potential customer harm and Reg E compliance exposure
Issue Action Plans:
Customer Look Back and Remediation (1014528)
 Merchant Data-Sharing Agreements (1014530)
 Dispute Flow (1014534)

Issue Title: Partial GAPS were identified across eight areas when assessing Australian and New Zealand Regulatory Requirements against current processes for Afterpay Complaint Management
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement Reference: RG 271.52
 Requirement: Financial firms may acknowledge a complaint verbally or in writing (email, post or social media channels). When determining the appropriate method of communication, we expect firms to take into account the method used by the complainant to lodge their complaint and any preferences they may have expressed about communication methods.
 

 # 1 Partial GAP identified: 
 Whilst the Complaint Standard Operating Procedures (‘SOPS’) currently note that a complaint requires to be acknowledged regardless of whether the complaint is received via digital or voice, the SOPS do not document the current processes for complaints received via post.
 Regulatory Requirement Reference: RG 271.75
 Requirement: A financial firm must provide a written IDR response, even where the complaint is closed by the end of the fifth business day
 (a) if the complainant requests a written response; 
 (b) if the complaint is about: (i) hardship; (ii) a declined insurance claim; or (iii) the value of an insurance claim;
 

 # 2 Partial GAP identified: 
 1 - GCMT identified through sample testing that the current requirements documented within the "AU Hardship+5 days" Standard Operating Procedure (design), may be resulting in some hardship complaints under 5 days old not being sent an IDR response. GCMT noted 2 samples where the complaint was related to financial difficulty with payments, however, was not sent an IDR response due to the issue category not being an "eligible" issue under the current SOP. This is further documented in Design Observation #1 below.  
 

 2 - GCMT noted the current SOPS do not explicitly reference that an IDR response is required for Tier 1 complaints. In practice, GCMT note that all Tier 1 complaints require an IDR response to be sent.
 Regulatory Requirement Reference: RG 271.89
 Requirement:Credit providers (including debt collectors), credit service providers, their credit representatives and unlicensed COI lenders must refrain from
 commencing or continuing with legal proceedings or any other enforcement action (i.e. debt collection activity) against the complainant. Unless the statute of limitations is about to expire, this applies:
 (a) while the complaint is being handled at IDR (during the 21 calendar
 days); and
 (b) for a reasonable time thereafter.
 

 Regulatory Requirement Reference: RG 271.90
 Requirement: This will enable the complaint to be genuinely dealt with at IDR. The ‘reasonable time thereafter’ will also allow the complainant the opportunity
 to lodge their complaint with AFCA if the complaint cannot be resolved at IDR
 

 Regulatory Requirement Reference: RG 271.91
 Requirement: We expect that a sufficient timeframe for a complainant to lodge a complaint with AFCA will be at least 14 calendar days after receiving the IDR
 response. This may be longer, depending on the particular circumstances of the complaint (e.g. if the complainant needs more time to lodge a dispute
 with AFCA because of accessibility issues).
 

 #3 Partial GAP identified: The process for the application of a collections exclusion, currently excludes Tier 3 complaints which are resolved in 5 days. Additionally, collections exclusions are currently removed when the complaint is resolved.
 

 Regulatory Requirement Reference: RG 271.131
 Requirement: Firms should encourage complaints and make it easy for people to voice their concerns by developing an IDR system that is readily accessible and easy to use. Firms should proactively identify people who might need additional assistance
 Regulatory Requirement Reference: RG 271.133
 Requirement: Firms should also implement proactive and innovative approaches to promoting awareness about the IDR process and sourcing complaints from vulnerable people and groups.
 Regulatory Requirement Reference: RG 271.134
 Requirement: The IDR process must be easy to understand and use, including by people with disability or language difficulties.
 Regulatory Requirement Reference: RG 271.135
 Requirement: This can be achieved by firms: 
 (a) ensuring that information provided to the public about the IDR process is available in a range of languages and formats (including large print, Braille or audiotape); 
 (b) using Australian Sign Language (AUSLAN) video presentations of material on their website; 
 (c) enabling people to adjust the font size of information on their website; 
 (d) offering text telephone (TTY) and the National Relay Service (NRS) to complainants; and 
 (e) offering translation services to complainants or making staff available who are cross-culturally trained.
 Regulatory Requirement Reference: RG 271.173
 Requirement: A firm’s complaints policy should explain: b) the options available to assist complainants who might need additional assistance to lodge a complaint
 

 Regulatory Requirement Reference: RG 271.174
 Requirement: The complaints policy should be readily available to the public, in a range of formats and languages. In particular, the policy should appear on the firm’s website in an accessible location.
 

 #4 Partial GAP identified: 
 GCMT noted the following: 
 1 - No proactive approach for sourcing complaints for vulnerable people and groups requiring additional assistance outside the handling of vulnerable customers once an indicator has been identified by an agent  
 2 - No information on the public website, help articles or consumer facing materials for additional assistance 
 3 - No assistance for individuals with language difficulties or disabilities on the public facing complaint policy or help Centre articles as required in RG 271.25 (a) - (e,)  
 4 - Afterpay complaint policy is only provided in English with no ability to change its format.
 Regulatory Requirement Reference: RG 271.148
 Requirement: We expect staff who deal with complaints to have the knowledge, skills and attributes to effectively perform their roles. This includes: 
 (a) knowledge of this regulatory guide, consumer protection laws relating to financial products and services, AFCA approaches and relevant industry codes of practice; 
 (d) awareness of cultural differences and the ability to identify and assist complainants who need additional assistance; 
 Regulatory Requirement Reference: RG 271.150
 Requirement: Firms should provide targeted induction and ongoing training to staff who handle complaints. Topics may include: 
 (b) the requirements of this guide; 
 (c) financial services consumer protection laws, AFCA position statements and industry codes of practice; 
 (e) how to identify and help complainants who need additional assistance; 
 

 #5 Partial GAP identified: 
 GCMT reviewed the Customer Success and Complaint Management training materials and noted that the training does not currently include specific training on the regulatory bodies, code of practice or other consumer laws relevant to Afterpay Australia for complaint handling. Additionally, the training does not include how to identify and assist complainants who need additional assistance from a language perspective.
 Regulatory Requirement Reference: RG 271.172
 Requirement: Complaint management documentation is a key component of a financial firm’s IDR process. Firms must have a publicly available, readily accessible complaints policy and an internal complaint management procedure. Firms must provide material that explains their IDR process free of charge to complainants.
 

 #6 Partial GAP identified: 
 The public website, complaint policy, help articles nor consumer facing materials currently state that the complaints process is free of charge
 

 Regulatory Requirement Reference: FDRS Rules Section 50 - 1
 Requirement: 1. A member must:
 b) publicize to customers those procedures for making a complaint, that the service is free of charge;
 

 #7 Partial GAP identified: The public website, complaint policy, help articles nor consumer facing materials currently state that the complaints process is free of charge
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Results Matrix attached - Agreed GCMT process
Remediation Strategy: MAPS have been assigned to address each partial GAP
Impacted Business Area:
Counsel - Cash Compliance
 Cash - Afterpay - Customer Operations
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: Rated in accordance with the issue management framework
Issue Action Plans:
Management will document the process by which postal mail complaints are received and managed. (1006612)
 Review and amend as required Trigger rules for Hardship Complaint routing. (1006614)
 Design and implement procedure to expand the application of Collections exclusions to all complaint tiers (1006616)
 Management will complete a formal review (incorporating Legal advice) of current accessibility requirements for customer support including IDR support for vulnerable customers (1006618)
 Management will work with the Training team to create a sub-module for AU regulatory bodies and commitments and additional assistance for language/cultural awareness. (1007260)
 Compliance will update the AU and NZ website to reference Complaints are "free of charge" (or equivalent wording) (1007262)

Business Unit: CCO Afterpay Customer Service - Group Lead
Issue Title: Customer Success and Complaint Management adequacy of complaint response and adherence to Standard Operating Procedures for complaint reporting fields can be improved
Issue Description:
Background;
 GCMT conducted testing on a sample of 220 complaint tickets across Tiers 1, 2 and 3 to verify that all required processes documented within the Complaint Standard Operating Procedures were followed by the Customer Success and/or Complaint Management Agent as part of the complaint handling and resolution. 
 

 GCMT identified two key complaint areas where the effectiveness rate across all tiers was under 85%.
 

 Key Complaint Area 1 - Adequacy of Complaint Response 
 GCMT tested a random sample of 220 complaints to assess if the response provided to the customer was adequate to address the customer's complaint in full. 
 

 Issue:
 GCMT identified an 83% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 17% exception rate (38 exceptions) where the complaint response was not adequate or only partially adequate to address the complaint. These results are broken down below by complaint tier, region and product:  
 

 Tier 3:
 GCMT tested a sample of 90 Tier 3 complaints to review if the response provided by the Customer Success agent was adequate to address all components of the customers complaint. 
 

 GCMT identified 17 exceptions (19%) where the response provided by the Customer Success agent was not adequate or only partially adequate to address the customer's complaint. This consisted of the following breakdown: 
 - 6 exceptions where the Customer Success agent response did not address the customers complaint in full and;
 - 11 exceptions where the response was only partially adequate to address the customer's complaint. 
 

 This consisted of the following breakdown by product and region: 
 1 - Afterpay Australia BNPL: 3 exceptions in full and 3 exceptions partially adequate
 2 - Afterpay Australia Plus Card: 2 exceptions in full and 4 exceptions partially adequate
 3 - Afterpay New Zealand BNPL: 1 exception in full and 4 exceptions partially adequate
 

 Tier 2: 
 GCMT tested a sample of 70 Tier 2 complaints to review if the response provided by the Customer Success agent and/or Complaint Management Agent was adequate to address all components of the customers complaint. 
 

 GCMT identified 10 exceptions (14%) where the response provided by the Customer Success agent was only partially adequate to address the customer's complaint. 
 

 This consisted of the following breakdown by product and region: 
 1 - Afterpay Australia BNPL: 3 exceptions
 2 - Afterpay Australia Plus Card: 3 exceptions
 3 - Afterpay New Zealand BNPL: 4 exceptions 
 

 Tier 1: 
 GCMT tested a sample of 60 Tier 1 complaints to review if the response provided by the Customer Success agent and/or Complaint Management Agent was adequate to address all components of the customers complaint. 
 

 GCMT identified 12 exceptions (20%) where the response provided by the Customer Success agent was not adequate or only partially adequate to address the customer's complaint. This consisted of the following breakdown: 
 

 - 4 exceptions where the Customer Success agent response did not address the customers complaint in full and;
 - 8 exceptions where the response was only partially adequate to address the customer's complaint. 
 

 This consisted of the following breakdown by product and region: 
 1 - Afterpay Australia BNPL: 1 not adequate and 4 partially adequate 
 2 - Afterpay Australia Plus Card: 1 not adequate 
 3 - Afterpay New Zealand BNPL: 2 not adequate and 4 partially adequate
 

 Complaint Area #8 – Complaint Reporting Fields 
 Procedure Requirement: The "How to Update Complaint Fields" Standard Operating Procedure requires agents to complete the mandatory fields in the Zendesk Ticket prior to the closure of a ticket. These results are broken down below by complaint tier, region and product: 
 

 Issue: 
 GCMT tested a sample of 220 complaints to verify that agents were completing all mandatory fields in the Zendesk Ticket prior to the closure of a ticket, with the selection accurate based on the nature of the complaint and the provided resolution.  
 GCMT identified an 83% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 17% exception rate where the complaint fields entered by the agent were incorrect based on the reviewed complaint. 
 

 Tier 3: 
 GCMT tested a sample of 90 Tier 3 complaints to verify that prior to the closure of a complaint on Zendesk, that the mandatory complaint fields were being updated by the agent, with the selection accurate based on the nature of the complaint and the provided resolution. 
 

 GCMT identified 14 exceptions (15%) where the complaint fields entered by the agent was incorrect based on the reviewed complaint. This consisted of the following breakdown: 
 - 12 exceptions where the resulting action was incorrect 
 - 1 exceptions where the root cause was incorrect 
 - 1 exceptions where the primary issue was incorrect 
 

 This consisted of the following breakdown by product and region: 
 1 - Afterpay Australia BNPL: 6 exceptions for resulting action and 1 exception for primary issue 
 2 - Afterpay Australia Plus Card: 3 exceptions for resulting action and 1 exception for root cause 
 3 - Afterpay New Zealand BNPL: 3 exceptions for resulting action 
 

 Tier 2: 
 GCMT tested a sample of 70 Tier 2 complaints to verify that prior to the closure of a complaint on Zendesk, that the mandatory complaint fields were being updated by the agent, with the selection accurate based on the nature of the complaint and the provided resolution. 
 

 GCMT identified 12 exceptions (17%) where the complaint fields entered by the agent was incorrect based on the reviewed complaint. This consisted of the following breakdown: 
 - 5 exception where the resulting action was incorrect 
 - 3 exception where the root cause was incorrect 
 - 2 exceptions where the secondary complaint field was not updated or was incorrect 
 - 1 exceptions where the primary issue was incorrect 
 - 1 exception where the product was incorrect 
 

 This consisted of the following breakdown by product and region: 
 1 - Afterpay Australia BNPL: 1 resulting action and 2 secondary issues 
 2 - Afterpay Australia Plus Card: 1 product and 1 primary issue 
 3 - Afterpay New Zealand BNPL: 3 root cause and 4 resulting actions 
 

 Tier 1: 
 GCMT tested a sample of 60 Tier 1 complaints to verify that prior to the closure of a complaint on Zendesk, that the mandatory complaint fields were being updated by the agent, with the selection accurate based on the nature of the complaint and the provided resolution. 
 

 GCMT identified 16 exceptions (27%) where the complaint fields entered by the agent was incorrect based on the reviewed complaint. This consisted of the following breakdown: 
 - 6 exceptions where the primary issue was incorrect 
 - 4 exception where the secondary issue was not updated or incorrect
 - 4 exceptions where the resulting action was incorrect 
 - 1 exception where the product type was incorrect 
 - 1 exception where the root cause was incorrect 
 

 This consisted of the following breakdown by product and region: 
 1 - Afterpay Australia BNPL: 2 resulting action, 1 primary issue and 1 secondary issues 
 2 - Afterpay Australia Plus Card: 3 primary issues, 2 secondary issues and 1 product 
 3 - Afterpay New Zealand BNPL: 2 primary issues, 2 resulting action, 1 root cause and 1 secondary issue

Business Unit: CCO Afterpay Complaint Operations - Group Lead
Issue Title: External Dispute Resolution Agent’s adequacy of complaint response and adherence to Standard Operating Procedures for the handling of AFCA and FDRS complaints can be improved
Issue Description:
Background:
 GCMT tested a sample of 35 Tier 1 (EDR) complaints to assess Complaint Management Agent responses provided to complainants as part of the External Dispute Resolution complaint handling. 
 

 Issue 1: 
 GCMT identified a 80% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 20% exception rate ( 7 exceptions) where the response provided was only partially adequate to address the complaint. 
 

 This consisted of the following breakdown by product and region: 
 1 - Afterpay Australia BNPL: 3 exceptions (15 AFCA complaints testing for BNPL) 
 2 - Afterpay Australia Plus Card: 2 exceptions (15 AFCA complaints testing for Plus Card) 
 3 - Afterpay New Zealand BNPL: 3 exceptions (10 FDRS complaints testing for BNPL)
 

 Background:
 GCMT tested a sample of 35 Tier 1 (EDR) complaints to assess Complaint Management Agent compliance with the External Dispute Resolution Complaint Standard Operating Procedures. 
 

 Issue 2: 
 GCMT identified a 3% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 97% exception rate (34 exceptions) where one or more steps was missed for the complaint case. The 97% exception rate consisted of 100% where a low-risk or administrative error was recorded.  
 

 GCMT noted the non-completion of the required steps were administrative only with no negative outcome for the customer. The types of exceptions are noted below, in order of most common exceptions identified: 
 1. Difference between AFCA case fee documented in the ZD ticket field and the AFCA complaints google sheet (tracker) 
 2. Incorrect Tier noted in the ZD ticket field for IDR Yes/No - However GCMT note that this has now been updated for the agent to select Yes or No as opposed to the Tier
 3. AFCA original ticket or closure ticket not merged into the primary ticket - However in lieu of this GCMT note that a note is still left by the agent 
 4. Slight difference in due date populated in the ZD ticket field and the AFCA complaints google sheet (tracker) i.e. 1 day 
 5. No case folder created in the google drive - There is varying applications of this between staff where some staff will upload a screenshot of the ZD ticket if there is not resolution agreement/final resolution document, whereas other team members will not create a folder at all. However, for all instances noted where no google drive was created, these were cases where no final resolution document was provided. 
 6. No due date populated in AFCA complaints google sheet (tracker) 
 7. Resulting action was incorrect 
 8. Resolution cost was incorrect 
 9. No decisioning template 
 10. EDR agent did not confirm the customers communication preference - Phone or Email 
 11. Legal tag for unfair treatment incorrectly applied 
 12. Collections exclusion not removed 
 13. Note not left on Admin Portal post closure of the case 
 

 This consisted of the following breakdown by product and region: 
 1 - Afterpay Australia BNPL: 15 exceptions (15 AFCA complaints testing for BNPL) 
 2 - Afterpay Australia Plus Card: 9 exceptions (10 AFCA complaints testing for Plus Card) 
 3 - Afterpay New Zealand BNPL: 10 exceptions (10 FDRS complaints testing for BNPL)
 

 Additionally, GCMT noted the following requirements documented within the SOP not being consistently completed across the board: 
 1. The "1 - AFCA New Case" SOP requires agents to fill in the Zendesk ticket fields in the Global RDR Form based on the AFCA complaint document. GCMT noted in testing that the requirement for "Customer Information" to be filled out, no longer exists as a field in the Zendesk ticket and should be removed from the SOP.
 2. The "1 - AFCA New Case" SOP has the following requirement "If the customer was previously handled by IDR did the agent update the ticket with the stage the complaint was handled and with the IDR ticket number". GCMT noted in testing that for all samples, where applicable the agent will note the "previous communication" ticket in the ZD Ticket Note however in all cases the stage the complaint was handled (complaint tier) was not documented. If this is no longer required, Management should remove reference to this notation in the SOP or alternatively re-communicate this requirement.

Business Unit: CCO Afterpay Customer Service - Group Lead
Issue Title: The eligibility criteria within the “AU Hardship and complaint under 5 days old” Standard Operating Procedure may be resulting in certain hardship complainants not receiving an Internal Dispute Resolution/written response in all instances.
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement Reference: RG 271.75
 Requirement: A financial firm must provide a written IDR response, even where the complaint is closed by the end of the fifth business day
 (a) if the complainant requests a written response; 
 (b) if the complaint is about: (i) hardship; (ii) a declined insurance claim; or (iii) the value of an insurance claim;
 

 Policy Requirement: “AU Hardship + complaint under 5 days old”
 Tickets will be assigned automatically to the “Hardship level 2” queue which once resolved will require a summary and AFCA details in the IDR response. The following criteria is required to be met: 
 1. Country = AU
 2. Complaint = Yes
 3. Not already an EDR complaint
 4. Any of the hardships issue categories below: 
 a. -Collections:: Financial hardship
 b. -Hardship:: Follow up
 c. -Hardship:: Reversal Requests
 d. -Payment Arrangement:: Escalation required either 4th time or 5 years plus
 e. -Payment Arrangement:: Payment Arrangement Cancelled
 f. -Payment Arrangement:: Payment Arrangement Created
 g. -Payment Arrangement:: Payment Arrangement Discharged reinstatement
 h. -Payment Arrangement:: Payment Arrangement More information required
 5. Ticket is under 5 days since created
 6. Not in relation to hardship reinstatement
 Issue: 
 The "AU Hardship + complaint under 5 days old SOP" requires the agent to respond with either the Complaint: ANZ Hardship complaint IDR simple response (AU Only) or Complaint: ANZ Hardship complaint IDR complex response (AU Only) macro. 
 

 GCMT noted in testing two samples where the customer came through the Hardship Form and had been requesting payments to be moved under the issue category "Change Payment Schedule" due to hardship or financial reasons. For these two samples, the customer expressed dissatisfaction, with the ticket tagged as a complaint. 
 

 No IDR macro was sent to the customer when responding to the complaint, however per the above SOP, the agent was following the correct practice as the Issue Category did not match one of the eight approved issue categories and was therefore not eligible to be considered as a Hardship (under 5 days) complaint. 
 

 However, as the complaint was still related to hardship and the customer's inability to make payments due to financial difficulties/reasons the issue category of "Change Payment Schedule" and other issue categories that may be related to financial hardship should be considered as part of the above criteria when determining if an IDR response macro is required to be sent. 
 

 This expanded approach will ensure compliance with RG 271 requirements and remove agent discretion.

Issue Title: FirstSource Internet Outage
Issue Description: On 10/23/2025 FirstSource experienced an internet outage caused by a fiber cut that impacted their internet.
RCA Description: On October 23, 2025, at 16:13 GMT, a service disruption occurred due to a fiber cut in Buffalo, NY, affecting multiple services. The outage lasted for 7 hours and 51 minutes, with services being restored at 00:04 GMT on October 24, 2025. The disruption had a significant impact on our customers, resulting in loss of connectivity.
Remediation Strategy: To restore services, the Lumen Network Operations Center (NOC) implemented a temporary workaround to bypass the damaged fiber. The key resolution milestones are as follows: * 17:38 GMT: Lumen discovered the service impact and initiated an investigation. * 17:52 GMT: Initial investigations indicated a fiber fault as the source of the service impact. * 18:17 GMT: Field Operations confirmed that a construction team had hit the buried cables. * 23:00 GMT (October 23, 2025): Excavation work began to expose the damaged fiber cables. * 00:04 GMT (October 24, 2025): Services were restored after implementing a temporary reroute. * 00:04 GMT (October 24, 2025): The Lumen NOC verified the stability of the restored services.
Not an Issue Justification: This issue did not impact Afterpay directly.

Issue Title: Cash Card Optionality Flow Navigation Bug Skipped Critical Follow-up Questions, Resulting in Incomplete Claims (INC-6100)
Issue Description:
On July 1, 2025, a SEV2 incident was declared when a logic bug was discovered in the Cash Card form navigation system. The issue was detected during a review to remove the optionality flag, and the system was stabilized within approximately 24 minutes. The root cause was identified as a navigation logic failure that did not properly handle optional question IDs, causing users to be routed through a fixed sequence instead of the intended dynamic, condition-based path.
 

 This bug resulted in critical follow-up questions being skipped for scenarios involving lost phones, lost cards, and shared personal information. The impact window extended from June 23, 2025, to July 1, 2025, affecting approximately 13,555 claim submissions. These submissions were processed without gathering all necessary information, potentially impacting customer support's ability to properly assess and resolve claims.
 

 The incident timeline spans from the initial impact on June 23, 2025, through detection and resolution on July 1, 2025, with stabilization achieved within 24 minutes of discovery.
RCA Description: N/A

Issue Title: Inadequate Device Recognition Leading to Unauthorized Transactions and Denied ATO Claims on Recycled Devices
Issue Description: A pattern has been identified in which customers experience unauthorized transactions on recycled or sold devices, resulting in subsequent account takeover (ATO) claims being denied. These denials occur under the “known device” policy, even though the transactions display clear indicators of fraud such as suspicious IP locations and activity patterns inconsistent with the customer’s typical behavior. The issue was discovered through complaint trend analysis and customer reports highlighting unauthorized access following device sales or trades. Initial observations indicate that active sessions often remain authenticated after device ownership changes, allowing unauthorized users to access accounts without re-authentication. The current policy’s inability to distinguish between legitimate device upgrades and compromised recycled devices has led to financial losses and erosion of customer trust. Although the current volume of such cases represents a small portion of total ATO incidents, the potential severity and regulatory sensitivity are high, given the unauthorized access to customer funds and information. Contributing factors include the persistence of authenticated sessions after device transfer, reliance on the “known device” policy that misclassifies recycled devices as trusted, gaps in session invalidation and re-verification processes, and limited detection of anomalous behavior on known devices.
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: Inadequate QA Sampling Methodology and Lack of Effective Error Remediation Process
Issue Description: The QA program’s current sampling methodology and remediation process are inadequate to ensure effective quality oversight and regulatory expectation for effectiveness. The existing approach (sampling only four cases per agent) lacks statistical validity or a risk-based rationale, making it insufficient to accurately assess agent performance or systemic risk. Compounding this, the program lacks an effective feedback mechanism for communicating errors and remediation requirements directly to front-line customer service agents. Independent validation from KPMG has reinforced these concerns, highlighting recurring deficiencies in sampling methodology, review scope, and feedback processes. These gaps are particularly concerning in areas such as complaints, where regulatory expectations exist despite limited prescriptive guidance. Addressing this issue requires the establishment of a justifiable sampling framework and a formalized communication process that ensures agents receive and act on remediation feedback. Additionally, stronger coordination between QA and the second line of defense is needed to define policy expectations, set sufficiency standards, and drive corrective actions that enhance oversight and compliance effectiveness.
RCA Description: It was confirmed that Quality Assurance (QA) samples 12 cases per agent, rather than the 4 cases per agent initially believed. Of the 12 sampled cases, 4 are selected for a detailed deep-dive review, which constitutes an appropriate and risk-aligned sampling methodology. For each reviewed case, QA assesses whether a complaint was made and whether it was accurately captured and appropriately tagged. In addition, a Remediation Policy has already been drafted by the RPEO Policy Governance Team, led by Andrew Flater’s team. The issue resulted from insufficient communication of the QA/QM program’s sampling and remediation design. This led to misinterpretation of a risk-aligned, statistically sufficient sampling model as inadequate, despite the controls operating as intended.
Remediation Strategy: No remediation needed. The sampling methodology is appropriate and defensible by the QA team and a Remediation Policy in order to set sufficiency standards, and drive corrective actions that enhance oversight and compliance effectiveness is already being drafted.
Not an Issue Justification:
The QA/QM program’s sampling approach is statistically valid and risk-based at the population and tier level, which is the appropriate standard for assessing systemic risk and quality oversight effectiveness. It was confirmed that QA/QM samples 12 cases per agent per month, rather than four as initially believed. Of these 12 sampled cases, four are selected for detailed deep-dive review, which constitutes a risk-aligned methodology balancing breadth of coverage with depth of review.
 

 Within each reviewed case, QA assesses whether:
 -A complaint was present,
 -The complaint was accurately identified,
 -The complaint was appropriately categorized and tagged in accordance with program standards.
 

 While the four deep-dive complaint reviews per agent function as a minimum review depth, aggregate sampling across risk tiers meets or exceeds accepted statistical confidence standards:
 -Tier 1 (highest risk) complaint populations meet or exceed a 95% confidence level with a 5% margin of error.
 -Tier 2 and Tier 3 populations meet or exceed a 90% confidence level with a 10% margin of error.
 

 Sampling scales appropriately with headcount and risk concentration. Mathematical caps on sample size at higher volumes are consistent with accepted statistical principles and do not diminish the program’s ability to detect systemic risk. This design supports regulatory expectations for risk-based, second-line quality oversight.
 

 Remediation and Feedback Controls
 The QA/QM program maintains a documented, auditable feedback mechanism that ensures identified errors are communicated to front-line agents in a timely manner. All quality reviews are completed within the month of interaction, recorded within the centralized QM tool (Observe AI), and communicated directly to agents via automated notifications. Agent acknowledgement is required, tracked, and reported monthly to operations leadership, establishing a closed-loop feedback control. Corrective action ownership appropriately resides with first-line operations, consistent with the program’s oversight role.
 

 A formal Remediation Policy has already been drafted by the RPEO Policy Governance Team, led by Andrew Flater’s organization. This policy establishes a standardized framework for addressing identified issues and remediation expectations, further strengthening the control environment. In parallel, QA is implementing automation enhancements, including auto-fail logic and multimodal review capabilities, with confirmed rollout timelines. These enhancements further strengthen detection and consistency but are not required for baseline program effectiveness.

Issue Title: Inadequate tooling and oversight controls for Cash Estate Management
Issue Description: The organization is facing issues with the improper storage and handling of Personally Identifiable Information (PII) across various systems and processes. These challenges arise due to a lack of sufficient tooling infrastructure and inadequate oversight mechanisms designed to ensure that PII is appropriately classified, encrypted, access-controlled, and monitored. Specific gaps include the absence of robust data discovery tools, missing automated PII detection capabilities, weak encryption protocols, and limited visibility into the storage locations and access patterns of PII. These deficiencies pose significant compliance risks and increase the likelihood of exposing sensitive customer and employee data.
Identifying Party: Cash Estates Management Lead - Lauren Wolfe
RCA Description:
The Unclaimed/ Escheatment & Deceased Estate Management Process workflow method used to collect and store customer PII is not Information Security Compliant. There is insufficient tooling infrastructure and lack of adequate oversight mechanisms to ensure PII is properly classified, encrypted, access-controlled, and monitored. In addition, there are inadequate data discovery tools, missing automated PII detection capabilities, insufficient encryption protocols, no option for PII redaction, and limited visibility into where PII is stored and how it is being accessed and protected.
 

 This occurred because the Personally Identifiable Information (PII) collected from Cash customers is currently being stored in, and accessed from the application Google Sheets. The decision to utilize Google Sheets to house the PII is a result of not having an alternative method available that is Info Security (InfoSec) compliant, and the team was not aware of InfoSec requirements at the time of them putting the Process together. Initially, the team did not engage Legal and Compliance when building out the Process to understand what was required, thus leading to the Process as the root cause.
Remediation Strategy:
-Email issue; discuss what is possible for Salesforce
 -Reduce access to Google Sheets
 -Remove ability to make a copy of Google Sheet
 -Migrate Estates Management to secure, dedicated tooling system
 

 See https://docs.google.com/document/d/1D2ry66jNOm1t8apWADh4k6Rvgw2Nlz9qVJaCuoeX_ak/edit?tab=t.ulflna1g43jg#heading=h.9d25yakz4o8a
Impacted Business Area:
Counsel - Legal - Cash
 Cash- Business Ops - Bitcoin
 Cash - Business Ops - Investing
 Cash - Product - Bitcoin
 Accounts Payable
 Complaints Program - Channel Lead
 Complaints Program - Group Lead
 Complaints Program - Team Lead
Issue Type: Gap – a needed process or control is missing entirely.
Issue Rating Justification: Customer impact places this under a high issue severity rating.
Issue Action Plans:
Secure Current PII Storage and Implement Controls (1028547)
 Migrate PII to Infosec-Compliant Storage System (1028554)
 Migrate Estates Management to Secure, Dedicated Tooling (1029206)

Issue Title: There is no control to alert the Debt Collection Agencies to pause collections activity when a customer self-serves and raises a merchant dispute through their Afterpay accounts.
Issue Description:
Background:
 As part of the new disputes process, Afterpay has implemented automated controls to restrict collection activity when disputes are submitted through the Admin Portal. These controls automatically prevent further payment attempts and prevents the account from being outsourced to a Debt Collection Agency (DCA). However, for accounts already outsourced to a DCA or on a Payment Arrangement, the Billing Disputes Overview [US Only - Not applicable to Canada] SOP requires manual intervention by agents to apply equivalent restrictions.
 Issue: 
 GCMT tested a sample of 15 disputes involving accounts that had been outsourced for collections and noted that in 5 instances, there was no evidence in Zendesk or related system activity showing that the DCA was notified to pause collection efforts during the dispute period. In each instance, the customer self-served and submitted a merchant dispute via their Afterpay accounts, which does not involve direct interaction with Customer Service agents and therefore does not trigger agent-led notifications to the DCA to suspend collection activity.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: Conflicting SOP guidance may have led Customer Service agents to use the "Dispute raised by our team member" Internal PDF as evidence instead of obtaining customer-supplied return proof.
Issue Description:
The Billing Dispute Overview SOP Requirement:
 To proceed with internal investigation of the dispute, agents must attempt to obtain the evidence (e.g. order tracking, cancellation or refund depending on the customer’s scenario) from the customer. If they are unable to obtain evidence from the customer, the agents will close out the billing dispute noting they were unable to obtain evidence. 
 2. Creating a New Dispute SOP Requirement
 If the customer did not submit any attachments, the agents can use the "Dispute raised by our team member" PDF from the SOP. 
 Issue: 
 In transactional testing of Merchant Billing Disputes and Refund Amount Billing Disputes that ultimately led to Merchant Disputes, GCMT observed 5 instances where the agents raised merchant disputes using the internal “Dispute raised by our team member” PDF document as evidence of return, rather than obtaining supporting evidence from the customer. This practice contradicts the Billing Dispute Overview SOP, which requires agents to make reasonable efforts to obtain evidence directly from the customer and to close the dispute if such evidence cannot be obtained.
 The inconsistency appears to arise from conflicting guidance in the "2. Creating a New Dispute" SOP, which permits agents to rely on the internal PDF in the absence of customer attachments. Service Excellence has confirmed that the Billing Dispute Overview SOP reflects the correct process.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Remediation Strategy: Update the procedures to provide consistent guidance

Business Unit: CCO Afterpay Quality Management - Group Lead
Issue Title: Quality Assurance grading of Afterpay billing disputes is not targeted, does not currently include an assessment for Customer Experience and/or Good Consumer Outcomes
Issue Description:
GCMT noted in sample testing of 40 billing disputes which were graded by the Quality Assurance Team, that the current scorecard and grading performed over complaints, do not include an assessment of the adequacy of the handling of disputes as a whole from a customer experience and/or a good consumer outcome perspective. Quality Assurance Management have confirmed that agents handling of disputes are currently graded from policy/process perspective only. 
 Furthermore, the current quality assurance program does not perform grading of billing disputes from an end-to-end perspective, but rather assesses an individual agent's response or actions as part of the dispute. GCMT notes that it is not uncommon for multiple agents to be involved in the resolution of an individual dispute. 
 Additionally, all tickets reviewed through the Maestro QA platform are selected randomly, and there is no targeted grading of dispute-related cases. Given the volume of billing disputes and their relevance to the customer journey and consumer protection, GCMT considers it appropriate for the QA program to implement targeted reviews of billing disputes.

Issue Title: Cash Card Optionality Flow Navigation Bug Skipped Critical Follow-up Questions, Resulting in Incomplete Claims (INC-6100)
Issue Description:
On July 1, 2025, a SEV2 incident was declared when a logic bug was discovered in the Cash Card form navigation system. The issue was detected during a review to remove the optionality flag, and the system was stabilized within approximately 24 minutes. The root cause was identified as a navigation logic failure that did not properly handle optional question IDs, causing users to be routed through a fixed sequence instead of the intended dynamic, condition-based path.
 

 This bug resulted in critical follow-up questions being skipped for scenarios involving lost phones, lost cards, and shared personal information. The impact window extended from June 23, 2025, to July 1, 2025, affecting approximately 13,555 claim submissions. These submissions were processed without gathering all necessary information, potentially impacting customer support's ability to properly assess and resolve claims.
 

 The incident timeline spans from the initial impact on June 23, 2025, through detection and resolution on July 1, 2025, with stabilization achieved within 24 minutes of discovery.
Explanation for Delay in Issue Creation: Issue was originally filed with Issue Management through Jira (https://block.atlassian.net/browse/INTAKEFORM-148) before current issue management program change went into effect. Under new ERM program, ownership of issue was transferred to FLRM causing a delay in filing.
RCA Description: Certain questions were missed for Cash App Card "Did Not Make Transaction" claims because there was a bug in the optionality implementation. The ID-matching logic wasn't updated to account for the _OPTIONAL suffix added in the latest questionnaire spec. These ID mismatching and resulting missing questions weren't caught in testing/staging because test coverage did not include scenarios with optionality enabled, edge-case answer paths, or verification that all required questions were shown and answered. As a result, not all branches of the code were tested. Since there is no formal process or checklist to ensure that new or changed questions IDs (and optionality logic) were fully mapped in code and tests, this class of issue remains possible. Additionally, monitoring wasn't set up to flag a drop in question completion rates for key fields, so the decline in evidence collection slipped past alert thresholds during rollout.
Remediation Strategy: Eng to implement and provide evidence of supporting fix for INC-6100 and work with RBPs to determine scope of impacted transactions/customers. Action plan will then be created to outline plans for remediation including refunding impacted customers when applicable, comms approval and distribution, and assessment of control enhancement with RBPs.
Impacted Business Area:
Cash - Engineering
 Risk Ops Disputes - Agent
 Risk Ops Disputes - Channel Lead
 Risk Ops Disputes - Group Lead
 Risk Ops Disputes - Team Lead
Issue Type: Gap – a needed process or control is missing entirely.
Issue Rating Justification: Operational Gap or Process Failure resulting in an impact to regulatory obligations ; large scope of impacted customers/disputes ; high-impact SEV rating of original incident
Issue Action Plans:
Send out customer comms (1019215)
 Implement control check for optionality in function reviews (1019217)
 Determine customer impact (1019886)
 Finalize affected customers (1019888)
 Review of entire affected population (1019890)
 Draft customer-facing comms (1019892)
 Get comms approval from Sutton (1019894)
 Run remediation for affected customers (1019896)
Closure Request Justification: Items have been closed and reviewed by issue management team. Reviewed by issue owner and approved to move forward with closure validation on 1/30/26
Issue Internal Validation Justification: Aligned with issue remediation as presented.

Issue Title: E-Sign Compliance Gap: Missing Processes for Mail-Based Consent Revocation and Paper Copy Requests
Issue Description:
Block's Cash App is currently experiencing compliance gaps related to E-Sign regulations and internal E-Sign policy implementation. While our Terms of Service explicitly state that consumers can request E-Sign consent revocation and paper copies of communications via mail to our physical address at no charge, we lack documented processes to fulfill these written requests. Currently, Cash App only maintains processes for handling E-Sign consent revocation through voice and chat channels, creating an inconsistency with our Terms of Service which specifically outlines mail as the method for such requests. This misalignment presents a significant risk of failing to properly fulfill consumer requests received through postal mail, potentially impacting our compliance with E-Sign regulations which mandate that consumers must have the right to withdraw consent and receive paper communications. The gap extends further as we also lack processes for handling paper copy requests across all channels, despite our Terms of Service guaranteeing this option to consumers. This compliance gap was identified during a partner's request for our E-Sign policy and procedures documentation. The root cause of these gaps has been traced to an oversight during the initial development of E-Sign related processes, where mail-based request handling was not incorporated into the operational procedures. To achieve full compliance and operational consistency, Cash App needs to: 
 Establish and document processes for handling mailed revocation requests
 Create procedures for fulfilling paper copy requests received via mail
 Extend the paper copy request capability to voice and messaging channels to maintain consistency with our existing revocation processes. 
 These improvements would ensure alignment between our stated Terms of Service, actual operational procedures, and regulatory requirements while better serving our consumers' rights under E-Sign regulations.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Cash App is out of e-sign compliance related to some requests received through customer success/frontline pertaining to Customers requesting (via direct mail): 1. A paper copy of the e-sign revocation , or 2. a paper copy of the e-sign document. The process was not built out on how to service e-sign revocation requests OR paper copy requests that come in from the physical mail and we don't have a process to fulfill paper copy requests via Voice or Messaging. Cash App has a process in place if a consumer calls or chats with them to request revocation, but missed the paper copy portion of the requirements and the physical mail aspect. The related section was missed when reviewing the terms of service when building out the process requirements. The process design focus was on the main way customers call in to contact us (Voice/Messaging) and the requirement around consent revocation digitally and missed the paper copy requests aspect. Customer Success missed ensuring this was part of the process requirement intent when the initial e-sign process was set up.
Remediation Strategy: Develop cross-functional process tooling within Customer Success(CS) where customers can contact the CS-Voice channel and advocates can support timely processing of these paper copy e-sign document requests.
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: Operational or process gap resulting in an update to internal policies, processes, or procedures
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information
