Issue Title: Merchant Periodic review backlog
Issue Description:
The UK merchant periodic review process has been identified as having a design flaw that allows merchants to remain indefinitely in an "Escalated to MLRO" status without timely resolution, creating a backlog of overdue periodic reviews and heightened AML/CTF regulatory risk. The root cause stems from:
 - No hard SLA or auto-enforcement for MLRO escalations (status can persist indefinitely).
 - Insufficient gating in Salesforce for managed merchants.
 - Ambiguous RACI and lack of WIP limits for MLRO queue; manual follow-ups only.
 This has resulted in merchants remaining in the Periodic Review cycle without advancing for extended periods without proper risk assessment completion, potentially exposing Block to regulatory compliance violations and reputational risk in the UK jurisdiction.
RCA Description:
The root cause stems from:
 No hard SLA or auto-enforcement for MLRO escalations (status can persist indefinitely).
 Insufficient gating in Salesforce for managed merchants.
 Ambiguous RACI and lack of WIP limits for MLRO queue; manual follow-ups only.
 This has resulted in merchants remaining in the Periodic Review cycle without advancing for extended periods without proper risk assessment completion, potentially exposing Block to regulatory compliance violations and reputational risk in the UK jurisdiction.
Remediation Strategy:
A plan has been drafted to support the raising of an Action Plan to cover:
 1. The remediation of the backlog
 2. Process and documentation design uplifts to address identified design flaws
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: Limited exposure in relation to the number of affected merchants, however design gap being in conflict with the spirit of the AML/CTF rules and regulations Clearpay is subject to.
Risk Domain: Compliance | Financial Crime | Anti-Money Laundering & Terrorist Financing (1000126)
Issue Action Plans: Merchant Periodic Review Backlog (1009919)

Issue Title: Removing Late Capture Check from Non-Fraud Workflows in CaC Disputes
Issue Description:
The Late Capture checkpoint was updated in all Cash App Card Knowledge Articles (KAs) in February 2025 to address the 10.3 Network Reject system error that prevented certain claims from being filed. At the time, agents were instructed to treat affected transactions as Late Captures and send an explanatory message to customers which became a routine checkpoint in all Cash App Card workflows over time. While this remains appropriate for unauthorized workflows (where timing and balance discrepancies are relevant), this step is not ideal for authorized workflows such as:
 - Goods/Services Not Received or Described
 - Refund Credit Not Processed
 - Paid by Other Means
 - Cancelled Subscriptions
 In these workflows, sending a Late Capture explanation does not help the customer resolve their claim. 
 

 This issue was initially identified in the findings of the First Line Risk Management Disputes Control Review that was conducted in January/February of 2025.
 

 Further analysis by Dispute Operations has shown that the 10.3 error is not uniquely tied to Late Captures and can occur for other, unrelated reasons. Out of 2,329 claims where a Late Capture response was sent in Q3 of 2025, 179 (7.7%) were cases where customers could have received an actionable resolution instead of a technical rejection. Of the 179 authorized claims where Late Capture messages were sent, only two encountered processing errors, neither being the 10.3 error. This leads to customers reaching out multiple times to obtain a proper resolution, duplicate cases in the queue, increased handling time, and customer frustration from unclear or unhelpful messaging.
RCA Description:
The Late Capture checkpoint was updated in all Cash Card Knowledge Articles (KAs) in February 2025 to address the 10.3 Network Reject system error that prevented certain claims from being filed.
 At the time, agents were instructed to treat affected transactions as Late Captures and send an explanatory message to customers. This approach ensured business continuity during the filing issue — not to redefine when Late Capture logic should apply.
 Because our tooling could not directly identify true Late Captures, agents relied on 10.3 Network Rejects as a proxy signal that one might be present. However, further analysis has shown that the 10.3 error is not uniquely tied to Late Captures and can occur for other, unrelated reasons.
 Over time, the Late Capture step became a routine checkpoint in all Cash Card workflows. While this remains appropriate for unauthorized workflows (where timing and balance discrepancies are relevant), it adds no value for Service Claims and authorized workflows, where there is minimal balance confusion and the customer’s issue typically requires direct resolution — not education about transaction timing.
 

 Out of 2,329 claims where a Late Capture response was sent in Q3, 179 (7.7%) were cases where customers could have received an actionable resolution instead of a technical rejection.
Remediation Strategy: The remediation strategy is to remove the Late Capture step (and any related 10.3 error instructions) from the Goods/Services Not Received, Goods/Services Not as Described, Refund Credit Not Processed, Paid by Other Means, and Cancel Subscriptions authorized workflows where the step is no longer required to prevent filing errors. This will be accomplished through a formal PCR that will be reviewed by relevant stakeholders in the Risk Ops, Compliance, Product, Legal, and ML business areas. Once the PCR is approved, recorded changes will be made to the relevant Knowledge Articles for Dispute Ops, and the Late Capture logic will be removed from CIF. Success criteria will be determined by Dispute Ops as they monitor the volume of filed claims and review for mishandled cases related to Late Captures.
Impacted Business Area:
Risk
 Cash - Risk ML
 Risk Ops Disputes - Agent
 Risk Ops Disputes - Channel Lead
 Risk Ops Disputes - Group Lead
 Risk Ops Disputes - Team Lead
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: While the change does not pose a regulatory or legal risk if left unaddressed (since claims are still being handled), it directly impacts process accuracy, customer fairness, and operational compliance alignment. Implementing this update ensures claims are properly investigated and resolved in accordance with intended procedures, which helps mitigate potential downstream risks such as escalations, customer dissatisfaction, or audit findings.
Issue Action Plans:
Program Change Request drafting and approval (1019221)
 External approval (1019223)
 Knowledge Article Removal (1019225)
 Internal Communications drafting and approval (1019227)
 CIF Removal (1019230)
 Send Internal Comms (1019232)
 Issue Validation (1019234)

Issue Title: Merchant invoicing rounding error
Issue Description:
Summary of the issue: 
 A New Zealand merchant, Ellerslie Automotive, identified that some of Afterpay's invoices did not match the amounts debited and disclosed to the end customer.
 Following a review, it was determined that a minor visual only glitch caused certain invoices to round “half even” instead of “half up.” This resulted in a discrepancy of at most 1 cent in rare cases (27 in the case of this merchant). The issue stemmed from the default rounding logic in the library used for PDF generation. It only affected amounts where the third decimal place was a 5 (e.g., $10.105 displayed as $10.10 instead of $10.11).
 

 In this particular merchant’s example, fees payable of $17.205 displayed as $17.20 (half even) but were correctly debited as $17.21 (half up).
 It has been confirmed that the glitch has since been resolved. Any new invoices or re-downloaded older ones will display the correct rounded values.
 To summarise, merchants were charged correctly, but a small number of invoices displayed incorrect values (only those with a third decimal of 5).
 

 Merchant interaction:
 The Technical Account Management team explained the cause of the issue to the impacted merchant several times, but she remained unsatisfied.
 A member of the SMB Sales team call the merchant to clarify that the charges were correct. The merchant acknowledged this and confirmed she raised the matter on principle, noting that discrepancies between invoice values and direct debits create unnecessary admin work for businesses and a company of our size should not have this happen. The merchant advised that she was happy with how the matter has been resolved, however did mention that she had raised the issue with Consumer NZ, who advised her they escalated it. The merchant sent a follow up email to Consumer NZ to advise she was satisfied with the outcome and it has been resolved.
RCA Description:
In July 2020, the merchant settlements team introduced a rounding inconsistency for invoice generation. The NumberFormatter utility, responsible for formatting currency amounts on merchant invoices, was implemented using Java's DecimalFormat default rounding mode (HALF_EVEN) instead of the standard HALF_UP rounding used throughout the rest of the settlements ecosystem, including bank file generation and settlement calculations. This discrepancy has had global impact across all regions, affecting approximately 348,008 invoices out of approximately 500 million total invoices generated since 2020, spanning ~30k unique merchants worldwide.
 The issue remained undetected for over 5 years due to its highly specific nature - it only manifested for amounts ending in exactly .X25, .X45, .X65, .X85, or .X05 where the hundredths digit is even (0, 2, 4, 6, 8). These edge cases represent less than 0.1% of all invoice amounts, and the discrepancy was limited to a single cent difference in displayed amounts. The small financial impact and statistical rarity of the occurrence meant that affected merchants either did not notice the discrepancy or did not report it as a concern. Additionally, the inconsistency only affected invoice display formatting rather than the underlying settlement calculations, further masking the issue from routine reconciliation processes.
Remediation Strategy:
A system fix has been implemented and tested, confirming that the issue is no longer continuing.
 

 Furthermore a response has been provided to the merchant confirming corrective actions and acknowledging the issue. There has been no monetary detriment identified.
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Low rated issue that is related to a display error in the merchant system. The correct debits have been processes and reflected correctly on the merchant settlement statements. No monetary detriment has been identified.
Closure Request Justification: The issue has been remediated and tested. Results have been provided to compliance for review. A response has also been provided to the merchant.
Issue Internal Validation Justification: No further actions required. Technology fix has been implemented and a response provided to the impacted merchant.

Issue Title: P2P Memo Sanction Controls and Outreach Failures Result in Prolonged Customer Account Lockouts
Issue Description: The current sanctions control system is creating significant customer harm by flagging innocent peer-to-peer payment memos such as typos, jokes, country names, and emojis, which triggers complete account freezes that lock customers out of their funds for weeks or months. When initial customer outreach fails through the primary email contact method, there are no backup communication channels, leaving customers isolated and uninformed about their account status while they lose access to essential payments for rent, wages, utilities, and family support. This communication breakdown, combined with sanction controls that lack contextual intelligence to filter obvious non-threats like "Cuban sandwich," forces customers to file multiple support cases with minimal updates while frontline support promises 48-hour resolution windows that are consistently missed. Discovery of this pattern through Complaint Insights analysis of regulatory complaints reveals systemic issues where the only effective resolution pathway requires customers to escalate to CFPB or state regulators, alleging their funds are being "stolen."
RCA Description:
Some customers are putting innocent memos on their transactions and are being flagged for sanctions keywords review.
 This sanctions keyword process was developed to flag all payments (a single a set of keywords found in the Sanctions Keywords Lists with potential or probable sanctions exposure). 
 The system cannot distinguish between threats and innocent content unless the customer provides additional context, and customer outreach for this process has up & downstream gaps that further exacerbate the problem. 
 " - The upstream customer outreach gap in collecting contact information is that customers are only required to provide either their phone # or email address, so communications are failing when only the phone # is on file as communications for this process are only able to be sent via email.
  - The downstream gap is that the operations team supporting review/resolution of responses is not sufficiently staffed for volumes so responses/resolutions to unfreeze the account are significantly delayed"
 "Overall failure is an inadequately developed process (within the Sanctions Operations CET/Product Teams) that lacks: 
  - Sufficient Collection of Contact Information at Account Creation (Gap in Advocate KA's)
  - Systemic real-time means to message customer for clarity in-App
  - Systemic intelligence/decisioning related to the sanctions control
  - Sufficient staffing to support quick resolution to customer responses
Remediation Strategy:
The business mitigated some of this concern by no longer requiring a balance freeze on accounts under investigation and has pivoted to using the Cash Flow Hold instead. Balance freezes have also been removed from customers with open cases.
 

 Included in the Sanctions Hold (aka Cash Flow Hold) adversity action is a balance freeze, and when applied, the customer receives an automated communication that serves as a notification of the restrictions on the account, compliant with CFPB regulations.
 

 Keywords tuning is completed annually and false positive rates are reviewed. An additional request from C-Core to improve false positive rates is currently underway. Also, as addressed in point 1 - balance freeze from the backlog of customers were removed where applicable.
 

 Queue monitoring tables exist in tableau and Ops Leads are responsible for reviewing and monitoring anomalies in alerting. The Sanctions team (GSC & Ops PM) has also built internal monitoring via G2 where alerts and summaries of queue behavior are sent to Slack.
Not an Issue Justification: Based on the implemented changes and ongoing improvements, this situation is unlikely to result in future issues. The business has fundamentally eliminated the problematic practice of applying additional account-level balance freezes during investigations, pivoting instead to using Cash Flow Hold, which provides more targeted restrictions while maintaining CFPB compliance through automated customer communications. All existing customers impacted by the previous process have had their balance freezes removed, and the remaining alerts in the backlog are being systematically cleared by Operations. Continuous monitoring and improvement systems are in place, including annual keywords tuning, active false positive rate reviews through the C-Core initiative, real-time queue monitoring via Tableau dashboards, and automated G2-based alert systems with Slack notifications. The combination of eliminating the root cause, enhanced detection accuracy, and multiple layers of monitoring creates comprehensive protection against similar issues recurring in the future.

Issue Title: Update Terms of Service and sponsorship flow to be compliant with Connecticut law
Issue Description:
What was identified:
 A new Connecticut child protection law (HB 7082), effective October 1, 2025, requires licensed money-sharing applications (e.g., Cash App) to obtain:
 An attestation from the adult confirming they are the minor’s parent or legal guardian, and
 

 Either (A) a copy of a government-issued photo ID, or (B) identity verification in accordance with the Bank Secrecy Act (BSA).
 Currently Cash App requires Sponsors to attest to their relationship with the teen, and all sponsors must complete identity verification (IDV), which may be electronic (eIDV), or document-based (dIDV). Additionally, the definition of “guardian” in the Terms of Service includes non-legal guardians (e.g., stepparents, grandparents, foster parents), which may not align with Connecticut’s legal definition.
 

 When it occurred:
 Bill passed by Connecticut legislature: May 2025
 Effective date: October 1, 2025
 Identified internally: November 2025
 

 How it was discovered:
 Cash App was named in testimony at the House Banking Committee by a lawmaker in February 2025, where they asserted a child had access to a Cash App Account without parent or guardian consent and was at risk of child trafficking. The lawmaker gave this testimony in support of SB 1338, which requires any licensed money sharing applications (Cash App) to collect a notarized statement from a person that is seeking to open an account for a minor (user under 18) attesting that they are the minor’s parent or legal guardian. The final bill did not include the notarization requirement. 
 

 Initial observations:
 The current attestation process complies with the law’s attestation requirement.
 All sponsors must complete identity verification (IDV) prior to becoming a sponsor, either electronically (eIDV) or with physical ID documentation (dIDV) in accordance with Block’s [10.2025] Anti-Money Laundering & Counter-Terrorist Financing Policy _ Block.pdf.
 Cash App’s broad “guardian” definition could create noncompliance risk in Connecticut.
 Existing Sponsorships may need review for compliance with the new law.
 

 Known contributing factors:
 Overlap between legislative changes and product roadmap (non-traditional family sponsorship initiative) increased risk of compliance misalignment.
RCA Description:
Primary root cause: Insufficient proactive monitoring of state-level child protection and verification laws as they intersect with Cash App’s sponsorship and minor account policies. Block lacked a formal, standardized Regulatory Change Management program to consistently monitor and address ongoing changes to applicable regulatory requirements at the time that HB7082 was signed into law. The current process was implemented on a go forward basis in September of 2025, accounting for obligations signed into law after the program implementation date. 
 

 Supporting evidence: The Connecticut law (HB 7082) went into effect in October 2025 but was identified internally in November 2025 during Legal, Policy, and Compliance discussions.
 

 Connection to the issue: Because regulatory changes were not consistently tracked before the RCM program’s implementation, the Connecticut requirements were not timely flagged and assessed for impact. As a result, the TOS and sponsorship verification processes were not updated to meet the new Connecticut requirements, resulting in a potential noncompliance risk.
Remediation Strategy:
High-Level Approach
 

 We will remediate the gap introduced by Connecticut HB 7082 by updating Cash App Families’ Terms of Service (ToS), attestation flow, and customer communications/re-attestations to ensure that sponsorship of teen accounts in Connecticut is limited to verified parents or legal guardians.
 

 Key objectives include achieving compliance with HB 7082, ensuring clear customer disclosure, and implementing location-based attestation gating. Major milestones are Legal approval of ToS updates, launch of the Connecticut-specific attestation flow, and completion of customer re-attestations. Success will be measured by 100% compliance for new and existing Connecticut sponsorships, closure of the related Protecht issue.
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: CT HB7082 requires parent/legal‑guardian attestation and ID verification for minor accounts; our sponsor attestation and eIDV meet those elements, but our broad “guardian” ToS definition may misalign with Connecticut’s legal definition, creating a localized compliance risk. The exposure is limited to Connecticut sponsorships with no evidence of active customer harm; risk is regulatory and contained, not organization‑wide.
Risk Domain: Regulatory | Existing and New Reg Adherence | Regulatory Change Management (1000138)
Issue Action Plans:
Update Terms of Service for Connecticut guardianship definition (1010566)
 Update FAQs for Connecticut guardianship definition (1010568)
 Implement Connecticut-specific attestation and verification flow (1010570)
 Develop and execute mitigation plan for sponsorships created after October 1, 2025 (1010574)
Closure Request Justification: All action plans have been completed. See attachments under the action plans for confirmation.
Issue Internal Validation Justification: Approving issue closure as all work is now complete.

Issue Title: Debt Collection Guide breach on 7964185
Issue Description:
In summary:
 

 1. A failure to identify hardship triggers 19/04/24
 2. Failure to respond to the customer’s email 19/04/24
 3. Failure to identify a complaint and inform Afterpay 24/06/24
 4. Delay in responding to customer’s email received 24/06/24 (7-day response time)
 5. Failure to deviate Debtrak workflows, based on the customer communication channel of choice clearly being email (albeit it was not explicitly requested)
 6. Failure to communicate all hardship offerings so that the customer could follow through with a specific request for financial assistance. (note this is not limited to this incident; it applies on all accounts where such communication is made via outbound emails via Debtrak workflows)
 7. Failure to incorporate payment moratoriums as a hardship offering to workflow emails (this is also not an isolated matter and applies to existing email templates)
 

 The customer had expressed temporary financial hardship to Probe by email on 19/04/2024. The customer’s email was triggered by a Debtrak workflow email sent to the customer the same day. There was also a letter sent to the customer on 15/04/24.
 The customer did not however request financial assistance in his email. He only provided the reason for non-payment which is deemed temporary hardship. Probe did not respond to this email and the customer lodged a complaint with AFCA (to Afterpay). On 07/05 workflow email sent by Probe. It is exactly the same email initially sent 19/04.
 Customer replied on 09/05 with very little to no information. On 24/06/24 the workflow sent the same email a 3rd time and whilst there was no direct response to the customer’s email of 09/05. This appears to have triggered the customer and hence replied to Probe. Probe’s response was on 01/07/24 (delayed response). A further workflow email was sent on 13/06 which was not responded to.
 

 Whilst our investigation has identified the above 4 issues, it has also been concluded that Probe did respond to the customer to provide hardship assistance. The latest response was on 01/07/24 which the customer did not respond to. And as the customer failed to request any type of assistance, we could not have responded with anything tangible. We needed to speak with the customer and unfortunately the customer would not answer our calls. Written communication to call us was also not responded to.
 Noting the AFCA complaint was received on 15/04/25, this is 9 months after Probe responded to the customer’s hardship email. It is therefore difficult to conclude that the customer did not contribute to the issue. Our investigation has partly concluded that the customer’s unwillingness to respond to hardship offerings contributed to this incident. However, whilst Probe’s communication by email provided hardship options in a form of a payment arrangement, it did not provide any option for a payment moratorium. As payment moratoriums is a known hardship offering as part of the Afterpay services, this should be communicated where appropriate. And in this instance, our investigation deemed it appropriate to do so.
RCA Description:
In summary:
 

 1. A failure to identify hardship triggers 19/04/24
 2. Failure to respond to the customer’s email 19/04/24
 3. Failure to identify a complaint and inform Afterpay 24/06/24
 4. Delay in responding to customer’s email received 24/06/24 (7-day response time)
 5. Failure to deviate Debtrak workflows, based on the customer communication channel of choice clearly being email (albeit it was not explicitly requested)

 7. Failure to incorporate payment moratoriums as a hardship offering to workflow emails (this is also not an isolated matter and applies to existing email templates)
 

 The customer had expressed temporary financial hardship to Probe by email on 19/04/2024. The customer’s email was triggered by a Debtrak workflow email sent to the customer the same day. There was also a letter sent to the customer on 15/04/24.
 The customer did not however request financial assistance in his email. He only provided the reason for non-payment which is deemed temporary hardship. Probe did not respond to this email and the customer lodged a complaint with AFCA (to Afterpay). On 07/05 workflow email sent by Probe. It is exactly the same email initially sent 19/04.
 Customer replied on 09/05 with very little to no information. On 24/06/24 the workflow sent the same email a 3rd time and whilst there was no direct response to the customer’s email of 09/05. This appears to have triggered the customer and hence replied to Probe. Probe’s response was on 01/07/24 (delayed response). A further workflow email was sent on 13/06 which was not responded to.
 

 Whilst our investigation has identified the above 4 issues, it has also been concluded that Probe did respond to the customer to provide hardship assistance. The latest response was on 01/07/24 which the customer did not respond to. And as the customer failed to request any type of assistance, we could not have responded with anything tangible. We needed to speak with the customer and unfortunately the customer would not answer our calls. Written communication to call us was also not responded to.
 Noting the AFCA complaint was received on 15/04/25, this is 9 months after Probe responded to the customer’s hardship email. It is therefore difficult to conclude that the customer did not contribute to the issue. Our investigation has partly concluded that the customer’s unwillingness to respond to hardship offerings contributed to this incident. However, whilst Probe’s communication by email provided hardship options in a form of a payment arrangement, it did not provide any option for a payment moratorium. As payment moratoriums is a known hardship offering as part of the Afterpay services, this should be communicated where appropriate. And in this instance, our investigation deemed it appropriate to do so.
Remediation Strategy:
The following remedial action is from Probe as recommended and requires review by the Operations Manager and Operations Director:
 

 1. Uplift the SOP to incorporate Communicate Channel of Choice and include agent instructions. Circulate to the team accordingly.
 2. Uplift the SOP to include a process for replying to customer emails (who/how/when).
 3. In April/May 2024, there was 1 dedicated resource responding to emails, with one back up. Probe now has 6 effectively monitoring the Email queue. 
 4. Circle Leaders conduct 3 audits per week on agents who are actioning email requests
 5. Circle Leaders now have backend access to the Afterpay Notification Gmail account, which allows them to monitor all email requests and is used for conducting audits

Issue Title: Debt Collection Guide breach on 10055068
Issue Description:
See Incident Form - Probe- Afterpay AFCA Complaint (AP ref10055068)
 Customer raised an AFCA complaint as he claims he told Probe numerous times he is to be contacted by email or sms due to his work sleep schedule. The customer claims Probe continually harassed him with calls causing a lot of loss sleep and undue stress. It is evident that much of the communication throughout the life of the account has been by email and it is clear we were responding to his emails.
 However, under DCG, 'harassment' means 'undue harassment' which includes outbound calls attempts that may have made the customer feel intimidated, demoralised, or exhausted.
 The customer's email of July 4 states his request to not receive calls during the day due to his work hours. And this was not adhered to, since the workflow continued to place the account on the dialler file after this date. We have counted 11 call attempts after July 4, up to 27/08.
 I can't confirm if similar requests were made prior to July 4, but as we have identified at least one instance of his request, it's enough to validate the allegation.

Issue Title: Cash App: Miscategorization of Recurring Deposits Leading to Loss of Green Status Benefits
Issue Description:
We have identified a significant customer impact issue regarding deposit categorization changes that are affecting customer experiences and benefits. The impact includes 5 regulatory complaints identified as of 10/9/25 and an estimated ~288 internal complaints over the last 12 months, with ~9% classified as Tier 1. Specifically, certain deposits that were previously uncategorized were later manually and incorrectly recategorized, disrupting customer accounts that had been receiving Paycheck Deposit Status Active (PDSA) benefits. These incorrect categorization changes resulted in several adverse customer impacts: 
 ATM fees at in-network ATMs
 Overdraft fees on Cash App Card transactions
 Lower interest rates
 Standard phone support with the possibility of longer wait times
 Fees for paper money deposits
 International transaction fees on purchases abroad
 No access to Borrow feature (in eligible states)
 No customized offers
 The situation was further complicated by limitations in our support infrastructure. Currently, customers and CCO have no clear pathway to request recategorization of these deposits, even in cases where the deposits should maintain their previous classification to preserve customer benefits and prevent service disruptions.
 

 This issue came to light during pre-launch preparations for Cash App Green, when the CCO Banking Program Manager, requested a review of PDSA-related customer complaints. The full scope of these impacts was only realized after engagement with the Complaints Insights team for detailed analysis. Addressing both the categorization inconsistency and implementing appropriate support channels for resolution have been identified as critical fast follows with the launch of Cash App Green.
RCA Description:
Inadequate Processes for Cross-Team Hand-Offs - The absence of systematic workflows to handle transaction classification edge cases requiring customer verification and cross-functional coordination.
 Process Gap: "CCO has not accounted for this edge case. We need to create a workflow that asks the customer for additional information (their paystub)"
 Information Deficiency: "We don't always get enough information from the depositor to make the call...but do not have a workflow to support that"
 Training Data Issues: "The labels used in training might not be accurate as human labelers do not have sufficient information”
 The workflow absence creates a direct causal chain: No Process → Information Gap → ML Misclassification → Benefit Loss. When the ML system encounters ambiguous deposits, it has no established process to flag for manual review or request customer documentation, defaulting to automated classification without sufficient data. This causes legitimate paychecks to be misclassified as non-paycheck deposits, removing customers' PDSA benefits eligibility. The issue creates a degradation feedback loop where poor classifications lead to inaccurate training data, worsening ML performance and creating more misclassifications.
Remediation Strategy: Complete the current implementation of the operational workflow for customer verification, finish the IT delivery of the SendSafely dropzone (ITX-147768), and finalize the Salesforce integration for case management.
Issue Type: Gap – a needed process or control is missing entirely.
Issue Rating Justification: Operational or process gap resulting in an update to internal policies, processes, or procedures
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: Cash App P2P Electronics Communication Service Provider
Issue Description: With the introduction of Pools Contributor Comments, Cash App’s P2P Offering now potentially qualifies as an Electronic Communication Service provider under the ECPA, requiring us to meet all associated regulatory requirements. Comments has launched to 100%, which means we are currently not in compliance with all requirements prior to release. We are working to operationalize all requirements in this document https://docs.google.com/document/d/1j4Ls6322O7-ySP5DC1BvBs6Vhxp-iZRf1qGfkOFofsI/edit?tab=t.e72c22voxx43. Work is underway to address each of the listed requirements as quickly as possible.
RCA Description: The introduction of Pools Contributor Comments would likely render Cash App a provider of an electronic communications system. The ECPA requires providers of electronic communications systems to (1) not disclose data regarding the electronic communications system to outside parties except in specific circumstances (most commonly, consumer consent to disclosure); (2) maintain processes for preserving and disclosing information to the U.S. government upon receipt of a valid legal request, such as a subpoena or warrant; and (3) make a report to the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children upon obtaining actual knowledge of facts or circumstances indicating CSAM or child sex trafficking
Remediation Strategy: Address and resolve each of the 3 listed legal requirements. Operational plan is being tracked here https://docs.google.com/document/d/1j4Ls6322O7-ySP5DC1BvBs6Vhxp-iZRf1qGfkOFofsI/edit?tab=t.e72c22voxx43.
Issue Type: Gap – a needed process or control is missing entirely.
Issue Rating Justification: Given the regulatory implications of not adhering to all legal requirements, considering this as Moderate. It is not considered high risk as this only impacts the P2P service and NCMEC reporting occurs today.

Issue Title: Collection agents’ compliance with the requirement to contact the Debt Collection Agency (where applicable) to close the account, when a breathing space request is processed by Clearpay requires uplift
Issue Description:
Policy Requirement: 
 The “Breathing Space (Collections Only)” Standard Operating procedure under “Accounts with a debt collection agency who are subject to Breathing Space (Debt Respite ONLY)” requires the collections agent to reach out to the debt collection agency on the same day to advise they need to close the account on their records and return to Clearpay due to breathing space hold. 
 

 Background: 
 GCMT conducted sample testing over 40 DRS breathing space requests which were received via the Insolvency Portal. Of the 40 DRS breathing space requests, 38 were able to be allocated to a Clearpay account. 
 Issue: 
 GCMT noted 14 customer accounts which per the Admin Portal history/notes were outsourced to a debt collection agency (‘DCA’) prior to the breathing space request being received. Of the 14 customer accounts GCMT noted 3 instances (21.5%) where per the Admin Portal notes, the DCA was contacted to close the account on the same date the breathing space request was processed by the collections agent. 
 

 For the remaining 11 customer accounts the following was noted: 
 ● 8 instances (57%) where the collections agent failed to contact the DCA. GCMT noted in testing one sample where the customer made a complaint as they were still receiving communications from the DCA during the breathing space period. 
 ● 3 instances (21.5%) where the collections agent contacted the DCA on the same date the breathing space was processed (confirmed by Collections Management) however this was not documented in the Admin Portal notes.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Results Matrix attached - GCMT process
Remediation Strategy: MAPS provided in RM
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Rated in accordance with issue management program
Issue Action Plans:
Feedback will be provided to collections agents and documented within MQA (1011236)
 QA to be performed internally by Collections Team Leader for 3 months (1011238)

Issue Title: There is an inconsistency in Collection agents’ processing of start-end dates on Admin Portal where the DRS Breathing Space request is received and has a start date commencing on a weekend.
Issue Description:
Background:
 Breathing Space notifications which are received by Clearpay via email and the Insolvency Portal will include the “Date the breathing space was entered on” and the “Date the breathing space ends”
 

 GCMT conducted sample testing over 40 DRS breathing space requests which were received via the Insolvency Portal. Of the 40 DRS breathing space requests, 38 were able to be allocated to a Clearpay account. 
 GCMT noted in practice, it is common for breathing space requests to be received by Clearpay the day before the required start date, however there are instances where the breathing space request is received on the same day that the breathing space is required to commence. 
 Issue: 
 GCMT noted inconsistencies in Collections processing of breathing space requests, for samples which were received and had a required start date commencing on a weekend or samples which were not processed on the same day (which had a start date on the date of notification). 
 

 Of the 38 samples, 10 samples were received on a weekend and/or not processed on the same day (which had a start date on the date of notification) resulting in the processing date by Clearpay Collections being one or two days after the required start date. Of the 10 samples GCMT noted three different approaches taken with regard to the start date entered into Admin Portal on the customers account: 
 ● Approach 1: 3 samples where the start date was backdated to match the required start date and end date per the insolvency portal; 
 ● Approach 2: 6 samples where the start date was the date the breathing space request was processed by the collections agent with maintenance of the same end date per the insolvency portal. This approach resulted in <60 days being processed by Clearpay due to the delay in processing due to the weekend. 
 ○ 1 sample processed for 57 days 
 ○ 3 samples processed for 58 days 
 ○ 2 samples processed for 59 days 
 ● Approach 3: 1 sample where the start date was the date the breathing space request was processed by the collections agent and the end date was extended by 2 days to meet the original 60 day request. (end date per insolvency portal = 5/11, end date processed on Admin Portal = 7/11) 
 

 Collections Management have confirmed that the correct process is for the breathing space request to be backdated to the original start date, with the same end-date maintained per the Insolvency Portal (Approach 1). The backdating of the start-date will not change the fact that the breathing space was processed after the start date, however ensures consistent records of the request period. 
 Additionally, GCMT noted that SOP does not currently document the expected process, which may be resulting in the differences noted in approach.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Results matrix attached - GCMT process
Remediation Strategy: MAP provided in RM
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Rated in accordance with the issue management program
Issue Action Plans:
Feedback will be provided to collections agents and documented within MQA (1011236)
 Update to Standard Operating Procedure (1011882)

Issue Title: MoneyBot Terms & Disclosures User Experience (UX) Change - Implementation Timing Risk
Issue Description:
A user experience (UX) change scheduled for 12/1/2025 will (1) relocate the hyperlink to terms and disclosures relevant for Moneybot from a customer halfsheet that was designed for the initial 0.01% launch to a menu in the upper right corner of the chat interface designed as a clickable three dots “...”, and (2) include a reference to “Moneybot risks” in the static Cash App Investing disclosure in the Stocks Applet. These terms and disclosures include Cash App Generative AI Terms of Use, the Cash App Terms of Service updated with AI product related terms, and Cash App Investing disclosures updated with AI product risks. These changes introduce regulatory exposure due to (1) moving the hyperlink terms and disclosures further from the Moneybot experience and that the placement behind the clickable three dots can be viewed by a regulator as hidden, and (2) the UX update being implemented ahead of the Stocks Applet disclosure update and the supporting processes needed to mitigate the associated risk.
 

 The primary risk is regulatory compliance with FINRA Rule 2210, Exchange Act 10b-5, E-SIGN Act, and other applicable regulations that require clear, conspicuous, and meaningful presentation of terms and disclosures. 
 

 To sufficiently mitigate these risks, Legal and Compliance have recommended four updates to be made: (1) Enhanced contextual in-line disclosures based on certain higher risk prompts, including new tax-free income and fractional shares risk notifications, plus strengthened stock list guardrails, (2) Updated Investing Applet disclosures with explicit MoneyBot reference (this will require a client release which is influencing the delay), 3) A reimbursement process for cases where MoneyBot interactions could reasonably be interpreted as investment advice resulting in losses and 4) Compliance testing of Moneybot prompts and responses, which will complement the testing framework established by Product and Engineering. 
 

 To manage the timing risk, MoneyBot's rollout will remain capped at 1% of non-Teen, IDV'd customers until at least the first three mitigation items are completed. This controlled approach balances the need to improve user experience while demonstrating best efforts to manage regulatory compliance requirements.The risk of ineffective disclosure presentation will persist until all mitigating controls are fully implemented and validated. Failure to properly sequence these changes could expose Block and Cash App Investing to regulatory enforcement actions, potential class action lawsuits, and personal liability for Registered Persons who approve changes that regulators deem non-compliant.
RCA Description: The primary root cause is a process breakdown in the sequencing, governance, and coordination of regulatory-sensitive UX changes, specifically the decision to implement the MoneyBot terms and disclosures relocation before all required mitigating controls and updated disclosures were ready. The issue arose because the product development timeline prioritized UX streamlining and customer drop-off reduction, but the supporting processes for regulatory review, dependency alignment, cross-functional readiness, and deployment sequencing were not sufficiently structured to ensure that the disclosure update, in-line mitigations, testing enhancements, and reimbursement safeguard were implemented in the proper order. This created a timing mismatch that resulted in disclosure placement becoming less conspicuous before the compensating controls designed to mitigate regulatory risk were fully in place. The gap in process design—rather than a technology failure or human error—allowed the UX change to advance despite regulatory exposure, directly leading to the identified compliance risk.
Remediation Strategy: To sufficiently mitigate these risks, Legal and Compliance have recommended four updates to be made: (1) Enhanced contextual in-line disclosures based on certain higher risk prompts, including new tax-free income and fractional shares risk notifications, plus strengthened stock list guardrails, (2) Updated Investing Applet disclosures with explicit MoneyBot reference (this will require a client release which is influencing the delay), 3) A reimbursement process for cases where MoneyBot interactions could reasonably be interpreted as investment advice resulting in losses and 4) Compliance testing of Moneybot prompts and responses, which will complement the testing framework established by Product and Engineering.
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: Significant but not critical consequences, affecting specific functions or business units, with limited external visibility. The issue may escalate to higher-impact consequences if not resolved in a reasonable timeframe.
Risk Domain: Regulatory | Existing and New Reg Adherence | Regulatory Change Management (1000138)
Issue Action Plans:
Create and/or strengthen 3 in-line disclosures (1011889)
 Add references to Moneybot in existing Investing Applet disclosures (1011891)
 Implement reimbursement process (1011893)
 Implement temporary testing framework (Compliance) (1011895)
Closure Request Justification: All action plans have been completed -- see individual action plans for closure justification and attachments
Issue Internal Validation Justification: action plan complete -- see individual action plans

Issue Title: TX App Store Accountability Act Compliance - Multi-Product Age Verification Requirements
Issue Description:
Texas SB2420 enacts the TX App Store Accountability Act, effective January 1, 2026, creates new requirements for app stores and app developers (including Block) for age verification and age rating compliance. The law requires:
 

 App stores to verify user ages into designated categories (U13, 13-15, 16-17, 18+) and obtain parental consent for minors
 Developers to assign age ratings to apps and implement systems to receive/use age verification signals from app stores
 Notification processes for "significant changes" to apps that trigger renewed parental consent
 

 This impacts multiple Block products: Cash App, Square, Afterpay, Tidal, and Bitkey; which currently do not meet the requirements outlined by the regulation. Similar laws in Utah (May 2026) and Louisiana (July 2026) will follow. The law is subject to ongoing litigation and constitutional challenges
RCA Description:
New Regulation - RCA method Regulation Impact Analysis 
 

 New Texas state legislation (SB2420) creates mandatory compliance requirements for app developers that did not previously exist. This is an external regulatory change requiring proactive compliance measures across multiple Block products with mobile applications in app stores. Block currently lack
Remediation Strategy:
Implement coordinated compliance program across all affected Block products including:
 Age rating assignments for all mobile apps
 API integration with Apple and Google app stores for age verification signals
 System development to receive, process, and act on age verification data, including controls to prevent the data from being shared externally and to delete the data after use for verification.
 Process establishment for identifying and reporting "significant changes"
 Cross-product coordination to ensure consistent approach
Issue Type: Gap – a needed process or control is missing entirely.
Issue Rating Justification: Significant regulatory and contractual compliance requirement affecting multiple products, but with manageable implementation timeline and low immediate risk of non-compliance. Failure to comply could result in regulatory penalties and potential of losing access in app stores, but risk is mitigated by coordinated approach and available implementation time.
Risk Domain: Regulatory | Existing and New Reg Adherence | Regulatory Change Management (1000138)

Business Unit: CCO Afterpay Complaint Operations - Group Lead
Issue Title: Afterpay Complaints_RPAA (Escalations to Counsel). Incomplete Zendesk–Admin Portal Integration Risks Data Loss
Issue Description: The integration between Zendesk and the Admin Portal is incomplete, resulting in resolution costs and complaint tags not being transferred as intended. These gaps compromise data accuracy and create a risk of missing valuable operational insights. As a result, the organization lacks a seamless exchange of key information needed for informed decision-making.
Explanation for Delay in Issue Creation: Legacy Issue ID#1258123
RCA Description: The cause of this issue is that there is no API connection between the customer communication system Zendesk and the servicing system Admin Portal.
Remediation Strategy:
Remediation is not feasible or appropriate in this case because the impacted AP functionality is scheduled to be fully migrated and decommissioned as part of the Salesforce implementation planned for 2026. The level of effort required to design, develop, test, and deploy a permanent fix in the current platform would be significant, would introduce change risk to a legacy system nearing end-of-life, and would divert scarce engineering and project resources away from the strategic Salesforce migration.
 

 Given the limited remaining lifespan of the current AP solution and the high cost (time, budget, and opportunity cost) of remediation relative to that lifespan, implementing a full fix is not commercially justified. The risk will instead be managed through interim/compensating controls and monitoring until the legacy AP system is retired, at which point the underlying issue will be eliminated as part of the transition to Salesforce.

Australia

 United Kingdom
 United States

Business Unit: Cash - PE - Financial Products - Bitcoin - Bitcoin Network
Issue Title: SAR Case Creation Failure for BTC queues
Issue Description: Between November 6 and December 5, 2025, escalations from newly deployed BTC Transaction Monitoring (TM) queues & EDD queues failed to generate the required Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) cases within Notary. Although analysts correctly used the SAR-escalation function, a backend mapping defect prevented case creation. As a result, 3,496 escalations were not routed for mandated SAR review, creating a significant backlog and introducing material regulatory and SLA risk. A corrective fix was deployed on December 7, 2025, and a structured backfill and staffing plan is underway to remediate the missed caseload.
RCA Description:
Primary Root Cause
 A backend configuration and mapping defect prevented escalations from the newly introduced BTC TM queues from triggering SAR-case creation in Notary. Although the front-end escalation option appeared functional to analysts, the underlying routing logic was never correctly mapped to the SAR-queue workflow.
 Contributing Factors
 Engineering Alignment Gap
 

 

 The BTC engineering team, which does not typically build compliance-critical workflows, developed the queues without complete alignment on compliance operational requirements.
 Key SAR-workflow dependencies (e.g., escalation routing, case-creation triggers) were not fully translated into engineering specifications.
 

 

 Lack of Dedicated Compliance Program Oversight
 

 

 No Compliance Ops program manager was available during queue go live to oversee or validate queue design, requirements, testing and deployment. 
 Ops leadership relied on BTC engineering’s understanding of compliance workflows, which resulted in functional gaps going undetected.
 

 

 Queue Worked by External Analysts
 

 

 Mainly external teams handling the queue and they did not identify the anomalies as they may not have full context of these workflows. 
 Hence, reduced SAR-case output was not immediately surfaced through standard operational feedback loops.
 

 

 Delayed Detection Through Volume Trending
 

 

 The issue became apparent only after Ops leadership noticed a decline in SAR-case creation volume that conflicted with expected increases following queue launches.
 Subsequent sampling confirmed that escalations from three queues were not producing SAR cases at all.
 

 

 Why Detection Was Delayed
 The defect was not visible to analysts due to the presence of the escalation option in the UI, lack of error messaging, and absence of real-time reconciliation between escalations submitted and SAR cases created. As a result, the issue persisted for approximately one month before being escalated.
Remediation Strategy:
Two remediation steps were identified:
 Correct backend escalation mapping
 

 

 Fix deployed December 7, 2025.
 Validation in progress. EDD queue appears to have been fixed however typology and pattern selection option are not available at the moment. TM queues still do not appear to be creating cases as of 12/09. 
 

 

 Backfill SAR cases for missed escalations
 

 

 In progress.
 Backfilled cases will include correct due dates aligned to actual escalation aging.
Issue Type: Gap – a needed process or control is missing entirely.
Issue Rating Justification: The issue is directly related to SAR filing regulatory obligation and exposes Block to potential regulatory fines and enforcement action, it has been assessed as High severity
Risk Domain: Compliance | Financial Crime | Anti-Money Laundering & Terrorist Financing (1000126)
Issue Action Plans:
Correct backend escalation mapping (1031199)
 Backfill SAR cases for missed escalation (1031841)
 Operations to complete investigation of cases created through backfill (1031843)
Closure Request Justification: See closure documentation within each action plan.

Issue Title: Cash App Customer Email Address Removal - Legacy Population Without Required Contact Information
Issue Description: Historically, Cash App's platform allowed customers to create an account with only a phone number AND we didn't prevent users from removing their last email (as long as they had one phone number left). While a preventive fix has been implemented to stop removing the LAST email on file, a legacy population of customers remains without email addresses, creating operational, regulatory, and customer service challenges across multiple business lines. This gap impacts customer communication capabilities, regulatory notification requirements, account security protocols, and business continuity processes that rely on email as a primary contact method.
RCA Description:
Today we require a single email address or SMS phone number (internally referred to as an Alias) in the sign-up flow. Historically, this was intentional to reduce onboarding friction and keep sign-up completion rates high. Our legacy account structure, which contains an account-alias uniqueness constraint, makes it impossible for customers to use the same phone number across multiple accounts. Because of this fact, we know customers use their second Aliases to create new accounts for different use cases. This not only makes it more difficult for the customer to manage these multiple accounts (vs. the experience that now exists under the Single Accountholder Model, which makes it more user friendly), it also makes it more difficult for Cash App to quickly and clearly identify multiple accounts across the same customer. 
 

 Additionally, since we don’t require all accounts to have an email, ensuring that we’re consistently sending out critical communications is difficult. With the launch of the Single Accountholder Model (SAM) customers will now be able to use multiple accounts free from that constraint - a given Alias or set of Aliases can now be used to manage multiple accounts. SAM unblocks Cash App’s ability to proactively collect more than one Alias from a customer in sign-up, including an email from everyone, and backfill existing Cash App users such that all accountholders have at least one linked SMS and one email address.
Remediation Strategy:
Remove the ability for customers to remove their email address - complete 
 For accounts with missing emails; launch a campaign to collect second alias
Impacted Business Area:
Cash App
 Cash - Business Ops - Investing
 Square Risk Product, Policy, and Operations
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: Lack of a definition for material fraud event to drive external reporting
Issue Description: Our organization lacks a formal, documented definition of “material fraud event” and an established, consistent notification process to escalate such events to Financial Operations. As a result, reporting to our banking partners is performed on an ad-hoc basis, increasing the risk of non-compliance with partner agreements and delaying the detection and mitigation of coordinated fraudulent activity.
RCA Description:
Our organization has never formalized what constitutes a “material fraud event” for Cash Card products nor assigned clear ownership for maintaining that definition. As a result:
 

 Fraud teams lack a standardized threshold or checklist to decide when an event must be reported as “material,” so each team applies its own informal criteria.
 No documented policy or workflow exists to automatically escalate qualifying fraud patterns to Financial Operations outside of the standard incident-management tool.
 Because escalation responsibilities and channels haven’t been defined or implemented in our fraud-monitoring platforms, alerts and reports rely on individual judgment and manual handoffs.
 In short, the absence of a codified definition and a dedicated escalation process—coupled with siloed tools and unclear roles—drives inconsistent, ad-hoc reporting of material fraud events.
Remediation Strategy: Create an agreed upon definition of material fraud event for third party reporting
Issue Type: Gap – a needed process or control is missing entirely.
Issue Rating Justification: The issue can impact reporting SLAs to third party
Issue Progress Updates: see agreed upon definition of material fraud event
Issue Action Plans: Create an Agreed upon Definition of Material Fraud Event (1015850)
Closure Request Justification: Definition has been drafted and shared
Issue Internal Validation Justification: Definition aligned, shared with first and second line stakeholders.

Issue Title: REF 2 - SEL Complaints Monitoring Q3 2025 - Complaints Management and QA
Issue Description:
Background:
 GCMT conducted testing on a sample of 47 complaints across Tiers 1, 2, and 3 (including 3- escalated) to verify that all required processes documented within the Complaint Standard Operating Procedures were followed by the Customer Success, Complaint Management, Sales and Account Management team members. 
 Policies/Standard Operating Procedures 
 Complaints Process Article Square Wise
 EU/UK Runbook
 Square UK Guidelines for Complaint Handling
 Flag a Complaint - SAM
 

 Issue Description: 
 GCMT identified a 66% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 34% exception rate (16 samples) where one or more requirements were not followed by the agent per the Standard Operating Procedure. The exceptions identified relate to the following areas: 
 ● Missed SLAs
 → Complaint Resolution
 1 Complaint not responded to within the 15 working days SLA (i.e., 19 business days) with no holding letter sent to the complainant, and this was not recorded in the breach register.
 → Complaint Acknowledgement
 1 Complaint acknowledgement not sent within the 3 business days SLA. Complaints Management stated that "due to a tooling issue, the country code was not assigned when the complaint was flagged. There are updates to the complaints Intake form, one of them being that country code is now a required field, so complaints cannot be filled out with null country. And, the country code will default when possible by pulling from the data tied to the regulator attached to the case, improving accuracy. Fix on track to roll out by Q4 2025".
 ● Missing Complaint Outcome Flag for Regulated Complaints
 4 Regulated Complaints not flagged in Salesforce. 
 ● Missing Regulated Complaint Flag
 2 Regulated Complaints not flagged in Salesforce.
 ● Incorrect Severity Tier
 4 Complaints incorrectly flagged as Tier 1 instead of Tier 3. 
 ● Customer Success Advocate (CSA) Feedback Process
 1 Complaint flagged by a Customer Success Advocate (CSA) with no Amazon Connect ID/Maestro ID, Regulator User Token, or email address recorded in the complaint form. As a result, the Complaint Manager (CM) was unable to respond to the complaint and did not provide feedback to the CSA about the missing fields. GCMT noted that the process for providing feedback to CSA’s is not formalised and this is only covered in the Complaints Management QA scorecard.
 ● Incorrect Communication Hyperlinks
 → Complaints Process hyperlink noted in the complaint acknowledgement emails is broken (i.e., hyperlink relates to a page noting an Error 404 Page not found).
 → Two Salesforce tickets state "click here to learn more about our Information Requests", however the hyperlink does not show anything specific to the Information Requests process.
 ● Design Observation - Updates to Procedures required to reflect current process and expectations 
 → Complaint Resolution SLA 
 The EU/UK Runbook states "15 day SLA for final response to be sent" and it also states that "any UK Complaint resolved beyond 15 days without exceptional circumstances must be promptly reported to both the Regulatory Governance Committee and the board", and this statement is in line with section 3 of the SEL Square - Complaints Requirement for the UK Market document. 
 Considering this, GCMT initially used the 15 days SLA for the transactional testing SLA calculation and identified 12 complaints resolved after the 15 days SLA (i.e., complaints resolved between 17 and 28 days). However, Complaints Management noted that the EU/UK Runbook is not clear as the SLA should be 15 business days, not 15 days.
 → Complaint Outcome for Unregulated Complaints 
 14 Unregulated Complaints were not flagged in Salesforce. Complaints Management noted that the complaint outcome flag is not explicitly required for unregulated complaints, however this process is not formalised in the EU/UK Runbook.
 __________________________________________________________________________________
 

 Complaints Management Quality Assurance 
 Background: 
 GCMT reviewed 15 Tier 3 complaint tickets which were graded by the Complaints Management Quality Assurance Team, to confirm the accuracy of the grading outcome and any exceptions identified.
 

 Issue Description: 
 GCMT identified a 93% effectiveness rate where GCMT was aligned with the grading outcomes and a 7% exception rate (1 sample) where GCMT identified one exception. The exception identified relates to the following: 
 ● Complaint Acknowledgement SLA Tracking and Reporting
 Missed complaint acknowledgement SLA for one complaint identified in the Complaint Management QA form. Complaints Management stated that the breach tracker is not used for missed complaint acknowledgement SLAs and these are reviewed during monthly metrics calculations for reporting. However, the missed complaint acknowledgement SLA is not formalised in the EU/UK Runbook.
 ● Complaints Management QA form
 → No formalised process for the completion of the Complaints Management QA form and feedback process.

Business Unit: Risk Ops Identity Controls - Team Lead
Issue Title: [Cash App] - Process Gap: Undefined Workflow for Unsolicited Minor Cash Card Reports
Issue Description:
When minors receive unsolicited Cash App cards, there is no defined process for how Customer Operations (CO) and Risk Operations teams should handle incoming reports or complaints. This creates inconsistent handling, potential compliance gaps, and unclear escalation paths.
 

 Context: eCBSV's fuzzy DOB matching logic (working as designed with prior risk acceptance) allows minors to be verified as adults, enabling account/Cash Card issuance to minors when they shouldn't be able to open accounts. Approximately 1,500 individuals have been impacted. eCBSV enhancements for exact DOB matching are planned for July 2026. A related issue exists covering broader ID Theft for Minors process enhancements (CCO and Risk Ops).
 

 Impact: No clear workflow for CO to triage unsolicited minor Cash Card reports; Risk Ops lacks defined decision-making framework for ID Theft cases involving minors; potential compliance exposure (consumer protection, regulatory reporting); inconsistent customer experience and resolution paths.
RCA Description:
Primary Root Cause: Missing process design and cross-functional alignment during Cash Card and eCBSV control implementation. The workflow for handling unsolicited minor Cash Card reports was never defined, leaving CCO and Risk Ops without clear guidance on triage, escalation, or resolution.
 

 Supporting Evidence:
 No CCO intake workflow exists for unsolicited minor Cash Card reports
 Risk Ops has no defined decision-making framework for ID Theft cases involving minors
 Ad-hoc handling has masked the absence of a formal process
 ~1,500 affected individuals indicate systemic gap, not isolated incidents
 Related ID Theft for Minors issue confirms broader process gaps
 Connection to Issue: The absence of a defined process directly caused inconsistent handling, compliance exposure, and poor customer experience. Without clear workflows and decision frameworks, CCO and Risk Ops teams cannot consistently triage, escalate, or resolve unsolicited minor Cash Card reports, leaving the organization exposed to regulatory and consumer protection risks.
Remediation Strategy:
Remediation Strategy
 Issue: [Cash App] - Process Gap: Undefined Workflow for Unsolicited Minor Cash Card Reports
 

 Approach: Establish end-to-end operational workflows and governance to identify, triage, and remediate cases of unsolicited cards issued to minors.
 

 Success Criteria: All workflows operational, customer population remediated, Legal/Compliance approved communications, ready for closure validation.
Issue Type: Gap – a needed process or control is missing entirely.
Issue Rating Justification:
Scope: ~1,500 affected individuals (minors with unauthorized accounts) represents a material population impact. (Still needs vetted/confirmed).
 

 Impact:
 

 Consumer protection exposure (minors unable to legally consent to account opening)
 Potential regulatory reporting obligations (COPPA, Fair Credit Reporting Act)
 Inconsistent customer experience and resolution paths
 Reputational risk if unsolicited minor accounts become public
 

 Urgency: Process gap is active and ongoing with no defined remediation path for existing population. New unsolicited minor Cash Card reports continue to arrive with no clear handling procedure.
 

 Compliance Risk: HIGH - Potential violations of consumer protection regulations and regulatory reporting requirements.
 

 Mitigating Factors:
 

 Related issue exists for broader ID Theft for Minors process work (reduces isolation of this gap)
 eCBSV control enhancements planned for July 2026 (provides future control improvement)
 Risk acceptance already documented for fuzzy matching logic (control is working as designed)
 

 Overall Assessment: HIGH severity due to active consumer protection exposure, material affected population size (~1,500), ongoing operational gap with no defined process, and regulatory compliance implications. This meets the "Critical, organization-wide consequences" threshold per the Program Document.
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: Promotional Offer Expiration Causing Customer Confusion
Issue Description: A test promotional offer was published on 9/1 and claimed by a number of customers before expiring the same day when it reached its budget limit. On 9/2, a separate public version of the offer went live. Customers who had claimed or viewed the original test offer believed it remained active on their cards and made qualifying purchases with the expectation of receiving a $10 reimbursement.
RCA Description: This Issue was identified by Peter Monterubio of the Cash Bugs Triage Team (CBT). The individual who set up the offer was new to setting up offers. They meant to only extend the offer as a test to a small subset of individuals on 9/1, instead the offer went live to a larger group.
Remediation Strategy: It was determined on 9/2 to reimburse customers. The CBT is responsible for these escalations and reimbursements.
Not an Issue Justification: This does not constitute a systemic issue. The $10 Anywhere Test promotional offer was intended for internal employee testing only; however, a limited number of customers were able to access the offer. Upon discovery by Peter Monterubio, remediation efforts were immediately initiated. A total of 16 cases were escalated to the Cash Bug Triage Team (CBT), the designated owner for promotional offer remediation. CBT reviewed eligibility and reimbursed all customers who met the offer criteria. No ongoing customer impact or unresolved complaints were identified.

Issue Title: Observation #1: Adverse Media Screening for Verified Accounts Control Effectiveness Discrepancies
Issue Description:
This issue is directly linked to Protecht ID 1260763, an issue that was originally self-identified and tracked 2 components of remediation agreed for this issue. The creation of this issue ID is intended to track the remaining action plan:
 

 Following the completion of operational effectiveness testing on Block’s selective adverse media screening process, two of the 25 verified customer accounts sampled did not undergo adverse media screening, despite meeting the
 documented eligibility criteria outlined on page 2 of Negative News Screening - Adyen Acquiring.pdf. This indicates a failure in the operational effectiveness of the remediation enhancements that were implemented. The screening gaps were identified based on null values in multiple screening system fields (e.g., Screen_Timestamp, Screen_Receipt_ID, and Lexis_Nexis_Response_ID), within neg_news_screening_report.csv, and were confirmed by Block at the time of data submission.
 Block's Compliance Analytics Lead acknowledged the issue during a working session held on 05/07/2025 and confirmed that an internal investigation determined both discrepancies were process failures. These accounts were eligible under the Institution’s defined risk score, inflow thresholds, and exclusion criteria, but were not captured by the production queue logic, documented in sqwave_25676_negative_news_screening.sql. While Block acknowledged the process failures, documentation of a completed root cause analysis or broader population impact assessment was not available at the time of testing.

Background:
 Per the Block Inc. Global Sanctions Compliance Program Standard, Block mandates Customers must undergo sanctions screening upon onboarding and on an ongoing daily basis throughout the course of their relationship with Block. Customers should not be allowed to transact until initial sanctions screening is completed and any alerts are cleared.
 

 The Cash Flow Holds adversity is designed to mitigate sanctions-related risk by restricting customer activity during investigations. As documented in the MVP Build Requirements development document and go/$adverseactions, the adversity should:
 Suspend all active sponsorships associated with the customer under review
 Restrict the ability to order new Cash Cards until the sanctions review is resolved
 

 Testing Results: 
 During testing of 30 sampled customers with a sanctions-related case opened during the review period, the following exceptions were identified:
 Sponsorship Suspension: The Cash Flow Holds adversity does not suspend active sponsorships. It only restricts new sponsorship requests or approvals. Five out of 30 tested samples maintained active sponsorships throughout their sanctions investigation.
 Card Ordering Restriction: The Cash Flow Holds adversity does not prevent customers from ordering a Cash Card. It only blocks card activation. 12 out of 30 tested samples were able to order a Cash Card during their sanctions investigation. 
 Customers cannot activate a Cash App Card without physically possessing it (as activation requires scanning the QR code or manually entering card details) and all Cash Card transactions are declined when the Cash Flow Holds adversity is applied.
 

 Impact
 The functionality gaps weaken the effectiveness of the Cash Flow Holds adversity during sanctions reviews. Active sponsorships were not suspended, allowing continued activity among potentially sanctioned users. Customers were also able to order Cash Cards while under review, creating potential risk if fulfillment or activation controls fail. Although secondary safeguards limit immediate exposure, the misalignment between documented requirements and system behavior represents a gap in control oversight and validation.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Refer to Issue Description and Results Matrix attached, which details the RCA. GCMT are exempt from completing a separate RCA.
Remediation Strategy: Refer to Issue Description and Results Matrix attached, which details the RCA. GCMT are exempt from completing a separate RCA.
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Rated in accordance with the issue severity matrix - no immediate regulatory implications, insignificant operational disruptions or low or no reputational visibility.
Risk Domain: Compliance | Financial Crime | Anti-Money Laundering & Terrorist Financing (1000126)
Issue Action Plans: Cash Flow Holds Adversity does not fully enforce documented sanctions restrictions during investigations (1022541)

Issue Title: Address Coverage Gaps in Customer Sanctions Screening
Issue Description:
Background
 Per the Block Inc. Global Sanctions Compliance Program Standard, Block mandates Customers must undergo sanctions screening upon onboarding and on an ongoing daily basis throughout the course of their relationship with Block. 
 

 For Customers onboarded as Individuals, screening is conducted on:
 The legal name of the customer; and
 The customer’s physical address, where collected.
 

 For Customers onboarded as Entities, screening is conducted on:
 The legal name of the Business
 The Business trading name or “Doing Business As” (“DBA”) name, if applicable;
 The Business address, where collected.
 The legal name of the activator (sign-up) individual;
 The physical address of the activator individual, where collected;
 The legal name of any additional Related Parties obtained during onboarding and at any point during the Customer relationship; and
 The physical address of any additional Related Parties obtained during onboarding and at any point during the Customer relationship.
 

 When a customer is onboarded, provides new personal information, or otherwise qualifies for a sanctions screening trigger (i.e., product intent), microservice Screendoor sources customer information from across Cash App and sends requests to BadActors for screening against the applicable compliance concerns (e.g., sanctions lists such as OFAC SDN). BadActors then transmits the information to third-party service provider LexisNexis Bridger to perform the actual screening.
 

 Issue
 When a customer is submitted for sanctions screening, only one associated address is submitted for screening. The customer's primary address is screened which is determined by the following address source ordering:
 CUSTOMER, (manually inputted customer address)
 CARD_ACTIVATION (a cash card was activated at this address),
 GOVERNMENT_ID, (pulled from a verified government id submitted by the customer)
 IDOLOGY_IDV, (returned from the eIDV provider Idology)
 ALLOY_IDV, (returned from the eIDV provider Alloy)
 TLO_ADDRESS_SEARCH, (returned from the eIDV provider TLO)
 If the customer enters an address that differs from the addresses returned by documentary or electronic identity verification sources, the customer-provided address overrides all others and is the only address screened.
 

 During testing of 30 sampled sanctions screenings, one sample was missing an address. The customer joined on 2/9/25 and completed IDV, including address collection, on 8/11/25. While we cannot confirm whether Screendoor sent the address to Bad Actors, it should have been included in the 8/11/25 and 10/1/25 screening requests. The address appears in the 10/1/25 screening but not in the 8/11/25 screening.
 

 Impact
 The identified gaps reduce the accuracy and consistency of information used for sanctions screening. While all Cash App customers are domestic which lowers sanctions risk and GCMT did not identify any missed screenings or positive sanctions matches, incomplete or missing addresses weaken the effectiveness of the screening process.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Refer to Issue Description and Results Matrix attached, which details the RCA. GCMT are exempt from completing a separate RCA.
Remediation Strategy: Refer to Issue Description and Results Matrix attached, which details the RCA. GCMT are exempt from completing a separate RCA.
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: Rated in accordance with the issue severity matrix - no immediate regulatory implications, insignificant operational disruptions or low or no reputational visibility.
Risk Domain: Compliance | Financial Crime | Anti-Money Laundering & Terrorist Financing (1000126)
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information
Issue Action Plans: Address Coverage Gaps in Customer Sanctions Screening (1022545)
