Issue Title: Compliance Detection Operations Out-of-SLA and Backlogged Case Remediation
Issue Description: In September 2025, a population of ~300 cases was identified as being Out-of-SLA or backlogged as a result of implementing improved SLA reporting mechanisms. The main causes for these cases breaching SLA were due to system tooling issues, ad hoc cases being mistakenly created or assigned to members of engineering or other operational teams, short SLAs (1 Calendar - 2 days) for SSP alerts where weekends impacted SLA adherence, and agent error.
RCA Description:
Define the problem: A relatively small population of ~300 Block Compliance alerts (Cash UUP, Cash TM, Square TM, Square UUP) were not addressed or resolved within their designated Service Level Agreement (SLA).
 

 Why? Cases fell out of SLA or became backlogged due to several factors:
 Tooling errors - 
 Block’s Case Management System (Regulator) prevented LegitScript alerts from being accessed and closed by agents due to a 501 error code related to missing website URLs. The 501 error code for the remaining cases was due to queue deprecation (https://github.com/squareup/regulator/pull/21848). These 11 cases were generated between 2019-2022. Slack
 Regulator reporting functionality did not provide visibility into open, Out-of-SLA and backlogged alerts. Open alerts previously unreported were identified when a new SLA Reporting Dashboard was implemented within Tableau in September 2025.
 People:
 A population of ad-hoc cases were unintentionally created by agents from Compliance or other Operational teams. These cases were not triggered by our system controls for AML/TF or UUP risk. Agents failed to recognize that a case had been created and would need to be closed out within the 30-day SLA, therefore breaching SLA and being reported as Out-of-SLA or backlogged.
 Process:
 Several of Block’s Square Secure Profile (SSP) queues have 1-2 calendar day SLA’s. Analysts are able to request information from the merchant via SSP. If a merchant response is received after-hours on Friday or over the weekend, these cases will be reported as Out-of-SLA until agents are back online to review them.
Remediation Strategy:
Key Objectives - 
 Remediate Out-of-SLA or backlogged alerts
 Implement operational tools and processes for more timely and accurate SLA reporting on a daily basis to prevent future SLA breaches
 Consistently maintain SLAs across all TM and UUP workflows
 Major milestones - 
 Create near real-time reporting on SLA status across business units - BIrd’s Eye View (Tableau)
 Remediate all Out-of-SLA or backlogged alerts
 Consistently maintain SLAs across all TM and UUP workflows
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Block’s Compliance Operations program, specifically the Cash TM, Cash UUP, Square TM and Square UUP teams had a population of alerts that had either fallen out-of-SLA or were backlogged. The alerts in this population largely consisted of SSP cases where Block was waiting on a response from a merchant, alerts that had become inaccessible due to system tooling errors, and ad hoc alerts mistakenly created by agents. Per the Block Enterprise Risk Taxonomy 2025, weakness in “Financial Crime” compliance relates to the risk of money laundering, fraud, bribery, or corruption due to compliance failures, such as weak AML controls leading to regulatory fines. The self-identified issue listed here falls within this level of risk. While these alerts may have remained open and breached SLA, our internal controls continued to monitor account activity and any subsequent alerts were investigated and actioned appropriately, including any reporting obligation that may have been necessary.
Risk Domain: Compliance | Financial Crime | Anti-Money Laundering & Terrorist Financing (1000126)
Issue Progress Updates: Issue is a retro issue that was opened after remediation had been completed. Issue owner is in the process of documenting the completed action plans for Protecht intake and the actions and issue will then be closed.
Issue Action Plans: Remediate out-of-SLA or backlogged alerts (1027890)
Closure Request Justification: See documented evidence memo (also submitted for action plan closure).
Issue Internal Validation Justification: Action Plan closed

Issue Title: Customer Success Agent adherence to Standard Operating Procedures requires uplift to ensure consistency in the handling of financial hardship requests and alignment to Square Loan’s financial hardship processes/requirements.
Issue Description:
Standard Operating Procedure: 
 • Square Wise - Resolve Square Loans Post Origination Questions 
 • Square Wise - Escalate Square Loans Questions 
 

 Background: 
 GCMT reviewed 40 requests received from Sellers, where the Seller was seeking payment and/or financial hardship assistance with their Square Loan to confirm if the request was handled/processed by the Customer Success agent in accordance with all requirements in the Standard Operating Procedure. This consisted of 18 payment plan requests, 20 hold rate relief requests and 2 Minimum Payment Delay Requests. 
 

 Issue: 
 GCMT identified a 50% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 50% exception rate (20 exceptions) where the agent did not follow all steps in the Standard Operating Procedure and/or the response provided to the Seller was not adequate. These results are broken down below by request type.
 

 Payment Plan 
 GCMT tested a sample of 18 payment plan requests and identified a 40% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 60% exception rate (10 samples). Detail relating to the type of exceptions have been documented below: 
 

 5 samples where Information provided to loan operations was not complete and/or inaccurate (for one of these samples incorrect information was also provided to the customer)
 2 samples where incorrect information was provided to the customer
 1 sample where the agent promised the customer a call back/email update which was not completed
 1 sample where no communication was made by either the CX agent or loans following escalation to loan operations about the request and what could be provided
 1 sample where the agent asked the customer what hold rate reduction (percentage) they wanted
 

 Hold Rate Relief 
 GCMT tested a sample of 20 Hold Rate Relief requests and identified a 55% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 45% exception rate (9 samples). Detail relating to the type of exceptions have been documented below: 
 3 samples where the agent asked the customer how long they would want the HRR for and/or the amount they would want the rate reduced by? As the standard HRR is always 1 month at 50%, the question to the customer is misrepresenting the process and could imply that the customer has input as to the interest rate they will pay and how long they will pay it for which is not the case. For one of the samples the escalation to loan operations was inaccurate.
 2 samples where the hold rate was offered which is not allowed per the Loans SOP
 1 sample where the agent provided a guarantee that the HRR would be processed
 1 sample where the agent promised to get in touch with them with an outcome however did this not occur
 1 sample where the IDV was not completed in full prior to discussing the account
 1 sample where the agent did not respond to the LO in the slack escalation resulting in the customer not receiving a response.
 

 Minimum Payment Delay 
 GCMT tested a sample of 2 Minimum Payment Delay requests and identified a 50% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 50% exception rate (1 sample). Detail relating to the type of exception have been documented below: 
 CS agent guaranteed to the Seller that the request for a HRR would be processed instead of stating that the request would be escalated and reviewed by the loans team
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: RM uploaded - GCMT agreed process
Remediation Strategy: Per MAPS in RM
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Rated in accordance with the issue management program
Issue Action Plans: CS and Loan Ops will collaborate on reviewing procedures and Square Wise articles (1023904)

Issue Title: The loans team provision of a payment plan to Sellers is at times unfair due to the design of the Square Wise standard operating procedure and the inconsistent delivery of those procedures by the Customer Success Agents
Issue Description:
Background:
 GCMT reviewed 18 payment plan requests to confirm if the request was handled/processed by the Customer Success Agent in accordance with all requirements in the Standard Operating Procedure. 
 

 Issue: 
 GCMT noted through sample testing that there is an inconsistency between customer success agent's approach when asking the Seller for information to refer their financial hardship request to the loans operations teams. 
 

 Customer Success agents (‘CSA’) can send the Seller’s payment plan request to the loans operations team without asking for any information from the Seller or a CSA asks the Seller for additional information i.e how much can you afford to pay and when can you start payments. Due to this inconsistency, the provision of Hardship to Sellers is not fair i.e not equal. 
 This may be attributed to the current “Square Wise - Escalate Square Loans Questions” standard operating procedure which requires Customer Success Agents to “Move to “Escalate to Australia Loan Ops,” and provide a brief description of the seller’s reason for needing relief.” 
 

 As the brief description obtained from the Seller varies, the loans agent inputs into the payment plan tool may result in inconsistent and therefore unfair outcomes for Sellers.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: RM Attached - GCMT agreed process
Remediation Strategy: MAPS provided in RM
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Rated in accordance with the issue management program
Issue Action Plans: Update to the communication process from Customer Service to Loan Operations (1023906)

Issue Title: Loan Operation Agents adherence to Standard Operating Procedures requires uplift to ensure consistency in the handling of financial hardship requests to ensure that we Treat Customers Fairly.
Issue Description:
Standard Operating Procedure: 
 • Global Banking Operations - Financial Hardship Framework 
 • Global Banking Operations - Loans Payment Plan 
 • Global Banking Operations - Hold Rate Relief Guide
 • Global Banking Operations - Extend Minimum Payment
 

 Background:
 GCMT reviewed 40 requests received from Sellers, where the Seller was seeking payment and/or financial hardship assistance with their Square Loan to confirm if the request was handled/processed by the loan agent in accordance with all requirements in the Standard Operating Procedure. This sample consisted of 18 payment plan requests, 20 hold rate relief requests and 2 Minimum Payment Delay Requests.
 

 Issue: 
 GCMT identified an 33% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 67% exception rate ( 27 exceptions) where the agent did not follow all steps in the Standard Operating Procedure and/or the response provided to the Seller was not adequate. These results are broken down below by request type. 
 

 Payment Plan 
 GCMT tested a sample of 18 payment plan requests and identified a 39% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 61% exception rate (11 samples). Detail relating to the type of exceptions have been documented below: 
 

 • 3 samples where the monthly amount offered as part of the financial hardship payment plan was higher than the minimum amount per the original plan contract
 • 2 samples where the customer responded to the initial email sent by the loans agent however no response was received. For one of these samples the customer followed up multiple times advising Square that they could not afford the payment plan offered, however no solution or additional approval was sought for the customer, with a response provided almost a month later. 
 • 2 samples where the delay auto-debit was not processed on the account when the request was received. For one of these samples, this resulted in the payment being taken out and a delay in processing the payment plan. 
 • 2 samples where there was a lengthy delay from the date of payment plan acceptance to processing. GCMT noted no customer harm as the hold was correctly placed by the agent. 
 • 1 sample where no follow up was made by the loans agent for the customer to confirm the payment plan amount (requested). Additionally no hold was put on the account as required by the SOP. 
 • 1 sample where there was no evidence of the payment plan confirmation being sent on Salesforce (No copy of the email visible) 
 

 Hold Rate Relief 
 GCMT tested a sample of 20 Hold Rate Relief requests and identified a 20% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 80% exception rate (16 samples) where the agent did not follow all steps in the Standard Operating Procedure and/or the response provided to the Seller was not adequate. These results are broken down below by request type. 
 

 • 12 samples where +1 day was not added to the 30 day period on regulator 
 • 6 samples where the 180 day eligibility (third hold) was not placed on the account 
 • 5 samples where the Seller responded to the initial email sent by the loans agent however no response was received. 
 • 1 sample where there was a lengthy delay to process the hold rate after Customer Success confirmation 
 • 1 sample where PMO approval was not obtained for the fourth hold rate. For this sample, there was also no response by loans after Customer Success obtained/provided more information per loan request, which promoted additional follow ups from the Seller. 
 • 1 sample where a hold rate was processed, however based on the sellers circumstances shared a payment plan would have been a more suitable option
 

 Minimum Payment Delay Requests 
 GCMT tested a sample of 2 Minimum Payment Delay requests and identified a 50% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 50% exception rate (1 sample). Detail relating to the type of exception have been documented below: 
 

 • The response provided by the loan agent to the Seller was factually incorrect.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: RM attached - Agreed GCMT process
Remediation Strategy: MAPS provided in RM
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Rated in accordance with the issue management program
Issue Action Plans: Refresher training and review for refunds where a debit has occured (1023908)

Issue Title: There is a lack of automated controls to support the management of FH requests and the current distinction between financial hardship PP/ non-financial hardship PP may be causing confusion resulting in non-compliance with SOPS
Issue Description:
Standard Operating Procedure: 
 • Global Banking Operations - Financial Hardship Framework 
 • Global Banking Operations - Loans Payment Plan 
 • Global Banking Operations - Hold Rate Relief Guide
 • Global Banking Operations - Extend Minimum Payment
 

 Background:
 GCMT reviewed 40 requests received from Sellers, where the Seller was seeking payment and/or financial hardship assistance with their Square Loan to confirm if the request was handled/processed by the loan agent in accordance with all requirements in the Standard Operating Procedure. This sample consisted of 18 payment plan requests, 20 hold rate relief requests and 2 Minimum Payment Delay Requests.
 

 Issue: 
 GCMT noted the following theme observations, through sample-testing of financial hardship requests. 
 Loan Operations - Manual processing & tracking of financial hardship cases
 GCMT noted in sample testing of financial hardship requests, multiple exceptions where the loan agent failed to follow up with a Seller or respond to an additional email or enquiry which was related to the financial hardship request and solution offered. GCMT noted that there is no automated solution or system control which assists loan agents in tracking all open and/or unresolved cases where a follow-up or response is required. The process is currently reliant on loan agents to manually process and re-review each case to determine if an additional email, enquiry or follow up is required which is not sustainable in the long-term and if volumes increase. 
 

 Additionally, GCMT noted that all processing is currently manual for financial hardship requests except for automated emails. Square AU does not currently provide self-service payment plan requests, which is offered in the US market. The facilitation of an automated solution would assist in the reduction of manual processing errors. 
 

 Financial Hardship VS Non-Financial Hardship Payment Plan Requests 
 The current "Banking Operations - Loans Payment Plan" Standard Operating Procedure, currently documents separate processes and allowances for payment plan requests which are due to a Seller experiencing financial hardship and "standard" payment plan requests which are not related to financial hardship. The key allowance is that for "standard" payment plan requests, the monthly amount offered under the payment plan does not need to be lower than the monthly minimum payment amount the Seller would have to repay under the original contract. 
 

 All payment plan requests should be classified as a financial hardship request, as a payment plan is requested when the Seller is not able to make payments due to financial difficulties or lack of income/cashflow. Additionally, GCMT noted in sample testing that the current allowances may be causing confusion and the key root cause for exceptions identified above relating to payment plans being offered by loans for financial hardship cases, which are not lower than the monthly minimum payment amount. 
 

 Payment Plan Macros 
 GCMT noted an inconsistency in the payment plan macro which is being sent to Sellers by Loan Agents. The macro which is being used in the majority of payment plan requests reviewed, does not include wording to let the Seller know that they are able to negotiate or respond if the payment plan which has been offered to them is not suitable for their needs. 
 

 Of the 18 payment plan requests sampled, GCMT noted 4 payment plan offers (macro) which included the additional wording " If the above payment plan solution does not work for you, please respond directly to this email with an amount that you can afford (Samples in April and May). The remaining 14 samples did not contain this wording or a similar reference. 
 

 Additionally, GCMT noted the following: 
 1 - The payment plan macros documented within the "Banking Operations - Loans Payment Plan and Square Loans - Macro" appear outdated and not reflective of current macros which are being utilized for financial hardship, when comparing their content and wording 
 2 - There is an inconsistency in relation to the information that is provided to the Seller about the payment plan offer i.e. one macro provides the total amount of monthly payments which will be due under the payment plan, whereas the other stays silent.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: RM attached - GCMT agreed process
Remediation Strategy: MAPS provided in RM
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Rated in accordance with the issue management program
Issue Action Plans:
Exploration of self -serve payment plans and review and updating of macros (1023910)
 Compliance will design, approve and embed a consumer protection policy and standard for vulnerable customers (1023912)

Issue Title: Loan Operation Agents adherence to Standard Operating Procedures requires uplift to ensure all eligibility requirements are met, prior to the approval of manual loans.
Issue Description:

 Background:
 GCMT reviewed a sample of 10 loans which were manually generated by the Loans team, for new Square Sellers to confirm if all eligibility requirements documented within the Standard Operating Procedure were met, prior to the loan being approved by the loans agent.

 GCMT identified a 50% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 50% exception rate (5 exceptions) one or more eligibility requirements were not met prior to the loan being approved by the loans agent. A breakdown of these exceptions are noted below by requirement (not sample):

 2 instances where the public search/PPSR documents were not uploaded to Regulator. As a result, GCMT could not verify if the search was conducted and satisfactory prior to approval.

Issue Title: Uplift non-sole proprietor seller’s entity type and verification
Issue Description: Square introduced a KYB onboarding process for non-sole proprietorship sellers that were onboarded via Web in May 2024. KYB Onboarding via App was introduced for Android in September 2025, and iOS in December 2025. Before these dates, all sellers, regardless of their business type, used a single onboarding experience. This experience did not take into consideration the entity type of the seller. At the time that the KYB onboarding process for non-sole props was implemented, there was no plan to actively remediate the pre-KYB population of non-sole prop sellers onboarded prior to the KYB implementation.
RCA Description:
No RCA required. 
 

 Why: When KYB was implemented it was known that any non-sole prop account that was created prior to the implementation would not have been KYB'd based on the new policy.
Remediation Strategy: The purpose of this issue is to create a remediation strategy to addressing the non-sole props that haven't been KYB'd
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: Sellers which opened their accounts prior to KYB being implemented within Square do meet the expected regulatory standard required for non-sole prop entities
Risk Domain: Regulatory | Existing and New Reg Adherence | Regulatory Change Management (1000138)

Issue Title: Square Seller KYC Data Integrity Gaps Across Multiple Products
Issue Description:
PLEASE NOTE: There is no set issue occurrence date or issue identification date - this has been a know issue at SQ as it built out its products. As a result, the date of issue creation was used. 
 

 

 Seller information updates are currently not synchronized across different products.
 

 For example: If a seller updates their address within payments, that change is not passed along to checking.
 

 This problem indicates gaps in KYC data governance, synchronization, and refresh processes, particularly for existing merchants and multiple product relationships. As a result, Block may be relying on incomplete or inaccurate customer information when performing customer risk assessments, ongoing due diligence, and transaction monitoring.
RCA Description: No RCA needed - The expansion of Square's product offerings has resulted in a lack of necessary interconnections between these individual products.
Remediation Strategy: As part of the action plan process there will be a strategy for remediation developed.

Issue Title: Inconsistent definition of "Initial Date of Complaint" may result in breach of ASIC RG 271 30-Day response requirement
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirements: 
 RG 271.56: A financial firm must provide an Internal Dispute Resolution (IDR) response to a complainant no later than 30 calendar days after receiving the complaint. However, in some cases a different timeframe applies
 

 RG 271.71: A financial firm does not need to provide an IDR response to a complainant if the firm closes the complaint by the end of the fifth business day after receipt because the firm has:
 (a) resolved the complaint to the complainant’s satisfaction (see RG 271.73); or
 (b) given the complainant an explanation and/or apology when the firm can take no further action to reasonably address the complaint (see RG 271.74).
 

 Standard / Procedure requirements: 
 Block Global Complaints Program Standard - For email: “The date a Complaint enters the business's email system or inbox. The timestamp of the email will serve as the Received Date.”
 AU Complaints Management Runbook - “The date when the complaint was first reported by the complainant and identified as meeting the definition of a complaint.”
 CS Process Complaints [Global] Standard Operating Procedure - “For email: choose the date the complainant sent the email.”
 

 Issue Description: 
 GCMT noted that there is an inconsistency in the documented definition for the recording of the “initial date of complaint”. Per the procedural requirements documented above, the Block Global Complaints Program Standard and the Global Customer Success Standard Operating Procedure, define the initial date of complaint as the date the email is received by Square. 
 

 In contrast, the AU Complaints Management Runbook defines the “initial date of complaint” as the date when the complaint was first reported by the complainant and has met the definition. 
 Complaint Management have confirmed that the current expectation is for advocates to be recording the "initial date of complaint" as the date the interaction met the complaint definition. 
 

 GCMT conducted testing on a sample of 40 complaint tickets and identified the following instances where the initial date was not recorded as the date the interaction met the complaint definition: 
 - 2 instances where the initial date was recorded per the date the initial enquiry was received; and
 - 3 instances where the initial date was recorded per the date the initial email was received
 

 In the remaining 35 instances, the initial date was recorded as the date the interaction met the complaint definition which was the same day the enquiry was received. 
 

 Consistent and accurate recording of the “initial date of complaint” is required to ensure Square Australia's compliance and monitoring of RG 271.56 and RG 271.71 which requires “a formal IDR response to be sent within 30 calendar days from the date of receipt.” 
 

 In longer conversations, particularly via email, a normal enquiry could escalate into a complaint at a later date. If the complaint date is recorded as when the enquiry was first received rather than when the interaction met the complaint definition, the 30-day IDR response clock would start earlier than required. This creates a risk that Square could inadvertently have less time to respond and miss the 30-day timeframe.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Results Matrix and final report attached - GCMT process
Remediation Strategy: MAPS provided in Results Matrix and final report
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: Rated in accordance with the Issue management program
Issue Action Plans: Review, assess and update definition for the "initial date of complaint" (1024566)

Issue Title: Customer Success and Complaint Management advocate adherence to Standard Operating Procedures for complaint reporting fields can be improved
Issue Description:
Background:
 GCMT conducted testing on a sample of 40 complaint tickets across Tiers 1, 2, 3-Escalated and 3 to verify that all required processes documented within the Complaint Standard Operating Procedures were followed by the Customer Success and/or Complaint Management advocate as part of the complaint handling and resolution.
 

 GCMT identified one key complaint area where the effectiveness rate across all tiers was under 85%.
 Complaint Area #6 – Complaint Reporting Fields 
 

 Procedure Requirement:
 CS Process Complaints [Global] Standard Operating Procedure: 
 Line of Business: Select Customer Success-Frontline from the dropdown menu
 Complaint Intake Channel: Select the channel through which you received the complaint: Choose from Phone, Email or Messaging, based on how the complaint was received
 Product: Choose the product most relevant to the complaint. 
 

 Complaints Management AU Runbook: 
 Closed Date: A closed date can be determined when at least one of the following has occurred or can be observed; 
 The complainant confirms satisfaction with the resolution (verbally or in writing);
 A final IDR response has been provided; 
 No further action is possible and this has been clearly communicated to the complainant (by any Frontline team, or by Complaints Management)
 

 Issue Description
 GCMT identified 12 out of 40 complaints where the complaint fields entered by Customer Success advocate (CSA) or Complaint Manager were incorrect. These consisted of: 
 - 3 instances where the line of business was incorrect;
 - 1 instance where the complaint intake channel was incorrect;
 - 1 instance where the product was incorrect;
 - 7 instances where the closed date was inaccurate or does not reflect the complaint resolution date:
  > In 5 of the 7 instances, the closed date corresponded to the date the Complaint Manager reviewed the complaint and marked it as closed, even though the complaint had already been resolved earlier by the CSA or SAS teams or when the IDR response was provided
  > In the remaining 2 instances, the complaint was recorded as closed despite the complaint still being managed by SAS/CSA with ongoing Seller communication.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Results Matrix and Final Report attached - GCMT process
Remediation Strategy: MAPS provided in Results Matrix and Final Report
Impacted Business Area:
CS Direct, Australia
 CS Direct, Complaints Management
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Rated in accordance with the Issue management program
Issue Action Plans: Feedback on ‘Closed Date’ will be addressed (1024568)

Issue Title: There are some transaction monitoring alerts out of SLA, documentation inconsistencies and case review comments lack evidence consistent with SEL Procedural requirements.
Issue Description:
Issue Description:
 GCMT noted that the Square TM Queue Tracker contains outdated information, including queues which are no longer utilized to monitor TM alerts in the UK i.e. "TRANSACTION LAUNDERING". Additionally, GCMT noted a queue which is currently utilized by Transaction Monitoring but not documented within the Capacity Model Operations Assumptions - October 2025 i.e. "pos_tm_gb". The 'pos_escalations_gb' queue SLA is listed as 30 days in the Capacity Model Operations Assumptions - October 2025 document and 7 days in the Square TM Queue Tracker. 
 

 GCMT identified the following issues in the Square Europe Ltd. (UK) Review Procedure | Square Transaction Monitoring Case Review Procedure:
 The procedure (Pg.3) contains the incorrect link to the Square TM Queue Tracker (see first point above),
 The "Possible Outcomes" Section (Pg. 6) contains an empty link on guidance for clearing alerts. 
 The procedure states "A case comment should be left using Square Europe Ltd. (UK) Reference: Best Practices for Case Comments | Square Transaction Monitoring guide" but this is not done in practice by all agents assessing a Transaction Monitoring case. GCMT confirmed with the Global Compliance Investigations TM and UUP Lead that this is not mandatory. 
 As a result of the procedure not being updated to reflect current requirements, GCMT also noted the documentation of TM alert analysis on Regulator was inconsistent with the procedures (see section Transaction Monitoring Testing of Alerts below for further details). 
 

 Transactional Testing
 SLAs
 GCMT noted here the SLA breaches for the queues below (period Jan 2025 through August 2025):
 → compl_square_gb_tm_capital_prepayment: 3 out of 3,145 alerts closed after the 30 day SLA. These alerts were closed between 1 and 2 days after the alert was generated. 
 → pos_escalations_gb: 12 out of 3,145 alerts closed after the 7 day SLA. These alerts were closed between 1 and 120 days after the alert was generated. 
 → tm_ad_hoc: 3 out of 3,145 alerts closed after the 30 day SLA. These alerts were closed between 57 and 60 days after the alert was generated. 
 

 A limited number of cases were identified as being out of SLA; however, the issue has been raised due to the underlying root cause. GCMT confirmed with management that the breaches resulted from incorrect case assignment. Management has confirmed that corrective actions will be implemented.
 

 Transaction Monitoring Alerts 
 GCMT reviewed 100 Transaction Monitoring (TM) alerts and found that 78 did not meet the full documentation standards outlined in the Square Europe Ltd. (UK) Review Procedure | Square Transaction Monitoring Case Review Procedure and Best Practices for Case Comments (see Page 8). These procedures require agents to conduct a thorough assessment and document their analysis, referencing all applicable areas for the queue.
 

 The procedure states that “A case comment should be left using the Square Europe Ltd. (UK) Reference: Best Practices for Case Comments | Square Transaction Monitoring guide”, however, this practice is not consistently followed by all agents reviewing TM cases. GCMT confirmed with the Global Compliance Investigations TM and UUP Lead that leaving a case comment in accordance with this guide is not currently mandatory.
 

 As the procedure has not been updated to reflect the current requirements, GCMT noted that the documentation of TM alert analysis on Regulator has been inconsistent with the written expectations. In many cases, GCMT could not verify whether agents assessed key elements—such as linked accounts, MCC, and NBF analysis—because this information was not documented on Regulator.
RCA Description: Refer to Results Matrix attached for RCA.
Remediation Strategy: Refer to Results Matrix attached for RCA.

Issue Rating Justification: The issue relates to an internal processes which are not in line with the annual requirement for review and approval of internal policies and procedures.
Risk Domain: Compliance | Financial Crime | Anti-Money Laundering & Terrorist Financing (1000126)
Issue Action Plans: Management will update SLA documentation, conduct transaction monitoring procedure reviews, perform SLA monitoring and training for transaction monitoring agents on revised requirements. (1025233)

Issue Title: Breach of Regulatory Service Level Agreement for Regulation E Disputes
Issue Description:
Cash Apps first line risk and controls team conducted risk and control testing in December of 2024, and issued findings in January of 2025. They issued a major finding related to Cash Apps disputes programs SLA adherence: 
 

 On an as needed basis, Backstop automatically initiates a refund to the customer when the volume of claims surpasses the established capacity, or there are processing delays, to confirm the established regulatory Service Level Agreement (SLA) to complete disputes investigation is not breached.
 

 CTT Reviewed all 84 Business Day 10 items that the Backstop provided refunds for in Q4 2024. CTT tested against if the item provided refund within the proper Reg E Timeframe and if proper communication was sent to the consumer. Disputron provided proper communication on 100% of the items reviewed. However, only 43% of items were refunded within the proper Regulation E timeframe. 
 

 There were common themes within the sample: 
 - Disputes were not being outsorted to the proper queues (file to network, refund, or manual review) after the Step Up verification. 
 - Step Up Verification possibly delyaing the backstop. 
 - Similar NOE days for the items that provided refunds after BD10.
Identifying Party: First Line Risk and Controls Testing Team
RCA Description: First Line of Defense Testing Results from 2-25
Not an Issue Justification: This is not an Issue as the deprecation of the Step Up program which is outlined in this PCR. https://docs.google.com/document/d/1zQjARZhZ9MZXL_qo_DBjg0ai3DdY-N2Nv63Kof4mWKc/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.for3ypnk7dmu

Issue Title: There were some instances of incorrect disposition for repeated identity verification failures, case comments and adding a tag for confirmed adverse media in accordance with the EDD C4B User Guide and the KYC Ops EDD Master Matrix requirements
Issue Description:
Regulation Requirement; Bank Secrecy Act/Anti-Money Laundering Requirements (31 CFR 1020.220) requires verification of customer identity ('IDV'), collection of specific identifying information, and identity verification procedures.
 Procedure Requirements; Per Enhanced Due Diligence ('EDD') C4B User Guide, specialists must verify identity alignment between claimed identity and account control through comprehensive review of IDV information, government-issued ID, selfie, and business verification details against the current account operator. During reviews of alerting accounts, specialists are required to examine the IDV Attempts tab to confirm business owner/control prong verification while reviewing Toolbox for business verification details including EIN and business name validation. All case comments must be clear, organized, accurate, and include all relevant investigative steps with sufficient justification for risk mitigation decisions.
 Per Adverse Media Screening and Review User Guide specialists must apply the Adverse Media compliance tag for all confirmed matches, and include detailed investigative findings supporting the case decision.
 

 Issue Description: 
 During testing of 60 sampled C4B customer accounts, the following exceptions were identified:
 1. Inconsistent escalation and cumulative assessment of repeated identity verification (IDV) failures: Testing identified inconsistent escalation and cumulative assessment of repeated IDV failures across two cases (1 of 31 sole proprietorships and 1 of 11 nonprofit organizations). In both cases, multiple IDV red flags, including repeated failed verification attempts with inconsistent personal information, were initially cleared during separate reviews. Subsequent Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) reviews later determined that the same IDV red flags warranted adverse action, including account termination or denylisting. In one instance, a nonprofit organization account remained active for approximately seven months before denylist action was taken. The delay or lack of escalation of identity verification red flags may indicate weaknesses within the IDV and subsequently affect the KYB program.
 

 2. Case comment quality regarding PEP status: Testing identified one instance where an analyst incorrectly documented a previous PEP-by-association determination, though original PEP tags remained in place. 
 

 3. Adverse Media tag: Testing identified one case where required Tier 2 adverse media tags for confirmed embezzlement activity were not applied to either personal ('Low' risk) or business ('Moderate' risk) accounts, deviating from KYC Ops EDD Master Matrix requirements. This tagging omission impacts customer-level monitoring controls and risk classification accuracy.
 

 The identified exceptions stem from insufficient analyst training and the absence of standardized steps in the current Authenticity Review procedures for evaluating cumulative IDV failure risk across multiple reviews. Therefore there is a need for enhanced analyst guidance on escalation thresholds for repeated IDV failures, as the current procedural framework and quality control mechanisms may not fully support consistent risk assessment across reviews.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: The identified exceptions stem from insufficient analyst training and the absence of standardized steps in the current Authenticity Review procedures for evaluating cumulative IDV failure risk across multiple reviews. Therefore there is a need for enhanced analyst guidance on escalation thresholds for repeated IDV failures, as the current procedural framework and quality control mechanisms may not fully support consistent risk assessment across reviews.
Remediation Strategy: KYC Operations will provide training to analysts and The QA team will formalize its review methodology through implementation of a standardized checklist that incorporates authenticity review components, ensuring consistent evaluation of customer identity verification decisions.
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification:
- No immediate regulatory implications
 - Insignificant operational disruptions
 - Low or no reputational visibility
Risk Domain: Compliance | Financial Crime | Anti-Money Laundering & Terrorist Financing (1000126)
Issue Action Plans:
KYC Operations to provide training to analysts (1028553)
 QA team will formalize a standardized checklist that incorporates authenticity review components (1028556)

Issue Title: Overdraft Autopay from Protect Direct Deposits
Issue Description:
1LoD received a complaint that a customer’s social security benefits were used to pay money owed for an overdrafted balance. This was an error. Typically, certain direct deposits or inflows into Cash App are exempted from being used to autopay down an overdraft charge. 
 

 This occurred because a change in the taxonomy that we use to categorize direct deposits into either protected or not did not include these types of payments. 
 

 See slack thread here: https://sq-block.slack.com/archives/C02MDLQ1SGN/p1767985354758799
RCA Description:
In April 2025, a large backward compatibility change was made to the list that determines what inflows are used to autopay down overdraft amounts. When that change went into effect, certain payment types that should have been exempted from autopay were now able to be used. In this case, that was a disability check. 
 

 See slack thread here: https://sq-block.slack.com/archives/C02MDLQ1SGN/p1767985354758799
Remediation Strategy: Update the taxonomy and confirm that protected direct deposits do not autopay down overdraft. Already completed
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Current understanding is that this is a contained issue and the fix has already been put in place
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: Elderly customers (80+) experience repeated Cash Card suspensions due to inactivity and address mismatch policy gaps
Issue Description:
The Complaints Insights team identified a pattern of complaints from elderly customers, which led to the discovery of this issue. Sutton/Marqeta (third party) has implemented an automated process that disables Cash Cards after 60 days of inactivity for customers aged 80 and older as part of their elder abuse prevention program. The decision to disable the card is based solely on card activity without consideration of other activity in the account. While customers do receive an in-app notification 24 hours in advance of the disabling, many elderly customers report unexpected suspension of their Cash App Cards following extended periods of inactivity, often not realizing their cards are disabled until attempting a transaction. This creates a significant user experience problem for a vulnerable population and may lead customers to perceive they are being treated unfairly or deceptively, particularly when the advance notification is missed or not understood.
 

 The situation is further complicated by a repetitive cycle of restrictions: even after customers complete verification requirements to restore card access, their cards may be re-blocked the next day due to a monthly blocking schedule that runs on the first day of every month. This creates ongoing barriers for elderly customers attempting to access their funds and reinforces perceptions of unfair or deceptive treatment. Additionally, card are disabled when the CIP Address obtained during initial account setup and identity validation differs from the fulfillment address, which may have legitimate explanations for elderly customers who maintain multiple residences—yet the lack of transparency around these blocking criteria may lead customers to believe their accounts are being restricted without valid reason or fair notice.
 

 Together, these issues create substantial friction and frustration for seniors trying to manage their finances through Cash App, while simultaneously damaging trust in the platform and potentially exposing the company to reputational harm and regulatory scrutiny regarding fair treatment of vulnerable populations.
Explanation for Delay in Issue Creation: Complaints Insights discovered this complaint trend and escalated through the IM Team intake. It took a bit of research from the proposed owning team (Issue Owner changed 3 times) and the need for them to connect with external/third party service provide (Marqeta) to dive into the intent of the account restriction process and confirm impacts tied to restricting elderly accounts. After that research was completed and we connected with Compliance and obtained their assessment (regulatory risk), it was determined to be issue worthy on 1/22/26.
RCA Description: Some Cash App Customers aged 80 and older have experienced repeated Cash Card suspensions due to Marqeta's automated 60-day inactivity policy and monthly re-blocking schedule, creating barriers to fund access for a vulnerable population. Despite receiving 24-hour advance notification, many elderly customers are unaware their cards are disabled until attempting a transaction with their card. Additionally, accounts are blocked when CIP and fulfillment addresses differ, which may have legitimate explanations for seniors with multiple residences. These policies were set by the card issuer (Sutton) and implemented by the Program Manager (Marqeta) in an attempt to protect this vulnerable population. These are legacy rules implemented some time ago, likely before Block's ML models and rules matured to their current state. Complaints Insights raised related complaint patterns indicating these rules may be ineffective as they are causing friction and poor customer experience to good customers.
Remediation Strategy: Either sunsetting these rules (as appropriate controls exist at Block) or modifying the rules allowing Block an opportunity to review the full account activity prior to determining if an account suspension is taken vs. no action.
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap potentially impacting a subset of the customer population within one business unit. Potential fines, regulatory scrutiny, reputation risk and loss of customer trust.
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: The 3 day internal Service Level Agreement for the Enhanced Customer Due Diligence processes cannot be accurately measured, monitored and reported due to the absence of systems or other timestamps.
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 The Enhanced & Ongoing Customer Due Diligence Procedure for Afterpay | United States, Published December 2024, requires:
 

 3.4 Service Level Agreement (SLA)
 Afterpay has a commitment to conduct ECDD if warranted and take action or request additional Source of Funds (SoF) / Source of Wealth (SoW) documentation if needed within 3 business days. Afterpay has an additional commitment to review SoF/SoW documentation upon receipt and take additional action or escalate within an additional 3 business days. If it is determined that Senior Management Approval (SMAP) is needed, SMAP will be completed within an additional 5 business days. Accounts will remain frozen until the ECDD review is complete.
 

 Issue Description:
 The current process relies on manually storing documentation outside of the Satori system, which retains only final review documentation without maintaining timestamps of initial customer contacts or interim case activities. This limitation meant that GCMT was unable to independently validate the business's self-reported SLA metrics or verify actual compliance with the required 3-day initial review timeframe. 
 

 During the June–August period temporary resourcing constraints, coverage limitations, and known SLA visibility challenges in Satori were understood and factored into leadership reporting. 
 

 While QA walkthroughs have not identified immediate concerns, the absence of systematic timestamp tracking creates challenges in monitoring and demonstrating compliance with established service level requirements.Due to this GCMT considers this issue to be low risk as it was brought to the attention of management prior to and concurrent with the GCMT review.
RCA Description: NA. GCMT issues are not required to complete an RCA.
Remediation Strategy: NA
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Minimal, localized consequences with negligible impact on overall business operations or reputation. Escalation is unlikely due to effective containment or the isolated nature of the issue.

Issue Title: Pay Monthly Adverse Action Notices (AAN) are not issued for certain declines due to multiple order controls not being configured to send an AAN.
Issue Description:
Requirement: The Equal Credit Opportunity Act requires financial institutions to notify applicants when adverse action is taken on their credit application.
 § 1002.9 Notifications.
 (a) Notification of action taken, ECOA notice, and statement of specific reasons —
 (1) When notification is required. A creditor shall notify an applicant of action taken within:
 (i) 30 days after receiving a completed application concerning the creditor's approval of, counteroffer to, or adverse action on the application;
 (ii) 30 days after taking adverse action on an incomplete application, unless notice is provided in accordance with paragraph (c) of this section;
 (iii) 30 days after taking adverse action on an existing account; or
 (iv) 90 days after notifying the applicant of a counteroffer if the applicant does not expressly accept or use the credit offered.
 Issue Description: 
 GCMT reviewed 50 pay monthly applications for sending of adverse actions notices and identified 4 from 50 applications whereby the credit model declined a Buy Now Pay Later and a Pay Monthly (PM) loan application(s) from the same customer, however due to incorrect mapping, did not send an adverse action notice (AAN) for the Pay Monthly declined application. 
 The root cause stems from the pay later core system's decline mechanism, which prevents multiple orders from being issued within a short timeframe (24 hours). This decline process operates independently of the risk underwriting system and was not configured to generate a PM AAN.
RCA Description: NA
Remediation Strategy: NA
Impacted Business Area: Afterpay Consumer Product, Strategy and Eng
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: Critical consequences that threaten regulatory compliance. The issue has the potential to escalate into significant operational risks if not addressed promptly.
Closure Request Justification:
1) Mapping for the decline main reason is "CL_ORDER_VELOCITY_DECLINE", sub main reason is "TOO_MANY_ORDERS" is mapped it B3 as of 1/29/2026.
 

 2) For ongoing: Afterpay deployed a fix on 1/29/2026 to ensure the decline reason above (in 1) is mapped to B3. This fix will ensure that the declines on 1/28/2026 are included in the AAN deployments that are sent the following morning (1/30/2026).
 -For the missed declines between 1/1/2026 - 1/27/2026 (34,786 total declines), a look back remedition was also completed during the AAN deployment on 1/30/2026 and was completed around 10:20AM PST (34,786 successful AANs sent).
 

 https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1eYEYZMv3qv0AEchjbXQM5Bp8og9GuytqD7yGIhGhCs4/edit?gid=1733750817#gid=1733750817
Issue Internal Validation Justification:
For Afterpay Pay Monthly - the decline main reason is "CL_ORDER_VELOCITY_DECLINE", sub main reason is "TOO_MANY_ORDERS", and is mapped to B3 as of 1/29/2026.
 

 For ongoing: Afterpay deployed a fix on 1/29/2026 to ensure the decline reason above is mapped to B3. This fix will ensure that the declines on 1/28/2026 are included in the AAN deployments that are sent the following morning (1/30/2026).
 

 -For the missed declines between 1/1/2026 - 1/27/2026 (34,786 total declines), a look back remediation was also completed during the AAN deployment on 1/30/2026 and was completed around 10:20AM PST (34,786 successful AANs sent).

Issue Title: C.03 Disputes Operations SLA Report Work Instructions
Issue Description:
On a daily basis, Disputes Operations Leadership reviews a report from Salesforce to monitor regulatory Service Level Agreement (SLA) timelines and to confirm cases are resolved within the established SLA. The review is documented via Google Sheets and Slack.
 

 There is no procedure regarding the way Business Day 10 items are worked. These items are manually worked differently than normal items that are not run up against the Reg E timeframe. For example, there are many items that are just written off on BD10 because of potential breach of SLA whereas if they were worked earlier, they would be investigated normally (filed to network, etc.) This could potentially cause UDAAP concerns as these are being handled differently outside of normal work instructions. Additionally, there is no work instruction for how the report is worked in general (when it's worked, how it's pulled, where it's retained, who is responsible, etc.)
RCA Description:
The Dispute OOSLA Reporting + Notification – Process Guide outlines how Out-of-SLA (OOSLA) warnings and resolutions are handled across Operations and Engineering.
 Operations (OSA) are responsible for acknowledging OOSLA alerts, validating whether alerts are legitimate, taking immediate remediation actions when possible, documenting outcomes, and communicating resolution status to stakeholders.Engineering is responsible for acknowledging OOSLA warnings, investigating root causes (temporary vs. systemic), resolving issues when feasible, and escalating to Operations for manual remediation when system fixes cannot meet regulatory timelines. Engineering also ensures required compliance events and logs are updated after remediation.
 

 This process is captured here and shares how we review and monitor cases that are alerted as approaching sla daily. We have slack channel where this is all captured.

Issue Description:
Policy Requirement: Per Cash App’s Acceptable Use Policy
 (https://cash.app/legal/us/en-us/acceptable-use-policy), activity involving illegal and other related substances and any equipment designed for making or using drugs, such as bongs, vaporizers, and hookahs is not allowed, and any violations may result in immediate account suspension or termination without notice. 
 

 Issue Description: 
 GCMT identified one customer account with a prohibited business activity within the sampled population of 17 small LLC (smLLC) accounts. Specifically, the account was found to be operating a vape business, which is explicitly prohibited under current acceptable use policy guidelines. 
 Customers initiating C4B account applications undergo a business identity verification process requiring mandatory data submission of business identifiers (name, address, and either EIN or SSN) and owner personal identification verification. The verification process initially attempts automated authentication through third-party vendors and state databases; however when automated verification fails ("eIDV Pending" status), accounts require manual review by analysts who validate business legitimacy through direct document examination.
 After an initial application under a numeric identifier failed Secretary of State verification, the business successfully passed automated KYB the following day by modifying its business name, despite maintaining visible vape-related identifiers in cashtags, profile imagery, and business address verification across both P2P and C4B accounts. Existing monitoring controls did not detect prohibited activity indicators in customer cashtags and business address verification against public sources and should be enhanced to address this gap.
 The post-onboarding keyword monitoring system also failed to detect explicit references to tobacco, vape, and marijuana retail operations in transaction memos, exposing a gap in the ongoing monitoring framework. While the account was ultimately denylisted for vape/marijuana adversities upon GCMT's escalation to Operations, the case highlights the need to strengthen controls across the customer lifecycle to prevent prohibited business activities on the platform and to mitigate compliance and reputational risks.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description:
After an initial application under a numeric identifier failed Secretary of State verification, the business successfully passed automated KYB the following day by modifying its business name, despite maintaining visible vape-related identifiers in cashtags, profile imagery, and business address verification across both P2P and C4B accounts. Existing monitoring controls did not detect prohibited activity indicators in customer cashtags and business address verification against public sources and should be enhanced to address this gap.
 The post-onboarding keyword monitoring system also failed to detect explicit references to tobacco, vape, and marijuana retail operations in transaction memos, exposing a gap in the ongoing monitoring framework.
Remediation Strategy: Based on the GCMT coverage assessment, this issue will leverage an existing Red Flag Coverage Assessment MAP Action ID # - 1018365 under Protecht Issue ID 1022549 to enhance detection coverage of Cash App customer behavior.
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification:
Potential fines with manageable implications
 - Operational inefficiencies impacting some 
  processes
 - Localized reputational risk
 - Increasing scrutiny or operational dependencies
Risk Domain: Compliance | Financial Crime | Anti-Money Laundering & Terrorist Financing (1000126)
Issue Progress Updates:
Based on the GCMT coverage assessment, this issue will leverage an existing Red Flag Coverage Assessment MAP Action ID # - 1018365 under Protecht Issue ID 1022549 to enhance detection coverage of Cash App customer behavior.
 Milestone 2 - This milestone addresses queue design and coverage gaps by utilizing comprehensive assessment results to develop enhanced rules that improve detection capabilities. It directly resolves the inconsistent alerting issues and comprehensive coverage of customer behavior patterns across risk levels. Target Date: 06/30/26
 The action owner has confirmed that any additional modifications identified through the GCMT finding will be incorporated into the scope of the ongoing assessment and review for the MAP.

Issue Title: Payment file NZ_afterpay_payment_events_2025-08-19T052129.csv processed twice on 19/08/2025
Issue Description:
A temporary network outage of indeterminate origin occurred around 19/08/2025 which impacted the processing of a payment file provided by Afterpay. This was due to a control record that should have been acknowledged as part of the payment process being bypassed, which resulted in the payment file NZ_afterpay_payment_events_2025-08-19T052129.csv being processed to the Collection House C5 system twice. The outcome was that 1187 payments across 183 accounts were allocated twice, resulting in the balance of those accounts being inaccurate. The issue was identified on 08/12/2025 as a result of an end of month QA check completed by Afterpay.
 Affected 183 accounts of which: 118 accounts are $00.00 (Of these CH had a balance owing on 55), 49 accounts match balance and 16 accounts need balance adjustment.
RCA Description:
A temporary network outage of indeterminate origin occurred around 19/08/2025 which impacted the processing of a payment file provided by Afterpay. This was due to a control record that should have been acknowledged as part of the payment process being bypassed, which resulted in the payment file NZ_afterpay_payment_events_2025-08-19T052129.csv being processed to the Collection House C5 system twice. The outcome was that 1187 payments across 183 accounts were allocated twice, resulting in the balance of those accounts being inaccurate. The issue was identified on 08/12/2025 as a result of an end of month QA check completed by Afterpay.

Remediation Strategy: 09/12/2025 – review of the account data to identify discrepancies. Information provided to client for review. 11/12/2025 – HEAT ticket raised to have the discrepancies corrected; $00.00 balance account closed and balance adjustments completed. 12/12/2025 – Confirmation of the HEAT ticket being completed. 28/12/2025 – Review of C5 payment processing controls, specifically preventative controls for payment duplication 02/02/2026 – Work order raised for the below, with an ETA 27/02/2026. • Technical preventative control to be developed for the Afterpay payment file, leveraging the unique payment identifier contained within that file. • Automated detective control to identify duplicated payments in C5 on a daily basis should the preventative control fail.

Issue Title: There are no controls in place to verify Non-Profit Organizations that are misclassified as Sole Proprietors at onboarding in order to complete ongoing/enhanced due diligence per FFIEC CDD Ongoing Due Diligence guidance and Know your Business Policy
Issue Description:
Regulatory Guidance; FFIEC BSA/AML Examination Manual - Core Requirements for CDD Ongoing Due Diligence and Account Maintenance. Financial institutions must develop procedures for maintaining and updating customer information on a risk basis. Specifically for legal entity changes, when a financial institution becomes aware of changes in ownership, business structure, or activity of a legal entity customer, it should assess the impact of the changes on the customer risk profile and whether additional due diligence is required. Changes to business structure through updated formation documents should trigger a review and update of customer information.
 Policy Requirement; Per Know Your Business (KYB) policy, Non-Profit Organizations require enhanced verification including EIN validation, control prong verification identifying a responsible individual, and are restricted to U.S.-based activities within specific MCCs (e.g., charities/churches), with mandatory Enhanced Due Diligence and ongoing monitoring due to their high-risk classification.
 

 Issue Description: 
 During testing of 31 sole proprietorship accounts, GCMT identified two entities operating as religious non-profit organizations (NPOs) that were incorrectly classified as sole proprietorships, evidencing a control gap in business entity verification. 
 For the two entities reviewed, GCMT observed indicators consistent with nonprofit religious organization activity, including business naming conventions (e.g., use of terms such as “church”), cashtag identifiers (e.g., references to “apostle”), and transaction patterns indicative of religious donations. Despite these indicators, the accounts were classified as sole proprietorships and maintained lower risk ratings under business consulting classifications
 Due to their misclassification, these accounts bypassed NPO-specific controls including beneficial ownership attestation and automatic high-risk classification, instead following the simplified sole proprietorship verification path. 
 The root cause stems from onboarding design decisions that rely on customer MCC self-classification and lack of system enforced controls to validate, correct, or reclassify entity type. 
 

 Impact:
 The absence of system-enforced validation controls during and post- onboarding creates a control weakness where NPOs can bypass mandatory EDD measures by incorrectly onboarding as sole proprietorships, directly deviating from FFIEC regulatory requirements to independently verify and understand the nature and purpose of customer relationships, and EDD Guidelines for high-risk entities. KYC Operations confirmed no automated controls exist to denylist accounts or retroactively correct entity classification, KYB flow, or MCC assignments post-onboarding. The reliance on manual intervention and analyst discretion, without automated controls, creates inconsistent risk assessment and monitoring protocols and EDD review timing, potentially understating true customer risk.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: The root cause stems from onboarding design decisions that rely on customer MCC self-classification and lack of system enforced controls to validate, correct, or reclassify entity type.
Remediation Strategy: The C4B Product team will address this issue as part of the KYB Refresh implementation planned for this year. The team will develop a plan through that program and review it in partnership with the ML team.
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification:
- Potential fines with manageable implications
 - Operational inefficiencies impacting some
 processes
 - Localized reputational risk
 - Increasing scrutiny or operational dependencies
Risk Domain: Compliance | Financial Crime | Anti-Money Laundering & Terrorist Financing (1000126)
Issue Action Plans: KYB Refresh implementation (1029867)

Issue Title: Cash For Business Customers onboarded after Q4 2023 are completing BTC on-chain and Lightning Network transfers to or from external wallets which is not permitted by the Cash for Business AML CFT and Sanctions Product Risk Assessments
Issue Description:
Requirement: 
 The Cash for Business AML CFT and Sanctions Product Risk Assessment identifies direct deposit and disbursement to third party virtual currency accounts or wallets as technically possible for legacy customers and indicates that this functionality is not permitted for customers onboarded after Q4 2023.
 

 Issue Description:
 GCMT identified verified KYB C4B business accounts that were onboarded after Q4 2023 and were able to conduct, and remain eligible for, BTC transactions involving direct deposit to and disbursement from external wallets during the review period from April 1 through October 1, 2025.
 For active C4B accounts during the review period, data analytics results indicate that ~45k customers conducted successful BTC on chain and Lightning Network transfers to or from external wallets. Further manual review showed: 
 - In one sample, the BTC deposit and withdrawal service appeared ineligible, while BTC on chain transactions continued to proceed.
 - In three additional samples, BTC P2P send and receive operations were ineligible, while other BTC transaction types remained eligible.
 This observed behavior is inconsistent with the Cash for Business Product Risk Assessment, which limits such functionality to legacy customers only. The results indicate a control enforcement gap in customer eligibility logic and expose the product to higher risk transaction flows beyond its assessed risk appetite.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: The results indicate a control enforcement gap in customer eligibility logic and expose the product to higher risk transaction flows beyond its assessed risk appetite.
Remediation Strategy: PCM will coordinate with product eng to disable BTC on-chain and lightning network deposits/withdrawal functionality for C4B accounts onboarded after Q4 2023.
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification:
- Potential fines with manageable implications
 - Operational inefficiencies impacting some
 processes
 - Localized reputational risk
 - Increasing scrutiny or operational dependencies
