Issue Title: Unverified C4B accounts continued to allow transactions
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement; Bank Secrecy Act and its implementing regulations, including 31 C.F.R. § 1022.210(d)(1)(iv)(A). Money services businesses are required to establish and maintain AML policies, procedures, and internal controls that provide for verification of customer identification. This includes the collection of specific identifying information and the application of identity verification procedures designed to reasonably ensure that the true identity of customers is known and validated, consistent with BSA and AML requirements.
 Policy Requirement; In accordance with the KYB standard for Cash for Business Customers, all business accounts are required to complete KYB verification prior to transacting, including the collection and validation of required business information such as legal name, address, and MCC and enforcement of approved transaction types.
 

 Issue Description: 
 GCMT identified Cash for Business accounts that are tagged as business accounts but remain KYB unverified and continue to transact on the platform. The population was identified using reasonable exclusion criteria to isolate accounts that should have been restricted under KYB remediation design.
 Specifically, testing focused on accounts that:
 - Conducted business activity during the review period from April 1 through October 1, 2025
 - Remained in an UNVERIFIED KYB status, which should trigger eligibility restrictions per KYB design documentation
 - Were active and not closed or denylisted
 To avoid overstating the issue, GCMT excluded transaction types that may be permitted for unverified accounts or are not customer-initiated, including:
 - Stock dividend payments
 - Stock corporate actions
 - Stock sell transactions permitted solely for liquidation of existing holdings
 

 Despite these exclusions, GCMT identified 357 accounts that continued to process customer-initiated transactions while remaining KYB unverified. The majority of observed activity involved BTC transfers to or from external wallets, with a smaller volume of direct deposit activity. The continued activity of these unverified business accounts indicates a control gap in the enforcement of KYB eligibility restrictions and exposes the program to regulatory and AML risk beyond its stated risk appetite.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: The continued activity of these unverified business accounts indicates a control gap in the enforcement of KYB eligibility restrictions and exposes the program to regulatory and AML risk beyond its stated risk appetite.
Remediation Strategy: C4B Product will work with the Bitcoin and Direct Deposit teams to further scope and implement controls that prevent business customers with unverified KYB status from transacting.
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification:
- Potential fines with manageable implications
 - Operational inefficiencies impacting some
 processes
 - Localized reputational risk
 - Increasing scrutiny or operational dependencies
Risk Domain: Compliance | Financial Crime | Anti-Money Laundering & Terrorist Financing (1000126)

Issue Title: Square Payroll: Expense Reimbursements (Tax Treatment) and Bonuses (Impact on Overtime Calculation)
Issue Description:
Expense Reimbursements (Tax Treatment)
 Square Payroll does not currently apply payroll taxation to expense reimbursements. Under IRS guidelines, expense reimbursements that fall under a non-accountable plan are considered taxable compensation when they do not meet the requirements for non-taxable reimbursement. These payments must be treated as wages and are required to be included in employees’ gross income, subject to applicable payroll taxes, and reported on Form W-2.
 

 Non-accountable plan expenses include payments such as flat-fee or allowance-based reimbursements where employees are not required to substantiate expenses with receipts or return excess amounts (e.g., a fixed $200 travel allowance). Failure to properly classify and tax these payments may result in non-compliance with federal and state tax regulations.
 

 Bonuses (Impact on Overtime Calculation)
 Square Payroll does not currently include bonus payments in the calculation of the overtime (OT) rate of pay. Under federal and state wage and hour regulations, nondiscretionary bonuses, generally those tied to employee performance or predefined criteria, must be included in the employee’s regular rate of pay when calculating overtime compensation for the period to which the bonus applies.
 

 Common examples of nondiscretionary bonuses include production bonuses, hiring bonuses, and attendance bonuses. Excluding these amounts from the regular rate of pay may result in underpayment of overtime wages and noncompliance with applicable wage and hour laws, creating regulatory, financial, and legal risk for Square and its sellers.
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: Frontline BPO Advocate (Voice and Messaging) tool box permission concerns.
Issue Description: During an investigation into an incorrectly actioned high dollar out of policy Peer to Peer (P2P) payment reversal by a frontline Voice advocate, it was discovered that a permission had been incorrectly assigned to every frontline BPO advocate in Toolbox. This permission issue is separate from but related to the initial concern of the advocate not following the correct workflow for this case which is described in several Frontline Risks (R.01, R.04, R.07, R.08). Several detective controls are in place to identify such actions: (C.01, C.06), however role based permission assignment is a preventative control, hence the concern of a potential breakdown and the need to perform an RCA to determine if this is a symptom of a bigger problem.
RCA Description:
Primary Root Cause:
 Inadequate or absent governance controls for validation the BPO role permissions align with intended advocate workflows and business requirements.
 Supporting evidence:
 The “Franklin_force_refund_payment” permission was present in the “GT Cash Advocate” role without business justification for Frontline awareness.
 7,762 BPO users had access to a high risk permission that was not part of their defined workflow.
  No viability exists into when, why or through what process this permission was added.
 Connection to the Issue:
 The lack of systematic permission validation allowed a misaligned permission to persist in a production role, enabling an unauthorized $3,000 P2P payment reversal.
Remediation Strategy:
Key Objectives
 Immediate containment - remove unauthorized access and address the individual case.
 Short term fix - Eliminate the permission gap that enabled the issue
 Long term prevention - Establish governance controls to prevent future occurrence.
 Major Milestones
 Immediate (Completed)
 Advocate removed from account by BPO partner
 “Franklin_force_refund_payment” permission removed from the “GT Cash Advocate” role in registry.
 Short Term (Suggested)
 Audit all permissions within the “GT Cash Advocate” role to identify any other misaligned permissions.
 Review other BPO roles for similar permission misalignments affecting high risk customer facing actions.
 Long term (Suggested)
 Establish a governance process requiring validation of BPO role permissions against business defined workflows prior to implementation or modification.
 Define ownership and accountability for periodic review of BPO role permissions.
 Implement visibility mechanism (e.g. change notifications, audit log) so business stakeholders can monitor permission changes to roles they own.
 Success Criteria
 No BPO roles contain permissions that are not explicitly approved for advocate workflows.
 All future permission changes to customer facing BPO roles are validated by the relevant business area before deployment.
 A recurring review cadence is in place to catch permission drift over time.
Impacted Business Area:
Cash Customer Operations
 Data - Identity & Access Management DS & Analytics
Not an Issue Justification: The issue has been tracked through the info sec risk register which has a quarterly reporting cadence so we did not need to open a separate issue.
Issue Type: Gap – a needed process or control is missing entirely.
Issue Rating Justification: A single Cash App Frontline customer service advocate (Within a BPO) processed an unauthorized $3,000 P2P payment reversal. Subsequent investigation revealed that this was not an isolated access issue - the “GT Cash Advocate” role, used by 7,762 BPO Advocates, systematically included the “Franklin_force_refund_payment” permission. This permission has no dollar ceiling, meaning all Frontline Advocates had the ability to force refund P2P payments of any amount without appropriate controls.

Issue Title: Enhance existing process and procedures for First Line of Defense Testing Program
Issue Description: A process gap was identified through the review of evidence for a CFPB Consent Order requirement where the FLOD testing team conducted testing in Jan/Feb 2025 and issued 4 findings (2 with moderate impact ratings and 2 with major impact ratings). There was no documentation of a response from management to these findings and the procedure in place assigned responsibility to the control owner to consider the filing of an Issue. The deficient process, including documentation of responses and the inclusion of a Risk Manager and/or Issue Management team for Issue intake led to these items not being properly addressed. Evidence of this process gap has been discussed with Internal Audit through the validation of the CFPB requirement.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Deficient procedures in first line testing, and related documentation, for dispositioning first line testing results led to this issue.
Remediation Strategy:
1. Enhance the first line testing procedures
 2. Conduct testing and provide documentation in alignment with new procedures.
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: This Issue is tied to a regulatory requirement from the CFPB consent order

Issue Title: #1 Documented rationale for discounting sanctions alerts was inadequate and inconsistent with procedures
Issue Description:
The Clearpay UK LexisNexis Bridger alert
 investigation Procedure sets forth requirements
 for documenting rationale for discounting
 sanctions alerts.
 

 IA performed sample testing on 25 name
 screening alerts (consisting of 13 Consumers and
 12 Merchants) and identified the following
 instances in which the documented rationale for
 discounting sanctions alerts was not adequate
 and/or consistent with the procedure:
 

 1) Review of all potential matches was not
 documented:
 IA observed that for 7 out of 25 alert IDs
 reviewed, where each alert ID may contain
 multiple fuzzy matches
 

 11 against sanctions lists,
 the analyst had not documented whether they
 discounted each of the 7 fuzzy matches.
 Therefore, IA could not determine if the analyst’s
 rationale for dispositioning these alert IDs,
 considering all associated matches, was
 appropriate.
 

 2) Alerts were inappropriately discounted as
 false positives due to insufficient information:
 IA noted that for 2 out of 25 alerts, which had a
 100% threshold score match on the forename
 and surname with the consumer, the alerts were
 discounted with comments stating: "Discounted
 in line with Policy due to insufficient information
 to confirm or negate a match." For these alerts,
 the discounting rationale lacked details of the
 checks undertaken to confirm that the customer
 was not the sanctioned subject. Therefore, IA
 cannot determine whether the analyst
 appropriately investigated and dispositioned
 these alert IDs. This is contrary to the Clearpay
 UK LexisNexis Bridger Alert Investigation
 Procedure, which requires that audit notes
 provide an explanation of how the investigator
 has come to their decision.
 

 3) Absence of evidence of date of birth (‘DOB’)
 / Year of Birth (‘YOB’) consideration:
 IA noted that for 7 out of 25 alert IDs, the analyst
 did not indicate in the audit notes (completed
 by Compliance and maintained in Lexis Nexis)
 whether a DOB/YOB was presented for the
 subject (subject is the sanctioned person).
 Therefore, IA cannot determine if the analyst
 considered this DOB/YOB data point when
 dispositioning these alerts.
 

 Failure to follow procedures for discounting
 sanctions alerts may result in instances in which
 Clearpay UK fails to identify sanctions violations.
 Additionally, there is a potential risk of non
 compliance with regulatory requirements and
 expectations, to maintain adequate records and
 audit trail demonstrating that sanctions alerts
 were appropriately reviewed and discounted.
 

 The MLRs and JMLSG guidance indicate the
 importance of documenting records of
 compliance with legal and regulatory
 obligations, including documenting review and
 investigation of alerts and rationale for
 disposition of alerts. The Money Laundering,
 Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds
 Regulations 2017, as amended by The Money
 Laundering and Terrorist Financing Regulations
 2019, 2020 and 2022 (“MLRs”), Section 40, FCA
 Handbook (SYSC) Section 3.2.20 R and 6.3.1 R,
 Joint Money Laundering Steering Group
 Guidance (JMLSG) 2023, Part I Section 8.4, Joint
 Money Laundering Steering Group Guidance
 (JMLSG) 2023, Part III Section 4.94 and 4.97.
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: #2 Absence of documented procedures for CRR methodology change management
Issue Description:
IA conducted a design review of existing policies
 and procedures and identified that Clearpay UK
 has not established documented procedures to
 manage changes to its CRR methodology.
 Specifically, Clearpay UK has not established:
 ● Clear triggers for initiating changes to its
 CRR methodology; and
 ● Defined criteria and processes for
 assessing the impact of CRR
 methodology changes on the existing
 customer base.
 Furthermore, there is no defined timeframe or
 process (independent of customer trigger
 events or scheduled periodic reviews) to
 remediate and apply additional risk mitigation
 measures (such as additional or enhanced due
 diligence) to customers following a CRR
 methodology update. As such, there is a risk that
 existing consumers/merchants remain on
 incorrect risk ratings following methodology
 updates, resulting in insufficient due diligence
 and a distorted view of overall risk exposure. For
 example, low risk customers that are elevated to
 a higher risk rating due to changes in the CRR
 methodology would remain at their outdated
 low risk rating and due diligence until their next
 five-year periodic review due date, and would
 not be subjected to enhanced due diligence
 until the five-year periodic review due date.
 

 JMLSG guidance emphasises that risk
 management is a continuous, dynamic process
 and that AML/CFT/CPF risk assessments are not
 one‐off exercises; firms must keep their risk
 management processes under regular review
 (Part I, 4.78)
 12
 . It also requires firms to apply CDD
 measures to existing customers at appropriate
 times on a risk‐sensitive basis.
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: #3 Lack of documented ML/TF risk appetite statement
Issue Description:
Clearpay UK lacks a formally documented
 standalone risk appetite statement to support
 the firm wide ML/TF risk assessment.
 

 The JMLSG expects that a firm’s risk appetite
 statement is determined and documented by
 Senior Management in order to aid the firm in
 effectively managing and mitigating threats and
 risks commensurate with the firm’s risk appetite.
 

 While we understand UK MLRs and JMLSG
 guidance do not provide guidance on how the
 risk appetite statement should be developed
 and documented, IA notes that industry practice
 is that risk appetite statements are documented
 and include defined risk metrics (such as Key
 Risk Indicators and Key Performance Indicators)
 and qualitative and quantitative risk tolerance
 thresholds.
 

 As a result of the lack of a documented risk
 appetite statement, there is a gap in the
 framework required for risk-based decision
 making and determining appropriate AML/CTF
 systems and controls in alignment with Clearpay
 UK’s risk tolerance appetite. Without a defined
 risk appetite, Clearpay UK may fail to adopt the
 most appropriate and relevant controls and
 measures to manage and mitigate AML/CTF
 risks and fail to comply with regulatory
 expectations.
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: #4 Ineffective data extraction and validation processes for sanctions alert data provided to IA
Issue Description:
As part of IA’s operational effectiveness testing
 over sanctions, IA requested the population of
 merchant sanctions screening alerts for the
 audit covered period from a third party system
 (LexisNexis Bridger). IA noted that the Data team
 required four (4) attempts over a 23-day period
 to retrieve the complete population of merchant
 sanctions screening alerts and needed to
 manually compose and sanitise the data (e.g.,
 manually filter) prior to its availability for IA's
 review on 27 November 2025.
 

 The following timeline details the errors
 identified across the four extraction attempts:
 

 ● 04 November 2025: An incomplete
 population of merchant sanctions alerts
 was initially provided to IA.
 ● 12 November 2025: The second
 population provided to IA was
 over-inclusive, containing sanctions,
 politically exposed persons (PEPs), and
 adverse media alerts, despite IA
 specifically requesting only sanctions
 alerts.
 ● 25 November 2025: The third population
 was inaccurate as it contained duplicate
 alerts.
 ● 27 November 2025: The fourth verified
 dataset was produced following manual
 composition and sanitisation by the Data
 Team.
 

 The absence of effective manual composition
 controls and sanitisation and validation of raw
 data provided to IA increases the likelihood that
 future internal and external reviews may use
 incomplete, irrelevant, or duplicate alert
 populations, resulting in challenges in drawing
 accurate conclusions and reducing confidence
 in audit results and sanctions compliance
 oversight.
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: #5 Lack of robust oversight and governance of self-identified issues with respect to the AML/CTF program
Issue Description:
Clearpay UK lacks a robust process for the
 oversight, governance and tracking of AML/CTF
 self-identified issues and their remediation.
 

 IA noted that Clearpay UK tracks AML/CTF/CPF
 and Sanctions self-identified issues and actions
 taken in a document known as the Clearpay
 Issues Log, and internal audit issues in Protecht.
 IA noted that there were seven self-identified
 issues tracked in the Clearpay Issues Log, and
 these were tracked without deadlines/targeted

 As a result, there is a risk that self-identified
 deficiencies may not be remediated in a timely
 manner, remediation activities are not fully or
 completely remediated, potentially leading to
 recurring control failures and regulatory
 non-compliance.
 

 The MLR and the JMLSG expects that
 effectiveness of systems and controls within
 Clearpay UK are driven by a combination of
 features such as having appropriate monitoring
 processes, with timely follow up of findings.

Issue Title: #6 Additional outsourcing requirements
Issue Description:
IA noted that Clearpay UK maintains the
 ‘Block_Clearpay AML_CTF_Sanctions
 Outsourcing Charter v1.1_Clearpay UK’ to ensure
 oversight of the outsourced services performed
 by Block Inc. However, this document does not
 include requirements for the following and is

 ● Assessing the AML/CTF risk associated
 with the outsourced functions, recording
 the assessment, and monitoring the risk
 on an ongoing basis
 ● Defining and ensuring records needed to
 comply with the MLRs are retained for
 the required period in (or retrievable
 from) the UK while having regard for UK

 information in line with UK data
 protection requirements
 The JMLSG expects that firms actively manage
 the additional risks introduced by outsourcing
 and obtain assurance that outsourcing providers
 meet the standards set out in the JMLSG
 guidance.
 

 As a result, there is a risk that Clearpay UK fails to
 perform adequate due diligence and oversight
 of vendors providing AML/CTF and Sanctions
 services, putting Clearpay UK at risk of
 operational challenges, data security
 vulnerabilities, and legal and regulatory risks.
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: #7 Lack of defined and documented AML/CTF and Sanctions Programme Management Information (MI) integrity procedures
Issue Description:
IA observed the MLRO prepares the reports
 submitted to the Clearpay UK Board of Directors
 and the Leadership team, however, Clearpay UK
 does not have defined and documented

 is:
 

 ● Consistently and accurately extracted
 from relevant systems and tools;
 ● Reconciled for completeness and
 accuracy; and
 ● Reported to Senior Management and the
 Board at an appropriate level of detail.
 

 As a result, there is a risk that this limits Clearpay
 UK’s ability to evidence that the MLRO provides

 to perform or discharge their responsibilities in

 Further, without proper controls over MI

 Management may receive inadequate,

Issue Title: Gaps Noted in the Adequacy of Quality Assurance Program Coverage and Execution Which Increase Risk of Untimely SAR/Sanction Amendments
Issue Description:
Block's internal policies require Quality Assurance (QA) reviews to assess compliance with AML and sanctions obligations, including defined timeframes for review completion and remediation. IA identified gaps in the design and execution of QA procedures across both Cash App and Square platforms affecting multiple AML processes. Specifically, IA identified:
 

 Cash App TM/SAR QA Remediation Delays: From 25 TM/SAR QA samples tested, 5 reviews were remediated beyond the required 30-day timeframe, with the longest taking 73 days.
 Cash App Sanctions QA Review Delays: In the total population during the covered period, 687 out of 1,572 sanctions QA reviews (43.7%) were not completed within the 30-day SLA.
 Square QA SLA Gaps: The Square Quality Assurance team managing TM, SARs, KYC, and sanctions QA queues lacked defined SLAs for QA review completion and remediation during the covered period.
 Cash App Financial Crimes Quality Assurance procedure for TM and SARs does not specify the SLA for conducting QA reviews and the SLA for resolving identified failures.
 Sanctions QA Coverage Gap: QA processes only occur at Level 1 and Level 2 sanctions reviews, with no QA procedures for Level 3 filing decisions, creating a gap in oversight for potential regulatory filing determinations.
 

 As a result, these QA gaps increase the risk of untimely SAR corrections or amendments, untimely identification of required reports to OFAC and associated actions, delayed identification and remediation of compliance issues, inconsistent application of QA standards, and incorrect regulatory filings. This exposes Block to regulatory fines, penalties, and reputational harm due to potential non-compliance with AML and Sanctions obligations.
RCA Description: Gaps in TM/SAR/Sanctions SLA process.
Remediation Strategy: See agreed upon Management Action Plan(s) in final report.
Impacted Legal Entities:
Block, Inc.
 Square Capital LLC
 Square Payroll Inc.

Process and/or control gaps that may result in:

Risk Domain: Compliance | Financial Crime | Anti-Money Laundering & Terrorist Financing (1000126)

Issue Title: Elder Financial Abuse reporting to state Adult Protective Services agencies are not being filed.
Issue Description: In November 2024, SIU identified that Block is unable to meet its Adult Protective Services (APS) reporting obligations for Elder Financial Exploitation (EFE) cases at scale. Contributing factors include state specific requirements, decentralized reporting mechanisms, manually intensive process and high rejection rates. In February 2025 a decision was made to pause reporting, re-examine the entire process and request additional guidance on the legal requirements. Internal and external counsel provided a State Survey identifying jurisdictions that have mandatory reporting requirements and applicability to each Block U.S. entity. Internal guidance to tighten the definition of EFE has been created to reduce over-reporting. Additional work is still required to clarify scope, define Block’s reporting approach, establish requirements and determine resourcing approach.
RCA Description: Lack of capacity to meet increasing volume. Contributing factors include over-reporting (all U.S. jurisdictions rather than only mandatory reporting jurisdictions, as well as overly broad guidance on what constitutes EFE).
Remediation Strategy: Clarify scope, define Block’s reporting approach, establish requirements and determine resourcing approach.
Issue Type: Gap – a needed process or control is missing entirely.
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap related to a subset of the customer population across multiple business units in one geography. No financial impact to customers. Potential fines and regulatory scrutiny.
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

However, if a user is required to enter the CIP/IDV flow and is declined, most likely due to IDV discrepancies requiring an adverse action notice (AAN) to be delivered, that user does not receive E-sign consent. This currently impacts Retro, but previously was an issue for Borrow (Protecht issue 1283863)
Explanation for Delay in Issue Creation: Entry form for issue was created at the time of incident discovery. However, due to shifting changes in departmental focus, the issue was not entered into the system.

 - Cash App currently provides E-sign language at account opening, however, Reg Counsel feels uncomfortable including 3rd party language, specific to the Borrow and Retro products, in that documentation.
 - The E-sign agreement offered for the impacted products should be sufficient for this requirement.
 - If a client abandons the loan flow, no AAN is required. As such, there is no concern about clients not receiving E-sign confirmation.

Issue Title: Training Program Control Gaps - Documentation, Consistency, and Completion Tracking
Issue Description:
The Learning & Development (L&D) program lacks formalized governance, program standards, and controls for training delivery across the organization.
 

 What was identified: Multiple gaps in training program consistency, content accuracy, and completion tracking. Training content varies significantly across brands (Cash App, Afterpay, Square) with inconsistent language and intent. Historical training records are not retained in an orderly fashion outside the LMS, forcing reliance on informal evidence (calendar screenshots). There is no mechanism to ensure training content aligns with established policies and procedures, and no system to track or enforce 100% completion rates across operational teams.
 

 When it occurred: Ongoing issue; identified during testing of complaints and appeals processes, with observations noted across multiple operational areas.
 

 Initial observations: (1) Operations leads are driving training content development rather than L&D, leading to inconsistent definitions and language; (2) L&D lacks a program-level governing document to ensure consistency; (3) Training content is not consistently aligned with source of truth documentation (policies and procedures); (4) No formal retention system for training evidence; (5) Completion rates and proficiency levels are not tracked or enforced; (6) Feedback on training is incorporated inconsistently across initiatives.
RCA Description:
Process Gap - No formal program standard or governance framework for L&D training across the organization
 People/Communication Gap - Operations leaders developing training content without L&D oversight or alignment with established policies
 Documentation Gap - Lack of formalized retention system for training evidence and records
Remediation Strategy:
Revise program standard to cover: consistency, completion rates, proficiency levels
 Implement mandatory annual training on L&D program standard for all operational teams
 Establish content quality assurance mechanism (audit materials for business alignment)
 Implement tracking system for 100% completion (using AirTable repository)
 Align training content with source of truth documentation (policies/procedures)
 Target closure: End of October (Q3 2026)
Impacted Countries:
Australia
 Bermuda

 France
 Ireland
 Japan
 Mexico
 Netherlands
 New Zealand
 Spain
 United Kingdom
 United States

Issue Title: BTC Continuous Monitoring Data Processing Failure (1a)
Issue Description: Block’s Bitcoin continuous monitoring system identifies post transaction risk signals associated with third party wallets and generates alerts for investigation. Certain data from our blockchain analytics vendor, Chainalysis, was not properly ingested into Block’s Bitcoin continuous monitoring system from approximately August 2023 until December 2025.
RCA Description:
The investigation into the missing rows concluded that the table has been dropping rows for the following reasons:
 Rolling look back window: The ETL for app_compliance.cash.blockchain_chainalysis _cm_kyt_alerts contained a join to the app_compliance.cash.banklin_txns_daily_pull table, which has a limited lookback period for included transactions. The lookback was originally a rolling 24 months (starting February 2022) but was changed to a rolling 18 months in late June 2023. As a result, any transaction that triggered an alert but fell outside this rolling lookback window was dropped from the final table feeding into alerting.
 

 Data from Chainalysis is joined with internal transaction data using the blockchain transaction ID / transaction hash, which corresponds to the reference column in the app_compliance.cash.transactions and downstream app_compliance.cash.banklin_txns_daily_pull tables. This join failed when the reference column was null or did not match for the following 2 categories:
 Deposit Reversals: When a deposit is refused by Cash App (subsequently reversed), it involves two blockchain transaction IDs (the original deposit and the customer's withdrawal to move the BTC). Only the original deposit blockchain ID is recorded in the transactions table. Since Chainalysis is sent both blockchain transactions IDs for continuous monitoring, the row corresponding to the withdrawal ID was dropped during the join.
 The app_compliance.cash.banklin_txns_daily_pull table contains only transactions that have a settled_at date (corresponding to completed transactions). For reversed transactions, the settled_at date is null. Consequently, when Chainalysis flagged the original deposit address (even though the transaction was reversed), the data did not populate the blockchain_chainalysis_cm_kyt_alerts table.
Remediation Strategy: Three workstreams were stood up: Technical Response, Remediation Strategy, and Regulatory Response, which manage the work through daily huddles. These workstreams have been tracking progress, removing blockers, and ensuring alignment across all teams. We aim to complete alert remediation using a timely, risk-based approach, while implementing enhanced controls to prevent future occurrences.
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Missing alerting related to severity categories from severe, high and moderate activity

Issue Title: BTC Continuous Monitoring Transaction Reporting Failure (1b)
Issue Description: As part of the investigation into missing data rows related to Chainalysis Continuous Monitoring alerts, the Compliance Engineering team identified that, in addition to the ETL processing behavior described in BTC Continuous Monitoring Data Processing Failure (1a), an upstream Product Engineering change implemented on November 11, 2025 resulted in Bitcoin withdrawal transactions processed after that date not generating Chainalysis Continuous Monitoring alerts in our system.
RCA Description: Compliance Engineering identified that upstream processing changes for Bitcoin withdrawals removed critical data fields from a source dataset, which subsequently impacted downstream compliance monitoring of Chainalysis Continuous Monitoring alerts.
Remediation Strategy: Three workstreams were stood up: Technical Response, Remediation Strategy, and Regulatory Response, which manage the work through daily huddles. These workstreams have been tracking progress, removing blockers, and ensuring alignment across all teams. We aim to complete alert remediation using a timely, risk-based approach, while implementing enhanced controls to prevent future occurrences.
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Missing alerting related to severity categories from severe, high and moderate activity

Issue Title: Missing P2P Payment Initiator Notes (#2)
Issue Description: A table inadvertently missed pulling in the initiator_notes field for some transactions, which has led to a minor gap in the alerting of two queues.
RCA Description: The core problem stemmed from the incremental ETL process used to populate the table. The process only pulled a limited amount of historical data from the source and updated the existing table with new information. While the main table for P2P transactional data checked for all changes within the last three days, the associated notes table only considered transactions created in the prior three days. Consequently, late-arriving transactions, or transactions older than three days that are updated within the last three days, would have a null initiator note because the note was populated at the time of the initial transaction.
Remediation Strategy:
Remove the 3 day lookback filter for the table fetching initiator notes and reload the full table.
 Conduct lookback analysis on both queues (March 2025 to present) and generate missed cases as required.
 Add validation testing for alert generation completeness
Issue Type: Gap – a needed process or control is missing entirely.
Issue Rating Justification:
Potential regulatory enforcement or material fines
 Regulatory penalties jeopardizing licenses
 Process failures affecting multiple business units (Cash Global + Cash US)
 Potential for broader regulatory or stakeholder scrutiny

Issue Title: Adverse Action Not Applied to Sponsor When Sponsored Account Closed Before Processing
Issue Description:
** ISSUE HAS BEEN RISK ACCEPTED on 11/18/25. SEE ATTACHED RISK ACCEPTANCE REQUEST FOR MORE DETAILS**
 

 GCMT Control Description
 When a sponsored account receives a denylist adverse action, Cash App applies a "strike" (SponsorshipStrikeAdversity) to the sponsor's account with a warning. If a second sponsored account for the same sponsor receives a denylist adverse action, the sponsor is not able to sponsor new accounts with their SSN (SponsorshipCreationAdversity). 
 

 Exception Results from All Eligible Control Events:
 Sponsors that did not have a corresponding sponsorship strike: 0.19% of the population of 32,435
 Corresponding sponsorship strike was applied more than 1 day after sponsored teen was denylisted: 0.18% of the population of 32,435 
 

 Manual Sample Review Results: 
 GCMT noted that out of 1 of 30 (3%) denylisted sponsored accounts reviewed, no strike was applied to the sponsor.
 

 Issue:
 A sponsored account was closed and the sponsorship canceled before an automated check could determine if the dependent was sponsored and a corresponding strike (SponsorshipStrikeAdversity or SponsorshipCreationAdversity) applied to the sponsor. This oversight could allow sponsors associated with teen accounts involved in prohibited activities to continue sponsoring new accounts without restriction if the sponsor account re-opens.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Cash App account restriction rules are designed to prevent sponsors associated with restricted or denylisted teen accounts from sponsoring new accounts, ensuring enforcement of consumer fraud and compliance controls. A gap exists where, in rare edge cases, a sponsorship may be closed before automated strike logic executes, allowing a sponsor to avoid restriction; this issue was identified during a 2LoD review of 1LoD activities in November 2025. The impact is limited to a very small population (<0.2% of events), driven by technical sequencing constraints across systems, and is owned by the internal Cash App Families product and engineering teams.
Remediation Strategy: This issue is being risk accepted. The organization has determined that accepting the risk is more appropriate than pursuing remediation at this time. The risk will be monitored on an ongoing basis, and this decision will be reassessed periodically or if circumstances change.
Risk Acceptance Justification:
The inherent risk is that a sponsor associated with a denylisted teen account could potentially sponsor new accounts without restriction if the sponsorship is closed before the automated strike logic executes. However, this exposure is extremely limited, occurring in <0.2% of all sponsorship events, and is further mitigated by the controls described in the Compensating Controls section. The residual risk is therefore low, as the control operates effectively in over 99.6% of cases.
 

 Remediation would be non-trivial, likely requiring engineering updates to the sponsorship closure process to ensure strike logic executes prior to account termination, along with coordination across systems and testing to validate end-to-end functionality. Note that determination of remediation actions has not yet been defined as Engineering has advised they do not have the bandwidth to review. Given the small exception population and low potential impact, this effort is disproportionate to the very low residual risk and is therefore not prioritized through the first half of 2026, as engineering resources remain focused on higher-impact initiatives.
 

 This risk acceptance will remain in place for the current year and will be re-evaluated during the 2026 annual review cycle to confirm continued appropriateness and determine whether remediation should be pursued.
 

 Sponsors are subject to ongoing risk and compliance monitoring. Denylisted or otherwise ineligible sponsors cannot create or accept new sponsorships until they are in good standing and meet identity verification requirements. Risk-based controls are applied at new sign-up and account re-engagement, ensuring any previously associated or high-risk sponsors are appropriately evaluated prior to sponsorship approval.
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: Present a low risk to the operations of Block, or represents a limited weakness in Block’s processes or controls. Corrective action to address the Issue(s) identified is required.

Issue Title: Retro / Borrow AAN decline language discrepancy
Issue Description: The language contained within a decline reason for users who fail IDV specifically identifies Borrow loans. Potential Retro borrowers who are receiving the same decline reasons, with language referring to a different product, could cause confusion.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Sample adverse action notices (AANs) provided to potential users who failed IDV were pulled in order to fill a request for a partner bank audit. It was discovered that the decline language stated, “you are currently ineligible for Borrow because of an issue with your social security number,” regardless of the loan product, specifically Retro loans. In other words, potential Retro users received decline reasons which referenced a different product.
Remediation Strategy: The loan-specific language will be removed from the decline reason, immediately. This will change the reason to, “you are currently ineligible for because of an issue…..” This will allow the reason to be standardized for use within AANs going forward.

Issue Title: Clearpay UK TM, UAR & SAR processes require enhancement across data access, detection rules, workflows, and training
Issue Description:
Clearpay’s TM, UAR, and SAR processes were reviewed in a comprehensive gap analysis across TM & SAR systems and processes, specifically addressing low SAR reporting rates in Clearpay UK, and assessing root causes across people, process, policy, and systems.
 

 The review aimed to ensure Clearpay UK effectively identifies, investigates, and reports suspicious transactions in line with UK regulatory expectations. While foundational processes are in place, the analysis highlights critical areas for improvement:
 

 1) Training/Guidance: Insufficient training for Customer Service and business staff on identifying red flags and specific scenarios requiring UAR submission
 2) Data Access: Investigators lack access to Risk Insights platform data (IP addresses, Device IDs, merchant-side information) despite this data being collected, limiting decision-making capabilities and SAR quality 
 3) ATO Detection: Significant inconsistency in ATO tagging, leading to possible missed potentially reportable cases and false positives.
 4) Investigation Scope/Investigator Training: Improvement opportunity to raise awareness of other typologies (money laundering, consumer-merchant collusion, falsified transactions) with investigators.
 5) Fraud Risk Monitoring Coverage: Some reportable fraud cases not comprehensively captured by current transaction monitoring rules and UAR process.
RCA Description: Primary root cause: Systemic gaps in training, data access, and detection capabilities are preventing effective identification and reporting of suspicious activities across multiple fraud typologies.
Remediation Strategy:
1) Fraud Risk Monitoring Coverage
 2) ATO Detection
 3) Data Access & testing
 4) Training/Guidance & communication to stakeholders
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: Rated high because identified deficiencies across training, data access, ATO detection, and monitoring coverage directly impair the ability to identify and report suspicious activity, resulting in suspected low SAR reporting rates. Critical organization-wide consequences because failure to report is a criminal offence and the issue points to material volumes of potentially reportable activity not being investigated and ultimately reported on.
Risk Domain: Compliance | Financial Crime | Anti-Money Laundering & Terrorist Financing (1000126)
Issue Progress Updates: On 2/25/2026, Nahilla Razaq confirmed that the issue is on track for timely closure.
Issue Action Plans:
TM & SAR Enhancement Plan - Fraud Integration (1034480)
 TM & SAR Enhancement Plan - All other actions (1035817)

Issue Title: Clearpay UK Tier 1 identity verification requires enhancement to mitigate impersonation risk and enhance documentation to evidence AML/CTF/PF program design
Issue Description:
Clearpay's Tier 1 identity verification process was reviewed against applicable MLR and JMLSG guidance. The analysis identified enhancements should be made to Clearpay’s Tier 1 identity verification process alongside updates to the wider AML/CTF/PF Program and documentation. 
 

 Documentation of Business Relationship Purpose: The purpose and intended nature of the business relationship are clear but not adequately documented within Clearpay’s program documentation.
 Impersonation Risk Assessment: Clearpay's Annual Risk Assessment could be improved to more directly assess impersonation risk.
 Reliability and Integrity of eIDV Data Sources: The assessment of the reliability and integrity of LexisNexis IDU's data sources is not adequately recorded in auditable evidence.
 Evidence for Tiered Verification Approach: The basis for Clearpay’s risk-based approach to tiered IDV is not adequately recorded in auditable evidence.
 Verification of Applicant Identity: Clearpay's eIDV approach does not apply a verification check to manage the risk of impersonation fraud or alternatively substantiate why this approach is risk-appropriate.
 Negative Screening: Clearpay is partially compliant with negative screening guidance.
RCA Description: Primary root cause: Clearpay's Tier 1 identity verification process lacks adequate documentation, auditable evidence, and risk-based controls to demonstrate comprehensive compliance with MLR and JMLSG regulations and guidance for managing impersonation fraud and negative screening requirements. Requirement must not have been identified, adequately understood, or sufficiently justified during initial design of IDV approach.
Remediation Strategy:
Update CDD Standard for Business Relationship Purpose and Tiered Verification Rationale.
 Enhance Annual Risk Assessment to Directly Assess Impersonation Risk
 Implement Digital ID Matching for Higher Impersonation Risk Customers Post-IDU.
 Conduct Review of LexisNexis IDU Provider Data Source Reliability and Integrity.
 Assess Integration of CIFAS Negative Screening into Tier 1 IDV Process
 

 Action to address the highest risk elements of this issue "Implement a vendor solution for Digital ID matching" has an earlier due date than the target closure date.
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: Rated issue as high due to non-compliance with regulatory requirement, and direct connection to impersonation risk which is a potential drive for consumer fraud which is Clearpay’s primary financial crime risk and could be exacerbated by Clearpay’s current approach to Tier 1 IDV.
Risk Domain: Compliance | Financial Crime | Anti-Money Laundering & Terrorist Financing (1000126)
Issue Action Plans:
Update the Consumer Due Diligence (CDD) Standard (1036465)
 Review the proposed approach of treating the purpose and nature of business relationship as 'self-evident' for all customers (1036468)
 Enhance Clearpay’s Annual Risk Assessment (1036470)
 Implement a vendor solution for Digital ID matching (1036472)
 Review of LexisNexis IDU (1036474)
 Assessment of the possible integration of CIFAS negative screening (1036476)
 Determine and ensure application of uplifted standards to relevant customers (1036478)
 Conduct a review and analysis of SAR data following implementation (1036480)
 Deliver training to all relevant staff on substantive CRA or CDD process changes (1036482)

Issue Title: Clearpay UK Merchant Customer Risk Rating Model does not align with relevant guidance.
Issue Description:
Clearpay UK’s merchant customer risk assessment model is compliant with MLRs in that merchants are subject to a customer risk assessment, however, Clearpay does not maintain an automated customer risk rating (“CRR”) model for merchants or apply risk factors on a weighted basis, such that the totality of relevant risk factors drive a merchant’s overall risk rating, and so that their rating is not unduly influenced by just one factor. This approach is not aligned with relevant guidance contained in JMLSG Part 1 sections 4.44 to 4.52 or the FCA’s expectations for a firm of Clearpay’s size and complexity. Clearpay should align with this guidance, particularly given the diverse range of merchants it serves.
 

 Not maintaining an automated and weighted CRR for merchants also means Clearpay is unable to apply risk-based controls in an automated fashion (enhanced monitoring controls are applied manually, and all merchants are subject to periodic review on the same cadence). Manual enhanced monitoring controls and uniform periodic review cadences across all merchants, regardless of their individual risk profiles, creates operational inefficiencies and may result in lagging risk mitigation for higher-risk merchant relationships and over-monitoring lower-risk merchants, ultimately compromising the effectiveness of Clearpay's financial crime prevention program.
RCA Description:
Primary root cause: Merchant risk rating is manual and relatively linear, reducing the ability for the merchant risk rating to be weighted and accurately reflecting the true risk profile. The scale and complexity of the business has increased over time, and the merchant CRR must be uplifted to remain proportionate. Product selected as category for RCA because growth of scale offering is the primary root cause for the issue.
 

 Supporting evidence: No rules based risk rating, one risk flag can skew the overall weighting, resulting in an inaccurate representation of the risk profile of our merchant population, while other relevant signals are not represented in the methodology.
Remediation Strategy:
Design weighted and risk-based methodology for merchant CRR
 Determine the scope (Direct / SUP)
 Implement for new merchants
 Deploy a plan to apply the rating to the existing customer base
 Scope automation opportunities for straight through workflows with escalation points where required
Impacted Business Area:
Counsel - Cash Compliance
 Counsel - Compliance Engineering and Technology
 Compliance Ops
 Counsel - CET - Data Engineering
Issue Type: Deficiency – the process or control does not meet required standards, policies, or regulatory expectations.
Issue Rating Justification: The current program is compliant with the requirements of the MLRs. However, the customer risk of merchants is wide ranging and therefore absence of automated weighted merchant CRR does materially impede the application of appropriate risk based AML/CTF/CPF controls across the merchant base and does not align with authoritative guidance on customer CRRs.
Risk Domain: Compliance | Financial Crime | Anti-Money Laundering & Terrorist Financing (1000126)
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: Clearpay UK Consumer Customer Risk Rating Model restricts Clearpay’s ability to (i) enhance the application of risk-based ML/TF controls, (ii) monitor the overall risk-profile of Clearpay’s consumer base.
Issue Description:
Clearpay UK’s consumer customer risk assessment model is compliant with MLRs in that merchants are subject to a customer risk assessment, characteristics that trigger EDD or permit simplified DD under the MLRs are accounted for in addition to other relevant triggers determined by Clearpay.
 

 Clearpay does not maintain an automated customer risk rating (CRR” model for consumers or apply risk factors on a weighted basis. Guidance indicates that this may be appropriate to ensure customer and activity profiles are accounted for in CRR, as well as impede the application of appropriate risk based AML/CTF/CPF controls across the consumer customer base. Relevant guidance contained in JMLSG Part 1 sections 4.44 to 4.52. However, given the low limits of Clearpay’s BNPL product and transaction monitoring controls this may still be achieved without an automated CRR.
 

 Not maintaining a CRR for consumers does mean Clearpay is unable to apply risk-based controls in an automated fashion, which could drive the development of disproportionately restrictive controls in the future (where risk factors are not accounted for well within Clearpay’s CRR model) and limit future product functionality.
RCA Description:
Primary root cause: Consumer risk rating is manual and narrow, reducing the ability for the consumer risk rating to be weighted and accurately reflecting the true risk profile. The scale and complexity of the business has increased over time, and the consumer CRR should be uplifted to remain proportionate. Product selected as category for RCA because growth of scale offering is the primary root cause for the issue.
 

 Supporting evidence: No rules based risk rated, one risk flag can skew the overall weighting, resulting in an inaccurate representation of the risk profile of our consumer population, as well as impede the application of appropriate risk based AML/CTF/CPF controls across the consumer customer base
Remediation Strategy:
Design weighted and risk-based methodology for consumer CRR
 Implement for new merchants
 Deploy a plan to apply the rating to the existing customer base
 Scope automation opportunities for straight through workflows with escalation points where required
Impacted Business Area:
Counsel - Cash Compliance
 Counsel - Compliance Engineering and Technology
 Counsel - CET - Compliance Data Science
 Compliance Ops
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: The current program is compliant with the requirements of the MLRs. However, automated weighted consumer CRR would better comply with guidance and regulatory expectations and improve the application of appropriate risk based AML/CTF/CPF controls across the consumer customer base, however its absence does not materially impede the application of appropriate risk-based controls given the relatively narrower range of consumer customer transaction volume (compared to customers of Merchant-side Product offerings) and the existence of specific risk-based fraud controls to mitigate the primary AML/CTF/CPF risk posed by consumers that is consumer fraud.
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: Lack of defined internal process for partner-confirmed Paper Money Deposit posting errors.
Issue Description:
Program Overview: Paper Money Deposits (PMD) allow Cash App customers to deposit physical cash into their Cash App balance by visiting one of our approved third-party retail vendors (e.g., Walmart, Walgreens, CVS).
 

 To complete a deposit:
 The customer visits a participating retail location.
 The customer either swipes their card or generates a barcode within the Cash App.
 The customer provides tangible cash to the cashier.
 The vendor accepts the cash, scans the barcode or processes the swipe.
 The vendor processes the transaction, and the deposit should then reflect in the customer’s Cash App account.
 

 The transaction is reported via ledgering to InComm, who works directly with the third-party vendor on our behalf.
 

 Current Issue: In certain cases, through reconciliation, the vendor identifies that cash was collected but the deposit did not post to the customer’s Cash App balance. InComm then sends an email to our team requesting that we apply an adjustment.
 

 These are not customer-initiated disputes.
 

 Current state process:
 InComm emails our team notifying us that an adjustment is required.
 We manually credit the customer’s Cash App account for the missing deposit.
 We reply to InComm confirming the adjustment has been completed.
 The process ends there.
 

 There is:
 No dispute or formal case created in our system tied to the customer.
 No standardized intake mechanism beyond email communication.
 No centralized tracking or reporting of these adjustments.
 No documented or formally defined internal process governing this workflow.
 

 This represents an informal, manual handling approach without structured oversight.
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: ATO Self Reported Queue Cases OOSLA in December 2025
Issue Description:
During daily report monitoring conducted by ATO lead Micheal Roberts, 4,268 cases in the ATO Self Reported queue with case creation dates of 12/2 - 12/7 were identified as falling out of the 10 business day service level (OOSLA). The total number of cases identified as impacted/out of SLA and eligible for remediation is 4,099. 
 

 Multiple factors have contributed to the rise of cases in the ATO Self Reported queue, and have limited Risk Operations' ability to pivot agents previously cross-trained in Self Reported to assist. These factors include: 
 -Team attrition
 -Elevated volumes in the ATO space
 -Mistransfers from Customer Operations
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: 107c - prohibited from misrepresenting, expressly or impliedly, to consumers
Issue Description:
CFPB Completed a review of Block Inc's Cash App mobile payment application and found that...

Business Unit: Risk Ops Identity Controls - Team Lead
Issue Title: dIDV Manual Review - Lack of corrective action for incorrectly processed pass/fail decisions
Issue Description:
The Cash Risk Operations Identity & Controls team, specifically the dIDV (manual Identity Verification) team, lacks process and tooling capabilities to correct an erroneous decision to either 1) pass or 2) fail a dIDV submission.This creates a control deficiency where correct action cannot be flagged, reversed, or formally documented in internal systems.
 

 Current vs. Expected State:
 - Current: Once a customer passes manual dIDV review, the decision is irreversible. No process exists to flag, document, or reverse erroneous approvals. Also, if a customer is failed, there is not a process to reverse that decision and inform the customer. The customer must decide to reattempt dIDV. This could lead to churn.
 Expected: dIDV team should have documented procedures and system tooling to reverse incorrect decisions, with appropriate audit trails and customer communications.
 - Control Context: If a customer’s identity is not verifiable via eIDV or if the customer is deemed higher risk, then customers are prompted to complete dIDV. 
 A customer is deemed high risk if they are engaging in high risk products or because they are flagged by our IDV risk model or rules. The dIDV process leverages the services of Persona to match the information on the collected government issue ID with the selfie provided by the customer. 
 In certain cases, customers may not be eligible to complete dIDV as a fallback to eIDV.
 

 Impact:
 1. Security Risk: Bad actors can bypass identity verification and gain access to: 
 1a. Send from Stored Balance/Load funds into Stored Balance
 1b. Access higher send/receive amounts
 1c. Access other products – e.g., Cash App Card, Investing, Lending, Bitcoin, Taxes
 1d. Transact on a device if another account has ever transacted on the same device 
 2. Operational Risk: Manual review team cannot formally document, track, or correct decisions
 3. Dependency Risk: Resolution requires cross-functional support (product, tooling, process)
 

 Current Compensating Controls: Back-end fraud detection systems may eventually identify bad actors through behavioral patterns
RCA Description:
The issue stems from a control design gap in the dIDV process. dIDV was implemented as an identity verification decision point without recourse capabilities, including documented procedures, compensating controls, or system functionality to reverse or flag incorrect approvals.
 

 In the current state, errors cannot be corrected once an approval is issued. This forces reliance on downstream, reactive backend controls and allows customers who do not meet IDV policy requirements to remain verified, increasing fraud and compliance risk. Nor can we correct a failure for a customer who should have been passed.
Remediation Strategy: The Cash Risk Operations (RO) Identity & Controls (I&C) team will partner with Product Compliance to define a reasonable process and policy for manual dIDV recourse, and with the First Line Risk Management (FLRM) Policy Governance team to formally document and memorialize the approved approach. The Cash RO I&C team will also collaborate with Product and Engineering to implement tooling that supports the defined recourse process. Additionally, the team will work with the Content team to ensure agents have clear, up-to-date knowledge articles or work instructions, and that these materials are effectively communicated once available.
Interested Parties:
Naina Jain
 Lauren Butters
 Rosa Brown
 Stephen Ritter
Impacted Business Area:
Cash - Product & Business
 Ecosystem Platform - Identity Platform
 Risk Ops Identity Controls - Specialist
 Risk Ops Identity Controls - Team Lead
 Cash Design - Trust - Identity & Controls
Issue Type: Gap – a needed process or control is missing entirely.
Issue Rating Justification:
- Regulatory Risk: Identity verification is fundamental to KYC/AML compliance. Inability to correct erroneous passes/failures creates compliance documentation gaps
 - Security Risk: Direct pathway for bad actors to bypass identity verification controls and access cash features with full account capabilities
 - Control Effectiveness: dIDV serves as final line-of-defense with no compensating controls for error correction. This is a critical control gap
 - Business Impact: Potential for fraud, money laundering, or other illicit activity through incorrectly verified accounts
 - Scope: Affects all customer accounts processed through dIDV workflow (potentially high volume)
 

 Risk Factors Elevating Severity:
 - No formal error correction process
 - No audit trail for reversals
 - Reliance on downstream controls (reactive vs. preventive)

Issue Title: Control design weaknesses and inadequate oversight in translation, assignment, and monitoring of Complaint Management Training Modules
Issue Description:
Background:
 GCMT conducted testing over the 2025 Square Japan Complaint Training records, to assess if mandatory complaint training was assigned and completed by all applicable team members. Mandatory complaint training and required recipients include the following:
 2025 Block Global Complaint Program Training: All relevant Staff Members will receive training at least annually covering applicable requirements and guidance to implement this Standard effectively.
 [JP] Square クレーム対応研修 [Complaints Management] Training module: All Square Japan Customer Service (CS) and CS Quality Assurance (CSQA) team members.
 Square Complaints Management Training for GSO and DET: All Square Japan Global Seller Onboarding (GSO) and Digital Engagement Team (DET) team members.
 SAM Square Complaints Management Training: All Square Japan Sales and Account Management (SAM) team members.
 Additionally, GCMT reviewed the current processes and controls in place for the translation, assignment and monitoring of complaint training.
 

 Issues:
 1. Assignment and Completion Monitoring 
 

 GCMT reviewed the 2025 training records for mandatory training modules and identified the following exceptions: 
 [JP] Square クレーム対応研修 [Complaints Management] Training module
 Out of 128 CS and 6 CSQA team members (including Business Partners Outsourced agents [BPOs]): 
 46 team members (26 were SQ employees, 20 were BPO) were not enrolled into the JP module.
 26 SQ team members, 7 CS and 1 CSQA team members were enrolled into the English version of the module, with 5 team members who also did not complete it.
 20 team members did not complete the JP module.
 2025 Block Global Complaints Program Training
 Out of 142 team members (including BPOs): 
 72 team members (5 SQ Employees and 67 BPOs) were not enrolled into the Block Global Compliance Program (GCP) Module. 
 1 team member did not complete the GCP module
 Square Complaints Management Training for GSO & DET (2025)
 1 DET employee was not assigned the refresher for Square Complaints Management Training for GSO & DET in 2025. 
 (Note: Only 1 DET employee is responsible for JP market)
 SAM Square Complaints Management Training
 SQ Complaints Management has confirmed that training has not been provided to JP SAM in 2025.
 

 DESIGN GAPS: 
 - Assignment of annual refreshers
 The process to assign Square Complaints annual training refreshers involves multiple parties and contains single points of failure with inadequate validation controls. The process is as follows: 
  > SQ Complaints submits Learning Experience Team (LXP) & Workforce Management team (WFM) JIRA ticket for refresher training.
  > WFM compiles the employee list and provides it to LXP. 
  > LXP assigns training on Block Academy based on the employee list provided.
  > WFM schedules completion time for CS advocates to complete the training.
  > Employees complete the training during scheduled time.
 (Note: Assignment based on Workday employee codes is not possible as there are known data issues) 
 

 During the March 2025 training assignment cycle, the WFM team member working on the ticket was let go as part of a restructure. LXP has indicated they believe the employee list they received was incomplete, and therefore only assigned training to the employees on that list. There was no validation control to verify that the employee list was complete before assignment. As the JIRA ticket was marked as completed, SQ Complaints Management assumed the training had been assigned appropriately to all required employees.
 Furthermore, Global Complaints Oversight (GCO) does not have oversight of the Block Global Complaints Program training assignment to Square employees, as this responsibility is delegated to the brand level. This creates a governance gap where no single party has end-to-end accountability for ensuring all required employees receive training. While GCO has a report to monitor completion rates of the training, GCO does not have oversight to ensure training assignment is accurate.
 

 - Training Completion Monitoring 
 SQ Complaints Management currently does not have oversight over the completion rates of the [JP] Square Square クレーム対応研修 [Complaints Management] Training module or for Complaints training in any other regions. Monitoring of training completion is usually delegated to team leads of the employees. However, this monitoring control breakdown occurred in two scenarios:
 Employees not assigned training: The team lead control could not function as it is dependent on employees first being assigned the training. 
 Employees who were assigned but did not complete training: The team lead follow-up control failed to ensure timely completion which can indicate a lack of effective escalation or enforcement when employees do not complete assigned training.
 The absence of centralized completion reporting to SQ Complaints Management resulted in both the assignment and completion of the training going undetected. Regular completion reporting would provide Complaints Management with visibility to identify gaps and escalate issues promptly.
 

 2. Translation of Complaints Management Training
 There is currently no Japanese translated version of the Block Global Complaints Program Training. As a result, Square Japan team members who were assigned the complaint training modules, were expected to complete the training in English which in most cases would be their second language. 
 The following was noted per conversations with GCO: 
 For the 2025 Block Global Complaints Program module, GCO team provided the training content to each of the brands (in this case Square) to determine who receives training given they had access to the appropriate employee lists across internal and BPO teams. 
 As L&D had not previously translated this training for other regions, there was discussion over a few weeks to determine the best path forward.
 It was finally determined that a Square Japan employee translate the full training content and for Square to assign the training.
 GCO did not have oversight on the completion of this translation process and assignment. 
 The current expectation for Square Japan team members to complete complaints training in English, inherently requires a level of English proficiency across all team members to ensure that responsibilities and requirements are fully understood. As most team members (including BPOs) converse with Square Sellers in Japanese, this is an unfair expectation. Providing a Japanese version would better align expectations and ensure comprehension across all team members, including BPO partners.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Results Matrix and final report attached - GCMT process
Remediation Strategy: MAPS provided in Results Matrix and final report
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: Rated in accordance with the Issue management program
Issue Action Plans:
Complaints Training Rollout to all teams (1036489)
 Monthly Training Completion Reports monitored by Complaints Management (1036491)
 Refresher to JP CS Leads on following up with advocates on training (1036493)
 Translation of Complaints into Japanese version (1036496)
 Enrollment and Monitoring Framework and Escalation processes (1037150)
 Escalation and consequence management processes for training non-completion (1037152)
 Implement enhanced training governance, system controls and processes (1037154)

Issue Title: Buyer surcharge complaints requires update to process documentation and establishment of reporting framework
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirements:
 Installment Sales Act Section 35-17-8(III): A business that has a contract for handling credit card numbers must investigate the matters prescribed in paragraph (1) about a seller affiliated with the intermediation of credit card purchases or service providers affiliated with the intermediation of credit card purchases that concluded the contract for handling credit card numbers periodically, or as necessary, in accordance with the provisions of Order of the Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry. 
 Enforcement Regulations Article 133-7(4): Status of complaints related to acts by merchants that fail to protect users’ interests.
 In light of the occurrence of complaints related to acts by merchants that fail to protect users’ interests, the business operator must conduct investigations at an appropriate frequency and in an appropriate manner.
 

 Background:
 Buyer surcharge complaints fall under the scope of the Act where they raise issues related to the appropriateness of card transactions, such as consent, authorization, charged amounts, or potential unauthorized use. While Square is not directly regulated under the Act, Square operates in line with the acquirer's (Sumitomo Mitsui Card Company [SMCC]) obligations and receives requests from the acquirer to investigate such complaints. Additionally, card brand rules prohibit surcharging in Japan.
 GCMT reviewed the Square Japan (SQJP) Square Wise 消費者センター / Buyerからのお問い合わせ [Consumer Center (NCAC) / Inquiry from Buyer] SOP which has been established to provide guidance for Customer Success agents when handling buyer and NCAC inquiries
 For buyer inquiries, this guidance requires CSAs to:
 Request additional details from buyers reporting surcharges (store name, transaction date/time, transaction amount, card brand, last 4 digits of card number).
 Inform buyers that "SQ will investigate the matter".
 As part of a monitoring control, SQJP Complaints Management currently reports all NCAC and buyer complaints related to unauthorised charges to Compliance Operations (APAC Detection Operations Team) and Risk (where required) on a monthly basis. The monthly sync enables Compliance Operations to review the unauthorised charges complaints ensuring that they have been handled and investigated appropriately and to provide advice to Complaints Management on case responses or feedback to their own team where needed.
 

 Issues: 
 1. Buyer Surcharge Guidance Misaligned with Current Practices
 GCMT identified the following gaps within the SQJP Square Wise Article Consumer Center (NCAC) / Inquiry from Buyer SOP:
 The SOP does not have an explicit escalation pathway: 
 While CSAs are instructed to state that Square will investigate, the SOP does not specify which team should receive the escalation for investigation. Customer Success has confirmed that CSAs are currently escalating buyer surcharge inquiries to Compliance Operations for investigation in practice.
 Although the Square Wise コンプライアンスチームについて [About the Compliance Operations Team] SOP provides guidance on cases requiring escalation to Compliance Operations, the SOP only mentions that the Compliance team monitors for seller surcharges. The SOP also does not explicitly instruct CSAs to escalate merchant surcharge cases to the Compliance team.
 Additionally, this SOP is not cross-referenced in the Consumer Center (NCAC) / Inquiry from Buyer SOP.
 The SOP does not have a requirement to flag a complaint when a buyer inquiry escalates to a complaint:
 Whilst GCMT transactional testing confirms that CSAs are raising buyer complaints in practice, the SOP does not reflect actual operational procedures. 
 

 2. Undefined Framework or Scope of Complaint Types Requiring Escalation from Complaints Management to Compliance Operations
 Buyer-initiated surcharge complaints are currently not reported from Complaints Management to Compliance Operations. Additionally, there is no documented framework specifying which complaint types require reporting from Complaints Management to Compliance Operations.
 Regular reporting would enable Compliance Operations to proactively identify merchants engaging in prohibited surcharging practices, rather than relying solely on ad-hoc escalations. While the NCAC complaints and buyer complaints are relatively low currently, as complaint volumes increase, the need for a formalized framework becomes increasingly important to ensure consistent handling and compliance with regulatory obligations.
 Additionally, with only one Complaints Manager currently supporting the Japan market, the absence of a documented framework creates significant knowledge retention risk.

Issue Title: Customer Success and Complaint Management Agent adherence Standard Operating Procedures for complaint reporting fields can be improved
Issue Description:
Background:
 GCMT conducted testing on a sample of 40 Seller and 12 Buyer/NCAC complaint tickets across Tiers 1, 2, 3-escalated and 3 to verify that all required processes documented within the Complaint Standard Operating Procedures were followed by the Customer Success and/or Complaint Management Agent as part of the complaint handling and resolution.
 GCMT identified one key complaint area where the effectiveness rate across all tiers was under 85%.
 Complaint Area #6 – Complaint Reporting Fields 
 Procedure Requirement:
 CS Process Complaints [Global] Standard Operating Procedure: 
 Line of Business: Select Customer Success-Frontline from the dropdown menu
 Complaint Intake Channel: Select the channel through which you received the complaint: Choose from Phone, Email or Messaging, based on how the complaint was received
 Product: Choose the product most relevant to the complaint. 
 

 Complaints Management AU Runbook: 
 Closed Date: A closed date can be determined when at least one of the following has occurred or can be observed; 
 a) The complainant confirms satisfaction with the resolution (verbally or in writing);
 b) A final IDR response has been provided; 
 c) No further action is possible and this has been clearly communicated to the complainant (by any Frontline team, or by Complaints Management)
 

 Issue:
 GCMT identified a 70% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 30% exception rate where the complaint fields entered by the agent were incorrect based on the reviewed complaint. 
 GCMT identified 16 out of 40 Seller and 13 Buyer/NCAC complaints where the complaint fields entered by Customer Success Agent or Complaint Manager were incorrect. These consisted of: 
 - 12 instances where the description of the complaint did not include all elements 
  > 10 instances where it did not include actions taken or offers made to address the complainant's concerns. 9 of them were recorded onto the case instead of the complaint form. 
  > 2 instances where it did not include specific reasons for complaint tier 
 - 4 instances where the initial acknowledgement date was inaccurate. 
 - 3 instances where the closed date was inaccurate or does not reflect the complaint resolution date
  > In all instances, the closed date corresponded to the date the Complaint Manager reviewed the complaint and marked it as closed, even though the complaint had already been resolved earlier by the CSA or SAS teams or when the IDR response was provided
 -1 instance where the initial date of complaint was inaccurate  
 -1 instance where the line of business was incorrect
 -1 instance where the resulting action was incorrect

Issue Title: Grading of complaint cases by CS Quality Assurance, can be uplifted to ensure all exceptions are being recorded, to improve agent behaviour and adherence to Standard Operating Procedures.
Issue Description:
Background:
 GCMT conducted testing on a sample of 30 complaints which were graded by the quality assurance team to confirm the accuracy of the grading outcome and any exceptions identified 
 Procedure Requirement: 
 Square Complaints Management Guide: "In addition to the Complaint Definition, at least one of the following Complaint Elements must be present in the interaction between Square and the Customer for a Complaint:
 - How the problem has negatively affected the complainant
 - How the problem has led to a negative perception of our company or what it offers
 - A belief that our company has breached their expectation of treatment" 
 

 Issue:
 GCMT identified a 70% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 30% exception rate where GCMT identified one or more issues not picked up by the grader or where GCMT did not agree with the grading assessment. This consisted of: 
 - 7 exceptions where the Grader marked the ticket as "Did not report Tier 3 complaint" however per GCMT review, these exceptions did not have any of the complaint elements present in the interaction as per the Square Complaints Management Guide.
 - 2 exceptions where Grader marked the grading criteria as "Reported inquiries and feedback from merchants as complaints" when the Grader should have marked it as "Did not report Tier 3 complaint". Whilst the grader correctly identified that the CSA did not flag a complaint, the grading criteria suggests that the CSA incorrectly flagged a complaint. 
 - 1 exception where the Grader did not pick up that the complaint should be a Tier 3 - Escalated when the customer suggested that service that they were provided by a prior agent seemed confrontational.
