Issue Title: Compliance Incident for 15079381
Issue Description:
On February 3, an agent sent an email to account holder #15079381. 
 

 Whilst the email was sent to the correct customer it was addressed to a different person (different Afterpay customer) within the email itself. Upon doing a name search on Debtrak, we believe the incorrect customer name was the customer for account #6528477.
 

 The email contained the incorrect customer name and ATP name:
 

 ● Incorrect customer name was ‘Darisha McKnight’
 ● Incorrect ATP was ‘Just Budget’ 
 

 ● The correct customer name should have been ‘Tabitha Culph’.
 ● The correct ATP name should have been ‘Salvation Army’.
 

 The incident was identified by Afterpay via notification by the customer. 
 

 Note: The agent identified the error immediately and sent a ‘please disregard’ follow-up email to the customer within minutes of the original email.
RCA Description:
On February 3, an agent sent an email to account holder #15079381. 
 

 Whilst the email was sent to the correct customer it was addressed to a different person (different Afterpay customer) within the email itself. Upon doing a name search on Debtrak, we believe the incorrect customer name was the customer for account #6528477.
 

 The email contained the incorrect customer name and ATP name:
 

 ● Incorrect customer name was ‘Darisha McKnight’
 ● Incorrect ATP was ‘Just Budget’ 
 

 ● The correct customer name should have been ‘Tabitha Culph’.
 ● The correct ATP name should have been ‘Salvation Army’.
 

 The incident was identified by Afterpay via notification by the customer. 
 

 Note: The agent identified the error immediately and sent a ‘please disregard’ follow-up email to the customer within minutes of the original email.
Remediation Strategy:
Immediate Action/Initial Response
 As the ‘please disregard’ email was sent to the customer immediately after the incident, no further immediate action is recommended. 
 

 On February 17, Alek (QA) sent an email to Nelson Dizon (Offshore Operations Manager) to request a formal explanation, which will then be used to discuss the matter with the Probe CL.
 

 Immediate Action/Initial Response
 As the ‘please disregard’ email was sent to the customer immediately after the incident, no further immediate action is recommended. 
 

 On February 17, Alek (QA) sent an email to Nelson Dizon (Offshore Operations Manager) to request a formal explanation, which will then be used to discuss the matter with the Probe CL.
 

 Remedial / Corrective Action (If required)
 - Detail who is responsible, costs, how effectiveness of changes will be measured and at what frequency
 The following recommendations are made to ensure this or similar incidents do not occur:
 1. Add the template the CL had used in this instance to the list of Debtrak templates, which will pre-populate all the data automatically. Note: All email templates are to be specific to a specific scenario within the correspondence tab and customer PI is to be automated by Debtrak to the body of the email. This extends to predetermined email content. The user shall be able to amend the content, but the initial content shall be determined by the system, not a person. Responsible: Operations
 2. Ensure that email responses (i.e.where we are responding to a customer’s email) are only sent via the Debtrak Correspondence tab, and by no other way. Provide communication to the team accordingly. Responsible: Operations
 3. Update the SOP to reflect the processes in point 1 and point 2 so there is no ambiguity. And also include a Delegation Matrix to the SOP, which shall include all delegated authority based on role (where free text emails will be captured, amongst other tasks). Responsible: QA
 4. Consider other common scenarios where an email to a customer is required, and create the required email templates on Debtrak for future use. Responsible: Operations
 5. Uplift the Afterpay Risk Register to capture the risk and document the controls in place (points 1 to 4 above) in order to mitigate the risk going forward. Responsible: QA
 6. Send an apology email/letter to the customer and reassure the customer that other than the names, the information provided on the original email was theirs, and not for another party. Responsible: Afterpay
 7. Raise an internal Compliance Incident and attach this IMR to it. Responsible: Operations
 

 Other comments:
 It is not recommended that disciplinary action is taken against the employee for the incident itself. As the system controls were not in place, the business had (albeit unintentionally) accepted the risk of this type of human error. There is therefore contributed negligence, and for instances where the risk should have been mitigated by the business, it is unfair to discipline the agent for a human error. 
 

 However, it is recommended that a warning is provided to the employee for not informing the Operations Manager of the error. As the CL identified their error and sent the ‘please disregard’ email, the CL was aware of the incident. The CL failed to inform management, and as they are a leader within the business, it is reasonable that the CL should know better.
 

 As all Probe employees are obligated to inform/escalate an incident upon identifying it, and as it is clear there was an opportunity to disclose the error to management, not doing so warrants disciplinary action in this instance. 
 

 As it is an isolated incident and the employee has otherwise been a high performing CL, a written warning is recommended, but not a first and final warning. This recommendation has been provided to the Operations Director (Adrian) and Operations Manager (Shane) for their consideration.
Not an Issue Justification: External vendor issue

Issue Title: Gaps Noted in the Adequacy of Quality Assurance Program Coverage and Execution Which Increase Risk of Untimely SAR/Sanction Amendments
Issue Description:
Block's internal policies require Quality Assurance (QA) reviews to assess compliance with AML and sanctions obligations, including defined timeframes for review completion and remediation. IA identified gaps in the design and execution of QA procedures across both Cash App and Square platforms affecting multiple AML processes. Specifically, IA identified:  
 1. Cash App TM/SAR QA Remediation Delays: From 25 TM/SAR QA samples tested, 5 reviews were remediated beyond the required 30-day timeframe, with the longest taking 73 days.  
 2. Cash App Sanctions QA Review Delays: In the total population during the covered period, 687 out of 1,572 sanctions QA reviews (43.7%) were not completed within the 30-day SLA.  
 3. Square QA SLA Gaps: The Square Quality Assurance team managing TM, SARs, KYC, and sanctions QA queues lacked defined SLAs for QA review completion and remediation during the covered period.  
 4. Cash App Financial Crimes Quality Assurance procedure for TM and SARs does not specify the SLA for conducting QA reviews and the SLA for resolving identified failures.  
 5. Sanctions QA Coverage Gap: QA processes only occur at Level 1 and Level 2 sanctions reviews, with no QA procedures for Level 3 filing decisions, creating a gap in oversight for potential regulatory filing determinations.  
 As a result, these QA gaps increase the risk of untimely SAR corrections or amendments, untimely identification of required reports to OFAC and associated actions, delayed identification and remediation of compliance issues, inconsistent application of QA standards, and incorrect regulatory filings. This exposes Block to regulatory fines, penalties, and reputational harm due to potential non-compliance with AML and Sanctions obligations.
Approval: Do Not Approve: Requires further Information

Issue Title: Uncle Sam' error resulting in loans incorrectly receiving MLA interest rates
Issue Description:
The Uncle Sam system–which cross-checks the identity of borrowers (Borrow and Retro) against the Department of Defense’s internal database to ensure the company charges correct MLA-directed interest rates–experienced a temporary outage. This unknown outage resulted in borrowers’ identities, in relation to military service, to return as “unknown.” Therefore, those borrowers’ loans ultimately received MLA-mandated interest rates/set-up fees, which are lower than standard rates/fees. This is due to standard protocol where the system defaults to applying the lower, MLA-mandiated rate if the system is not able to access the DoD’s database or is identified as “unknown.” This is to ensure that the MLA is not violated. No batch processing issue monitoring existed for Uncle Sam.
 

 The issue was discovered during a standard review of the loan portfolio. It is estimated that 33% of loans underwritten during the time period of 12/9-24/25 were impacted, resulting in substantial loss in revenue.
 

 Because of the outage, the system correctly followed standard procedure by defaulting to charge the reduced rate/fee if the system was unable to access Uncle Sam information; this is done as a safeguard to prevent MLA violations. For example, Borrow and Retro loans were underwritten at 2.5% from 5% and 4.4% from 7.5%, respectively.
 

 All impacted loans were identified for any other discrepancies. None were found, and in the name of preventing possible consumer harm, the loans were allowed to be repaid as underwritten and the loss of revenue written off.
Explanation for Delay in Issue Creation: Was only recently made aware of the issue
RCA Description:
Uncle-Sam successfully received the DoD batch response file, however, a bug in the batch file parsing logic caused the file to fail parsing. As a result, the batch refresh job set the MLA status to be “UNKNOWN,” while ‘last_refreshed_at’ message was still updated to the batch job run time. This caused the real-time API to treat the status as non-expired and return “UNKNOWN” as well.
 

 When the UCML is unable to cross-reference a potential borrower with Uncle Sam (and ultimately the DoD), the system defaults to charging the MLA-outlined interest rate/set-up fee, which is lower than standard rates/fees; this was put in place to avoid violating the MLA by mistake. Therefore, the control was correctly followed.
Remediation Strategy: Initially, the parsing logic to correctly handle DoD batch response format was fixed. Secondarily, the team suggested using an alternative source of Lexis-Nexis, which is already in use, as an alternative avenue to access information about the possibility that a borrower could be on the DoD database, and this subject to lower MLA-outlined rates/fees. In the interim, the access was fixed and the default workflow will remain in place.
Interested Parties:
Corey Chamberlain
 Mia Bockus
 Greg Sager
Issue Type: Weakness – the process or control exists but is not strong enough or is not operating effectively to manage the risk.
Issue Rating Justification: The system correctly followed the control to apply MLA-outlined rates/fees to borrowers’ loans when it was unable to receive info from Uncle Sam/DoD
Issue Action Plans: Uncle Sam' error fix (1037815)

Issue Title: Sample testing of supplier contract practices identified gaps in the engagement and ongoing management of suppliers
Issue Description:
Through understanding the contract engagement process across a sample of five existing suppliers, it was identified that a number of gaps in the contracting and ongoing management of these supplier arrangements were noted. Given the absence of a formalised procurement function, and associated policies and process, these gaps are not unexpected.
 Identified gaps are outlined below (ordered by key activity in the supplier value chain). A detailed overview of observation and potential risk exposures in outlined in Appendix C.
 

 3.1.1 Competitive sourcing and formalised supplier evaluation not evidenced in four of five sampled contracts. 
 A formalised procurement process requires that an appropriate sourcing strategy be defined for a procurement activity outlining the approach to market, rationale for this approach and supplier evaluation methodology. Assessment of the sourcing and supplier evaluation approaches for the sample of five existing suppliers identified that there was no evidence of a documented sourcing strategy demonstrating that a competitive process was undertaken for four of the five sampled suppliers (Probe, CTM Professional Services, Message Media and TEKsystems). The only existing supplier arrangement where a competitive sourcing process was undertaken was the arrangement with Stripe Payments. A documented approach to supplier evaluation could also be evidenced for the arrangement with Stripe. Where sourcing of a supplier is not subject to a competitive sourcing process and a formalised evaluation approach, there is an increased risk that APT does not realise cost benefits associated with supplier competition (typically in the range of 8-15%) and that the selected supplier may not meet the performance expectations required by APT. 
 

 3.1.2 Third party risk assessment as part of supplier due diligence not evidenced in four of five sampled contracts. 
 Prior to entering into an arrangement with the successful supplier, a third-party risk assessment should be performed to understand the suitability of the third party to manage key risks inherent in the services provided by the supplier (such as IT Security, Privacy, Anti-Money Laundering and Compliance). APT have recently established a risk assessment process (currently detailed within the draft Contract Management Lifecycle process document), however this process was not formalised at the time of engaging the five sampled suppliers. Sample testing of supplier risk assessments identified that;
 • No formalised risk assessments could be evidenced or were understood to occur for the following suppliers:
  o Probe
  o CTM Professional Services
  o TEKsystems
 • It was represented that an IT Security and Privacy risk assessment was completed for the arrangement with Message Media, however no evidenced was provided
 • An IT Security and Privacy risk assessment was conducted and documented for the arrangement with Stripe Payments
 

 Where a third-party risk assessment is not performed, the level of risk exposure relating to continuity of operations and reputational damage may increase as a result of inadequate processes and controls maintained at the third party that were
 not detected by APT as part of supplier due diligence.
 

 3.1.3 Legal review of draft contracts was not evidenced for four of five sampled suppliers.
 Prior to executing a contract, the contract should be reviewed by legal subject matter experts to ensure the contract contains appropriate protections for APT and does not include any significant terms which may be unfavourable to APT in the circumstance where the contract departs from APT standard terms. The legal review should be formally captured to enable to Delegated Authority executing the contract to be informed that appropriate due diligence occurred. Sample testing to evidence Legal Review of the contract prior to execution from a Delegated Authority, along with assessment if the contract was executed in accordance with the Delegation of Authority identified: 
 • Legal review of the draft contract and a completed Legal review form outlining sign off from Legal (and Risk and Compliance) could be evidenced for one sampled arrangement (Stripe Payments).
 • It was represented that a legal review was completed, however no evidenced was provided for the following two sampled arrangements:
  o Message Media
  o CTM Professional Services
 • Legal review of the original contract with Probe and TEKsystems could not be evidenced (note the contract with Probe was executed in 2016).
 • Agreements with two suppliers (Probe and TEKsystems) were executed prior to the implementation of ‘version 1’ of the Delegation of Authority, as such assessment to determine if contract was executed in accordance the Delegation could not performed.
 • Contract approval and execution in accordance with the Delegation of Authority could be evidenced for Stripe Payments and Message Media.
 • It was represented that the contract with CTM Professional Services was executed in accordance with the Delegation of Authority, however no evidence was provided.
 

 3.1.4 Formalised ongoing assessment of third-party risks has not historically been conducted.
 Key risks that are owned by APT, but maintained by the third party should be assessed on an ongoing basis to ensure that risks are being appropriately managed by the third party, limiting the likelihood of any exposure to APT. For three of the five sampled suppliers (Message Media, CTM Professional Services and TEKsystems), there was no evidence that formalised assessment of third-party risks occurs on a defined basis. For the arrangement with Probe, while there was limited historic assessment of third-party risks, a specific ‘audit questionnaire’ has been recently developed and will be shortly distributed to Probe. The Audit covers both operations and compliance and requires representatives of Probe to provide answers and supporting documentation to a number of questions focused on operational, compliance, IT Security and Privacy risk. For the arrangement with Stripe, assurance reports demonstrating compliance with PCI-DSS requirements along with the effectiveness of control operated by Stripe relating to security, availability, confidentiality and processing integrity is provided to APT and reviewed on an annual basis.
 

 3.1.5 Formalised supplier management plans not defined for all sampled suppliers.
 Monitoring the performance of suppliers through clearly defined service level agreements and performance metrics helps to ensure that APT receives the services it is requiring to a standard that either meets or exceeds expectation. It was understood that monitoring of supplier performance was performed across all five suppliers (evidence of such monitoring was only provided for the arrangements with Probe and Stripe). It was however noted that no formalised supplier management plan outlining the nature and extent of performance monitoring mechanisms (i.e. monthly SLA reports, quarterly relationship reviews) is created across all five suppliers. While this does not present a significant risk exposure, supplier management plans do form an effective governance mechanism to ensure that appropriate performance monitoring mechanisms are defined and continue to be performed, irrespective in changes in contract owners from APT
Issue Action Plans:
1.1a 1.1b Assess the risk exposure of gaps identified (1000675)
 1.2 Based on risk assessment (1.1) conduct retrospective remediation activity (as required) (1000677)
 1.1c Existing supplier risk assessment (1000698)
Closure Request Justification: Legacy Afterpay Issue all actions closed

Issue Title: Policies, processes and system capability to embed the future state procurement and supplier management framework are not fully implemented
Issue Description:
APT are currently in the process of developing and implementing the supporting policies and procedures that will define the future state processes and controls. At the time of this review (October 2020), processes and controls related to procurement strategy, supplier risk assessments and contract review and approval had been drafted. Through our sample-based testing of historical supplier engagement and management, we assessed how the future state would address any process gaps and risk exposures identified through testing. Acknowledging that the establishment of the future state processes and controls is a work in progress and is being developed concurrently by the Procurement function (who are also supporting the business on active procurement opportunities), we identified that the following gaps from our assessment of the current state processes and controls (from sample testing) which should be prioritised to mitigate any future risk exposures. 
 

 3.2.1 Absence of a third-party risk management policy defining the need for ongoing assessment of third-party risk.
 The draft Contract Management Lifecycle Policy outlines that assessment of third-party risks are to be performed based on the categorisation of the contract as part of supplier due diligence. The risk assessment covers IT Security, Privacy, Finance, Anti-Money Laundering and Modern Slavery. We however note that here is currently no documented policy or process outlining the operating model, roles and responsibilities and requirements relating to the nature and extent of ongoing assessment of third-party risk. It is recommended that a policy/process document is defined which articulates the expectations and approach on how supplier risks relating to IT Security, Privacy, Anti-Money Laundering and Modern Slavery are assessed on an ongoing basis, including ongoing involvement with internal subject matter experts. Typically, we note that the nature and extent of ongoing risk assessment is based upon categorisation of specific arrangements having regard of the criticality of the arrangement to APT and level of risk. As an ‘annual audit’ questionnaire has recently developed for assessing operational and compliance risks for one sampled supplier (Probe), this assessment template could be leveraged to build ongoing risk assessment templates. Further it is recommended that where an ongoing risk assessment of a third-party supplier is required to be performed, a formal supplier management plan articulating these requirements is developed.
 

 3.2.2 Absence of a documented sourcing policy.
 The ‘Sourcing Policy’ has not currently been drafted. As a result, the expected sourcing process (i.e. Request for Quote, Request for Proposal), the key attributes that determine which sourcing strategy is used (i.e. risk assessment, expected value of procurement, nature of supplier market) and establishment of process to manage conflict of interest is not currently defined. In the absence of a documented sourcing policy, there is an increased risk future sourcing activity may not be subject to appropriate competitive processes, limiting the opportunity for additional value to be captured through cost reduction associated with strategic and competitive sourcing 
 

 3.2.3 Absence of a formalised supplier evaluation methodology
 There is no documented policy or process outlining the evaluation methodology and roles and responsibilities (including involvement of risk and compliance Subject Matter Experts for supplier evaluation) related to supplier evaluation under a competitive sourcing strategy. Based on our sample testing, where a competitive sourcing strategy (i.e. Request for proposal) was performed, the evaluation methodology was developed specifically for each procurement. Such a process is results in duplication of effort and lack of consistency in evaluation approach between different procurement activities. We note that supplier evaluation methodologies were specifically developed for the evaluation of suppliers in the recent global RFP on customer service and also for the appointment of a global acquirer (Stripe). It is recommended that these methodologies are leveraged in the development of a standardised supplier evaluation methodology 
 

 3.2.4 Lack of a centralised repository of third-party supplier
 APT do not maintain a central repository of all third-party supplier arrangements and contracts. This is a result of the historic absence of a central function to establish a single source of truth repository of arrangements and contracts and a lack of supporting contract management system. The absence of a central repository of arrangements and contracts increases the risk that contractual documentation is not retaining in accordance with legislative and regulatory requirements (typically 7 years) and limits the ability to centrally monitor and analyse aspects of the contract such as expiry date, commercial terms, risk and performance measures. We note that the implementation of a contract management system is one of the key project deliverables in establishing the Procurement Function.
 

 3.2.5 Lack of detailed reporting on third party spend
 There is currently a limited ability to efficiently analyse spend with third parties’ suppliers across departments, regions and categories as spend analysis dashboard reports have not yet been developed (it is acknowledged that development of such reporting capability has commenced). The absence of detailed spend reports limits the ability to identify opportunities where value may be enhanced through supplier consolidation or renegotiation of existing terms.

 2.1b Supplier evaluation methodology (1000681)

 2.1f Other policies (1000689)

Issue Title: Customer communication policies are not comprehensive and adherence is not monitored.
Issue Description:
Our review of APT communication guidelines and Debt Collection Agencies documented communication strategies noted the following governing requirements and definitions have not been defined and documented:
 • Definition of the type of messages sent to customers (i.e. delineation between what is considered a service, marketing or collections message) and the timing of these messages;
 • Baseline standard (in line with risk appetite and applicable regional regulatory requirements) for the frequency and appropriate hours of contact for each type and method of communication with a customer globally for both Afterpay and the collection agencies (whilst frequency is documented in the individual agency strategies (the tolerance of no greater than one communications every three days has not been formally documented); and
 • Ongoing monitoring in place to ensure customer communications action are aligned APT communication strategies and protocols including messages sent to customers are aligned with their choice of communications method. 
 

 We performed sample testing of 20 customers (15 in collections and 5 in hardship). 
 

 Our testing noted: 
 

 SMS sent to customers on public holiday
 • 5 SMS sent to customers on a public holiday from APT. Whilst these were payment reminders in all instances and were prior to the customers falling into collections (i.e. 1 day past due), management has confirmed that collections messages (e.g. overdue reminders for overdue instalment where non-payment may result in late fees) can be sent on a public holiday by the payment scheduler to customers in collections.
 

 SMS sent outside of 8am - 9pm (refer to table below)
 • 3 instances where APT was sending SMS outside of 8am – 9pm
 • 2 UK customers with a combined 89 SMS relating to collections reminders when the customers were in collections sent outside of 8am – 9pm
 • AFCA sample (ANZ) received 20 SMS (payment reminders) outside 8am – 9pm
 

 High volume in communications to customers by APT (refer to table below)
 • Prior to being referred to a collection agency, customers would receive communications (service and collections messages) from APT at an instalment level. Our sample testing noted the following instances of potential excessive contact from APT to customers.

Issue Title: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (“APRA”) Reporting Policy should be updated to include key information relevant to the preparation and submission of the EFS reporting
Issue Description:
A documented APRA Reporting Policy exists (effective October 2020) that provides background on Afterpay Australia Pty Ltd’s APRA reporting requirements and the high-level processes performed to prepare, review, and submit the required EFS reporting. 
 Whilst the policy exists and does include high-level reference to controls and processes, review of this policy identified that this should be updated to enhance the level of detail captured, to provide greater detail on areas such as:
 

 • Ownership and accountabilities for EFS reporting, including key responsibilities and reporting lines
 • Interpretation of requirements, identification and definition of critical data elements (including data lineage)
 • Individual control points performed within the process, and specific controls relied on performed as part of other processes (including roles and responsibilities) 
 • Key controls around non-accounting data (including HR/payroll information, and resident / non-resident split)
 • Specific judgements made on allocation and reporting. 
 

 When updating the policy, management should also ensure that the guidance on Special Purpose Vehicles (SPV’s) is captured accurately within the policy and based on Afterpay’s process. 
 It was noted that currently the reporting is prepared by the ANZ Financial Controller, and reviewed by the Group Financial Controller, however in a scenario where another individual took over part of the process, an updated policy would be beneficial to ensure expectations are clearly documented.

Issue Title: There is a lack of in house documented investigation & handling processes
Issue Description:
Recommendation Details
 Through a video interview with Amanda Street - Company Secretary and Nat McKaig - Deputy Company Secretary on 12 October 2020 it was identified that whilst the Company Secretary is the Executive Owner of the policy, the role does not have visibility of the investigation process. It ought to be noted here that Afterpay has not received a Whistleblower disclosure through Your Call. 
 

 Through a video interview with Shanyn Payne - Global HR Director on 20 October 2020, the Global HR Director stated that she was not aware of a documented investigation process other than what is written in the Whistleblower Policy.
 

 The review identified that the only known documented investigation process is what is currently in the Whistleblower Policy.
 

 3.1 How disclosures are investigated 
 Your concerns should have reasonable grounds. You are encouraged to make a report even if your concerns turn out to be incorrect. When we investigate a report, we focus on the substance of the disclosure and not the motives behind why it was made. 
 

 The Whistleblower Protection Officer will decide if the information you have disclosed warrants investigation or verification and may take further action. Investigations are conducted fairly, independently (without bias) and in strict confidence.  
 

 When possible and appropriate, a person being investigated will have an opportunity to respond to the information in the report that involves them. The Whistleblower Protection Officer will acknowledge receipt of the information you have disclosed and keep you informed of the progress of the investigation if possible.  
 

 Afterpay is committed to protecting the wellbeing, and ensuring the fair treatment, of all its employees, including those who are mentioned in disclosures made under this policy. 
 

 If any employee requires additional support in relation to responding to an investigation or allegation, they may contact the Whistleblower Protection Officer (whistleblower@afterpay.com) or their HR Business Partner or alternatively access the Employee Assistance Program.  
 

 

 This review also identified that the Whistleblower Policy lists a number of avenues someone can report/make a disclosure being:
 1.Your Call - online or by phone
 2. Via whistleblower@afterpay.com
 3. Chief Enterprise Risk Officer (cassandra.williams@afterpay.com)
 4.Human Resources Director (shanyn.payne@afterpay.com)
 5.our directors, officers or senior managers; 
 6.our internal or external auditors, members of an audit team conducting an audit, or actuaries; 
 7. if it is a tax related matter, our officers or employees with tax duties, or our registered tax agents or BAS agents.
 

 Where a disclosure is received via Your Call the matter is triaged, de-identified and disseminated to Afterpay. The Your Call system is designed to ensure disclosures and the identity of a Whistleblower is protected (where required) and to disseminate relevant information to Authorised Officers at Afterpay and record interactions between the Whistleblower, Your Call and Afterpay. Your Call has a documented process on how each Your Call representative will handle and respond to a Whistleblower and disseminate information to and from Afterpay.
 

 For reporting avenues 2-7 listed above, the review could not find a documented process on what a ‘Recipient’ should do when a Whistleblower contacts Afterpay to make a report.
 

 During the review a test email was sent to whistleblower@afterpay.com to ascertain where and by whom the email is received. The response received was an automated email stating “Your message wasn't delivered to whistleblower@afterpay.com because
 the address couldn't be found, or is unable to receive mail.”
Closure Request Justification: Procedure drafted, circulated and approved

Issue Title: No ongoing awareness of Afterpay’s Code of Conduct
Issue Description:
Recommendation
 It is recommended that Afterpay conducts an annual awareness campaign for all employees globally on the Code of Conduct.
 

 Agreed Management Action
 Notify all staff globally of the Code of Conduct and add the Code of Conduct to a compliance training module.
 

 As at 17 March 2021 VP. People ANZ was to provide an action date for the awareness campaign to be circulated to the global staff. 
 

 

 

 

 

 

 Background
 All new employees are provided with a copy of the Code of Conduct as part of induction.
 

 In November 2019 Afterpay AU engaged a third party to provide refresher training of its HR Policy & Code of Conduct (see Appendix 1) during a series of face to face sessions. In December 2019 similar sessions were delivered to employees in the US and Canada, however the Head of Compliance & MLRO UK, stated no such sessions occurred in the UK. 
 

 In August 2020 an updated Code of Conduct was approved by the Afterpay Board and was published on Workplace in the ‘People Policies’ section and on Afterpay’s public facing website. In February 2021 the Code of Conduct was added to the newly implemented intranet in the ‘Knowledge Library’ section. On 5 February 2021 Afterpay’s weekly Newsletter, which is emailed to all employees globally, made mention of the Knowledge Library but not the Code of Conduct or any specific policy. 
 

 

 Recommendation Details
 A review of GO1 training records identified that there is no stand alone training course for Afterpay’s Code of Conduct in Go1. 
 

 A review of the content of the ‘Conflicts of Interest and Anti-Bribery & Corruption’ and the ‘Workplace Health and Safety for Employees’ training courses could not identify any reference to the Code of Conduct.
 

 A review of Workplace, Newsletters and emails could not identify reference to or announcement of Afterpay’s Code of Conduct to Afterpay’s employees since the face to face training in November 2019 in Australia and New Zealand. The Head of Compliance USA confirms the same for the USA and Canada since December 2019.
 

 It is noted that the Code of Conduct was available for all staff to access via Confluence and Workplace from November 2019 to present (Knowledge Library). Further, it is also noted that the four core values of Afterpay’s Code of Conduct:
 -Keep it Real
 -Be Brave
 -Do the Right Thing
 -Shape the Future
 are displayed prominently in the footer of the weekly Newsletter as well as in various locations on Afterpays website.
 

 This review was unable to locate records of staff that had commenced employment prior to the November 2019 face to face training sessions being provided with or receiving training (including awareness training) of Afterpay’s Code of Conduct.

Issue Title: 1. Compliance Team determine a satisfactory completion rate to be observed for all compliance related training.

Issue Title: 2. Not all gifts, benefits or hospitality listed in Gift & Hospitality register(s).
Issue Description:
Recommendation 2
 It is recommended that an awareness campaign is presented to all staff of the existence of the Gifts & Hospitality Register. It should also be considered whether a targeted campaign for areas of the business with a higher likelihood of giving/receiving gifts (Eg; Sales, Marketing & Procurement staff) and those that have relationships with third party vendors.
 

 Agreed Management Action
 After finalisation of a new Gifts and Hospitality Policy, an employee training and awareness campaign will be undertaken. This campaign will also be timed in accordance with the global compliance training calendar.
 

 

 

 

 

 Finding & Recommendation Details
 A review of the Gift Register identified that whilst some staff have declared receiving gifts (mostly hospitality) there is no record of the gifts Afterpay have given to third parties. 
 

 A review of the #apd-global-21 Slack channel identified a number of gifts that were provided to ‘influencers’ during the 25-26 March Afterpay Day sales event. The messages indicate the gifts were valued at over $1000 (unknown currency).
 

 The lack of entries in the gift register for gifts given by Afterpay indicate that at this year's Afterpay Day sales gift giving process was not followed in its entirety. It is assumed that budget was set aside for these gifts and a process was undertaken to choose the recipients of the gifts. It is also noted that at least one GLT member was tagged in the slack channel and therefore this is, at the very least indicates tacit approval/knowledge of the gift giving. 
 

 Approval process
 A review of the entries in the gift register showed that no gift required formal approval as they were valued at under the $499 threshold, did not involve a public official or were a prohibited benefit.
 

 

 For noting:
 A review of the individual gifts received, mostly hospitality, was not reviewed as part of this process. By virtue that the hospitality received was documented indicates that due process was followed.
Issue Action Plans: 2. An awareness campaign is presented to all staff of the existence of the Gifts & Hospitality Register. (1001087)

Issue Title: 3. Anti-bribery and Corruption Policy be reviewed and amended to include accurate reference to where/how a staff member may record receiving or giving a gift/benefit
Issue Description:
Recommendation 3
 It is recommended that the Anti-bribery and Corruption Policy be reviewed and amended to include accurate reference to where/how a staff member may record receiving or giving a gift/benefit.
 

 Agreed Management Action
 Global Compliance will undertake a review of the Anti-Bribery and Corruption Policy. This will give consideration as to whether a separate Gifts and Entertainment Policy should be developed.
 

 

 Finding & Recommendation Details
 Despite an 80% global completion rate of the combined “Conflicts of Interest and Anti-Bribery & Corruption Training” module there are only eight entries in the register with seven coming from AU staff and one from UK staff. Email confirmation from Compliance representatives in the UK, North America and NZ confirmed that there are no known similar gift registers in those jurisdictions.
 

 Location of the Global Gifts Register
 The Global Gifts Register is located on Afterpay’s G-suite with access limited to the Compliance Team and Company Secretariat teams only. The current version of the policy does not provide direction to the reader of where to go to locate the current register. The policy does contain outdated links to a previous register which is no longer operational.
Issue Action Plans: 3. Anti-bribery and Corruption Policy be reviewed and amended to include accurate reference to where/how a staff member may record receiving or giving a gift/benefit. (1001089)

Issue Title: No centralised communication of policy approval/implementation or awareness training.
Issue Description:
Afterpay’s Global Incident Management Policy was approved by the ORC on 20 February 2020. The ORC Meeting Outstanding Action Items 20 February 2020 show the following comments against the action item “Incident Management: Develop and implement a system to record ‘company wide’ incidents.”
 Incident Management Portal ‘live’ 1st January 2020 - 5 x minor incidents logged
 Incident management Policy included in pack for ‘approval’ (Note the Policy was approved by the ORC on this day)
 Training Conducted with Customer Service, Finance, HR, Compliance
 ACTIONS FOR COMMITTEE: All Executive to please take an action to communicate ‘NEW’ policy and processes to all leadership teams, referring to Risk & Compliance for any additional support
 

 As the ‘ORC Meeting Outstanding Action Items 20 February 2020’ shows that the communication to staff of the Incident Management Policy was not centralised. A review of the Senior Manager, Monitoring & Assurance emails failed to locate an email from any Executive as to the approval or existence of the Global Incident Management Policy, the Incident Management Portal or Incident Management Processes. It is not known which staff were communicated to regarding the approval or existence of the Global Incident Management Policy, the Incident Management Portal or Incident Management Processes. 
 

 As it has been more than 12 months since the approval of the Global Incident Management Policy and with the significant increase in staffing numbers it is recommended that the Policy Owner looks to start afresh with a new communication and awareness campaign.
Issue Action Plans: 1. Following a comprehensive review of the Global Incident Management Policy, that a centralised communication and awareness training programs of work be conducted and tracked. (1001091)

Issue Title: 2.0 Global Incident Management Policy requires reviewing. Consideration should be given to consolidating the various incident/breach management policies
Issue Description:
Managing Incidents
 The Global Incident Management Policy is published as an enterprise wide policy and that the approach taken to identify, notify/log, investigate, close and conduct post incident reviews should be uniform across the business, however this is not the case. In workshops held by the Operational Risk Team in relation to a project to implement Incident Management Recording software (Protecht), it became apparent that anecdotally, incidents are managed differently by departments and not all types of incidents are managed the same way. 
 

 This may be a result of the policy not being widely publicised and training not provided outside of “Customer Service, Finance, HR, Compliance” teams. There also seems to be confusion with which policy or procedure to follow as a result of identifying an incident. During this review the following documents were identified as either approved or in draft.
 

 -Global - Incident Management Policy
 -Global - Business Resilience Policy DRAFT
 -AU - Money by Afterpay Reporting Policy DRAFT
 -UK - Clearpay Business Continuity Plan
 

 Whilst all four documents generally follow the Global Incident Management Policy Incident Management Process, there are some differences in the approach to managing an incident. For example, IT Security and Technology Operations will manage incidents differently, depending on the severity of the incident.
 

 During interviews with key staff similar comments were provided in relation to how incidents are managed. Staff commented on how they would like to see more consistency in the approach to managing incidents, greater awareness training on incident management, more rigour around escalation, prioritisation & remediation.
 

 Policy v Process - Supporting documentation
 The review also identified a lack of procedural documentation to assist staff in managing and reporting incidents. It was noted that the Privacy and It Security teams have drafted response plans to follow once a breach is identified and the Technologies Team has several procedural documents to assist in managing incidents.
 

 Whilst the Global Incident Management Policy is largely a principle based document there is some prescription as to how to manage an incident. The Money by Afterpay Reporting Policy and Clearpay Business Continuity Plan also contain significant prescription.
 

 Internal escalation/notification 
 In all documents referenced above there is a lack of succinct internal escalation reporting processes. Where reference to internal escalation processes there is a disconnect with who the incident is to be escalated to. Depending on the policy, internal escalation includes reporting to the ‘Senior Leadership Team’, a ‘Compliance Committee’ or as in the Global Incident Management Policy, ‘Advise a senior manager’.
 

 It is also noted that there is no mention of internal escalation process to inform Company Secretary (re: Continuous Disclosure obligations), Investor Relations (Re: managing investor enquiries and expectations) or Communications/Corporate Affairs (Re: timely public communications).
 

 Impact Levels
 A review of the impact levels outlined in Appendix 1 of the Global Incident Management Policy identified that there are some differences to the impact rating outlined in the Global Risk Management Framework and the impact levels currently in Protect. Consideration should be given to aligning these impact levels/ratings where possible.
Issue Rating Justification: NA
Issue Action Plans:
2. Consider amending the policy to be an Incident & Breach Reporting and Internal Notification Policy. (1001093)
 3. Policy is supported by underlying documents to assist with how to manage an incident, including who to contact and within what timeframes. (1001095)
 4. Consideration be given to align ‘impact levels’ in the Global Incident Management Policy with those listed in the Global Risk Management Framework. (1001097)

Issue Title: 3. Not all incidents are recorded in the Incident Management Portal or managed as per the Global Incident Management Policy
Issue Description:
Afterpay implemented an Incident Management Portal on 1 January 2020. The Portal, housed in Confluence, is where all global incidents are to be recorded.
 

 In late 2020 and early 2021 Afterpay moved (via soft launch) the recording of Incidents into the Protecht system.
 Identification: incidents have been reported and classified appropriately
 A review of the Incident Registers identified that some incidents are being reported however a disparity in the numbers of incidents was identified.
 Each month the Director, Enterprise Risk Governance and Operational Risk requests from the Privacy and Information Security Teams (amongst others) for the number of incidents identified each month. This data is maintained in the 2021 Ops Risk Dashboard. 
 

 * A reconciliation of the 2021 Ops Risk Dashboard, Incident Management Portal and Protecht identified the recording of Privacy and Information Security incidents that occured in January and February 2021. This reconciliation identified the following:
 

 One of the reasons for this disparity in the numbers of matters is likely due to the number of platforms being used to record and manage incidents. For example, the Technologies Operations Team utilises the #productions-incident Slack channel as a platform to raise and manage incidents where the Privacy Team uses Confluence. It is apparent that not all of these matters are being captured on the 2021 Ops Risk Dashboard, confluence or protecht. Another reason for this is that where an incident is managed by the Technology Operations team, it may from time to time also elicit an incident involving, for example, Privacy and IT Security Teams. The Privacy Team enters an incident into Confluence, however the IT Security Team may, in some instances, not raise an incident as it is being managed through the #productios-incident slack channel process.
 

 Investigated: incidents have been investigated and corrective/preventative actions completed in full
 The review did not identify an incident that was not fully investigated or where corrective/preventative actions are incomplete. However it is noted that some incidents remain active.
 

 Post Incident Reviews (PIR) have been completed and stored for major incidents
 Through interviews with the Director, Technologies Operations, Senior Manager, Privacy and Security Governance, Risk and Compliance Manager it was identified that not all incidents received a post incident review. In all of these three areas PIRs are conducted for only the more severe incidents. However, where a PIR is conducted in all circumstances they are stored in various locations. The PIRs are stored in the Slack channels (see example) for Some IT Security and Technology incidents and in Confluence for Privacy incidents.
Issue Action Plans: 5. Global implementation of the centralised Protecht incident reporting system is prioritised (1001099)

Issue Title: There are users that have access within Microsoft Dynamics to post and approve the same journal entry, with no periodic review performed
Issue Description:
When Microsoft Dynamics was implemented, there were a number of individuals within the Finance team who were provided with access to create and approve the same journal entry within Microsoft Dynamics. When this was the case, verbal approval was obtained prior to posting the journal.
 It was noted that there are still users that have access to post and approve the same journal entry within Microsoft Dynamics. Management have noted that this functionality isn’t really used, aside from when posting reclassification journals.
 We have been unable to obtain a system generated listing of users with the ability to both create and approve journals; however, management have indicated that there are approximately five individuals with this access within the Finance team. It was noted that there is no periodic review that has been implemented as a control to identify instances where
 journals have been created and approved by the same individual. It was also noted that an access listing of users with the ability to create and approve journal entries is not formally reviewed on a periodic basis.
 We have been unable to obtain manual journal data that includes the poster and approver in order to quantify this observation. 
 Management should perform a review over access rights as it relates to manual journals. Where a user is required to have access to both post and approve the same journal, the rationale for this should be formally documented, and a periodic review should be established to ensure that the journal is accurate
Issue Action Plans:

Issue Title: Commentary into period on period movements as part of the movement analysis performed within the EFS reporting spreadsheet should be clearly documented.
Issue Description:
Once the EFS reporting spreadsheet has been prepared by the ANZ Financial Controller, the Asia Pacific CFO will review the disclosures, and discuss significant movements by performing a month on month (or quarter on quarter) movement analysis.
 Through review of the APRA reporting spreadsheet, and discussions with management, it was noted that this analysis is primarily done verbally during a meeting between the ANZ Financial Controller and the Group Financial Controller.
 Management should enhance the level of control documentation and rigour surrounding the review of significant movements as part of the movement analysis to ensure that commentary is clearly documented, where required. This should include:
 • Establishment of a defined threshold for ‘significant movements’ requiring investigation as part of the movement analysis
 • Commentary provided within the spreadsheet for all movements above the defined threshold, as well as any unusual balances
 Afterpay should consider the data quality attributes and thresholds in RPG702 to ensure the precision of the controls meets APRA's expectations.
Engagement Title: EFS Review - Economic and Financial Statistics (1000284)
Issue Action Plans:
2.1 'Significant movement' threshold established and policy documented (1001538)
 2.2 Control documentation increased surrounding movement analysis within the APRA reporting spreadsheet (1001540)

Issue Title: Consider implementation of a checklist to evidence key control activities within the process to prepare, review, and submit the EFS reporting to APRA
Issue Description:
There are multiple steps performed within the process to prepare, review, and submit the EFS reporting to APRA. A number of these checks are currently performed as part of the process to update the EFS reporting spreadsheet.
 Management should consider the implementation of a checklist to clearly document the completion of key control activities. Whilst we understand the activities (as noted below) are steps within the process to prepare the EFS reporting spreadsheet, they are currently not specifically defined as controls within the EFS policy and are not always clearly documented as
 control activities within the EFS reporting spreadsheet. A checklist would be a simple way to demonstrate that each of the control checks has been performed and could be included in the overall review of the reporting spreadsheet prior to submission.
 This checklist should include key activities, such as:
 • Review of the mapping file from the prior period to ensure it is updated for the current period where needed, for instances including:
 o Where a new account has been created
 o Where something unusual has happened during the period (e.g. where a bank account went into overdraft and rather than being mapped to cash the overdraft needs to be mapped as a liability)
 • Check to ensure that the APRA balance sheet balances
 • Reconciliation of data included within the spreadsheet against the Trial Balance
 • Review of non-accounting data (HR/payroll information, and resident/non-resident split).
Engagement Title: EFS Review - Economic and Financial Statistics (1000284)
Issue Action Plans: 3.1 EFS reporting checklist (1001543)

Issue Title: Lack of formalised policies and/or procedures detailing the process and required approvals for comarketing commitments and payments including documented roles and responsibilities has resulted in inconsistent practices
Issue Description:
Despite management being able to clearly articulate the process for co-marketing commitments / payments and relevant approvals, there are no documented global policies, procedures or frameworks detailing key requirements, approvals and documentation retention requirements for the co-marketing process.
 Additionally, roles and responsibilities across the co-marketing process including key handover points between business units have not been formally documented. Whilst it was noted that stakeholders interviewed had a good understanding of roles and responsibilities and the key handover points, this was not reflected in any formalised documentation. Additionally, it was noted that the marketing team is not required to be involved in determining the co-marketing commitment amount.
 

 Due to the lack of formalised process and policy documentation, it was noted that there are inconsistent practices occurring across the co-marketing process between regions. This includes inconsistencies in the determination of the co-marketing commitment amount, the use of insertion orders (agreement between the merchant and Afterpay on the specifics details for each campaign) and the process to review a campaign and capture any lessons learned. We conducted sample testing across 9 merchants from a range of regions to trace transactions through the entire comarketing process. An overview of the sample testing findings is detailed below, with details at an individual merchant level
 included in Appendix C, including the value of each sample. Sample testing noted the following inconsistencies with articulated co-marketing processes:
 • For 5 out of 9 merchant samples selected, no documentation was provided to evidence the determination and/or establishment of the co-marketing commitment figure.
 • For 3 out of 9 merchants sampled, approval of the co-marketing commitment figure prior to incorporation into the contract could not be evidenced.
 • For 4 out of 9 merchants sampled, evidence of Afterpay approval of all co-marketing spend prior to campaign could not be evidenced.
 For one merchant with a co-marketing value of £1,954,909, documentation was very limited due to the co-marketing agreement pre-dating the majority of the current team. As a result, no evidence was provided to support:
 • Any insertion orders completed
 • Afterpay approval of any co-marketing spend prior to campaign
 • Completed reporting templates for campaign
 • Evidence of campaign review and/or lessons learned
 • Finance tracking of actual co-marketing spend
 • Any evidence of purchase orders raised and corresponding approvals.
 It should be noted that dependent on the specific merchant, Afterpay may have differing levels of ability to enforce processes such as being able to drive co-marketing activities and selection of media suppliers. As such, a nuanced approach should be enacted, for example, through the use of merchant tiering or key account planning.
Issue Rating Justification: TBD
Issue Action Plans:
1.1 Global Co-Marketing Policy (1001545)
 1.2 Implement management control to enforce compliance with the Co-Marketing Policy (1001547)
Closure Request Justification: This issue was identified before the Afterpay acquisition and may no longer be relevant/applicable since processes have changed. The closure of this issue has been aligned with Internal Audit and the relevant stakeholders

Issue Title: The process, including key considerations, for determining the co-marketing commitment amount with merchants prior to contract signature should be formally defined
Issue Description:
Prior to original contract signature with a merchant, the Sales team is responsible for determining and agreeing both the rate and the co-marketing commitment amount with the merchant for incorporation into the contract. These amounts are currently approved by the Head of Sales via email, however management advised that this will occur using Salesforce
 moving forward. A pricing matrix which considers the profitability of the merchant and subsequent revenue for Afterpay is used as a tool to determine the co-marketing figure for inclusion in the signed contract.
 

 Similarly, Client Success is responsible for determining the ongoing co-marketing commitment amount for contract renewals. Like initial co-marketing amounts, these are approved via email by the Head of Client Success prior to incorporation into a renewed contract. Management indicated that the Client Success teams determine the co-marketing
 commitment figure by utilising the pricing matrix (consistent with new agreements) and based on the prior contract, the expected revenue in a given year. It is noted that the marketing team are currently not formally involved in the determination of the co-marketing commitment amount for new contracts or for contract renewals.
 

 Despite this, sample testing across nine (9) merchants noted that for only four commitments use of the pricing matrix to determine the co-marketing figure was evidenced.
 We were unable to evidence any formal documentation defining key considerations and associated weightings when establishing the co-marketing commitment amount. Additionally, management advised that there is no formal benchmarking process occurring to ensure that merchants of the same size, value, ROI and/or exclusivity arrangements are benchmarked against each other to drive consistency.
 

 Sample testing across 9 merchants noted the following:
 • For 4 out of 9 merchant samples selected, the global pricing matrix had been used to determine profitability of the merchant and subsequent revenue for Afterpay.
 • For the remaining 5 out of 9 merchant samples selected, no documentation was provided to evidence the determination and/or establishment of the co-marketing commitment figure.
 • Additionally, for 3 out of 9 merchants sampled, approval of the co-marketing figure prior to incorporation into the contract could not be evidenced.
 

 For specific sample testing details at an individual merchant level (including value) refer to Appendix C.
Issue Action Plans:
2.1 Develop process and set of key considerations for determining marketing commitment amount (1001549)
 2.2 Implement a management control to enforce compliance with co-marketing commitment process (1001551)
 2.3 Centralised merchant and campaign benchmarking system (1001553)
Closure Request Justification: Legacy Afterpay Issue all actions closed

Issue Title: Lack of validation occurring over merchant invoices including evidence of original invoices where third party media suppliers are involved
Issue Description:
Co-marketing campaigns are either controlled by Afterpay or the merchant depending on the agreement and relationship between Afterpay and the merchant. For example, Management advised that some bigger merchants prefer to control the campaign through their own media suppliers rather than utilising Afterpay’s, whereas others prefer Afterpay to drive the
 campaign.
 

 For those campaigns controlled by the merchant, there is no validation occurring over merchant invoices to ensure invoices reflect actual costs incurred. This includes cross-checking of original invoices where third party media suppliers are involved.
 • For example, Afterpay may receive an invoice from a merchant or that merchant’s media agency for TV spots, but there is no oversight of the invoice that originated from the third-party media supplier (e.g. Channel 10) for these spots.
 • Further, merchants are not required to provide a breakdown of the co-marketing amount for each campaign detailing the costs incurred by Afterpay and those incurred by the merchant.
 

 Sample testing across 9 merchants noted the following:
 • For 6 out of 9 merchants sampled, no validation or assurance was evidenced against merchant invoices submitted to Afterpay.
 o Further, it is noted that a number of invoices have been submitted for whole numbers (e.g. two invoices for $1,250,000) with no breakdown as to what that amount includes.
 • For the remaining 3 out of 9 merchants sampled, there has been no spend to date for any validation to occur.
 

 For specific sample testing details at an individual merchant level refer to Appendix C.
 

 There is an opportunity to conduct sample audits to ensure that the costs that Afterpay incur correspond to the costs incurred by the merchant and/or their agencies.
Impacted Legal Entities:
Afterpay Australia Pty Ltd
 Afterpay NZ Limited
Issue Rating Justification: TBD
Issue Action Plans:
3.1 Merchant agreements to contain provisions to allow APT the right to audit media supplier costs (1001555)
 3.2 Develop process to conduct periodic audits of merchant invoices submitted to Afterpay (co-marketing) (1001557)

Issue Title: There is limited reporting over the effectiveness and performance against key metrics in relation to campaigns
Issue Description:
i) Merchant reporting on performance of campaigns
 It is noted that some merchants are contractually required to provide reporting to Afterpay before invoices are processed. However, this reporting is limited to population of a number of metrics on a Google sheet including; reach, link clicks, impressions, amount spent and revenue. Some merchants provide more in-depth reporting to Afterpay; however, this differs
 on a merchant by merchant basis and is not consistent. Some merchants do not provide any reporting prior to invoice. There is no validation or supporting documentation uploaded to evidence the reporting metrics provided. Further, reporting metrics for merchants are not collaborated into a central document for tracking, monitoring and benchmarking purposes. 
 

 Sample testing noted the following:
 • For 3 out of 9 merchants sampled, detailed reporting has been evidenced for each campaign however this is not in the google sheet template.
 • For 1 out of 9 merchants, no reporting could be evidenced due to campaigns pre-dating the majority of the current team and as a result documentation is limited.
 • For 4 out of 9 merchants sampled, either the co-marketing spend has not commenced or the campaign is still in progress meaning no reporting is due yet.
 

 For specific sample testing details at an individual merchant level refer to Appendix C.
 

 ii) Afterpay reporting on effectiveness of campaigns
 Additionally, there is limited reporting from an Afterpay perspective on the effectiveness of marketing campaigns, key lessons learned and recommendations going forward. The Quarterly Marketing Business Review highlights learnings under the partner marketing model objectives and key results (‘OKR’), however this is from a global view and does not comment on specific campaigns for each merchant. 
 

 Sample testing noted the following:
 • For 3 out of 9 merchants sampled, no reporting from an Afterpay perspective on the effectiveness of merchant campaigns could be evidenced.
 • For 5 out of 9 merchants sampled, either the co-marketing spend has not commenced or the campaign is still in progress meaning no reporting is due yet.
 

 For specific sample testing details at an individual merchant level refer to Appendix C.
 

 Management noted in interviews that they are aware of the lack of internal reporting on merchant campaigns and are in the process of developing this going forward
Issue Rating Justification: Rating provided by PwC
Issue Action Plans:
4.1 Merchant reporting required to evidence co-marketing spend (1001559)
 4.2 Centralised system to collate merchant reporting (1001561)
 4.3 Consistent reporting on effectiveness of merchant campaigns (1001563)

Issue Title: Oversight and management of credit process and model requires strengthening
Issue Description:
Our review noted that key activities to identify and understand credit risk appetite, assign responsibility, assess and monitor credit process and model have not been fully formalised, documented and embedded. Specifically:
 

 1 The Customer Risk Policy should be reviewed regularly consulting a broader range of key stakeholders The prescribed Risk Committee quarterly review of the Customer Risk Policy has not been conducted since the policy was introduced in June 2020, albeit being outlined in the customer risk policy. There is no formalised requirement to consult with key stakeholders such as Operations or Treasury to change the credit risk settings. For example, Treasury should be informed of significant changes to the credit risk settings for communications to the Funder, but are not listed as a stakeholder in the Customer Risk Policy. Experiments of changes to the account limits (“experiments”) require customer service to be informed to answer customer queries. The Operations stakeholders consulted in our review were not aware of the Customer Risk Policy fee waiver thresholds in place, indicating that the Customer Risk Policy may not be widely known or understood, presenting compliance risks.
 

 2 The following core management responsibilities require inclusion in the Customer Risk Policy:
 – Detailed roles and responsibilities (e.g. RACI matrix) which maps out the tasks, milestone, monitoring deliverables and key decision;
 – Ownership and management of the account limit model;
 – Credit risk monitoring activities against tolerances defined within the Policy
 – Alignment with regulatory requirements or guidance. For example, the maximum limit of the Buy Now Pay Later Code of Conduct.
Issue Action Plans: 1 Review Customer Risk Policy (1001565)
Closure Request Justification: All actions have been closed. Await PWC independent assessment

Issue Title: Duplicate Accounts
Issue Description:
Compliance Monitoring & Testing Team perform monitoring of duplicate accounts and are preparing for assessment of Afterpay’s adherence to the BNPL Code of Conduct and Design and Distribution Obligations in Australia. Our conclusion reached is that Afterpay has control limitations that permit a consumer to hold more than one Afterpay account. Analysis indicates that between ~4.1K and ~129K customers in Australia and globally between ~61K and ~479K hold more than one Afterpay account as @ 11 July 2021.
 

 There are risk considerations relating to regulatory expectations and obligations (Australia only); (globally) financial risk; contractual risk and harm to our customers.
 

 In Australia, there are requirements under the BNPL Code of Practice (effective 1 March 2021) to: 
 1. Not provide additional products or new transactional amounts without first completing suitability checks e.g being up to date with repayments and not being a vulnerable customer.
 2. Conduct additional checks such as income and expense (or credit) checks when a customer’s total balance exceeds a total loan value of $3,001 AUD (as at June 2021, based on current logic there are 415 AU accounts with total credit limits exceeding $3,001 AUD but this number will continue to increase without adequate controls).
 3. Under new Design and Distribution Obligations (DDO) (effective October 2021), Afterpay is required to develop and publicly display a Target Market Determination (TMD). Our proposed TMD contains a commitment which states: “A customer may not be onboarded if they were previously an Afterpay customer”. Consequences of non-compliance with DDO and distributing to customers outside a TMD can attract civil and criminal penalties. ASIC is looking at very closely at this area and we have been in discussions with ASIC who have provided us with guidance on the content of our TMD. 
 As a result, there is a risk that we will not meet our Australian regulatory obligations under the BNPL Code and DDO (when DDO comes into effect).
 While not directly linked to regulatory expectations or obligations in other markets, Afterpay also needs to consider whether the prevalence of these same issues in those locations, amongst other things, may be leading to customer harm and whether this issue may create external scrutiny and potential reputational damage.
Issue Action Plans:
Establishment of appropriate controls (1001833)
 Scope and implementation approach to solutions (1001835)
 Remediation Approach (1001837)
Closure Request Justification: closed related action 1001837 on 10 August 2022.

Issue Title: There is insufficient oversight and preliminary assessment of new products, initiatives and changes to identify the need for privacy advice in a timely manner
Issue Description:
Through discussion with management and upon inspection of sampled documentation, it was observed that Afterpay has established a robust Privacy by Design Framework through which privacy impact assessments (PIAs), Privacy by Design (PbD) Checklists and privacy threshold assessments are conducted for initiatives and products that involve personal information and require privacy advice.
 

 When projects are brought to the attention of the Privacy or Legal teams for review, the analysis of privacy risks is thorough, and proportionate to the complexity and sensitivity of projects. Threshold assessments are short form analyses of key indicators of privacy risk used to determine whether data privacy regulations would apply to an initiative and if a full privacy impact assessment is warranted. A privacy impact assessment (or Data Protection Impact Assessment, for E.U. GDPR and UK geographies) documents projects and analyses privacy risks in detail and makes recommendations to mitigate any unaddressed risks. The Legal Team is engaged as part of the product development process providing legal advisory
 services documented in JIRA ticket, where legal sign-off is required before a product is launched.
 

 However, while the Privacy by Design Framework is well designed, the operation of this control involves a number of gaps. There is insufficient oversight and assessment of new products, initiatives and changes across the organisation to identify whether privacy advice is required. This limits the operationalisation of the Privacy by Designed Framework and
 administration of adequate privacy advice at an appropriate stage of the product development and/or change lifecycle. No formal checks are applied via a change approval process to ensure required privacy due diligence and risk analysis have been undertaken.
 

 Review of product development roadmaps for the last financial year as compared with available privacy threshold assessments, design checklists and impact assessments show that a number of projects and initiatives may not have been brought to the attention of the privacy or legal teams for review, or were not raised early enough in the development
 lifecycle to provide upfront advice on privacy requirements. To the extent that roadmap projects could be reconciled with completed impact assessments and legal reviews, it appears that up to half of projects undertaken within FY21 may not have been assessed by relevant subject matter experts for privacy risk. For example, projects such as ‘Consumer Portal
 Personalisation’, ‘Curated Product Collections’ and ‘Product Matching’ do not have a recorded legal review, threshold assessment nor privacy impact assessment. The absence of a completed threshold assessment suggests the applicability of privacy regulations to these projects may not have been considered or brought to the attention of an appropriate SME.
Engagement Title: Privacy and Data Protection Compliance (follow up to FY20 review) (1000365)
Issue Action Plans:
1. Product Governance model (product development lifecycle and business model) (1002324)
 2. Privacy by Design Framework (1002326)

Issue Title: The practices for Quality Assurance and Monitoring can be improved to increase the effectiveness of customer service delivery and oversight.
Issue Description:
our testing identified deficiencies in the CSO effectiveness for delivering Standard Operating Procedures (SOP) and SEDT completion of QA.
 CSO Effectiveness - SOP Testing
 A 48% effectiveness rate for CSO adherence when delivering SOP consisting of 30% voice and 18% digital. One contributing factor for this effectiveness score is evidenced from our testing of complaints whereby;
 81% (17/21) of complaints requiring escalation, were not processed. 
 60% (24/40) of complaints status and resolution outcomes were not completed. 
 SEDT & OTPP Effectiveness - QA Testing
 A 70% effectiveness rate for SEDT completion of QA consisting of 34% voice and 36% digital. One contributing factor for this effectiveness score is evidenced from our testing of the SEDT and OTPP graders assessment for financial hardship/move payments, whereby the;
 Grader effectiveness is 61% (39 from 64 assessments) consisting of voice 36% and digital 25%. 
 When comparing grader effectiveness of 61% with the CSO effectiveness 44% (22 from 50 tickets) for delivering the same SOP, there is an opportunity to improve the effectiveness for both the CSO adherence to the delivery of the SOP and the graders assessments. 
 Other contributing factors to the overall effectiveness of the QA program are; 
 52% of CSO’s were not assigned the monthly monitoring quotas (3 tickets per week for existing agents) for the months of August and September. (leave and training was not included in this percentage)
 For SOP grading purposes, the questions on the QA grading form is limited to asking “did the team member follow all business processes relevant to the situation”. As there are no specific questions or references to the SOP being graded, this design may not be supportive of assisting the Grader in assessing against SOP.  
 Customer Experience (CE) has no visibility to the close the loop activities for OTPP CSO’s resulting from the QA activity E.g feedback, coaching and/or remediation. 
 CE has no periodic analysis (overall) of grading outcomes to support identification and correction of broader issues that may not be apparent in everyday CSO grading.
 

 Monitoring 
 CE does not monitor L1 and L2 complaint toll gates on a day-to-day basis. Two complaint samples were identified where no updates were provided to the customer until the 6th and 12th business days.
 CE has no oversight/visibility of the automatically generated customer correspondence e.g., Payment Arrangements. As a result, there is no assurance that the letter is sent and that the content reflects the agreed payment arrangement.
Engagement Title: M&T Q2 FY22 – Customer Experience Review (1000465)
Issue Action Plans:
CSO QA Ticket Quotas & Oversight of CSO Feedback. (1002423)
 QA Training Program (1002425)

 The SOP content for the reinstatement of previous financial hardship, payment arrangement and written off accounts is misaligned resulting in variances within process delivery. E.g. The UK SOP for written off accounts has not been updated from 90 to 180 days when assessing written off accounts for reinstatement.
