Issue Title: The European Union Customer Experience (CE) Standard Operating Procedures (SOP) are not commensurate with the service delivery and there are deficiencies in the content and adherence to Regional CE SOP.
Issue Description:
The EU HC SOP library contains one SOP and the regional HC SOP libraries have some deficiencies which is resulting in the EU and regional CE not being delivered in line with expected CE standards.
 For example, we identified an EU CSO requesting a customer to provide their identification to close their account, however upon escalation to the local team leader (‘TL’), the TL determined that the identification was not required. Notwithstanding there may be plausible regional/local variances for account closure requirements, in the absence of a suitable SOP, the CE will not be delivered and aligned with CE standards.
 Further testing of the CE SOP, Macros and procedures identified procedural improvements and content updates to improve adherence to the CE service delivery; 
 Regional SOP
 “Move payments/Change/Defer payment date/Up to 21 days” – Update wording to distinguish between the 21 maximum rule and the 4-request in 90 days rule. 
 ANZ/US SOP
 “Vulnerable Customers” SOP – Update to include escalation processes where self-harm or threats to life have occurred. This could be similar to the established UK SOP.
 ANZ SOP
 There is no SOP for assisting agents to explain to customers the timing of applying a late fee. The 1 day grace period was removed in ANZ mid year.
 Regional Macro
  “Payment Arrangement: We need some more information” – Update wording to notify the customer that stopped payments will be redrawn after 48 hours. 
 UK & Regional Complaints Procedures
 We identified that;
 Level 2 UK Escalations is not taking custody of escalated complaints from Level 0 (LO) – Digital and Level 1 (L1) Voice. Rather L2 is communicating with LO & L1 through messaging resulting in incorrect complaint resolution(s). 
 Two complaints where the ticket was closed incorrectly, where further investigation/escalation was pending.
 

 Further, we suggest the below will assist with ensuring procedural adherence to CE SOP: 
 Duplicate complaint tickets should be reviewed by the CSO prior to closure to ensure that the original complaint is being managed in accordance with Standard Operating Procedures. 
 A review of complaints classified as Impasse to identify skill gaps and improve complaint resolution outcomes
 

 Regional Letter of Authority (LOA) Procedures & SOP
 We identified;
 

 Three instances where the minimum customer criteria was not evident and one instance where an LOA was signed and dated in 2017, however these LOA’s were accepted. Therefore we suggest that the acceptance criteria for LOA’s should be updated in the SOP.
 Three instances where the LOA admin portal notes were incomplete. We suggest that CE should implement a distinguished labelling for noting adding/removing an LOA on the Admin Portal. 
 LOA’s whereby the photocopied identification was not redacted prior to ticket closure. 
 

 Regional Manual Identification Procedures 
 In testing of 25 samples, we identified;: 
 44% (11/25) where the supplied identification/verification documentation were not redacted. 
 16% (4/25) where the privacy terms were not provided and no consent was obtained. 
 

 Regional Financial hardship Procedures & SOP
 In testing of 19 voice samples whereby a hardship payment arrangement was created, we identified 100% defect rate for the agent not asking/confirming the customers’ preferred card payment. We noted that the requirement to ask this question was not included in the ‘How to create a payment arrangement’ SOP.
 Afterpay’s commitment to supporting customers in financial hardship includes understanding and planning that best suits a customer’s circumstances and needs. Testing noted 20 defects from 40 hardships sampled highlighting where: 
 CSO’s did not demonstrate an understanding of, or an accurate response to the customer’s situation.
 CSO’s were involved in the interaction with the customer, and did not adequately review original (preceding) interactions with the account holder resulting in a negative customer experience.
 CSO’s included additional freelance responses in the Macro (customer written communication), which were out of place and/or inaccurate. 
 Process enhancements
 

 Notifying the customer of the full outstanding balance prior to setting up the arrangement. 
 Macro “Payment:First,Second,Third request to change payment in past 3 months” – Standardize the order level information for inclusion in the macro.
Engagement Title: M&T Q2 FY22 – Customer Experience Review (1000465)

SOPS for EU all updated during migration to single platform to ensure consistency between EU and UK.

A heavy global focus on QA has lifted competency and we are seeing a reduction in variation of actual approach versus documented standard process, in addition the QA. folks are showing results that generate significant reduction in variation to the L2 sample reviews which shows we are now more consistent than before.

Issue Title: The Debt Collections Agencies can improve their compliance performance reporting and their quality assurance activities.
Issue Description:
1. The DCA’s reporting has varying levels of compliance content for each compliance category within the DCA MPR/QBR reporting.
 ANZ 
 Collection House (CLH)Business Review (BR) Q3 September provided commentary for increased complaint volume by one for April when compared to March.
 Probe Monthly Performance Review (MPR) October Quality Assurance (QA) and Q3 QBR for September displays the QA percentage scores, however there is no reference to the volumes of QA conducted, similar to CLH, in order for the audience to better understand the overall sampling size and exception rates. 
 US/CA
 First Source MPR and Q3 QBR October 21 does not include information relating to quality assurance and complaints. 
 Indebted MPR October 21 for Canada and the US does not include information for complaints.
 UK
 Intrum CMS Q3 BR indicates that no complaints have been received in the previous 12 months (slide 20) however the current period metric indicates that there have been 11 complaints received (slide 18). Also, Breathing Space is being reported as a dispute/query. 
 

 2. The DCA’s do not have a standardized approach to their quality assurance activities. Their quota levels of QA monitoring and testing activities vary as do the types of QA activities by channel - calls/emails.  
 US/CA
 Indebted complete QA for one step in a process and capture this as ‘one’ QA completed. e.g a customer raises a concern via email. Indebted would QA their first response to this, however would not review subsequent emails to ascertain if the customer's query has been processed correctly and in full.
 Indebted QA quota’s indicate a requirement to complete 5 calls per agent per month. 
 Their Canada MPR for October 21 indicates that 13 calls were received, however only one call was QA’d.
 MPR October 21 US - 53 calls were received however no calls were QA’d.
 A similar theme exists for the QA in the Q3 QBR October reporting.  
 UK
 The Intrum QA form has a different format than other DCA forms. The call outcome is based on a Pass/Fail rather than an overall percentage score. Our testing of 10 graded samples identified; 
 Four samples whereby the section for ‘case type - portability’ was not completed by the grader;
 Six samples where the grader noted ‘passed' for the question - “was the Direct Debit Guarantee offered to the customer with interest and charges explained…” however, no direct debit payment or arrangement was completed.
Issue Action Plans:
Uplift QA Reporting (1002872)
 Uplift Compliance Reporting (1002874)
 Uplift Complaints Reporting (1002876)
 Indebted to Uplift their QA approach (1002878)
 Intrum to uplift their QA processes (1002880)

Issue Title: There are no formalized External Dispute Resolution (EDR) procedures for some regions and the processing of EDR complaints can be improved.
Issue Description:
Our testing identified improvements for the below framework and processes elements for handling EDR complaints. 
 ANZ - A review of 15 ANZ EDR complaints processed identified 9 process exceptions;
 Six whereby notes and/or collections exclusion date(s) were not actioned on a customers account
 Three whereby Line 1 or 2 continued to communicate with a customer during the investigation stage of an EDR complaint. 
 US - A review of 14 US complaints identified six low level exceptions specifically related to the quality of information provided in response to complaints. Whilst, we acknowledge that responses to the BBB are kept generic due to being published in the public domain, the responses to the customers would benefit from being more pertinent to address their concerns raised. 
 US - No SOPS are published on the US Help Center for the handling of EDR complaints. Our testing of 14 samples identified that standard procedures similar to ANZ EDR processes are not being completed such as;
 A customer's account is not noted when a EDR complaint is received and closed (albeit notes are placed for unfreezing an account or good will gestures ). 
 Collections exclusions are not placed on the account notes. 
 US - There are no case files recorded and retained for CFPB complaints managed by the Compliance team.  
 EU - For Italy and Spain EDR complaints, we met with the Senior Legal Counsels who provided us with a comprehensive understanding of the process and requirements for responding to EDR Complaints (known as “SAC”). To date, Clearpay has not received any SAC complaints. The global complaints policy states that “All SAC Complaints must be resolved by Clearpay (Europe) Legal & Compliance Team”. Notwithstanding this, we expect that the operational teams will have carriage of the investigations and resolution of these complaints and therefore consideration should be given for the establishment of standard operating procedures, so in the future, operational staff can have procedures for processing complaints to meet the regulatory requirements.
Issue Action Plans:
Complaint Standard Operations Feedback (1002884)
 Uplift US Complaint Procedures (1002888)
 European Standard Operating Procedures (1002894)

Issue Title: Clearpay UK Legal Breathing Space (BS) requests are not being completed in accordance with the processing timelines set out in the Debt Respite Scheme (DRS) regulations and the Afterpay BS Standard Operating Procedures can be updated to meet the standards of the DRS regulations.
Issue Description:
Despite this, our testing identified Clearpay processes do not meet the requirements of the Debt Respite Scheme Regulations.
 Section 2.9 of the regulations requires that “A breathing space (legal) will start the day after the debtor’s details are put onto the breathing space register…”
 Our testing indicated that 10 of 25 legal breathing space samples reviewed were processed greater than one and up to 8 business days post receipt of the notification from the register. By not processing and commencing breathing space - one day following receipt of the debtors details, Afterpay may not comply with Section 2.9 (herein) and Section 3.1 which requires a “stop…..on fees, charges..repayments….any contact….or recovery action….”. 
 

 Further review of the UK Breathing Space SOPS identified opportunities to update the procedures to align and adhere to the regulations; 
 

 Section 3.1 requires that “If you have only received notification about one debt, but you are owed two debts then you should consider applying the protections…(per section 3.3) to both debts and, in all cases, you should tell the debt adviser about the additional debt. It is the debt adviser who will decide if the additional debt qualifies for the breathing space.”
 

 Clearpay UK registers 120 duplicate customer accounts on average per month however the APT SOPs do not require a search for additional (duplicate) customer accounts, in addition to those accounts advised to us by the IS. 
 

 Section 3.16 requires that “... Once a standard (legal) breathing space starts, the debtor cannot ask for it to be canceled”
 

 However, the APT Breathing space SOP indicates that a customer or third party can cancel at any time i.e it does not clearly explain that a customer can cancel a non legal breathing space and a debt advisor can cancel a legal breathing space.
 Section 3.2 titled “If you have sold the debt” requires that Afterpay would notify the creditor of the breathing space and the IS of the creditors contact details. In practice APT does not sell their accounts to a creditor, however does practice the principles of section 3.2 with the DCA by notifying the DCA of the Breathing Space request. This is not documented within the BS SOP
Issue Action Plans:
Breathing Space procession and controls (1002905)
 Breathing Space uplift for standard operating procedures (1002907)
 Regulatory Change (1002909)

Issue Title: Customers are transacting without completing IDV/KYC
The Afterpay Buy Now Pay Later (BNPL) Code Obligations Register does not record all processes and controls for compliance with BNPL code requirements.
Issue Description:
Background
 The Buy Now Pay Later (BNPL) code (“the code”) requirements and Afterpay’s process and controls to record compliance with the code are set out in the Afterpay BNPL Code Obligations Register. 
 Operational and enabling business units are responsible for a material proportion of the process and controls for compliance with the code requirements. Select processes and controls are formalized within surrounding Afterpay frameworks and policies e.g The Global Complaints Policy, Anti-Money Laundering/Counter Terror Financing Program and Marketing Policy. Presently, Afterpay Compliance are progressing key initiatives and actions in relation to Afterpay’s ongoing compliance with the BNPL Code.
 Issue
 A review of the Afterpay BNPL Code Obligations Register, which documents all BNPL code requirements, identified that not all established processes and/or controls are recorded to demonstrate compliance with the code requirements.

Issue Title: The formalized timeframes for processing European Union (‘EU’) Standard Due Diligence (SDD) for High and Medium Risk customers is not being achieved.
Issue Description:
Our testing identified that the EU CO team is not issuing the request for ID and/or SOF from customers within five days and the EU AML team is not blocking accounts within 20 days of that request.  
 47% of the 411 customers (279 High and 132 Medium Risk) were contacted within the 5 day timeline. 
 60% of the 346 customers who did not provide ID/SOF (252 High and 94 Medium Risk) had their accounts blocked within the 20 day timeline from the date of the EU CO customer communication. 
 We understand that there has been a renewed focus on adherence to the processing timelines since April 2022. Upon review of alerts processing in April and May 2022, there was a 29% adherence rate, 13 of 45 communications sent within the 5 day processing timeline and 47% adherence, 16 of 34 accounts blocked within the 20 day timeline. 
 

 As noted in the background, the formalized timelines for processing SDD are inconsistent. The “Hard ID for High and Medium risks” SOP provides the EU AML team with an additional five days to process an account block i.e up to 20 days from when the EU CO team request a customer to provide ID and/or SOF, when compared to the EU Clearpay Consumer Onboarding SOP which provides up to 15 days.
Engagement Title: M&T Q2 22 – Merchant Onboarding and AML Quality Assurance Program Review (1002180)
Issue Action Plans:
Update the EU Clearpay Consumer Onboarding Standard Operating Procedures to provide up to 20 days for processing for Standard Due Diligence customer reviews (1011338)
 Implement a Quality Assurance (QA) process that reviews the processing of Standard Due Diligence for High and Medium Risk customers.  (1011980)
 Implement a Quality Assurance (QA) process that reviews the processing of Standard Due Diligence for High and Medium Risk customers. (1011982)

Issue Title: Clearpay UK merchants are not publishing Clearpay marketing collateral in accordance with the Clearpay UK Marketing Legal Guide on their websites and social media channels.
Issue Description:
From a sample of 50 merchants, Monitoring and Testing identified that Merchants were not publishing Clearpay marketing collateral on their website in accordance with the Clearpay Marketing Legal Guide; 
 4 of 50 (8%) merchants did not display the Clearpay terms and conditions link where the Clearpay payment service is being promoted.
 4 of 50 (8%) merchants displayed the incorrect/no Clearpay disclaimer. This consisted of 3 incorrect disclaimers when promoting Clearpay as ‘no interest’ and one where they did not display a disclaimer for a Clearpay banner ad.
 4 of 50 (8%) merchants created their own version of Clearpay frequently asked questions (FAQ’s) which varies from the approved Clearpay FAQ’s content, published for merchants on the Clearpay developer portal. 
 9 of 50 (18%) merchants used wording, which may misrepresent the product or may be considered as false and misleading.
 6 of 50 (12%) merchants who referenced Clearpay in their social media posts between January and June 2022, did not use disclaimers and/or links to Clearpay's terms and conditions. 
 Note: 12 of 50 merchants referenced Clearpay in their social media posts. 
 From a sample of 11 merchant requests for Clearpay approval of their marketing assets, one merchant did not incorporate Compliance feedback into their website banner.

Issue Title: SEL-SIL Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery Q2 2022
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 81. Financial institutions should put BCPs in place to ensure that they can react appropriately to potential failure scenarios and that they are able to recover the operations of their critical business activities after disruptions within a recovery time objective (RTO, the maximum time within which a system or process must be restored after an incident) and a recovery point objective (RPO, the maximum time period during which it is acceptable for data to be lost in the event of an incident). In cases of severe business disruption that trigger specific business continuity plans, financial institutions should prioritise business continuity actions using risk- based approach, which can be based on the risk assessments carried out under Section 3.3.3. For PSPs this may include, for example, facilitating the further processing of critical transactions while remediation efforts continue.
 

 Testing Results:
 ITM found that the Business Impact Analysis (BIA) instructions provided to business units include Recovery Point Objectives (RPO) and Real Time Objectives (RTO). However, the Business Impact Analysis report prepared by the BCM team excludes RPO and RTO information. Additionally, ITM tested 20 disaster recovery plans and found that 12 plans (60%) do not include RPO and RTO information.

Issue Title: The Square AU complaints framework is not compliant with ASIC’s Regulatory Guide (RG) 271 - Internal Dispute Resolution and its internal AFSL Complaints Policy
Issue Description:
Square AU does not have workflows, customer communication templates/macros, standard operating procedures (SOP) and management reporting in order to provide IDR responses required by RG 271. This includes the following:
 - Workflows/tracking for the Acknowledgement of customer complaints at day 1 and the provision of an IDR response on or before day 5 and 30. 
 - Customer communication response content containing outcomes, actions taken, reasons for deciding to reject a complaint, delay notifications and the customers right to contact the Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) and their contact details.  
 - SOP for permitting interested persons in a complaint to be identified, notified and have their views considered in the fair handling of a complaint.
 - The Monthly Square Complaints and the Quarterly Square AU Board Reporting do not contain the following;
  > The time taken to acknowledge, resolve and/or finalize complaints
  > The number of complaints resolved, unresolved, abandoned or withdrawn
  > Details of the amounts paid to complainants to resolve complaints
  >The number of complaints escalated to AFCA is not in the Monthly Square Complaints Reporting.
 - The AU Complaints Management Quality Assurance rubric (evaluation form) does not include all RG271 requirements e.g timeline to respond and macro content. (Noting requirements will vary based on any new SOP, Workflow and macros created). 
 - Loan statement reminders that include details about how a complainant can access AFCA. 
 - Complaints policy published on the Square Website. Separately, update the Square Help Centre Article referencing that we will “respond in writing within 30 working days and no later than 45 calendar days”.
 - A website that accommodates people with disabilities or language difficulties. 
 Please note - Based on our sample tested, ITM notes that circa 85% of complaints are resolved within 5 days and therefore a significant majority of complaints may only require a day 1 IDR acknowledgement.  
 

 Gap Analysis for ASIC IDR Data Reporting: 
 To support the development of Square AU’s obligation to report IDR Data to ASIC per IDR Data Reporting Instrument 2022/205 due by August 2023, ITM completed a Gap Analysis of Square AU’s complaints data alignment with the ASIC IDR Reporting Handbook requirements. 
 In summary, there are seven mandatory data elements in the Square Systems that may require remapping to align with ASIC’s reporting requirements.
 

 Training
 - For October 2022, we identified 20 Customer Success Advocates (CSA’s) consisting of 10 Square CSA’s and 10 Business Process Outsourced Advocates who were not registered for the Complaints Management Training.  
 - No Australia specific completion rates were available for the Sales and Account Management teams. We noted that complaints training completion rates are 12% globally (64 of 504 registered).
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: FY22 Q4 ITM Square AU Complaints Management Review (1005481)
Issue Rating Justification: Issue rated in accordance with methodology
Closure Request Justification: Issue was not closed when MAPS were closed at their due date - closing now for reporting purposes

Issue Title: The practices for adherence to complaints standard operating procedures and quality assurance can be improved [Afterpay BNPL - Global]
Issue Description:
Global Agents - Tier 1 Complaint Handling
 GCMT conducted testing on a global random sample of 85 Tier 1 complaints. The agents effectiveness rate for Complaint Handling per the standard operating procedures was 31% with country compliance ranging from 25% to 40% effectiveness. The 69% ineffective rate consisted of 47% medium/high and 22% low/administrative exceptions. Please refer to appendix B for exception rating definition and country breakdowns.
 

 The following themes were identified: 
 ● Complaints are not being escalated to Tier 2 (Escalations Team) where required; 
 ● Duplicate tickets relating to the same issue or complaint are not being handled in accordance with the duplicate tickets process, resulting in increased customer dissatisfaction with process; 
 ● Complaint reporting fields on Zendesk are not being completed accurately prior to closure. 
 

 Agents - Complaint Identification and Tagging
 GCMT conducted testing on a global random sample of 212 digital customer enquiries and 70 outbound calls to determine agent compliance with complaint identification.
 North America (NA) - Complaint Untagging 
 GCMT tested a targeted sample of 25 NA customer enquiries submitted on complaints forms, where the customer explicitly states that the enquiry was not a complaint. 12 of 25 enquiries (48%) were untagged as complaints on Zendesk by agents in accordance with the standard operating procedures. 
 

 Global Escalations - Tier 2 Complaint Handling 
 GCMT tested a targeted sample of 73 Tier 2 complaints. The agents effectiveness rate for handling Tier 2 complaints per the standard operating procedures was 18% with country compliance ranging from 0% to 35%. The 82% ineffective rate consisted of 64% low/administrative and 18% medium/high exceptions.  
 

 The following themes were identified: 
 ● Complaint Handling Time-Frames/Actions were not being adhered to as required per the SOP. 
 ○ In Australia and New Zealand 10 or 25 complaints received their first response after day 5 (up to day 17) and for North America 4 of 25 complaints received their first response after day 5.  
 ○ In Australia, 9 of 10 complaints have not provided an update to the customer on day 9 per the  
 the Buy Now Pay Later Code of Practice
 ○ For all regions, 43 of 44 complaints did not receive the ‘complaints still pending (48 hour update)communication per the internal SOP requirement. 
 ○ Two complaints were resolved and closed outside of the internal SOP 21 day period. 
 ● For NA and UK, 33 of 43 complaints sampled, a collections exclusion was not applied to a customer’s account.
 ● For the EU the current Tier 2 complaint handling SOP does not align to current practices. EU Op’s have different macros and account notations that vary from the published SOP.  
 

 Global Quality Assurance - Graded Complaints 
 GCMT tested 30 Graded Tier 2 complaints and re-performed grading against the requirements of the SOP to determine if the grading outcome (by exception not percentage) was aligned. There was a 46% effectiveness rating as one or more errors were not noted in the QA grading scorecard per the Tier 2 Complaint Handling SOPS. These errors were low-risk from a customer resolution and outcomes perspective.  
 

 Global Quality Assurance - Graded Enquiries for Identification of Missed Complaints 
 GCMT tested 75 general graded tickets (45 Outbound and 30 Digital) to determine if the grader identified missed complaints by the agent and noted this in section 1.1 of the Effortless Experience Scorecard (EES). 
 

 Australia Only - Compliance with ASIC’s Regulatory Guide 271 - Internal Dispute Resolution 
 The current Australian Afterpay Complaint Management Program does not align with the below RG 271 requirements.
 -Analysis and Reporting of Complaint Metrics does not currently include “Time to acknowledge Complaints” 
 -The Tier 1 Complaint Handling SOP does not instruct an agent to provide a written response for hardship related complaints.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Global Customer Experience Review for Afterpay/Clearpay  (1007462)
Impacted Countries:
United Kingdom
 Australia
 United States
 New Zealand
Impacted Legal Entities:
Afterpay Australia Pty Ltd
 Afterpay US Services
 Afterpay NZ Limited
Issue Rating Justification: Complaint handling is higher risk from a compliance and operational view point. Finding included multiple observations.
Issue Action Plans:
Compliance with SOP - Tier 1, Complaint Tagging/Identification and Complaint Untagging (1031781)
 1.04 Compliance with SOP - Tier 2 (1031785)
 1.05 - Complaint QA Tier 2 (1031787)
 1.06 - Complaint QA - Missed Complaints (1031789)
 1.07 - Complaints AU RG 271 (1031792)

Issue Title: The practices for AT, TPR (all regions) and MI procedures in ANZ can be uplifted to align with SOPs
Issue Description:
GCMT tested compliance with standard operating procedures for Account Takeovers, Third Party Requests and Manual Identification procedures. Below results indicate an effectiveness rate below 50% for the sample tested. 
 

 Account Takeover 
 Global agent effectiveness for processing Account Takeover in accordance with the SOPS was noted at 2% as 44 of 45 samples tested identified that agents did not complete an average of three steps within the SOP when actioning and responding to customer requests. 
 

 These included: 
 ● Frontline agents incorrectly declined customer’s ATO claim without proper escalation to the investigations team.
 ● Investigations communicate timeframes to customers for investigations, however these are not being consistently met in Australia and New Zealand and North America;  
 ○ For ANZ, 78% of samples processed outside time frame of 10 business days 
 ○ For NA, 57% of samples processed outside time frame of 10 business days 
 ● Frontline agents and Investigations are not acknowledging or responding to multiple follow ups and queries from customers during the ATO investigation time-period.  
 ● Frontline agents are not completing security verification/checks prior to proceeding with the customer enquiry/request; 
 ● The Account Takeover, Claims, New Accounts and Reinstatement SOP requires frontline agents to advise customers that “they will need to continue making repayments for legitimate orders” – This wording is not included in any of the current outbound template macros used by frontline agents. 
 It was evident in sample-testing, that the above themes are causing high customer friction and dissatisfaction, which is expressed in the ticket or via the customer satisfaction survey. 
 

 Third Party Requests - Mobile and Email Address 
 Global agent effectiveness is 43% for processing Third-Party Requests in accordance with the SOPS as 28 of 49 samples tested identified that the agent did not complete one or more steps within the SOP when responding to and actioning the customer’s request. 
 This included: 
 ● Frontline agents are asking Third parties (non Afterpay customers) to complete a security verification/checks, prior to responding to their enquiry. This causes dissatisfaction for third parties as some or all of their personal details (e.g email or mobile number) have been used to open an account and they are naturally reluctant to provide additional personal information. 
 ● Frontline agents generally complete searches for third party email addresses on the local Admin Portal that the third party has emailed from. ITM testing identified instances where an email address was located on a consumer account in a different region. 
 

 Manual Identification  Global agent effectiveness is 24% for processing in accordance with manual identification regional SOPS as 34 of 45 samples tested identified that an agent did not complete an average of one or more steps within the SOP when responding to and actioning customer requests. The cause of exceptions varied between regions. Themes included;
 

 Australia
 ● 100% 15 of 15 samples tested identified that agents are not documenting within the Zendesk Ticket or Admin Portal notes if a check and subsequent match has been confirmed for the customer’s address during the Manual IDV process. 
 

 New Zealand 
 ● There are inconsistencies between the ID requirements that are published on the Afterpay Webpage (HelpCentre SOP), the outbound customer communications (macros) and the ID that is being collected for manual verification. 
 ● Agents are communicating or requesting the manual identification requirements incorrectly and/or inconsistently i.e requesting a New Zealand Passport and a New Zealand Drivers Licence for verification when this is not required. I.e. A customer only has to provide a Passport or Drivers Licence
 ○ This is resulting in a high number of eligible customers being declined for not being able to provide both a New Zealand Passport and a New Zealand Drivers Licence. 
 

 United Kingdom 
 ● Testing identified agents documenting the customer’s full ID document number/reference in the Admin Portal Note in 2 of 15 manual id samples which is not required due to internal privacy controls.

Issue Title: Line 0 agents are not escalating Monthly Payment enquiries to the Support Monthly Payments team due to non adherence to the Monthly Payments Standard Operating Procedures.
Issue Description:
As the Monthly Payment product is regulated in the US, customer enquiries for this product are to be referred to a Monthly Payments Support or Specialist Team. 
 

 The Support team responds to general customer enquiries which are considered non restricted activities e.g a change payment or turn off auto payment requests.
 

 The Specialist team responds to customer enquiries which are considered to be restricted license activities e.g
 

 ● Payment arrangement (incl break. Multiple requests)
 ● Complaints
 ● Outbound calls
 ● Early Payout Figures
 ● Cease & Desist requests
 ● Interest refund
 

 GCMT tested a targeted sample of customer enquiries and identified that 27 of 44 non restricted activities (general queries) were not escalated to the Monthly Payments Support Team, rather Line 0 responded to the query. 
 

 Furthermore, GCMT tested a targeted sample of escalated customer enquiries to the Monthly Payments Support Team. Line 0 Agents have to undertake some processes before escalation occurs.
 

 GCMT identified that the standard operating procedures were not followed for 32 of 95 escalated enquiries.
 The themes included:
 ● 18 instances where one or more administration tasks were not completed e.g’s. escalation macro not sent and no note placed on an account. 
 ● 12 instances where the enquiry was escalated but the enquiry was not related to Monthly Payments and should have been resolved at the Line 0/1 level. 
 ● 1 instance where a security check was not completed prior to escalation or after the escalation by the specialist team. 
 ● 1 instance where the agent advised the customer repeatedly that there was no monthly payment product
  
 There is currently no targeted Quality Assurance to monitor Line 0 or Line 1 compliance with the handling of Monthly Payment Enquiries.

Issue Title: Incomplete Reg E Policy
Issue Description:
Regulation E Policy
 

 Requirement:
 No direct requirement per Regulation E. 
 

 The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) states within the March 2022 Exam Manual the following:
 

 To maintain legal compliance, an institution must develop and maintain a sound compliance management system (CMS) that is integrated into the overall framework for product design, delivery, and administration across their entire product and service lifecycle. Ultimately,
 compliance should be part of the day-to-day responsibilities of management and the employees of a supervised entity; issues should be self-identified; and corrective action should be initiated by the entity.
 

 A CMS is how an institution:
 • Establishes its compliance responsibilities;
 • Communicates those responsibilities to employees;
 • Ensures that responsibilities for meeting legal requirements and internal policies and procedures are incorporated into business processes;
 • Reviews operations to ensure responsibilities are carried out and legal requirements are met; and
 • Takes corrective action and updates tools, systems, and materials as necessary.
 

 An effective CMS commonly has two interdependent control components:
 • Board and Management Oversight; and
 • Compliance Program, which includes:
   Policies and procedures;
   Training;
   Monitoring and/or audit; and
   Consumer complaint response.
 

 Testing Results:
 Cash App maintains a Regulation E (EFTA) Policy for the lending product, however, it is limited to only that product at this time. The Cash Card and Peer-to-Peer products are not included. Based on the foundation set forth by the CFPB, the current state of the Regulation E Policy is a compliance framework design failure as not all compliance responsibilities are detailed in the Policy's current form.

Issue Title: Inadequate Error Resolution Procedures (Reg E) [Cash App - US]
Issue Description:
Error Resolution Procedures
 

 Requirement:
 While Regulation E § 1005.11 is too large to paste here, it generally, defines the term error and describes the steps the consumer must take when asserting an error in order to receive the protection of the EFTA and 12 CFR 1005.11, and the procedures that a financial institution must follow to resolve an alleged error under this section. 
 

 Testing Results:
 ITM reviewed all 21 Procedures/SOPs provided by the business that relate to Regulation E. The results are broken down to 1) general procedure enhancements: which encompases all 21 reviewed; 2) individual procedure enhancements: which contains one off procedural gaps; and 3) other procedure enhancements: which speaks to procedures that do not directly apply to resolving errors but could benefit from additional content.
 

 General procedure enhancements:
 1. Procedures all presume that a dispute/error will be resolved within a SLA of 10 days. There is guidance within a knowledge article that generally references time limits for an investigation in the instance a case is not resolved within the SLA, however it is not detailed with steps agents must take should the investigation take up to 45 days. 
 2. No strong guidance on a reassertion of error. The procedures maintain a step in which the agent checks for duplicate cases, however, it is unclear if this process is meant to clarify the lack of responsibility to investigate in the case where a customer makes a reassertion of the same error. Additionally there is no mention of the fact that the financial institution has no further error resolution responsibilities if the consumer voluntarily withdraws the notice alleging an error. A consumer who has withdrawn an allegation of error has the right to reassert the allegation unless the financial institution had already complied with all of the error resolution requirements before the allegation was withdrawn. The consumer must do so, however, within the original 60-day period.
 3. Many procedures do not contain an option for the agent to deny a claim if they determines no error or different error occurred.
 

 Individual procedure enhancements:
 > Internal Disputes - Shazam Writeoff Guidelines: lacks guidance/steps for an agent to state that no or a different error occurred.
 > Internal Disputes - Account Funding Transactions: This procedure outlines the steps for fraudulent account funding. This would be considered an ‘unauthorized electronic funds transfer’ and there lacks any guidance around meeting Regulation E requirements.
 

 Other procedure enhancements:
 While beneficial to include all regulatory requirements like other knowledge articles, there is no direct requirement for each procedure to include every aspect of the rule. In the following cases, the procedures listed apply to advocates that do not directly investigate a Reg E claim, however, this is part of the intake process and procedure enhancements should be considered to include Regulation E basics at minimum:
 > Voice - Cash Card Disputes IRF 
 > Email and Messaging - Cash Card Disputes - IRF
 > P2P - Disputes and Unauthorized Charges
 > Cash Card Dispute - ACH Claims

Issue Title: Required Enhancement to the Training Program
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 Per the CFPB exam objectives, "Education of an entity’s board of directors, management, and staff is essential to maintaining an effective compliance program. Board members should receive sufficient information to enable them to understand the entity’s responsibilities and the commensurate resource requirements.
 

 Management and staff should receive specific, comprehensive training that reinforces and helps implement written policies and procedures. Requirements for compliance with Federal consumer financial laws, including prohibitions against unlawful discrimination and unfair, deceptive, and abusive acts and practices, should be incorporated into training for all relevant officers and employees, including audit personnel.
 

 - Compliance training is comprehensive, timely, and specifically tailored to the particular responsibilities of the staff receiving it, including those responsible for product development, marketing and customer service.  
 

 - The compliance training program is updated proactively in advance of the rollout of new or changed products or the effective date of new or changed consumer protection laws and regulations to ensure that all staff is aware of compliance responsibilities. 
 

 - Training is consistent with policies and procedures and designed to reinforce those policies and procedures. 
 

 - Compliance professionals have access to training that is necessary to administer a compliance program that is tailored to the supervised entity’s risk profile, business strategy, and operations." 
 

 Testing Results:

 2. Not all Block employees in Sales and Marketing, Operations and Design that interact with the credit card product received relevant compliance training.
 

 3. 12 of 18 compliance training courses were not tailored to the businesses products and policies and procedures. These courses were:
 Consumer Credit Products
 Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices for the Financial Institution
 Handling Consumer Complaints
 The Red Flags Rule 
 Red Flags of Identity Theft
 Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (GLBA) - Opt Out
 Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act - Opt In
 The Telemarketing Consumer Fraud and Abuse Prevention Act
 Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (FDCPA)
 Fair Lending Overview
 Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) and;
 Electronic Funds Transfer Act - Regulation E.

Issue Title: SIL Conduct Risk (particular focus on Vulnerable Customers Framework Assessment
Issue Description:
Finding Description:
 Medium Risk Finding:
 The SIL Conduct Risk framework is not consistent with the CBI Consumer Protection Code 2012 and Guide to Consumer Protection Risk Assessment 2017 requirements.
 

 Several processes need to be established in order to demonstrate a Conduct Risk framework for vulnerable customers and to uplift the existing Conduct Risk framework. 
  
 1. The Square and Cash Customer Success (CS), Sales & Account Management (SAM) and Marketing functions do not have procedures, systems, controls, staff training/coaching, Post Sales Handling and Marketing control check processes for handling vulnerable customers. 
 

 2. The Square Product Counsel has a “Seller Footers” guide in place which outlines the regulatory disclosure statements requirements for various types of communications with Sellers; however some functions that communicate with Sellers are not referenced in the guide. 
 

 ITM identified no Regulatory disclosure statements on communications for; SAM - 100% (50 of 50) , CS - 50% (15 of 30), Risk/Fraud Operations - 24% (9 of 37); and: Compliance Operations - 3% (1 of 60) and legal entity information was inaccurate for 24% (9 of 60) Compliance Operations and for 27% (10 of 37) Risk/Fraud Operations communications. 
 

 3. The Cash App Product Counsel has no documented process in place to ensure that regulatory disclosure statement requirements are included in appropriate documentation and communications e.g The Privacy Notice on the Cash App’s website does not contain a regulatory disclosure statement. A review of a log containing 117 Cash App emails to Cash App users sent in Q1 2022 identified that 74% (87) did not contain regulatory disclosure statements.
 

 4. The Industry Relations and Operations Squareup International Consumer Protection Policy does not include record retention requirements.
 

 5. The Marketing and Legal “IE Marketing Review Guide” does not include regulatory disclosure statement requirements.
 

 6. EU Compliance does not have periodic monitoring procedures in place to to perform monitoring of Sales, Customer Success and Marketing functions.
 

 7. The Industry Relations and Operations Squareup International Consumer Protection Policy does not include requirements relating to telephone contact with consumers. ITM reviewed a sample of 30 call recordings and noted 27% (8 of 30) calls whereby Sales Representatives did not inform the consumer that the call was being recorded.
 

 8. SAM does not have a formalized procedure in place for handling a consumer request not to make a personal visit or telephone contact with them.

Issue Title: SEL Conduct Risk (particular focus on Vulnerable Customers) Framework Assessment
Issue Description:
Medium Risk Finding:
 The SEL Conduct Risk framework is not compliant with FCA FG 21/1 Guidance for firms on the fair treatment of vulnerable customers.
 

 Finding Description:
 Several processes and practices have to be established and/or uplifted in order to demonstrate a Conduct Risk framework for the SEL business. 
 

 1. The Square and Cash Customer Success (CS), Sales & Account Management (SAM), Product Management and Marketing functions do not have a framework in place including procedures, systems, controls, and staff training/coaching for handling vulnerable customers.
 

 2. The Squareup Europe Consumer Protection Policy, overseen by Industry Relations and Operations does not include the below areas:
 > Equality Act 2010, Mental Capacity Act 2005, temporary vulnerability, point of sale, post-contractual information and record retention requirements.
 > Bereavement and relationship breakdown as characteristics of vulnerability;
 > Protected characteristics covered in the Equality Act 2010;
 > Understanding how vulnerability can be perpetuated or worsened by SEL’s own actions, or inaction;
 > Understanding how vulnerable consumers may be more likely to be susceptible to behavioural biases;
 > Situations and circumstances that may lead to a consumer in their target market or customer base becoming vulnerable;
 > Characteristics of vulnerability as per FCA's 4 key drivers, and;
 > Reference to ensure that the label 'vulnerable' should not be used when interacting with consumers.
 

 3. The Marketing and Legal UK “Marketing Review Guide” does not include regulatory disclosure statement requirements. 
 

 4. The UK Compliance “Final Vulnerable Customer Training” does not include the steps to encourage disclosure of customer vulnerability. Further, no periodic monitoring was performed during the review scope period and the Consumer Protection Risk / TCF Management Information (MI) document does not include vulnerable customers.
 

 5. The Square Product Counsel has a “Seller Footers” guide in place which outlines the regulatory disclosure statements requirements for various types of communications with Sellers; however some functions that communicate with Sellers are not referenced in the guide. 
 

 ITM identified no payment service provider regulatory details on communications for;
 > Marketing emails - 40% (2 of 5)
 > Transactional emails - 67% (2 of 3) 
 > CS - 66% (23 of 35)

Issue Title: EFTA Policy Misalignment
Issue Description:
Requirement: EFTA policy misalignment to regulatory expectations including a statement referencing internal controls around ACH/NACHA expectations (12 CFR 1005), documentation around state requirements (12 CFR 1005), lack of internal control documented around methods of repayment (12 CFR 1005.10), and associated EFTA topics not present in the policy. These topics include disclosures (12 CFR 1005.4 & .7), unauthorized transfer claims (12 CFR 1005.6 & .11), liabilities (12 CFR 1005.6), periodic statements with sufficient information (12 CFR 1005.9), and record retention (12 CFR 1005.13(b)).
 

 Testing Results: The EFTA policy lacks information around the following topics:
  1) Introduction Section - Statement regarding ACH / NACHA / CFR Reference 12 CFR 1005 (EFTA Policy Page 2) - Unknown internal control as ACH operations is housed outside of Cash App Lending.
  2) Policy Statement Section - State Requirements Supplement / CFR Reference 12 CFR 1005 (EFTA Policy Page 2) - Unknown internal control as state requirements supplement is housed outside of Cash App Lending. There is a Confluence location listing the state requirements however it was created for Square Capital.
  3) Program Requirements Section - Method of Loan Repayment / CFR Reference: Electronic Funds Transfer Act (EFTA) aka Regulation E / Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) / 12 CFR 1005.10; CFPB Manual pages: 1005.10 (688, 760) (EFTA Policy Pages 5-6) - Unknown internal control as policy statement does not identify that #1 (first) choice in loan repayment is pulling the funds from the Cash App Stored Ballance (aka wallet). The #2 (secondary) choice for loan repayment is from the debit card linked to the Cash App account as a funding source. The customer does NOT choose the method of repayment. The loan terms and functionality is spelled out in the loan agreement.
  4) EFTA topics referenced in 12 CFR 1005 that are not documented in Cash App Lending's EFTA Policy:
  a) Required disclosures are given at the time the account is opened or prior to the first electronic funds transfer (“EFT”) / 12 CFR 1005.4 and 1005.7
  b) Unauthorized transfer claims are processed in compliance with the regulation / 12 CFR 1005.6 and 1005.11
  c) Liability for unauthorized transfer claims is assessed in compliance with the regulation / 12 CFR 1005.6
  d) Review a sample of periodic statements for each type of account in which electronic fund transfers occur to determine that they contain sufficient information for the consumer to identify transactions adequately and that they otherwise comply with regulatory requirements / 12 CFR 1005.9
  e) Determine that the financial institution is maintaining records of compliance for a period of not less than two years from the date disclosures are required to be made or action is required to be taken / 12 CFR 1005.13(b)

Issue Title: Red Flags Policy Misalignment [Cash App Lending - US ]
Issue Description:
Requirement: Identity Theft Rules 16 CFR, Part 681
 

 Testing Results:
  a. The Red Flags Policy, including the Red Flags Spreadsheet, does not reflect actual practices:
  i. The Policy describes review of credit reports for fraud alerts, credit freeze, address discrepancies, and inconsistent patterns of activity; however, this is no longer conducted. As of February 2022, TransUnion (TU) screening is for MLA purposes only. The inaccurate information was also found in the Spreadsheet.
 

  b. The Red Flags Policy does not detail key Red Flag/Identity Theft Prevention Program components:
  i.  Periodic risk assessments. Note: Although a Risk Assessment was conducted, the requirement to conduct a periodic risk assessment was not detailed in the Policy.
  ii.  Board of directors, committee thereof, or senior management employee oversight, development, implementation, and administration of the Program including reporting obligations, report content, and approval of material changes.
  iii. Oversight of service provider arrangements. 
 

  c. The Red Flags Policy does not consistently include activities/controls outside of Cash App Lending. Cash App Account activities/controls are only included for some Red Flags. There are other Cash App Account level activities/controls that apply to a Red Flag but are not incorporated.
  i.  Cash App Lending does not issue cards; however, the Cash App Account offers the Cash Card. (Red Flag: Change of address request is followed by a request for new, additional, or replace card).
  ii. Cash App Lending does not conduct transaction monitoring of purchases/spending patterns; however, transaction monitoring occurs with the Cash App Account. (Red Flag: Material change in spending patterns.)
  iii. Cash App Lending requires a minimum duration of activity for loan eligibility; however, there was no information whether monitoring activities occur at the Account level. (Red Flag: Potentially suspicious activity when an account that has been inactive (at the Account level) is used.) 
 

  d. The Red Flags Policy information regarding SSN screening against database issuance, the Social Security Administration's Death Master File, and correlation between the SSN range and date of birth could not be verified in existing Health Risk Procedures.
 

  e. The Red Flags Policy, including the Red Flags Spreadsheet, listed activities/controls that do not correlate to the Red Flag:
  i. Page 6-8 - Suspicious Personal Identifying Information
  - #3: The business activity detailed does not address the Red Flag as it speaks to the permitted use of the same address and phone numbers for multiple applicants and does not consider the Red Flag component of the address or phone number being associated with known fraudulent activity.
  - #6: The activity detailed does not consider the Red Flag component of an unusually large number of other persons opening accounts with the same address or phone number.
  - #8: The Red Flag is in relation to PII provided not being consistent with PII on file with the business and the response focuses on Cash App not limiting the number of accounts a customer may have and that access requires credentials and 2FA. 
  ii. Pages 8-9 - Unusual Use of, or Suspicious Activity Related to, the Covered Account
  - #2: The Borrow product information for this Red Flag is not accurate as it stated that only one payment is due per loan but installment options are available. Additionally, the Borrow product is not a revolving line of credit. 
 

  f. Cash App Borrow Red Flags Risk Assessment (RA)
  i. The RA incorporated controls at the Cash App Account level; however, it does not specify when the controls are at the Account or Borrow/Lending level. As a ""Borrow"" risk assessment, the additional clarity will assist end users.
  1. RA Red Flags Controls: 
  - Documents appear altered/forged
  - Photograph/physical description on the identification is not consistent with the appearance of the applicant
  - Other information on the identification is not consistent with information provided by the person opening a new covered account or customer presenting the identification
  - The Social Security Number (SSN) has not been issued or is listed on the Social Security Administration’s Death Master File. 
  - Personal identifying information provided by the customer is not consistent with other personal identifying information provided by the customer. For example, there is a lack of correlation between the SSN range and date of birth. 
  - The person opening the covered account or the customer fails to provide all required personal identifying information on an application or in response to notification that the application is incomplete.
  ii. The Assessment response is not clear for the following:
  1. Challenge question control listed ""No;"" however, this Red Flag does not apply; thus, an ""N/A"" response is appropriate.
 

  g. The Red Flags Policy, Spreadsheet, and Risk Assessment (RA) do not align whether it is the response and/or rationale for the following Red Flags:
  i. Nonpayment when there is no history of late or missed payments;
  ii. Additional Red Flags;
  iii. A consumer reporting agency provides a notice of a credit freeze in response to a request for a consumer report;
  iv. A consumer report indicates a pattern of activity that is inconsistent with the history and usual pattern of activity of an applicant or customer. (The Policy/Spreadsheet does not list all specific examples from the Identity Theft Rules and the Risk Assessment (RA) is listed 3/4 examples from the Identity Theft Rules.)
  v. Address does not match any address in the consumer report;
  vi. Address on an application is the same as the address provided on a fraudulent application;
  vii. Address or telephone number provided is the same as or similar to the account number or telephone number submitted by an unusually large number of other persons opening accounts or other customers; and
  viii. A material change in electronic fund transfer patterns in connection with a deposit account.
 

  h. The last Red Flags Policy revision date is February 2020. The business indicated there is an annual policy review process; however it would appear this Policy has not been reviewed in two years. This is further evidenced due to missing author and reviewer information and reference to a "Training Policy" that is not available as there is instead a "Lending Training Procedure."
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Red Flags (FCRA and Regulation V) ITM Review 2022 - US - Cash Lending (1009458)

Issue Action Plans: Red Flags Policy Misalignment (1039745)

February 24, 2025: February 24, 2024: The business created the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) Policy which includes requirements for detecting, preventing, and mitigating identity theft- 
 

  1. Page 16 outlines the requirement for-

  b. Program Administration Methods-
  i. Approval from the Board of Directors or an appropriate committee; 
  ii. Involving a designated Employee in the oversight, development, implementation and administration of the Program;
  iii. Training employees, as necessary; and
  iv. Exercising oversight of service provider arrangements. 
 

  2. Pages 17 highlights-
  a. Red Flags categories:
  i. Alerts, Notifications, or Warnings from a Consumer Reporting Agency; 
  ii. Suspicious Documents;
  iii. Suspicious Personal Identifying Information;
  iv. Suspicious Account activity; and

  b. The detection of Red Flags for new and existing accounts, and mitigation of identity theft such as monitoring, customer contact, and closing accounts.
 

  c. Page 18-19 provides additional examples for Alerts, Notifications, or Warnings from a Consumer Reporting Agency; Suspicious Documents; and Suspicious Account Activity. 
 

 Refer to the Action Plan for supporting documentation.
 

 Note: Although the Identity Theft Program requirements are in the FCRA Policy, a written Identity Theft Prevention Program has yet to be established. The business opened an issue, #1194106, to address the gap. Therefore, no additional tracking by GCMT for a written Identity Theft Program is required.
 

 The management action plan as it pertains to a Red Flags framework is complete.

Issue Title: Cash Lending Collections Policy - Post-Dated Checks Revision
Issue Description:
Physical Checks - Coda Webpage and Cash Lending Collections Policy
 

 Requirement:
  • Cash Lending Collections Policy, Page 6 - Postdated Checks: "There are a number of prohibitions concerning a debt collector’s use of post-dated checks. The Lending Team does not honor post-dated check requests. Checks will be credited as of the date in which they are received."
 

  • FDCPA - Reg. F, 12 CFR, Part 1006.22(c)(1) Unfair or unconscionable means: "A debt collector must not: Accept from any person a check or other payment instrument postdated by more than five days unless such person is notified in writing of the debt collector’s intent to deposit such check or instrument not more than ten, nor less than three, days prior to such deposit."
 

 Testing Results:
 The Coda Webpage - Physical Checks (https://coda.io/d/CASH-APP-LENDING-OPERATIONS_dc4psNpW1wx/Physical-Checks_suwJm?googleOnly=true#_luBXA) does not detail the prohibited activity of honoring postdated check requests as stated in the Cash Lending Collections Policy. Additionally, the Cash Lending Collections Policy (https://docs.google.com/document/d/15qAhhjxD63k6XPntHstU2VMIsPau2B13/edit) states that ""Checks will be credited as of the date in which they are received"" which does not follow the FDCPA (Reg. F) requirement of a written notification of deposit information for checks postdated by more than five days.

Issue Title: Complaint Handling - QT Message for New Customers
Issue Description:
Complaint Handling
 

 Requirement: 
 Lending Operations Complaint Procedures, Pages 2-3 Requirements and Definitions:
  • Requirements: Consider whether the complaint has systemic implications to other customer accounts (i.e. a complaint that pertains to issues arising from changes to processes or procedures that impact multiple customers) and, if so, take appropriate action to resolve the issues for those customers.
 

  • Definitions: 1. Complaint - Any expression of dissatisfaction by an individual, business entity, decision, or interaction in which a response or resolution is explicitly or implicitly requested. However, the following are not considered Complaints: 
  a. Inquiries - A request for information from a customer. customer inquiries may include questions regarding existing accounts, new product or marketing offers and general requests for information. The request does not contain an expression of dissatisfaction."
  
 Testing Results:
 Based on a sample size of 56 invalid complaints, one complaint (52454994) for a new Borrow customer without prior Borrow activity was identified as valid and given inaccurate information.
  a. The customer was informed on 6/13/2022, 8:00 am and 6/14/2022, 7:22 am that if their limit changes, they will be notified through a push notification or in-app alert; however, notification did not occur.
  b. The case was marked as invalid but was a valid complaint as the customer was dissatisfied with the expiration push notification template as the amount communicated ($100) at the time of push notification varied from the amount at time of borrow ($50). (Customer limit was lowered May 27, 2022. Expiring notice was sent June 12, 2022.)
  c. Customer was informed on 6/14/22, 8:21 am that if their limit is lowered, an adverse action notice (AAN) is available within the app; however, the customer does not have an AAN. The customer's limit decreased occurred prior to offer acceptance.

Issue Title: Lack of Operational Procedures for ACH Record Retention and Electronic Records [Square Capital - US]
Issue Description:
NACHA Requirement: ACH Record Retention & Electronic Records (NACHA Audit Workbook, General Rules Obligations (“GRO”) Section 2)
  Requirement documented within the ACH_Program_Assessment_Square_Inc_2021-06-29 Observation Summary, Page 5
  Verify that a Record of each Entry is retained for six years from the date the Entry was Transmitted, except as otherwise expressly provided in these Rules. Verify that a printout or reproduction of the information relating to the Entry can be provided, if requested by the Participating DFI’s customer or any other Participating DFI or ACH Operator that originated, Transmitted, or received the Entry. When a Record required by these Rules is created or retained in an Electronic form, verify that the Electronic form (a) accurately reflects the information in the Record, and (b) is capable of being accurately reproduced for later reference, whether by Transmission, printing, or otherwise.
  Testing Objective (Impact):
  A Third-Party Sender (performing the obligations of an ODFI) must retain a Record of each Entry for six years from the date the Entry was Transmitted, except as otherwise expressly provided in these Rules.
  A Third-Party Sender (performing the obligations of an ODFI) must, if requested by its customer or any other Participating DFI or ACH Operator that originated, Transmitted, or received the Entry, provide the requester with a printout or reproduction of the information relating to the Entry.
  A Record required by these Rules to be in writing may be created or retained in an Electronic form that (a) accurately reflects the information in the Record, and (b) is capable of being accurately reproduced for later reference, whether by Transmission, printing, or otherwise.
  NACHA Operating Rule Reference(s):
  Subsection 1.4.1, Retention Requirement for Records of Entries, OR 3
  Subsection 1.4.2, Provision Requirement for Records of Entries, OR 3
  Subsection 1.4.3, Electronic Record Creation and Retention, OR 3
  NACHA Operating Guidelines Reference(s):
  General: Records and Record Retention, OG 22
  Third-Party: Electronic Records/Record Retention, OG 292-293
  Testing Results: Partially Met; No operational procedures, however, electronic records were stated to be maintained
  Information was requested via the ACH Ad-Hoc Review 2021 project file: IR6 and IR7 requested Record Retention and Electronic Records documentation from the business. The business confirmed during the ad-hoc review project that electronic records can be reproduced.

Issue Title: Lack of Operational Procedures Around Risk ACH Management (Monitoring the Originator's origination and return activities) [Square Capital - US ]
Issue Description:
NACHA Requirement: Risk Management (NACHA Audit Workbook, Third Party Senders ("TPS") Section 2)
  Requirement documented within the ACH_Program_Assessment_Square_Inc_2021-06-29 Observation Summary, Page 5
  Verify that the Third-Party Sender has assessed the risks of the Originator’s or the nested Third-Party Sender’s ACH activity, and has established, implemented and periodically reviewed an exposure limit for each Originator or nested Third-Party Sender. Verify that the Third-Party Sender has established and implemented procedures to monitor the Originator’s or nested Third-Party Sender’s origination and Return activity across multiple Settlement Dates; enforce restrictions on the types of Entries that may be originated; and enforce the exposure limit.
  Testing Objectives (Impact):
  A Third-Party Sender (performing the obligations of an ODFI) must perform due diligence with respect to the Originator or nested Third-Party Sender sufficient to form a reasonable belief that the Originator or nested Third-Party Sender has the capacity to perform its obligations in conformance with the Rules. In addition, the Third-Party Sender must: (a) assess the nature of the Originator’s or nested Third-Party Sender’s ACH activity and the risks it presents, (b) establish, implement and periodically reviewed an exposure limit for each Originator and/or nested Third-Party Sender, and (c) establish and implement procedures to: (i) monitor the Originator’s or nested Third-Party Sender’s origination and return activity across multiple Settlement Dates, (ii) enforce restrictions on the types of Entries that may be originated, and (iii) enforce the exposure limit.
  NACHA Operating Rule Reference(s):
  Subsection 2.2.3, ODFI Risk Management, OR 7
  NACHA Operating Guidelines Reference(s):
  ODFI Risk Management, OG 35-42 and OG 64-65
  Testing Results: Partially Met
  No additional comments at the time of this assessment.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Description:
NACHA Requirement: Provision of Notice of Change (“NOC”) and Correction NOC Information to Originators (NACHA Audit Workbook, Third Party Senders (""TPS"") Section 5)
  Requirement documented within the ACH_Program_Assessment_Square_Inc_2021-06-29 Observation Summary, Page 6
  Verify that information relating to NOCs and Corrected NOCs is provided to each Originator or nested Third-Party Sender within two Banking Days of the Settlement Date of the NOC or Corrected NOC in accordance with Appendix Five (Notification of Change). For CIE and credit WEB Entries, verify that information relating to NOCs and Corrected NOCs is provided to any Third-Party Service Provider initiating such Entries on behalf of the consumer Originator. 
  Testing Objective (Impact):
  For each NOC or corrected NOC it receives, a Third-Party Sender must provide the Originator with the following information within two Banking Days of the Settlement Date of the NOC or corrected NOC; (a) Company Name, (b) Company Identification, (c) Company Entry Description, (d) Effective Entry Date, (e) DFI Account Number, (f) Individual Name/Receiving Company Name, (g) Individual Identification Number, (h) Change Code, (i) Original Entry Trace Number, (j) Original RDFI Identification, and (k) Corrected Data. 
  The Originator or Third-Party Sender must make the changes specified in the NOC or corrected NOC within six Banking Days of receipt of the NOC information or prior to initiating another Entry to the Receiver’s account; (m) In the case of CIE and credit WEB Entries, the Third-Party Service Provider (rather than the consumer Originator) must make the changes specified in the NOC.
  A Third-Party Sender may refuse an NOC or corrected NOC for all Entries except IAT Entries if the NOC or corrected NOC; (a) contains incorrect information, (b) does not contain all information required by Appendix Five, or (c) otherwise fails to comply with Appendix Five. To refuse a NOC or corrected NOC, the Third-Party Sender must Transmit a refused NOC complying with Appendix Five to the ACH Operator within fifteen days of receipt of the NOC or corrected NOC. 
  NACHA Operating Rule Reference(s):
  Subsection 2.11.1, ODFI and Originator Action on Notification of Change (NOC), OR 35-36
  Subsection 2.11.2, ODFI Right to Refuse Notification of Change Entries, OR 36
  Appendix Five, Notification of Change, OR 183-199
  NACHA Operating Guidelines Reference(s):
  Notifications of Change, OG 59-60

Issue Title: Lack of Operational Procedures Around ACH Reversals [Square Capital - US]
Issue Description:
NACHA Requirement: Reversals (NACHA Audit Workbook, Third Party Senders (""TPS"") Section 8)
  Requirement documented within the ACH_Program_Assessment_Square_Inc_2021-06-29 Observation Summary, Page 6
  Verify that Reversing Entries and Reversing Files are initiated in accordance with the requirements of these Rules.
  Testing Objective (Impact):
  An Originator or Third-Party Sender initiating a Reversing File to correct an Erroneous File must concurrently initiate a Correcting File corresponding to the Erroneous File, unless the Erroneous File was a duplicate. An Originator or the Third-Party Sender must Transmit each Reversing File and, when appropriate, a corresponding Correcting File, to the ACH Operator within five Banking Days after the Settlement Date of the Erroneous File. The Originator or Third-Party Sender must Transmit the Reversing File and any corresponding Correcting File to the ACH Operator within twenty-four hours of the discovery of the Erroneous File. Any debit Entry within the Reversing File must not contain an Effective Entry Date that is earlier than the Effective Entry Date of the credit Entry to which it relates.
  An Originator or Third-Party Sender may initiate a Reversing Entry to correct an Erroneous Entry previously initiated to a Receiver’s account. The Reversing Entry must be Transmitted to the ACH Operator in such time as to be Transmitted or made available to the RDFI within five Banking Days following the Settlement Date of the Erroneous Entry. A debit Reversing Entry must not contain an Effective Entry Date that is earlier than the Effective Entry Date of the credit Entry to which it relates.
  The Originator or Third-Party Sender must submit a Reversing Entry within a batch that includes the word “REVERSAL” in the Company Entry Description field of the Company/Batch Header Record. The description “REVERSAL” must replace the original content of the Company Entry Description field transmitted in the original batch, including content otherwise required by these Rules. 
  NACHA Operating Rule Reference(s):
  Section 2.8, Reversing Files, OR 32
  Section 2.9, Reversing Entries, OR 32-34
  NACHA Operating Guidelines Reference(s):
  ODFIs and Reversals, OG 60-63
  Originators and Reversals and Reclamations, OG 91-93
  Third-Party: Reversals/Erroneous Entries, OG 294
  Testing Results: Partially Met
  No additional comments at the time of this assessment

Issue Title: The processes for Hardware bankruptcy are not being followed in accordance with the Capital Collection BSO Procedure.
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 Capital Collections Bankruptcy SOP V2
 Introduction/Background
 When a seller with an active Square Capital plan is the debtor in a bankruptcy proceeding, Square Capital, as servicer or current creditor, will cease collection activity. Upon notice of a merchant’s bankruptcy filing, Square Capital will stop withholding loan payments via application of a repayment rate to credit card processing activity on the Square, Inc. platform and will not initiate ACH debits to any linked bank account during the pendency of the case/proceeding. Square Capital will also not accept any other medium of payment during the bankruptcy proceedings other than any distributions received through bankruptcy proceedings after filing proof of claims. Square Capital will cease collections communications and any other efforts to collect the loan. 
 

 Hardware Installments Financing Bankruptcy
 Effective 04/29/20, BK DRI’s will track Hardware Financing Bankruptcies. This additional procedure will include Hardware Financing payments to be suspended, pending the bankruptcy outcome. Any bankruptcy notices received and addressed to Square will be included in the following procedure. 
 Steps 1 - 5 outline specific actions required including updating systems, notating the loan on a tracker, and filing a ticket to suspend communication/payments. 
 

 Testing Results:
 In the 4 cases of Bankruptcy that contained an active, unpaid hardware installment loan, the process to cease collections and notate the tracker were not followed. The payment schedule and activity log shows that the company is still attempting to collect on the debt via monthly card transactions. In each case, these payments fail but there is no indication that was caused by internal actions.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: US Hardware Financing does not have a formalized Compliance Program in place. [Square Hardware Financing - US]
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 Compliance Management System - CFPB Examination Guide (August 2017)
 A sound Compliance Program is essential to the efficient and successful operation of the supervised entity. A Compliance Program includes the following components:
 • Policies and procedures;
 • Training;
 • Monitoring and/or audit; and
 • Consumer complaint response.
 An institution should establish a formal, written Compliance Program, and that program generally should be administered by a chief compliance officer. In addition to being a planned and organized effort to guide the entity’s compliance activities, a written program represents an essential source document that may serve as a training and reference tool for employees. A well planned, implemented, and maintained Compliance Program may prevent or reduce regulatory violations, protect consumers from non-compliance and associated harms, and help align business strategies with outcomes. 
 

 Testing Results:
 While guidance from the CFPB typically applies to consumer purpose products, it is also industry best practice to follow the guidance for commercial purpose programs. Considering this, the Hardware Installment Program does not maintain any governing Policies that set forth compliance standards and for the product based on the applicable requirements of: the Equal Credit Opportunity Act (ECOA), the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA), Section 5 of the FTC Act on Unfair or Deceptive Acts or Practices (UDAP), the Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (E-Sign), and relevant servicing and collections standards. 
 

 Additionally, procedures are not reviewed on a regular cadence. This contributed to: 1) an out of date the fraud procedure that refers to an escalation process that no longer exists, and 2) an incomplete set of documented decline reasons and macros.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: REF 2 - SIL Client Onboarding Q2 2023
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 33.— (1) A designated person shall apply the measures specified in F43[subsection (2)], in relation to a customer of the designated person—
 F44[(c) prior to carrying out any service for the customer, if, having regard to the circumstances, including—
 (i) the customer, or the type of customer, concerned,
 (ii) the type of any business relationship which the person has with the customer.
 

 Testing Results:
 A sample containing 225 merchants manually onboarded in Q4 2022 was reviewed and it was noted that 2 French Partnerships (0.9% of the population) were onboarded. GCMT also reviewed a sample of 2,375 merchants automatically onboarded and identified 1 FR Partnership (0.04% of the population) was automatically onboarded, however the account was subsequently reviewed by EU Compliance. 
 The onboarding process for FR Partnerships is outlined in the 'France Expansion Seller Product Compliance Requirements Document (CRD)', however this is not formalised in the SIL Square EEA KYC Procedures. 
 In light of this, GCMT liaised with EU Compliance for clarification and they noted that the correct entity type for these accounts should be 'association', and not 'partnership'. As the option for selecting 'association' as entity type is not available for merchants at the present, EU Compliance is currently advising merchants to register as partnership. EU Compliance also noted that they are onboarding France associations (incl. Spain) manually in the same way as GAA clubs in Ireland.
 Furthermore, GCMT reviewed a sample of 20 Quality Control reviews, one Spanish Limited Partnership was onboarded, however there is no formalized process for onboarding ‘Partnerships’ in Spain (including France and Ireland) within the SIL EEA KYC procedures.
Issue Rating Justification: It is unlikely that the finding will lead to regulatory criticism or reputational exposure. The finding relates to isolated deviations from Low Risk internal policies and procedures. The issue can be corrected in the ordinary course of business.
