Issue Title: The Monitoring review process was not completed in February, March and April 2023.
Issue Description:
Internal Policy Requirement:
 The Cash App Investing (CAI) Team currently has a monitoring review process in place of retail communications, internal communications, and correspondence. The review process is described in the CAI Compliance - Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) and periodic review (weekly, monthly, and quarterly) is conducted to determine for any violations of the firm's policies, meets industry rules and regulations and content standards and disclosures. This review is documented on the Advertising Records Database Tracking (google sheet) and Internal/Correspondence Tracking (google sheet) which includes various tabs review of communication and correspondence topics.
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT identified that the monitoring review process was not completed in February, March and April 2023. GCMT confirms that the monitoring review process was effective when performed in the period between 1 July 2022 and 30 June 2023.
RCA Description: The process is single point sensitive so when the DRI was out-of-the-office the monitoring process was not completed.
Issue Rating Justification: Low rated issue as internal policy requirement
Issue Progress Updates: GCMT recommends developing a contingency plan for key tasks and processes to ensure proper coverage is maintained when the primary responsible party is out of the office.
Issue Action Plans: Improving CAI compliance with internal policy requirement (1049600)
Closure Request Justification:
Received an email from Issue Owner William Matthews on February 6, 2024 with attached SOP Section and Corresponding Asana Tasks (google document) outline the CAI MAP Compliance Response: 
 

 CAI MAP Compliance Response:
 The root cause of the issue lied in the handover document that was provided to the individual covering. 
 

 The document stated “See SOPs. The scheduled dates are in Asana in the Communications and Advertising projects”
 

 The individual did not go through the SOPs but instead relied exclusively on the scheduled dates in Asana, a task management tool. Not all tasks however were included in Asana. 
 

 In order to eliminate this point of failure in the future, CAI Compliance is implementing a new procedures in its SOPs,
 

 Coverage of Designated Persons
 During absences of designated persons, each task to be completed should be appropriately recorded in a tool with the due date and the covering person assigned as the DRI. If a task can not be completed in time, a rationale should be entered as to the reasons why. 
 

 We are also ensuring that the SOP tasks have a matching Asana task which has been done as shown by the screenshots showing the one to one mapping.

Issue Title: BNPL AU - FX and charging for credit
Issue Description: This is a potential issue in relation to FX charges where customers use BNPL in AU and NZ to purchase from overseas merchants. The business asked whether whether they could change the way Afterpay charged FX fees from a spread across the major 4 banks (being a quasi retail rate) to a flat rate that made margin. Under the exemption in AU under which the BNPL product is issued, Afterpay cannot charge for credit (there is a specific exception relation to late fees). Charging for credit would take BNPL out of its exemption and make it regulated credit (ie create changes to onboarding, responsible lending checks, disclosure, reporting, governance). Legal advice was provided at the time this feature was implemented and is being reassessed in light of recent case law and whether changes need to be made to the current process to charge the Big 4 bank spread or whether we need to move to charging a a Visa / MC “or close” rate.
Issue Rating Justification: Unlikely based on current investigation but there would be consequences if Afterpay was not able to rely on the previous legal advice and had been operating outside credit exemption
Issue Progress Updates:
Made a recommendation to the business is that they move to a Visa or Mastercard rate or “close to”, which is covered by initial external advice. This may change if Legal are able to find any Bakers advice blessing the Big 4 spread approach, but in any case, Legal will need to consider this structure in light of case law.
 Current wholesale to Big 4 spread is approx AU$300-500k p.a for Australia and NZ$1M p.a. in New Zealand.
 It is likely that the “no charge for credit” restriction to fall away in AU when the BNPL Regs change.
 The business is now investigating what needs to happen to move to a Visa / MC “or close” rate.  
 Legal will engage external counsel in NZ to establish whether the change needs to occur there too.

Issue Title: The Square customer complaints processes can be uplifted to improve the effectiveness of handling complaints in accordance with standard operational procedures and regulatory requirements
Issue Description:
Complaint Processing and Handling 
 GCMT conducted testing of 21 Square complaints. The effectiveness rate for Complaint Handling as per the standard operating procedures (SOP) and regulation requirements was 87% for Square. 
 

 The areas for uplift consisted of: 
 The 14% ineffectiveness rate for Square (3 of 21 samples) relates to regulatory requirement exceptions. 
 - For 1 of 6 formal complaints, the final response did not include a statement to inform the complainant that if they remained dissatisfied with our response, their right to refer their complaint to the Financial Services and Pensions Ombudsman (FSPO).
 - For 3 of 15 non-complaints, there were missed opportunities to record a formal complaint. This consisted of: 
 >1 instance where the Customer Success Advocate (CSA) asked the Seller if they would like to make a formal complaint. When the Seller remained silent, the agent did not follow up to establish if they would like to make a formal complaint.
 >2 instances where the CSA did not offer the formal complaints process.
 

 Complaint Identification and Tagging 
 GCMT conducted testing on a random sample of 40 customer enquiries to determine CSA effectiveness for identifying complaints. We noted the following results: 
 4 out of 5 cases which were complaints, were not captured as a complaint. 
 

 Complaints Training 
 22 Square CSAs based in the Dublin Office were assigned the 2021 Complaints Training in July 2022. This resulted in them not being recorded as having completed the 2022 Complaints Training module.
 

 Oversight over Complaints Management processes
 There is no oversight or quality assurance activity performed by Square Complaints Management on the Ireland Complaints Manager processes to monitor the:
 - Use of the appropriate quick texts and template documents
 - Timeliness to respond to a complainant; and
 - Appropriateness of responses to the complaints scenarios.
Issue Rating Justification:
- Non-compliance with laws/regulations, supervisory guidance, or Block policy that if not corrected could subject the organization to regulatory criticism or have an entity level impact; and
 - The Issue relates to an internal control or process that is associated with a moderate risk activity that has a materially negative impact on the product (includes instances in which controls or other safeguards are noted to be absent).

Issue Title: The Square customer complaints processes can be uplifted to improve the effectiveness of handling complaints in accordance with standard operational procedures and regulatory requirements.
Issue Description:
Complaint Processing and Handling 
 GCMT conducted testing on a random sample of 60 Square (30 formal and 30 informal) complaints. The effectiveness rate for Complaint Handling per the standard operating procedures (SOP) and regulation requirements was 73% for Square. 
 

 The areas for uplift consisted of: 
 The 27% ineffectiveness rate for Square (16 of 60 samples) consists of 15% regulatory and 12% SOP exceptions.  
 

 Regulatory requirement exceptions 
 - For 3 of 60 complaints, Customer Success Advocates (CSA) did not acknowledge the complaint/use the appropriate macros to acknowledge the complaint or when offering to file a complaint. 
 - For 5 of 30 formal complaints, our final responses did not include;
 > A statement to inform the complainant that if they remain dissatisfied with our response, they may now refer their complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS)
 > The FOS website address; and
 > The time limits for a complainant to refer their complaint to the FOS.
 - 3 of 60 complaints were flagged as non-formal complaints instead of formal complaints, therefore the formal complaints process was not followed. As a result, one complaint was not provided a final response within 15 business days from the receipt of the complaint.
 

 SOP requirement exceptions
 -For 9 of 30 non-formal complaints, there were missed opportunities to record a formal complaint. This consisted of:
 >7 instances where a CSA did not offer the formal complaints process.
 >2 instances where the CSA asked the Seller if they would like to make a formal complaint. When the Seller remained silent, the agent did not follow up to establish if they would like to make a formal complaint.  
 

 Complaint Identification and Tagging 
 For 40 customer enquiries, we identified 4 out of 6 cases with expression of dissatisfaction where the CSA did not offer to file a formal complaint and did not flag the complaint on Salesforce. 
 

 Graded Customer Enquiries for Identification of Missed Complaints 
 For 60 graded customer enquiries, we identified 1 out of 2 instances where the CSA had offered to file a formal complaint upon identifying the dissatisfaction but had not flagged it on Salesforce. This was not picked up by the CSQA Grader. 
 

 Oversight over Complaints Management processes
 There is no oversight or quality assurance activity performed by Square Complaints Management on the UK Complaints Manager processes to monitor the:
 Use of the appropriate quick texts and template documents
 Timeliness to respond to a complainant; and
 Appropriateness of responses to the complaints scenarios. 
 

 Square - Compliance with FCA Handbook - DISP Dispute Resolution: Complaints
 The current Square Complaint Management Program does not align with the below FCA DISP requirements.
 - There is no formal or informal feedback loop with learnings for individuals or the department resulting from SEL Ombudsman complaint patterns. 
 - Square currently does not collect data around redress / settlements for complaints to be able to report this to the FCA in the annual Payment Services and Electronic Money Complaints Return.
Issue Rating Justification:
- Non-compliance with laws/regulations, supervisory guidance, or Block policy that if not corrected could subject the organization to regulatory criticism or have an entity level impact; and
 - The Issue relates to an internal control or process that is associated with a moderate risk activity that has a materially negative impact on the product (includes instances in which controls or other safeguards are noted to be absent).

Issue Title: The Cash App FSPO tracker is not designed for managing and reporting Ombudsman Complaints
Issue Description:
Cash App records and manages complaints received from the Financial Services and Pensions Ombudsman (FSPO) in the IE Complaint Tracker. 
 

 The tracker data points are not pertinent to FSPO Complaints as: 
 - The data fields do not cater for tracking multiple due dates related to requests received from the Ombudsman for a single complaint. Rather, the data fields track when our first response is due to be provided to the Ombudsman. Therefore, a CERT Associate has to manually check when additional Ombudsman requests are due to be provided.
 - The “Date Resolved” field is not used to capture the resolved date, rather the date entered here is being used to capture Cash App’s first response date to the Ombudsman. As a result the date of Ombudsman closure is not being captured and could lead to incorrect reporting of FSPO resolution timeframes. 
 - The fields "Date Holding Letter Sent" and "Date Response Due >15 days" are used within the tracker. However, these dates are applicable to internal dispute resolution complaints not Ombudsman Complaints.
 - There is no field to track whether any remediation e.g. refunds provided to the complainant as instructed by the Ombudsman.
 

 GCMT identified that the Cash App UK/IE Monthly Complaints Report outlines that the Ombudsman dictates the response due date for each complaint; however the processing of complaints must still meet the SLA’s of 3/15/35 business days. These SLAs reported are not consistent with the FSPO complaint resolution timelines.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: The issue detracts from sound governance, internal control, or risk management principles.
Issue Progress Updates:
Update the various data points within tooling to uplift the existing handling of FSPO complaints:
 - Track due dates of multiple requests from the FSPO to ensure that all required due dates are met
 - Use the date of FSPO complaint closure as the ""Date Resolved"" 
 - Remove ""Date Holding Letter Sent"" and ""Date Response Due > 15 days"" data fields to avoid confusion of the internal dispute resolution requirements with Ombudsman requirements
 - Add a field to track whether the (any) remediation (e.g. refunds back to the complainant as instructed by the Ombudsman) has been completed.

Issue Title: The Cash App customer complaints processes can be uplifted to improve in accordance with SOP and regulatory requirements.
Issue Description:
Complaint Processing and Handling 
 GCMT conducted testing on a random sample of 60 Cash complaints. The effectiveness rate for Complaint Handling per the standard operating procedures (SOP) and regulation requirements was 75% for Cash App.
 

 The areas for uplift consisted of: 
 The 25% ineffectiveness rate for Cash App (15 of 60 samples) consists of 6% regulatory and 19% SOP exceptions.
 Regulatory requirement exceptions

 - 3 of 60 complaints whereby Cash Customer Operations (CCO) Advocates/Escalations team did not acknowledge a complaint via chat/messaging or email. However, the complaints were escalated to the Cash Executive Relations Team (CERT) and customers were provided a final response.
 

 SOP requirement exceptions
 -5 of 8 instances (from 60 samples) whereby CERT did not provide an update to the customer where their complaint could not be resolved within 3 working days per the published (public) Cash App Support Complaints Process Article. The internal UK Complaint Handling Procedure does not align with the processes in place and with the content of the public article as there is no reference to the 3 working day customer update.
 -9 of 60 instances whereby CCO Advocates did not identify and escalate a complaint in an earlier case related to the same customer dissatisfaction. 
 >For 3 of 9 instances CCO did not raise a complaint when a customer requested manager escalation. GCMT notes the UK Complaints Salesforce Knowledge Article does not align with the Handling Pushback and Manager Escalations Salesforce Knowledge Article for the requirement to raise a complaint, when a customer requests for manager escalation. 
 

 Complaint Identification and Tagging 
 For 60 customer enquiries, we identified 21 out of 25 cases with expressions of dissatisfaction where CCO Advocates failed to acknowledge by using the “Cash App Global {Complaints - General}” quick text. 
 

 Graded Customer Enquiries for Identification of Missed Complaints 
 GCMT tested 60 general Cash Customer Operations Quality Assurance graded tickets to determine if the grader identified missed complaints by the Advocates and noted this in the scorecards. 
 There were 4 complaints out of 60 graded customer enquiries. We identified 2 instance where the CCO Advocate did not identify or flag the complaint from the customer interaction. This was not picked up by the CCO QA Grader.

Issue Title: The Cash App UK Financial Ombudsman Services tracker is not designed for managing and reporting Ombudsman Complaints [Cash App UK]
Issue Description:
Cash App records and manages complaints received from the UK Financial Ombudsman Services (FOS) in a UK complaints tracker.
 

 The tracker data points are not pertinent to Ombudsman Complaints as: 
 - The data fields do not cater for tracking multiple due dates related to requests received from the Ombudsman for a single complaint. Rather, the data fields track when our first response is due to be provided to the Ombudsman. Therefore, a CERT Associate has to manually check when additional Ombudsman requests are due to be provided.
 From sample testing, we identified 7 of 18 complaints where there were delays in responding to the FOS requests.
 The "Date Response Due" field is auto-populated with the date after 15 business days of the initial date of complaint received from the FOS. The due date set by the FOS for Cash App to provide the complaint response is usually 10 business days from the date of initial complaint received. This misalignment in due dates may cause CERT to respond to FOS late. From sample testing, we identified 3 of 7 FOS responses that were provided past the 10 business days response time.  
 

 - The “Date Resolved” field is not used to capture the complaint resolution date, rather the date entered here is being used to capture Cash App’s first response date to the FOS. As a result, the complaint resolution date is not being captured and could lead to incorrect reporting of FOS resolution timeframes. 
 

 - The fields "Date Holding Letter Sent" and "Date Response Due >15 days" are used within the tracker. However, these dates are applicable to internal dispute resolution complaints not Ombudsman Complaints.
 - There is no field to track whether any remediation has been completed e.g. refunds provided to the complainant as instructed by the Ombudsman. 
 

 GCMT identified that the Cash App UK/IE Monthly Complaints Report outlines that the Ombudsman dictates the response due date for each complaint; however the processing of complaints must still meet the SLA’s of 3/15/35 business days. These SLAs imposed do not meet the Ombudsman Complaints timelines as they may request multiple responses or information to be provided within set timelines, when we are responding to a complaint.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: The issue detracts from sound governance, internal control, or risk management principles.
Issue Progress Updates:
Update the various data points within tooling to uplift the existing handling of FOS complaints:
 - Track due dates of multiple requests from the FOS to ensure that all required due dates are met
 - Use the date of FOS complaint closure as the "Date Resolved" 
 - Remove "Date Holding Letter Sent" and "Date Response Due > 15 days" data fields to avoid confusion of the internal dispute resolution requirements with Ombudsman requirements
 - Add a field to track whether the (any) remediation (e.g. refunds back to the complainant as instructed by the Ombudsman) has been completed.
Closure Request Justification:
Closure completed -- see action plan for details. 
 

 CCPM Insights team developed and deployed a new and improved FOS Google Sheet tracker for use by CCOT CERT Global Specialists, the people responsible for managing FOS complaints. This sheet remediates the issues identified in the GCMT finding, and resulted in the deprecation of the previous tracker.
 

 Cash Business Systems team implemented requested changes to the Global Complaints Record form in Cash App’s customer relationship management tool, CF1. However this tool is does not replace the FOS Google Sheet to track and manage FOS Complaints as originally intended in the MAP. Given that UK Cash App product was being deprecated, the impetus to update the tooling in CF1 to the extent that CCOT CERT detailed in the original remediation plan changed. Both the team and partners on tooling acknowledge that the complaint tracking in the sheet is more than sufficient for managing and reporting on post-product deprecation complaints.

Issue Title: Square does not have a formalized Data Retention Policy and practices. [Square Canada]
Issue Description:
Background:
 The PIPEDA privacy principles for data retention and deletion require the following:
 Principle 4.5 states that,“personal information shall be retained only as long as necessary for the fulfillment of those purposes.”; 
 Principle 4.5.2 states that,“organizations should develop guidelines and implement procedures with respect to the retention of personal information. These guidelines should include minimum and maximum retention periods.”; &
 Principle 4.5.3 states that,“personal information that is no longer required to fulfill the identified purposes should be destroyed, erased, or made anonymous. Organizations shall develop guidelines and implement procedures to govern the destruction of personal information.”
 

 Finding Description: 
 GCMT reviewed a SPADE dated 2021 stating that, “Square does not have a data retention policy or a systematic mechanism across products and services to delete data after any set period of time, which subjects Square to legal risk of non-compliance with GDPR in Europe and CRPA in California. Further, Square’s practice of retaining data indefinitely places us at greater risk of high penalties for failure to implement data minimization principles, especially in the event of a data breach or other privacy incident.”
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Consumer Protections: Canada’s Anti-Spam Legislation (CASL) & Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA) (1012090)
Issue Rating Justification: The draft Block Data Retention Policy is not formalized and there are no current practices in place to support the implementation of the data framework for the retention and deletion of customers personal information within the customer lifecycle.
Issue Action Plans: Data Retention Updates (1054259)
Closure Request Justification: Supporting attachments/links are in the Action Plan

Issue Title: No direct hyperlink to our Privacy Policy and does not require a customer to tick a ‘checkbox’
Issue Description:
Background:
 The Consent and Openness principles of PIPEDA require the following:
 The knowledge and consent of the individual are required for the collection, use, or disclosure of personal information, except where inappropriate;
 Consent can be obtained in many ways such as through signing an application form, a checkbox, orally, or at time of use of a product or service;
 An organization must make detailed information about its policies and practices relating to the management of personal information publicly and readily available.
 

 Finding Description: 
 The Canada customer account creation process obtains a customers agreement (consent) for Square Terms and ESign consent "by (them) continuing" to create an account with us. Whilst there are different permitted ways of obtaining consent, the Canada process varies when compared to the Square approach in other markets (e.g, see the U.S. and U.K extract below.). In those markets a customer is required to 'tick' a checkbox to accept the Square Terms, Privacy Policy and ESign consent.
Engagement Title: Consumer Protections: Canada’s Anti-Spam Legislation (CASL) & Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA) (1012090)
Issue Rating Justification: GCMT confirmed that the Canada approach to obtaining consent is okay and legally enforceable as users can access and agree to the Privacy Policy (Notice) by selecting and reviewing the General Terms. Notwithstanding this, the Canadian approach to obtaining a customers consent to the Square Privacy Policy (Notice) can be uplifted to request a customer to click a 'checkbox" to align with the customer consent process in other markets, unless there is a business or regulatory reason not to follow the process of other markets.
Issue Action Plans: Update Square Canada account signup page (1054883)

Issue Title: Inability for customers to make complaints against Afterpay by mail
Issue Description:
AAPL's terms of service section '8.2 Disputes between you and us', state: 
 

 (b) If you wish to raise a dispute or submit a complaint to us, you should do so by contacting us using the details listed below. Disputes should be raised with us as soon as possible.
 

 Email: info@afterpay.com.au
 

 Mail: GPO Box 2269, Melbourne, VIC 3001, Australia
 

 AAPL does not have a process in place to manage and process complaints sent by customers in the mail.
Issue Rating Justification: While there is a direct risk to non-compliance with AU complaint handling obligations, it's unlikely customer's will try to correspond with us by mail to submit a complaint, particularly given AAPL's product are digitally native targeted to digitally literate consumers. One example that may present challenges is incarcerated customers without access to digital communication channels.
Issue Progress Updates: AAPL operationalise a process to accept and manage complaints sent by mail in line with AU complaint handling obligations. Alternatively, Legal and Compliance review whether the terms of service should be changed to remove the option for a customer to submit a complaint by mail.
Closure Request Justification: This issue has been reviewed by the ANZ Legal team and Jane Francis confirmed it is non-reportable, with no further Legal or Compliance action required so can be closed.

Issue Title: The Anti-Bribery and Corruption Risk Assessment has not been reviewed and kept current. [Square and Cash App - UK]
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement / Best Practice Guidance;
 There is an expectation for firms to have an updated Risk Assessment in place, which is periodic, informed, documented and kept up to date. The below requirements were tested.
 

 FCA Handbook, Guidance and Principles, Risk Assessment - FCG 2.2.4, FGC 6.2.2. 
 FCA Handbook, Guidance and Principles, Dealing with third parties - FCG 6.2.4
 The Bribery Act 2010, Principle 3 - Risk Assessment
 The Bribery Act 2010, Principle 4 - Due Diligence 
 

 Specifically, the Bribery Act 2010 - Principle 3, Risk Assessment states that the commercial organization assesses the nature and extent of its exposure to potential external and internal risks of bribery on its behalf by persons associated with it through performing a risk assessment. The assessment is periodic, informed and documented. 
 

 Finding Description:
 The most recent Global Anti-Bribery and Corruption Risk Assessment was completed in 2021. The Assessment rated inherent and residual risks as mostly high/medium and included recommendations to reduce the residual risk level. However, the Risk Assessment has not been kept current and the original recommendations have not been actively followed up to inform ABC activities, controls and ultimately risk levels. At the time that the Risk Assessment was completed, there were no formal requirements and timelines in place for the business to remediate the risks and recommendations that were identified in the assessment. 
 

 As an example, the risk identified for Third Party Invoicing with critical recommendations has not been closed off. This risk has linkages to the Financial Conduct Authority guidelines section 6.2.4 Dealing with Third Parties whereby "We (the FCA) expect firms to take adequate and risk-sensitive measures to address the risk that a third party acting on behalf of the firm may engage in corruption".
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Anti-Bribery and Corruption / Gifts and Entertainment Review (1013400)
Issue Rating Justification: The issue has been rated as moderate, in line with the Result Rating matrix. The moderate rating in the matrix applies to non compliance with procedures and policies and is for an issue which cannot be corrected in the ordinary case of business. As an updated ethics assessment needs to be performed, with coverage of anti-bribery and corruption, therefore the moderate rating is appropriate as this is a regulatory requirement (Per the UK Bribery Act 2010).
Issue Progress Updates:
1) Perform a Risk Assessment for Anti-Bribery and Corruption on an periodic basis as expected by the regulatory obligations listed in the The Bribery Act 2010 (Principle 3 - Risk Assessment). 
 2) Implement a process to track the risk items which are identified during the Risk Assessment and assign designated risk owners to each item to ensure there is a plan to remediate the risks.
Issue Action Plans: Anti Bribery and Corruption / Gifts and Entertainment 2023 Review - Ethics Risk Assessment for 2024 (1058180)

Issue Title: Customer Due Diligence [Cash App Lending - US ]
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement:
 FFIEC BSA/AML Examination Manual, Customer Due Diligence: 
 

 At a minimum, the bank must establish risk-based CDD procedures that - 
  - Enable the bank to understand the nature and purpose of the customer relationship in order to develop a customer risk profile. 
  - Enable the bank to conduct ongoing monitoring 
  - for the purpose of identifying and reporting suspicious transactions and,
  - on a risk basis, to maintain and update customer information, including information regarding the beneficial owner(s) of legal entity customers.
 

 Customers that pose higher money laundering or terrorist financing risks, (i.e.,higher risk profile customers), present increased risk exposure to banks. As a result, due diligence policies, procedures, and processes should define both when and what additional customer information will be collected based on the customer risk profile and the specific risks posed...Based on the customer risk profile, the bank may consider obtaining, at account opening (and throughout the relationship), more customer information in order to understand the nature and purpose of the customer relationship... Performing an appropriate level of ongoing due diligence that is commensurate with the customer’s risk profile is especially critical in understanding the customer’s transactions in order to assist the bank in determining when transactions are potentially suspicious. 
 

 31 CFR §1022.210, 31 CFR §1020.210 
 

 Policy Requirement: (ARCHIVED)
 Cash Lending BSA/AML Policy (ARCHIVED) 
  - Page 4, Mitigating Factors states, "Cash Lending mitigates the money-laundering risks associated with its Customers in several ways for example, by: Monitoring potentially unusual prepayment transactional activity to capture excessive pre-payments by the Customer for suspicious activity;
  - Page 7, Enhanced Due Diligence states, "Cash Lending recognizes that certain Customers or products may pose higher money-laundering or terrorist-financing risks and therefore may require the application of Enhanced Due Diligence (“EDD”) procedures. The criteria for whether Cash Lending should apply EDD procedures is based primarily on the product offering, but may also include one or more of the following: (i) Cash Lending’s BSA/AML/OFAC risk assessment, (ii) the Customer’s prior account activity, (iii) explanations or lack thereof for changes in the Customer’s linked bank account, (iv) activity on other related Cash Lending account relationships, or (v) excessive loan overpayment.
 

 The Cash Lending Enhanced Due Diligence Program states: "The specific trigger parameters in place include but are not limited to: An out-of-pattern single payment of at least $5,000; Multiple payments to the same loan or to loans under the same Social Security Number (SSN) that totals at least $5,000; and A payment that exceeds the outstanding loan balance by at least $5,000."
 

 Testing Result:
 There are no Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) processes in place for Cash Borrow/ Lending customers. The Cash Lending Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) Program describes Cash Lending parameters triggering EDD reviews, however these parameters are not in place. GCMT notes a Cash Compliance Customer Onboarding and Management Cash App Enhanced Due Diligence Procedure is in place which outlines the EDD activities for Cash App. This process holistically reviews a customer’s Cash App account during EDD. However, the signal prompting an EDD review does not include lending features.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: A moderate yet undesirable level of risk to Block that requires management attention to mitigate the risk adequately. Remedial corrective actions must be addressed in a reasonable timeframe.
Issue Action Plans:
Customer Due Diligence - Review and archive outdated policy documents (1059507)
 Customer Due Diligence - Lending Criteria for EDD Reviews (1059511)
Closure Request Justification:
Action 1059507 
 04-30-24: Cash Customer Operations (CCO) archived the outdated Cash Lending EDD Program document since a Cash App EDD Program is in place. Archive evidence was provided on April 19, 2024.
 

 This management action plan is complete.
 

 Action 1059511
 July 8, 2025: The business launched the continuous Customer Risk Rating (cCRR) model v2 on May 19, 2025. During model training, the business tested Borrow features that are listed in the ‘CashApp cCRR v2 Model Specs Tables’ > ‘all_features_used_in_training’ tab. The final cCRR model v2 includes the ‘ratio of the customer’s average weekly Borrow Repayment total transaction count in the past 14 days relative to the weekly average from the customer’s historical behavior in the past three months.’ Refer to the ‘Cash App cCRR vs Model Spec Tables’ > ‘final_model_selected_features_01_07_tune_25’ tab > Row 552. 
 

 Model design included a training period of active Cash App accounts between January 1, 2020 - March 31, 2024. The model assessed an account’s AML risk by reviewing the account’s creation date and any negative action date to capture a variety of behaviors and aid model development. For model design and specification details, refer to the Cash App - US cCRR v2 System Design and Model Spec documents.
 

 All Cash App accounts receive a cCRR score seven days after account creation and every seven days for any attempted money movement within the preceding 30 days. The score is formed on the account level which links customer information by SSN for a holistic view. The scores will assign a risk level of high, medium, and low. Refer to the Cash App - US cCRR v2 System Design and Model Spec.
 

 Account scores resulting in a high risk level will prompt an EDD review. The EDD team holistically reviews the customer’s account such as customer information (adverse media, criminal records, PEP status, etc.), connected activities, linked bank information, and transactions analysis including Borrow activity (payment sources, reasonable customer activity, unusual velocity, etc.) Refer to the EDD Walkthrough example.
 

 Refer to the Action Plan for supporting documentation. 
 

 This management action plan is complete.

Issue Title: Questionable Activity Reports (QARs)
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement 
 FFIEC Examination Manual, Suspicious Activity Reporting: Suspicious activity monitoring and reporting are critical internal controls. Proper monitoring and reporting processes are essential to ensuring that the bank has an adequate and effective BSA compliance program. Appropriate policies, procedures, and processes should be in place to monitor and identify unusual activity. 
 

 31 CFR §1022.320, 31 CFR §1020.320 
 

 Policy Requirement: (ARCHIVED)
 The Cash Lending BSA/AML Policy Page 12 states (I)- Questionable Activity Investigation Reports: "Cash Lending is not required to file a suspicious activity report (“SAR”) for suspicious activity and instead follows specific business partner reporting obligations established by contract to refer potentially suspicious activity to the business partner. Cash Lending is required to notify its business partner of suspicious activity by sending a Questionable Activity Investigation (“QAI”) Report to the business partner within thirty (30) calendar days of detection of the potentially suspicious activity."
 

 Testing Result: 
 Questionable Activity Reports (QAR) were recently implemented by the Financial Crimes team in July 2023. However, there are no formalized guides in place for the QAR process.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

QAR Process Guide (1059513)

FinCrimes developed formal training for the Transaction Monitoring and Suspicious Activity Reporting teams covering red flags and AML risks for the Borrow product. Issue Title: Information Sharing [Cash App Lending - US]
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement:
 FFIEC BSA/AML Examination Manual, Assessing Compliance with BSA Regulatory Requirements - Special Information Sharing Procedures
 A law enforcement agency investigating terrorist activity or money laundering may request that FinCEN solicit, on the investigating agency's behalf, certain information from a financial institution or a group of financial institutions. 
 

 31 CFR 1010.520
 

 Policy Requirement: (ARCHIVED) 
 The Cash Lending BSA & AML Policy (ARCHIVED), pages 15-16, 314 (a) and (b) states, "Square Capital is not a financial institution; however, our business partner may receive such requests and pass them to Square Capital, in which case Square Capital follows all guidelines and requirements for handling 314(a) and 314(b) requests".
 

 The Cash Lending 314 (a) & (b) Policy page 5 states, "The Bank Partner is the loan originator for Cash Lending loans. They are the regulated entity for purposes of 314(a) requests and receive any such requests directly from FinCEN. They in turn send these requests to Cash Lending for handling, usually on a bi-weekly cadence." 
 

 The Cash Lending 314 (a) & (b) Policy page 6 states, "Section 314(b) addresses information sharing permitted between financial institutions. Participation in the information sharing process within Section 314(b) is voluntary. The Bank Partner does not participate in the 314(b) program at this time. Should that situation change, this section will be modified to accommodate any alterations to Cash App Lending requirements."
 

 Testing Results:
 The Cash Lending 314(a) and 314(b) Policy and the Servicing Team related SAR Procedures were implemented for receiving 314(a) requests from the bank partner. However, GCMT was unable to identify a team or process responsible for receiving and processing these requests from the bank partner.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: A moderate yet undesirable level of risk to Block that requires management attention to mitigate the risk adequately. Remedial corrective actions must be addressed in a reasonable timeframe.
Issue Action Plans: Information Sharing - Partner Bank Expectations and Obligations (1060171)
Closure Request Justification:
The following process had been updated to the attached 314(a) procedures for Cash App. No exceptions noted. Issue deemed closed. 
 Cash App Lending Products
 Our 314(a) information sharing process for Cash Lending products is fully automated and occurs on a bi-weekly basis. The process is as follows:
 Data Transmission: First Electronic Bank (FEB) transmits a CSV file directly from the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) containing 314(a) subject lists to Block via SFTP. This encrypted file is sent to a designated FEB folder location (toFEB/FinCEN).
 Matching Criteria: Block screens all the accounts of all Cash App Lending borrowers against the 314(a) subject list provided by FEB. Matches are identified based on the following criteria:
 Name + Date of Birth (DOB) + Zip Code; or
 Full Nine-Digit Social Security Number (SSN)
 Report Generation: Block generates an external report for transmission back to FEB, via SFTP, with the results of any matches, if there are no matches the report is still generated with 0 results. 
 External Report Details: The external report sent to FEB includes the following data points for each identified match: Tracking number, date of search, match method, customer’s full name, account number, account open date, account status, SSN, DOB, street, city, state, zip code, and country. 
 Record Retention: An internal report of all matches is stored securely within an S3 bucket for internal record-keeping and review.

Issue Title: Cash Lending OFAC Policy - Blocked Property Reporting [Cash App Lending - US]
Issue Description:
Policy Requirement: 
 The OFAC Policy (Cash Lending), Pages 4-7 Governance, Roles, and Responsibilities and Blocked Property Access page 4 states; "Bank Partner - Sends the Blocked Property report to OFAC within 10 business calendar days of the blocking action; and Where Cash Lending is holding blocked property as of June 30 in a given year, submit an annual Blocked Property report to OFAC by September 30."
 Page 7 states: 
 A. "In the event that a Potential Hit is confirmed with OFAC, the Cash Lending Team: notifies the Bank Partner within one business day of the determination."
 B. "In the event that Cash Lending identifies a Confirmed Hit under this process, Cash Lending has an affirmative obligation to report the matter to the Bank Partner within 5 business days of the blocking action as stated above. 
 

 Testing Results:
 The OFAC Policy (Cash Lending)”policy” does not align with the current practices of reporting to the bank partner. 
 The policy states that Cash Lending reports confirmed matches to the bank partner within one business day. However, Cash Lending currently provides OFAC Periodic Screening Results, including confirmed hit(s), to the bank partner on a monthly basis. 
 

 To date, there have been no Blocked Property events, however should an account be blocked at anytime, there is no provision to capture this within the existing reporting to the bank partner.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: A moderate yet undesirable level of risk to Block that requires management attention to mitigate the risk adequately. Remedial corrective actions must be addressed in a reasonable timeframe.
Issue Action Plans: Blocked Property Reporting - Program updates and obligations (1060178)
Closure Request Justification:
March 18, 2025: Cash Lending OFAC reporting to its bank partner, First Electronic Bank (FEB), occurs monthly via the “OFAC Periodic Screening Results” report submitted by Strategic Partnership. Data for the report is compiled from the data from the FEB Borrow Monthly OFAC Report.
 The FEB Borrow Monthly OFAC Report provides a list of potential OFAC screening hits. The most recent report, February 2025, had two false positives results.
 The most recent February 2025 OFAC Periodic Screening Results reported to FEB accurately reflect the searched records and false positives.
 Additionally, the business implemented a weekly BSA report which provides the ‘Number Borrow Customers w/o 7d OFAC / % Borrow Customers w/o 7d OFAC.” February weekly reporting and the most recent week, commencing March 10, 2025, shows that all Borrow Customers were screened for OFAC within 7 days of disbursing a loan (an FEB requirement).
 

 The Cash Lending OFAC Policy has been archived. Cash Lending follows the block level AML/CTF/Sanctions-related Policies. The Global Sanctions Compliance Program Standard, Page 10, Section 4.2.4 requires a daily sanctions screening which exceeds FEB requirements. The daily screening is fulfilled at the Cash App account level. This process is completed for Cash App Lending in addition to OFAC screening prior to loan disbursement as demonstrated in the BSA Report (FEB requires screening within 7 days of loan disbursement).
 

 Refer to the Action Plan for supporting documentation. 
 

 This management action plan is complete.

Issue Title: REF 2 - SEL Client Onboarding Q2 2023
Issue Description:
1. Requirement:
 5.3.246 The firm should obtain the following in relation to the charity or church body:
 ➢ Full name and address
 ➢ Nature of body’s activities and objects
 ➢ Name(s) of Settlor(s) [if any]
 ➢ Names of all trustees (or equivalent)
 ➢ Names or classes of beneficiaries
 5.3.247 The existence of the charity can be verified from a number of different sources, depending on whether the charity is registered or not, a place of worship or an independent school or college.
 

 1. Testing Results:
 The SEL Onboarding Flow and the IDV Managed Payments UK Procedure do not include the information collection process for charities, and the UK Seller KYC Standard Operating Procedure (Master) does not include the following requirements:
 ➢ Name(s) of Settlor(s) [if any]
 ➢ Names or classes of beneficiaries
 

 

 2. Requirement:
 5.3.38 Firms should recognise that some documents are more easily forged or counterfeited than others. If suspicions are raised in relation to any document offered, firms should take whatever practical and proportionate steps are available to establish whether the document offered has been reported as lost or stolen.
 

 2. Testing Results:
 SEL doesn't have a documented process outlining the steps available to establish whether the document offered has been reported as lost or stolen.
 

 

 3. Requirement:
 Regulation 30A(1)
 5.3.129A Firms must obtain proof of registration or an excerpt of the register of the company, unregistered company, the limited liability partnership as the case may be, or the registrar in the case of an eligible Scottish partnership, before establishing a business relationship (with UK entities). The information required relates to persons of significant control (PSC) as per the PSC registers and may be obtained from the customer, Companies House, or a third party provider.
 If the firm finds a discrepancy between information relating to the beneficial ownership of the company which it collects as above, and information which becomes available to it whilst carrying out its duties under the ML Regulations (during its onboarding process), the discrepancy must be reported to Companies House.
 Beneficial ownership in this context means a person of significant control (PSC) per the information held in the PSC register and not as defined in the ML Regulations. Information on the PSC register may thus differ from other beneficial ownership information and not necessarily be inaccurate.
 Discrepancies should be material to be reportable. For example, a material discrepancy would arise when there is a missing or different person (legal or natural) recorded, as compared between information in the PSC register and information obtained at onboarding. The material discrepancy report should be made as soon as reasonably possible when discovered. A discrepancy itself does not prohibit the onboarding of a customer – the nature and relevance of the discrepancy may be assessed by firms with their CDD process and risk-based approach during onboarding, and considering whether there are reasonable grounds for suspicion. A discrepancy report is not a substitute for a suspicious transaction report (SAR) and the requirement to submit a SAR where appropriate remains,
 Firms are not required to check for or report discrepancies involving existing customers.
 For further information (including what Companies House could
 constitute as a material discrepancy) see:
 https://www.gov.uk/guidance/report-a-discrepancy-about-a-beneficialowner-on-the-psc-register-by-an-obliged-entity#when-to-make-adiscrepancy-report.
 

 3. Testing Results:
 SEL doesn't have a documented process in place for reporting discrepancies of information relating to the beneficial ownership to the Companies House.
 

 

 4. Requirement:
 5.3.154 Some consideration should be given as to whether documents relied upon are forged. In addition, if they are in a foreign language, appropriate steps should be taken to be reasonably satisfied that the documents in fact provide evidence of the customer’s identity.
 

 4. Testing Results:
 SEL doesn't have a documented process in place for forged documents, and documents in different languages.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: It is unlikely that the finding will lead to regulatory criticism or reputational exposure.
Issue Progress Updates:
1. Compliance Operations should include the below requirements in the UK Seller KYC Standard Operating Procedure (Master).
 ➢ Name(s) of Settlor(s) [if any]
 ➢ Names or classes of beneficiaries 
 

 2. Compliance Operations should document the process (e.g., procedure or guideline) which outlines practical steps on identifing whether documents provided by the customer have been reported as lost or stolen. 
 

 3. Compliance Operations should document the process (e.g., procedure or guideline) for reporting material discrepancies of information relating to the beneficial ownership (a person of significant control) of a company to the Companies house. 
 

 4. Compliance Operations should document the process (e.g., procedure or guideline) for identifying whether customer documents are forged. In addition, the framework should include a process for analysing customer documents which are in a foreign language to ensure the documents represent the true identity of the customer.
Issue Action Plans:
1. charity or church body requirement (1060827)
 2. documents lost or stolen requirement (1060829)
 3. discrepancies of information relating to the beneficial ownership to the Companies House requirement (1060831)
 4. forged documents, and documents in different languages requirement (1060833)

 → 2 (0.4%) accounts incorrectly activated manually
 → 1 (0.2%) account deactivated (see comment in row 15)

 • 8 (0.6%) missing bank account information for bank linking for beneficial owners (see comment in row 16 above)
 • 1,127 (83.8%) missing phone number for beneficial owners (see comment in row 15 above)
 

 ➤ Partnerships, General Partnerships and Unincorporated Associations - 32 (1.7% of the total population)
 • 28 (87.5%) missing business trading address
 • 23 (71.9%) missing beneficial ownership
 • 9 (28.1%) missing phone number for beneficial owners (see comment in row 15 above)

 2. Compliance Operations should ensure that the information required for the different merchant typologies is obtained in line with the 'UK Seller KYC Standard Operating Procedure (Master)' once updated by UK Compliance (see row 15 above).

Issue Title: 9 Square EU merchants onboarded between July 1, 2022 and June 30, 2023 were not screened at the onboarding. [Square - SIL]
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement:
 Section 10 of International Financial Sanctions (10.3.3 Screening Customers against Sanctions Lists) in the Anti-Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism Guidelines for the Financial Sector (Revised 23 June 2021) states that: 
 Firms should have effective screening systems appropriate to the nature, size and risk of their business. Screening new and existing customers and payments against the relevant and up to date EU and UN lists helps ensure that Firms will not breach the sanctions regulations. Customer screening should take place at the time of customer take-on and at regular intervals thereafter.  
 

 Policy Requirement
 The ’SIL EEA Seller Screening Procedure’ (pg 4) states “The Merchant information (including account holder, directors, business partners, beneficial owners and business name) is screened for possible Sanction and PEP matches within 24 hours of fully enrolling for an account as part of Square’s onboarding process”.
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT identified a total of 9 Square EU merchants onboarded between July 1, 2022 and June 30, 2023, not screened at the onboarding.
 

 Square Trusted Identity stated that when the Compliance Operations team uses the 'activate' action in the system, it manually activates the seller and bypasses all other requirements our automated systems expect. Those requirements are not marked as met in the square systems, which means that there are downstream side effects - like screening not being conducted. 
 

 When the Compliance Operations team uses the 'approve' action in the system, it manually verifies the legal entity of the seller. If that was the only activation requirement outstanding, then the seller will be activated too. If they still needed to link their bank account, they will still need to do this. By manually verifying the legal entity with this action, it ensures that other downstream systems behave in the expected way - case in point, screening will occur.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: SIL Adverse Media, Sanctions and PEP Screening Review Q3, 2023 (1014078)
Issue Rating Justification: This issue is rated as medium as some merchants were not being screened at onboarding and this is not in line with the regulatory requirements. AML is a high risk area for Europe (Ireland).
Issue Action Plans:
1. Compliance Ops SIL SOP update (1072704)
 2. Compliance Operations will screen the merchants missed at onboarding, and will work with the engineering team to uncover why these merchants were not screened. (1091195)
Closure Request Justification: A new issue has been created at Issue ID 1127176 and approved by Keith Roche.

Issue Title: Line 0 agents are advising customers incorrectly of an amount outstanding and/or before their account can be reinstated.
Issue Description:
Full Title:
 When customers have settled their account balance with a Debt Collection Agency for a lesser amount, Line 0 agents are advising customers incorrectly of an amount outstanding and/or before their account can be reinstated.  
 

 Background
 Afterpay/Clearpay run settlement campaigns with DCAs, whereby the DCAs send settlement offers to customers that have a “Written Off” account status with an overdue balance. When a customer accepts the offer and makes the payment, the collections team notes the overdue account as “satisfied” i.e paid in full. Afterpay/Clearpay will not reinstate the customer’s account following the payment. Due to current system limitations of the Admin Portal, the customer’s account shows an amount owing i.e the balance cannot be adjusted to $0. 
 

 Finding
 GCMT tested 75 global samples (random) of DCA settlement offers that were accepted and paid by customer’s, whereby 25 customers had reached out to Line 0 agents to enquire about their account balance or the possibility of account reinstatement. The Line 0 agents effectiveness rate for providing the correct information on the customer’s enquiry was 60%. The 40% ineffectiveness rate consisted of 10 exceptions from 25 inquiries across United States (US) and United Kingdom (UK):
 

 US
 -For two samples, when a customer enquired to reopen their account, the Line 0 agent incorrectly advised there was an amount outstanding to Afterpay and to log in to make payment to bring their account up to date. 
 

 UK 
 -For 6 of 23 samples, where a customer enquired why their account had an outstanding balance when they had settled their account with the DCA, the Line 0 agent incorrectly advised that the customer’s account has an amount outstanding to Clearpay and to log in to make payment to bring their account up to date.
 -2 of 23 samples, where a customer provided documents to be verified for a new account after settling the account with the DCA, the Line 0 agent advised the customer was only allowed 1 account and they are required to settle the outstanding balance in the main account before they can use Clearpay. Both customers paid the remaining balance, however only 1 customer was notified that Clearpay was declining the service by a different Line 0 agent. 
 

 GCMT noted the following customer interactions which may indicate an opportunity to uplift the process for handling by Line 0 agents for DCA Settlement Campaign conditions:
 -1 customer said they were misled on the discount and thought they were able to use their account again when the Collections agent advised that Clearpay was declining the service.
 -4 email interactions where a Line 0 agent incorrectly advised the customer that their account has an amount outstanding to Clearpay but this was corrected by another Line 0 or Collections agent. 
 

 GCMT identified the following variances in the regional standard operating procedures;
 The US DCA Settlement and UK CRS Settlement Campaign SOPs do not have a requirement to specifically note “Do Not Reinstate” on the customer’s account when the settlement paid is considered “Paid In Full” by the customer. However, the Australia and New Zealand (ANZ) DCA Settlement SOP contains this.
 When a customer enquires on their availability to spend, the US and ANZ DCA Settlement SOP outlines the requirement to use the “Account Status:: is Written Off Paid in Full” macro which communicates that the account has been repaid and closed and that Afterpay is declining the use of services. The UK also practises the use of the same macro, however, the UK CRS Settlement Campaign SOP does not specify the use of this macro when the customer enquires about their account balance after having accepted the settlement offer and paid the agreed amount.
 

 Separately, GCMT noted 5 accounts selected for the UK CRS Settlement Campaign where the Collections agent noted the account as “Paid in full”, however the customer has not paid or settled the account.
RCA Description: Process and People
Issue Rating Justification:
The Issue relates to an internal control or process that is associated with a moderate risk activity that has a materially negative impact on the product (includes instances in which controls or other safeguards are noted to be absent);
 

 Non-compliance with laws/regulations, supervisory guidance, or Block policy that if not corrected could subject the organization to regulatory criticism or have an entity level impact;
Issue Action Plans:
US Action 1 (1065443)
 US Action 2 (1065447)
 UK Action 1 (1065451)
 UK Action 2 (1065454)
 UK Action 3 (1065457)

Issue Title: The practices for Insolvency (UK), C&D (North America) and Deceased Accounts (Global) can be uplifted to align with SOP
Issue Description:
GCMT tested compliance with standard operating procedures (SOP) for Insolvency (UK), Cease and Desist (US), Letter of Authority (ANZ), Deceased Accounts (Global) procedures and identified a general effectiveness rate of below 60% with the exception of the Deceased Accounts process which was 77% effective.
 

 Insolvency (UK) 
 The UK Insolvency standard operating procedures consists of bankruptcy, sequestration, debt agreement scheme, trust deed, individual voluntary arrangement and debt relief orders.
 

 Collections agent effectiveness for processing Insolvency in accordance with the SOP was 25% as 30 of 40 samples tested identified agents did not complete one or more steps within the SOP when responding or actioning the customer/authorised 3rd party’s request. 
 

 The areas for uplift consisted of:
 In 14 of 40 samples, the agent did not send “Collections:: Insolvency Customer Account Closed” macro to notify the customer that they will no longer be able to access their Clearpay account and be permitted to use Clearpay’s services when customer’s insolvency documents have been received. The instances were related to the following insolvency samples:
 -2 Bankruptcy samples
 -4 Debt Agreement Scheme samples
 -2 Trust Deed samples
 -5 Individual Voluntary Arrangement (IVA) samples
 -1 Debt Relief Order sample
 

 Quality Assurance on Collections Processes: GCMT tested 5 UK Debt Agreement Proposal and Bankruptcy graded tickets and re-performed grading against the requirements of the SOP to determine if the grading outcome (by exception not percentage) was aligned. We noted 1 of 5 samples where the grader did not identify when the agent did not send the “Collections:: Insolvency Customer Account Closed” macro when the insolvency documents were received. 
 -3 of 10 IVA samples (from 40 insolvency samples) where the agent did not update the customer account on Admin Portal as per the SOP:
  > In 1 instance, the agent did not update the customer’s account to be put on Hardship upon receiving notification of the IVA approval, which led the account to be selected for the CRS settlement campaign and collections activity resumed when an IVA was in place. 
  > For 2 instances, the agent did not cancel Hardship on the customer account and did not amend the Exclusion Date on Admin Portal to enable the account to be outsourced to a DCA following the termination of the IVA. Additionally, the “Collections:: Insolvency Cancelled – Customer” macro was not sent to the customer to advise that the IVA was terminated.
 -1 of 40 samples where the agent did not amend the Exclusion Date on Admin Portal to ensure the account was excluded from being outsourced to the DCA when a bankruptcy document was provided.
 

 For the below exceptions, customers have been advised correctly, however some account actioning has not been completed; 
 - 4 of 40 samples where the Collections or Line 0 agent did not use the appropriate macro to advise the customer about the Breathing Space Hold on the account upon insolvency notification. 
  > In 3 instances, “Payment Arrangement:: Breathing Space (UK)” macro is used instead of the “UK Nominated Third Party - Breathing Space Applied” macro as required by the SOP. 2 out of 3 instances were sent by a frontline customer service agent. 
  > In 1 instance, the team member did not use any macros but correctly advised the customer of the 30 days breathing space to allow bankruptcy documents to be sent over to Clearpay.
 -16 out of 40 samples where the Admin portal notes did not contain “#insolvency” or “#Insolvency documentation received” tags when processing a customer’s insolvency. 
 

 Cease and Desist (North America)
 Collections agent effectiveness was 53% for processing Cease and Desist requests received from DCAs in accordance with the SOP as 14 of 30 samples tested identified that the agent did not complete one or more steps within the SOP when responding to and actioning the DCA (customer’s) request. 
 

 The areas for uplift consisted of:
 - 13 of 30 samples where the agent did not send the “Collections:: Cease and Desist Received (North America)” macro to notify the customer that the account has returned to Afterpay after the customer had requested a Cease and Desist from the DCA. 
  > For 7 instances, the customer mentioned that they have legal representation or that they will take legal action on any further communication. Collections confirmed that the macro is not required to be sent to the customer in these instances, however this is not reflected within the SOP. 
  - 6 of 30 samples where the agent did not amend the Exclusion Date on the Admin Portal to ensure the account was excluded from being outsourced to the DCA again.
 - 12 of 30 samples where the account on Admin Portal was not updated with notes on the Cease and Desist Request received from the DCA.
 

 Quality Assurance on Collections Processes: There is opportunity for increased QA on Cease and Desist requests. Out of 537 tickets graded between 1 February 2023 to 31 July 2023, there were only 3 tickets that were related to DCA Cease and Desist. 
 

 Deceased Accounts (Global)
 Global Collections agent effectiveness was 77% for processing in accordance with deceased regional SOPs as 7 of 30 samples tested identified that an agent did not complete an average of one or more steps within the SOP when responding to and actioning customer requests. 
 

 The areas for uplift consisted of:
 - 4 of 30 samples where the “Deceased account: Account Closed” macro was not sent when responding to the third-party to confirm closure of the account and that the debts are not liable by the third-party. 
 Australia:
 -1 of 12 samples where the account was not closed upon receiving NSW Coroners Court Confirmation of Death document. The agent requested a Death Certificate from the third-party, however this was contrary to another sample whereby a coroner's confirmation was satisfactory evidence to close the account. Afterpay does not currently have a list of acceptable death confirmation documents to process the closure of the account.  
 US: 
 - 4 of 12 samples where the customer's account notes on Admin Portal were not updated with the complete information on death certificate information such as the registration number, name, date issued as required by the SOP.
 - 2 of 12 instances where the Manual Write off Register was not updated with the account balance and the customer details.
RCA Description: Process and People
Issue Rating Justification: Non-compliance with laws/regulations, supervisory guidance, or Block policy that if not corrected could subject the organization to regulatory criticism or have an entity level impact;
Issue Action Plans:
Insolvency (UK) (1076013)
 Insolvency (UK) 2 (1076018)
 Cease and Desist (NA) (1076663)
 Deceased Accounts (1076668)

Issue Title: QA over DCA Interactions can be uplifted to align with the Afterpay Global External DCA Grading Guide
Issue Description:
Full Title: Quality Assurance over DCA Interactions can be uplifted to align with the Afterpay Global External DCA Grading Guide and obtain a greater level of assurance that DCAs are delivering in accordance with Afterpay standards.
 

 Finding
 GCMT tested 55 (40 phone and 15 digital) graded DCA interactions and re-performed grading against the requirements of the Afterpay Global External DCA Grading Guide. There was an 85% effectiveness rating as there were 7 calls whereby one requirement of the Afterpay Global External DCA Grading Guide was not identified by the grader in the QA grading scorecard. These exceptions were non-compliant with Afterpay SOPs or impacted customer resolution. All calls were graded 92% or above.
 

 These included:
 2 samples where the DCA agent did not verify the customer at the beginning of the call before disclosing the balance of the customer’s account. 
 1 sample where the DCA agent did not disclose that they were working on behalf of Afterpay. The agent did not disclose that the customer’s balance was related to Afterpay after customer verification. 
 1 sample where the DCA agent did not ask affordability questions to further understand the customer’s financial situation and the ability to hold the payment arrangement when the customer said they were dependent on their pension. 
 1 sample where the DCA agent did not advise the customer that a follow up will be conducted in 3 month’s time as the payment arrangement exceeds 3 months. 
 1 sample where the DCA agent did not raise a dispute liability query to Clearpay for investigation through Zendesk. 
 1 instance where the DCA agent used informal and unprofessional phrases when probing the customer. Additionally, the customer was frustrated when the customer further asked questions about the usage of Afterpay. Whilst the agent’s communication and conduct was noted in the comments, the grader did not mark down the score.
RCA Description: People
Issue Rating Justification: The issue is related to a risk management practice, but overall risk management for the function is adequate
Issue Action Plans:
QA Action 1 (1066117)
 QA Action 2 (1066120)

Issue Title: Lending Operations ECOA Procedures
Issue Description:
Procedure Requirement:
 The Lending Operations ECOA Procedures, Page 5 Decision - Notice for Revised offer states, "Cash App Lending Operations sends written notification to an applicant of a revised offer once it’s been determined that it is unable to offer the initial credit requested. The notice is sent within thirty (30) calendar days from the offer submission date and includes:
  ● Indication that an adverse action is taken on the original offer
  ● The adverse reason
  ● The revised offer."
 

 Testing Results:
 There is no process for customers to apply for credit. Cash Lending provides varying lending limits to qualifying Cash App and/or Borrow customers based in part on their existing accounts’ activity. 
 

 Therefore, there are no circumstances for Cash App Lending to provide revised offers of credit to a customer based on declining their amount requested.
Issue Rating Justification: An Issue that presents a minor level of risk, or represents a limited weakness in Block’s processes or controls. Remedial corrective action is required, resulting in improved processes or controls.
Issue Action Plans: Lending Operations ECOA Procedures (1066133)
Closure Request Justification:
Cash Customer Operations (CCO) updated the Lending Operations ECOA Procedures, Page 5 Decision - Notice for Revised Offers, to reflect that written notifications of revised offers are sent when the “initial credit offer” has changed, rather than when “initial credit requested” have changed. The modification accurately describes the process since Cash Borrow customers do not apply for the product but are qualified based in part on account activities.
 

 This management action plan is complete.

Issue Title: Complaints Management SOP & Runbook require updates [Square Canada]
Issue Description:
Requirements: The ‘Code Complaint Workflow’ sections – within both the 1. US/CA Complaints Management SOP and the 2. Complaints Management Runbook - US/CA – require Complaints Management to review the code-related case and triage them to confirm whether they apply to Canada’s Code of Conduct categories and, if deemed not applicable, complaints management must send the ""CA Code of Conduct {N/A}"" macro to the complainant. 
 

 Additionally, the Workflow states that, “A Complaints Manager will add [the] complaint to the tracker” (referring to the ‘CA Code of Conduct Complaints Tracker’) 
 

 Testing Results: In the one reviewed instance of an alleged Code of Conduct complaint, Complaints Management did not adhere to internal processes by:
 1) Not sending the “CA Code of Conduct N/A” macro (GCMT confirmed the macro’s destination no longer works, rendering the contents unreachable); &
 2) Not logging the complaint in the designated “CA Code of Conduct Tracker” contrary to the SOP & Runbook requirement.
 

 Impact: Macros are utilized as a control to operationalize consistent and clear messaging. Such tooling should be readily accessible and available in order to promote timely, consistent, and compliant responses to customers. Additionally, documentation standards for retention and tracking should be adhered to in order to provide supporting evidence of compliance. Given Square’s requirement to report Code complaints to the payment card networks on a semiannual basis, Square’s documentation of Code complaints should support efficient and reliable reporting.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Square Canada, Inc. Code Complaints Program Q4 2023 (1015384)
RCA Description: Human Error and broken hyperlink for Macro
Issue Rating Justification: Internal procedural requirements were not met, while Regulatory requirements were.
Issue Action Plans: Update Complaints Management SOP & Runbook (1067425)

Issue Title: REF 2 - SEL Adverse Media, Sanctions and PEP Screening Q3 2023
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 The Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 (the 'MLRs'), Regulation 40, states the following with respect to record retention:
 (2) The records are—
 (a) a copy of any documents and information obtained by the relevant person to satisfy the customer due diligence requirements in regulations 28, 29 and 33* to 37;
 (3) Subject to paragraph (4), the period is five years beginning on the date on which the relevant person knows, or has reasonable grounds to believe—
 (a) that the transaction is complete, for records relating to an occasional transaction; or
 (b) that the business relationship has come to an end for records relating to—
 (i) any transaction which occurs as part of a business relationship, or
 (ii) customer due diligence measures taken in connection with that relationship.
 

 *33.—(1) A relevant person must apply enhanced customer due diligence measures and enhanced ongoing monitoring, in addition to the customer due diligence measures required under regulation 28 and, if applicable, regulation 29, to manage and mitigate the risks arising—
 (a)in any case identified as one where there is a high risk of money laundering or terrorist financing—
 (i) by the relevant person under regulation 18(1), or
 (ii) in information made available to the relevant person under regulations 17(9) and 47;
 (b) in any business relationship or transaction with a person established in a high-risk third country;
 (d) if a relevant person has determined that a customer or potential customer is a PEP, or a family member or known close associate of a PEP (in accordance with regulation 35);
 (g) in any other case which by its nature can present a higher risk of money laundering or terrorist financing.
 

 Testing Results:
 ➤ Cash UK
 GCMT reviewed a sample of 213 Cash UK accounts closed between June and December 2018 and noted that no screening records were available prior to the closure of 84% (179 out of 213) of the accounts. The Compliance Audit Engineering function informed GCMT that they do not know why certain screening records are missing in the database as their function got the ownership of Sanctions screening in late 2022. Anything prior to that, they cannot help fetch the information from the databases so they may not have enough context if certain records are missing. 
 

 ➤ Square UK
 GCMT reviewed a sample of 116 Square UK accounts closed in Q2 & Q3 2018 and Compliance Audit Engineering could not confirm the type of screenings performed for these accounts as this information is only available from December 2019 onwards.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: SEL Adverse Media, Sanctions and PEP Screening Q3 2023 (1015380)
Issue Rating Justification: The issue cannot be corrected in the ordinary course of business. The finding may expose Square to heightened scrutiny and criticism.
Issue Action Plans: Record Retention (1067435)
Closure Request Justification:
Completed and verified. Backup docs saved below:

Issue Title: REF 4 - SEL Transactional Testing - Merchants not screened in accordance with requirements [Square - SEL]
Issue Description:
➤ Square Trusted Identity:
 Requirement:
 The Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG) Guidance, Part 3, 4: Compliance with the UK financial sanctions regime, Screening of customers and transactions, Timing of screening
 4.80 All customers should be screened during the establishment of a business relationship or as soon as possible after the business relationship has commenced. Firms should be aware of the risks associated with screening customers after a business relationship has been established and/or services have been provided i.e., that they may transact with a sanctioned party in breach of sanctions prohibitions. Firms must be aware of the absolute restrictions embedded in the financial sanctions regime. Where there is any delay in screening, firms face a risk of breaching the legislation.
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT noted that a total of 3 Square UK merchants onboarded between July 1, 2022 and June 30, 2023 were only screened after their first payment. Foundational Compliance Engineering stated that 'for merchants who are eventually screened, but not at onboarding, this is a known issue scheduled to fix by sometime in Q1 2024.' 
 The list of 3 Square UK merchants is available here:
 https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1EF7sRJADMvUEKLVzD6HlAY3CkfSt6zDfXK5lTgW56L0/edit#gid=519853891
 

 ➤ Square Trusted Identity & Square Compliance Operations:
 Requirement:
 The Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG) Guidance, Part 3, 4: Compliance with the UK financial sanctions regime, Screening of customers and transactions, Timing of screening
 4.80 All customers should be screened during the establishment of a business relationship or as soon as possible after the business relationship has commenced. Firms should be aware of the risks associated with screening customers after a business relationship has been established and/or services have been provided i.e., that they may transact with a sanctioned party in breach of sanctions prohibitions. Firms must be aware of the absolute restrictions embedded in the financial sanctions regime. Where there is any delay in screening, firms face a risk of breaching the legislation.
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT identified a total of 279 Square UK merchants oboarded between July 1, 2022 and June 30, 2023, not screened at the onboarding. The list of 279 Square UK merchants is available here:
 https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1EF7sRJADMvUEKLVzD6HlAY3CkfSt6zDfXK5lTgW56L0/edit#gid=1275210848
 Square Trusted Identity stated that when the Compliance Operations team uses the 'activate' action in the system, it manually activates the seller and bypasses all other requirements our automated systems expect. Those requirements are not marked as met in the square systems, which means that there are downstream side effects - like screening not being conducted. 
 When the Compliance Operations team uses the 'approve' action in the system, it manually verifies the legal entity of the seller. If that was the only activation requirement outstanding, then the seller will be activated too. If they still needed to link their bank account, they will still need to do this. By manually verifying the legal entity with this action, it ensures that other downstream systems behave in the expected way - case in point, screening will occur.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: SEL Adverse Media, Sanctions and PEP Screening Q3 2023 (1015380)
Issue Rating Justification: The issue cannot be corrected in the ordinary course of business. The finding may expose Square to heightened scrutiny and criticism.
Issue Action Plans:
Square customer onboarding screening (1068095)
 1. Compliance Ops UK SOP update (1068098)
 2. Compliance Operations review of missing merchant screenings (1068100)

Issue Title: SFS TPRM Ongoing Monitoring not performed at required frequency

 Based on testing performed, ongoing monitoring was not performed quarterly in accordance with procedure for 5 of 5 Tier 1 Vendors. The control was not performed during 2Q 2023, and 3Q 2023. 
 

 Failure to perform ongoing monitoring could result in non-compliance with regulatory guidance.
RCA Description: The root cause stems from a failure to adhere to policy or procedure.
Issue Rating Justification: If the risk is not mitigated, the issue could result in non-compliance with regulatory guidance. Risk is heightened by rating and criticality of vendors (Tier 1).
