Issue Title: Updates needed to SFS Third Party Risk Management Policy and Program
Issue Description:
Per Block's Global Compliance Policy on Policies, a compliance policy establishes requirements to comply with applicable laws, regulations and rules. Compliance documents are expected to be accurate, up-to-date, and operationalized. 
 

 The SFS Third Party Risk Management Policy and Program (MCRC Approved 9-19-23) is not consistent with regulatory guidance (FIL-29-2023). The following areas of guidance is not addressed in the policy:
 - Due Diligence considerations;
 - Ongoing Monitoring considerations;
 - Independent Reviews; and 
 - Documentation Retention
 

 Outdated or inaccurate policies could lead to regulatory criticism.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: The root cause of this issue stems from deficiencies in the policy.
Issue Rating Justification: This issue stems from deficiencies in the policy which could impact how processes are executed. TPRM (formerly Vendor Management) has Moderate risk impact to SFS (Per the latest SFS Monitoring Risk Rating) therefore this issue is rated Medium.
Issue Action Plans: SFS Third Party Risk Management Policy and Program (1068116)
Closure Request Justification:
GCMT obtained and reviewed a copy of the updated SFS Third Party Risk Management Policy and Program for required updates. GCMT observed the following:
 1. Due diligence considerations were added on page 7.
 2. Ongoing monitoring considerations were added on page 10. 
 3. Independent Reviews were documented on page 12. 
 4. Documentation Retention was documented on page 12. 
 

 GCMT concludes updates sufficiently aligns with TPRM guidance.

Issue Title: SFS Good bank ATO notifications not performed
Issue Description:
Square Loan ATO SOP, page 11 states:
 Depending on whether the plan is tagged as the “ATO” or “Good bank
 ATO” plan tag, the specific plan tag will automatically trigger an email to
 be sent to the seller, confirming that the loan was canceled. See Auto
 Loan Cancellation doc.
 1. If the loan is tagged as “ATO”, the seller will be notified the loan is
 canceled and that they will not be liable for the loan.
 2. If the loan is tagged as “Good bank ATO” and the funds were sent
 to the seller’s Good Bank, the seller will be notified that SFS will
 debit the seller’s linked bank account for recovery of funds in order
 to cancel the loan.

Issue Title: The Lending EFTA Procedures and the practices for resolving errors can be uplifted to align with the § 1005.11 (c)(1)
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement
 § 1005.11 Procedures for resolving errors (c) Time limits and extent of investigation 
 (1) Ten-day period. A financial institution shall investigate promptly and, except as otherwise provided in this paragraph (c), 
 Shall determine whether an error occurred within 10 business days of receiving a notice of error. 
 The institution shall report the results to the consumer within three business days after completing its investigation.
 The institution shall correct the error within one business day after determining that an error occurred.
 

 Finding Description
 The Lending Operations Electronic Funds Transfer Act (EFTA) and Payments Procedures does not contain the requirements on time limits of three business days to respond to customers, and one business day to correct the error. 
 

 GCMT identified that 4 of 30 Reg E dispute cases reviewed did not follow the time limits for resolving errors required under § 1005.11 (c)(1). 
 Specifically;
 three cases with errors were not corrected within one business day after determining that an error occurred. 
 one case whereby an error was not corrected within one business day after determining that an error occurred and the result of the error was not reported to the consumer within three business days after completing an investigation.
RCA Description: Control Does Not Exist
Issue Rating Justification:
- Non-compliance with laws/regulations, supervisory guidance, or Block policy that if not corrected could subject the organization to regulatory criticism or have an entity level impact; and
 - The Issue relates to an internal control or process that is associated with a moderate risk activity that has a materially negative impact on the product (includes instances in which controls or other safeguards are noted to be absent).
 A moderate yet undesirable level of risk to Block that requires management attention to mitigate the risk adequately. Remedial corrective actions must be addressed in a reasonable timeframe.
Issue Action Plans: Uplift Lending Operation's process and procedures to align with § 1005.11 Procedures for resolving errors (1068749)
Closure Request Justification:
#104914 - Time limits and extent of investigation
 Lending operations updated the worksheet with ATO/Risk Ops to include "Added Date" column to identify ATO delivery and completed by Lending Operations to signify time limits per team, as evidence in the updated tracker - Self Reported ATO with Loans
 

 

 As of 2/26/23, the available testing samples are not sufficient to validate the transactional effectiveness of the updated control. As a result, the effectiveness review will be tested in the next scheduled Lending Reg E review. No exceptions noted. 
 

 This management action plan is deemed complete. MX 02-26-24

Issue Title: There is no data field to accurately track, identify and action "Stop Payment" requests from consumers [Cash App Lending - US]
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement
  § 1005.10 Preauthorized transfers (c) Consumer's right to stop payment
 (1) Notice. A consumer may stop payment of a preauthorized electronic fund transfer from the consumer's account by notifying the financial institution orally or in writing at least three business days before the scheduled date of the transfer.
 

 (2) Written confirmation. The financial institution may require the consumer to give written confirmation of a stop-payment order within 14 days of an oral notification. An institution that requires written confirmation shall inform the consumer of the requirement and provide the address where confirmation must be sent when the consumer gives the oral notification. An oral stop-payment order ceases to be binding after 14 days if the consumer fails to provide the required written confirmation.
 

 Finding Description
 There is no data field in place to accurately track "Stop Payment" requests from Lending consumers. Currently, a "stop payment" inquiry does not hold a specific indicator for the Lending Operations to quickly and precisely identify this type of inquiry being submitted. Instead, the Lending Operations team manually assesses and identifies if any inquiries serve as a request from a consumer for us to stop a payment. 
 

 This design and approach limits the Lending Operations' teams’ ability to identify and accurately track Stop Payment requests, which may result in missing/mis-categorizing requests and/or the inability to test a reliable population of stop payment requests.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification:
- Non-compliance with laws/regulations, supervisory guidance, or Block policy that if not corrected could subject the organization to regulatory criticism or have an entity level impact; and
 - The Issue relates to an internal control or process that is associated with a moderate risk activity that has a materially negative impact on the product (includes instances in which controls or other safeguards are noted to be absent).
 A moderate yet undesirable level of risk to Block that requires management attention to mitigate the risk adequately. Remedial corrective actions must be addressed in a reasonable timeframe.
Issue Action Plans: There is no data field to accurately track, identify and action "Stop Payment" requests from consumers (1068753)
Closure Request Justification:
April 29, 2025: Cash Lending customers may request to cancel autopay by contacting Cash App or through a self-service option within the app. Requests from customers are manually tracked and the self-service feature is automatically tracked in the Toolbox system. During Q1 EFTA testing, a sample size of 30 customer requests and 25 self-initiated autopay cancellations were reviewed to assess whether requests and actions were processed accurately. There were no issues identified.
 

 Refer to the Action Plan for supporting documentation.
 

 This management action plan is complete.

Issue Title: The Lending MLA Procedures contain outdated information in relation to how customers receive a MAPR statement
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement
 Mandatory Loan Disclosures (§ 232.6)
 If a creditor extends consumer credit (including any consumer credit originated or extended through the internet) to a covered borrower, the creditor must provide the covered borrower with certain information before or at the time the borrower becomes obligated on the transaction or establishes an account for the consumer credit:
 • A statement of the Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR) applicable to the extension of consumer credit; 
 

 Finding Description
 The Lending Operations MLA Procedures contain outdated information regarding how the customers receive the required MAPR statement. 
 

 The MLA rule requires the creditor to provide the statement of the MAPR to each covered borrower before or at the time the borrower becomes obligated on the transaction. The required statement is currently being disclosed as part of the Borrow Terms that serves as an individual loan contract. 
 

 However, the procedures describe the legacy process of a pop up disclosure that is conditioned to be displayed only when the Department of Defense returns results of covered borrowers (confirmed MLA status).
Issue Rating Justification: An Issue that presents a minor level of risk, or represents a limited weakness in Block’s processes or controls. Remedial corrective action is required, resulting in improved processes or controls.
Issue Action Plans: The Lending MLA Procedures require an update to reflect how MAPR statement is received (1068756)

Issue Title: The below processes and practices can be uplifted in design and their operating effectiveness [Square Loans - AU]
Issue Description:
1 - Financial Hardship - Payment Plan Offerings
 Per Section 10 of the ‘Global Loans Hardship and Forbearance Standard Operating Procedure’, where a
 Merchant informs Square of General Financial Hardship and requests relief for their outstanding loan “as every
 Merchant’s financial circumstances differ, any request for financial hardship relief will be handled on a
 case-by-case basis and escalated to the Banking PMO for review”
 GCMT’s review of the current Standard Operating Procedures (SOP) of payment plan offerings for financial
 hardship and sample based testing of 25 payment plan requests identified the following:
 1. There is no methodology in the current generation of payment plan offers which is resulting in
 inconsistencies between the payment plan offered to different Sellers. I.e. Term of payment plan,
 commencement date and monthly repayment amount.
 For all samples tested, no attempt was made by the loan operations agent or escalating Customer
 Success Advocate to request information to ensure that the inputs entered for the payment plan offer
 will be appropriate for their financial circumstances e.g Repayment amount they can afford to pay on a
 monthly basis.
 2. There is no flexibility or negotiation between Seller’s suffering financial hardship and Loan Operation
 Agents providing the payment plan offer. This is resulting in payment plans on occasion being offered to
 Seller’s which are higher on a monthly basis than their existing 60 day requirement (when converted to
 monthly).
 In one sample tested where a customer questioned the affordability of the monthly payment which was
 higher than their existing obligation, a response was provided that “the minimum payment obligation will
 only pay 50% of their loan’.
 3. There is no distinction between the approach, macro and notation of payment plans which are generated
 proactively by loans for Seller’s who “are unwilling or unable to repay the outstanding balance at the time
 of loan maturity, deactivation or breach”.
 a. Per Sample Testing, the current approach where a payment plan offer macro is sent by loan
 operations proactively, is causing Seller confusion as no additional context is provided prior to
 the payment plan offer or in the current payment plan offer macro which is used interchangeably
 for financial hardship payment plan requests which are Seller driven/requested. I.e. First
 sentence of the macro is “Thank-you for working with us to resolve your outstanding loan
 balance”.
 

 2- Payoff Statements - Seller Requests for Account Documentation
 Square Loans have established template documents for Loan Operation Agents to utilize, where a Seller has
 contacted us to obtain formal documentation for current or historic Square Loans. The generation of these
 statements are manual and reliant on the Loan Operation Agent to accurately populate the inputs following a
 review of Regulator.
 GCMT tested a sample of 25 Seller requests for Payoff Statements and identified the following:
 1. There are inconsistencies in the agent population of loan information when utilizing the same Payoff
 Statement template. The different approaches were noted below:
 Agents include;
 ○ the origination amount (excluding the fixed fee) for "Loan Amount"
 ○ the origination amount and the fixed fee (total amount) for "Loan Amount"
 ○ line in the template to note Loan Fee and to separate the Origination Amount and Loan Fee.
 

 3- Decline in Processing - Manual Application Review (Declined Applications)
 Per the ‘Global Loans Manual Credit Review’ SOP, where a Seller application has been queued for Manual Review
 due to an application blocker relating to Seller Processing Activity, the Loan Operations Agent will request from
 the Seller an explanation for a decline in their sales processing and/or sales processing gap.
 GCMT tested a sample of 25 manually reviewed declined applications. 4 of the 25 declined applications were
 sent a macro requesting an explanation from the seller for a decline in their sales processing. Seller. responses
 appeared reasonable in nature with no material risk concerns e.g a short holiday or conducting renovations.
 However these Sellers were subsequently declined for a loan. The current SOP does not provide guidance or a
 rule for the assessment of the responses provided by the Seller, to ensure that a full and fair assessment is
 being undertaken and to reduce the decline of Seller’s who are within Square’s risk appetite.
 

 4- Cancellation of Square Loans Application - Communication
 There is currently no automatic or manual communication which is sent to a customer following the successful
 cancellation of a loan which is resulting in repeat contacts made to the Square Customer Success Team.
 

 5-Public Record Checks for Loans exceeding $75,000 - Manual Application Review
 Per Section 9.10 of the ‘Global Loans Manual Credit Review’ SOP’, where a loan application exceeds $75,000 an
 individual public record search is required to be conducted to ensure that the individual is not bankrupt, a
 disqualified director or has a current or recent financial claim lodged against them.
 The current SOP does not detail which individual the check should be conducted on for companies, partnerships
 or trusts (i.e. Activation Person, Beneficial Owner or Director). GCMT noted that in practice, the majority of Loan
 Operation Agents are conducting the searches on the Activation Person Only, who in most cases are one of the
 listed Directors but not the Beneficial Owner.
 Testing identified two instances where the Public Record Check was conducted on the Activation Person only,
 who was not a Director or Beneficial Owner of the registered Seller. As the Activation Person is not required to
 be the Beneficial Owner or a Director of the Seller, there is a risk that in some instances, the public record checks
 are being conducted on an individual who is not material to the intended assessment of the risk of future default
 for a Square Loan.
 

 6- Quality Assurance Program - Manual Application Reviews
 The current Quality Assurance Program for Loans is solely focused on Loan Operation Agent’s compliance with
 the requirements for Manual Application Reviews. Grading is conducted on a weekly basis with the random
 selection of 1 manually approved application and 1 manually declined application, per Loan Operation Agent.
 GCMT reviewed the current grading scorecard and performed grade-the-grader testing on a sample of 10 graded
 tickets and noted the following:
 1. Independence of Grading - Grading is currently being conducted by a Loan Operations Agent based in
 Canada. The Analyst is responsible for grading tickets which have been processed by their Team Lead.
 2. The current Grading Scorecard can be improved with the following observations noted:
 a) The current grading scorecard:
 i) Does not include two requirements included within the ‘Global Loans Manual Credit
 Review Standard Operating Procedure’.
 ii) Includes grading criteria which are not included within the ‘Global Loans Manual Credit
 Review Standard Operating Procedure’ as required processing steps.
 iii) For a number of grading questions/criteria, some question wording is asking the Grader to
 confirm certain actions were completed at the time of processing a manual application.
 However, the grader is unable to determine or verify that these are completed after an
 application is processed i.e there are no data points or screen captures to establish this
 for some questions being asked.
 iv) Does not include a grading criteria for where a request for more information is sent to a
 seller, if the response has been reviewed and is satisfactory for loan approvals. The only
 grading criteria is relating to "If the agent did SSP (request information) the Seller, did the
 Seller leave a clear comment on what the agent is SSP'ing the Seller for"
 3. Grade-the Grader testing was performed to assess the accuracy of the scores recorded.
 a) For four out of ten samples graded, the Grader incorrectly selected N/A in the scorecard where
 the answer should have been YES
 b) For two of the samples tested the grader selected YES for the one or more grading questions,
 however per review of regulator, there was no evidence that these were completed to
 substantiate the YES.
 Per conversations with Loan Operations Management, GCMT note that the Quality Assurance Program is
 currently limited to pre-onboarding activities due to resourcing constraints.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: Low rated finding as loans is not regulated in Australia, and the review was undertaken from an operational perspective and internal compliance with SOPS
Issue Action Plans:
New Financial Hardship Program to be created and deployed internationally (excluding US) (1079314)
 Macro Audit for Collections (1079317)
 Payoff Template - Update (1079960)
 Cancellation Emails - Exploration (1079963)
 Credit Review SOP - Update (1079966)
 Quality Assurance - Resource Allocation and new program (1079969)
Closure Request Justification:
All action items have been closed with the exception of action ID 1079963 which has been risk accepted by the business in accordance with the Block issue Management Program. 
 

 Formal risk acceptance sign-off attached as well as the communication email. The attached document provides the business rationale for risk acceptance and current mitigation in its place.

Issue Title: In some cases, systems are not generating the below communications and reports in accordance with policy requirements [Square Loans - AU]
Issue Description:
The establishment and ongoing servicing of Square Loans for multiple processes is in part reliant on system
 based controls and automation. GCMT’s sample based testing identified exceptions which may indicate
 potential defects in the system. The below exceptions have been classified by the key processing activities
 reviewed.
 

 Onboarding Loan Offers = Minimum Processing Activity
 Per the eligibility requirements for the AU Onboarding Loans Flow, a credit decision will be made after 5 days of
 processing on Square, where all other requirements are met. Where the credit decision is satisfactory, the
 system will automatically trigger an offer which will be viewable on the Seller’s Square Loans Dashboard.
 GCMT tested a sample of 6 approved onboarding loan offers, and noted the below:
 ● Two samples were noted where pre-approved onboarding offers were provided before the policy
 requirement ‘ Minimum 5 days of processing activity’ was met.
 ○ One Seller was provided offers on Day 2 of processing
 ○ One Seller was provided offers on Day 4 of processing
 

 Mail Deliveries - Regulator
 The majority of communications post an account change has been automated by Regulator and stored within
 Regulator ‘Mail Deliveries’. As part of testing, where an automated mail delivery is expected, GCMT reviewed
 each sample to determine if the appropriate communication was triggered by the system and the accuracy of
 the pre-populated content. The below processes tested, identified;
 1. Deactivations = Mail Deliveries
 ● For 2 out of 15 samples, the Seller was not sent the auto-generated email notifying them that their
 Square account has been deactivated. For both samples an entry was noted in Admin Actions on the
 same date of deactivation to note 'sent compliance/deactivate_and_close_custom'.
 ● For 1 of the 2 samples, there was no related email on Salesforce. For the other sample there was an
 email which was sent from Capital PPP titled "Message from the Square Loans team" however there was
 no email content when opened.
 2. Application Cancellations = Mail Deliveries
 ● For 9 out of 15 samples tested, where the Seller's loan was canceled in the system, the Seller did not
 receive an automatic email notification (Per Mail Deliveries on Regulator) confirming that their request to
 cancel had been received.
 ● For 6 out of 15 samples, the Seller received the automatic email notification titled
 Loan_Cancellation_Recieved"
 

 3. Auto-Approved Applications: First Payment Report- Misalignment between transactions and amounts
 on payment reporting = Mail Deliveries
 ● For 21 out of 25 samples, the amount quoted in the notification did not match the transactions on
 Regulator for the same time-zones applied in the notification. The first payment reports which are
 provided to Seller's note the total amount of transactions which are processed within the 24 hour period
 with a time-frame starting at 8AM AEST and ending at 8AM AEST the following day. Square Loans
 Management have confirmed that this is a known issue which impacts all markets for Square Loans. Due
 to the significant resourcing cost, Square Loans have a manual process in place to provide accurate
 amounts, should a Seller contact Square about the discrepancies.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: Low rated finding as loans is not regulated in Australia, and the review was undertaken from an operational perspective and internal compliance with SOPS

Pre-Approval Offers - Investigation (1079972)
 Auto-Generated Emails - Investigation (1079975)
 Auto generated notification - Loan Cancellation (1079978)
 First Payment Reports - Investigation (1079981)

Management have formally risk accepted the linked management action plan with a formal risk acceptance sign-off attached as well as the communication email. The attached document provides the business rationale for risk acceptance and current mitigation in its place.

Issue Title: The practices for the handling of the below processes can be improved to align with SOPS and management of SOPS  [Square Loans - AU]
Issue Description:
The practices for the handling and processing of bankruptcy requests, loan deactivations, payment plans and
 manually declined application can be improved to align with Standard Operating Procedures and management
 of Standard Operating Procedures can be improved to ensure ongoing alignment with current and expected
 practices.
 

 GCMT conducted testing across eleven key servicing activities and 226 individual requests/cases to determine
 Loan Operation Agents compliance with the relevant Standard Operating Procedures (SOP) at the time of
 processing.
 

 Updates to Standard Operating Procedures
 GCMT identified the following areas where the Standard Operating Procedure should be updated to ensure
 Confidential
 7
 alignment with current practices and/or future expectations.
 1 - Bankruptcy
 ● Documented write-off process not specific to Australian based procedures.
 ● Add Reference to the shared Insolvency SOP between Credit Risk and Compliance.
 ● Add Reference to detail receipt of notifications and communications methods specific to Loan
 Operations during Bankruptcy Proceedings.
 2- Payment Plan Requests
 ● Removal of requirement in Section 10 - Eligibility, which states all payment plans require Banking PMO
 approval
 3- Hold Rate Relief
 ● Update to include a requirement for agents to enter a note where explicit approval is sought.
 ● Review to be undertaken to ensure consistency in requirements between the ‘Hold Rate Relief’ and
 ‘Hardship SOP’.
 ● Update/removal for the requirement in Section 5 “ in order for a hold rate to be approved/applied, the
 customer must explicitly state that they are having cash flow issues due to the daily hold rate on
 transactions. Where this is not confirmed explicitly and a hold rate is approved this requires additional
 approval from the BPM”. Testing noted this is not being performed in 24% of the sample tested.
 Management has confirmed that Loan Operation Agents have discretion to apply.
 4- Loan Application Cancellations
 ● Review requirement that agents will facilitate the cancellation when contacted by the Seller. Testing
 noted that in practice, Agents are not completing loan cancellations on behalf of the Seller or confirming
 eligibility, when contacted via Salesforce. Agents are instead sending the Seller's instructions (macro) on
 how to cancel the loan themselves through the Dashboard.
 5- Auto Approved Applications
 ● Review of Credit Product terms for daily hold rate commencement (48 hours). Testing noted this is
 occurring after 48 hours following the loan activation date (One day after the listed date in the loan
 contract - possible time-zone root cause)
 

 

 GCMT identified three servicing activities which had a non-compliance rate of 45% and above, where the Loan
 Operation Agents did not process in accordance with all requirements in the SOP. Per conversations with Square
 Loans Management, the root cause of these issues are either due to an outdated SOP’s or agent error.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: Low rated finding as loans is not regulated in Australia, and the review was undertaken from an operational perspective and internal compliance with SOPS

Bankruptcy SOP to be reviewed and revised in addition to wider goal to align processes globally (1079984)
 Hold Rate Macro - Review (1079987)

Issue Title: The practices for the handling of Square Loan enquiries by Customer Success Advocates can be improved to align with SOPS [Square Loans - AU]
Issue Description:
GCMT conducted testing on a random sample of 100 Square Loan enquiries which were received via Phone and
 Email by Customer Success in August 2023. The Advocates effectiveness rate for Square Loan Handling per the
 Standard Operating Procedures was 72%. The non-compliance rates and key themes have been detailed below:
 

 Phone Calls
 Out of 50 samples tested, 18 samples (36%) were noted where the customer's enquiry/question/request was
 not resolved in full by the Customer Success Advocate. This consisted of;
 ● 6 samples whereby the incorrect information was provided or process was not followed per the call
 scripting and handbook
 ● 5 samples whereby an Incomplete response was provided to the customer's questions
 ● 3 samples whereby a follow up action communicated to the customer was not completed following the
 call
 ● 3 samples whereby escalated tickets and/or notes on Salesforce did not include adequate detail
 ● 1 whereby the incorrect macro used
 Additionally, a common theme noted in testing is that Advocates would place the Seller on lengthy holds during
 the resolution of the Seller's enquiry. The purpose of the hold was to either review the Seller's regulator account
 to provide a direct answer to the Seller's enquiry or to attempt to escalate a request or query to the Loan
 Operations team. Hold times ranged up to 10 minutes + in total over the call, and in all cases tested for
 attempted escalation, the Advocate did not return with a response and instead advised the Seller that they will
 be contacted via email.
 This may be resulting in the overall average case handling times for loan phone enquiries appearing higher than
 required and not reflective of the actual time an Advocate is speaking with a Seller to resolve their query.
 

 Emails
 Out of 50 samples tested, 10 samples (20%) were noted where the customer's enquiry/question/request was
 not resolved in full by the Customer Success Advocate. This consisted of;
 ● 2 samples whereby the incorrect information was provided and/or process not followed per SOPs and
 handbook.
 ● 4 samples whereby an incomplete response was provided to the customer's questions.
 ● 2 samples whereby a follow up action communicated to the customer was not completed following the
 initial customer communication.
 ● 2 samples whereby a complaint was not recorded.
 

 Quality Assurance
 There is currently no targeted quality assurance program for Square Loan Enquiries. The current Quality
 Assurance Program for Customer Success Advocates is conducted across all Square Products, with the
 randomized selection of two Customer Contacts, per Advocate on a weekly basis. As a result, the chance of
 selecting a loan enquiry for grading is minimal.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: Low rated finding as loans is not regulated in Australia, and the review was undertaken from an operational perspective and internal compliance with SOPS
Issue Action Plans:
AU Direct to work with Loans Enablement DRI, Loans Ops, CSCO and CS Talent Enablement to implement improved knowledge management related to Square Loans content and Enablement Programs (1079996)
 AU Direct to work with Loans Ops to review the escalation path and workflow for Loans related escalations and actions (1079999)
 Review of Macro Handbook and Guides (1080002)
Closure Request Justification: Issue can be closed as at 6/11 all action plans have been completed successfully by action plan owners

Issue Title: Processes for escalation of complaints and the intake from the website complaints email address can be uplifted [Square Australia]
Issue Description:
The Square Complaints Management processes for escalation of first point resolution complaints and the intake flow from the website complaints email address can be uplifted to improve the customer journey, recording and responding to complaints and time to resolution reporting.
 

 Escalation from first point resolution
 Per the AU Complaints Process Salesforce Knowledge Article (‘SFK’), where a Customer Service Agent (‘CSA’) has not been able to resolve a complaint to the customer’s satisfaction, the Seller is referred to the ‘Complaint Process Support Article’ and provided with a ‘Complaints (Pre-Complaint Information) Macro’ to file a formal complaint with Square. This escalation can be completed by the customer directly sending their complaints to the complaints-au@squareup.com or the customer has the option for the agent to escalate to the AU inbox on the customers behalf. 
 GCMT’s review of the AU Complaints Process SFK and testing of 30 complaints received through Complaints AU inbox identified the following:
 - CSAs are not escalating customers’ complaints to the AU inbox per the process notified to the customer in the Pre-Complaint Information Macro.
 For 2 out of 30 cases, the complainant replied to the CSA and CC’d the Complaints-AU inbox however the CSA did not escalate the complaint nor did the Complaints Management team respond to the customer.
  > In 1 instance, the CSA mentioned that they have escalated to the relevant teams but did not escalate the complaint to Complaints Management.  
  > In 1 instance, the CSA amended the macro to advise the complainant to write to the Complaints AU inbox on a separate thread, after the complainant had CC’d the Complaints AU inbox on the email thread.  
 - In cases where a customer remains dissatisfied with the resolution by a CSA, they are sent the Pre-Complaint Information Macro containing a link to the Complaint Process Support Article. This directs customers to send their escalated complaint in writing to the Complaints-AU email. Despite the complainant previously providing their complaint to the CSA for first-attempt resolution via phone or email, the current process requires the complainant to repeat the complaint details, which can lead to increased complainant frustration and delays. 
 In terms of customer accessibility, an escalation of the original complaint seems more customer centric and less of a barrier to have their complaint resolved.
 

 Intake flow and manual processes - impacts on issue volume reporting 
 The website complaint email address directs customers to send their complaint to the Complaints Management team. The website intake flow could be uplifted to improve the customer journey and our internal manual processes by replacing the Square website complaints email address with the Customer Success intake form. 
 The AU Complaints email address is not configured to upload complaint cases into Salesforce. This creates manual processes for the Complaints Management team to record the complaint and/or search for existing complaints before manually entering and allocating these within Salesforce.
 Where the complaint is noted as a ‘new complaint’, Complaints Management will input and flag the complaint as a Salesforce case, and then refer the complaint to Customer Success to attempt first-point resolution. 
 - GCMT identified 7 instances where the Complaints Manager did not flag the complaint on Salesforce. There is currently no requirement within the Complaints Management AU Runbook to perform this, but it is currently a practice. This will result in underreporting complaints volumes.
 - GCMT identified 4 instances where the Complaints Manager duplicated a Complaint Flag for an existing complaint. By entering an additional complaint flag this will lead to over reporting of complaints volumes. 
 

 If it is an existing complaint, Complaints Management will complete a search to identify that complaint. These practices are not currently documented within the AU IDR Frontline Complaints Handling Process deck or AU Complaints Management Runbook. 
 - The current Complaints AU inbox, including the facilitation of automated complaint acknowledgement emails is managed via Google Groups. Management have confirmed known limitations including a lack of visibility or retention of automated emails and responses (occasionally) which have been sent directly to complainants via the complaint inbox. As a workaround, Management introduced tagging mechanisms in October 2023 to enhance visibility and effective response tracking. Additionally, Complaint Managers are required to duplicate the original complaint and their corresponding responses onto a new and duplicated Salesforce case. 
 In effect, we are creating more work for the teams and there is a risk that Square are losing pertinent complaint documentation and responses. 
 

 Complaint Reporting - Recording of Complaint Information leading to inaccurate Reporting
 Per the ‘AU Complaints Process’ SFK, where an expression of dissatisfaction, Complaint Taxonomy, or request to file a formal complaint has been received, the CSA must Flag the Salesforce case as a complaint with the input of all relevant details including the Initial Date of Complaint.
 The current fields available on Salesforce are not designed to allow the required recording of separate dates for the handling of a complaint by CSA and the escalation to Complaints Management. This may be resulting in inaccuracies in the recording of key complaint dates used for reporting and measurement of individual complaint SLAs at each Tier/Group and may distort the actual timeframe for resolution across the end-to-end complaint lifecycle.
 Sample testing noted the below process themes:
 - If the complaint has not been flagged previously by the CSA, the Complaints Management team flags the complaint on Salesforce, using the email received date now as the initial complaint date. This does not reflect the actual complaint date received during the initial CSA interaction. This approach will create inaccuracies in the time to resolution reporting.
 - Occasionally, the Complaints Manager logs the initial complaint date and resolution date as the same day when the initial complaint was received on a prior date. Complaint Managers were logging cases in Salesforce post-investigation and upon closure. 
 - If a complaint has been flagged by a CSA, a Complaints Manager may enter a first point resolution date. On occasion the complaint may be escalated again. When the Complaints Manager receives and resolves the complaint, they do not update the new final resolution date on Salesforce. 
 - GCMT identified four instances where the Flag-a-Complaint was duplicated, this resulted in different dates being logged between the first case and the second.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Q4 2023 Square AU Complaint Management Program Review (1016707)
RCA Description: Control Design Issue
Issue Rating Justification: The design of the control has numerous impact on the customer journey, recording and responding to complaints and time to resolution reporting.
Issue Progress Updates:
[15/02/2024] Square Complaints Management Lead has stated that "We will not be changing the AU inbox at this time. Complaints are directed there, and without a more substantial investment in tooling, no alternatives exist. The intake form on Squareup.com requires sign in to a Square account. This would restrict non-sellers from filing complaints, which is not something we can consider. " 
 GCMT notes that the Square will be making substantial changes to the Complaint Program in April 2024. GCMT will review the results of this issue raised when the new program and processes is in place.
Closure Request Justification: Closure completed - See all action plans for details.

Issue Title: The practices for the handling and processing of complaints can be improved to align with SOPs and RG271 [Square AU]
Issue Description:
Customer Service Agent (CSA) – First Point Resolution
 Complaint Identification and Flagging
 GCMT conducted testing on a random sample of 40 customer enquiries, to determine CSA compliance for identifying complaints in accordance with the AU Complaints Process Salesforce Knowledge Article (SFK)..
 We identified 4 out of 40 customer enquiries where expression of dissatisfaction was noted which could be interpreted as a complaint taxonomy. For 3 of 4 cases, the CSA acknowledged the customer's frustration, however, did not flag the case as a complaint on Salesforce.
 

 Escalation by CSAs for unresolved complaints or where a formal complaint has been requested by the Complainant. 
 GCMT conducted testing on a random sample of 30 flagged complaints (22 calls, 4 emails and 4 messaging) to determine if the CSA has resolved the complaint, otherwise was the complaint escalated for a formal response from the Complaints Management team in accordance with the AU Complaints Process SFK.
 Sample testing noted the below exceptions/themes:
 - In 6 out of 30 samples (5 phone, 1 messaging), the CSA missed opportunities to refer the customer to the Complaints Process Support Centre Article and/or send an email using the Complaints {Pre-complaint Information} macro where the customer explicitly said they would like to make a complaint after first point resolution was provided by the CSA:
  > In 1 phone case, the CSA did not refer the customer to the Complaints Process Support Centre Article during the call when the customer mentioned that they would like to make a complaint, but had provided the pre-complaint information in an email after the call.
  > In 1 phone case, the caller was not an authorised representative or account holder, hence the CSA was unable to provide any information around the account. However, the CSA could have referred them to the Complaints Process Support Centre Article.
 - GCMT identified 12 cases where customers did not explicitly state that they would like to make a complaint. For 3 instances, the CSA flagged a complaint correctly and marked it as resolved. However the customer's concern was not resolved at first resolution as the CSA agent has to send the query to another department for decision. There is no requirement within the AU Complaints Process SFK for the CSA agent to send the Complaints {Pre-complaint Information} macro to the customer. Consideration could be given to making this a process for the CSA agent to complete and/or leave the complaint as unresolved and escalate to the Complaints Management team to monitor it to closure.
 

 Complaints Management – Escalated/Formal Complaint 
 Complaint Processing and Handling of escalated/formal complaints via Complaints-AU Inbox
 GCMT conducted testing on a random sample of 30 escalated formal complaints and identified 12 instances where the complaint was not processed in line with the AU IDR Frontline Complaints Handling Process deck and RG 271.66, .71 & .75. This consisted of;
 - 9 instances were noted where a formal Final Response was not provided to the complainant. This consisted of; 
  > 4 cases where the complainant explicitly requested a response from Complaints Management in accordance with RG 271.75.
  > 5 cases where resolution was not provided to the complainant in 5 business days which triggers the requirement to provide a formal Final Response under RG 271.71.
 - 3 instances were noted where a Delay Notification was not provided to the customer prior to the expiry of 30 days time frame whereby the formal Final Response response was subsequently provided after 30 days in accordance with RG 271.66. 
 There exceptions occurred during a period where there was no oversight or quality assurance activity performed by Square Complaints Management on the AU Complaints Manager processes. Complaints Management team has recently commenced a quality assurance control in Australia in December 2023.
 

 EDR/AFCA Complaints Processing and Handling
 GCMT conducted testing on a random sample of 15 Australian Financial Complaints Authority (‘AFCA’) complaints to determine if the complaint was processed in accordance with the AU Complaints Management Runbook. 
 Of the 15 complaints, GCMT identified 3 instances where GCMT was unable to sight responses provided to AFCA and evidence was not provided by Complaints Management. The responses were not saved within Complaints Management AFCA folders and due to the limitation of Google Groups, we were unable to sight the responses sent. However, we noted that AFCA has provided acknowledgement of the receipt of responses. 
 As part of the review, GCMT also reviewed Complaint Management alignment and consistency between complaint recording on GRIT board and Salesforce. Of the 15 AFCA complaints tested, GCMT concluded that dates recorded for complaints received and resolved were accurate on GRIT board. However, discrepancies were noted in the creation and recording of the AFCA complaint in Salesforce. 
 Of the 15 complaints, GCMT identified 6 instances where a Salesforce case was not created by Complaint Management for the AFCA complaint and 8 instances where the complaint dates recorded on Salesforce did not align to the dates recorded on GRIT board. 
 GCMT noted in practice that Complaint Managers were logging cases in Salesforce post-investigation and upon closure, resulting in the same date being recorded for both the initial complaint and resolution date.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Q4 2023 Square AU Complaint Management Program Review (1016707)
Issue Rating Justification: Non-compliance with laws/regulations, supervisory guidance, or Block policy that if not corrected could subject the organization to regulatory criticism or have an entity level impact
Closure Request Justification:
Closure completed - See all action plans for details. 
 

 The new complaints program commencing in April 2024 have altered the complaints handling processes. As a result, the some of the exceptions previously raised are no longer relevant. 
 Training has been provided to the advocates on the updated complaint program. Complaint Managers have received guidance on the requirements for storing complaint documentation and ensuring that complaint information such as dates are accurate across systems (such as GRIT and Salesforce)

Issue Title: Improvements needed to Cash Lending complaint handling
Issue Description:
The Lending Operations Complaint Program (Page 4) defines a complaint as “an expression of dissatisfaction by an individual, business entity, decision, or interaction in which a response or resolution is explicitly or implicitly requested. Inquiries are defined as “a communication that includes general questions, comments, or opinions about a Cash App product, process, service, decision, or interaction. Inquiries do not include disputes. 
 

 Members of Cash App Lending Operations must understand the difference between these two forms of feedback to know when action is needed. If the customer is expressing dissatisfaction that would fall under the taxonomies utilized for complaints, it should be recorded as a Complaint."
 

 GCMT tested a random sample of 79 customer inquiries and identified 10 inquiries that met the definition of a complaint. There was a 70% effectiveness rate at identifying complaints within customer inquiries as 3 of 10 (30%) customer inquiries were not identified and logged as a complaint.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: The root cause of this issue stems from human error, and failure to follow procedure requirements.
Issue Rating Justification: The issue presents minor risk as the control effectiveness rate is 70%. The issue stems from human error and is not indicative of a systemic problem.
Issue Action Plans: Improvements needed to Cash Lending complaint handling (1080019)
Closure Request Justification:
GCMT received Complaints training log (with rates of completion of assigned complaints training courses) on 3.18.24 from Britni Diaz, Lending Operations Lead for Cash App. GCMT noted that all applicable employees completed the four courses in “Cash Complaints Management Program- Foundational Training.” The four courses are: complaints management initiative, complaints identification & categorization, complaints process 3a, and complaints process 3B. GCMT noted that there were three (3) employees that did not complete the complaints training due to the fact they were on leave. However, GCMT noted that these three individuals are enrolled in the applicable complaint training courses and therefore, will be complemented upon their return to work. See attached Google sheet “Final complaints training log” for evidence of completion of complaints training for all applicable employees (attached in the hyperlink section in the Status Updates tab)
 

 GCMT concludes the issue and action plan have been remediated.

Issue Title: REF #1 SIL Enhanced Due Diligence Q4 2023
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 Sections 37 to 39 of the CJA 2010 prescribes a number of circumstances in which Firms are required to apply EDD measures, including the following:
 • Where the customer, or the customer’s beneficial owner, is a politically exposed person (PEP);
 • Where a Firm enters into a correspondent relationship with a respondent institution from a non-EEA state;
 • Where a Firm deals with a customer (whether a natural person or legal entity) established in high-risk third countries; and
 • To a business relationship or transaction that they have identified as presenting a higher degree of risk.
 Firms should also apply risk proportionate levels of EDD measures in those situations where it is commensurate to the ML/TF risk they have identified. In circumstances in which a firm has determined that customers or business scenarios present a higher ML/TF risk, EDD measures should be applied.
 

 Testing Results:
 The Service Level Agreements (SLAs) for the approved PEP Enhanced Monitoring are not documented in the SIL Square EEA Screening Procedure, and the SLAs for the Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) queues (i.e., CRR Initial, CRR Intrinsic, CRR Ongoing, CRR SSP) are not documented in the SIL EEA Seller Know Your Customer Procedures.
 Additionally, GCMT reviewed a sample of 89 EDD alerts (i.e., IE-29, ES-30, FR-30) and noted that the IE EDD alerts were reviewed between 0 to 33 days after the alert was generated, however the timelines for the ES & FR EDD review of alerts were considerably longer. The ES EDD alerts were reviewed between 45 to 185 days after the alert alert was generated and the FR EDD alerts were reviewed between 83 and 280 days after the alert was generated.
 The transactional data testing extract for EDD is available here, and the transactional data testing extract for approved PEPs is available here.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: The finding may expose Square to heightened scrutiny and criticism.

 4.68 Firms should keep a written record of their screening policy and be able to justify the timescales and frequency of screening, resolution of screening matches and regulatory reporting if required.

Issue Title: REF #6 - SEL Transaction Monitoring, Enhanced Due Diligence, Suspicious Activity Reporting Q4 2023
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 The Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG) Guidance (2020 Revised Version), Part 1, Chapter 5 Customer Due Diligence, 5.5 Enhanced Due Diligence.
 5.5.13 Individuals who have, or have had, a high political profile, or hold, or have held, public office, can pose a higher money laundering risk to firms as their position may make them vulnerable to corruption. This risk also extends to members of their immediate families and to known close associates. PEP status itself does not, of course, incriminate individuals or entities. It does, however, put the customer, or the beneficial owner, into a higher risk category. The level of risk associated with any PEP, family member or close associate (and the extent of EDD measures to be applied) must be considered on a case-by-case basis. 
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT noted that enhanced monitoring has not been performed on 11% (3 out of 28) of the UK approved PEPs. It was also noted that the PEP classes (i.e., low, medium and high risk PEPs), which are noted in the EU/UK Compliance Tracker (column M), are not documented in the Square [UK] Watchlist Process v9 or the UK Seller KYC Standard Operating Procedure (Master).
 

 GCMT reviewed the UK Approved PEPs log available in the EU/UK Compliance Tracker and identified the following discrepancies:
 • 7% (2 out of 28): accounts deactivated in Regulator, however this information is not reflecting the UK PEPs log.
 • 43% (12 out of 28): accounts contained inaccurate/blank cells in the UK PEPs log.
 • 18% (5 out of 28): accounts contained the #PEP:FP hashtag in Regulator, however this information is not reflecting the UK PEPs log. Additionally, it was noted that enhanced monitoring alerts were generated for 11% (3 out of 28) after the #PEP:FP hashtag was added to the accounts.
 • 7% (2 out of 28): accounts were PEP reversed in the system, however this information is not reflecting the UK PEPs log and enhanced monitoring is not required.

Issue Title: Improper removal of CBD seller account restrictions
Issue Description:
Requirement: 
 SFS Cannabinoid (CBD) Business Controls SOP (Page 3), states when a customer is first identified as selling CBD, an SSP is automatically sent requesting COA documents. The customer’s POS account is automatically frozen (unit-level) while the SSP is outstanding. This freeze extends to the customer’s SFS savings account (if applicable) in the form of a full restriction, preventing all movement of funds into and out of savings.
 Additionally, CBD sellers being deactivated by Square are subject to Square’s delayed deactivation process, which allows accounts being deactivated for certain terms of service or similar violations to be unfrozen for 30 days prior to the deactivation. The savings account should remain frozen through deactivation.
 

 Testing Result:
 The control implemented to remediate SFS Issue 129 (Past-due CBD cases) is not operating effectively. When POS accounts are unfrozen and scheduled for delayed deactivation, the savings restriction is erroneously lifted, allowing funds to enter/exit the account. In addition, account restrictions were placed between 7-8 calendar days instead of automatically when the SSP is sent as stated in procedures.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: The root cause of this stems from a data error.
Issue Rating Justification: Deficiencies identified have BSA/AML risk impact and could lead to non-compliance with Money Laundering regulations.
Issue Action Plans: CBD seller account restrictions (1084593)
Closure Request Justification: 4/3/24: GCMT performed validating testing to confirm the issue has been remediated. A sample of CBD seller accounts was tested to confirm the restriction/freeze to the savings account did not lift when the seller POS account was scheduled for deactivation. In addition, GCMT validated procedures were uplifted to align with the process.

Issue Title: Complaint Handling [Cash App - US]
Issue Description:
Requirement: 
 The Global Complaint Policy, Page 6 - Policy Statement states, "It is Block’s policy to ensure all complaints are identified and resolved in an effective manner. Additionally, Block complies with legal, regulatory, and contractual requirements governing the handling of customer complaints arising from the financial products and services offered across all jurisdictions in which it operates."
 

 Finding Description: 
 GCMT tested a random sample of 180 complaints and 120 customer inquiries/support cases for four products Bitcoin, P2P, Cash Card, and Cash App Taxes.
 

 Complaints:
 GCMT conducted testing on a random sample of 180 complaints across Bitcoin, P2P, Cash Card and Cash App Taxes for the purposes of determining that an appropriate resolution was provided to a customer.
 

 The effectiveness rate for complaint resolution was 83% whereby 30 of 180 customer complaints reviewed did not receive an adequate response to address their complaint.
 

 The 17% ineffective rate consisted of:
 17 of 180 (10%) customer complaints did not receive an adequate response to address their complaint. This consisted of complaints for Bitcoin (8), P2P (7), Cash Card (1), and Cash App Taxes (1). 
 13 of 180 (7%) customer complaints were provided a partial response to address the complaint. This consisted of complaints for Cash Card (7), Bitcoin (5) and P2P (1).  
 

 The key themes noted for exceptions where the complaint was not appropriately or only partially addressed by an agent included: 
  - Providing incorrect, or inaccurate resolution/responses based on the complaint or information provided e.g. The customers requested refunds of their Bitcoin withdrawal “priority” fee that they paid for their transaction to be completed within a 10 minute ‘advertised’ estimated withdrawal speed. The complaint resolution did not provide the customer with a refund due to Cash app advising the customer that ‘we are unable to guarantee specific withdrawal timeframes due to the blockchains variation over time’. 
 

 The resolution provided no notice to the customer of how long the transaction took to be completed. Further, a reconciliation of the customer correspondence to the time stamps of the transaction initiation and completion indicate variances in hours and minutes due to time zones and universal time used in our systems. The root cause at the time for these customer claims were an internal severity incident and blockchain congestion.
 

 If a full reconciliation did not take place to validate the customer's complaint, a determination as to the time it took for the transaction to take place and/or that there was an internal severity issue, the customer should be provided with a refund as we did not meet the advertised time for the transaction to take place. Not doing so in these circumstances could be considered to be unfair and deceptive. 
 

  - Not understanding or noticeably confused about the complaint or information provided, resulting in the customer being asked to repeat themselves multiple times causing increased frustration
  
  - Not escalating to a Team Leader or Management where requested explicitly by the Customer multiple times or the Customer has mentioned a 'trigger' for escalation per the Complaint Handling standard operating procedures 
 

  - Not reviewing prior case notes or relevant documents when attempting to resolve the cases for customers complaining about an ongoing issue or prior interaction.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Cash App Complaints (P2P, Cash Card, Bitcoin, Cash App Taxes) - 2023 GCMT Review (1017366)
Issue Rating Justification: The issue presents a minor level of risk, or represents a limited weakness in Block's processes or controls.
Issue Progress Updates: 04-16-24: Validation for action plan #1085251 completed.
Issue Action Plans:
Complaint Handling (1085251)
 BTC Complaints - Withdrawal Speed (1085255)
Closure Request Justification:
January 22, 2025: Cash Product Bitcoin conducted an analysis of the BTC withdrawal timeframes and determined that >95% of the Priority transactions are completed in about 20 minutes. Therefore, the following updates were made:
 

 ‘Speed Selection’ screen (Image 1 - Speed Selection Screen): 
 The ‘Priority’ option was revised to reflect ‘~20 minutes’ (previously ~10 minutes).
 The network congestion status is shown at the top of the ‘Speed Selection.’ 
 Network Traffic will reflect the options below. (Image 2 - Network Traffic Language)
 Normal: “Demand is normal and transactions should process as expected” or
 Higher than usual: “Demand is higher than usual and may lead to higher fees and possible delays.”
    
 Customers may view the status of pending transactions by selecting the transaction in their Activity screen. The ‘Bitcoin withdrawal’ > Transaction details was also updated to display the Network traffic at the time of withdrawal until the transaction is completed; see Image 3 - Bitcoin Withdrawal Transaction Details. The network traffic status is removed from the screen once the transaction is complete; see Image 4 - Bitcoin Withdrawal Completed.  
 

 Due to >95% reliability for Priority withdrawals, and >99% reliability for Standard withdrawals, there were no changes to Cash Customer Operations (CCO) customer talking points. Customer cases regarding missed SLAs caused by network congestion are handled through customer education. The staff inform customers of bitcoin network traffic. There are no refunds since congestion is based on the bitcoin network, and not business processes. If missed SLAs are due to business activities (such as SEVs), customer cases will be reviewed for escalation and refunds. 
 

 Refer to the Action Plan for supporting documentation. 
 

 This management action plan is complete.

Issue Title: Regulation E Dispute Case Handling [Cash App - US]
Issue Description:
Requirement: 
 Regulation E — Electronic Fund Transfer Act
 12 C.F.R. §1005.11(d) Procedure When an Error Did Not Occur or Did Not Occur as Alleged
 After an institution completes its investigation, if it determines an error did not occur, or one occurred that differs in the manner or amount the consumer alleged, the institution must notify the consumer in writing of the results of its investigation and the consumer’s right to request the documents the institution relied on in making its determination.
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT performed testing of 324 dispute cases from the following workflows to confirm the appropriate handling of Reg E dispute cases for Cash Card and P2P related claims. The testing was performed against the Knowledge Articles of each respective workflow as well as regulatory guidance described under the Official interpretation of 11(c)(4) Investigation.
 1. P2P (Legacy)
 2. P2P (Disputron)
 3. Cash Card (10 days)
 4. Cash Card (45 days)*
 5. Loss Claims 
 6. Step-up abusers 
 

 Testing results concluded an error rate of 9.6% (31 cases) where the agents failed to follow the workflows adequately to deliver the desired dispute decision results. 
 

 The key themes for exceptions where the workflows were not adequately followed include:
 a. The denial reason used to deny the customer was inaccurate 
 b. The dispute decision was inaccurate (error/no error) 
 c. Agent did not follow the Knowledge Article for appropriate escalation/transfers to ATO or Dispute Abuser
 d. Agent did not review all transactions and activities required by the Knowledge Articles.
 e. Agent mishandled the filing to the network  
 f. Agent manual errors (i.e. failed to provide an attachment; provided attachment on a wrong case/customer)
 g. Agent followed a legacy workflow that had been retired prior to the case decision date
 

 * There was no operational error observed for samples selected on this workflow.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Rating Justification: An Issue that presents a critical or significant unacceptable level of risk to Block that needs prompt corrective action by management to mitigate the risk adequately.
Issue Action Plans: Regulation E Dispute Case Handling (1086568)
Closure Request Justification: Closing evidence validated by GCMT. See action plan for details.

Issue Title: Dispute Workflows (When Errors Occur in a Different Manner or Amount) [Cash App - US]
Issue Description:
Requirement: 
 Regulation E — Electronic Fund Transfer Act §1005.11(d) Procedure When an Error Did Not Occur or Did Not Occur as Alleged
 Procedures if a financial institution determines no error or different error occurred. In addition to following the procedures specified in paragraph (c) of this section, the financial institution shall follow the procedures set forth in this paragraph (d) if it determines that no error occurred or that an error occurred in a manner or amount different from that described by the consumer:
 

 Official interpretation of 11(d) Procedures if Financial Institution Determines No Error or Different Error Occurred
 1. Error different from that alleged. When a financial institution determines that an error occurred in a manner or amount different from that described by the consumer, it must comply with the requirements of both §§ 1005.11(c) and (d), as relevant. The institution may give the notice of correction and the explanation separately or in a combined form.
 

 Testing Results: 
 The dispute workflows are not appropriately designed to allow a proper investigation and response when an error occurred in a manner or amount different from that described by the consumer. Specifically, GCMT noted:
  1. There is no dispute response template in place to address the scenario where an error occurred in a different manner or amount. For the total of 418 cases reviewed across all active deny reasons for the in-scope populations, the dispute response was limited to "no error occurred".
 

  2. The current workflows require the agents to initiate any case investigation based on claim type submitted, and do not allow any re-categorization when the customer might have submitted the claim through a secondary reason. As an example, a customer who selected "I didn't make this transaction" for an ATM withdrawal error case was dispositioned with the denial reason of "System Reliability" without further investigation. The workflow design prevents the agents from performing the adequate investigation to determine if an error occurred in a manner or amount different from that described by the consumer.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Cash App Consumer Protection (P2P, Cash Card, Gift Card, and Bitcoin) - 2023 GCMT Review (1018021)
Issue Rating Justification: An Issue that presents a critical or significant unacceptable level of risk to Block that needs prompt corrective action by management to mitigate the risk adequately.
Issue Action Plans:
Dispute workflows (System/agent should be able to change the error type based on the investigation) - P2P ONLY (1086572)
 Dispute workflows (System/agent should be able to change the error type based on the investigation) - $Pay & Cash Card (1364510)
Closure Request Justification:
Both action plans have been validated by GCMT. 
 Furthermore, GMCT will conduct further testing of the CIF framework during 2026. Monitoring of P2P dispute processing is currently underway and will be closed in February 2026. Cash Card's dispute processing will be monitored during March and April and the overarching design and effectiveness of the entire dispute workflow will be reviewed in Q3 2026. 
 The attached documentation and the additional GCMT tests are sufficient evidence to close this action and the overarching issue.

Issue Title: Periodic statements to customers do not contain all required information consistent with the Regulatory requirements [Cash App - US]
Issue Description:
Requirement: Regulation E — Electronic Fund Transfer Act 12 C.F.R.§ 1005.9 Receipts at electronic terminals; periodic statements. § 1005.9( b) Periodic Statements.
 For an account to or from which electronic fund transfers can be made, a financial institution shall send a periodic statement for each monthly cycle in which an electronic fund transfer has occurred; and shall send a periodic statement at least quarterly if no transfer has occurred. The statement shall set forth the following information, as applicable. 
 Note; GCMT have included the relevant prescribed information in the finding below.
 Finding Description: GCMT testing concluded that the following sections of the statement were out of compliance with the Reg E requirement stated under § 1005.9(b), based on the in-scope account statements reviewed: 
 § 1005.9(b)(1)(i) The amount of the transfer. Statements must reflect each electronic fund transfer to or from the consumer’s account.
 Periodic statements do not currently reflect provisional credits for customers who filed disputes (this includes the granting and reversal of provisional credit).
 

 § 1005.9(b)(1)(iii) The type of transfer and type of account to or from which funds were transferred
 "Type of transfer" of a fund is not sufficiently disclosed for a consumer to accurately identify the transfer type. The business should ensure that statements reflect transfer types via consistent nomenclature (e.g. Cash Card transaction; Cash App Pay Transaction; P2P transfer; Add funds; Paper Money Deposit; Cash Out (standard), Direct Deposit).
 "Type of account" to/from which funds are transferred is not adequately disclosed. The statement does not have a designated field to reflect the type of account for each fund transferred. The "description" field used does not consistently reflect the required information and lacks strong indicators to distinguish between account types, as well as incoming/outgoing transactions.
 

 § 1005.9(b)(1)(iv) For a transfer initiated by the consumer at an electronic terminal (except for a deposit of cash or a check, draft, or similar paper instrument), the terminal location described in paragraph (a)(5)
 The statement does not disclose the required terminal location data for applicable transfer initiated at an electronic terminal. The following were not noted on the statement: (a) the street address; (b) a generally accepted name for the specific location; or (c) the name of the owner or operator of the terminal if someone other than the account-holding institution. 
 

 § 1005.9(b)(1)(v) The name of any third party to or from whom funds were transferred.
 9(b)(1)(v)(1)Recurring payments by government agency - The statement does not meet this requirement as the third-party name for recurring payments from Federal, state, or local governments are not actively identified to allow disclosure adjustment. 
 9(b)(1)(v)(2) Consumer as third-party payee - For in-app P2P transactions, a full name is not displayed (i.e. Walker). For external transfers, the card number instead of the name is disclosed.
 9(b)(1)(v)(3) Terminal location/third party - The statement does not disclose any terminal location data. 
 9(b)(1)(v)(4)Account-holding institution as third party - For Paper Money Deposit transactions, the institution detail is shown as blank in all statement transactions reviewed for November - mid December, 2023.  
 

 § 1005.9(b)(2) The customer’s account number 
 The statement does not appropriately disclose the account number for all customers. For customers with Cash Cards, the statement discloses their account number with the third party bank information. However, for non Cash Card users without a banking account number, an alternative (i.e. Cashtag) was not identified. 
 

 § 1005.9(b)(3) Fees assessed against the account during the statement period
 Fees are not disclosed appropriately for ATM transactions. The statement is designed to only disclose Cash App Fee, Cash App Fee Reimbursement and Government Fee. As a result, any fees charged by ATM operators are not disclosed to the customer on the statement.
 Fees disclosed require improvement to include additional details for type/reason of charges. The statement currently does not have a field to disclose any supporting details of the fees charged (i.e. EFT/non-electronic services; fixed fees/per-item fees, charged by Cash App or other means etc.) 
 

 § 1005.9(b)(6) A telephone number the consumer may call to ascertain whether preauthorized transfers to the consumer's account have occurred 
 The statement disclosed a customer service telephone number with the instruction to reach out for any errors/questions. However, the statement does not explicitly disclose that the consumer may call the Cash App customer phone line to ascertain whether preauthorized transfers to the consumer's account have occurred.

Issue Title: There are no formal procedures surrounding Regulation E change in terms disclosures and record retention.
Issue Description:
Requirement: Regulation E - Electronic Fund Transfer Act § 1005.8 Change in terms notice; error resolution notice
 (1) Prior notice required. A financial institution shall mail or deliver a written notice to the consumer, at least 21 days before the effective date, of any change in a term or condition required to be disclosed under § 1005.7(b) of this part if the change would result in:
 (i) Increased fees for the consumer;
 (ii) Increased liability for the consumer;
 (iii) Fewer types of available electronic fund transfers; or
 (iv) Stricter limitations on the frequency or dollar amount of transfers.
 

 Finding Description: GCMT noted the "Change in Terms Disclosures" requirement is accurately captured in the Electronic Fund Transfer Act (Regulation E) Policy. However, for a critical requirement, there were no formal procedures in place to outline the responsibilities and guidance for cross functional teams. 
 

 GCMT noted that the change in a term or condition could be initiated at individual product line level within Cash App. The control relies on Product Counsel to timely raise any change fact pattern to Regulatory Counsel to verify if a notification is required. Product Counsel is then responsible for coordinating with the business teams to send out a change in terms notice if applicable. 
 

 The procedures should also establish adequate record retention requirements for clear tracking of the version of change in terms notice shared, impacted consumer base, distribution list, and the specific elements of changes that were approved and distributed. The lack of procedures could increase the control risk and does not permit effective testing to demonstrate compliance.

Issue Title: Investigation Response - No Error Occurred [Cash App - US]
Issue Description:
Requirement: 
 Regulation E—Electronic Fund Transfer Act §1005.11 Procedures for resolving errors.
 (a) Definition of error —1) Types of transfers or inquiries covered. The term “error” means:
 (i) An unauthorized electronic fund transfer; (ii) An incorrect electronic fund transfer to or from the consumer's account; (iii) The omission of an electronic fund transfer from a periodic statement; (iv) A computational or bookkeeping error made by the financial institution relating to an electronic fund transfer;(v) The consumer's receipt of an incorrect amount of money from an electronic terminal; (vi) An electronic fund transfer not identified in accordance with § 1005.9 or § 1005.10(a); or (vii) The consumer's request for documentation required by § 1005.9 or § 1005.10(a) or for additional information or clarification concerning an electronic fund transfer, including a request the consumer makes to determine whether an error exists under paragraphs (a)(1)(i) through (vi) of this section.
 

 (d) Procedures if a financial institution determines no error or different error occurred. In addition to following the procedures specified in paragraph (c) of this section, the financial institution shall follow the procedures set forth in this paragraph (d) if it determines that no error occurred or that an error occurred in a manner or amount different from that described by the consumer:
 

 Testing Results: 
 For Reg E purpose, an "error" is specifically defined under § 1005.11 Procedures for resolving errors. The denial conclusion of "no error occurred" should confirm none of the Reg E errors occurred on the disputed claim. As a result, the resolution response of "Based on the details provided we determined that no error occurred. Therefore, we have denied your dispute" is not an accurate conclusion of case investigation for all scenarios where a no refund decision is concluded. GCMT specifically noted the following scenarios where an "no error occurred" conclusion is not appropriate: 
  1. Any cases denied due to untimely notification from the customer (i.e. Sixty_Day_Non_Fraud)
  2. Any cases denied due to the case being withdrawn from the customer ( i.e. Cash Card:"CLAIM_WITHDRAWN")
  3. Any cases denied due to a duplicate claim had been identified (i.e. DUPLICATE_TRANSACTION_REG_E)

Issue Title: Consumer Limited Liability [Cash App - US]
Issue Description:
Requirement: 
 Regulation E — Electronic Fund Transfer Act § 1005.6 Liability of consumer for unauthorized transfers.
 § 1005.6(b)(2) Timely notice not given. 
 If the consumer fails to notify the financial institution within two business days after learning of the loss or theft of the access device, the consumer's liability shall not exceed the lesser of $500 or the sum of:
 (i) $50 or the amount of unauthorized transfers that occur within the two business days, whichever is less; and
 (ii) The amount of unauthorized transfers that occur after the close of two business days and before notice to the institution, provided the institution establishes that these transfers would not have occurred had the consumer notified the institution within that two-day period.
 

 § 1005.6(b)(3) Periodic statement; timely notice not given. 
 A consumer must report an unauthorized electronic fund transfer that appears on a periodic statement within 60 days of the financial institution's transmittal of the statement to avoid liability for subsequent transfers. If the consumer fails to do so, the consumer's liability shall not exceed the amount of the unauthorized transfers that occur after the close of the 60 days and before notice to the institution, and that the institution establishes would not have occurred had the consumer notified the institution within the 60-day period. When an access device is involved in the unauthorized transfer, the consumer may be liable for other amounts set forth in paragraphs (b)(1) or (b)(2) of this section, as applicable.
 

 § 1005.6(b)(6) Liability under state law or agreement. 
 If state law or an agreement between the consumer and the financial institution imposes less liability than is provided by this section, the consumer's liability shall not exceed the amount imposed under the state law or agreement.
 

 Testing Results:
 The handling of dispute cases where a timely notice is not given does not fully meet the regulatory requirement. 
  1. The Disputron workflow currently processes all cases at an individual transfer level. However, certain sections of Reg E (i.e. § 1005.6 - Liability of consumer for unauthorized transfers) emphasized on the consideration of customer’s limited liabilities over a period of time or at an incident/event level (i.e. amount of transfers). The current workflow does not sufficiently demonstrate the institution’s consideration of the applicable rules. 
 

  2. On manually decisioned cases, cases are denied with denial reasons that are directly tied to notice timeliness (e.g. FRAUD_60_DAY_P2P) without demonstrating adequate considerations on customer's limited liabilities on unauthorized transfers.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
