Issue Title: Automated Disputron - Case Documentation [Cash App - US]
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 Regulation E — Electronic Fund Transfer Act 12 C.F.R. §1005.11(d) Procedure When an Error Did Not Occur or Did Not Occur as Alleged
 After an institution completes its investigation, if it determines an error did not occur, or one occurred that differs in the manner or amount the consumer alleged, the institution must notify the consumer in writing of the results of its investigation and the consumer’s right to request the documents the institution relied on in making its determination.
 (c) Time limits and extent of investigation (4) Investigation - Official interpretation of 11(c)(4) Investigation. 5. No EFT agreement.
 When there is no agreement between the institution and the third party for the type of EFT involved, the financial institution must review any relevant information within the institution's own records for the particular account to resolve the consumer's claim. The extent of the investigation required may vary depending on the facts and circumstances. However, a financial institution may not limit its investigation solely to the payment instructions where additional information within its own records pertaining to the particular account in question could help to resolve a consumer's claim. Information that may be reviewed as part of an investigation might include:i. The ACH transaction records for the transfer; ii. The transaction history of the particular account for a reasonable period of time immediately preceding the allegation of error; iii. Whether the check number of the transaction in question is notably out-of-sequence; iv. The location of either the transaction or the payee in question relative to the consumer's place of residence and habitual transaction area; v. Information relative to the account in question within the control of the institution's third-party service providers if the financial institution reasonably believes that it may have records or other information that could be dispositive; or vi. Any other information appropriate to resolve the claim.
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT noted that the dispute program needs to improve its documentation standards to meet the regulatory expectations: 
  1. There is a lack of audit trails to demonstrate the reasonableness of dispute case investigations in both manual and automated Reg E case decisions.The current dispute program does not require any case documentation outside of the system generated activity log in Toolbox. The activity log only displays the final denial reason of the case (e.g. [Paid By Other Means (Reg E)] Denied by automation) without any audit trails to showcase the investigation and decisioning actions occurred to conclude the result. 
 

  2. The change management for Disputron automation rules should be formally established to ensure any new rules and changes to existing rules are sufficiently documented, approved and timely published to applicable parties (e.g. manual review teams) when an impact is identified. A proposal document is created today when a new rule is planned to be implemented for Legal and third-party banking partners’ approvals where it’s applicable. The documents should be continuously tracked for its accuracy and a standard should be established to define where a change management approval is required.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: CRA public file does not have a comprehensive products and services listing with applicable bank fees
Issue Description:
In accordance with 12 CFR 228.43 Content and availability of public file, a bank shall maintain a public file that includes the following information; 
 

 12 CFR 228.43 (a)(5) Information Available to the public - A list of services (including hours of operation, available loan and deposit products, and transaction fees) generally offered at the bank's branches and descriptions of material differences in the availability or cost of services at particular branches, if any. At its option, a bank may include information regarding the availability of alternative systems for delivering retail banking services (e.g., ATMs, ATMs not owned or operated by or exclusively for the bank, banking by telephone or computer, loan production offices, and bank-at-work or bank-by-mail programs);
 

 12 CFR 228.43(b)(3) Small banks - A small bank or a bank that was a small bank during the prior calendar year shall include in its public file: (i) The bank's loan-to-deposit ratio for each quarter of the prior calendar year and, at its option, additional data on its loan-to-deposit ratio.
 

 12 CFR 228.43(e) Updating - Except as otherwise provided in this section, a bank shall ensure that the information required by this section is current as of April 1 of each year.
 

 Testing Result:
 GCMT noted the following in the two public files (April 2023 and June 2023):
 1. In both public files, The "Additional Savings Deposit Account Terms" was included; however, GCMT was unable to confirm the information in the Savings deposit account terms (i.e., transaction fees) was current as of April 1 because the effective date was missing. 
 2. In both public files, a complete list of SFS loan products including applicable fees with an effective date was not included. 
 3. In both public files, the bank's loan-to-deposit ratio for each quarter of the prior calendar year was not included.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: The issue stems from a failure to adhere to policy and procedure
Issue Rating Justification: This issue has regulatory (Community Reinvestment Act) impact, and could result in regulatory non-compliance if the risk is not mitigated.
Issue Action Plans: Update CRA public file checklist (1089212)
Closure Request Justification: Brandon Kelly provided CRA checklist on 04/19/2024. The CRA checklist includes: bullet points to check if the CRA public file has the following: 1. products and services listing with applicable bank fees. 2. bank's loan-to-deposit ratio. GCMT notes Action Plan and Issue have been addressed successfully.

Issue Title: REF #1 - SIL Protected Disclosures Q1 2024 [Square - SIL] - Transcript or minutes review process not included in the Speak Up Policy
Issue Description:
Regulatory requirement:
 Section 6A(2)(b) of the Protected Disclosures Act 2014 (Revised, Updated to 22 July 2023) states that internal reporting channels and procedures shall enable reports to be made by telephone or through other voice messaging systems and, upon request by the reporting person, by means of a physical meeting with the employer or a designated person, as the case may be, within a reasonable period from the making of the request.
 

 Section 16C(7) states that where a reporting person requests a meeting in person with a member of staff designated under section 6A, 7A, 10B, 10C, 10D or 10E for the purposes therein stated— 
 (a) the person to whom this section applies shall ensure, subject to the consent of the reporting person, that complete and accurate records of the meeting are kept in a durable and retrievable form, and
 (b) the person to whom this section applies may document the meeting in the form of—
 (i) an audio recording of the conversation in a durable and retrievable form, or
 (ii) accurate minutes of the meeting prepared by a member of staff so designated, and where the reporting person has disclosed his or her identity, the person to whom this section applies shall offer that reporting person the opportunity to check, verify and agree the minutes of the meeting by signing them.
 

 Testing Results:
 The SIL Speak Up Policy does not include the process for physical meeting upon request by the reporting person.
 

 Regulatory requirement:
 Section 16C(6) states that where a reporting person has disclosed his or her identity, and the person to whom this section applies has documented the reporting in the form of a transcript or minutes in accordance with subsection (4) or (5), as the case may be, the person to whom this section applies shall offer the reporting person the opportunity to check, rectify and agree by way of signature the transcript or minutes.
 

 Testing Results:
 The SIL Speak Up Policy does not include the transcript or minutes review process as deemed appropriate in accordance with the regulatory requirement.
 

 Regulatory requirement:
 Section 16(3)(a) of the Protected Disclosures Act 2014 (Revised, Updated to 22 July 2023) states that where the identity of the reporting person or any other information referred to in subsection (1) is disclosed to another person in accordance with paragraph (a) or (b)(ii) of subsection (2), the reporting person shall be notified, in writing, before their identity or the information concerned is disclosed unless such notification would jeopardise—
 (i) the effective investigation of the relevant wrongdoing concerned,
 (ii) the prevention of serious risk to the security of the State, public health, public safety or the environment, or
 (iii) the prevention of crime or the prosecution of a criminal offence.
 (b) A notification under paragraph (a) shall include the reasons for the disclosure referred to in that paragraph.
 

 Testing Results:
 The SIL Speak Up Policy does not include the process for notifying the reporting person in writing before their identity is disclosed to another person as deemed appropriate in accordance with the regulatory requirement.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: It is unlikely that the finding will lead to regulatory criticism or reputational exposure. The finding relates to isolated deviations from Low Risk internal policies and procedures. The issue can be corrected in the ordinary course of business.
Issue Action Plans:

 REF #1 - SIL Protected Disclosures Q1 2024 (Employment Counsel & Employee Relations) (1090525)
 REF #1 - SIL Protected Disclosures Q1 2024 (Employment Counsel & Employee Relations) (1090530)

Issue Title: System Logic Issue allowing New York State residents to access the Lightning Network
Issue Description: The New York State Department of Financial Services (NYDFS) did not approve the use of the Lightning Network (LN) for New York State (NYS) resident customers. As a result, Cash App must exclude these customers from accessing the network. During the review, Testing identified that some customers were able to access the LN due to a system logic discrepancy "the bug". The bug caused an error in the systems logic causing it to think the customer is residing somewhere other then NYS due to the customer having a debit card or a home address linked to a different State on the platform. This would trick the system into thinking they were not residing in New York and granted access to the LN.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: Square CSA are not consistently providing the Call Recording Disclaimer and/or completing IDV for Outbound Calls [Square AU]
Issue Description:
Privacy Disclaimer
 Per the ‘TOOLS Call Recording Disclaimer’ Salesforce Knowledge Article (‘SFK’), where a Square Customer
 Success Advocate (‘CSA’) is making an outbound call, including returning a disconnected call, a privacy
 disclaimer must be read.
 The following disclaimer is mandated and requires explicit affirmative consent from the caller “Just to let you
 know, this call may be monitored or recorded, for quality and training purposes - is that okay?".
 GCMT’s review of 50 outbound calls made by Square CSA during the review period, identified 5 calls (10%)
 where the Square CSA did not provide the disclaimer.
 

 Customer Identification
 Along with the disclaimer, there are certain levels of verification that a CSA is required to complete that dictates
 what details can be discussed or the type of action that can be performed over the phone, as required by the
 “Verify Seller” Knowledge Article and “Seller Actions and Verification Levels” Salesforce Knowledge Article.
 In the same sample of 50 calls, GCMT noted 7 instances (14% - consisting of 5 Square CSA and 2 Business
 Process Outsourced advocates) where the Square CSA did not complete P1 level verification (Customer legal
 name and email address) when discussing account particulars and/or remotely accessing the customer’s
 dashboard.
 Additionally, in one call, a Square CSA did not provide the privacy disclaimer or verify the customer's identity.
 

 Quality Assurance
 The current Quality Assurance program for Customer Success Advocates, does not include the review of
 Outbound Calls. Quality Assurance reviews are limited to two inbound calls per week, for each Advocate.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Rating Justification: Rated low in accordance with GCMT issue rating methodology.

Issue Title: The automated controls for the charging and cancellations of monthly subscription fees are not operating as designed
Issue Description:
Plus Card Subscription Fee Cap
 Background
 The Afterpay Plus Card is operating under an approved exemption under Singular Sign Section 6(5) requirements of the National Credit Code which requires the application of a Maximum Fee cap for ‘charges of credit’ to consumers in a 12 month period. Per ‘Section 51 of the National Consumer Credit Protection Regulations 2010’, the prescribed maximum charge for the contract is $200 for the first year, $125 for the year after. 
 Afterpay Plus Card has been established with a $9.99 a month subscription fee, which is under the annual prescribed maximum charge. 
 Finding
 GCMT identified 12 customer Plus Card Subscriptions where the customer was double charged the monthly $9.99 Subscription Fee during the period 1st of May 2023 - 31st of January 2024. 
 For all 12 samples reviewed, the duplicate charge was due to customers temporarily pausing their subscription, then reactivating the Plus card Subscription the very next day. This resulted in Square charging the subscription fee again upon reactivation for a total of $19.98 instead of $9.99.
 GCMT did not identify any customer requests for refunds on these duplicated charges. As all the customers are still within the first year of Afterpay Plus Card, these customers also did not pass the $200 a year threshold.
 There is currently no reporting or monitoring in place over the deduction of monthly subscription fees and the annual fee cap requirements. 
 Prior to October 2023, the automatic deduction of Plus Card Subscription Fees were facilitated through the Square Payment Platform from user’s nominated credit or debit cards. From October 2023, all Plus Card Subscription Fees were planned to be deducted through an ‘Afterpay Subscription Platform’ that has been integrated into the consumer’s Afterpay Account and existing payment methods. We understand that not all customers will be migrated to the Afterpay Platform until circa the end of April 2024. 
 

 Automatic Cancellations of Plus Card Subscription 
 Background
 Per the ‘Plus Card Product Requirement Document’ where a customer’s status changes in their billing cycle and the grace period has been exceeded (where applicable), the Plus Card Subscription will be immediately canceled. The applicable status changes include the following: 
 - Disabled
 - On-Hold (3 days grace period)
 - Suspended (28 days grace period)
 - Hardship or Payment Arrangement (28 days grace period)
 - Close
 - Frozen
 - Self-Service Exclusion
 (Grace periods were introduced to recognise the temporary changes in customers’ account statuses. For example, where a customer might report fraudulent transactions upon which the account could be temporarily suspended only to be reinstated after due diligence and investigations completed by Afterpay. )
 Finding
 GCMT identified 8 out of 79 samples (10%) where the customer's Plus Card subscription did not automatically cancel when their status changed to "Payment Arrangement", "On Hold" or "Self-Exclusion" 
 - 3 samples where the customers account was in "Payment Arrangement"
 - 2 samples where the customers account was "On Hold"
 - 3 sample where the customer self excluded from using Afterpay 
 There is currently no monitoring in place over automatic cancellations triggered by status cha
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Q1 2024 Afterpay Plus Card Post Implementation Review (1018696)
RCA Description: Exceptions occurred due to bugs within the coding.
Issue Rating Justification: Non-compliance with laws/regulations, supervisory guidance, or Block policy that if not corrected could subject the organization to regulatory criticism or have an entity level impact

Issue Title: Cash Borrow non-compliance with CAN-SPAM Act disclosures/opt-out mechanism [Cash App Lending - US]
Issue Description:
Cash Borrow commercial electronic email messages do not contain CAN SPAM Act regulatory disclosures and customers are not being provided with a mechanism to Opt Out when receiving email commercial electronic messages.
 

 Requirement:
 15 USC Ch. 103: CONTROLLING THE ASSAULT OF NON-SOLICITED PORNOGRAPHY AND MARKETING
 §7702. Definitions
 (2)(A) The term "commercial electronic mail message" means any electronic mail message the primary purpose of which is the commercial advertisement or promotion of a commercial product or service
 

 §7704. Other protections for users of commercial electronic mail
 (5) Inclusion of identifier, opt-out, and physical address in commercial electronic mail
 (A) It is unlawful for any person to initiate the transmission of any commercial electronic mail message to a protected computer unless the message provides—
 (i) clear and conspicuous identification that the message is an advertisement or solicitation;
 (ii) clear and conspicuous notice of the opportunity to decline to receive further commercial electronic mail messages from the sender; and
 (iii) a valid physical postal address of the sender.
 

 Testing Results:
 Cash Borrow Offers sent via email do not include: 1) Identification that the email message is an advertisement, 2) A clear and conspicuous opt-out mechanism, and 3) A valid physical postal address of the sender.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

RCA Description: Control does not exist
Issue Rating Justification: May represent violation of requirement set forth "explicitly" in law, which could result in an enforcement action via a regulator and/or a material fine.
Issue Action Plans: Uplift of customer electronic messages (1101759)
Closure Request Justification: Action plan has been validated.

Issue Title: Opportunity to enhance the SOPs and macros & to process Plus Card reinstatement and refund requests in line with the SOP [Afterpay AU]
Issue Description:
Reinstatement of accounts 
 Per the ‘Plus Card | Cancellation of Membership | Remediation’ SOP, all escalated tickets for reinstatement are required to be reviewed by the Global FinRec team to confirm that the customer meets one of the acceptable scenarios. Following approval, the FinRec agent is required to complete a google form, which is then forwarded to the Engineering team for manual resetting of the customer's eligibility to sign up for Plus Card. 
 Management confirmed that these Google Form requests are compiled into the ‘Plus Card Remediation Requests Listing.’
 Through data analysis of reinstated Plus Card Subscriptions against the Plus Card Remediation Requests Listing, GCMT identified the following: 
 - 2 samples where the Plus Card Subscription was reinstated without approval from the Global FinRec team; and
  > Of the 2 samples, we identified 1 instance where the customer’s Plus Card Subscription was reinstated; however, the customer was advised by an agent that their subscription would not be reinstated due to the customer’s overdue Plus Card Subscription payment exceeding the grace period of 7 days.
 

 Subscription Fee Refunds and Cancellations 
 Per the ‘Global Fin Rec | Afterpay Plus Card Subscription Fee Refunds / Cancellations SOP’ customers may be eligible for a refund where certain criteria is met. 
 GCMT tested 20 customer requests for a Plus Card Subscription Fee refund and identified 4 exceptions (20%) relating to: 
 - 3 instances (75%) where the customer was declined a refund of the Plus Card subscription fee when they had met the criteria for accidental sign ups (i.e. did not make an out of network purchase and [prior to December 2023] contacted customer service within 48 hours of signing up) 
 - 1 instance (25%) where the customer was provided a refund when the reason provided by the customer was not acceptable under the SOP. 
 

 Agent compliance with Plus Card Macros 
 GCMT noted that in some instances agents were not sending the required macros and/or sending the incorrect macro based on the enquiry/request and Standard Operating Procedure which was resulting in enquiries only being partially addressed. 
 The following exceptions were noted: 
 - ‘Helping Customers with Plus Card’- For 86 samples tested, GCMT noted 8 exceptions (9%)
  > 6 exceptions where the incorrect macro was provided 
  > 2 exceptions where a macro was not utilized in the response
 - ‘Plus Card | Cancelation Of Membership | Remediation SOP' - For 24 samples tested, GCMT noted 4 exceptions (16%). All four exceptions related to the agent not utilizing a macro when providing a response. However 3 out of the 4 exceptions were escalated as a complaint. 
 - ‘Afterpay Plus Card:: Billing Error Acknowledgement’ - For 3 samples tested, GCMT identified a FinRec agent not utilizing the acknowledgement macro when providing a response. However in all instances the FinRec agent responded immediately with an outcome of the investigation and the resolution to provide a refund to the customer. 
 

 Plus Card Macros - Updates 
 GCMT noted the following macros where an update may be required to reflect current practices/expectations: 
 - The "Afterpay Plus Card:: Refund of monthly fee confirmation" macro is not reflective of the communication provided to the customers in practice. Currently the macro only advises that "It may take up to 10 business days for the funds to return to you, depending on your financial institution." However, in practice the agents are providing further information such as:
  > Please be advised that although the subscription for the Afterpay Plus Card was already canceled and the fee has been refunded, the customer will still be able to continue using the Plus Card until the current billing cycle ends
  > Once the card billing period expires, the Afterpay Plus Card will be replaced with the Afterpay Card.
  > Please do not remove the preferred payment method or the refund cannot be processed
  > When a subscription has been canceled, customers are only able to sign up again after a 12-month wait time.
 This has resulted in inconsistent information provided to customers in 14 samples. 
 - The ‘Helping Customers with Afterpay Plus Card SOP’ does not state which macro the agent is required to use for customers who have the status "On waiting list" and have contacted Afterpay.  
  > Management has confirmed that the "Afterpay Plus Card:: Held out group" macro should be sent to customers that have the "Customer on waiting list" status.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Q1 2024 Afterpay Plus Card Post Implementation Review (1018696)
RCA Description: Human error. Inconsistent procedures and macros led to inconsistent practices.
Issue Rating Justification: Processes / controls are designed appropriately - exceptions were minor and administrative.
Closure Request Justification:
Closure completed - See all action plans for details. 
 

 SOPs and Macros have been reviewed and updated as well as communicated to the team members. 
 Training has been provided to team members on relevant SOPs to ensure that procedures and correct macros are utilised.

Issue Title: Transactional Testing Findings - Q1 Afterpay Pay Monthly and Plus Card Complaint Monitoring [Afterpay Pay Monthly and Plus Card - US]
Issue Description:
1. The identification and logging of complaints from Pay Monthly and Plus Card general inquiries can be improved to increase the capturing, resolution and reporting of complaints.
 GCMT tested a random sample of 158 customer inquiries and identified 14 inquiries that met the definition of a complaint. The effectiveness rate for complaint identification was 64% whereby 5 of 14 (36%) inquiries were not identified and logged as a complaint. 
 

 2. The responses and resolutions provided by Customer Service Agents can be improved for Pay Monthly and Plus Card Tier 1 Complaints. 
 Plus Card
 Tier 1: 84% effectiveness rate consisting of a 16% exception rate where the complaint was only partially resolved (4%) or not resolved in full (12%).  
 Tier 1: 76% effectiveness rate consisting of a 24% exception rate where the complaint was only partially resolved (18%) or not resolved in full (6%).
 

 3. The practices for the handling and escalating of Pay Monthly complaints by unlicensed and licensed agents in accordance with Standard Operating Procedures requires improvement. 
 GCMT tested a sample of 49 Pay Monthly Tier 1 complaints and 16 Tier 2 Complaints and noted the effectiveness rate for compliance with Pay Monthly SOP's was 71% consisting of a 29% exception rate and 69% effectiveness rate for Tier 2 consisting of a 31% exception rate. 
 

 Tier 1 - Non-Compliance with the SOP was due to the following exceptions which are not related to licenced activities;
 ● A frontline General Customer Service agent attempted first point resolution instead of escalating through to the Pay Monthly Support Team
 ● Delay in first response by the frontline agent (to escalate through to Pay Monthly Support team) or by the Pay Monthly teams themselves (>4 business days) 
 ● Incorrect complaint resolution field 
 ● Non-Complaint which was not untagged by the agent 
 ● No notes left on the customer's account per Admin Portal 
 

 Tier 2 - Non-Compliance with the SOP was due to the following exceptions which are not related to licenced activities;
 ● A frontline General Customer Service agent attempted first point resolution instead of escalating through to the Pay Monthly Support Team
 ● Collections exclusion was not placed on the account 
 ● The complaint was escalated to the licensed support team, with no first point resolution by the unlicensed Support Agent (When the complaint was still Tier 1) 
 ● Call back request was incorrectly escalated to the General Support Call Back team, instead of an unlicensed Support Agent at first instance causing delays
 ●  Incorrect complaint resolution field 
 

 4. The current Standard Operating Procedures for raising Pay Monthly Merchant Disputes on behalf of the customer, can be uplifted to include minimum criteria and eligibility. 
 Finding 
 GCMT review of Pay Monthly Tier 1 and Tier 2 complaints, noted that the majority of the samples were due to the customer complaining to Afterpay that their goods have not been received, are faulty or a refund had not yet been received despite a considerable time-frame since a Merchant refund confirmation was received.
 

 GCMT identified discrepancies in agent handling/responses to these complaints with the following themes noted:
 ● The current ‘Background to Merchant Disputes and Order Mismatches’ SOP does not currently contain detail about what information is required to be obtained by the agent or the eligibility criteria for a dispute to be raised on behalf of the customer. This is resulting in inconsistency on when Merchant Disputes are offered to the customer as part of the complaint resolution or where a Merchant Dispute was raised with insufficient evidence provided by the customer. 
 ● The level of information relating to the Merchant Dispute process provided to customers predominantly via phone conversations varies. For example some agents may advise customers of the 14 day time-frame for investigation and post that time-frame if there is no response the case will be closed in favor of the customer, where others remain silent. 
 ● Per the Afterpay Pay Monthly Responsibility Matrix, Merchant disputes are able to be processed by the unlicensed team (‘Pay Monthly Support Team’) however in practice these complaints are still being escalated through to the licenced team (‘Pay Monthly Licensed team) for processing which is resulting in an increase to total handling time.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Global Customer Experience Review for Afterpay/Clearpay  (1007462)
Impacted Legal Entities:
Square Capital, LLC
 Afterpay US Services
Issue Rating Justification: Rated in accordance with the Block Issue Management Program Rating Guidance.

Deep Dive into Pay Monthly Complaints (1103750)
 Team refresher of requirements and enhancement to QA review for Pay Monthly  (1103753)
 Review of Merchant Disputes SOP (1103755)
 Mapping Review of Pay Monthly licensed versus Non licensed (1103759)
Closure Request Justification: All actions have been submitted and verified by GCMT for closure.

Issue Title: The Square AU COI Policy has not been designed in accordance with the nature, scale, and complexity of the Business. [Square - AU]
Issue Description:
Requirement: RG 181.32
 It is important that a licensee’s conflicts management arrangements are designed with their own particular circumstances in mind. We encourage licensees to ensure that their conflict management arrangements are designed or tailored according to the nature, scale, and complexity of their business.
 

 Background: 
 GCMT conducted a GAP analysis between the applicable requirements in RG 181 – Conflicts of Interest and the current Square Australia Conflicts of Interest Policy. The analysis was conducted with consideration to Square Australia’s current operating environment, product and services offered and remuneration practices. 
 

 Finding: 
 The current formalized policy generally acknowledges and documents the RG 181 requirements on a word-to-word basis but does not contain adequate detail relating to the processes and practices in place to effectively identify, monitor and manage Conflicts of Interest (‘COI’) in accordance with RG 181 and their AFSL. 
 GCMT identified the COI Policy does not contain the below sections or details in accordance with RG 181.
 

 RG 181.35
 (Reporting Lines and Internal Structures)  The current structures and reporting lines for Conflicts of Interest including the Roles and responsibilities;
 ● Between Direct Responsible Individuals (‘DRI’) in Square Australia
 ● Block divisions such as the Global Ethics and Code of Conduct Team and the Governance/Board committees and;or:
 ● Escalation protocols should be documented where applicable. 
 RG 181.31RG 181.45
 (Monitoring of COI andDocumentation and Record Keeping)
 The record keeping for compliance monitoring activities related to COI for the processes in place to evidence monitoring of procedural controls detailed within the ‘Monitoring Section’ and Annexure A – Table of COI to ensure effective control mitigation of 'controlled' or ‘approved’ conflicts of interest.
 

 RG 181.29RG 181.34
 (Management of COI)  
 ● The process for the management, assessment and reporting for employees disclosed conflicts of interest. 
 ● A methodology, legend, or risk assessment to understand the overall outcome or required actions following the assignment of the three key COI criteria being the likelihood, consequences, and qualitative risk outcome for each individual COI within the Annexure A - Table of COI. E.g., if the COI will be controlled or avoided. 
 ● The process for identification of breaches with the COI policy. 
 

 RG 181.28/RG 181.42
 (Controlling COI and Avoiding COI) The requirements for ‘Controlling and Avoiding Conflicts of Interest’ was assessed in accordance with ASIC RG 181 guidance: 23 self-assessment questions. Based on this review the following GAPS were noted with the Policy.
 ● No clear section for the ‘identification of COI’ i.e., through annual review, employee disclosures and BAU monitoring. 
 ● No reference to related documents/policies i.e. Insider Trading Policy, Global Block Code of Business Conduct or Square Financial Services Guide.  
 

 RG 181.36
 (Information Barriers)  The information barriers in place to help Square AU manage their conflicts of interest. 
 

 To a lesser extent, the below sections of the policy could benefit from a bit more context as to their applicability and alignment to Square AU and RG 181.
 

 RG 181.38/RG 181.39
 (Remuneration practices) 
 The current remuneration practices in Square Australia and if there is a perceived risk of a conflict of interest arising which would require disclosure to Sellers. E.g., acknowledging the nature of commission-based remuneration for sales representatives and low complexity of product/service offerings. 
 

 RG 181.49 - RG 181.54/RG 181.58
 (Disclosing COI)  
 The current references to disclosure of COI in the policy are word-for-word from the relevant sub-section of RG 181. There is no notation of the situations relevant to Square Australia of where a disclosure would arise or if a disclosure would not be expected unless in an extremely rare circumstance. 
 

 RG 181.28/RG 181.42
 (Controlling COI and Avoiding COI) 
 Detail of Monitoring controls in place over unfair treatment of customers and remuneration
 

 GCMT were requested by the Square AU Compliance to verify if Conflicts of Interest are considered as part of the Square Australia Procurement/Third Party processes. GCMT met with the Procurement Team and were advised that there are no current processes in place for COI.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: Issue rated Medium/Moderate per issue rating methodology
Issue Action Plans: Review and update the existing Square Australia Conflicts of Interest Policy and Risk Assessment  (1112978)

Issue Title: The Square AU COI Policy contains processes and controls which are either not in place or can be improved [Square - AU]
Issue Description:
Background: 
 As an extension of the GAP analysis conducted to RG 181, where processes and controls were documented within the Square Conflicts of Interest (‘COI’) Policy and Annexure A -Conflicts of Interest Table, GCMT reviewed the design effectiveness of the controls in place and if they are operationalised as at April 2024.
 

 Finding: 
 1- GCMT identified that the monitoring processes and controls documented within the COI are not currently in place.
 A) The COI Policy states “Square AU’s Employee Conflict of Interest Policy is set forth in the Square AU Employee Handbook.”
 ● The Square AU Employee Handbook as of April 2024 does not reference the Square Australia Conflict of Interest Policy. Reference is only made to the Global Block Code of Business Conduct and Ethics Program which includes COI. 
 ● Additionally, the Employee Conflicts of Interest Policy documented in Annexure B of the COI policy, only contains information relating to Outside Activities and does not consider other Conflicts of Interest requirements. 
 B) The COI Policy states “Directors and other representatives such as managers or other employees who will be involved in the provision of financial services under the AFSL must on commencement of employment, and at least every 12 months thereafter must submit the ‘Pro Forma Disclosure of Personal Interest’ to the CO. The Compliance Officer must maintain a central registry of all relevant interest disclosure documents. “ This control is detailed within Annexure A – COI Table as a current control for managing several of the identified COI. 
 ● In practice, there is no current requirement at onboarding or 12 months thereafter for the Pro Forma Disclosure of Personal Interest to be completed and submitted. No disclosures have been historically received by the CO as this control has not been operationalized. 
 C) The COI Policy states, per Annexure A – COI Table “Employees encouraged to reach out to the legal team directly for questions concerning conflicts of interest.”
 ● Per the Global Block Code of Conduct Business Conduct Program employees are directed to the established Go Links or Ethics inbox for any questions concerning COI.
 D) The COI Policy States “The CO is responsible for ongoing review over ‘The treatment of all customers fairly so that financial services are not provided in a manner that unfairly puts the interests of Square AU ahead of customers (e.g. failure to disclose fees transparently); unfairly puts the interests of one customer ahead of the interests of other customers; and uses knowledge about customers in a way that is likely to advance Square AU’s own interest without sufficient disclosure to affected customers.”
 ● There is no current reporting or documentation of operational (frontline) controls which will allow this to be evidenced as being reviewed by the Compliance Officer on an ongoing basis.
 

 2- GCMT identified Roles and Responsibilities documented within the policy are not reflective of current practices and delegations or require additional detail. 
 The COI Policy states the below roles and responsibilities which are wholly assigned and delegated to the Compliance Officer.
 Per conversations with Compliance Management these are either currently delegated to another team/individual or should not be the sole responsibility of the CO based on current operating rhythms at Square Australia and Block. 
 A) “COI training and education of all relevant officers, employees, and representatives, including monitoring of training compliance.”
 ● All COI training including content, assignment and monitoring for completion is the responsibility of the Global Block Ethics team. 
 B) “Review of the remuneration practices of the business and ensuring it is operating efficiently, honestly, and fairly.”
 ● Remuneration is reviewed on a global Block level by the Block People team. Whilst the CO may be involved in the review of Remuneration in terms of product/service fees, in practice this would be a multi-layered collaboration between teams. 
 C) “The Square Australia Conflicts of Interest Policy is owned by the Chief Compliance Officer Australia and the DRI is Georgia Westbrook or their designees.”
 ● The roles and responsibilities of the DRI versus the owner of the policy is not documented. i.e., if the need arose for a COI disclosure to be established this would be the role of AU Legal Counsel. 
 

 3- GCMT identified references to documentation or supporting guidelines within the policy which are not currently in place or in operation. 
 A) “Square AU will evaluate and assess conflicts of interest in accordance with the qualitative risk analysis method set forth in Annexure D.”
 ● The current policy does not contain Annexure D or a qualitative risk analysis method. 
 

 4- GCMT identified monitoring processes and controls which are documented within the policy which require improvement to increase their design and operating effectiveness.
 A) The COI Policy states “The CO is responsible for ‘reviewing any customer files and records prior to the Board Meeting to identify any complaints that may indicate the existence of a conflict of interest that has not been previously considered by the Board’. The CO will monitor compliance through ‘a review of Complaints reporting to identify whether any disputes are a result of conflicts of interest between Square AU (including its representatives) and customers’.” 
 ● The review of complaints is currently being conducted by the Compliance Officer through the monthly reporting issued by Square AU Complaints and where required random ad-hoc sampling. 
 ● Monthly Complaint reporting does not currently allow for identification and reporting of complaints which are related to Conflicts of Interest. To enhance the effectiveness of the control, new processes or flags should be established at the frontline by Square Complaints to allow escalation and visibility by the Compliance Officer.
 B) The COI Policy States “Square AU must keep, for at least 7 years, records of conflicts identified, and actions taken, and any reports given to the Board, Square AU’s shareholders or senior management about conflicts of interest matters.”
 ● Per the GAP analysis, the responsibility for employee disclosed COI is held with the Global Block Ethics team. There are no agreed processes or reporting which have been agreed between the Square CO and the Global Block Ethics team to ensure adequate record retention.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: Issue rating of medium/moderate assigned based on rating methodology

Closure Request Justification:
Please note the conflicts of intrest polocy has been fully reviewed and updated to reflect currrent practices. See attached responses as evidence of the works to update the program.

Issue Title: Lending does not apply any adverse actions when a customer’ s IDV status is revoked [Cash App Lending, SUP and Retro - US]
Issue Description:
Requirement: Per the Identity Verification Program | Cash App | US, if red flags are identified after onboarding and verification, the account should be appropriately actioned with the corresponding adverse action.
 Testing Results: Lending currently does not apply any adverse actions when a customers’ IDV status is revoked. The revoked status is used when there are identified issues with a customers’ IDV status. This will transition the IDV status back to an unverified state (GREEN); this is necessary to ensure customers complete IDV and have limited access to products they shouldn't be eligible for while there is a discrepancy in their IDV status. Lending should apply adverse actions to the “IDV Revoked” status or perform an analysis to support the decision to opt out.

Issue Title: There is no process in place to allow adequate and timely handling of Lending ID theft cases [Cash App Lending, SUP and Retro - US]
Issue Description:
Requirement: Appendix A to 16 CFR 681—Interagency Guidelines on Identity Theft Detection, Prevention, and Mitigation. Section 681.1 of this part requires each financial institution and creditor that offers or maintains one or more covered accounts to develop and provide for the continued administration of a written Program to detect, prevent, and mitigate identity theft in connection with the opening of a covered account or any existing covered account.
 

 Testing Results: GCMT noted that while Cash ID theft Risk Operations investigate and respond to submissions of potential ID thefts, there was no further escalation established for any confirmed ID theft cases to be reviewed and handled by Lending Operations. 
 

 For the review period, GCMT performed analytics and identified 4% ( 3 out of 85) of the unique confirmed ID thefts were Cash Lending customers which should have been escalated. 
 

 Cash Lending needs to establish the escalation path and operating procedures to allow continued administration of its ID theft program in response to 16 CFR 681. The procedures should also be reviewed by Legal/Product Counsel to ensure the handling process meets the requirements of any other applicable Lending and Consumer Protection rules (i.e. FCRA, FACTA), as well as the pending Cash Lending Red Flag Policy.

Issue Title: REF 2 - Significant Purge Rates for calls [Square UK]
Issue Description:
Finding Description
 In total, GCMT assessed 175 interactions (calls and emails) for SAM between Jan 1st to 31, December 2023 with an effectiveness rate of 100% in accordance with the FCA Principles for fair communication to Sellers.
 

 However, GCMT identified that 77% of Sales calls and 34.1% of Account Management calls were purged by the automated call recording mechanism for the reason that an Account Executive and Account Manager did not provide call recording language to Sellers in accordance with the SAM Call Recording Language Guide. 
 

 As GCMT was restricted to assessing calls that were not purged due to non compliance, it is arguable that a window into broader sales practices is being lost and if not corrected can be detrimental to the oversight activities of Sales and Account Management practices for ensuring fair communications with Sellers..
 

 Separately, whilst GCMT did not test the control for purging calls, we identified;
 

 •12 Sales and 2 Account Management cases where data was not available to review on Gong or Salesforce. It is not clear if these are purged or not logged. 
 •2 Sales and 5 Account Management cases where call recording language was not used but the record is stored on Gong and Salesforce, which is not consistent with the automatic deletion of calls in the system when they are non compliant.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: Medium is appropriate for this finding as using call recording language on merchant calls is a regulatory requirement.
Issue Action Plans: Ref 2 - Reduce Purge Rates and Increase use of call recording language (1114952)
Closure Request Justification: The Call Recording Language Policy has been updated to ensure appropriate accountability measures are addressed. For SAM, GCMT reviewed evidence from SAM which shows the percentage of calls which have been purged in the last few months have decreased. UK Account Management have regular check ins via 121s to address high purge rates. Purge rates are also discussed in UK Sales teams to ensure appropriate accountability. GCMT reviewed the enablement training to confirm call recording language is covered.

Issue Title: The SSN max limit control design is limited to block new accounts created by customers who already have 5 active accounts [Cash App US]
Issue Description:
Requirement
 When an existing CashApp customer attempts to IDV a new account after already having 5 active verified accounts, CashApp automatically denylists the newly created account by verifying the SSN already has 5 active verified accounts.
 

 Test Results:
 For 59 out of 60 samples reviewed, an SSN that was already linked to 5 active, verified accounts was used during IDV, and the customers were consequently able to continue transacting on Cash App using an unverified Cash App account. This resulted in:  
 

 - 59 new accounts created which were not denylisted due to the control design.  These new accounts are not verified as they fall into the bracket of ‘green accounts’ i.e they do not need to be verified below a lifetime transaction limit of $1,500. Management advised that the control is designed to denylist the SSN and account after identity verification is completed and passed. If the customer is not successfully verified, the control does not denylist the account, and the customer is allowed to transact on the unverified account. The control determined 36 of the 59 new accounts were using an SSN that exceeded the max account rule and as a result the SSN was denylisted. 
 

 - For 1 new account that successfully passed IDV, it was not denylisted. The control failed to identify certain accounts already connected to the SSN. 
 

 GCMT observed that 55% (33 of 60) of the accounts identified as exceptions were active on Cash App during the review time period.
 

 There are limitations in the design of the control due to the fact that not all transaction types have been integrated into the control. For example, an account can have an active Cash App Card, however those transactions would not be included in the definition of “active” for the accounts. In addition, the control verifies active accounts for individuals by reviewing the 30 day period prior to sign up. Customers can circumvent the control by not performing activity for 30 days on an existing account which prevents the system from qualifying the account connected to the SSN as "active", thereby allowing the individual to create more than 5 accounts on Cash App.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: The root cause of the deficiencies identified are under investigation by the business unit.
Issue Rating Justification: The deficiencies identified during the review have moderate risk impact to the business unit and requires an action plan to correct. There are no explicit regulatory requirements tied to the control.
Issue Action Plans: The SSN max limit system control is limited in its design to block new accounts created by customers who already have 5 active accounts. (1116262)
Closure Request Justification: Refer to the related Action plan record ID 1116262 for closure details.

Issue Title: The control to detect and block Emulator devices is limited in its design due to data delays. [Cash App - US]
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 When the customer attempts to create an account or log into an existing account, Device-Integrity triggers DEVICE_ATTESTATION via Apple or Google Servers. If Apple or Google indicate the device is likely to be an emulator, that account creation or login attempt is blocked.
 

 Testing Results:
 For IOS devices, there is no control in place due to delays in receiving attestation data. 
 

 For 18 out of 60 sign up attempts reviewed, the device used to create an account was an Emulator. The summary is as follows:
 

 - Data confirming the use of an Emulator was not processed in time prior to account sign up for two (2) accounts. 
 - Nine (9) accounts were created on a version of Cash App that were not fully integrated into the control. 
 - For seven (7) accounts, data confirming the use of an Emulator was received after sign up. The system control did not take action to block the accounts upon receipt of the data. 
 

 61% (11 of 18) of the exceptions were active accounts.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: The root cause of this issue stems from data delays and customers using versions of cash app that is not integrated into the control.
Issue Rating Justification: The deficiencies identified present moderate risk impact to the business unit, and action plans are required to correct. There is no explicit regulatory requirement tied to this control.
Issue Action Plans: The control to detect and block Emulator devices is limited in its design due to data delays.  (1116265)
Closure Request Justification: Refer to action ID 1116265 for validation details.

Issue Title: Product documentation needs to be uplifted to align with the design of the controls implemented. [Cash App - US]
Issue Description:
Testing result:
 Cash App Product Health teams created and maintains multiple product and user experience documents for the recently implemented confidence controls. Upon review as part of testing the post implementation controls in Phase two it was noted that documentation is inconsistent and/or outdated. 
 

 Examples include, but are not limited to:
 - Control objective 10 - 5 active accounts per SSN: The PRD (Lowering Max # of Active Accounts with Same SSN from 10 to 5) does not align with the current design of the control. The PRD states the control objective is to limit an SSN to 5 active accounts. According to management, the control objective is to limit an SSN to 5 active verified accounts; 
 

 - Confidence Item 10 - 5 active accounts per SSN: Lowering Max # of Active Accounts with Same SSN from 10 to 5 PRD states “denylist accounts at dIVD with 10+ monthly active accounts”, while the IDV Max # of Active Accounts per SSN Proposal states “denylist accounts at dIVD that have > 4 active accounts”; and  
 

 - Lowering Max # of Active Accounts with Same SSN from 10 to 5 PRD states the strict active account definition (ML Definition) is “any money movement” while the IDV Max # of Active Accounts per SSN Proposal states “any attempted money movement” is considered active. 
 

 - Confidence Item 14 - Device denylist: 2023-05-24: Device Sign up Denylist PRD states a device will be denylisted for 270 days for a SAR level 1 of 2 while the Post Implementation Tracker states a device will be denylisted indefinitely for SAR 1 and 9 months for SAR 2, and the 2023.06 CRD-Health Confidence-Platform Restrictions document states the device is blocked for 6 months. 
 

 - Confidence Item 10 - 5 active accounts per SSN: Lowering Max # of Active Accounts with Same SSN from 10 to 5 PRD dated May 22, 2023 is missing approvals and incomplete. 
 

 - Confidence Item 14 - Device denylist: 2023-05-04 Device Sign Up Denylist PRD dated May 24, 2023 is missing approvals and incomplete. 
 

 - Confidence Item 16 - Email denylist, and Confidence Item 17 - Phone number denylist: Platform Restrictions: Alias Denylist & Account Limit PRD dated May 4, 2023 states the status is still in-review, and approvals are missing.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Root cause is People - Multiple product documents were created but not kept current.
Issue Rating Justification: Outdated product documentation for implemented controls has low risk impact to the business. There are no explicit regulatory requirements tied to this finding.
Issue Action Plans: Product documentation needs to be uplifted to align with the design of controls implemented. (1116271)
Closure Request Justification: Refer to the related action record for closure details.

Issue Title: Accounts can be created with VOIP phone numbers when data is not received or received late from the vendor.
Issue Description:
Requirements:
 When the customer attempts to create an account using a phone number alias, Mir (Health ML Risk Pipeline System) evaluates the line type by sending the information to a third party (Ekata). If the line type returned is VOIP, Mir instructs Franklin to block the account creation and notify the customer to use a non-VOIP line type.
 

 Test Results:
 GCMT reviewed 131 sign up attempts, and the following observations were made: 
 - For 60 accounts, data was missing from Ekata to determine whether the phone number used to sign up for Cash App is a VOIP line. 
 - For 56 accounts, the phone number was not VOIP at the time of sign up but was later ported to voip. 
 - For 11 accounts, the data was missing but later retrieved. At onboarding, VOIP data is sometimes not evaluated in real time and the business has advised that generally accounts will not be verified against Ekata 0.05% of the time. 
 

 GCMT noted that of the sample population at the time of the test, 24% (31 of 131) of the exceptions were active accounts. 
 

 

 Additionally, two (2) VOIP phone numbers were linked to accounts post sign up. 
 There is no control to identify and block voip numbers on accounts, should they be linked to the account post sign up or if a phone number is ported to a voip line at any stage of the account lifecycle.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Root cause stems from Process. The control is limited in its design when there are data delays.
Issue Rating Justification: This issue has moderate impact to the business unit; however, there is no explicit regulatory requirement tied to this control.
Closure Request Justification:
Management has accepted and agreed to the finding and will accept the risk. Please refer to management's response and rationale below:
 

 Upon review of the exceptions, Management confirmed that Ekata did not provide data for the samples at the time of risk evaluation when the account was opened. Management has measured the frequency of this and found that it happens at a rate of roughly ~6bps. 
 

 Further, Management validated that the provided phone numbers were not VOIP at the time of risk evaluation. The phone numbers in question were either previously VOIP or ported to VOIP at a later date.
 

 Of the samples, Management confirmed that two of the accounts linked a VOIP phone number after onboarding; while this is a known enhancement which Management plans to address, there has been no assertion in the control description that this control is currently in place. 
 

 Finally, 2 of the provided examples were instances where the call to the vendor failed. Management has measured that this happens at a rate of 0.5bps.

Issue Title: Denylisted financial instruments are not blocked from linking to accounts already previously linked to.
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 Based on certain criteria associated with a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR), a subsequent SSN and account denylist ensues, along with a denylist adversity to the linked financial instrument to prevent future linking to new accounts. 
 

 Testing Results:
 31 out of 60 denylisted financial instruments reviewed were allowed to re-link to a previously linked to account by design of the control. Relinking of accounts to denylisted instruments is permitted so that customers may cash-out funds. Post re-linking of the denylisted instrument, accounts performed P2P payments, cash card and BTC transactions thereby potentially allowing bad activity to continue to occur. 
 

 51% (16 of 31) of the accounts were denylisted subsequent to re-linking to the financial instrument due to various Compliance reasons identified through a separate review process.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Root cause is Process related. The control is limited in its design to block linking to Cash App accounts previously linked to denylisted financial instruments.
Issue Rating Justification: The deficiencies identified as moderate impact to the business unit; however, there are no explicit regulatory requirements tied to the control.
Closure Request Justification:
Management has accepted and agreed upon the finding and will accept the risk. Please refer to management's response and rationale below: 
 

 Management is accepting the risk associated with accounts being able to re-link a financial instrument if that instrument has already been linked to the account. This control is designed in such a way that: (1) only allows customers to re-link the instrument to an account it was already linked to that same account, not new accounts; (2) avoids scenarios where customers may not be able to access their funds (allowing customers to withdraw); and (3) provides ability for customers who may have been impacted by fraud to continue use of their financial instrument. 
 

 In support of this risk acceptance, Management further recognizes other efforts that are underway to mitigate risks associated with alias linking. For example, the “Big Sweep” is a project Compliance is managing that will effectively lookback and apply denylists to both assets (e.g., financial instruments) and accounts that otherwise would have been denylisted subject to Platform Restrictions that were launched in Q3 2023.

Issue Title: Denylisted phone numbers can be linked to new accounts with the assistance of Cash App customer support agents.
Issue Description:
Requirement:  
 When an account is SAR'd and denylisted by Compliance, Guardrails applies a denylist adversity to the linked phone number that prevents the phone number from being linked to another account for 9 months, when a SAR level 1 or 2 is filed.
 

 Testing Results: 3 out of 60 denylisted phone numbers successfully linked to a new account after a SAR 1 or SAR 2 was filed with the assistance of a customer support agent.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Root cause is People related. Cash App customer support agents failed to follow established procedures.
Issue Rating Justification: The issue has moderate risk impact to the business; however, there are no explicit regulatory requirements tied to the control.
Issue Action Plans: Denylisted phone numbers can be linked to new accounts with the assistance of Cash App customer support agents. (1116274)
Closure Request Justification: Management updated the knowledge article for Cash App agents to review as a refresher of the process. The link is at: https://my.sqprod.co/news/cco_workflow_updates/workflow-update-asset-denylist-appeals-updates-and-clarifications-live-5-6 (access is restricted). The action plan has been completed. The issue is remediated.

Issue Title: Ref 1 - There is no Social Media Guidance for Account Executives and Account Managers
Issue Description:
GCMT conducted testing on Sales and Account Management process and documentation. GCMT identified that there was no guidance for Account Executives and Account Managers to present information to a Seller in a fair and balanced way through various communication channels.
 

 As an example, GCMT identified Account Executives who owned social media accounts for the purposes of contacting potential Sellers; however as there is no guidance for communication in social channels, there is a risk that communication may not be fair and clear.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: It is a regulatory requirement to ensure there is guidance for individuals in customer facing roles to present information in a clear, balanced and non misleading way.
Issue Action Plans: Ref 1 - Document strategy for representatives to use social media (1116279)
Closure Request Justification: GCMT reviewed the Remediation Evidence and noted this is adequate to close the MAP. The evidence is stored in the 'Remediation' Evidence folder. The SAM team presented documentation whichs presents the research performed for the use of social media within the sales team. attached the research. A guidance document has been drafted by the Head of Sales, UK for sales executives. The guidance details th 'fair clear and not misleading' rule and other applicable regulatory obligations. It will be uploaded to Highspot and shared with new hires during Onboarding.

Issue Title: Ref 3 - There is no control to review onboarding training for SAM members on a regular basis.
Issue Description: GCMT note that whilst Sales and Account Management have an established Looker Dashboard which is linked to the SAM Growth Academy, the current Dashboard is used manually on an ad-hoc basis to verify if a member of the SAM Team has completed the required onboarding and ongoing training is assigned. Therefore, onboarding training completion rates for SAM are not consistently monitored.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: Training is monitored on an ad-hoc basis therefore this issue is marked as low.
Issue Action Plans: Ref 3 - To create a control for review of onboarding training for SAM members on a regular basis. (1116922)

Issue Title: Ref 4 - No control in place for manually entered rates by Sales/AM into CPT [Square UK]
Issue Description:
GCMT noted that there is no control in place to review manually entered rates by an AE/AM to ensure they are accurate, as agreed with the seller and that they are completed in an appropriate time frame.
 

 GCMT confirmed with Finance and Strategy - Deal Desk that 11 refund requests were raised during 2023. This occurs when for example when an AE or AM applies the rate to the merchant's account late or if a merchant sets up a new account and the rate isn't applied to the new account in a timely manner.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: This is rated as a low and no further actions will be completed on this as there were marginal errors in place as a result of the CPT.
Issue Progress Updates: This issue will be Risk Accepted by the SAM team, therefore an Action Item is not due.
Issue Action Plans: Ref 4 - This issue will be Risk Accepted by the SAM team. (1116925)
Closure Request Justification:
The risk will be accepted by the UK SAM team and no further actions will be completed on this.
 

 In 2023 UK SAM had only 11 errors (less than 1 a month) for 10 sellers across the entirety of the team, which is marginal. Should a refund request due to an incorrect rate be raised, the SAM team will proceed with the existing protocol and liaise with F&S to apply the correct one and proceed to a refund of fees if applicable.

Issue Title: Ref 5 - QA documentation enhancement
Issue Description:
GCMT identified that the SAM QA UK Consumer Duty Playbook does not include: 
 Members of the Strategic team for monitoring 
 

 In addition, the EU/UK Consumer Protection Rubric and SAM QA UK Consumer Duty Playbook does not include:
 Grading criteria and /or guidance for assessing the AE/AM for compliance with fair, clear and not misleading customers in their communication with Sellers.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: This is marked as a low as minor enhancements are needed to update the QA guidelines and procedures for SAM.
Issue Action Plans: Ref 5 - SAM QA Enhancement (1116928)
Closure Request Justification:
The Rubric includes a question to cater for "Grading criteria and /or guidance for assessing the AE/AM for compliance with fair, clear and not misleading customers in their communication with Sellers". This Rubric is also available in the Playbook. They have yet to update the Google form as these changes to go live on June 1st, the BPO team already knows about this upcoming change.
 

 The other actions required asked to update current workflow to include Strategy Representatives and enablement BPO Representatives to distinguish between genuine vulnerability and vulnerability arising from operational issues. These changes to the workflow and Playbook came into effect on May 1st. They have already reported out on April's numbers regarding the the "distinction between genuine vulnerability and vulnerability arising from operational issues”. These insights have been shared with second line monitoring.

Issue Title: Denylisted email addresses that are not normalized are not blocked from linking to an account. [Cash App US]
Issue Description:
Requirement: 
 Based on certain criteria associated with a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR), a subsequent SSN and account denylist ensues, along with a denylist adversity to the linked email address to prevent future linking to new accounts.
 

 Testing Results: 
 For 45 out of 60 samples reviewed, denylisted emails were not blocked from linking to another account. Forty-two (42) email addresses were a permutation of the same email address. Forty-five (45) email addresses reviewed contained domains that were not normalized. Email normalization is only applied to the following; "gmail.com", "googlemail.com", "hotmail.com", "live.com", and "outlook.com".
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: The root cause is Process related. The control is limited in its design to linking to Cash App accounts if the email address is not normalized within the control.
Issue Rating Justification: The deficiencies identified present moderate risk impact to the business unit; however, there are no explicit regulatory requirements tied to the control.
Issue Action Plans: Denylisted email addresses that are not normalized are not blocked from linking to an account. (1116933)
Closure Request Justification: 10/17/2024: GCMT performed validation testing confirming remediation has been completed as stated in the management action plan. Refer to the attached documents for validation details.

Issue Title: SIL/SEL - AML/CTF/FS Training
Issue Description:
Role specific and tailored AML/CTF/FS training has not been provided to teams such as CS; Sales, and AM. Firms should provide AML/CFT training which is specific to the role carried out
 by the member of staff.  Role specific and tailored AML/CTF/FS training for CS, Sales and AM teams is to be created and delivered in 2024 by the local SIL and SEL AML Compliance Team. Plan is to identify gaps in Q1 and to create the training in Q2 this would be rolled out as part of the Q3 AML/CTF training to teams. 
 Content creation already started.
RCA Description: Existing AML training was generic in content and needs to be enhanced to include role specific AML examples
Impacted Legal Entities:
Squareup Europe Ltd.
 Squareup International Ltd.
Issue Rating Justification: Rating is based on the fact that existing controls are in place, this risk will enhance the current control. Not breaching any AML law/guidance
Issue Action Plans: Prepare revised training deck and roll out same to SIL/SEL. (1208030)
