Issue Title: Individual Accountability Framework (IAF)
Issue Description:
Implementation of new CBI regulations. CBI regulations and guidance was due to be published in Sept with an effective date of 29 December 2023. However, they have not yet been released. If released at this point, with an unmoved effective date, there is a real risk of not implementing within the timeframe. To address issues identified, we need to complete the following:  
 1) Map all requirements and identify gaps
 2) CF and PCF initial training on IAF
 3) Further CF training on IAF
 4) CF and PCF notification 
 5) Conduct Standard Policy
 6) Update F&P policy
 7) Create Conduct Standard Procedure
 8) Update F&P procedure
 9) Develop other IAF controls
RCA Description: New regulations
Issue Rating Justification: Rated as moderate based on the fact that if the CBI stick to its original implementation date and we are not prepared we could potentially be in breach.
Issue Action Plans: Map requirements & Draft/issue training. (1218575)
Closure Request Justification: Closed based on action plan

Issue Title: Steps to Collect and Verify Source of Wealth and Source of Funds Information
Issue Description:
AAPL’s procedures do not include sufficient steps for collecting, verifying, and assessing source of
 wealth or source of funds (SOW/SOF) information from consumers aligned with AUSTRAC regulatory
 requirements and guidance, and such information is not being collected or verified in practice. Per the
 Rules, Chapter 15, 15.10(1)-(2) and Chapter 4, 4.13(3), reporting entities must apply reasonable
 measures to collect and verify a customer’s SOW/SOF.
 

 AUSTRAC’s guidance includes practical procedures to collect SOW/SOF information and supporting
 documentation from the customer or from available secondary sources and databases.
 Currently, the ANZ Enhanced Consumer Due Diligence Instruction states that the debit/credit card
 linked to the customer’s account is deemed the SOW/SOF. AAPL’s procedures do not include steps to
 establish and assess SOW/SOF that are described in AUSTRAC’s guidance (e.g., requesting
 information / evidence of income history / assets).
 

 Management explained that the business is comfortable treating linked payment instruments as the
 SOW/SOF given that consumers (i) generally operate with low transaction limits in place and (ii) can
 link payment instruments issued by Australian regulated financial institutions only. These factors are
 consistent with the conclusions noted in AAPL’s latest ML / TF risk assessment, which states that SOW
 is generally not an essential factor for Afterpay to consider in establishing a customer’s risk profile.
 However, IA has interpreted AUSTRAC’s guidance, which states explicitly that reporting entities cannot
 assume that no further action is needed when customer funds are from a bank account, as requiring
 additional procedures to validate SOW/SOF (though such procedures may be risk-based).
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Requirement & Regulatory Guidance
Issue Action Plans:
Update ANZ Enhanced Consumer Due Diligence Instruction (1112414)
 Remediate Impacted High Risk Customers (1112416)

Issue Title: Employee Due Diligence Operating Effectiveness Exceptions
Issue Description:
In one instance where an employee was transferred to a high-risk role (which was the only occasion this occurred in the review period), AAPL did not perform employee due diligence in accordance with its ANZ Employee Due Diligence Standard, section 4.2.
 

 The ANZ Employee Due Diligence Standard is maintained by the Compliance team, and Talent
 Acquisition is responsible for the execution of employee due diligence in accordance with the Standard. Management explained that transfers to high risk roles occur infrequently, and that a procedural update implemented only one month prior to the transfer sample contributed to the Talent Acquisition team’s error.
Issue Rating Justification: Policies & Procedures
Issue Action Plans:
Remediation of Employee Due Diligence Checks (1112430)
 Implementation of Employee Due Diligence Monitoring Control (1112432)
 Update ANZ Employee Due Diligence Standard (1112434)
 Employee Due Diligence Targeted Training (1112436)

Issue Title: Enhanced Customer Due Diligence Operating Effectiveness Exceptions
Issue Description:
IA reviewed samples of (i) 17 compliance cases related to high-risk customers / merchants and (ii) 25 compliance cases related to PEPs. In 2 of the high-risk customer / merchant cases and 3 of the PEP cases, AAPL did not apply certain manual review steps in accordance with its ANZ Enhanced Customer Due Diligence procedures as follows:
 

 1. SMAP and / or OCDD reviews were not performed when a merchant was high risk due to an
 adverse media hit (one merchant); and
 

 2. Approval details were not documented completely on the SMAP (five customers).
 

 The Compliance and Financial Crime teams confirmed the steps noted above had not been applied
 and / or documented as a result of human error.
Issue Rating Justification: Policies & Procedures
Issue Action Plans:
Remediation of Enhanced Customer Due Diligence Samples (1112438)
 Consideration of Regular Reconciliation of High Risk Customer Registers (1112440)

Issue Title: KYC / Part B Program Documentation
Issue Description:
Section 2.6 of AAPL’s AML / CTF Program Part B notes that the business supports customer identity verification from documentation (in accordance with the AML / CTF Rules, Part 4.9); however, the specific standards / thresholds in place to enable this method (e.g., list of acceptable documents, procedures to identify forgeries or stolen IDs) have not been clearly defined or documented.
 

 Management confirmed that AAPL does not perform pure document-based identity verification in
 practice, and that all customer onboarding scenarios require validating KYC information against
 electronic data sources. Accordingly, IA was unable to test any document-based verification samples
 during our fieldwork.
Issue Rating Justification: Policies & Procedures
Issue Action Plans: Update AAPL AML/CTF Program Part B (1112442)

Issue Title: Transaction Monitoring Program Documentation
Issue Description:
AAPL’s requirements for manual monitoring have not been documented sufficiently and / or accurately in the AML/CTF program and related procedures, specifically:
 

 1. Procedures relating to the manual monitoring reports referred to in the AML/CTF program Part A, section 7.1.2, are not documented or established in practice. Currently, section 7.1.2 of AAPL’s AML/CTF Program Part A states that reports are generated and disseminated to staff or review.
 

 Management explained that section 7.1.2 is not up to date, and manual monitoring is supported by a system-driven workflow in which Unusual Activity Reports (UARs) are submitted by staff and investigated by the AML team.
 

 2. Procedures established for monitoring of customers on the “watched” list managed by the AML team (e.g., customers subject to an Unusual Activity Report) are not documented (though they are carried out in practice).
Issue Rating Justification: Policies & Procedures
Issue Action Plans:
Update Section 7.1.2 of AAPL AML/CTF Program Part A (1112444)
 Update SOPs and Instructions to Accurately Reflect Watched Lists (1112446)

Issue Title: Merchant Verification Procedures
Issue Description:
While merchants are not considered formal AAPL customers subject to the AML / CTF Program, the
 service AAPL provides to merchants exposes the company to certain ML / TF risks. Among the controls in place to mitigate the risk posed by merchants is the entity verification standard defined in AAPL’s Merchant Vetting Instruction:
 ● Sole Traders - AAPL collects the Sole Trader’s Name, Date of Birth, Residential Address, and ABN. The entity type and ABN provided by the merchant are matched to the corresponding data reflected in the Australian Business Register’s database. The merchant’s personal information is subject to electronic verification procedures via the Green ID or DVS platforms.
 ● Partnerships - AAPL collects the Partnership’s Name and ABN; the entity type and ABN provided by the merchant are matched to the corresponding data reflected in the Australian Business Register’s database. AAPL also collects the Name, Date of Birth, and Residential Address of one “Controller” / Beneficial Owner, which are subject to electronic verification procedures via the Green ID or DVS platforms.
 ● Private Companies - AAPL collects the Private Company’s Name and ABN; the entity type and ABN provided by the merchant are matched to the corresponding data reflected in the Australian Business Register’s database. AAPL also collects the Name, Date of Birth, and Residential Address of one “Controller” / Beneficial Owner, which are subject to electronic verification procedures via the Green ID or DVS platforms.
 ● Trusts - AAPL collects the Trust’s Name and ABN; the entity type and ABN provided by the merchant are matched to the corresponding data reflected in the Australian Business Register’s database. AAPL also collects the Name, Date of Birth, and Residential Address of one “Controller” / Beneficial Owner, which are subject to electronic verification procedures via the Green ID or DVS platforms.
 ● Public Companies - AAPL collects the Public Company’s Name and ABN; the entity type and ABN provided by the merchant are matched to the corresponding data reflected in the Australian Business Register’s database. AAPL also collects the Name, Date of Birth, and Residential Address of one “Controller” / Beneficial Owner, which are subject to electronic verification procedures via the Green ID or DVS platforms.
 

 IA noted the following gaps in AAPL’s Merchant Vetting Instruction:
 

 Controller Definition - The Section 10 Appendix of the Merchant Vetting Instruction defines the types of individuals who can represent the merchant’s Controller. For the Private Company, Public Company, and Trust entity types, the list of potential Controllers includes the merchant’s external accountant, which is a role lacking a sufficient level of management control / influence.
 

 Verifying Controller Relationships - The Merchant Vetting Instruction does not include procedures to validate the nature of the merchant’s relationship to the nominated Controller (where applicable).
Issue Rating Justification: Policies & Procedures
Issue Action Plans:
Review Merchant Vetting Instruction (1112448)
 Design and Implementation of Merchant Relationship/Controller Procedures (1112450)
 Review and Update Merchant Onboarding Portal (1112452)

Issue Title: Lack of Adequate Procedures to Assess Source of Wealth
Issue Description:
Afterpay US’s procedures do not include sufficient steps for collecting, verifying, and assessing source of wealth (SOW) information from consumers aligned with regulatory guidance. The Enhanced Customer Due Diligence (ECDD) Instruction (an internal procedural document) states that Afterpay must carry out ECDD procedures when a suspicion arises in relation to the Source of Funds (SOF) or SOW of the consumer; however, the document specifies that for Afterpay’s purposes,
 the SOF / SOW is considered the consumer’s linked debit or credit card (i.e., the payment instrument used to repay loans). This definition of SOW is not aligned with applicable regulatory guidance from the Financial Action Task Force (FATF), which defines (i) SOW as the origin of the customer’s entire body of wealth / assets and (ii) SOF as the origin of the particular funds which are the subject of the business relationship.
Engagement Title: 2023 Afterpay US (Pay Monthly & Afterpay Plus Card) Audit (1020666)
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation
Issue Action Plans: Enhance SOF/SOW Collection Process and Enhanced Consumer Due Diligence Instruction (1112482)

Issue Title: Lack of Comprehensive Customer Risk Rating Methodology
Issue Description: Afterpay US has not implemented a Customer Risk Rating (CRR) model / methodology to comprehensively risk-rate its full population of customers based on attributes such as geographic location, PEP status, employment status, transactional activity, and product usage. Currently, all Afterpay US customers are considered Low risk at onboarding by default; higher risk ratings are assigned only as a result of manual due diligence reviews triggered by Transaction Monitoring or Sanctions / PEP alerts. As the broader suite of AML program controls is risk-based in that higher-risk customers are subject to more frequent and robust monitoring, the existing approach to CRR increases the likelihood that consumers displaying certain higher-risk attributes are not being subject to the appropriate level of due diligence.
Engagement Title: 2023 Afterpay US (Pay Monthly & Afterpay Plus Card) Audit (1020666)
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Guidance
Issue Action Plans:
Develop Quantitative CRR Model to Generate Continuous Customer Risk Rating (1112478)
 CRR model scores - Backfill for Afterpay consumers (1112480)

Issue Title: Lack of Documented SLAs for Reviewing / Clearing Compliance Alerts
Issue Description:
Although Afterpay US tracks the time to clear all Transaction Monitoring and Sanctions alerts against defined SLAs (i.e., time allotted to perform and complete alert reviews), it has not specified these SLAs in its relevant procedural documents. In practice, the business monitors team performance against the following SLAs:
 ● Transaction Monitoring (Pay Monthly) - 10 days
 ● Transaction Monitoring (Plus Card) - 30 days
 ● Sanctions (All Consumers) - 2 days
 ● Enhanced Customer Due Diligence (All Consumers) - 3 days
Engagement Title: 2023 Afterpay US (Pay Monthly & Afterpay Plus Card) Audit (1020666)
Issue Rating Justification: Policies & Procedures
Issue Action Plans: Add SLA Adherence Information to Relevant Procedures (1112486)

Issue Title: Lack of Procedures for Investigating Employee Referrals
Issue Description: While Afterpay US has implemented a process to receive and investigate instances of potentially suspicious activity reported / referred by employees, the process is not described or documented formally within Afterpay’s AML policies and procedures. Per inquiry with the Afterpay US Compliance team, the process mirrors what is described in the Afterpay AU Manual Transaction Monitoring / Unusual Activity Report (UAR) Instruction; however, page 1 of this document states that it is applicable to Australia only.
Engagement Title: 2023 Afterpay US (Pay Monthly & Afterpay Plus Card) Audit (1020666)
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation
Issue Progress Updates: Action marked as complete at time of final report issuance (no due date identified). Due to a system limitation, the due date in Protecht matches the date of report issuance.
Issue Action Plans: Update Afterpay TM/UAR Instruction Procedure (1112484)

Issue Title: Lack of a Documented Procedures for OFAC License
Issue Description: Block has not documented a procedure related to the handling of OFAC licenses, which authorize the company to complete transactions previously blocked as Sanctions matches and return funds to impacted customers. Although Block processed two blocked transactions pursuant to OFAC licenses appropriately throughout the covered period, the company’s increasing engagement with product /service offerings that facilitate cross-border transactions (e.g., Bitcoin withdrawals) will likely result in larger numbers of blocked / rejected transactions and related OFAC licenses over time.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Guidance
Issue Progress Updates: Action marked as complete at time of final report issuance (no due date identified). Due to a system limitation, the due date in Protecht matches the date of report issuance.
Issue Action Plans: Development of Procedure Document for OFAC License (1112304)

Issue Title: Analysis for Changes in Inherent Risk Ratings
Issue Description:
To prepare the annual AML / CTF and Sanctions Risk Assessment, the Compliance team completes an Inherent Risk Questionnaire and Control Environment Effectiveness Questionnaire for each product on a spreadsheet. Management’s responses drive the calculation of risk-weighted Inherent Risk and Control Environment Effectiveness scores via automated formulas, which are mapped to a matrix defining Residual Risk levels (e.g., “Moderate” Inherent Risk and “Adequate” Control Environment Effectiveness yields “Moderate” Residual Risk).
 

 The risk assessment framework / methodology does not require Management to review the results of the automated inherent risk score calculations for reasonableness considering prevailing business conditions and expectations. As a result, the noted reductions in the inherent risk ratings for the Cash App Gold and Bitcoin products (from High to Moderate) were not assessed for reasonableness prior to finalizing the results.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation
Issue Progress Updates: Action marked as complete at time of final report issuance (no due date identified). Due to a system limitation, the due date in Protecht matches the date of report issuance.
Issue Action Plans:
Update AML/CTF & Sanctions Risk Assessment Program Document (1112306)
 Head of Compliance Approvals for Risk Rating Overrides (1112308)

Issue Title: Insufficient Alert / Case SLA Monitoring
Issue Description:
The Compliance team has not established appropriate alert SLA monitoring controls / procedures to ensure that resource gaps and related case backlogs will be closed / addressed timely.
 

 Across a sample of 75 compliance alerts tested by Internal Audit, 35 were not reviewed and closed by the relevant Compliance Operations teams within the 30-day SLA required per Block’s internal procedures (an exception rate of ~47%). Management’s performance reporting should enable the timely identification of such backlogs, and remedial procedures should allow for quick staffing adjustments as needed.
Issue Rating Justification: Policy & Procedures
Issue Progress Updates: Action marked as complete at time of final report issuance (no due date identified). Due to a system limitation, the due date in Protecht matches the date of report issuance.
Issue Action Plans: Development of Capacity Model to Address Resource Gaps (1112310)

Issue Title: Senior Management Approval of AML Program
Issue Description:
Section 18.2 of the Block, Inc. BSA / AML Program document states that the document is subject to review and approval by the Governance Lead, Compliance Lead, Global Chief Compliance Officer, and General Counsel on an annual basis at minimum. However, IA was unable to obtain evidence that such reviews were performed, and that the program was approved as required, throughout the Covered Period.
 

 Per inquiry with the Compliance team, Management is undertaking a review of the Compliance Policy framework in which the hierarchy / structure of all policy and program documents will be assessed. As such, Management prioritized its review / assessment of the Global AML / CTF Policy document over the Block, Inc. BSA / AML Program document.
Issue Rating Justification: Policy & Procedures
Issue Action Plans: Update BSA/AML Program with Management Approval (1112312)

Issue Title: Sponsor Due Diligence for Cash App for Teens /Family Accounts
Issue Description:
Block permits minors aged 13 - 17 to onboard with Cash App when invited by an adult sponsor. Sponsors are the legal owners of related Teen accounts, while the Teen customers are considered authorized users. Adult Cash App users can sponsor up to six minors on the platform. Minors who successfully onboard through a sponsor can access the P2P, Cash Card, Direct Deposit, Bitcoin, and Stock Investing products / features. 
 

 The Cash App Terms of Service state that minors must obtain consent from a parent or legal guardian to use a sponsored account. Although Block collects information from both the adult sponsors and teen users (i.e., Name, Date of Birth, and email / phone number) and verifies the sponsor’s identity, the company has not implemented due diligence procedures / controls designed to validate the nature of the relationship between the adult sponsor and teen user.
 

 Per inquiry with the Compliance team, prior to the issuance of this report, Management has identified this as a prioritized workstream and has been working to implement controls designed to substantiate the appropriateness of the relationship between a sponsor and its sponsored account(s).
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation
Issue Action Plans: Implementation of Controls for Sponsored Cash Accounts (1111676)

Issue Title: Unclear Compliance Requirements for New Product Launches
Issue Description:
The company’s AML program documentation does not clearly define (i) when pre-launch assessments of AML / CTF / Sanctions risks and controls need to be completed and (ii) how such pre-launch assessments should be documented. Both the AML / CTF Policy and AML Program document state only that BSA / AML and Sanctions risk assessments will be updated when (i) new products or services are launched or (ii) material changes are made to existing products; the documents do not provide definitions of “new product” or “material change”, nor do they specify how DRIs should evidence that all pertinent risk factors have been considered and addressed prior to launch.
 

 As a result, Internal Audit identified:
 ● One Cash App product reported by Compliance as new for which a formal risk assessment was not performed prior to launch (i.e., Instant Demand Deposit Accounts). Per inquiry with the Compliance team, Management determined that this product did not materially impact Cash App’s risk profile, but the analysis supporting this conclusion was not documented.
 ● One Square product reported by Compliance as new for which the pre-launch risk assessment was not documented completely across all relevant processes (i.e., Unlinked Refunds).
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Guidance
Issue Action Plans:
Update AML Program with Product Risk Assessment information (1111678)
 Update Product Launch Documentation (Square) (1112300)
 Update Product Launch Documentation (Cash) (1112302)

Issue Title: Third Party Bank Accounts Linked to Block Accounts
Issue Description:
Block allows bank accounts that are not in the Block customer’s name (i.e., third party bank accounts) to be linked to the customer’s Block account without having implemented any mechanisms to assess the risk profile of the third party account owner. Therefore, Block has no reasonable basis to conclude that such third parties (i) have a legitimate relationship with Block’s customer and (ii) do not substantially increase the risk of money laundering / terrorist financing. Furthermore, the most recent AML risk assessments for Cash App and Square Managed Payments do not consider the potential to link third party bank accounts. 
 

 Guidance from Blocks federal functional regulators (FinCEN / IRS) indicate that controls should be commensurate with the specific levels of risk faced by the business. Page 43 of FinCEN and IRS’s BSA/AML Examination Manual for MSBs states, MSBs are required per 31 CFR 103.125 to implement an effective anti-money laundering program that is reasonably designed to prevent the MSB from being used to facilitate money laundering and terrorist financing. The anti-money laundering program must be written and must be commensurate with the MSB's risk profile, and, per page 44: The level of sophistication of the internal controls should be commensurate with the size, structure, risk, and complexity of the MSB. Given the aforementioned lack of procedures in place to risk assess linked third party bank accounts, Internal Audit believes that Block’s AML program with respect to third parties is not commensurate with the risk profile of the business. As funds held in a customer’s Square Managed Payments or Cash App stored balance can be distributed to the bank account of an individual or entity who has not been subject to Block’s onboarding procedures, the linked third party may be considered the ultimate controller or beneficiary of the underlying Block account, which increases the susceptibility of Block’s services to illicit use.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation
Issue Action Plans:
Automated Verification Control - Customer is Beneficial Owner (1112492)
 System Improvement - Identifying Customer/Bank Relationship (1112494)
 Bank Account Controls - Customer/Bank Relationship (1112496)
Closure Request Justification: Closing this issue since both the actions have been closed and IA has completed validation. IA has shared a document to evidence the validation which is uploaded as an attachment to this issue titled "Square Audit Closure Memo [2022 AML Audit_Third Party Bank Account Linking].pdf"

Issue Title: Late Reporting of Blocked/Rejected Transactions
Issue Description:
Block’s Sanctions team and case monitoring controls are not sufficiently structured / designed to ensure that transactions blocked or rejected due to OFAC matches are reported within the 10 business day timeframe required by OFAC. Existing oversight mechanisms do not effectively track OFAC reporting activities to ensure blocked / rejected transactions are reported to OFAC by the Global Sanctions Team within applicable regulatory deadlines.
 

 As a result, Internal Audit identified:
 ● 68 rejected Cash App transactions reported to OFAC more than 10 business days after the rejection date;
 ● 453 rejected Square transactions reported to OFAC more than 10 business days after the rejection date; and
 ● 11 blocked Square transactions reported to OFAC more than 10 business days after the blocked date.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Requirement
Issue Action Plans: Global Sanctions Team Enhancements (1111674)

Issue Title: Incomplete Transaction Monitoring Coverage Assessment
Issue Description:
CAI Compliance has not established a process or mechanism to ensure that enterprise-wide compliance initiatives undertaken by CAI’s parent company (Block, Inc.) have considered relevant CAI-specific risks in the scoping phase. As a result, the vendor-assisted transaction monitoring (TM) coverage assessment performed during the covered period failed to capture all of the AML red flags / typologies applicable to broker-dealers listed in FINRA Regulatory Notice 19-18.
 

 The Block, Inc. Compliance team defined the scope of the TM coverage assessment project, which considered relevant AML regulatory guidance for the company’s international subsidiaries; the failure to include the FINRA guidance applicable to CAI / the Equities product was an oversight. Although CAI’s TM rule coverage was reviewed separately during the annual risk assessment process, the business should be included in similar, more comprehensive assessments going forward.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Guidance
Issue Action Plans:
Identification of Red Flags related to CAI Broker-Dealer Product Offerings (1112340)
 Establish Annual Reminders related to Coverage Assessment (1123613)

Issue Title: Lack of CAI / Equities-Specific Training to the CAI Board of Directors
Issue Description: CAI has not provided its Board of Directors with appropriate, equities-specific BSA / AML and Sanctions training. As the Board of Directors is responsible for overseeing CAI’s ongoing compliance with its BSA / AML obligations, it is expected to receive training tailored to CAI’s products and services.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation
Issue Progress Updates: Issue/action marked as complete at time of final report issuance (no due date identified). Due to a system limitation, the due date in Protecht matches the date of report issuance.
Issue Action Plans: CAI Specific Training to Board (1112344)

Issue Title: Inadequate Queue Performance Monitoring
Issue Description: CAI does not have sufficient controls in place to monitor the functionality and ongoing performance of queues / alerts in its transaction monitoring system, as the business failed to identify that two Enhanced Due Diligence queues did not generate any alerts between February 2022 and May 2023 until August 2023. Remediation of the failed queues resulted in a backlog of approximately 1,000 alerts requiring investigation. The queue failure can be attributed to an issue with the underlying code logic of an upstream data table, where the improper application of filters prevented the eligible queues from alerting.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Guidance
Issue Action Plans:
Development of Queue Trend Reporting (1112330)
 Documentation of Escalation Workflow (1112332)
 Remediate EDD Equities Backlog (1112334)
 Assessment of Current Data Quality Checks (1112336)
 Develop Queue Monitoring (1112338)

Issue Title: Sponsor Due Diligence – Families Investing Accounts
Issue Description:
CAI permits minors aged 13-17 to buy and sell securities through Cash App with approval from an adult “sponsor.” Sponsors are the legal owners of related Families accounts, while the “Teens” are
 considered authorized users. Adult Cash App users can sponsor up to six minors on the platform. CAI’s Terms of Service state that adult sponsors must be the parent or legal guardian of the teen user. Although CAI (i) collects and verifies information from the adult sponsors and (ii) collects certain information from the Teen users (e.g., Name, Date of Birth, and email/phone number), the company has not implemented due diligence procedures / controls designed to validate the nature of the relationship between the adult sponsor and teen user.
 

 Per inquiry with the Compliance team, prior to the issuance of this report, Management has identified this as a prioritized workstream, and has been working to implement controls designed to substantiate the appropriateness of the relationship between a sponsor and its sponsored account(s).
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation
Issue Action Plans: Implementation of Due Diligence Procedures (1112342)

Issue Title: Inadequate AML/CFT Program Governance
Issue Description:
Clearpay UK governance framework around the AML/CFT Program is not sufficiently robust to ensure (1) regulatory requirements, (2) regulatory expectations and (3) internal practices are duly documented reflecting current processes in place. Based on IA’s fieldwork and interviews conducted with Clearpay UK AML employees, all the processes referenced below within points 1 & 2 & 3 appear to be operating consistently with IA’s understanding of applicable regulatory requirements; however, IA was not able to validate them from a design perspective, as these processes and decisions were not formally or accurately documented.
 

 According to MLRs 19.1.c and 86, and JMLSG 1.29, all firms are required as a general obligation to establish and maintain a record in writing of adequate and appropriate policies, controls and procedures to mitigate and manage effectively the risks of money laundering and terrorist financing.
 

 The following shortcomings were identified during the review:
 

 1. Absence of documentation of some regulatory requirements in Clearpay UK's AML/CTF policy and procedures – Regulatory requirement 
 Clearpay UK did not have a mechanism to identify gaps in documentation within its AML/CFT Policy and procedures. IA identified that neither the policy or the related procedures include a formal documented description of the below practices established by Clearpay UK to mitigate and manage effectively the risks of money laundering and terrorist financing:
 1) Clearpay UK’s AML/CTF Policy did not include the steps it would take for assessing and reporting discrepancies identified in the beneficial owner information provided by the merchant, as required by MLR 30A(1) and guidance from JMLSG Part 1 Section 5.3.129A.
 2) Clearpay UK’s AML/CTF Policy and Merchant Onboarding SOP did not include the measures taken to determine and verify (a) law to which the merchant is subject to, (b) its constitution (whether set out in its articles of association or other governing documents), as required by MLR 28(3) and guidance from JMLSG Part 1 Section 5.3.152.
 3) Clearpay UK’s AML/CTF Policy did not include the steps to be followed to obtain consent from the NCA to continue business with the customers (i.e.both consumers and merchants) included in Suspicious Activity Reports (“SARs”) filled, as required by the local regulations.
 

 2. Absence of documentation for certain key AML/CFT risk-based decisions, processes, and controls – Regulatory Expectation
 Clearpay UK did not have adequate documented protocols or processes to meet the regulatory expectation of having formal documentation for critical risk-based decisions, processes and controls essential for ensuring proper governance of the AML/CFT program. During the review, IA identified that the following elements were not documented:
 1) Methodology applied to assess the AML risk associated with its consumers and merchants (i.e., a Customer Risk Rating Methodology).
 2) Reviews performed to assess the AML risk for merchants connected to higher-risk countries
 3) Service Level Agreements (SLAs) in place for the review of SARs cases, Sanctions and Transaction Monitoring alerts.
 4) Risk-based criteria for the request and validation of Source of Wealth/Source of Funds (“SOW/SOF”) at onboarding for customers identified as PEPs.
 5) Controls in place to prevent the risk of tipping off ongoing investigations to customers once a suspicious activity is under Nominated Officer (i.e. MLRO) investigation and/or a related SAR has been submitted to the National Crime Agency (“NCA”).
 6) Risk-based criteria substantiating the following Customer Due Diligence controls:
 i. PEP/Sanctions screening conducted to consumers after onboarding (at Day+1), and
 ii. Adverse Media Screening not conducted at onboarding for high risk consumers and
 only conducted once an Ongoing Customer Due Diligence (“OCDD”) is triggered.
 

 3. Insufficient periodic review of AML/CFT procedures – Regulatory Expectation
 Clearpay UK did not establish a systematic process for regularly reviewing AML/CFT procedures. This lack of regular oversight hinders the organization's ability to ensure that these procedures remain current, accurate, and in alignment with regulatory requirements, thereby impeding the development of a more effective and robust compliance framework.
 

 IA noted that certain key AML/CFT documents, including Customer Due Diligence (CDD), Enhanced Customer Due Diligence (ECDD), Ongoing Customer Due Diligence (OCDD), and Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) procedures, have not been subjected to review by the MLRO since March, June, August, or September 2022. Indeed, the most recent review was carried out by the former MLRO. 
 

 Furthermore, there is currently no established periodic review cadence specified in the existing procedures. IA also noted that due to the absence of periodical review and insufficient oversight of procedures, Clearpay UK MLRO had not identified the misalignment between the current ECDD procedure and the practices carried out by the AML/CFT team during the validation of high-risk consumers. The procedure mandates conducting adverse media screening for all newly identified high-risk consumers during onboarding, but the existing control applies this verification only during periodic consumer reviews. Clearpay UK confirmed the discrepancy between the procedure and intended control , indicating the necessity for the procedure to be updated.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Requirement
Issue Action Plans:
Update AML Program Documentation (1112454)
 Assessment of Risk-Based Decisions and Processes (1112460)
 Improved governance of AML/CTF Policy, Program and Procedure Documentation (1112466)

Issue Title: Periodic and Event Driven Reviews
Issue Description: Clearpay UK Onboarding/Compliance was asked to conduct ongoing reviews of existing merchants (Medium and Low risk) every five years starting from the initial onboarding completion date. This refresh cadence for Low and Medium risk merchants has been considered proportionate given the Low AML risk rating of Clearpay’s Merchant Services, and is in line with the recommendations issued by Clearpay’s AML Auditors during the 2021 review.
Issue Rating Justification: Best Practice
Issue Action Plans: Low/Medium Risk Cadence (1112476)

Issue Title: Misapplication of AML/CFT procedures
Issue Description:
Clearpay UK did not provide adequate oversight for specific internal procedures to ensure compliance with the documented internal requirements. During the review, IA identified the following shortcomings:
 

 ● Clearpay UK does not have an effective mechanism in place to ensure SARs are not submitted to the NCA without the formal MLRO approval, as required by the Clearpay UK’s SAR procedure. It was identified through our testing procedures that for 4 out of 5 SARs filled (all of them related to consumers), the MLRO participated in the SARs review process but evidence of the final approvals were not documented nor kept.
 ● Clearpay UK does not have a process in place to ensure AML/CFT Analysts follow a consistent approach in line with the ECDD procedure documentation requirements when validating high-risk customers. It was identified through our testing that 4 out of 8 high-risk customers subject to ECDD (2 consumers and 2 merchants), the Clearpay UK Analyst did not document notes summarizing the due diligence applied within its case management system (Satori), as required by the ECDD procedure.
Issue Rating Justification: Policies & Procedures
Issue Action Plans: MRLO Approval Mechanism for SARs (1112472)

Issue Title: Inadequate Training Assignment Controls
Issue Description: Square Canada has not implemented a monitoring / reconciliation control at the point of training assignment to ensure that all employees required to complete annual training are enrolled in the appropriate campaign. As a result, IA identified one Square Canada employee who was not enrolled in the AML training campaign issued during the covered period erroneously.
Issue Rating Justification: Policy & Procedures
Issue Action Plans: Training Enhancements (1112322)

Issue Title: Lack of Effective Identity Verification for Entity Customers
Issue Description:
Square Canada has not yet implemented a process to verify the identities of (i) customers that are legal entities or (ii) related beneficial owners in accordance with the requirements set forth in FINTRACs PCMLTFA Knowing your client guidance. Furthermore, Square Canada is not collecting details regarding the individuals with the authority to (i) sign contracts (i.e., power to bind) and (ii) order / initiate transactions (i.e., authorized to order) on behalf of its customers that are legal entities as required by FINTRACs PCMLTFA Record keeping requirements for money services businesses and foreign money services businesses.
 

 IA understands that Square Canada’s transition to a registered / regulated MSB entity occurred as the result of the Government making sudden and unexpected changes to AML / CTF legislation absent any advance notice. Management has prioritized efforts underway to enhance customer onboarding controls to comply with the additional regulatory requirements, and has been in communication with FINTRAC regarding the remediation timeline.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Requirement
Issue Action Plans: Identity Verification Enhancements (1112314)

Issue Title: Overly Generic Business Descriptions for Merchants
Issue Description:
Square Canada does not require its merchant customers to provide sufficiently detailed descriptions of their underlying business activity. Throughout the covered period, Square Canada merchants were
 permitted to onboard / transact by providing generic descriptions such as “Professional Services” or “Casual Individual Use” only across all applicable data fields. 
 

 According to FINTRAC’s “Record keeping requirements for money services businesses and foreign money services businesses” guidance: “When you are required to keep records about clients, you should be as descriptive as possible. Being descriptive when recording the nature of the principal business or occupation of a client will help determine whether a transaction or activity is consistent with what would be expected for that client. For example, when the client's occupation is "manager", the record should reflect the area of management, such as "hotel reservations manager" or "retail clothing store manager." When an entity's principal business area is "sales", the record should specify the type of sales, such as "pharmaceutical sales" or "retail sales”.”
 

 Per inquiry with the Square Canada CCO, the aforementioned FINTRAC guidance is typically applied to occupational activity descriptions maintained for individuals / consumers, although it was acknowledged that merchant customers are technically in scope. Furthermore, the CCO explained that Square Canada employs sophisticated machine learning models and accompanying monitoring queues to gauge whether a transaction or activity is consistent with what would be expected for any given client; this approach significantly reduces any dependency Square Canada would otherwise have on business descriptions collected directly from the client. However, IA recommends that Square Canada update its onboarding flow to require customers to provide a sufficiently detailed description of their business activity to meet the standard set forth in FINTRAC’s guidance.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Guidance
Issue Action Plans: Assessment of Controls for Customers with Generic Business Descriptions (1112324)

Issue Title: Inadequate Descriptions of the Purpose of Fundraising
Issue Description:
**ISSUE WAS RISK ACCEPTED AS OF 11/25/2024** See status updates for additional details.
 

 Square Canada does not require merchant customers using its platform to raise funds / seek donations to provide a sufficiently detailed description of the purpose for which funds are being raised. Throughout the covered period, Square Canada merchants were permitted to use the platform to raise donations for which the data fields used to describe the purpose were either blank or generic (e.g., “Donation”, “General Donation”).
 

 According to FINTRAC’s “Record keeping requirements for money services businesses and foreign money services businesses” guidance: “If you are an MSB or FMSB that provides crowdfunding platform services to persons or entities in Canada, you must...keep a record of the purpose for which the funds or virtual currency are being raised.”
 

 Per inquiry with the Square Canada CCO, customers using the e-commerce platform for fundraising purposes already accept / acknowledge terms and conditions stating that funds can not be raised on behalf of third parties or for personal reasons. Furthermore, Square Canada can reference supplemental merchant records to determine whether or not a given fundraising campaign aligns with a customer’s expected activity (e.g., the customer is a charity). However, IA recommends that Square Canada require customers using its service for fundraising to provide detailed descriptions of the underlying purpose to meet the standard set forth in FINTRAC’s guidance.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation
Issue Action Plans: Assessment of Controls for Customers with Generic Crowdfunding Descriptions (1112326)

Issue Title: Inadequate Documentation Supporting the Closure of PEP Alerts
Issue Description: Square Canada’s PEP investigation procedures do not require case analysts to provide sufficient evidence that an alert subject’s office departure date has been considered in the course of the investigation. FINTRAC’s guidance states that “Once you determine that a person is a family member of a domestic PEP or HIO, they remain a family member of a domestic PEP or HIO until five years after the domestic PEP or HIO has left office.” Square Canada’s PEP procedures do not require investigators to document the date the PEP left office when this rationale is applied to clear alerts.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Guidance
Issue Action Plans: Revision of PEP Procedures (1112328)

Issue Title: TM Program Governance - Absence of local oversight on key TM processes and tasks
Issue Description:
Absence of local oversight on key TM processes and tasks - SIL’s CCO/MLRO has not been involved in TM processes and tasks initiated at the Block, Inc. parent company level that may impact the SIL AML/CFT Program, such as changes to the TM rules or the Rules Coverage Assessment, to ensure the local AML regulatory requirements and pertinent risks are included and assessed:
 

 1. SIL CCO/MLRO approval was not sought when changes were made to the local TM rules
 SIL’s Model Change Management procedure requires changes to the TM rules to be reviewed and
 approved by the Square Head of Financial Crime, Square Compliance Operations Lead and the SIL
 CCO/MLRO. However, the SIL CCO/MLRO approval requirement defined in the procedure is not being
 followed in practice. IA reviewed a sample of 5 TM rule changes and noted that while all were approved
 by the Square Head of Financial Crime and Square Compliance Operations lead, none was approved
 by the CCO/MLRO, as required by procedure. There is not a clear process to communicate proposed
 changes to the SIL CCO/MLRO.
 2. SIL CCO/MLRO was not involved in the Rules Coverage Assessment
 A TM Rules Coverage Assessment for the two active SIL products (at the time) was completed in
 February 2023 by a third party consultant. The purpose of this assessment was to (i) identify key TM
 risk indicators prescribed by regulatory bodies providing oversight of Block’s various legal entities and
 (ii) map such indicators to each entity’s existing TM rules in production. For SIL, the consultant’s final
 report noted that approximately half of the risk indicators applicable to Square and Cash App products
 were not covered by an existing TM rule.
 IA noted that the assessment completed by the third party consultant was reviewed and approved by
 the Square Compliance Operations Lead and Head of Cash App Compliance; however, local SIL
 MLRO/CCO participation in the assessment was not required. SIL’s CCO informed IA that no actions to
 review and/or implement the risk indicator gaps identified as part of the assessment have been
 communicated to the local team. There is not a clear process to engage with local teams when
 conducting global assessments that may affect local entities. In line with industry practice and to
 ascertain the appropriateness of Rules Coverage Assessment, the assessment should be reviewed /
 approved by someone with local regulatory knowledge and authority, as for instance SIL CCO/MLRO,
 to ensure it is aligned with local risks and/or regulatory requirements.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation & Policies & Procedures
Issue Action Plans:
Implementation of New Change Management Procedure (1112346)
 Involvement of Leads during Coverage Assessment Development (1112348)

Issue Title: TM System Effectiveness - Alerts not reviewed due to issues in the Regulator system
Issue Description:
Alerts not reviewed due to issues in the Regulator system - SIL Compliance informed IA about two issues with the transaction monitoring alert management system (Regulator) that were identified in April 2023. These issues impacted five TM queues and resulted in (i) the delayed review / closure of 474 alerts (outside the defined SLA) and (ii) the delayed filing of 26 Suspicious Transaction Reports with the relevant authorities (outside the defined SLA). As of the date of this report, all backlogged alerts have been reviewed. 
 

 Issue #1: Changes in the product backend table not communicated to Compliance.
 - For two TM rules specific to Ireland, there were no alerts generated throughout March 2023.
 Per inquiry with SIL Compliance, the underlying models stopped running in the wake of a
 change implemented to a key backend data table by the Product team. A change in one query
 was not duly tested and the changeset to activate it was rejected resulting in no alerts
 generated.
 Issue #2: Incorrect coding of TM rules since inception.
 - For three TM rules specific to SIL (one rule each for Ireland, France and Spain), there were no
 alerts generated since the inception of the business in each market (i.e., June 2021 for Ireland
 and February 2022 for Spain and France). Per inquiry with SIL Compliance, the initial rule
 configuration was incorrectly designed, allocating incorrect country codes, resulting in alerts not
 presented in any of the systems (Snoop, Regulator, or Snowflake) that feed into the SQ
 Transaction Monitoring team’s Looker dashboards.
 - Internal Audit noted that issue #2 was not identified as part of the Annual Tuning and
 Optimization exercise that was completed in July 2022 due to the gaps in the underlying tuning
 and optimization methodology for the Square Managed Product. Management communicated
 to us that the issue was not identified because the thresholds included in the tuning and
 optimization annual exercise were not accurately defined and calibrated to the low customer
 base and low volume of alerts in the EU market.
 Internal Audit has obtained evidence that SIL addressed the TM rules code errors identified, updated
 codes were reviewed, tested and approved before being implemented and real-time monitoring controls
 are in place to identify and flag to the Square FinCrimes Data team any system related issues. In
 addition, Internal Audit obtained confirmation from SIL management that since the implementation of
 these controls, no issues related to coding or logic failures have been identified.
 Notwithstanding the above, Internal Audit considers that, although real-time data monitoring controls
 have been launched, Management should have had other processes/controls to identify these issues
 through standard compliance processes (e.g. Queue Tuning / Optimization, alert management KPI
 tracking / reporting, etc.). IA recommends that Management revisit how such processes have been
 designed and implement required enhancements such as lowering SIL’s thresholds adjusting it to EU
 the business activity, implementing new Key Performance Indicators (“KPIs”) and revisit the Tuning and
 Optimization Methodology to ensure TM rules in place remain accurate and complete. SIL
 management confirmed to IA that all these actions are currently work in progress and expected to be
 implemented and published before the end of 2023.
 CBI AML/CFT guidelines section 5.3.2 Transaction Monitoring states that if an automated system
 (Transaction Monitoring system) is employed, the rules, scenarios, and thresholds should be regularly
 reviewed and tested to ensure that they continue to detect identified risks and emerging risks.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Guidance & Regulatory Requirement
Issue Action Plans:
Update Tuning and Optimization Methodology (1112350)
 Development and Implementation of ETL Monitoring Process (1112352)

Issue Title: Suspicious Transaction Monitoring - Misapplication of the SIL Suspicious Transaction Reporting Procedure in relation to the review and approval of Cash App SARs
Issue Description:
Misapplication of the SIL Suspicious Transaction Reporting Procedure in relation to the review and approval of Cash App SARs - IA identified one Cash App SAR that did not go through a secondary review or challenge before being submitted to the relevant authorities. The SIL (Cash App) Suspicious Transaction Reporting Procedure (Roles and Responsibilities Section) requires the SAR Team to escalate any potential suspicious activity to the Chief Compliance Officer for review and approval. Management advised that for the one instance noted the second level of review and approval was not
 obtained due to the low value of the reported SAR. However, this is an erroneous application of the
 SAR procedure because the document does not provide details of any thresholds for escalating the
 suspicious transactions to the CCO/MLRO. Management further advised that following this case, AML
 investigators are now required to submit all cases with their recommendations (regardless of the EUR
 value involved), to the MLRO or delegate for approval before submission to the relevant authorities.
 CBI AML/CFT guidelines Section 7.4 Internal Reporting of Suspicious Transactions states: ‘In relation
 to the identification and escalation of internal reports, Firms should ensure that:
 - Operational procedures for staff on filing an internal report (‘internal reporting procedures’) are
 adequately documented and that the internal reporting procedure captures all suspicious transaction
 reporting requirements as prescribed under the CJA 2010. For example the internal reporting
 procedures should include at least:
 o All required steps for the reporting of suspicions from staff to the MLRO, or any other person(s)
 charged under the Firm’s internal reporting process with investigating suspicions, and from the MLRO
 to the authorities.’
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Guidance & Policies & Procedures
Issue Action Plans: Creation of Email Reminders for Required Secondary SAR Reviews (N/A Product Wind Down) (1112354)

Issue Title: Policy and Procedures - Unclear ownership of Change Management procedure (Square product) and TM Rulebook (Cash App product)
Issue Description:
Unclear ownership of Change Management procedure (Square product) and TM Rulebook (Cash App product) - There is not a clear ownership at SIL to ensure Change Management procedure (Square product) and TM Rulebook (Cash App product) are duly and timely reviewed, updated, and contain an accurate description of the current processes in place. We have identified the following shortcomings in content: 1. Model Change Management Procedure
 ● The document was last reviewed in January 2021, no further reviews nor updates have been
 incorporated since then.
 ● The document does not outline the current approval requirements in place regarding changes
 to the codes. In practice, there is an approval workflow in place at SIL where all code changes
 go through a peer review and a manager approval but this is not documented in the Model
 Change Management Procedure.
 2. Cash App TM Rulebook
 ● The TM Rulebook for Cash App product is in draft and has not been approved by the SIL
 MLRO/CCO.
 ● The process of setting Cash App TM thresholds is not documented within the TM Rulebook.
 SIL should formally document, regularly review and approve processes in place to ensure the
 operational practices are consistently applied.
 Per section 54(4) of the Criminal Justice Act (CJA): “A designated person shall ensure that policies,
 controls, and procedures adopted in accordance with this section are approved by senior management
 and shall keep such policies, controls and procedures under review, in particular when there are
 changes to the business profile or risk profile of the designated person.”
 CBI AML/CFT guidelines Section 6.7 Policies and Procedures states: ‘When developing AML/CFT
 policies, controls and procedures (“Policies”), Firms should inter alia maintain a detailed documented
 suite of Policies, which are:
 ● supplemented by guidance and supporting procedures;
 ● accurately reflect operational practices; and
 ● fully demonstrate consideration of and compliance with all legal and regulatory requirements’
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Guidance & Regulatory Requirement
Issue Progress Updates: Target completion date marked as complete on report. Using Issue Identified Date as completion date.
Issue Action Plans: Update Change Management Procedure (N/A Product Wind Down) (1112356)

Issue Title: Transaction Monitoring System Effectiveness - Cash App product Annual tuning and optimization exercise of the TM system has not been completed
Issue Description:
Cash App product Annual tuning and optimization exercise of the TM system has not been completed - SIL has not conducted a review of the TM system (Regulator) for the Cash App product to identify potential issues regarding TM rules, scenarios, thresholds, queue logic and queue productivity, as required by the CBI AML/CFT guidelines. The purpose of the tuning exercise is to optimize existing transaction monitoring thresholds,
 standardise queue logic and increase queue productivity. Management informed that since the Cash
 App product is not yet available to the general public, the annual tuning and optimization exercise has
 not been completed; however, this is not in line with the regulatory expectations.
 CBI AML/CFT guidelines section 5.3.2 Transaction Monitoring states that if an automated system
 (Transaction Monitoring system) is employed, the rules, scenarios, and thresholds should be regularly
 reviewed and tested to ensure that they continue to detect identified risks and emerging risks.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Guidance
Issue Progress Updates: Target completion date marked as N/A on report. Using Issue Identified Date as completion date.

Issue Title: 1.1 - Internal Procedures - Current SIL AML/CFT Program lacks mandatory documented content tailored to itsSpanish operations to comply with the Spanish AML/CFT requirements.
Issue Description:
1.1 Current SIL AML/CFT Program lacks mandatory documented content tailored to its
 Spanish operations to comply with the Spanish AML/CFT requirements.
 Current SIL AML/CFT Program does not include nor accurately describe the following mandatory processes
 adhered to SIL Spanish operations and to the Spanish AML regulations:
 ● Customer Identification and Know Your Customer duties:
 ○ Acceptance policy (see further details on Def. 2.1.f),
 ○ Due Diligence Measures (see further details on Def. 2.1.a/b),
 ○ Ongoing Due Diligence (see further details on Def. 2.1.g),
 ○ Beneficial Owners (see further details on Def. 2.1.d)
 ○ PEP definition (see further details on Def. 2.1.e) and screening (see further details on Def.
 2.1.c).
 

 ● Record Keeping duties (see further details on Def. 3.1).
 ● Document the Committees in place for the AML/CTF governance.
 

 Article 33 of Royal Decree 304/2014 requires that all AML/CTF procedures and controls in place must be
 documented in an AML/CTF Manual; however, SIL has not adjusted the AML/CFT Program to the Spanish
 operations and maintains the same AML/CFT Program as the one applied to the activity carried out in Ireland
 and under the Irish AML/CFT regulations.
 

 Additional deficiencies have been noted in relation to the current SIL AML/CFT Program, these are outlined in
 Def. 1.2 below classified as Recommendation for Improvement due to been driven from Recommendations
 on Internal Control Measures for the Prevention of Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing developed by
 the Executive Service.
Issue Rating Justification: Rectification
Issue Progress Updates: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval process.
Issue Action Plans: 1.1 SIL AML/CTF Program Document - Spanish Annex (1112360)

Issue Title: 1.2e - Internal Procedures - SIL´s AML/CFT Program and/or relevant procedural documents lack guidance around specific information that should be included in SARs submitted to the Executive Service
Issue Description:
1.2e. SIL ́s AML/CFT Program and/or relevant procedural documents lack guidance around specific
 information that should be included in SARs submitted to the Executive Service to provide detail
 regarding the reported transaction/activity, persons involved in the transaction/activity, and SIL’s
 investigation. Such information would include: (i) Relation and identification of the natural or legal
 persons participating in the operation and concept of their participation in it; (ii) Known activity of
 the natural or legal persons participating in the transaction and correspondence between the
 activity and the transaction; (iii) List of referenced transactions, including the dates to which they
 occur, the nature/currency in which they are carried out, amount, place(s) of execution, purpose,
 and payment/collection instruments used; (iv) Steps taken by SIL to investigate the reported
 transaction; (v) Statement of the circumstances from which it may be inferred that there is an
 indication or certainty of a relationship with money laundering or the financing of terrorism or that
 reveal the lack of economic, professional or business justification for carrying out the operation; (vi)
 Inclusion of the measures taken in relation to the parties involved in the reported suspicious
 transaction, and in the event of continuing the relationship, justification for why. (Article 3.11 of
 Executive Service Recommendations)
Issue Rating Justification: Improvement
Issue Progress Updates: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval process.
Issue Action Plans: 1.2e Procedure Enhancement Project - Square FinCrimes Compliance (1112370)

Issue Title: 1.2f - Internal Procedures - SIL does not have a procedure for the submission of the negative half-yearly report to the Executive Service.
Issue Description:

 Service. (Article 3.8.f of Executive Service Recommendations) According to Article 27 of Royal Decree
 304/2014, a monthly declaration must be sent via DMO 3.0 (tool from the Executive Service) of
 transactions conducted with high-risk jurisdictions amounting to €30.000 or higher. If there were no
 transactions to declare during the six months, a negative declaration must be sent.
Issue Rating Justification: Improvement
Issue Progress Updates: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval process.
Issue Action Plans: 1.2f Executive Service/AML Request Responses (1112372)

Issue Title: 1.2g - Internal Procedures - SIL does not have a procedure for responding to information request by the Executive Service or other AML/CTF authorities
Issue Description:

 other AML/CTF authorities, detailing: (i) Persons, body or department responsible for responding to
 the requests including the means for submitting the responses; (ii) the duty to Incorporate into the
 responses all information that is considered relevant to the case for a better understanding of its
 sense; (iii) That any request received related to AML/CFT is brought to the attention of the persons
 or departments in charge of such prevention tasks, and that the latter must also keep a record of all
 requests received, as well as of the answers given; and (iv) a requirement to evaluate whether
 special analysis (i.e. review of potentially suspicious activity) should be performed on the subject of
 the requests. (Article 3.12 of Executive Service Recommendations)
Issue Rating Justification: Improvement

Therefore, SIL does not perform enhanced due diligence to customers identified as high risk.

 applied specific due diligence measures based on merchant AML/CFT risk typology. Therefore, SIL
 does not perform enhanced due diligence to customers identified as high risk, as required by Article

Issue Title: 2.1b - Internal Procedures - SIL does not have a control in place to validate, prior to the activation of the account, the information provided by potential customers on the nature of their business.
Issue Description:
2.1b. SIL does not have a control in place to validate, prior to the activation of the account, the
 information provided by potential customers on the nature of their business. As established in
 Article 5 of Law 10/2010 and Article 10 of Royal Decree 304/2014, regulated entities, as part of the
 due diligence applied to potential customers, should verify the activity or nature of the potential
 customer’s business through documentary evidence provided by the potential customers or other
 sources of information. It should be noted that during our review of a sample of 100 SIL merchants,
 we identified 20 merchants whose actual activity was not consistent with their declared business
 type.
Issue Rating Justification: Rectification
Issue Progress Updates: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval process.
Issue Action Plans: 2.1b CRR Approach Evaluation (1112380)

Issue Title: 2.1c - Internal Procedures - SIL performs sanctions and PEP screenings 24 hours after a customer’s account is activated, which means that the customer can fully operate and be settled. This is not in line with Spanish regulatory requirements for due diligence on potential customers to be completed prior to onboarding.
Issue Description:
2.1c. SIL performs sanctions and PEP screenings 24 hours after a customer’s account is activated, which
 means that the customer can fully operate and be settled. This is not in line with Spanish regulatory
 requirements for due diligence on potential customers to be completed prior to onboarding. As
 established in Article 7.3 of Law 10/2010, regulated entities should not start a business relationship
 until the due diligence measures have been completed. As SIL does not perform sanctions and PEP screenings until after the customer’s account is activated, it does not have a complete
 understanding of the specific customer risks prior to onboarding.
Issue Rating Justification: Rectification
Issue Progress Updates: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval process.
Issue Action Plans: 2.1c Sanctions/PEP Screening - Activiation Delay (1112382)

Issue Title: 2.1d - Internal Procedures - SIL’s definition of beneficial ownership in its internal policies does not include all aspects of beneficial ownership.
Issue Description:
2.1d. SIL’s definition of beneficial ownership in its internal policies does not include all aspects of
 beneficial ownership included in Article 8 of Royal Decree 304/2014, as it does not define beneficial
 ownership in situations where there are no owners with at least a 25% share, or beneficial
 ownership of merchants identified as Foundations/Charities.
Issue Rating Justification: Rectification
Issue Progress Updates: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval process.
Issue Action Plans: 2.1d Policy and Procedures Update - Beneficial Owner Definition (1112384)

Issue Title: 2.1g - Internal Procedures - SIL has not put in place controls (i.e., ongoing reviews) nor established a frequency for validating and, if applicable, updating customer information and documentation based on AML/CTF risk as part of its ongoing due diligence process.
Issue Description:
2.1g. SIL has not put in place controls (i.e., ongoing reviews) nor established a frequency for validating
 and, if applicable, updating customer information and documentation based on AML/CTF risk as
 part of its ongoing due diligence process, as established in Article 11 of Royal Decree 304/2014,
 where it states that regulated entities must perform periodic reviews in order to ensure that
 customer documents, data and information obtained are up to date. For high-risk customers this
 review must be at least annually. It should be noted that in 7 out of the 100 files for new merchants
 sampled, the identification documents of the account holder and/or related persons were expired.
Issue Rating Justification: Rectification
Issue Progress Updates: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval process.
Issue Action Plans: 2.1g Ongoing Due Dilligence Control (1112390)

Issue Title: 2.2 - Internal Procedures - SIL did not comply with its KYC internal procedure
Issue Description:
2.2 SIL did not comply with its KYC internal procedure in the following cases when onboarding merchants
 sampled:
 a. SIL does not gather a written description of the merchant’s business type, as required by the
 Automated Onboarding section of the SIL KYC Procedure (page 5).
 b. In 2 out of the 100 new merchants sampled, a customer was able to register with a driver's license,
 an identification document not accepted according to Appendix B - Acceptable ID Documents for
 Sole Traders and Individuals in the KYC Procedure (page 24).
 c. SIL does not always collect information accrediting the web presence of merchant’s business during
 onboarding, as required in the Automated Onboarding section of the KYC Procedure (page 5). There
 is a field to collect this information from merchants but it is not required to be completed to move
 an application forward.
Issue Rating Justification: Rectification
Issue Progress Updates: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval process.
Issue Action Plans: 2.2 Collection of Information - Identification and KYC Process (1112392)

Issue Title: 3.1 - Record Keeping - SIL’s procedure for the record keeping of customer and transaction documentation as included in its AML/CFT Program is not aligned with current Spanish regulations to guarantee its adequate storage, management, availability, and accessibility
Issue Description:
3.1- SIL’s procedure for the record keeping of customer and transaction documentation as included in its AML/CFT Program is not aligned with current Spanish regulations to guarantee its adequate storage, management, availability, and accessibility. The procedure does not outline:
 a. The obligation to keep the documentation for a period of 10 years and that, after five years from
 the termination of the business relationship or the execution of the occasional transaction, the
 documentation kept shall only be accessible by SIL's internal control bodies, including the technical
 prevention units, and, if applicable, those in charge of its legal defense.
 b. Method/means to guarantee their adequate management and immediate availability.

Issue Title: 4.1 - Transaction Monitoring - SIL´s AML/CFT Program does not include steps or actions that executives and/or employees can take to report transactions potentially indicative of money laundering directly to the Executive Service in situations where after reporting such activity internally, SIL has not informed the executive/employee of the outcome of the reported transaction.
Issue Description:
4.1.- SIL´s AML/CFT Program does not include steps or actions that executives and/or employees can take to report transactions potentially indicative of money laundering directly to the Executive Service in situations where after reporting such activity internally, SIL has not informed the executive/employee of the outcome of the reported transaction, as required by Spanish regulations Article 18.4 of Law 10/2010 “The directors or
 employees of the regulated entities may communicate directly to the Executive Service those operations of
 which they are aware and in respect of which they consider that there are indications or certainty of being
 related to money laundering or the financing of terrorism, in the cases in which, having been revealed
 internally, the regulated entity had not informed the communicating manager or employee of the course given
 to his communication”.
Issue Rating Justification: Rectification

Issue Description:

 investigations sampled, the periods of analysis were 67, 162, and 208 days.
Issue Rating Justification: Rectification
Issue Progress Updates: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval process.
Issue Action Plans: 4.2 SIL STR Investigation Workstream - Additional Trained Staff (1112402)

Issue Title: 4.3 - Transaction Monitoring - The SAR sent to the Executive Service by means of Form F19-1 did not include the decision made or expected to be made by SIL on whether to continue the business relationship with the merchant (i.e., subject of the SAR).
Issue Description:
4.3- The Suspicious Activity Report (“SAR”) sent to the Executive Service by means of Form F19-1 did not include
 the decision made or expected to be made by SIL on whether to continue the business relationship with the
 merchant (i.e., subject of the SAR), as established in Article 26.3 of Royal Decree 304/2014 “In SARS,
 information will be included on the decision adopted or that is expected to be adopted by the regulated entity
 regarding the continuation or interruption of the business relationship with the client or clients that participate
 in the operation, as well as the justification for this decision”.
Issue Rating Justification: Rectification
Issue Progress Updates: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval process.
Issue Action Plans: 4.3 Retention of Customer Relationships (1112404)

Issue Title: 4.4 - Transaction Monitoring - SIL did not report an identified suspicious activity to the Executive Service in a timely manner in line with Spanish regulatory requirements.
Issue Description:
4.4- SIL did not report an identified suspicious activity to the Executive Service in a timely manner in line with
 Spanish regulatory requirements. According to Article 18.2 of Law 10/2010, of April 28, “regulated entities
 must report suspicious activities to Executive Service without delay”; however, SIL did not report suspicious
 activity to the Executive Service until nearly three months after the investigation concluded. Specifically, the
 investigation concluded on October 14, 2022, and it was submitted to the Financial Intelligence Unit of Ireland
 on November 4, 2022, but it was not reported to the Executive Service until January 10, 2023. SIL did not have
 access to SEPBLAC’s SAR filing system for a portion of the covered period.
Issue Rating Justification: Rectification
Issue Progress Updates: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval process.
Issue Action Plans: 4.4 Timely Notifications to Conform to Industry Best Practice (1112406)

 3.0 (tool from the Executive Service) of transactions conducted with high-risk jurisdictions amounting to
 €30.000 or higher. If there were no transactions to declare during the six months, a negative declaration must

Issue Title: 6.1 - Training - SIL's Regulatory Governance Committee did not approve its AML/CTF annual training plan corresponding toyear 2023
Issue Description:
6.1-SIL ́s Regulatory Governance Committee did not approve its AML/CTF annual training plan corresponding to
 year 2023 which is a requirement per Article 29 of Law 10/2010:
 “The regulated entities will adopt the appropriate measures so that their employees are aware of the
 requirements derived from this Law.
 These measures will include the duly accredited participation of employees in specific ongoing training courses
 aimed at detecting operations that may be related to money laundering or terrorist financing and instructing
 them on how to proceed in such cases. The training actions will be the subject of an annual plan that, designed
 based on the risks of the business sector of the obligated subject, will be approved by the internal control body”
Issue Rating Justification: Rectification
Issue Progress Updates: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval process.
Issue Action Plans: 6.1 AML/CFT Annual Training Plan (1112410)

Issue Title: Sanctions Screening not Occurring due to Name Field Validation Failures (Cash)
Issue Description:
In connection with monthly sanctions screening reporting provided to one of Cash App’s bank partners, Cash Compliance Analytics (CAX) identified a population of customers that are not being rescreened in accordance with Block’s Global Sanctions Compliance Policy and regulatory expectations. As a result of this issue, there is a population of customers who maintain accounts with Cash App but are not being screened to ensure they are not subject to sanctions.
 

 It was identified that the Rescreenable Flag in BadActors, which determines whether customer information is sent for rescreening, is set to False for some customers. Additional investigation revealed that the Rescreenable Flag was set to False because of validation failures arising from data provided in the name field of the screening request. Specifically, upstream systems are sending names that are failing validation by our screening vendor, Lexis. These validation failures are the result of one or more of the following scenarios: 1) the name is too short, including instances where no last name is provided; 2) the name contains an emoji; 3) the name contains non-standard characters; 4) the name contains only symbols like ... or ---,.
 

 While initially the issue was identified in the context of rescreens, the underlying root cause (failed name validation) also applies to initial screens.
RCA Description: Upstream systems are sending invalid screen requests to BadActors.
Impacted Legal Entities:
Block, Inc.
 Cash App Investing, LLC
Issue Rating Justification: This issue is impacting the screening of our customers against sanctions watchlists, which is a regulatory expectation. The failure to screen our customer presents heightened regulatory risks as it prevents us from confirming that we do not maintain accounts on our platform with sanctioned parties.

Additionally, necessary summaries and documentation/evidence to support the action plan was completed successfully are also lacking. The one evidenced linked to the action plan appears to be an overview/requirements doc, but it's missing approvals, has open comments, and cannot be used as sufficient evidence of issue closure on its own. Please see this Action Plan Support document to guide you in providing the necessary documentation to close this issue; if you have any questions, please reach out to Estefania Scherer. Additionally, please identify a new action plan approver as Tobi is no longer with the company. The action plan approver must review supporting documentation and summaries prior to resubmitting the issue for closure.

Additionally, countries involved was updated to only include US and legal entity was updated to remove Square entities as this specific issue only covered Cash App.
