Issue Title: Hardware Installments France & Spain: Sanctions Screening Flag Failure
Issue Description: On 5/1/24 Estefania Scherer on Global Sanctions Compliance ("GSC") notified the Square Loans product team that the two product intents created for the Hardware Installments launch in France ("FR") and Spain ("ES"), #97 for FR and #96 for ES, in October 2023 (Jira link attached below under “Hyperlink”) were not deployed by the Capital-Shop team at launch because the Capital Shop team did not enable the sanctions screening flag. This resulted in screening requests not being sent as required by the product team for sanctions screening prior to issuing the HWI via the product intents for all FR and ES applicants from GA (i.e., live product launch) on 10/12/23 through 5/1/2024 when Jared Korotzer on Square Loans engineering deployed the fix (i.e., enabled the sanctions screening flag). These two product intents work by doing a one-time screen of the merchant prior to issuing the installment plan as EU merchants are first screened at onboarding and only monthly subsequently. This means that while sellers would have been screened at seller onboarding and monthly thereafter through the product intents that screen their Managed Payments seller profiles (#57 for ES and #55 for FR), any FR and ES Hardware Installment plans that were issued between October 2023 and May 1, 2024 were not sanctions screened immediately prior to issuance of the installment plan, which could have resulted in Squareup International Ltd. and Block Inc. issuing a HWI to a sanctioned party because FR and ES sellers are only screened on a monthly cadence through product intents #55 and #57, respectively. This issue is rated high as it is related to a screening gap for non-U.S. customers.
RCA Description: People and Process: Capital-Shop failed to deploy the sanctions screening flag at product launch despite the screening requirement being included in the PRD process and engineering teams building the capability to initiate screening at launch. The ultimate root cause of this was lack of adherence to the change management process given employee turnover that occurred between the project scope/build and testing/launch. The main individuals responsible for the PRD and project scope were no longer with the company and/or the team when the project was launched. This caused the Capital-Shop team to miss testing the screening requirement before launch, which is inconsistent with the PRD process design that includes testing of requirements.
Issue Rating Justification: This issue represents high risk as it includes a failure to adhere to internal guidance provided by the Global Sanctions team prior to product launch related to sanctions screening of a non-U.S. customer population that resulted in a screening gap.
Issue Action Plans:
Action Plan 1: Enable Sanctions Screening Flag (1118916)
 Action Plan 2- File retro-SEV and investigate root cause (1195673)
 Action Plan 3: Rescreen ES and FR HWI applications (1195678)
 Action Plan 4: Support: PRD Update (1196121)
 Action Plan 5: Sustainability (1196124)
 Action Plan 6 Support: Product Intent #96 and #97 Decision (1196127)
Closure Request Justification:
All action plans under this issue have been reviewed and approved for closure on 10/31/2024.
 

 Overall issue approved to be closed.

Issue Title: The practices for the handling and processing of privacy requests and complaints through the Privacy AU Inbox requires improvement, as well as the flagging of complaints by Square Australia Customer Success Advocates [Square AU]
Issue Description:
Background
 Square Australia Sellers and Customers can make a privacy complaint or inquiry to Square Australia through multiple channels including phone or email. Square Australia has established a separate inbox ‘privacy-au@squareup.com’ that is publicly displayed on
 websites and the Privacy Policy, for customers to enquire or raise privacy related matters. The inbox is managed by Square Australia Privacy Counsel. Sellers and Customers can also make contact for privacy matters through the General Square Australia contact channels, which is managed by Square Customer Success. 
 

 Square Australia Privacy Inbox
 GCMT identified that there are no Standard Operating Procedures or Salesforce Knowledge Articles relating to the handling and management of Privacy related concerns which are received through the Privacy Inbox or by Customer Success.
 

 GCMT conducted testing of 21 emails (the full population) received in the Privacy inbox between 1 January 2023 and 31 Dec 2023 and noted the below exceptions/themes:
 ● In 14 samples, Square Australia did not respond to the email. The request consisted of:
 o 7 requests to remove personal information
 o 6 requests/queries related to unlinking of card information from email and phone number for digital receipts
 o 1 request to view information held by Square Australia
 ● In 6 samples, Square Australia did not fully address the customer’s request
 o For 5 samples, customers requested to unlink their card information from digital receipts and for Square Australia to delete their personal information. In all samples, Square provided a generic explanation of how digital receipts work and links for customers to follow instructions to unlink their card information. While there is no legal obligation to delete personal information in Australia, additional requests to delete all data were not addressed.
 o In one sample, the customer requested to have their ID deleted after completing reactivation. The Agent response included a link to the US website and follow up clarification email from the customer was not responded to.
 ● 5 samples were not flagged as a complaint and therefore were not subject to the formal complaints management process and/or included in monthly complaint reporting.
 ● 1 sample was responded to, 4 months after Square was first contacted. 
 

 Square Australia Customer Success -Customer Enquiries - Missed Complaints
 ● In 7 of 30 Seller/Customer privacy enquiries that met the definition of a complaint, the Square Australia CSA did not flag a complaint in Salesforce, therefore was not subject to the formal complaints management process and/or included in monthly complaint reporting.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Australia Privacy Principle – Use or Disclosure of Personal Information (1018694)
Issue Rating Justification: Rated in accordance with the Issue Management Program methodology
Issue Action Plans:
Privacy complaints will be reviewed and catalogued with the new tooling and policy, (1100436)
 Updates to the CS Complaints Management Program and associated training, will launch Mar 26th. All advocates across all sites and teams will complete mandatory training 12th-22th March. (1100439)

Issue Title: Project Zeppelin - KYC - Debit card linking process and controls review
Issue Description:
Existing system control does not adequately detect or monitor debit card linking activities for potential AML issues.
 

 [Medium criticality #05]
RCA Description:
1.) Evaluate current debit card linking controls 
 2.) Based on 1, ensure that controls are designed to effectively mitigate risk
Issue Rating Justification: The rating was issued by the third party review who identified this issue.
Issue Action Plans: Update debit card linking process (1120880)
Closure Request Justification:
Cash App has implemented a range of controls to further reduce the risk of debit card linking and connected AML issues. Those controls include limitations on the number of accounts a financial instrument can be linked to, limitations on number of attempts and successful links, as well as enhanced monitoring in the event there is a mismatch between the name on the card/bank account with the Cash App account.
 

 1. Velocity Controls
 -Debit / prepaid cards - 2 successful links per day and 3 successful links per month
 -Credit cards - 2 successful links per day and 3 successful links per month
 -Bank accounts - 2 successful per day / month
 -Attempted links - 4 unique instruments per day and 6 unique instruments per month
 

 2. Rule of 5
 -A financial instrument may only be linked to a total of 5 accounts lifetime. Accounts who previously linked the instrument may continue to link to that financial instrument
 

 3.Name matching on Bank and Card
 -COM has expanded the bank account mismatch queue to include all linked card instruments that are currently stored (based on information we get from Square). The criteria for the Card Name queue is based on 2 or more successful card linkages in 30 days, 2 or more mismatched ZIP codes obtained from cards during the review period, and if they have 2 or more mismatched names identified between the card instruments and successful IDV attempts all time.
 -COM queue currently identifies moderate risk accounts (based on CRR score) where there is a name mismatch between a Verified Cash App account and a linked bank account (linked via Plaid). Moderate risk customers receive a due diligence review to confirm the customer is in control of the funds associated with the account(s). During the due diligence review the customer’s verified name (including names provided during attempts to verify their identity and the customer’s name as provided on the government issued ID associated with the account) are reviewed for consistency with the linked bank account.
 -COM has expanded monitoring over low risk customers. A new queue alerts lower (low and moderate) risk customers based on a more restrictive set of criteria (e.g., 2 mismatched bank accounts). A Verified Cash App account will alert if it has 2 or more successful unique bank linkages in 30 days, 2 or more mismatched ZIP codes obtained from Plaid during the review period, and if they have 2 or more mismatches names identified between the linked instrument (Plaid) and successful IDV attempts all time.
 -Additionally, another new queue alerts low and moderate risk customers for due diligence that have 2 or more successful instrument linkages in 30 days, 2 or more mismatched ZIP codes identified between linked instrument and successful IDV attempts during the review period, and exceeds inflow ($1,065) or outflow ($780) thresholds in association with a linked instrument with no associated name (aggregated at the SSN level).

Issue Title: Insufficient substantiation of Sponsor's Parental or Guardian Relationship to Teen
Issue Description:
The Cash App Families product allowed adults to sponsor teens without sufficient controls to substantiate that the sponsor was the minor’s parent or legal guardian. Specifically, the product design did not independently collect, or cross-reference, relationship and age information, which limited Cash App’s ability to assess the appropriateness of the sponsorship.
 This created potential for sponsorships that may not align with Cash App’s Terms of Service, and introduced risks related to teen user safety and reputational impact.
RCA Description: The initial design of the Families sponsorship flow did not contain the appropriate checks to substantiate the sponsor–teen relationship. As a result, the process lacked mechanisms to better assess whether sponsorships aligned with the intended policy that only parents or legal guardians may sponsor minors.
Issue Rating Justification: P0 in the Source of Truth Doc. Therefore translated to M risk rating due to impact and priority.
Issue Progress Updates: Brian Kearns updated issue name, description, and Root Caue at the direction of Banking PCM lead (Luke Zhu).
Issue Action Plans: Sponsorship Relationship Substantiation Controls (1120904)
Closure Request Justification:
7/16/2025- Daniella Corcuera submitting the issue for closure on behalf of Luke Zhu. Please see comments below.
 

 Brian Kearns submitted for closure on behalf of Luke Zhu. Issue remediation was completed prior to Issue owner's departure. 
 

 On May 7, 2024, Cash App rolled Sponsor Attestation to 100%. This launched included, inter alia, the following changes to better substantiate the appropriateness of the relationship between a sponsor and its sponsored account(s):
 Independently collect relationship from the minor and the sponsor
 - Require the minor to select their relationship with the sponsor: a) parent b) guardian
 - Require the sponsor to select their relationship with the sponsor: a) parent b) guardian
 Independently collect the teen’s birthday from the sponsor.
 Require sponsors to see the full legal name used by the minor.
 Require sponsors to attest that they are the parent or guardian of the minor.
 Implement age and relationship based heuristics to deny sponsorships that may be inconsistent with our terms of service. Decline sponsorship if:
 - DOB entered by the minor and the sponsor mismatch
 - Relationship entered by the sponsor is “Parent” and the sponsor age is <28
 

 See attached evidence: Airtable screenshot, CRD, and PRD.

Issue Title: There is no record keeping for consumer and merchant records that are watchlist screened on a daily and quarterly basis.
Issue Description:
Afterpay sends daily new and reactivated existing customer and merchant batch files to Bridger
 LexisNexis for the purposes of ongoing watchlist screening. Active customers and merchants
 are then screened on a quarterly basis.
 There is no current audit trail on the Afterpay or Bridger systems to evidence the full customer
 lists that were passed to Bridger for watchlist screening.
 GCMT tested a random sample of 420 new customers (210 consumer and 210 merchant)
 watchlist screening records contained within the quarterly batch files (December 2023 and
 March 2024) for the period July 2023 through February 2024.
 GCMT identified that all of the 420 (100%) new customers account information were present in
 the December 2023 and March 2024 quarterly batch files.
 However, as there are no screening records or audit trail contained within Afterpay or Bridger
 systems, in effect, there is no record that the customer was screened unless an alert case is
 created in Bridger. Whilst GCMT acknowledges that results of screening conducted at
 onboarding for Merchants are documented within the ‘PEP Screening’ information field within
 Salesforce, GCMT identified through sample testing of 80 newly onboarded Merchants that 10
 Merchants did not have a record of screening completion and result in Salesforce. GCMT
 confirmed that for all 10 Merchants, onboarding screening was conducted through verification
 of an individual and entity file.
 This process, designed for batch daily and quarterly screening, limits the ability for the business
 to evidence onboarding and quarterly screening that is occurring for consumers and merchants.
 GCMT has been advised that since February, there is a new table which stores a record of the
 data file containing customers and merchants that have been sent to Bridger for screening.

Issue Title: Absence of Compliance Management System Policy Applicable to Credit Card
Issue Description: Square does not have a formalized CMS Program governing the credit card product. Specifically, there is not a formalized Program document connecting the CMS for the credit card product with the enterprise-wide CMS and there is a lack of clarity of the roles and responsibilities between the enterprise and the credit card product team.
Hyperlinks: https://drive.google.com/drive/u/0/folders/1Lh27vqhu8ZiCssOQnTr1bs7QIlvyochu|SQ Capital CMS Policy Approved and executed
RCA Description: Square did not formalize or document a CMS Program to govern the direction of the program at the product level when the credit card product was initially launched because it relied on the CMS Program at the enterprise-level.
Issue Rating Justification: Issue rating provided by Crowe who follow their own risk rating methodology. Refer to Crowe audit report for further information.
Issue Progress Updates:
Specifically, there is not a formalized Program document connecting the CMS for the credit card product with the enterprise-wide CMS and there is a lack of clarity of the roles and responsibilities between the enterprise and the credit card product team.
 

 Criteria:
 Financial institutions should maintain a CMS Program in accordance with the principles established in the CFPB Compliance Management Review Examination Manual. Specifically, the Examination Manual states, “to maintain legal compliance, an institution must develop and maintain a sound CMS that is integrated into the overall framework for product design, delivery, and administration across their entire product and service lifecycle. Ultimately, compliance should be part of the day-to-day responsibilities of management and the employees of a supervised entity; issues should be self-identified; and corrective action should be initiated by the entity. Institutions are also expected to manage relationships with service providers to ensure that service providers effectively manage compliance with Federal consumer financial laws applicable to the product or service being provided.” An overview of how the CMS operates and its objectives are usually contained within a CMS policy and plan that governs the program and identifies all individuals and groups involved in the program. 
 

  
 Implications: 
 Not having a CMS Program specific to the credit card product in place can lead to compliance failures due to a lack ownership and accountability over key compliance functions, such as monitoring and testing, or policy and procedure governance. Roles and responsibilities not being formally defined can also lead to duplication of efforts and inefficient use of resources. Additionally, a CMS Program can define compliance budgeting and staffing targets, so a lack thereof can lead to compliance failures due to lack of personnel expertise or adequate funding
Issue Action Plans:
Square Capital must have a minimum of 3 voting members - Update the Square Capital CMS program for the Square Credit Card Program to reflect it. (1128152)
 Square Credit Card - Creation of Square Capital CMS program and/or inclusion of the Square Capital CMS program into the Block level CMS.  (1128155)
 Square Credit Card - Board Members Training on the CMS program and Lending Regulations (1128158)
Closure Request Justification: Tyler Hand and OBJ have been approved as SQ Capital Board members. Both have been trained on the CMS policy . Evidences are attached. Celtic has closed this issue on their end.

Issue Title: Third Party Due Diligence Procedures Fail to Consider Credit Card Requirements
Issue Description: Square’s third-party due diligence review should include a thorough analysis of a potential vendor’s ability to comply with consumer protection regulations (UDAP, ECOA, etc.) specific to the credit card product.
RCA Description:
The enterprise-wide third-party risk management program was set up with a focus on financial crimes for vendor due diligence purposes, and the process does not include other regulatory compliance considerations vendors may be subject to and responsible for. 
 

 Finding Type: Control Design
Issue Rating Justification: Issue rating provided by Crowe who follow their own risk rating methodology. Refer to Crowe audit report for further information.
Issue Progress Updates:
Condition:
 Square’s third-party due diligence review does not include a thorough analysis of a potential vendor’s ability to comply with consumer protection regulations (UDAP, ECOA, etc.) specific to the credit card product. 
 

 Criteria:
 As part of an effective CMS, a financial institution’s third-party risk management program should include due diligence which is comprehensive of all laws and regulations the prospective vendor is expected to comply with as it relates to the service or product support they will be providing. Due diligence should include risk assessment of relevant regulatory risks, and the effectiveness of the prospective vendor’s controls for these risks. 
 

 Implications: 
 Initial due diligence not including a review of a potential vendor’s ability to comply with laws and regulations specific to the credit card product may result in compliance failures and negative outcomes for customers. 
 

 Crowe Recommendation:
 Vendor engagement request surveys and initial due diligence activities should be expanded beyond the scope of BSA/AML/OFAC and information security considerations to also determine if onboarding the vendor introduces other compliance risks specific to the credit card product (e.g., ECOA, UDAP, FACTA, SCRA, TILA, and/or privacy risks). The expansion of the scope of initial due diligence will allow management to adequately evaluate whether a potential vendor’s compliance risks are appropriately mitigated or are minimal enough to be accepted.
Issue Action Plans:
Initial Due Diligence Procedure Enhancement (1) (1128801)
 Square Credit Card - Launch Due Diligence Questionnaire Assessment on those Key Third Party vendors identified that support the Square Credit Card Program (1128806)
 Square Credit Card - Third Party Due Diligence Procedure Enhancement (1128811)
Closure Request Justification:
A new Vendor Risk Assessment framework has been developed to specifically address regulatory requirements applicable to all Block products including credit card operation and consumer/commercial customer protection. Key enhancements include dedicated questions to address specific third party activities that would be used to evaluate if a third party will support or perform activities for Block Inc., or its subsidiaries to identify impacts related to compliance with credit card-related regulations (e.g., Regulation Z, TILA, UDAAP, and ECOA) and consumer protection laws, focusing on potential risks related to unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts and practices (UDAAP) and compliance with requirements under SCRA and ECOA. Also, a regulations list user guide has been added so third party relationship owners can identify potential regulations that would be in scope for the services being performed. Additionally, to ensure thorough evaluation of vendor compliance, a detailed questionnaire has been created and incorporated into the vendor onboarding and ongoing monitoring processes. This questionnaire includes:
  • Regulatory Compliance Questions: Specific questions about policies, procedures, and system capabilities to comply with credit card laws, including TILA, UDAAP, ECOA, and SCRA.
  • Documentation Requirements: Vendors will be required to provide supporting documentation, such as compliance policies, audit reports, and training certifications.
 

 

 

 

 To ensure there is a consistent and repeatable process during the SME review process a Square Compliance TPRM Procedure was developed and implemented as part of the vendor risk assessment process. This process sets forth a way in which the Square Compliance team conducts initial due diligence on all Third-Parties including Vendors prior to entering in a contractual agreement with Block for the delivery of services to Square, as well as periodic due diligence refreshes on all existing Third Parties according to the risk tiers defined in the TPRM policy.  
 

 

 The “Vendor’s Due Diligence” review process is conducted prior to contract execution. Square Compliance Subject Matter Experts “SMEs” are assigned Vendor Engagement Tickets (VER) to them in the Thinksmart TPRM tool (“ThinkSmart”) used by Block for the initial due diligence launch and analysis including ongoing risk reassessments. The SME reviews the VER ticket submitted by the requestor and makes a determination that a Compliance Due Diligence Questionnaire (“DDQ”) is required and review information or artifacts provided by the third party complete the review.
 

 The Compliance Third Party Due Diligence Procedure outlines the process to ensure that SMEs have a consistent process flow to navigate the ThinkSmart system to submit their approvals prior to third party contract execution. 
 

 Supporting Documents:
 

 https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1zwaYN_Xzr135L9ehwUL6iyv9dV_obiVkcSSLiLMmJFM/edit?gid=549670790#gid=549670790

Issue Title: Model Risk Governance Policy Missing Model Override and Overlay Requirements
Issue Description: The Model Risk Governance Policy does not address use of model overlays or model overrides. Model overlays and overrides include adjustments made to the model output after results have been generated. These adjustments to model outputs can be made to address model shortcomings and/or account for human expert judgement.
RCA Description: The financial institution did not consider the significance of requiring model overrides and overlays in their Model Risk Governance policies due to Management oversight.
Issue Rating Justification: Issue rating provided by Crowe who follow their own risk rating methodology. Refer to Crowe audit report for further information.
Issue Progress Updates:
Condition: 
 The Model Risk Governance procedures do not address use of model overlays or model overrides. Model overlays and overrides include adjustments made to the model output after results have already been generated. These adjustments to model outputs can be made to address model shortcomings and/or account for human expert judgement. 
 

 Criteria: 
 Model Risk Governance procedures should set an expectation for documentation, oversight, and governance for use of model overlays. Additionally, they should require for all model overlays and overrides to be reviewed for approval. These adjustments should be documented for transparency into model adjustments and outputs, monitoring bias and overuse of overlays, and maintaining an accurate audit trail of decision making and impact
 

 Implication:
 If use and understanding of proxy data is not documented and reviewed, the Company risks misuse of proxy data due to not understanding the data’s limitations. This can lead to inaccurate data being used or misrepresented relationships – both of which could lead to misleading model results. Misuse of proxy data can also have impacts on fair lending and other regulatory impacts in addition to model and financial risk
 

 Crowe Recommendation:
 Square should develop additional controls as part of their procedures to document their use of model overrides or any other manual adjustments made to model outputs. These adjustments should be documented for transparency so there is an accurate audit trail for both the adjustments and their support. These adjustments should be reviewed to assess if the model has become over-reliant on overlays and if they are applied in a biased way. If any negative impacts come to light as part of this review, procedures should be developed for remediation to address the area of impact and assess if there are other impacted areas.
Issue Action Plans: Square Credit Card - Square Capital Model Risk Governance Policy must be revised to address the use of model overlays or model overrides (1150589)
Closure Request Justification: Action ID has been accepted by Celtic, therefore this item is closed.

Issue Title: Model Risk Governance Policy Missing Proxy Data Requirements
Issue Description: There is no requirement in the Model Risk Governance policy that states that use of proxy data must be documented and reviewed. Proxy data includes any third-party or peer data that is used to either supplement a lack of internal data or represent external variables.
RCA Description: The financial institution did not consider the significance of formalizing how proxy data must be documented and reviewed in the Model Risk Governance policies due to Management oversight.
Issue Rating Justification: Issue rating provided by Crowe who follow their own risk rating methodology. Refer to Crowe audit report for further information.
Issue Progress Updates:
Condition: 
 There is no requirement in the Model Risk Governance policy that states that use of proxy data must be documented and reviewed. Proxy data includes any third-party or peer data that is used to either supplement a lack of internal data or represent external variables. In fair lending and credit scoring, peer data, a form of proxy data, can include demographics data and loan approval rates from peer institutions. Examples of proxy data can include transaction history data for borrowers with limited credit history. Another common use of proxy data would include public macroeconomic data like GDP growth or unemployment rates.
 

 Criteria: 
 Model Risk Governance policies should require for use of proxy data to be documented and reviewed to understand their limitations and if the proxy data is still relevant, reliable, and reflective of the Company’s behavior. 
 

 Crowe Recommendation: 
 Square should develop additional controls as part of their procedures to document their use of model overrides or any other manual adjustments made to model outputs. These adjustments should be documented for transparency so there is an accurate audit trail for both the adjustments and their support. These adjustments should be reviewed to assess if the model has become over-reliant on overlays and if they are applied in a biased way. If any negative impacts come to light as part of this review, procedures should be developed for remediation to address the area of impact and assess if there are other impacted areas.
Issue Action Plans: Square Credit Card - Square Capital Model Risk Governance Policy must be revised to include usage of proxy data. (1150592)
Closure Request Justification: Closing this issue as we do not use proxy data models. This item should not have been accepted as a finding.

Issue Title: Model Risk Governance Policy Missing Testing Framework and Data Quality Documentation Requirement
Issue Description:
The Model Risk Governance policy’s framework for reviewing data quality testing and performance testing currently does not address the following: 
  
 - Framework for Assessing and Remediating Performance Testing Issues: The Model Risk Governance policy outlines the need for performance testing, but it does not outline a framework for reviewing identified performance issues in a repeatable manner. Issues raised as part of performance testing are documented and reviewed with more senior parties in the same way as any other issue noted in the independent review. 
  - Documentation of Data Quality Issues: There is no requirement in the Model Risk Governance policy that states that issues noted in data quality testing need to be logged and reviewed to understand where the data issue stems from. There is no framework for determining if issues need to be raised for further review to understand if there are systemic issues.
RCA Description: The enterprise Model Risk Governance policy was developed in November of 2021 and has not undergone development since the initial release. Due to the infancy of the policy, it lacks the comprehensiveness and granularity of a more developed MRM policy.
Issue Rating Justification: Issue rating provided by Crowe who follow their own risk rating methodology. Refer to Crowe audit report for further information.
Issue Progress Updates:
Crowe Recommendation: 
 The credit card product team should be reviewing and formally approving their model validation plans before a model goes into production. The scope of validation plans can also be widened by using sensitivity analysis to understand the impact of different model inputs on the results. This can aid in understanding limitations or over-exposure to different model inputs and can also act as a check for a model’s directional consistency. Documented model assumptions and limitations should also be reviewed periodically for accuracy and currency to aid Square in understanding if they are addressing different model limitations.
Issue Action Plans: Square Credit Card - Square Capital Model Risk Governance Policy must be revised to include a framework for performance testing and the documentation of quality testing and performance. (1150595)
Closure Request Justification: Issue has been resolved.

Issue Title: ACH risk assessment does not include risks and controls specific to SFS [Square Financial Services - US]
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 General Rules - Subsection 1.2.4 Risk Assessments of the NACHA Operating Rules states a participating DFI and a Third-Party Sender must:
 (a) conduct, or have conducted, an assessment of the risks of its ACH activities; 
 (b) implement, or have implemented, a risk management program on the basis of such an assessment; and 
 (c) comply with the requirements of its regulator(s) with respect to such assessment and risk management program. 
 

 Testing Result:
 The ACH risks documented in the Enterprise Risk Assessment (ERA) is limited. During the 2023 Audit, WesPay Advisors provided a recommendation to expand the ERA to include risks and controls specific to SFS as referenced in the Enhanced Audit Report. While progress has been made, GCMT noted the Risk Assessment has not been updated to fully address WesPay recommendation.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: The root cause stems from a Process weakness. The design of the Risk Assessment does not adequately address NACHA guidelines.
Issue Rating Justification: Issue is derived from a weakness in the process that presents moderate risk to operations. There are not explicit regulatory requirements tied to the process.
Issue Action Plans: ACH risk assessment does not include risks and controls specific to SFS (1129471)
Closure Request Justification:
Comments added by CRW due to technical issues encountered by GCMT teammate who validated the action plan for closure. Per @fsarpas; Action Plan Owner- Ivette Cisneros-Iriarte, provided GCMT with the enhanced risk assessment, "2024 NACHA Target Risk Assessment" on 7/30/2024. The "NACHA ERA 20232024" tab includes risk and controls specific to SFS incorporating feedback from WesPay Advisors. Risk were captured in the first few columns (A-F) and then rating depending on impact/likelihood of said risks. Control information can be found in columns AP to BH. Final residual risk rating can be found in column BM. 
 GCMT considers Action Plan remediated. No exceptions noted.

Issue Title: ML/TF Risk Assessment
Issue Description:
Gaps in Risk Assessment Follow-Up and Reporting Protocols - The 2021 AML and Sanctions Risk Assessment results indicate that the Cash App and Bitcoin products have “High” residual risk ratings, and that multiple controls applicable to the Cash App, Square, and Lending businesses are “Weak” or ineffective. However, the Global AML & Sanctions Risk Assessment Framework does not define specific actions Management should take in response to the risk assessment results (e.g., develop Management Action Plans to enhance “Weak” controls, document rationale for accepting “High” residual risks, etc.) Accordingly, Management did not develop any such action plans or document risk acceptance rationales in the wake of the 2021 AML and Sanctions Risk Assessment. 
 

 Furthermore, ARC Pre-Read Materials prepared to communicate the findings did not include a sufficient level of detail regarding “Weak” controls and “High” residual risks. The Pre-Read conveys residual risk ratings at the country / market level only, and does not specify (i) the AML program controls noted as weak or ineffective nor (ii) the products / business units with high residual risk.

Issue Title: Onboarding and Customer Due Diligence
Issue Description:
Validation of Customer’s Payment Instrument - SEL does not have a control in place to verify whether or not all cards/bank accounts linked to a customer account are in the name of the account holder. Furthermore, in the event that a SEL customer has linked a bank account or payment instrument owned by a third party (i.e., non-customer) to his or her SEL account, SEL has not implemented any mechanisms or procedures to (i) assess the risk profile of the third party and (ii) perform controls commensurate with his or her ML / TF risk level.
 

 As SEL products currently permit individuals or entities who have not been subject to SEL’s onboarding procedures to (i) fund SEL customer transactions and / or (ii) receive proceeds from a SEL customer’s stored balance, UK regulators would expect SEL’s AML / CTF program to include controls commensurate with the risk introduced by this functionality.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation
Issue Progress Updates: Observation 2 (Cash)

Closure Request Justification: Original due date: 12/22/2022. Later extended to 1/31/2023, ultimately extended to 2/10/2023 (implementation plan only).

Issue Title: Absence of Periodic Review Cycle for Low and Moderate Risk Customers
Issue Description: SEL does not have a documented criteria or rationale for not performing periodic validation of KYC information for customers risk-rated as “Medium” and “Low” based on the ML/TF risk posed by the customer, as expected by industry good practices. SEL only conducts annual reviews for customers risk-rated as “High,” and the process was implemented in January 2024. Low or moderate risks are currently only reviewed following trigger events.
Issue Rating Justification:
● The lack of periodic reviews or KYC refreshes for low and moderate-risk customers may result in outdated customer information, potentially leading to ineffective customer risk assessments and increased exposure to ML/TF risks.
  
 ● Outdated customer information may hinder the ability to detect and respond to changes in the customer's risk profile, potentially leading to non-compliance with regulatory requirements and increased ML/TF risks.
Issue Action Plans: Performing KYC on a Risk Based Approach (1130794)

Issue Title: Absence of CDD Information for Onboarded Merchant
Issue Description:
IA observed that for 1 of the 25 customers sampled for onboarding and CDD, SEL did not correctly identify the Beneficial Owners (“BOs”). 
 

 In accordance with the ‘UK Seller KYC Standard Operating Procedures, SEL must “identify the ownership structure of the entity”; and “identify and verify all Ultimate Beneficial Owners (UBOs) with 25% or more ownership or voting rights.” In this instance, SEL did not identify the fact that the individual listed on the business record was the BO. 
 

 According to SEL management, this was due to human error.
Issue Rating Justification:
● Inadequate identification of Shareholders/BOs may lead to increased ML/TF risk, and financial sanctions risk. 
 

 ● Inaccurate identification information may result in difficulties in demonstrating compliance to external reviewers, such as Regulators or Auditors.
  
 ● Failings in CDD processes may result in regulatory non-compliance and potential fines or penalties.
Issue Action Plans: Identification of Beneficial Owners and Sample Lookback (1138129)

Issue Title: Absence of SLAs for Sanctions for Screening Alert Reviews
Issue Description:
SEL has not documented the timelines required to complete sanctions screening alert reviews (i.e., SLAs) within its policies or procedures. 
 

 While there is no specific regulatory requirement that explicitly mandates the provision of SLAs for reviewing sanctions screening alerts, it is considered industry good practice to establish SLAs to ensure timely and effective reviews of these time sensitive alerts.
Issue Rating Justification:
● The lack of an SLA for reviewing sanctions screening alerts may allow the customer to make payments to/from sanctions targets. 
 

 ● The lack of an SLA for sanctions screening alerts may indicate a gap in risk management practices, and the potential for inadequate mitigation of sanctions-related risks.
Issue Action Plans: Sanction Screening Procedure Update (1130803)

Issue Title: Lack of Clear Rationale in Enterprise-Wide Risk Assessment (“EWRA”) Methodology for Severity Ratings
Issue Description:
The SEL EWRA methodology lacks a clear definition of the issue ratings (high, medium, or low) used to assign to findings coming out of the AML/CTF Risk Assessment. This absence of a well-defined system for categorising the risk ratings of identified issues undermines the effectiveness of the EWRA process. 
 

 IA did not identify any discrepancies in the allocation of risk ratings to findings identified during the EWRA; however, establishing specific guidelines for defining risk ratings would enhance the reliability and integrity of the EWRA, ensuring that all AML/CTF-related findings are evaluated and addressed appropriately and uniformly.
Issue Rating Justification: ● Without clear definitions for issue risk ratings, the assessment results can be inconsistent and unreliable, leading to potential discrepancies in how risks are identified, prioritised, and mitigated.
Issue Action Plans: Review and Update Programme Documentation (1130806)

Issue Title: Beneficial Owner Information is not updated for loans [Square Financial Services - US ]
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 SFS Issue 142 was opened due to deficiencies identified in the Credit Operations Beneficial Ownership process. The original issue states Credit Operations reviews entity type for loans >$75k. During their review, they may identify the business applied as a sole proprietorship but is actually an LLC or other business type. When this happens, Ops sends SSPs to the customer to provide beneficial owner information. Ops procedures require them to update the application in Regulator with the BO information provided by the customer. Operations is not currently adhering to the procedural requirements to update the application in Regulator (dating back to Bank launch). 
 

 By not updating the application, beneficial owners are not subject to IDV or sanctions screening. The loans are not compliant with CDD Rule and OFAC requirements.
 

 Testing Result:
 GCMT reviewed a sample of 5 loans originated after 4/1/2024. For 4 of 5 loans reviewed, beneficial owners were provided by the customer in response to the SSP; however, Credit Ops failed to update Regulator with the beneficial owner information. Based on testing performed, the issue and risk has not been remediated.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Root cause is People related and stems from a failure to follow policy and procedures. The tasks are highly manual and require staff to update system of records with beneficial owner information.
Issue Rating Justification: The finding has high risk impact to the business and Block and is tied to specific BSA AML regulatory requirements.
Issue Action Plans: Beneficial Owner Information is not updated for loans (1130811)
Closure Request Justification:
GCMT obtained and inspected remediation evidence to demonstrate completion of the action plan. Refer to the attached links for a summary of validation procedures performed. Action plan complete pending GCMT validation testing. 
 

 12/10/24 Update: Refer to the action plan 1130811 for closure details.

Issue Title: One ACH exception was not processed timely, and the related procedure requires an uplift to explain timing requirements
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 Article Three - Rights and Responsibilities of RDFIs and their Receivers, Section 3.8 RDFI's Right to Transmit Return Entries of the NACHA Operating Rules states an RDFI must transmit a return entry to its ACH Operator by the ACH Operator's deposit deadline for the return entry to be made available to the ODFI no later than the opening of business on the second banking day following the settlement date of the original entry. 
 

 Testing Result:
 GCMT assessed the ACH Returns process and observed the following: 
 - For 1 of 5 samples reviewed, a debit ACH entry in the amount of $12.08 was returned greater than 2 business days following the settlement date of the original entry; and  
 - Related procedures do not describe the timing requirements for ACH returns.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: The root cause stems from failure to perform the task timely. In addition, the procedure was did not clearly communicate the timing requirements to process the ACH transaction.
Issue Rating Justification: The process is not guided by a regulation but rather NACHA Operating rules. One exception was identified which is deemed an isolated incident.
Issue Action Plans: One ACH exception was not processed timely, and the related procedure requires an uplift to explain timing requirements (1131443)

Issue Title: The Physical payments procedure does not align with policy requirements to process payments when received, and payment processing procedures are not followed.
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 SFS Payments Policy, pages 5-6 states physical payments must be applied effectively as of the date the check was received. When the Bank processes any payment towards a plan, it will notify the customer of the credit timely. 
 SFS Physical Payments SOP, page 7 states all physical payments that have been received and are identifiable must be applied to the loan of the corresponding account within 5 business days from the “initial value date” depending on the check amount. For check payments > $5,000.00, wait 14 days to confirm that the payment has been successfully processed. Also, page 18 states a returned payment notice should be sent via Salesforce if the physical payment fails. 
 

 Testing Result:
 The design of the payments process does not comply with policy requirements. There is a waiting period to process physical payments to confirm payments are successful. However, physical payments that are not processed when received can not be applied effectively as of the date received due to a system limitation. 
 

 Also, GCMT reviewed a sample of loan payments and observed the following: 
 a. 5 of 10 samples were not processed timely within the SLA defined in the SOP; 
 b. 2 of 10 samples were not sent a notice of timely credit as stated in the policy; 
 c. 1 of 10 samples was not sent a returned payment notice, although the physical payment failed; and 
 d. 2 of 10 samples were not sent a notice of credit refund when the physical payment resulted in an overpayment.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: The root cause is a process or product/service design weakness due to physical payments not being applied as of the date they are received, physical payments are not being applied within the SLA, and notices are not being provided.
Issue Rating Justification: The issue is rated as Medium due to non-key elements of internal policies and procedures not being met.
Issue Progress Updates: See "Closure Details" tab and attached transaction testing and control enhancements evidence.
Issue Action Plans: The Physical payments procedure does not align with policy requirements to process payments when received, and payment processing procedures are not followed. (1131449)
Closure Request Justification: GCMT obtained and assessed evidence demonstrating enhancements to the control. GCMT performed validation testing over the physical check monthly audit. GCMT confirmed, management is conducting the monthly audit as required to ensure policy and procedures are followed. There were no exceptions noted from the testing performed. GCMT concludes the action plan is complete and the risk and issue are remediated.

Issue Title: SFS Right to Financial Privacy SOP requires an uplift
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 The SFS Right to Financial Privacy Act standard operating procedure does not align with the RFPA policy. Procedures that are not reflective of current processes can create potential operational and compliance risks.
 

 Testing Result:
 GCMT noted the Right to Financial Privacy Act SOP does not align with the policy and contains outdated references as follows:
 •The minimum required information that must be maintained for each request is not described in the SOP (i.e., date of the request, name of the government authority, identification of the records disclosed); 
 •The RFPA applicability requirements are not described in the SOP (i.e., come from the federal government, apply to an individual person or a partnership with five or fewer people, seek confidential financial information);
 •The SOP does not describe records applicable to SFS that do not qualify as a protected record ( i.e., records relating to an account established under a fictitious name); and 
 •References to former RFPA DRI Arvon Perteet should be removed.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Root cause stems from weaknesses in procedures. (Process related)
Issue Rating Justification: The issue is strictly procedure related and there were no errors identified as a result of the needed updates. There is low risk impact to the business unit.
Issue Action Plans: SFS Right to Financial Privacy SOP requires an uplift (1131456)
Closure Request Justification: Action plan owner provided RFPA SOP, link attached to the Action Plan Details. Action plan is considered remediated. No exceptions noted.

Issue Title: Cash App does not add the cash value of a Gift Card to a recipient's Cash App Balance, when they do not accept a Gift Card within 30 days
Issue Description:
The Cash App Terms of Service, Gift Cards Section (6) states: "If the Recipient does not accept the Gift Card within 30 days of receipt, the cash value of the Gift Card will be added to their Cash App Balance."
 

 GCMT confirmed that Gift Cards, if not accepted by the recipient within 30 days, do NOT get added to the recipient’s Cash App balance. As configured, there is currently no time limit for how long a gift card payment can stay at "WAITING_ON_RECIPIENT". 
 

 As the design of the control does not align with the Cash App Terms of Service, GCMT determined that this may give rise to being a ‘deceptive’ practice as defined under the Dodd-Frank Act. 
 A representation, omission, actor practice is deceptive when
 (1) The representation, omission, act, or practice misleads or is likely to mislead the consumer;
 (2) The consumer’s interpretation of the representation, omission, act, or practice is reasonable
 under the circumstances; and
 (3) The misleading representation, omission, act, or practice is material.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Electronic Funds Transfer Act (Regulation E) Subpart A (1023304)
Issue Rating Justification: Confirmed high-risk given regulatory area, as confirmed with Legal as well.
Issue Action Plans:
Short term retroactive fix for Cash App Gift Cards (1132102)
 Long term solution for Cash App Gift Cards (1132105)

Issue Title: One stop payment request of a preauthorized transfer was not honored timely
Issue Description:
Requirement / Regulatory Guidance:
 Electronic Funds Transfer Act - Regulation E § 1005.10 Preauthorized transfers. 
 (c) Consumer's right to stop payment —
 (1) Notice. A consumer may stop payment of a preauthorized electronic fund transfer from the consumer's account by notifying the financial institution orally or in writing at least three business days before the scheduled date of the transfer.
 

 Testing Results: In one instance, the customer made a request to stop a preauthorized transfer at least three days prior to the payment due date and we did not honor this request in a timely manner causing the payment to be processed.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Per the Issues Management Program (May 2024), People: Human performance (action or inaction), training deficiencies, or undefined / poorly defined roles and responsibilities resulting in errors, control breakdowns, customer complaints, or operational losses.
Issue Rating Justification: Per the Issues Management Program (May 2024), meets the definition of low: a "one-off exception" is identified that does not appear indicative of a broken process / control.
Issue Action Plans: Training Refresh / SLA Updates (1132110)
Closure Request Justification:
The actions taken to remediate have been completed and validated. The team has enacted new processes to ensure the timely processing of stop payments by:
 - creating and prioritizing a dedicated queue
 - training all relevant employees on the new process while reiterating the importance of Reg E timelines, and
 - adding additional staff (headcount)
 All actions taken meet the stated action plan and add a strong control to respond and honor stop payments when requested 3 days prior to the scheduled payment date.

Issue Title: Sanctions Screening not Occurring due to Name Field Validation Failures (SQ)
Issue Description:
In connection with monthly sanctions screening reporting provided to one of Cash App’s bank partners, Cash Compliance Analytics (CAX) identified a population of Cash App customers that were not being rescreened in accordance with Block’s Global Sanctions Compliance Policy and regulatory expectations. This triggered additional investigation by Foundational Compliance Engineering (FCE), which confirmed that the issue also impacts Square customers. As a result of this issue, there is a population of customers who maintain accounts with Square but are not being screened to ensure they are not subject to sanctions.
 

 It was identified that the Rescreenable Flag in BadActors, which determines whether customer information is sent for rescreening, is set to False for some customers. Additional investigation revealed that the Rescreenable Flag was set to False because of validation failures arising from data provided in the name field of the screening request. Specifically, upstream systems are sending names that are failing validation by our screening vendor, Lexis. These validation failures are the result of one or more of the following scenarios: 
 The name is too short, including instances where no last name is provided.
 The name contains an emoji;
 The name contains non-standard characters;
 The name contains only symbols like ... or ---,.
 

 While initially the issue was identified in the context of rescreens, the underlying root cause (failed name validation) also applies to initial screens.
RCA Description: Process: Customers are allowed to create and maintain accounts using names that are either invalid or incomplete.The screening process was not designed to ensure upstream systems sending screening requests to BadActors are only sending data that can be accepted by our screening vendor (e.g., full legal name, no emojis, etc.) This results in upstream systems sending invalid screening requests to BadActors.
Impacted Countries:
Canada
 United Kingdom
 France
 Ireland
 Spain
 Japan
 Australia
 United States
Impacted Legal Entities:
Square Canada, Inc.
 Squareup Europe Ltd.
 Squareup International Ltd.
 Square K.K.
 Square AU Pty. Ltd.
 Block, Inc.
 Square Capital, LLC
 Square Payroll, Inc
Issue Rating Justification: This issue is impacting the screening of our customers against sanctions watchlists, which is a regulatory expectation. The failure to screen our customer presents heightened regulatory risks as it prevents us from confirming that we do not maintain accounts on our platform with sanctioned parties.
Issue Progress Updates:
Action Plan is being assessed and will be added at a later date by the Issue Owner. 
 

 At initial upload, this issue was thought to impact both Square and Square Financial Services (SFS), however, upon further review it was determined that SFS is not impacted and should be removed from scope of issue. See hyperlinked document for rationale.
Issue Action Plans:
Goal 1: Forward Fix Implementation (1322853)
 Goal 2: Implement Upstream Monitoring for Screening Failures (1322857)
 Goal 3a: Remediation Process: Reporting (1323502)
 Goal 3b: Remediation of Population (1323511)
Closure Request Justification:
Supporting documentation and evidence supporting closure of all 4 APs as well as sustainability has been uploaded to Protecht. 
 

 Issue has been approved for closure on 02/17/2026.

Issue Title: Policies and Procedures: Gaps in the current SAR Procedure
Issue Description:
Suspicious Activity Procedure
 POCA requires regulated firms to make a report to the authorities where they know, suspect, or have reasonable grounds for knowing or suspecting that a person is attempting or engaged in money laundering or terrorist financing.
 

 Additionally, Chapter 6 of the JMLSG Guidance notes that “...all staff must raise an internal report where they have knowledge or suspicion, or where there are reasonable grounds for having knowledge or suspicion, that another person is engaged in money laundering. The firm’s nominated officer must consider all internal reports and make an external report to the NCA (National Crime Agency) as soon as is practicable if he/she considers that there is knowledge, suspicion, or reasonable grounds for knowledge and suspicion.”
 

 BDO’s review found that the ‘SEL Square Suspicious Activity Reporting Guidelines and Procedures’
 (‘SAR Procedure’) provides a detailed overview of the financial crime framework in place at SEL, however our review noted the following gaps in the current SAR Procedure:
 - the current SAR Procedure does not include any reference to POCA requirements or the requirements from the Money Laundering Regulations in relation to the offences and submitting SARs.
 - under the current framework, suspicious activity is reported to Compliance to review and to conduct the investigation, once this has been completed and concluded that a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) needs to be reported to the NCA, it is then escalated to the MLRO to submit. This approach would not be considered to be in line with legal and regulatory expectations, in particular s330 of POCA requires SEL to ensure that where an employee has grounds for knowledge or suspicion that a person or customer is engaged in, or attempting, money laundering or terrorist financing, they must report this to the nominated officer or their alternate. Additionally, Regulation 21(5) of the MLR requires the nominated officer to consider each report from an employee and determine if it gives grounds
 for knowledge or suspicion. In discussion with SEL’s MLRO, it was advised that Compliance will review the suspicious activity and submit a report to the MLRO with their investigation and suggested outcome, the MLRO will review the report and conduct their own investigation and determine whether the activity requires reporting to the NCA. However, this approach does not align with the documented approach in the SAR Procedure.
 -the SAR Procedure does not clarify that once an employee has reported their suspicion in an appropriate manner to the Nominated Officer, or to an individual to whom the Nominated Officer has delegated the responsibility, they have fully satisfied his statutory obligation.
 - the SAR Procedure does not clarify that where an internal SAR has been raised to the Nominated Officer, until the Nominated Officer advises the member of staff that no report to the NCA is to be made, further transactions or activity in respect of that customer should be reported to the Nominated Officer as they arise.
 - Additionally, the SAR procedure makes reference to DAML SARs, but it does not define what a DAML SAR is.
Issue Progress Updates:
It is recommended SEL:
 

 27.1 enhances the SAR Procedure to include specific references to POCA and the Money Laundering Regulations to outline the offences and the requirement to submit a SAR.
 

 27.2 Updates the SAR Procedure to outline the current reporting process that all possible suspicious activity is reported to the Nominated Officer/MLRO to review and conduct the investigation and ultimately determine if a SAR needs to be filed with the
 NCA.
 

 27.3 updates the SAR Procedure to include operational requirements, including:
 - clarifying that once an employee has reported their suspicion to the Nominated Officer, they have satisfied their statutory obligation;
 - clarifying that where an internal SAR has been raised to the Nominated Officer, until the Nominated Officer advises the member of staff that no report to the NCA is to be made, further transactions or activity in respect of that customer should be reported to the Nominated Officer as they arise;
 

 27.4 Update the SAR Procedure to outline the definition of a DAML SAR and in what circumstances a DAML would be required.
 

 Note: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces
Issue Action Plans:
Enhance SAR Procedure to include regulatory references (1134549)
 Update SAR procedures to reflect current reporting processes (1134551)
 Update SAR procedures to include operational requirements (1134553)
 Update SAR procedures to include DAML SARs (1134555)

Issue Title: Policies and Procedures: Gaps in the current sanctions framework
Issue Description:
Sanctions Policies
 

 Although screening is not a legal requirement in its own right, firms are expected to have effective screening systems which are commensurate to the size, complexity and risk profile of their businesses.
 

 The FCA, in its Financial Crime Guide to Countering Financial Crime Risk notes that “Although screening itself is not a legal requirement, screening new customers...helps to ensure that firms will not breach the sanctions regime.”
 

 There are a number of Global sanctions policies and procedures in place which outline the approach the Group takes to ensure sanctions compliance globally. The current policies and procedures include the:
 - Global Economic Sanctions Policy;
 - Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) Global Policy; and
 - Sanctions Compliance – Square Standard Operating Procedure
 

 BDO’s review noted the following gaps in the current sanctions’ framework:
 - Is heavily focused on US requirements.
 - There is no reference to His Majesty's Treasury (HMT), The Office of Financial Sanctions Implementation (OFSI), the EU
 or United Nations sanctions.
 - It does not outline who in the UK is responsible for reporting possible sanctions breaches and when the MLRO should be notified of a possible breach.
 - It does not clarify that where funds have been frozen or where the Firm has knowledge or a suspicion that the financial sanctions measures have been or are being contravened, or that a customer is a listed person or entity, or a person acting on behalf of a listed person or entity, the Firm must report to OFSI, and it may also need to consider whether it must report to the NCA.
 - it does not state that that a failure to maintain the freezing of funds for a sanctioned person or entity opens them to prosecution.
 

 As outlined in SEL’s current framework, all policies and procedures should be reviewed on a least an annual basis and approved by the Board of Directors. BDO’s review noted that the following sanctions policies and procedures have not been updated within the last 12 months:
 - The Global Economic Sanctions Policy was last updated in September 2021; and
 - The OFAC Global Policy was last updated in May 2020.
Issue Progress Updates:
Recommendations:
 

 28.1 review and update current framework to reflect the UK requirements; and
 

 28.2 outline who is responsible for reporting sanctions breaches to OFSI. clarifying that where funds have been frozen or where the Firm has knowledge or a suspicion that the financial sanctions measures have been or are being contravened, or that a customer is a listed person or entity, or a person acting on behalf of a listed person or entity, the Firm must report to OFSI, and it may also need to consider whether it must report to the NCA.
 

 28.3 ensure that all policies and procedures are reviewed on an annual basis, as outlined in the current framework.
 

 Note: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces
Issue Action Plans:
Update framework to reflect UK requirements (1134557)
 Outline OSFI reporting responsibilities (1134559)
 Ensure annual review of policies and procedures (1134561)
Closure Request Justification: Note: Subir Devaiah is identified as action owner as the original owner (Eric Medoff) is no longer employed at Block.

Issue Title: Financial Crime Governance Structures: Lack of evidence that senior management are actively engaged in the firm’s approach to addressing the risks
Issue Description:
Chapter 2 of the FCA Financial Crime Guide states that senior management are expected to take clear responsibility for managing financial crime risks, which should be treated in the same manner as other risks faced by the business. There should be evidence that senior management are actively engaged in the firm’s approach to addressing the risks.
 

 ‘The SquareUp Europe Limited’s Approach to Compliance’ seeks to outline how the Firm has implemented a three lines of defence model. The document states that senior Leadership is accountable for SEL’s overall management of Regulatory Compliance Risk and ensuring a “tone from the top.” The CCO holds overall responsibility for the assessment and oversight of Senior Leadership’s management of Regulatory Compliance Risks. The CCO also reports on material instances of non-compliance, compliance issues and oversees measures taken by the business to remediate issues or implement new or revised controls to both the SEL Board and the RGC.
 

 SquareUp Europe Limited – Terms of Reference of the Board of Directors November 2019
 

 BDO’s review noted that the terms of reference (‘ToR’) for the Board of Directors does not specifically make reference to the Board being ultimately responsible for financial crime risks. The ToR states that the Board is responsible for:
 - Ensuring that the Company maintains a sound system of audit, risk management, compliance, and internal control, including effective and efficient operations, internal financial controls and compliance with applicable law and regulation; and
 - Reviewing, approving, and guiding the Company’s strategy, major plans of action, risk appetite and policies, annual budgets, and business plans; setting performance objectives for management; ensuring the efficacy of internal controls; monitoring
 strategy implementation and corporate performance; and overseeing major capital expenditures and major contracts.
 

 The Skilled Person reviewed the agendas from the last four Board Meetings and observed that the MLRO provides a compliance update for the Board of Directors covering AML and Complaints. Additionally, we observed that:
 

 - In Q4 2021 the MLRO presented the Annual MLRO Report for review and approval;
 - In Q1 2022 the MLRO presented the SEL AML & CTF Program for review and approval and the annual independent testing and monitoring for review; and
 - In Q2 2022 the MLRO presented the Global AML & Sanctions Risk Assessment Framework for review.
 

 BDO’s review of the Board Minutes provided, noted that whilst they captured the MLRO presenting the items listed above, they do not capture any discussion or challenge of the information by the Board. Additionally, the Board Minutes from the last four meetings do not capture discussion around SEL’s risk management process in respect of financial crime risk and the appropriateness of the current control framework to manage the risks.
 

 The MI provided to the Skilled Person included data relating to Compliance’s operational risk for the quarter, and provides high level data on the number of cases created in respect of:
 

 - Watchlist (i.e., PEP, sanctions, and adverse media)
 - Transaction monitoring
 - SAR Reviews
 - Know Your Customer
 - Enhanced Due Diligence
 - Compliance Escalation
 

 The Skilled Person noted that whilst MI on the number of cases dealt with in the period, no further detail is provided on the performance of the financial crime framework and whether it remains appropriate for SEL.
 

 As part of this review the Skilled Person attended the Board Committee meeting on 21 September 2022 and observed that financial crime issues were not openly discussed by the members. The data provided to the Board highlighted a significant increase in the number of SAR reviews (increase of 194% compared to the previous quarter), but the Board pack only included a brief explanation as to why there was a significant increase in SAR escalations. There are further increases in change across the watchlist alerts (13%), transaction monitoring alerts (12%), KYC issues (13%) and compliance escalation (20%); but no commentary as to the reason for this increase, or whether any actions were proposed to reduce the risks identified.
Issue Action Plans:
Ensure Board minutes accurately capture information provided to Board (1134563)
 Enhance Management Information provided to Board (1134565)
 Provide overview of financial crime risks (1134567)
