Issue Title: Compliance and Assurance Testing: Second line testing frequency and remediation tracking
Issue Description:
Compliance Monitoring Framework
 

 Regulation 19(3)(e) of MLR 2017 requires firms to monitor and manage compliance with the systems and controls set out within the AML Policy and Handbook.
 

 Section 2.2.7 FCA FCG states “firms should ensure that its approach to reviewing the effectiveness of financial crime controls is comprehensive”
 

 BDO’s review found that SEL’s business model is almost wholly outsourced and relies heavily on Block and Group systems and controls to manage and monitor SEL’S financial crime risks. Under the current structure, the Global Policy and Governance team supports SEL in a number of functions, including testing and monitoring of compliance controls across all SEL products and services. Under the current framework, the Independent Compliance Testing & Monitoring team, within the Global Policy and Governance team, are responsible for providing assurance to the MLRO in SEL.
 

 Based on discussions with the Head of the Independent Compliance Testing & Monitoring team, who joined Block in September, we understand that there has been a programme of work in place for Block and over the next few months this will be reviewed and expanded where required. Under the current process, we understand that financial crime is subject to a review on an annual basis.
 

 Whilst we understand financial crime is subject to a review on an annual basis as part of the Independent Compliance Testing and Monitoring team’s annual plan, the Skilled Person believes financial crime specific monitoring and testing reviews should be conducted on a more frequent basis and should be informed by the results of the BWRA, QC results, and any external factors such as change of regulations.
 

 The Head of the Independent Compliance Testing & Monitoring team advised that under the current process any issues identified during a review are initially discussed with the relevant business line and any recommendations stemming from that will be discussed and agreed with the business line. After the testing is completed, a risk matrix is prepared and presented to the relevant stakeholders. This is then followed by a draft report with the issues documented and then the issues are tracked with the stakeholders till closure. However, we note that the ICTM team are not always responsible for ensuring that the agreed actions have been closed within the agreed timeframe.
 

 The MLRO, in his interview, advised that he receives fortnightly results of the Quality Control (‘QC’) process, which is completed by the relevant fist line teams. However, there is no additional Compliance Monitoring Plan (‘CMP’) in place at SEL.
Issue Progress Updates:
Recommendations:
 

 32.1 ensures that the Firm’s approach to compliance testing is appropriately documented, including those that are outsourced to Group entities.
 

 32.2 introduces a financial crime compliance monitoring and testing plan, that specifically covers SEL’s financial crime framework including its systems and controls, policies and procedures, risk assessments, and data feeds. On an annual basis, 2LoD should plan the monitoring and testing review to be conducted for the forthcoming year, and use the results of the BWRA, results of QC tests, and external factors such as change in regulations etc to inform the plan.
 

 32.3 ensures that the results of the QC process, the BWRA results and any regulatory changes are considered and factored into the monitoring plan.
 

 Note: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces
Issue Action Plans:
Ensure compliance testing program is appropriately documented (1134573)
 Introduce a financial crimes testing and monitoring program (1134575)
 Update monitoring plan to include QC results, BWRA results, and regulatory changes (1134577)

Issue Title: Compliance and Assurance Testing: Compliance Monitoring Output
Issue Description:
Compliance Monitoring Output
 

 Under the current design of the CMP, the scope of any reviews to be completed, the areas to be covered and the frequency of the testing is designed by the Independent Compliance Testing and Monitoring Team and not by the Compliance Officer and MLRO at SEL.
 

 Based on discussions with both the Head of the Independent Compliance Testing and Monitoring Team and the MLRO, we understand that the MLRO would be involved in discussions with the development of the CMP and any reviews that have been scheduled, however the regulatory universe and high-risk areas to be monitored are determined by the Independent Compliance Testing and Monitoring Team.
 

 During Q1 2022, Block’s Global Independent Testing & Monitoring (‘ITM’) team undertook a review of SEL’s AML framework and sought to test the internal control framework against regulatory requirements, internal policies, and procedures, and assess the adequacy of internal controls for the 12 months to 31 December 2021. The review focused on:
 

 - Customer screening and transaction monitoring;
 - Suspicious Activity Reporting;
 - Bulk closures;
 - Know Your Customer requirements;
 - PEP screening;
 - Identity verification; and
 - Management Information
 

 The review focused on control and transactional testing across the SEL customer population. The Skilled Person notes that at the time of the ITM review, SEL had 34,437 customers but the sample size selected was only 60 customers which amounted to less than 0.2% of SEL’s registered customer base. Although this number appears low, this is in-line with SEL’s approach to use nonstatisical sampling due to size of the Firm’s outputs. For example, if the compliance risk is very high or high, SEL will select a minimum sample size of 60.
 

 BDO’s review of the Independent Testing and Monitoring Compliance Review Report found that it does not provide an overview of the control testing that was completed as part of the review and the results of the review. Additionally, the report does not provide an overview of the overall effectiveness of the control framework in place at SEL. 
 

 The Final Results Matrix that was prepared as part of the review only identifies one enhancement opportunity for SEL to implement. BDO’s review noted that the Results Matrix does not include any reference to the design and control effectiveness that was completed as part of the review but simply states that “ITM did not observe any material adverse results throughout the course of this independent compliance review”.
 

 Finally, BDO requested the underlying data of the controls tested by ITM, for BDO to assess the effectiveness of the ITM team but including the sampled files and alerts within control testing sample. However, BDO was not provided with this information, and was unable to appropriately assess the effectiveness of the ITM team. However, given the number of design findings identified by the Skilled Person compared to the ITM team, BDO can conclude that the ITM review was ineffective. Upon the issuance of the draft report, SEL provided the Skilled Person with the ‘Final Testing Workbook’ that demonstrated the tests completed, including:
 

 - a gap analysis against SEL’s procedures and the MLR 17; and
 - control testing on KYC, transaction monitoring, and SARs. 
 

 Whilst the workpaper does contain good levels of detail on the testing approaching and checks completed as part of the testing, ITM identified no issues whilst conducting the gap analysis or completing the testing and therefore, BDO continue to conclude the testing was ineffective. For example, SEL’s ‘Final Testing Workbook’ has assessed its procedure framework to be in-line with SAR reporting requirements as per POCA and the MLR 2017, however, as per finding 27, the Skilled Person identified a number of gaps in relation to the Firm’s SAR Procedure, including:
 

 - the current SAR Procedure does not include any reference to POCA requirements or the requirements from the Money Laundering Regulations in relation to the offences and submitting SARs; and
 - the SAR Procedure does not clarify that once an employee has reported their suspicion in an appropriate manner to the Nominated Officer, or to an individual to whom the Nominated Officer has delegated the responsibility, they have fully satisfied his statutory obligation.
 

 A further example is ITM identified that SEL’s procedures made reference to complying with Regulation 18 of the MLR 17 and whilst the Skilled Person does not disagree with this assessment, the Skilled Person has identified gaps and provided recommendations to improve the output of the AML and Sanctions Risk Assessment as per recommendation F34.
 

 Finally, the Skilled Persons identified SEL’s ‘Square Anti-Money Laundering and Counter Terrorist Financing Program SquareUp Europe’ Procedure lacks specificity as to how SEL are complying with the UK regulatory framework, for example, the ‘Program Document’ includes detail on PEPs, but does not specifically state EDD is required, and as part of the EDD process, SEL must obtain evidence of Source of Funds (’SoF’). However, the ‘Final Testing Workbook’ has concluded the procedure is in-line with Regulation 35 of the MLR 17.
Issue Progress Updates:
Recommendations:
 

 33.1 ensures that the sample size selected is an appropriate reflection of the customer base.
 

 33.2 ensures that all independent monitoring that is conducted clearly outlines the control testing completed and the results of the control testing.
 

 33.3 provides an overview of the overall effectiveness of the control framework in place at SEL.
 

 33.4 Ensures the results, include reference to the design and control effectiveness of the control framework subject to the review.
 

 Note: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces
Issue Action Plans:
Ensure appropriate sample size (1134579)
 Ensure monitoring outlines control testing and results (1134581)
 Provide an overview of the overall effectiveness of the control framework in place at SEL (1134583)
 Ensure results include reference to design and control effectiveness (1134585)

Issue Title: Global AML & Sanctions Risk Assessment Framework Output Deficiencies
Issue Description:
Global AML & Sanctions Risk Assessment Framework Output 
 

 Although the Global AML & Sanctions Risk Assessment Framework outlines how the Residual Risk and Control environment is assessed as part of the BWRA process, the review noted that within the current iteration of the BWRA, which was approved by the MLRO in June 2022, the overall assessment of the controls within each of the categories outlined above has not been documented within the BWRA. 
 

 Further, BDO’s review noted that the BWRA includes limited use of quantitative metrics when assessing either the inherent or residual financial crime risk posed to SEL. On review of the BWRA, the Skilled Person identified the following issues: 
 

 - A number of inherent risks have been included that do not align with the current business model, for example: 
 o Risk 30 - 34 – does the product accept physical cash transactions. As SEL is not a cash-based business the Skilled Person questions if it is necessary to include this within the BWRA. 
 

 - BDO’s review noted that a number of the inherent risks that have been identified by SEL are what we consider to be a residual risk after the control has been applied to mitigate the perceived inherent risk, for example: 
 o Risk 28 – does the product allow a customer to remain anonymous (ownership can be obscured) and the threshold has been listed as LOW - “Full IDV & KYC required before the customer can use the product”.
 o Risk 64 – are transactional limits in place for the product and the threshed has been listed as LOW - “Conservative limits in place and cannot be circumvented”.
 o Risk 83 – does the product collect the source of funds from the customer? and the threshold has been listed as MODERATE - “SoF is not collected up-front but may be triggered based on customer’s activity or an information request”.
 - A number of the inherent risks identified within the BWRA
 have not selected a response to the thresholds that have been included in the document, for example: 
 o Risk 22 
 o Risk 47 
 o Risk 64 
 o Risk 93 
 o Risk 98 
 o Risk 102 
 o Risk 124
 - In respect of control effectiveness, a number of the risks identified in relation to non-payment processing, the
 threshold identifies a strong, weak or adequate threshold to assess the control, but no threshold has been selected: o Risk 504 o Risk 516 o Risk 517 o Risk 532
 

 Finally, BDO’s review noted that the control environment was assessed as strong where it was determined that a control exists, is fully documented, and appears to be performing as designed, no additional supporting documentation was provided to the Skilled Person to illustrate how each control was assessed as performing as designed. Additionally, where controls have been identified as being weak, there is no evidence within the BWRA or supporting documents of the actions to be taken to address the weakness identified, the action owner or action plan.
Issue Progress Updates:
Recommendations:
 

 34.1 ensures that the overall assessment of the controls within each of the categories outlined has been documented within the BWRA.
 

 34.2 ensures that the BWRA documents how the effectiveness of each control has been assessed.
 

 34.3 includes both qualitative and quantitative data as part of the BWRA process.
 

 34.4 considers completing a risk assessment on a product-by-product basis, which considers the inherent and residual risk posed by each product independently, and ultimately feed that into the overall internet and residual risk rating for SEL.
 

 34.5 ensures that any weaknesses identified in the control framework are assigned an action, and action owner and a timeline for completion.
 

 34.6 ensure that the inherent risks considered as part of the BWRA process fully align to the business model.
 

 34.7 ensure that the inherent risks identified as part of the process are documented as the risk that they would pose to the business before the control environment has been considered.
 

 34.8 Ensure that the thresholds are addressed for each inherent risk and control effectiveness
 

 Note: Subir Devaiah is Issue Owner because the original issue owner (Chaquel Roye) is no longer employed at Block. Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces
Issue Action Plans:
Document overall assessment of controls within BWRA (1134587)
 Ensure that BWRA documents effectiveness of assessment (1134589)
 Include qualitative and quantitative data in BWRA process (1134591)

Issue Title: Financial Crime Risk Appetite: Unclear alignment between RAS and BWRA
Issue Description:
Financial Crime Risk Appetite 
 As part of this review SEL provided the Skilled Person with a copy of the draft SquareUp Europe Limited - Risk Appetite Qualitative Statement. BDO’s review noted the following: 
 

 - the current iteration of the Risk Appetite Statement (’RAS’) states that the Firm “…has no appetite for financial crime risk breaches or incidents that threaten the viability of SEL or that cause significant damage to our reputation, our staff, our clients, counterparties or the markets or communities in which we operate. As such we do not carry out business where the residual financial crime risk is unacceptable…”. Whilst the Firm acknowledges it has no appetite for financial crime risk breaches or incidents that threaten the viability of SEL, there are no KRI’s or KPI’s considered as part of the RAS process. As a result, it is not clear how SEL however it is not clear how SEL monitors and tracks adherence with its RAS on an ongoing bass. There is an expectation that firms will use quantitative data to develop its risk appetite. 
 

 It is not clear how the RAS and BWRA are aligned.

Issue Title: Customer Risk Assessment (CRA) Methodology Deficiencies
Issue Description:
Customer Risk Rating (’CRR’) Model
 

 SEL’s CRR model is used to assess the risk of its Sellers at the point of registration and continuously thereafter.
 

 The attributes consider for the CRR model are as follows:
 - Geography – the jurisdiction / incorporation country of the Seller;
 - Customer Risk – adverse media, transaction monitoring, PEP status, Merchant Category Code (’MCC’), and legal entity structure;
 - Age of the Relationships; and
 - Product risk.
 

 The CRR is calculated on weighted scoring system to determine the most appropriate risk for the Seller. The scoring is as follows:
 

 - Geography – SEL only enters into business with UK incorporated business, UK sole traders and partnerships, and therefore, the risk is considered low; 
 - Customer Risk – PEP status, adverse media, and transaction monitoring (i.e., unusual payment patterns in-line with the Seller’s MCC and unusual timings of payments in-line with the Seller’s MCC) are all high-risk override factors;
 - Customer Risk – MCC code scored from low to high based on the risk of the Seller, and legal structure as follows: 
  o Company – High risk 
  o Sole Proprietor, Limited Partnership – Moderate risk; and
  o General Partnership – Low risk
 - Age of Relationship: 
 o < 90 days – High risk; 
 o >= 90 days and <540 days – Moderate risk; and 
 o >= 540 days – Low risk 
 - Product Risk – Managed Payments – Moderate to High risk.
 

 Whilst the Firm is using a number of expected risk factors to assess its Seller’s risk, the CRR model is missing a number of key factors as follows: 
 - Geographical risk factors do not include assessing the risk in which the Seller operates and the jurisdictions to which its beneficial owner has links to (i.e., if the Ultimate Beneficial Owner (UBO) is a resident in a jurisdiction different to the incorporation country). These are factors considered within Annex 4-II of the JMLSG.
 - Customer risk factors do not include the age of the business, for acquiring services, this can be an indication of how trustworthy the business is (i.e., a corporate entity who has been operating for > 10 years who has audited accounts available in comparison to nearly incorporated entity), and whether there is any indication of a complex structure (i.e., bearer shares, nominee shareholder etc). 
 - Transaction monitoring is used as an override function and is only applicable for the ongoing assessment of customer risk, therefore this is not applied at registration. SEL should include the estimated average value and volume of transaction, and the estimated annual revenue. This will also enhance SEL’s ability to accurate assess and monitor the Seller’s activity on an on-going basis
 - Product risk does not include how payment will be taken by the Seller (i.e., in person or online) and whether payments are to be taken in one location, on-the-go, or at an event.
 

 In addition, the Skilled Person does not agree with SEL’s assessment of legal risk. For example, although predominately for the use of Banks, Wolfsberg guidance and industry expectations, companies (i.e., limited companies) are generally considered medium / moderate risk. Further, the inherent risk of Sole Proprietors, due to lack of legal and business formality, increases the likelihood of such structures to be used as fronts for illegal activity. 
 

 SEL has allocated each risk factor with an assigned weighting based on a variety of factors and guidance provided by the JMLSG, European Banking Authority (’EBA’), and the FCA, as well as industry knowledge and expertise at SEL. The weighting per risk factor are as follows: 
 - Geography – 10%; 
 - Customer Information – MCC – 25% and Legal structure 20% - total of 45%;
 - Age – 15%; and 
 - Product – 30%
 

 Based on SEL’s business model and customer types, the Skilled Person agrees with the weighted factors provided.
 

 The results of the overall assessment will categorise Sellers as either: 
 - high risk; 
 - moderate risk; or 
 - low risk.
 

 The results of the CRR model will indicate the level of due diligence applied, however, only limited due diligence is conducted at the point of registration, as SEL intend to assess customer activity and whether its product will be used before applying due diligence above and beyond identifying and verifying corporate existence and its associated persons. Therefore, the CRR model is executed on a 90-day basis.
 

 Whilst the CRR model has been outlined above, SEL has been in the process of designing and fully integrating a new machine
 learning customer risk model called the Activation Customer Risk Rating (‘aCRR’) model. At the point of conducting the review and receiving the CDD Walkthrough session on 10 October both the CRR and aCRR models where being applied in conjunction with one another, however, during an ad-hoc walkthrough on the aCRR model and ongoing due diligence on the 10th November, the Skilled Person was informed the CRR model had been decommissioned and at the point of registration, the aCRR model was the model being used to assess Seller’s risk.
 

 aCRR
 

 The aCRR model is a machine learning tool that assess customer risk at the point of on-boarding and continuously thereafter as a result of assessing thousands of variables. 
 

 SEL advised the purpose of implementing this model is to enable the Firm to continuously capture a snapshot of a Sellers risk at any point in time, which in turn, would inform the level of due diligence and trigger an alert if there was any change in the Sellers behaviour during the course of the business relationship.
 

 Whilst the Skilled Person has not been provided with a list of all the variables and factors considered to assess a Sellers risk, we understand this will include factors such as: 
 - location and time zone based on the IP address;  self-attested information during the registration process;
 - identification and verification information;  any negative new identified on the Seller’s IP and email addresses provided;
 - where there is any transaction processed out of normal working hours for the Seller;
 - historical transaction activity based on signal from customers sharing the same IP address; and
 - social media / web presence / negative news hits
 

 The idea behind the model is that traditional customer risk assessments will group customers as either low, moderate, or high risk based on inherent risk ratings, without assessing any positive or negative behaviours that will, as a result, change the risk of the customer to be outcome based by assessing a number of variables (i.e., a low risk customer does not necessarily mean the customer poses no risk to SEL, and a high-risk customer may not pose as much risk as originally considered.) As such, the aCRR model will not ‘bin’ customers into risk categories, rather Sellers will be rated on a scale of zero to one, with zero indicating minimal risk and one, high risk, the final outcomes are as follows: 
 - Nuclear: The highest scoring sellers (model scores above the Nuclear threshold) will be denied activation;
 - Elevated: Sellers with model scores below the Nuclear threshold but above the Elevated threshold will be allowed to successfully activate and begin processing, but will be sent to agents for review under specific business situations; and
 - Typical: Sellers with model scores below the Elevatedcthreshold will be allowed to successfully activate andcbegin processing.
 

 Although the Skilled Person has not seen the full list of variablescand factors considered as part of the aCRR, results of globalctesting suggest the model is working as anticipated. For example,cof the Sellers within the highest 1% of the aCRR model score, 98.7% of them eventually received a negative action, and more than 50% negatively actioned Seller had risk scores in the top 2%. The testing results provide evidence the machine learning model is accomplished in identifying the predictability of Sellers that would eventually pose a risk to the SEL.
 

 However, in-line with SEL ‘Square Compliance Activation CRR System Design’ document, it is noted that business type (i.e.,
 MCC) is not a signal that is available to the aCRR model, which at on-boarding, is one of the key factors in assessing a Seller’s risk. BDO also note at the point of conducting this Skilled Persons review, the aCRR model is only applied at on-boarding and not yet implemented on a continuous basis.
 

 Whilst the testing of the aCRR model suggests the model is successful in identifying the probability that a Seller will eventually present a risk to SEL, the Skilled Person questions whether the machine learning tool will be able to accurately capture the business risk of a Seller at the point of on-boarding, as at present, the registration process lacks detailed questions to gather this information (please see Business Relationship subsection below.) However, as noted, the aCRR model has only been in operation at the point of on-boarding, in isolation for a short period of time, and the Skilled Person has not been able to review the variables and factors that form part of the machine learning model, and therefore, are unable to comment on the true adequacy and effectiveness of the tool.
 

 Merchant Category Risk Assessment
 

 Assessing the risk of the industry in which a Seller operates in, is an important aspect of assessing Seller’s risk in-line with SEL’s business model.
 

 As such, SEL has assessed 162 MCC and risk categorised them from low to high. Whilst for the most part, the Skilled Person agrees with SEL’s risk categorisation, we believe the following should have their risk ratings increased:
 - Food stores convenience stores and specialty markets – low;
 - Restaurants – low;
 - Health and beauty spas – moderate;
 - Tanning salon – low;
 - Hair removal – low.
Issue Action Plans:
Enhance CRR model (1134609)
 Modify risk of sole proprieters and limited companies (1134611)
 Document aCRR variables and factors (1134613)
 Ensure risk factors are considered in CRR model (1134615)
 Increase various MCC risk ratings (1134617)

Issue Title: CDD/EDD: Limited guidance related in KYC and CDD procedures
Issue Description:
Identification and Verification 
 Regulation 28(3) of the MLR requires where a customer is a body corporate, firms must obtain and verify: i. the name of the body corporate; ii. its company number or other registration number; iii. the address of its registered office, and if different, its principal place of business; In addition, firms must take reasonable measures to determine and verify: i. the law to which the body corporate is subject, and its constitution (whether set out in its articles of association or other governing documents); ii. the full names of the board of directors (or if there is no board, the members of the equivalent management body) and the senior persons responsible for the operations of the body corporate. Where the customer is beneficially owned by another person, firms must: a) identify the beneficial owner; b) take reasonable measures to verify the identity of the beneficial owner so that the relevant person is satisfied that it knows who the beneficial owner is; and c) if the beneficial owner is a legal person, trust, company, foundation, or similar legal arrangement take reasonable measures to understand the ownership and control structure of that legal person, trust, company, foundation, or similar legal arrangement. Limited Companies In respect of identifying the Merchant, and its associated individuals, for companies, upon entering its CHRN all relevant information is extracted from Companies House and prepopulated into the registration form (i.e., Business Name, Beneficial Owner(s), and Directors.) For Beneficial Owners, their name, address, and date of birth is extracted from Companies House, and for Directors, only their name is extracted. Further, the individual completing the registration form is also required to provide personal information (i.e., full name, home address, date of birth, and phone number.) Partnerships and Sole Traders Where a partnership is a general partnership, the approach to conducting identification and verification is as above, however, if the partnership falls outside of a general partnership, SEL require the following information to complete identification and verification checks:  business name;  business address;  ownership structure o 100% owned; o One or several owners own 25% or more; or o No owner owns 25% or more.  Full name, address, and date of birth of any beneficial owner. The same questions as above are a requirement for sole trader also. In the event SEL is unable to identify the existence of a beneficial owner or director at the point of registration, this triggers a case within Regulator for a manual review and in the event this information is missing , a Request for Information (’RFI’) is sent to the Merchant to provide the details. The account is deactivated until the Merchant is able to provide information on the beneficial owners and directors. In addition to the points raised above, the Skilled Person identified the follows gaps:  there is limited guidance provided within SEL’s KYC and CDD Procedures in respect of manual verification of corporate entities and their ownership structures would be conducted if electronical identification and verification fails (i.e., if Companies House has incorrect beneficial ownership information, what SEL would constitute adequate documentation to verify the true ownership structure);  the KYC and CDD Procedures lack guidance in providing specific definition of beneficial owners with respect to different corporate formations (i.e., limited companies, partnerships, and charitable trusts); and  whilst establishing a relationship with a Seller whose shares are issued in bearer form, or where a nominee shareholder exists is unlikely based on SEL’s customer base, the KYC and CDD Procedures do not outline whether SEL would enter into a business relationship in such situations and if so, the specific CDD requirements applied to both situations. Individual Identification and Verification SEL conducts electronic verification on beneficial owners and the applicant (if different to the beneficial owner) via LexisNexis IDU and in order for an individual to pass electronic verification, SEL requires two points of verification via different data sources within IDU as follows:  one source must match on name and residential address; and  the other source must match on either the name and residential address or the name and date of birth. Account holders receive four attempts for pass ID&V and in the event all four attempts fail, the account holder will be provided the option to contact SEL Customer Success Team who will escalate the case to SEL Compliance. In addition to IDU, SEL utilises Trulioo where if electronic verification fails through IDU, SEL Compliance will attempt to seek successful verification via Trulioo. If all attempts of electronic verification fail, SEL Compliance will send an RFI to the account holder to provide proof of identification providing one of the following:  passport;  driving license;  residence permit card; and  national ID card. In addition, the account holder is required to provide a utility bill issued within the past 90 days for address verification. The copy of documents is scrutinised by SEL Compliance to ensure this matches the information provided at the registration process and Onfido is used to verify the validity of identification document provided. Where electronic verification fails, the account will be deactivated until the successful completion of the manual approach outlined above. Whilst SEL’s approach to identifying and verifying individuals is adequate, providing account holders four attempts to pass electronic verification before having to provide manual documentation may increase the risk of successful impersonation. However, SEL provided the Skilled Person the following data in relation to electronic verification attempts:
 - 74.99% complete their enrolment on the first attempt;
 - 95.21% complete their enrolment on the first two attempts; and
 - 98.26% complete their enrolment in the first three attempts.
 

 The figures provided by SEL indicates the fast majority of customers complete electronic verification within two attempts, and therefore, the aforementioned risk is mitigated.
Issue Progress Updates:
Recommendations:
 

 37.1 It is recommended SEL: continues to monitor the attempt success rate for complete identification and verification
 

 Note: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces
Issue Action Plans: Monitor attempt success rate for IDV (1134619)

Issue Title: CDD/EDD: Lack of information to fully understand the Seller
Issue Description:
Nature and Purpose of the Business Relationship 
 Regulation 28 of the MLR 2017 requires firms to assess, and where appropriate obtain information on, the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship or occasional transaction.
 As part of the registration process, SEL requires account holders to state: 
 - the type of business; 
 - business category; 
 - estimated annual revenue; 
 - how payments will be processed (i.e., in store online, at an event).
 

 Whilst this is key information, in order to fully understand the intended nature of business and the expected activity of Sellers, SEL would be required to obtain more information at the registration stage such as: 
 - description of the business (i.e., products and services sold);
 - average value of products and services; 
 - expected value of transaction (either weekly or monthly);
 - expected volume of transactions (either weekly or monthly); and
 - countries of operation (online point of sale only).
 

 Where SEL does not obtain enough information to fully understand the Seller, this may lead to limitations in providing SEL with meaningful information to adequately conduct ongoing monitoring and assess / investigate transaction monitoring alerts.
 Further, post-Sellers completing the identification and verification process outlined in the Identification and Verification sub-section, SEL will delay conducting ‘Business Verification Checks’ (i.e., purpose of the account, business information,
 merchant’s online presence e.g., website, address check, phone number, and the relevance of the applied MCC) until the Seller has actively used SEL’s services over a 90 day period and the level of activity, based on machine learning models will determine if Business Verification Checks are to be conducted (the model is further outlined withing the ongoing monitoring section of this report). Therefore, providing further limitations in SEL’s ability to fully understand the Seller it is establishing business relationships with at the point of on-boarding. During the control testing, we identified: 
 - 13/30 CDD files reviewed had not had business verification checks conducted on the Seller,
 - 4/30 files reviewed the business verification checks were completed either over 90 days relationship age or after the Skilled Person had specifically requested the evidence. The Skilled Person notes that for the 17 CDD files where business verification checks had been completed, this was limited to identifying web presence only.
 

 In contrast, we identified occasions where low risk Sellers had business verification checks completed, outlining an inconsistency in SEL’s approach to conducting business verification checks.
 

 In addition, 5/30 CDD files reviewed, the MCC of the Seller did not accurately align with the Seller’s business model, and 3/5 of the files noted, the MCC was ‘individual / sole trader’ without any further investigation or business verification checks
 completed to truly understand the Seller’s business model / activity.
 

 Whilst the inherent risk of money laundering and terrorist financing is relatively low in respect of merchant acquiring activity in comparison to payment accounts, the business activity of SEL’s sellers presents the highest inherent risk to the business, especially as SEL provides services to small businesses, many of which may not register corporate existence, have an online or public presence, and in conjunction with accepting card payments via SEL’s services, are also cash intensive, it is of imperative importance SEL understand its Seller’s business activity as soon as practically possible. Where SEL is not capturing the appropriate information at the point of on-boarding and conducting business verification checks, it raises questions as to how SEL ensure it fully understands the business relationships in which it is engaging.
 

 Whilst we have noted SEL delays its ‘Business Verification Checks’ the Firm’s sophisticated transaction monitoring / ongoing monitoring tool (as transaction monitoring and ongoing monitoring sub-sections) is capable of identifying unusual activity and behaviour which may prompt the need for ‘Business Verification Checks’ to be completed before the 90-day period. For example, 2/30 CDD files, the ongoing monitoring rules captured the word ‘Charity’ in the Seller’s name, however the MCC selected was not a charity. Another example, in 1/30 CDD files reviewed, the ongoing monitoring tool captured potentia ‘self-payments’ being processed. In all three instances, the account was frozen and an RFI sent to the Seller."
Issue Progress Updates:
Recommendations:
 

 38.1 Enhance its registration process to capture critical information about its Sellers to ensure SEL fully understands the type of Seller it is at on- boarding, and can adequately perform ‘Business Verification Checks’, ongoing monitoring, and investigate transaction monitoring alerts. 
 

 38.2 Conducts business verification checks either at the point of on-boarding or within a shorter time frame than > 90 days post onboarding. 
 

 Note: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces
Issue Action Plans:
Enhance registration process (1134621)
 Conduct business verification sooner than 90 days (1134623)

Issue Title: CDD/EDD: PEP enhancements required
Issue Description:
F.40 Identification of PEPs and Sanctioned Entities 
 Regulation 35(1) of the MLR 2017 states that firms must have in place appropriate risk-management systems and procedures to determine whether a customer or the beneficial owner of a customer is a PEP or a family member or known close associate of a PEP, and to manage the enhanced risks arising from business relationships or transactions with such a customer. Part III of the JMLSG Guidance illustrates that firms should take an approach which is appropriate for their business model, when assessing where and how their business is most likely to encounter sanctioned parties. 
 Within 24 hours of registration, Sellers and its associated persons are screened against appropriate sanctions lists using Lexis Nexis Bridger. SEL will not pay out any funds across its entire product offering until sanction screening is complete. In the event any true sanction matches are identified at the point of registration, the account is deactivated, and this is escalated to the Trade and Sanctions Team. Screening is also conducted against PEP and adverse media / enforcement lists; however, this service is outsourced rather than being conducted in-house. In an interview with the Head of the KYC & Sanctions Team, it was advised the outsourcing arrangement is a legacy approach, and the rationale of which is believed to be the high number of alerts generated at the point of registration. 
 The Head of the KYC & Sanctions team continued to advise that he is comfortable with this approach and the service provided is of high standard. It is also noted SEL conduct in-house re-screening every 30 days post the on-boarding date. Whilst SEL’s approach to identifying whether relationships are established with sanctioned entities or person(s) and PEPs is adequate, where a PEP connection is identified, this is escalated to the MLRO who will assess the severity of the connection and provide guidance on the next steps and the level of EDD to be applied. 
 Although we have established EDD is applied to PEPs as per enhanced due diligence sub-section, SEL do not have a documented PEP materiality assessment to guide the MLRO in determining the risk of each individual PEP case. As part of the control testing of 30 CDD files, 10 files had generated screening alerts that required manual investigation. Of the 10 files with screening alerts:  3/10 identified a true name match which was escalated to compliance for further review. However, despite 2/10 alerts being true matches, SEL has not applied EDD or provided sufficient rationale as to why the alert could be discounted. It is noted for both alerts, the PEPs position was local council. For the remaining alert, the Sellers account was frozen due to ongoing monitoring identifying suspected ‘self-payments’ and therefore, payments were not processed on this account. SEL sent an RFI to the Seller, which included questioning the individuals PEP status. Whilst SEL has taken the right steps to identify the PEP, it is noted the audit trail was updated with additional checks post the Skilled Persons sample request. Too add, the individuals SoW was identified and verified using LinkedIn, which is not an appropriate source to verify wealth; and the individuals SoW was identified and verified using LinkedIn, which is not an appropriate source to verify wealth.
Issue Progress Updates:
Recommendations: 
 

 40.1 Consider whether initial PEP screening is conducted inhouse. For example, considering re-screening is conducted every 30 days post on-boarding, SEL is effectively delaying in-house screening to a later day.
 

 40.2 has a documented approach to assessing the materiality PEP’s pose to the Firm on a case-by-case basis.
 

 40.3 Ensure that where PEPs are being discounted, the investigation and rationale to discount are clearly documented. The importance of strong commentary if enhanced where the alert is a true match but has been discounted due to PEP position and status.
 

 Note: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces
Issue Action Plans:
Consider conducting initial PEP screening inhouse (1134633)
 Update SEL PEP process (1134635)
 Ensure strong documentation in instances where PEPs are discounted (1134637)

Issue Title: CDD/EDD: Implement process to identify change in corporate information
Issue Description:
Ongoing Customer Due Diligence 
 As already noted within this Skilled Person’s report, SEL’s approach to applying customer due diligence is limited at the point of on-boarding (i.e., identification and verification of the Seller’s corporate existence and associated individuals) and then additional due diligence measures are applied based on ongoing monitoring alerts. The two ongoing monitoring alerts have been briefly touched on throughout this report and are: 
  CRR Ongoing; and  GB Pos Business Verification. The first queue includes Sellers that have been categorised as high risk and in the event the relationship is >90 days and the GPV threshold has been breached, EDD will be applied (see EDD subsection for more information). The second queue is for Sellers that have triggered the need for a review to be conducted based on updated results of the CRR model. As outlined within the Customer Risk Section of this Report, transaction activity is an override function within the CRR model, and every 90 days the CRR model is refreshed to assess transaction monitoring results, and machine learning algorithms that assess processing patters (i.e., pattern of payments is not inline with the Seller’s MCC, time of payments is not in-line with the Seller’s MCC and attempts of ‘self-payments.) 
 The idea behind the model is it will be able to identify where Sellers are not behaving in-line with other Seller’s with the same MCC and this, therefore, may suggest wrongdoing or misclassification. SEL’s new aCRR model as detailed within Customer Risk Assessment and EDD section of this Report, will eliminate the above process, and assess Seller’s risk on a continuous day-by-day basis against thousands of variables, assessing the behaviour of the Seller, as well as its activity. Where there is a change in the behaviour of a Seller or its activity, this will trigger the requirement for a manual review to be conducted. This process will run alongside SEL’s transaction monitoring framework and customer screening framework to provide a sophisticated continuous assessment of customer risk on a dayto-day basis. However, as previously noted, it is not expected the aCRR model for continuous monitoring will be implemented towards the end of Q4 2022 This approach, therefore, eliminates the need for SEL to implement the traditional risk based periodic review process. However, both the current and new ongoing monitoring process do not capture change in corporate information (i.e., address, beneficial owners, and directors).
Issue Progress Updates:
Recommendations: 
 

 41.1 Implement a tool / process that is capable of identifying change in corporate information (i.e., address, beneficial owners, and directors) either electronically or manually, to ensure customer information is accurate and up to date.
 

 Note: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces
Issue Action Plans: Ensure merchant information is accurate up to date (1134639)

Issue Title: CDD/EDD: Lack of formal approval process for higher-risk relationships
Issue Description:
Escalation and Sign-off Channels 
 JMLSG 4.62 outlines examples of EDD measures that could be applied for higher risk business relationships, one of which is obtain the approval of senior management to commence or continue the business relationship. 
 Whilst the Skilled Person has established that SEL’s MLRO is responsible for approving the commencement and continuation of PEP relationships, no other relationships that represent higher risk factors such as high-risk MCC or as a result of SEL’s CRR or aCRR require approval of any kind, whether by the MLRO or Head of the KYC and Sanctions team. 
 As SEL has not established a formal approval process for its higher-risk relationships, this does not provide senior management the opportunity to determine whether they are comfortable with the high-risk factors identified and whether this is in-line with SEL’s risk appetite.
Issue Progress Updates:
Recommendations:
 

 42.1 Design and implement a formal approval process for its high-risk business relationships.

Issue Action Plans: Implement formal approval process for high-risk business relationships (1134641)

Issue Title: Transaction Monitoring: Gap in TM coverage to track deviation of expected or actual customer activity
Issue Description:
Transaction Risk Assessment 
 Section 5.7.10 of the JMLSG notes that effective monitoring is likely to be based on a considered identification of transaction characteristics, such as the unusual nature of a transaction, abnormal size or frequency for that customer or peer group, the nature of a series of transactions (for example, a number of cash credits) the geographic destination or origin of a payment (for example, to or from a high-risk country), and the parties concerned: for example, a request to make a payment to or from a person on a sanctions list. JMLSG (ANNEX 5-III) outlines that firms must ensure that the thresholds they set are at a reasonable level and that they have systems in place to identify linked transactions which, taken together, would exceed that threshold. In relation to the governance applied to the management of thresholds, rules, and scenarios the Firm has conducted a risk assessment relating to how different recognised financial crime typologies are mitigated by the Firm’s controls, including the queues used. This assessment is conducted at a global level and includes multiple references to the three queues used in the UK. These three queues are also set globally in response to the typology assessment and are tuned for GBP transactions and the UK market. The Firm’s Document Square (POS_Payroll_Capital) Typology Assessment 2021 contains 143 financial crime red flags from a diverse set of sources. 
 These include the FCA, FATF, FinCEN (the US FIU and AML/CTF banking regulator) and FINTRAC (the Canadian FIU). Each red flag is mapped against a particular typology type with ‘High Risk Jurisdiction – International Cards’ and ‘Profile Deviation (value/volume)’ being two examples. Many of the red flags are covered by the Firm’s TM queues. The Firm’s scenarios are also wide ranging and therefore capture a broad range of activity that could be indicative of many of the red flags listed by the Firm in its assessment. In addition to the list of 143 red flags the Square (POS_Payroll_Capital) Typology Assessment 2021 reviews the Firm’s coverage of the red flags by the Firm’s controls. The 143 red flags are grouped into typologies and analysed by market and activity such as point of sale transactions. Where coverage is missing the Firm has recognised where gaps may exist and referenced a phase 2 implementation programme to enhance coverage. This analysis of risks presented by the typologies is what forms the basis of the Firm’s TM system. For example, the FATF defined red flag of ‘multiple same day transactions’ falls within the broader typology of ‘profile deviation (value/volume) as does the AUSTRAC (the Australian FIU) red flag of ‘high volume of transactions within a short period. Both these risks are addressed by elements within the TM rules and scenarios described below. Another FATF red flag ‘multiple appearances of the same parties in transactions over a short period of time’ is grouped within the Firm’s typology ‘Related parties: Merchants purchasing their own products/services’. This risk is also what the Firm tries to address through components of its ‘gb_pan_concentration’ scenario described below. 
 Overall, the assessment looks at broader controls than just TM, like invoice screening for compliance with OFAC sanctions, but can be considered adequate as a method of evaluating how the Firm’s queues are mapped against and informed by recognised financial crime risks posed to the Firm. Further the assessment recognises that coverage can be improved and so governance of the management of thresholds needs to develop. Drawing upon global organisations like FATF and international FIUs as sources of red flags is an advantage owing to the size, scale, and international nature of the UK market. As a result, the Skilled Person considers this aspect of the Firm’s controls is adequate.
Issue Progress Updates:
Recommendations: 
 

 43.1 Address the gap in its TM coverage to track deviation of expected or actual customer activity.

Issue Title: Transaction Monitoring: Use the information it holds on Merchant Line of Business to target UK customers
Issue Description:
Transaction Risk Assessment Section 5.7.10 of the JMLSG notes that effective monitoring is likely to be based on a considered identification of transaction characteristics, such as the unusual nature of a transaction, abnormal size or frequency for that customer or peer group, the nature of a series of transactions (for example, number of cash credits) the geographic destination or origin of a payment (for example, to or from a high-risk country), and the parties concerned: for example, a request to make a payment to or from a person on a sanctions list. JMLSG (ANNEX 5-III) outlines that firms must ensure that the thresholds they set are at a reasonable level and that they have systems in place to identify linked transactions which, taken together, would exceed that threshold.

 – International Cards’ and ‘Profile Deviation(value/volume)’ being two examples. Many of the
 red flags are covered by the Firm’s TM queues. The Firm’s scenarios are also wide ranging and therefore capture a broad range of activity that could be indicative of many of the red flags listed by the Firmin its assessment.
 In addition to the list of 143 red flags the Square(POS_Payroll_Capital) Typology Assessment 2021 reviews the Firm’s coverage of the red flags by theFirm’s controls. The 143 red flags are grouped into typologies and analysed by market and activity such as point of sale transactions. Where coverage is missing the Firm has recognised where gaps may exist and referenced a phase 2 implementation programme to enhance coverage.
 This analysis of risks presented by the typologies is what forms the basis of the Firm’s TM system.  Another FATF red flag ‘multiple appearances of the same parties in transactions over a short period of time’ is grouped within the Firm’s typology ‘Related Parties: Merchants purchasing their own products/services’. This risk is also what the Firm Tries to address through components of its‘gb_pan_concentration’ scenario described below.Overall, the assessment looks at broader controlsthan just TM, like invoice screening for compliancewith OFAC sanctions, but can be consideredadequate as a method of evaluating how the Firm’squeues are mapped against andinformed byrecognised financial crime risks posed to the Firm.Further the assessmentrecognises that coveragecan be improved and so governance of the
 management of thresholds needs to develop.Drawing upon global organisations like FATF and international FIUs as sources of red flags is an advantage owing to the size, scale, and
 international nature of the UK market. As a result,the Skilled Person considers this aspect of the Firm’s controls is adequate.

 44.1 Use the information it holds on Merchant Line of Business to target UK customers. Due to the Firm’s ability to view and manipulate data, it could configure its systems to assess and compare the behaviour of customer types like taxi firms, jewellers, and hairdressers, for example, on a risk rated basis. This would enhance the Firm’s ability to track and analyse deviations from usual or expected activity and enhance the confidence with which analysts can make conclusions about whether activity may be suspicious.

Issue Title: Transaction Monitoring: seven-day lookback component of queues may trigger unproductive alerts
Issue Description:
Threshold Configuration 
 With regards to regular testing Page 6 of the ‘Square TM Modelling Program’ states that the Firm operates a continuous and automated (Below the Line) BTL sampling, model monitoring, and BTL and Above the Line (ATL) productivity evaluation system. This testing methodology assesses whether the thresholds and current production of alerts are not too high. The consequences of not testing in this way could result in the potential to miss productive alerts. To test the system resilience further, the Firm randomly selects ‘auto cleared’ cases on an ongoing basis for review by TM analysts. The analyst sees these as typical alerts (rather than BTL) and reviews them like any other. Clearance of these alerts helps to confirm the validity of the automated clearing process whereas high levels of escalation in respect of these alerts would provide indicators that the model may need retraining or retuning. For example, if the BTL testing demonstrated that many of the auto cleared alerts were being escalated by analysts this would demonstrate that the threshold may be too high. BTL and ATL testing is a recognised methodology for approximating the right value level of transactions at which there are likely to be productive alerts that are generated by an automated TM system. In the Firm’s case it tests alerts across the customer population to ascertain what it believes should be the appropriate level that alerts should be triggered. Although this method only tests a sample of the lower value alerts produced it does provide a strong indicator of the transaction value at which system generated alerts are likely to be either negatively or positively actioned by analysts. During an interview, the Firm could comfortably explain this testing methodology, why it was relevant to the controls in place and the impact that the testing can have on changing thresholds from time to time. In conclusion, the Skilled Person found that the configuration and testing of the Firm’s TM systems is adequate in most respects. The use of sophisticated technology to support the way in which the Firm manages its obligations under Regulation 28(11)(a) is important context in this respect. However, BDO found that the Firm could make improvements to the configuration of some aspects of its TM systems. The existing scenarios, while capturing large numbers of alerts, do not seek to detect significant deviation from usual or expected activity. For example, in respect of value and volume the Firm does not have a queue that tracks deviation with a customer on expected activity, or deviation against actual activity. Further, the Skilled Person found that the seven-day lookback component of the Firm’s queues may play a role in triggering unproductive alerts. BDO recognises that analysts review alerted activity over a much longer period. However, this is once an alert has been generated. The seven-day lookback for the Firm’s queues could be triggering more alerts, whereas a longer lookback period could enable the queue to review a more balanced overview of customer transactions.
Issue Progress Updates:
Recommendations:
 

 45.1 Address the gap in its TM coverage to track deviation of expected or actual customer activity.
 

 45.2 Conduct a review of the seven-day lookback component of its TM queues and considers introducing rules that assess activity over extended timelines, in addition to the seven-day lookback component.

Ideate new system of tracking deviations from expected customer behavior (F45) (1134647)
