Issue Title: Transaction Monitoring: Enhance QC on transaction monitoring to improve the adequacy of appropriate investigation and rationale for closing alerts
Issue Description:
Quality Control 
 The Square TM Program document explains that the Financial Crimes Technology team generates MI on analyst scores. This is generated at both an individual and team level as a dashboard. The Skilled Person also established through the interview process with the MLRO, the Quality Control team that sits within the business will conduct regular testing on the quality of closure of transaction monitoring / ongoing monitoring alerts. The results of which are fed back to the MLRO on a fortnightly basis. B. The ‘Square Compliance QC (UK) Quality Control Program United Kingdom’ procedure outlines the objectives, responsibility, scope, road map, review process and outcomes of its quality control program. The Quality Control team aims to review between 1 and 4% of all cases generated for each TM queue. 
 The Quality Control review process can result in an alert being cleared. If it is not cleared, then there are three possible judgements: 
 - Minor – decision was technically correct but there was a better outcome 
 - Significant area missed – procedural clarification needed or transaction level TOSV missed 
 - Finding – misinterpretation or false premises For an analyst that has been with the Firm for four weeks or longer the Quality Control team expects that more than 85% of alerts reviewed will result in them being cleared without disagreement. The Quality Control team then reports on this process by providing weekly and monthly feedback to analysts. The weekly reports incorporate both team and individual metrics as well as qualitative feedback. The monthly report is for the Compliance team and contains high level Quality Control observations and views on the broader status of the Quality Control Compliance Program. 
 From our review of 20 TM alerts BDO observed that the closure of alerts was adequate. This included the correct escalation routes and appropriate hashtag usage in line with the Firm’s procedures. However, the Skilled Person found that, in 8/20 alerts, generic discounting language was used, and improvement could be made. Alerts were closed within seven days also in line with the Firm’s procedures for the UK market. This indicates that the Firm’s Quality Control team is performing an effective oversight role for the 1LoD.
Issue Progress Updates:
Recommendations:
 

 47.1 Ensure those who are responsible for conducting QC on transaction monitoring are full trained in relation to the adequacy of appropriate investigation and rationale for closing alerts.
 

 Note: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces
Issue Action Plans: Review QC training (1134651)

Issue Title: Transaction Monitoring: Enhance rationale for closing alerts
Issue Description:
TM Alert Review
 

 Section 5.7.3 of the JMLSG highlights the essentials of any system of monitoring are that reports / alerts are reviewed promptly by the right person and appropriate action is taken on the findings of any further examination.
 

 To conduct its ongoing monitoring, as required by the MLR, the Firm can access, manipulate, and analyse significant amounts of relevant information about customer activity. This is enabled by integrated data sets that can be accessed and interrogated to provide a range of insights based on different data points. Much of this data can be visualised in a variety of formats. The graph can show the entire transaction history of a customer (simpler with lower numbers of transactions) with transactions and their value appearing in a scatter formation according to the axis for date and value. Further, transactions are colour coded to represent, for example, different types of customer cards being used. The graph also displays dashboard style information on other areas of the screen relating to: 
 

  Payments: The total value and number of payments the customer has generated and the average (assumed to be mean) payment size.
  Card data: The number of card present transactions as a percentage and total number of card transactions. It also displays the percentage of cards used that were declined. Further it provides a breakdown of information about the Bank Identification Numbers (BIN) and Primary Account Numbers (PAN) of cards being used.
  A ‘Compliance Panel’ dashboard: This contains information like the percentage of prepaid cards being used at the customer, the percentage of ‘out of hours’ transactions and the GPV for the last 60 days.
 

 The above is not an exhaustive list and there is also information available about chargeback history, wire transfers, refunds, and feedback on the Firm’s customer. Analysts can also generate the visualisation of connections between the customer and other components of the Firm’s database. This allows the user to map connections at various levels. This aspect of the graph can display connections to telephone numbers, email addresses, browser fingerprints, bank accounts, card readers and merchant devices.
 

 In addition to the Transaction Graph, analysts can view a customer’s transaction history in detail. This information contains date and time of the payment, amount, card used (a partial card number appears for security and counter fraud purposes), merchant device used, method (point of sale or card not present) and often the descriptions of items sold by the Firm’s customer. Further, the jurisdiction in which the card was issued is visible. During interviews, the Skilled Person confirmed that, if invoices have been created by the customer within the Firm’s platform, then these will be visible to analysts conducting the ongoing monitoring. However, the quality of this information is reliant upon accurate and, detailed information provided by customers at the onboarding stage. Without this data the ability of analysts to make informed decisions about transactions based on expected customer activity will be limited. This point relates to Finding and Recommendation outlined within the Customer Risk sub-section of this Report, and the Skilled Person believes that the Firm needs to make improvements in this area to collect more detailed and accurate information at onboarding.
 

 Transaction data can also be filtered to select pertinent information that helps the Firm to draw conclusions and make decisions. The format is tabulated so that header rows allow users to select date ranges, transaction status and if, for example, the purchase was contactless. In conjunction with the substantial quantity of information available in the Transaction Graph and associated dashboards the ability to view selected transaction data in a different detailed format means the Firm can make informed judgements about the financial crime risks posed by a customer.
 

 TM Control Testing Results
 

 In 8/20 alerts tested, the Skilled Person found that the analysts have relied upon generic discounting rationales when investigating alerts. In these cases, the rationale lacks context,
 like the value/volume and type of transactions that have been assessed to understand whether this is in line with expected activity for the customer. For example, one alert described ‘healthy transaction activity’ (The Long Shot ref: L3QEJ2MG9XQ3W). The rationale lacked information about why the volume and value of card payments represented usual customer activity. Further, investigation steps are sometimes not outlined and the reason these have been taken to give the Firm comfort that the activity is not unusual or suspicious. Based on BDO’s review of the alerts, no unusual or suspicious activity was detected. The finding relates to the way in which the Firm sometimes structured rationales.
 

 In 7/20 alerts tested, the Skilled Person could not see evidence that the Merchant’s Line of Business and associated transaction levels or activity was considered as a factor determining the investigation outcome. This finding is linked to, and supports, the design, which covers targeted comparative analysis by Merchant Line of Business to aid decision making on alerts.
 

 For example, in one case there is clear evidence of investigation, requests for information and consideration of whether the
 business is legitimate. This includes asking for further clarification when an invoice provided lacks detail. The customer is given the benefit of the doubt because he has an active taxi operator’s licence in London. However, the mean payments of 0.31 per day and the average transaction size of GBP 246.56 and PAN diversity of 27% are not considered. This is complicated by the fact that it is difficult for the Firm to assess if this kind of activity is comparable to other taxi operators and companies in London or the UK.
 

 In another example, the analyst observed that activity is generally consistent but was hampered by the lack of ability to know whether this was anticipated or in line with customers sharing the Merchant Line of Business of ‘personal services’ (which BDO notes is generic).
Issue Progress Updates:
Recommendations:
 

 48.1 Provide guidance in relation to best practice for drafting, relevant, focused, and detailed yet succinct rationales for discounting alerts. Following a review of customer activity.
 48.2 Deliver training to
 ensure that application of the guidance is understood and
 followed. 
 48.3 Always consider the Merchant Line of Business when investigating TM alerts.
 

 Note: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces
Issue Action Plans:
Enhance TM procedures to include additional guidance regarding discounting alerts (1134653)
 Ensure guidance is communicated during training (1134655)
 Consider merchant LOB during TM investigations (1134657)

Issue Title: Customer and Payments sanctions screening systems: Enhance procedures outlining expectations and processes relating to customer screening review, escalation, and disposition
Issue Description:
Customer Screening
 Regulation 35(1) of the MLR 2017 states that firms must have in place appropriate risk-management systems and procedures to determine whether a customer or the beneficial owner of a customer is a PEP or a family member or known close associate of a PEP, and to manage the enhanced risks arising from business relationships or transactions with such a customer. Per Chapter 7 of the FCA Financial Crime guide for firms, firms should have effective, up-to-date screening systems appropriate to the nature, size, and risk of its business for screening new customers and payments against the Consolidated List. Screening existing customer when new names are added to the lists, helps to ensure that firms will not breach the sanctions regime. 
 Part III of the JMLSG Guidance illustrates that firms should take an approach which is appropriate for their business model, when assessing where and how their business is most likely to encounter sanctioned parties, and to focus resources and tailor systems and controls accordingly. The guidance goes on to state that firms should have processes to manage the risk of conducting business with or on behalf of sanctioned individuals and entities. For the purposes of Sanctions, PEP lists and adverse media resources the Firm uses two vendors. The Firm’s ‘Global Sanctions Policy’ outlines that LexisNexis is used to provide current lists of all sanctioned entities and PEPS, their relatives, and close associates (‘RCA’). For adverse media reporting the Firm uses a secondary vendor, Computer Services Inc. In the event that that LexisNexis watchlist information is not available the Firm will use the secondary vendor Computer Services Inc for PEP and sanctions screening in addition to the adverse media screening. 
 The Firm’s fuzzy logic is set based on a threshold of how close the name could be to a Watchlist entity. For example, the first/last name may be flipped, or an address can have one mis-key or date of birth can also have one mis-key. It also picks up on potential ‘leet’ words (e.g., ‘Pau1’ for ‘Paul’) as part of the configuration. The threshold is found by completing BTL and ATL testing in a comparable way to the threshold testing for automated TM systems. 
 The ‘Lexis Nexis Bridger Tuning Analysis’ document describes how the Firm’s tuning process consists of two key testing components. Firstly, configurations testing, and sensitivity analysis takes place and is followed by historical productivity analysis to determine the threshold score. Historical productivity analyses on sanctions screening vendors are also performed on an annual basis. The document provides comprehensive and clear information about each step taken. 
 Based on the configuration analysis and the historical productivity analysis conducted by the Firm, the Lexis Nexis Bridger thresholds were set at 94% for both individuals and businesses. This was set after comprehensive tuning and testing that has been explained clearly in the Firm’s documentation. The Lexis Nexis Bridger Tuning Analysis Document explains how a sample was created and then categorised prior to testing of configurations and watchlist settings. The document subsequently presents analysis of the results of sample testing for both individuals and businesses. The next step involved historical productivity analysis that looked at previous alerts that had been productive. This part of the process helped the Firm to determine the thresholds so that the configuration analysis and historical productivity testing combined to produce the proposed threshold of 94%. 
 

 In most respects the Skilled Person found that customer screening systems and processes, including the system used, fuzzy matching logic, lists screened against, data feeds and system calibration are adequate and effective. However, the Skilled Person found that the Firm would benefit from further details in its procedures outlining expectations and processes relating to customer screening review, escalation, and disposition. The Firm has detailed documentation in respect of global sanctions and how its systems are configured and function. What is missing is some clarity about the role and expectations to cover alert review, investigation, and escalation. In terms of expectations, BDO found that screening analysts often conduct wider reviews of customer activity as part of the Firm’s approach to ongoing monitoring, rather than retaining a narrow focus on the alert in question. This approach was confirmed during interview and BDO understands that this is encouraged by the Firm. Adopting this approach helps employees to gain wider awareness and experience of the Firm’s financial crime systems and controls outside of the screening environment. This is an area of good practice that is worth documenting. The Firm screens all customers against watchlists for PEPs, sanctions, and adverse media at the point of onboarding. Thereafter customer screening takes place every 30 days after the date of onboarding. In practice this means that the Firm screens alerts generated by the results of customer screening daily. However, the Skilled Person found that there is a small risk that one of the Firm’s remitting customers is put on a Sanctions List and the Firm ends up processing payments for a sanctioned entity during the 30-day period between screening. Based on the Firm’s customer profile of UK businesses this is a highly unlikely scenario. However, since the Firm does not conduct screening of payments the risk is higher. The Firm also explained that its riskbased approach to screening customers every 30 days is partly because its experience over the past seven years has not involved the scenario described above. 
 

 However, the Skilled Person could not find evidence that the Firm has articulated how this risk-based approach to customer screening has been adopted in line with an assessment of its inherent risks. The Firm’s BWRA assesses the inherent as high risk. Consequently, the Firm is not able to demonstrate how its approach to screening every 30 days is aligned to its inherent risk. The Skilled Person found that HM Treasury is referred to in the ‘Global Sanctions Compliance Program’ under section 4.0 'Applicable Sanctions Administrations', but there is no reference in the Firm’s policies and procedures to confirm the sanctions regimes covered by its screening programme. For example, the various sanctions regimes operating like the UN, EU and UK are not explicitly listed. Without specific reference to the various types of sanctions, the implication is that the Firm’s screening programme does not cover all applicable regimes. The Skilled Person found that the ‘Lexis Nexis Bridger Tuning Analysis’ has not been updated or reviewed since the end of January 2021. BDO understands that, from feedback provided during an initial observation and finding session on 11 November 2022, an updated version is in the process of being drafted.
Issue Progress Updates:
Recommendations: 
 

 49.1 SEL Draft or amend its policies and procedures to provide details relating to how customer screening analysts
 and compliance review, escalate and disposition alerts. Further this should set out the Firm’s expectations for analyst reviewing alerts. 
 

 49.2 Conduct a review, at least annually, of the approach to screening customers every 30 days. The Firm should ensure that the review is documented. 
 

 49.3 Ensure that the inherent customer risk assessment in the BWRA and resulting approach to screening customers every 30 days are aligned. 
 

 49.4 Updates its documentation on sanctions to confirm that the Firm covers all applicable sanctions regimes as part of its customer screening framework and controls. 
 

 49.5 Lexis Nexis Bridge Tuning Analysis is reviewed and, if necessary, updated at the earliest opportunity. 
 

 Notes: Issue originally owned by Eric Medoff; no longer employed by Block. Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces
Issue Action Plans:
Enhance Sanctions Operations procedures and training (1134659)
 Conduct and document annual review process of customer screening (1134661)
 Align customer risk assessment and approach to customer screening (1134663)
 Update documentation to ensure all application sanctions regimes are included (1134665)
 Review and update LexisNexis Tuning Analysis (1134667)

Issue Title: Customer and Payments sanctions screening systems: Ensure that those responsible for reviewing customer screening alerts are aware of other factors that may require the need for escalation/concern about customers
Issue Description:
Customer Screening Control Testing Results  
 Part III of the JMLSG Guidance illustrates that firms should take an approach which is appropriate for their business model, when assessing where and how their business is most likely to encounter sanctioned parties, and to focus resources and tailor systems and controls accordingly. The guidance goes on to state that firms should have processes to manage the risk of conducting business with or on behalf of sanctioned individuals and entities. 
 The Firm does not conduct payment screening, which is considered appropriate for the Firm’s business. Following negotiation with the FCA, BDO agreed to select a sample of 30 customer screening alerts for review. The Firm is predominantly involved in providing merchant services to its customers for them to process payments made by their own customers. Many of the Firm’s customers are businesses with their own customers paying for services or products with debit, credit, or prepaid cards. These payments can be either at point of sale, online or over the telephone, where the customer card is not present. Consequently, the Firm’s risk exposure is based on its customer population rather than, for example, managing international money transfers money via the SWIFT network. Screening its customers is therefore an appropriate and practical way for the Firm to fulfil its regulatory obligations. 
 The Firm uses a system called ‘Bad Actors’ to manage the process of consolidating watchlists and generating alerts for review in the case management system ‘Regulator. Bad Actors has been set up to work with multiple vendors, considering the Firm uses two vendors to supply its screening information. Potential matches against watchlists or adverse media results are tuned into cases in Regulator for analysts to review. This component of the process is largely similar to the way in which TM alerts are created, reviewed, escalated and dispositioned. 
 Analysis of the customer screening sample demonstrated the successful use of the Firm’s systems that are in place to manage these financial crime risks. 
 For example, the sample meant BDO could track how the automated monthly bulk closure of alerts for some PEP and Sanctions potential matches works in practice. In two cases, one PEP and one adverse media, it was possible to see the Watchlist hits being generated, which were followed by manual review, escalation to compliance and appropriate dispositions. In the adverse media case, the hit was a true positive but, following analysis, an appropriate rationale was given for discounting the positive match. This alert related to a merchant that was accused of selling fertility treatments without evidence that they have any impact of fertility or increasing the likelihood of pregnancy (‘Get a Drip’ ref: 7KRQEVF2B23JB). These were allegations included adverse comments from a medical expert. However, there was no indication of regulatory findings, litigation, or allegations of fraud. The alert was therefore discounted as not relating to financial crime. 
 For a period thereafter, Watchlist hits were closed in bulk soon after they had been generated as part of the monthly bulk closures. Further Watchlist hits then were subsequently reviewed by analysts, then compliance before a similar disposition was made. In this way, high levels of repetition and working on duplicate Watchlist hits have been avoided due to the Firm’s automated bulk closure model. However, by ensuring that manual review and escalation occurs periodically the risk is being managed appropriately. With the adverse media case, for example, there is a possibility that new media emerges. A similar discounting rationale and the same result after each manual review ensures that the Firm, and the model underpinning the monthly bulk closures, can be confident that the approach is working. In 1/ 30 customer screening samples, the Skilled Person found a point worth raising (ref: 7MEJ5JWVQGWZX). Further, this was not related to the screening process because the alert in question was appropriately dispositioned. However, the search of Companies House, which was part of the analyst’s associated investigation, has highlighted a separate problem: the customer’s limited company was dissolved on 9 July 2019. The dissolution of the company is recorded as occurring almost three years before the Watchlist hit was generated by Bad Actors on 7 June 2022. There is no evidence that the analyst has added a comment to the case that the companies house record shows the customer’s limited company was dissolved on 9 July 2019, nearly 3 years before the alert's creation date. Although this example relates to a regular customer screening alert, this search of the customer’s Companies House record should have presented an opportunity to contact the customer for further information and explanation. 
 However, based on information the Firm provided about the merchant being screened, the Skilled Person notes that the customer was deactivated and therefore unable to process payments. What was not visible to BDO was the reasons underpinning deactivation of the customer.
Issue Progress Updates:
Recommendations:
 

 50.1 It is recommended SEL ensures that all those responsible for reviewing customer screening alerts are aware of other factors that may require the need for escalation/concern about customers.
 

 Note: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces
Issue Action Plans: Enhance procedures and training to include information about additional/potential red flags (1134669)

Issue Title: Training: Review content and implement capture of quiz scores and number of attempts
Issue Description:
Regulation 24(1)(a) of the MLR 2017 requires firms to take appropriate measures to ensure that its
 relevant employees are:
 

 i. made aware of the law relating to money laundering and terrorist financing, and to the requirements of data protection,
 which are relevant to the implementation of these Regulations; and
 ii. regularly given training in how to recognise and deal with transactions and other activities or situations which may be
 related to money laundering or terrorist financing.
 

 The Block ‘Anti-Money Laundering Procedure’ states that:
 

 “Training should include details on regulatory requirements and the financial institution’s internal policies, procedures, and processes. The training must also explain employees’ responsibilities and ensure they understand their role in maintaining an effective AML/CTF and Sanctions compliance program...”
 

 Under the current arrangements in place at SEL, all new employees are required to complete AML, CTF and Sanctions training within 30 days of joining the Firm, and then on an annual basis thereafter. The Firm has implemented a Computer Based Training (‘CBT’) Programme, which is currently provided by Thomson Reuters.
 

 The current Anti-Money Laundering CBT programme utilised by the Firm and covers a range of topics, including:
 

 51 Introduction to AML;
 52 Customer Due Diligence;
 53 Financial Sanctions;
 54 Roles & Responsibilities; and
 55 National Laws & Regulation.
 

 The current training package allows SEL to customise the course content to reflect the business model, products and services offered and client types. The review noted that the training material has not been tailored to payment services or the specific policies and procedures in place at SEL. For example, the current training module does not outline how SEL meets its regulatory requirement in respect of the customer due diligence, it doesn’t outline the systems and controls used by SEL to on- board new clients and why this is appropriate. Additionally, in respect of sanctions, the material does not outline that the risk of financial sanctions may be low as SEL only process payments for UK based clients.
 

 Each training module contains an end-of-training assessment, and staff are required to achieve an overall mark of at least 80% to pass. Those achieving less than 80% are required to complete the session again and this is tracked through SEL’s Learning Management System. In situations where an employee fails the assessment for the second time, a live virtual training session will be scheduled.
 

 Regulation 24(1)(b) of the MLR 2017 requires firms to maintain a record in writing of the training given to its relevant employees. SEL utilises the Workday Business System to track the completion of each module assigned and completed by employees. The review noted that the training log provided does not include the result of the assessment taken at the end of the session and it does not include the number of attempts taken to pass the module.
Issue Progress Updates:
Recommendations:
 

 51.1 Review the information collected in the training log to include the result of the end of module test and the number of attempts it took to pass the test at the end of the module.
 

 51.2 Review the current content within the Thomson Reuters storyboard and look to customise the content to make it relevant to payment services and the current SEL business model.
 

 Note: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces
Issue Action Plans:
Review and update training log information (1134671)
 Review and customize Thomas Reuters content (1134673)

Issue Title: Training: Conduct formal training needs assessment and track all training
Issue Description:
Training Material Outlined in SEL’s Policies and Procedures  
 

 JMLSG Part 1 Chapter 7 states that firms should: 
 

 “…train staff on how its products and services may be used as a vehicle for money laundering or terrorist financing, and in the firm’s procedures for managing this risk…Relevant employees should be trained in what they need to know in order to carry out their particular role.” 
 

 ‘The Anti-Money Laundering & Counter Terrorist Financing Program SquareUp Europe Limited’ states that: 
 

 “…SEL provides more in-depth AML training programs to: 
 

 • Local Customer Success teams; 
 • the Sales and Account Executive team; 
 • the Board of Directors, in person/via video conference (on an annual basis); and 
 • Compliance personnel whereby a training program is tailored to their compliance role and regulatory issues and risk specific to their region. 
 

 The Firm has not provided the Skilled Person with training records evidencing any additional AML training that has been provided to the categories listed in the ‘Anti-Money Laundering & Counter Terrorist Financing Program SquareUp Europe Limited.’ Additionally, the Skilled Person noted that the Firm does not complete a Training Needs Assessment on new or existing employees. Additionally, the Firm does not maintain a separate training needs plan."

Issue Title: Noncompliance with Securities Exchange Act of 1934 240.15c3-3 (j): Treatment of Credit Balances
Issue Description: Firms are prohibited from transferring a customer’s free credit balance to another account or institution unless (1) the customer provides specific authorization for the transfer; or (2) the transfer is to a product in the firm’s Sweep Program (subject to certain provisions).Sweep Program transfers can only be to a money market fund (“MMF”) or to an FDIC insured bank account. Per an agreement entered into with Drivewealth on 9/11/2019, the customer free credit balances were required to be automatically transferred daily to Block, Inc., where the customers' free credit balances are deposited into an omnibus account in Block's name. Customers cannot opt out of this arrangement. Because these transfers are obligatory and automatic, they do not satisfy the 15c3-3(j)(2)(i) requirement that customers provide specific authorization for the transfer. Further, Block’s omnibus account does not satisfy Sweep Program requirements because it is neither a SIPC-protected MMF nor is it an FDIC-protected bank account. As a result, the Firm has violated 15c3-3(j)(2).

Issue Description: When a CAI customer sells a security, Block immediately credits their Cash App Balance with the anticipated proceeds. On 04/28/2022 the Firm confirmed to FINRA Staff that CAI customers’ accounts are margin accounts. Issue Title:
Noncompliance with FINRA Rule 3110 (a): Supervisory System; FINRA Rule 3110 (b) (1):
 Written Procedures – General Requirements; and FINRA Rule 4210 (f) (8) (B) (iv)
 Special Requirements for Pattern Day Traders
Issue Description: FINRA Rule 4210(f)(8)(B)(i) defines day trading to mean the purchasing and selling or the selling and purchasing of the same security on the same day. FINRA Rule 4210(f)(8)(B)(ii) defines a pattern day trader as a customer who executes four or more day trades within five business days (unless the day trades are 6% or less of total trades during the same period). FINRA Rule 4210(f)(8)(B)(iv)(a) requires pattern day traders to have at least $25,000 of equity.Staff reviewed the firm’s purchase and sales blotter submitted for this examination and identified at least one account that engaged in pattern day trading. Because the Firm did not require pattern day traders to establish and maintain at least $25,000 in equity, the Firm violated 4210(f)(8)(B)(iv)a.Section 27.7 of the Firm’s WSPs is the only section that discusses any day trading controls. This section simply states the Firm “enforces . . . day trading controls.” There is no discussion of what the controls are or how they will be enforced. Because the Firm failed to maintain a proper supervisory system to review pattern day trading, and the Firm’s WSPs do not contain adequate procedures related to day trading and pattern day trading, the firm violated FINRA Rules 3110(a) and 3110(b)(1).
Issue Progress Updates:
Updating WSPs in the short term to reflect preventative controls; a detective control will be built by Q4 '22 to identify future instances of this occurrence. Once the detective control is built, the WSPs will then be updated to discuss the new detective control.

Issue Title: Noncompliance with FINRA Rule 2090: Know Your Customer; FINRA Rule 3110 (b) (1): Written Procedures – General Requirements; and FINRA Rule 3310 (f) (i): Anti-Money Laundering Compliance Program
Issue Description: FINRA Rule 3310(f)(i)—and 31 C.F.R. § 1023.210(b)(5)(i)—requires member firms to establish a written anti-money laundering (“AML”) program reasonably designed to achieve and monitor the firm’s compliance with the Bank Secrecy Act and implementing regulations. The AML program must include procedures for understanding the nature and purpose of customer relationships for the purpose of developing a customer risk profile. The Firm has stated that because the Firm does not make recommendations it does not collect certain customer profile information, such as income and net worth. As a result, the Firm has violated FINRA Rule 3310(f)(i) because it has not established a reasonably designed, risk based program for developing customer risk profiles. FINRA Rule 2090 requires member firms to use reasonable diligence in regard to the opening and maintenance of every account and to know (and retain) the essential facts concerning every customer. Because the Firm does not use reasonable diligence to know essential facts about every customer to comply with FINRA Rule 3110(f)(i)—and 31 C.F.R. § 1023.210(b)(5)(i)—the Firm has violated FINRA Rule 2090. Additionally, because the Firm’s WSPs do not state how the Firm will comply with FINRA Rule 2090 when opening (or maintaining) an account, the Firm has violated FINRA Rule 3110(b)(1).

Issue Title:

 Communications with the Public – Content Standards
Issue Description: FINRA Rule 2210(d)(1)(B) prohibits firms from making misleading statements or claims in any communication. The Firm’s Customer Account Agreement (“CAA”) states orders executed pursuant to the auto-invest feature (aka, “Recurring Order”) and Customer Orders are placed as market orders. Additionally, the app screenshots display to customers that transaction order type is “Market.” However, the CAA also directs customers to DriveWealth's trading disclosure, which states that all orders are placed as “not held” orders, which carry time and price discretion. By describing discretionary orders as Market Orders the Firm mislead customers about best execution requirements and violated FINRA Rule 2210(d)(1)(B).

Issue Title: Governance and Oversight of AML/CFT/FS Compliance - Roles, Responsibilities and Reporting Requirements
Issue Description:
Recommendation : The Firm must ensure that regular and appropriate discussion and challenge in respect of AML/CFT compliance takes place at Board and
 RGC level and that such discussion and challenge is recorded in the meeting minutes.
 

 

 Issue Details: In response to the Central Bank of Ireland’s (‘Central Bank’) Request For Information (‘RFI’), the Firm advised that the RGC, which is a Senior
 Management Committee with responsibility for AML/CFT and FS oversight (but which is not a sub-committee of the Board), is the main forum
 for AML/CFT and FS discussion. The RGC meets monthly and is attended by the Firm’s CEO (who is also Executive Director and COO) and
 the Chief Compliance Officer (CCO)/MLRO. The Firm provided Board meeting minutes and Pre-Read Board memos for the period Q2 2020
 to Q2 2021. It is unclear from the Pre-Read Memos as to whether or not they are intended solely for the Board of the Firm as they include
 “updates” on the UK and Australian Group entities also. RGC meeting minutes for the period Q2 2020 to Q3 2021, and July 2021, were
 provided.
 The Firm advised that “Compliance Program Updates” are provided to the RGC on a monthly basis, to the Board on a quarterly basis, and also,
 to Square Inc.’s Audit and Risk Committee (‘ARC’) on a quarterly basis. During the inspection meeting that was held with the Firm in November
 2021, the Firm advised that AML/CFT was a standing agenda item for both the monthly RGC meetings and the quarterly Board meetings.
 However, from a review of the minutes for both the Board and the RGC, it is unclear as to what discussion or challenge took place in respect
 of AML/CFT and FS. For example, the Board meeting minutes for Q1 2021 advise that the CCO provided an update on the compliance
 procedures and results and informed the Board of the hiring of a new MLRO however, there were no further details contained in the minutes
 themselves or the Pre-Read Memo as to what “results” or other Management Information (‘MI’) was provided. The minutes further advise
 that a representative presented the EU Compliance Operating Model and the UK and EU AML and Regulator Compliance Plan. There is no
 detail in the minutes as to what discussion took place in relation to these topics as the minutes only advise “The Board asked questions and
 discussion ensued”.
 Similarly, the minutes of the Q2 2021 Board meeting refer to the CCO providing an update on the Firm’s compliance procedures and results
 and advise “The Board asked questions and discussion ensued” but no details on the topics that were discussed or queried was provided in the
 meeting minutes. The Q3 2021 Board meeting minutes included an update from the CCO in relation to, inter alia, the Firm’s compliance
 procedures, and note that the Firm’s Business Risk Assessment (‘BRA’) and methodology was presented to the Board. The minutes advise
 “The Board acknowledged the results of the risk review and agreed with the action plan presented by management”, however, again there was no
 evidence in the minutes that any discussion or challenge of the Firm’s BRA took place.
 In relation to the RGC minutes provided, there is no detail contained therein in relation to what AML/CFT MI was provided or discussed. For
 example, the minutes of May 2021 provided an update on EU AML legislative changes but there were no updates or discussions evident,
 which related directly to the Firm’s AML/CFT risk and control framework. Similarly, for the meetings held in June and July 2021, there is no
 reference to the provision of any AML/CFT MI or relevant discussions in relation to the Firm, even though the Firm advised, both during the
 inspection meeting and in its presentation at this meeting, that AML/CFT is a standing item agenda with compliance updates provided.
 Following a further request for information to the Firm in April 2022, the Firm provided a “Regulatory Compliance MI” template but it is unclear
 if this is used when meetings of the RGC are held. A MI template for the Board was also provided in May 2022, but again, it is not evident from
 the Board meeting minutes provided, as to what MI was presented or discussed at these Board meetings.
 Furthermore, there are no Terms of Reference in place for either the Board or the RCG, and as such, it is unclear what oversight functions are
 performed by both respectively, in relation to AML/CFT and FS compliance oversight. In addition, the Firm’s AML Program, as updated April
 2022, does not set out the role and functions of the Board in respect of AML/CFT & FS compliance oversight. The AML Program provides that
 the AML Program is approved by the Board but does not set out any other roles or responsibilities of the Board in terms of AML/CFT & FS
 oversight and there is no reference whatsoever to the RGC in the AML Programs.
 During the inspection meeting that was held in November 2021, the Firm advised that the Firm’s BRA is approved by the CCO and that a
 synopsis of the BRA is provided to the Board. Section 30A(5) of the CJA 2010 provides that a BRA “shall be approved by senior management”.
 It is noted that the CCO is a member of the senior management team, however, the CCO is also involved in the drafting of the Firm’s BRA.
 The Central Bank would expect that the person involved in the drafting of the BRA would not be involved in the approval of the BRA and that
 the BRA should be reviewed, discussed, challenged and approved by at least, a senior management committee, or by the Board itself. The
 Firm’s Board is ultimately responsible for ensuring that the Firm is operating in compliance with the provisions contained in the CJA 2010.
 As set out in section 6.2 of the Central Bank’s AML and CFT Guidelines for the Financial Sector, the Central Bank expects the Board to
 demonstrate effective governance and oversight of a Firm’s AML/CFT compliance framework – this is not evident from the material reviewed.
 In conclusion, Authorised Officers were unable to evidence that the Firm has a clearly documented and operational AML/CFT governance
 framework, which clearly sets out roles and responsibilities, and reporting requirements, in order to ensure that the Firm’s compliance with
 its AML/CFT obligations is subject to sufficient oversight and challenge at a Senior Management and Board level.

Issue Title: AML/CFT/FS Policies and Procedures - Review of AML Policies and Procedures
Issue Description:
Recommendation: The Firm is required to undertake a review of its AML/CTF Programs and procedures, and update where necessary, to ensure
 consistency in the drafting, formatting and layout and to ensure procedures are aligned to the AML Programs. Furthermore, the Firm
 must ensure that the AML Programs are in alignment with the Firm’s BRA and that the Firm can evidence and demonstrate that it is
 operating in compliance with the CJA 2010. The Firm is also required to take note of points 1-10 as set out in the details section
 above, noting that the issues set out are non-exhaustive.
 

 

 Details: In response to the Central Bank’s RFI, the Firm initially submitted a total of 9 documents – one of which being the Firm’s AML & CTF
 Program, which was subsequently split into two individual AML & CTF Programs – one for the Seller Managed Payments Product and
 one for the Cash App Product. Copies of both were provided.
 The following was noted in respect of the Firm’s Seller Managed Payments AML & CTF Program (non-exhaustive):
 1. It is unclear why references to GBP and not Euro are included.
 2. The Program advises that “Square maintains KYC processes/procedures in line with this AML/CTF Program”. Where underlying
 operational procedures are in existence, the name of the procedure and where it can be accessed should be referenced – in
 order for all staff to be aware of the existence of the actual underlying operational procedure in question.
 3. The Program provides a list of information that is required at onboarding but does not advise of what documentation a
 potential customer is required to provide (this is not noted in the “Seller Know Your Customer Procedures” either).
 4. The Seller AML Program provides “High Risk Jurisdictions are not currently an issue”, however, the Firm’s BRA provides (Products
 and Services Risk) that the product (IE Managed Payments) can transfer funds across international borders (especially to and
 from high-risk countries).
 The following was noted in respect of the Firm’s Draft Cash App AML & CTF Program (non-exhaustive):
 5. The Program refers to two different Cash App products, IE Gold and IE Green, but no further information is provided in relation
 the differences between both products and what each product entails.
 6. The Program provides that “Cash App utilises document IDV” and a table is provided which sets out the customer information
 required at onboarding but does not specify what documentation a customer is required to provide (this is not noted in the
 “Draft KYC and EDD Procedure” either).
 7. References are made to the Firm maintaining KYC processes/procedures in line with the Program – specific references to
 underlying operational procedures that are in existence should be set out.
 8. The Program provides that “High Risk jurisdictions are not currently an issue” however, the Draft Cash App AML/Sanctions Risk
 Assessment Tool provides that the IE Gold product can transfer funds across international borders (to and from high risk
 countries).
 There is a lack of consistency between both AML/CTF Programs. For example, the sections detailing roles and responsibilities, who the
 programs apply to, and the style and format of the Programs. It is further noted that the Cash App AML & CTF Program is in draft form.
 In addition, not all underlying procedures would appear to be aligned to the AML Programs. For example, the Draft Cash App AML
 Program refers to two Cash App products (IE Green and IE Gold) whereas the Draft KYC and EDD Procedure for the Cash App product
 does not make any reference to the IE Green and IE Gold Cash App products, respectively.
 

 The following was noted in respect of the Firm’s AML/CFT procedures in general (procedure specific findings are set out as separate
 findings in this report). The flowing list is non-exhaustive:
 9. A single STR procedure is in place for both the Seller Managed Payments product and the Cash App product whereas, for
 example, separate Financial Sanctions screening procedures and Transaction Monitoring procedures are in place for both
 products. If the Firm intends to treat both products completely separate in terms of AML/CFT & FS compliance, it is unclear
 why in some instances, the same procedure is in existence for both products whereas in other instances, separate procedures
 are in place.
 10. There is a lack of consistency in the formatting, layout and titles of procedures. Some procedures include the Square logo
 whereas others do not and some procedures are titled “Square” with others titled “Squareup International Limited”. The Firm
 should ensure consistency in procedures throughout and should also ensure that reference is made in procedures to other
 procedures/programs that should be read and reviewed in conjunction with that procedure (it is noted that reference to the
 Cash App AML Program is referenced in the Draft Cash App KYC/EDD Procedure). Also, where the Firm uses acronyms in its
 Programs/Procedures, full definitions of the acronyms used should be set out.
 In addition to the above, it is noted that the Firm’s STR procedure and the KYC/EDD Procedure for the Cash App are in draft form. The
 Firm should have ensured that all procedures were fully approved, prior to the Firm’s operations going live. It is noted that the Cash
 App has yet to be fully rolled out to the general public, however, this product is currently offered to staff and to their family and friends.
 The Central Bank understands that procedures are updated from time to time but staff should be in a position to rely on the previously
 approved version and not rely on a draft version. It is imperative that the Firm ensures that all of its AML/CFT & FS programs and
 procedures are finalised and approved.
 Overall, there is a lack of coherency in the Programs and Procedures the Firm has in place. As set out in the Central Bank’s AML/CFT
 Guidelines for the Financial Sector, Firms should, inter alia, “fully demonstrate consideration of and compliance with all legal and regulatory
 requirements” when developing AML/CFT policies controls and procedures. This is not entirely evident from the documents reviewed.

Issue Title: CDD Procedures - Review/Update Seller and Cash App CDD/KYC procedure
Issue Description:
Recommendation: The Firm is required to review and update both its Seller and Cash App CDD/KYC procedures in order to provide clear end-to-end details of the customer onboarding process, to include the type of documentation that is accepted at onboarding and to include details on how data from the outsourced provider is fed into/transferred to the Firm’s Customer Risk Rating Model/Case Management System.
 

 

 

 Details: In response to the Central Bank’s RFI and further information requests, the Firm provided two procedures in respect of CDD – one for the
 Seller Managed Payments product and one for the Cash App product. It is noted that the Firm has outsourced the collection and verification
 of customer information to a third party provider and it is the Central Bank’s understanding that the CDD/KYC procedures provided are for
 “KYC reviews” in the event that a customer is not automatically onboarded or where a customer is deemed high risk and/or where EDD is
 required. The following was noted (non-exhaustive):
 Seller Know Your Customer Procedures
 1. The customer onboarding process is not clearly set out, i.e. the end-to-end customer onboarding journey from the customer entering
 their onboarding details online/via the Square app, to the customer being onboarded by the Firm, e.g. how a customer’s data is fed
 into the Firm’s Customer Risk Rating (‘CRR’) Model and Case Management System (Regulator) and how a customer’s risk rating is
 assigned at onboarding - according to the customer’s profile. It is noted that ID & V is undertaken by a third party but details of the
 end to end customer onboarding journey should be set out, including how cases are referred to Compliance for review.
 2. The procedure does not set out a list of documentation that is acceptable for onboarding. It is noted that the Firm’s AML Program
 sets out a list of information that is required at onboarding but no details are provided in relation to the type of documentation that
 a customer is required to provide and what documentation is accepted by the Firm. It is also unclear as to whether or not there are
 separate onboarding information and documentation requirements in place for incorporated and unincorporated entities and it is
 unclear as to how an unincorporated entity’s address is verified and whether or not the Firm places reliance on the Companies
 Registration Office for the verification of an incorporated entity’s address.
 3. This procedure would not appear to be finalised. For example, section 1.2 provides “One of the core elements of this program is
 conducting Business Verification (“BV”) reviews in order to ensure the information on our Merchants and their businesses is complete and
 accurate. These queues are tuned according to Machine Learning models. The queue names are as follows: ● TBD” and section 1.d.i.2
 provides “For Moodys the format is.........”.
 4. It is unclear why three version controls are included in this procedure.
 5. The procedure refers to the Irish Companies Registration Office (‘CRO’), where a search can be carried out to see if a company is
 registered with the CRO but there is no reference to the CROs equivalent in Spain or France, where the Firm is currently providing
 services on a Freedom of Services basis
 6. The procedure provides (in relation to EDD) “Merchants are identified primarily through the Customer Risk Rating (CRR) Model” and the
 procedure refers to a document titled “Customer Risk Rating Methodology & Framework v0.1”. This document was not provided to
 Authorised Officers.
 Draft KYC and EDD Procedure Cash App
 7. The end to end customer onboarding process is not set out (similar to point 1 above – noting that the Customer Risk Rating Model
 has yet to be implemented for the Cash App product, as advised in the Draft Cash App AML and CTF Program).
 8. The procedure advises of the information that is collected at onboarding (such as full name, date of birth) but a footnote is included
 which advises that the collection of information is “Subject to specific thresholds”. These thresholds are not set out and as such, it is
 unclear from this procedure, as to what information is required for customer onboarding.
 9. The procedure provides that a customer’s home address is obtained but not verified and that a customer’s date of birth is
 collected. This is at variance with what is advised in the Firm’s Cash App AML and CTF Program where it provides that a
 customer’s residential address is not collected at onboarding for the IE Green Cash App product (the Program provides, in respect
 of residential address, “Subset of users who link a bank account for cash-out”) nor is a customer’s date of birth collected for the IE
 Green Cash App product.
 10. There are conflicting statements in the procedure, such as “Where the identity of these customers is not satisfactorily verified, the
 account creation request is denied” and “The below process(i.e. Onboarding Review – Manual Process) is triggered when IDV fails...”.
 11. During the meeting which took place in November 2021, the Firm advised that Simplified Due Diligence (‘SDD’) is applied to the
 Cash App product. However, the decision to apply SDD to all Cash App customers would not appear to be documented. This is a
 requirement pursuant to section 34A(3) of the CJA 2010.
 12. The procedure provides “Enhanced due diligence reviews are conducted on accounts that present a higher risk for money
 laundering or terrorist financing”, however, it is unclear from the procedure as to how a customer is noted as being high risk at the
 outset and when Enhanced Customer Due Diligence (‘EDD’) should be applied to that customer, given the limited information
 requested from a customer at onboarding stage. It would appear that a customer is only deemed high risk if a transaction
 monitoring alert is triggered or if the customer is a PEP.
 13. Reference is made to a “UK bank card” – it is unclear why this reference is included.
 Further to the above, the frequency of when a refresh of CDD is required/when up to date customer information is required, is not
 documented.
 In summary, the Firm has not clearly evidenced how it is operating in compliance with the provisions contained in the CJA 2010, in respect
 of CDD.
Issue Progress Updates: Note: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces

Issue Title: STR Procedures - Update/Amend Procedures
Issue Description:
Recommendation: The Firm is required to review its STR procedure and update/amend where necessary – taking the above detail into consideration.
 

 

 Details: The procedure provided by the Firm for the identification and reporting suspicious transactions is currently in draft form (as noted in Finding
 2 above). The procedure, which is titled “Draft Suspicious Transaction Reporting Procedure Squareup International Limited” is the STR procedure
 for both the Seller and Cash App products. STR investigations are undertaken by Square Inc. but this is soon to be outsourced to
 AMLRightSource. The following was noted (non-exhaustive):
 1. Page 3 of the procedure references the FIU as being both An Garda Síochána and the Revenue Commissioners – this is factually
 incorrect.
 2. The procedure provides “Non-high priority alerts are associated with low volume and /or low velocity activities which have low values”. The
 Central Bank refers the Firm to section 7.2 of the Central Bank’s AML and CFT Guidelines for the Financial Sector, which provides
 “Firms should note that there is no minimum monetary threshold for reporting and no amount should be considered too low for suspicion. This
 is particularly important when considering potential terrorist financing transactions, which often involve very small amounts of money”.
 3. The procedure provides that “Non-Priority alerts will be processed within 90 days of the alert generation”. There is no documented
 rationale set out for this review timeframe and the Firm should note that the CJA 2010 stipulates that STRs are to be reported “as
 soon as practicable”. The 90 day timeframe for the investigation/processing and subsequent discounting or reporting of what the
 Firm deems to be “non-priority” alerts would not appear to be “as soon as practicable”.
 No sample testing of STRs was conducted as part of this AML/CFT & FS inspection as the Firm advised that no STRs had been reported –
 either internally or externally.

Issue Title: Financial Sanctions (‘FS’) - Enhance Sanctions Procedures
Issue Description:
Recommendation: The Firm is required to review and update its FS procedures in order to (1) make it absolutely clear what process should be taken in the
 event of a true FS match, and, (2) set out exactly to whom the Firm reports true FS matches to and requests derogations from.
 

 

 Details: In response to the Central Bank’s RFI, the Firm provided separate procedures for the Seller Managed Payments product and the Cash App
 product: (1) Seller Watchlist Procedures (‘Seller Procedure’) – this includes PEP screening and (2) Sanctions Compliance - Cash App Standard
 Operating Procedures (‘Cash App Procedure’). The latter appears to be a global Squareup procedure and would not appear to be specifically
 drafted for the Firm and also makes reference to “Bitcoin transfers”.
 Section 5.2 of the Seller procedure sets out the process in respect of a true FS match. It is unclear from this section if the Firm is operating in
 compliance with its International FS obligations (e.g. section 5.2 “SAN: True Hit” provides “5.2.1 Approve with enhanced monitoring” and 5.2.2
 “Approve without enhanced monitoring”).
 The procedures do not specifically set out, which competent authorities in the State a true FS match should be reported to and the procedure
 does not set out to whom the Firm would apply for a FS derogation.
 There is a lack of consistency between both procedures, for example, the Cash App Procedure makes reference to escalating matters to a
 “Sanctions Lead” and to the “Global Head of Sanctions” and provides a Global Sanctions email address to which matters should be escalated
 – no similar escalation references are included in the Seller Procedure.
 The frequency of FS screening is not set out.
Issue Progress Updates: Note: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces
Issue Action Plans: FS Procedure Review - True Match Reporting (1134735)

Issue Title: TM Process and TM Procedures - Documentation of Responsible Party for TM rules/thresholds
Issue Description:
Recommendation: The Firm is required to document the party responsible for the setting, review and updating of TM rules/thresholds and the approval process in this regard.
 

 

 Details: The Firm would not appear to have documented, who is responsible for the setting of TM rules/thresholds, and the review and updating of
 such rules/thresholds – either at a Global or at a Firm level. During the meeting held with the Firm in November 2021, the Firm advised that
 rules coverage was assessed at a global level, taking into account EBA/Irish guidance, and that the Chief Compliance Officer for Ireland (and
 their team) meet with the Global analytics team to review and discuss TM rules with TM rules for Ireland signed off by the Chief Compliance
 Officer for Ireland. This process is not evident from a review of the documentation received from the Firm and the Central Bank would expect
 the Firm to have evidenced both its effective oversight and input into the TM rules applicable to its business operations.
 In response to the Central Bank’s RFI, the Firm provided two TM review procedures: (i) Squareup International Limited [EEA] Seller
 Transaction Monitoring Procedures (‘Seller TM Procedure’), and, (ii) Cash IE Transaction Monitoring Procedural Document – Squareup
 International Ltd (‘Cash App TM Procedure’).
 In relation to the Seller TM Procedure, it is unclear if TM reviews are undertaken by the UK Compliance Team or the IE Compliance Team as
 section 2 refers to the IE Compliance Team whereas section 3 refers to the UK Compliance Team. Reference is made in the Seller TM
 Procedure to reporting a SAR to the National Crime Agency and not the Irish Financial Intelligence Unit (‘FIU’).
 Neither procedure contains details on the process of raising an STR (should an STR be warranted following a TM review) nor do the
 procedures contain any reference to the STR procedures that are in place.

Issue Title: Risk Assessment Methodology - Update to Comply with CJA 2010
Issue Description:
Recommendation: The Firm is required to update the existing group Risk Assessment Methodology or include an addendum to the existing methodology, which refers to all relevant guidance reviewed and considered by the Firm when drafting its Risk Assessment in order to comply with the provisions contained in the CJA 2010 and in order to clearly evidence what guidance the Firm has reviewed when drafting its Risk Assessment.
 

 Details:  The Central Bank notes that the Firm drafts its Risk Assessment in line with the global methodology/framework titled “Global AML & Sanctions
 Risk Assessment Framework” that is in place. There is no separate methodology in place specific to the Firm. The methodology in place contains
 no reference to any guidance or material relevant to the Firm that the Firm reviewed prior to drafting its Risk Assessment, despite the Firm
 advising during the inspection meeting in November 2021, that the NRA, the Central Bank’s AML/CTF Guidelines for the Financial Sector,
 and the EBA’s Risk Factor Guidelines were taken into consideration when drafting its Risk Assessment.. It is noted that references are made
 to the Wolfsberg Guidance. The Firm’s Seller (Managed Payments) Risk Assessment makes no reference to any guidance that the Firm has
 taken into consideration, however, it is noted that the Firm’s Draft Cash App Risk Assessment provides “Risk factors discussed in the Irish
 National Risk Assessment on ML/ATF [sic] and the EU Banking Authority Guidelines for Electronic Money Issuers were considered when conducting this
 assessment”.
 It should be noted that section 30(A) 2 of the CJA 2010 requires firms to have regard to the NRA, any guidance issued by the Central Bank
 and any guidance issued by the EBA. In addition, the Central Bank’s AML/CFT Guidelines for the Financial Sector refers to further sources of
 information/guidance that a firm should consider when drafting its Risk Assessment.
 Whilst the Central Bank accepts that the methodology in place is for the global Square operations, the methodology must reference (either
 by way of amendments to the exiting methodology or by way of an addendum to the existing methodology) Irish legislative requirements, as
 the Firm is obliged to comply with such requirements, and the Firm must be in a position to evidence the fact that it has reviewed and taken
 into consideration, all relevant guidance applicable to the Irish entity (as required by the CJA 2010).
 The Firm is required to update the existing group Risk Assessment Methodology or include an addendum to the existing methodology,
 which refers to all relevant guidance reviewed and considered by the Firm when drafting its Risk Assessment in order to comply with the
 provisions contained in the CJA 2010 and in order to clearly evidence what guidance the Firm has reviewed when drafting its Risk
 Assessment.
Issue Progress Updates: Note: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces
Issue Action Plans: Risk Assessment Methodology - Irish Legislation (1134743)
Closure Request Justification: DRI (Chris Cataldo) no longer with the company. Subir has been listed as the DRI on all issues that have previous employees listed.

Issue Title: Risk Assessment - Alignment of Control Framework
Issue Description:
Recommendation: The Firm is required to ensure that its control framework is aligned to the inherent risks it has identified for ML/TF and FS risk.
 

 

 

 

 

 Details: The Firm provided two Risk Assessments to the Central Bank: 1. Seller Managed Payments Risk Assessment and 2. Draft Cash App Risk
 Assessment (it is noted that the Cash App has not yet been launched to the general public and is only available to the Firm’s staff, their family
 and friends).
 It is unclear, form both Risk Assessment Tools provided, how the Firm has aligned its AML/CFT and FS control framework to the inherent
 risks identified for both the Seller Managed Payments product and the Cash App product. The Firm’s inherent risk categories for the Seller
 Managed Payments product consist of: Products and Services Risk (Delivery Channel Risk is incorporated into Products and Services Risk),
 Customer Risk, Geographic Risk and Operational Risk. However, the Firm has not set out how its controls mitigate each of the inherent risks
 as, for example, the Firm has advised that its controls include “CIP, KYC/CDD Program”, “Transaction Monitoring and Controls”, and “Training
 Program”, but it is unclear how these align with the Firm’s inherent risk categories.
 It is unclear why references to what KYC/CDD information is required in the United States, is included in the Risk Assessment tools for the
 Firm – given that both Risk Assessments would appear to be Risk Assessments solely for the Irish entity.
 The Risk Assessment Tools include a tab detailing a sanctions residual risk rating but it is unclear how the Firm has derived its inherent
 sanctions risk.
 It is unclear why the Cash App Risk Assessment Tool includes a separate Delivery Channel inherent risk category whereas the Seller Risk
 Assessment Tool has incorporated Delivery Channel inherent risk into the Products and Services category.
 In a document titled “Cash App AML Risk Assessment Addendum”, which was provided to the Central Bank in respect of the Cash App Risk
 Assessment, the following is advised: “The product is only usable by those customers who have already passed KYC at a credit institution as either a
 bank account or a bank card (which necessitates a bank account) are required. This means that both the sender and the receive [sic] will have passed
 KYC checks at their banking institution. Although SIL does not place direct reliance on the KYC checks, it can take these measures into account to reduce
 the residual risk”. This is not specified in the Firm’s Draft Cash App Risk Assessment Tool nor is this advised in the Firm’s AML Cash App
 Program or the Draft Cash App KYC and EDD procedure.
 It is unclear as to whether or not TF has been considered as there is no reference to TF risk in either Risk Assessment Tool.

Issue Title: Compliance Monitoring and Assurance Testing of the Firm’s AML/CFT and FS Control Framework
Issue Description:
Recommendation: The Firm is required to implement a detailed and formalised Compliance Monitoring and Assurance Testing plan, which includes details of what areas are subject to testing, the frequency of such testing and to whom the results of such testing are presented to. The Firm must ensure that its Compliance Monitoring and Assurance Testing plan encompasses all areas of its AML/CFT and FS compliance framework and its three lines of defence.
 

 

 

 

 Details: In response to the Central Bank’s RFI, the Firm advised “Squareup International Limited’s (SIL) 2021 Compliance Independent Testing and
 Monitoring Plan is a risk-based, calendar-year plan that prioritizes testing activities for areas identified as elevated risk. Since SIL commenced regulated
 activities in March 2021 with a limited number of beta Sellers and became generally available in May 2021, the AML/CTF/FS will not be tested this
 year. During our 2022 planning process, the Compliance Independent Testing and Monitoring team will continue to include AML/CTF/FS in scope for
 planning and will include testing AML/CTF/FS as part of the SIL 2022 Annual Test Plan”. The Firm further advised “SIL’s alerts and cases are subject
 to quality control testing conducted by the Compliance Quality Control Team. The cases are selected via a randomised sample of agents' actions from
 Compliance operations queues. As at August 2021, the Compliance QC Team has completed reviews on 4 (four) compliance cases covering Enhanced
 Due Diligence (Customer Risk Rating & LegitScript queue) and Customer Due Diligence & Sanctions (IE Adhoc queue). The results of these reviews
 showed that 3 had “Agree” reviews and 1 with ‘’Disagree’’ and feedback was provided to the Analyst”. Both statements would appear to be at
 variance with each other and it is unclear if the latter statement was in relation to the Firm or a separate Group entity.
 In a further request to the Firm, the Firm advised “The Quality Control team (QC) supports the EU Seller compliance program by reviewing
 operational decisions to ensure consistency and adherence to procedures. The results identify opportunities for training and development, as well as
 continuous improvement to the EU Seller Compliance operations program” and the Firm provided a copy of its “Quality Compliance Program EU
 Seller Compliance”. This document provides that its scope is in relation to “...the Ireland POS CRR Initial Review, Ireland Ad Hoc and Legitscript
 queues” and further provides “End of Q1 2022 - Aim to expand QC program to cover the remaining Ireland and France Seller Compliance Operations
 queues within the following areas: Identity Verification (IDV), Know Your Customer (KYC), Sanctions, Transaction Monitoring, Suspicious Transaction
 Reports (STR)”. As such, this document would not appear to set out the full scope of AML/CFT and FS Compliance Monitoring and Assurance
 Testing.
 The Firm also provided screenshots of two Quality Control dashboards, one titled “Manager QC Ireland” [which appears to be a dashboard
 detailing the outcome of Customer Risk Rating (‘CRR’) reviews, a review of screening for Terms of Service Violations (‘TOSV’) and review of
 “IE Ad Hoc” (CDD and Sanctions)] and the second titled “Seller Compliance QC Data Records” (which appears to be a dashboard detailing
 KYC/EDD Seller reviews). The Firm also provided an excel spreadsheet titled “Third Party Vendor List Assurance Tracker” which the Firm
 advised was evidence of the quarterly vendor assurance of Sanctions Screening tools.
 In a further request to the Firm, the Firm advised (on 29/04/22) that the Compliance Independent Testing and Monitoring (‘ITM’) Team had
 incorporated AML/CTF/FS in scope for the Firm’s 2022 Annual Compliance Test Plan and provided an extract of the ITM schedule for Q1
 2022. The ITM schedule would appear to be in draft form as it advised “Scope to be confirmed however initial suggested areas for review include...”.
 The scope was set out in relation to the UK Seller and Cash App products with the following advice in respect of the Firm “Same areas as UK”.
 Whilst it is evident from the information provided by the Firm that testing of elements of the Firm’s AML/CFT and FS control framework is
 carried out, the Firm would not appear to have a formalised Compliance Monitoring and Assurance Testing plan in place, to include, inter alia,
 assurance testing of ID & V, STRs, PEP and FS screening, TM alerts and where the results of such testing are maintained and to whom they
 are reported to.
Issue Progress Updates: Note: Elizabeth Henry is Issue Approver as the local CCO/MLRO as this is an external review and there is no internal approval proces
Issue Action Plans: Monitoring and Testing Program (1134753)
