Issue Title: R34 - BWRA Enhancement
Issue Description:
The Skilled Person’s review of SEL’s 2023 BWRA identified that the Firm has made progress with refining its approach to assess its financial crime risks. As noted in our Stage One review, SEL’s BWRA methodology is defined by the Global AML & Sanctions Risk Assessment Framework which outlines how the Residual Risk and Control environment is assessed as part of the BWRA process. Our examination of SEL’s 2023 BWRA found that it clearly outlines the UK specific regulatory requirements which its assessment considers. For example, SEL's BWRA acknowledges that the MLR 2017 requires firms to undertake a money laundering and terrorist financing risk assessment, in order to assess their risks and controls, taking in consideration the size and nature of the business. Thereby, we noted that SEL's BWRA outlines the ways in which it adheres to local regulatory standards.
 Further, the format of the BWRA has been enhanced as the Firm more clearly demonstrates its individual product risk assessments. The Firm uses its products as the foundation of its assessment whereby each section includes an assessment of its products against other risk factors, such a customer, geographic, transactions, and delivery channels. Further, this includes dedicated assessments against the following SEL products:
 ● CashApp;
 ● Cash Green;
 ● Managed Payments; and
 ● Square Non-CCA Loan.
 In addition, during an interview with the MLRO, the Skilled Person was informed that SEL has updated its approach to calculate its inherent risk and control effectiveness scores within its BWRA following our Stage One review. The Firm opined that, while the BWRA methodology mostly remains the same, SEL has engaged with a third party provided, PwC, for the use of a risk score calculator tool.
 However, the Skilled Person’s review of SEL’s financial crime BWRA identified the following limitations with the Firm’s approach:
 ● The Firm’s BWRA Methodology has not been updated to illustrate how it implements PwC’s risk scoring tool within its financial crime risk assessments. Additionally, the Firm has not demonstrated within the design of its BWRA Methodology that it understands how PwC’s risk scoring tool operates in practice.
 ● As a result of not being provided with PwC’s licenced risk scoring tool, BDO were unable to clearly identify how SEL arrived at its inherent risk and control effectiveness scores. There is also limited narrative contained within the BWRA to support its assessment and to demonstrate how it arrived at its respective risk scores. For example, we noted specific control effectiveness scores within the BWRA. This includes the effectiveness scores:
 o Cash Gold – 1.99;
 o Cash Green – 1.84;
 o Managed Payments – 2.26; and
 o Non-CCA Loans – 2.22.
 ● The Skilled Person found that there is no clarity as to how these control effectiveness scores for each product have been achieved and why there is reasonable variance between the allocated scores.
 ● BDO’s assessment of SEL’s BWRA, and supporting methodology documentation, found that while the Firm's inherent risk assessments references global typologies, such as FATF, it is not clear as to how such typologies has informed its inherent risk assessments. 
 ● There is insufficient narrative to explain how the effectiveness of its control framework is suitable to mitigate its inherent risk exposure. For example, the BWRA includes statements without supporting explanations to evidence its rationale. For example, 'Cash Customer' were assessed as low risk "due to the recent limits implemented".
 ● The Firm's control effectiveness assessments do not appear to consider the effectiveness of how its controls perform in practice. While the Skilled Person acknowledges that SEL’s financial crime controls are subject to ongoing reviews via outsourced QC, CMP, and IA testing, there is no evidence provided within the BWRA to outline how the results of any financial crime testing has informed its overall control effectiveness assessments. 
 ● SEL’s describes its inherent risks at a high level only and does not clearly demonstrate how its specific products may lead to financial crime. For example, during an interview with the MLRO, the interviewee highlighted that a key risk with its product relates to its “card not present” factor. This relates to instances where consumers may purchase products via SEL merchants using technology, such as ‘Apple Pay’ and ‘Google Pay’, whereby a physical banking card is not present. Despite this, BDO found no reference to SEL’s “card not present” risk within its BWRA.
 

 As an observation, the Skilled Person noted that the Firm’s BWRA incorporates quantitative data relating to externally submitted SARs and the total number of low, medium, and high risk merchants. While the Skilled Person acknowledges this area of good practice, the Firm could further this approach by incorporating additional KRI and KPI information to support its inherent risk assessments. For example, the Firm could consider incorporating the total number of active PEP customers to identify the likelihood and impact that its PEP customers may have on its business.
Issue Rating Justification:
"As acknowledged by the Skilled Person, SEL has made significant progress in improving its business-wide risk assessment. 
 

 The Firm provided BDO with the BWRA methodology titled 'Global AML & Sanctions Risk Assessment Framework' and the final BWRA report from 2023 which contains all of the information needed to understand the Firm's inherent AML/CFT risk as well as the effectiveness of its controls.
 

 As BDO is aware, the Firm is part of a larger group and a global framework has been adopted for the production of the BWRA in order to drive consistency across the group in line with established industry practice. The documentation provided includes detailed descriptions of how various typologies and external sources have been taken into account in the methodology section of the BWRA (from page 32). However, many of these typologies are not relevant to the SEL's business in light of the straightforward single product, single transaction type, offered to UK residents who must have a linked UK bank account. 
 

 Furthermore, each inherent risk is rated and an explanation is provided for each rating from page 19 of the BWRA. For example, the following were considered for AML risk: Customer Risk, Distribution Channels Risk, Emerging Risk, Geographic Risk, Operational/Employee Risk, Products and Service Risk (which includes transactions). 
 

 The BWRA shows the assessment of the inherent risk, the relevant control and the residual risk for each control in place all of which is based on the controls assessment questionnaire. 
 

 The Firm is committed to maintaining a high standard when it comes to our annual risk assessment and will continue to assess its program on an annual basis making updates where necessary but is of the view that no immediate remediation is required as this does not represent a 'substantial weakness in the control'."
Issue Action Plans:
R34 - BWRA Enhancement - Risk Scoring Documentation Update: MAP 1/2 (1138048)
 R34 - BWRA Enhancement - Control Assessment Requirement Update: MAP 2/2 (1138050)

Issue Title: R35 - Financial Crime Risk Appetite Statement
Issue Rating Justification:
"As discussed during the MLRO Interview and referenced in a follow-up note dated Feb 13, due to the unexpected leave of absence of the Firm's Chief Risk Officer, the Firm has engaged Deloitte to assist with embedding its enterprise risk management framework. 
 

 The next phase of Deloitte’s engagement to be carried out in Q1 2024 and beyond will be the placement of a Deloitte secondee in the Firm's enterprise risk management function. 
 

 This individual will analyse the Firm's risk register; undertake a review of all of its inputs, related controls (incl. any testing); set the risk appetite metrics for the remaining risk categories (other than Finance, which have been set) thus, finalising the Firm's risk appetite statement for board review and approval; review any product assessments for planned launches later in 2024; continue the risk awareness required to develop the framework with various stakeholders across the business; and provide relevant governance updates (e.g., to RGC, board / risk committee). 
 

 As party of the initial S166 the Firm considered the recommendations around KPIs and KRIs and adopted those that it felt were appropriate to its business model and AML/CFT risk profile. These KRIs and KPIs have been reported to the Firm's Outsourcing Oversight Committee, its Regulatory Governance Committee and its Board over the course of 2023.
 

 The firm will continue to assess the appropriateness of the KPI/KRIs it monitors and will adjust and enhance its approach where required but is of the view that no immediate remediation is required as this does not represent a 'substantial weakness in the control'."

Issue Title: R36 - Customer Risk Rating (‘CRR’) Model
Issue Rating Justification:
"As acknowledged by the Skilled Person, SEL has made significant enhancements to its customer risk assessment programme.
 

 The customer risk assessment uses customer risk factors and data collected at onboarding to assess the customer, and to inform the level of the due diligence that should be applied. The assessment is not influenced by the level of the due diligence applied, rather it is the other way around.
 

 Firstly, the level of due diligence required by a scenario is defined in the firm's SEL Square 2023 AML/CTF Program and in the UK Seller KYC Standard Operating Procedure, where standard and enhanced due diligence measures are outlined in detail, including the process for analysts to follow. SEL therefore does not believe it necessary to restate this in the aCRR and cCRR policy documents.
 

 The firm has considered the recommendation about broadening the intrinsic risk factors and would like to provide some clarification. Both the filing of a SAR and the identification of material adverse media relating to financial crime issues result in account closure, therefore the risk level of the account is not relevant. The CRR already uses hundreds of data points that consider the merchant type, activity fraud risk, and SEL's fraud systems and rules additionally monitor this.
 

 The Firm is of the view that these recommendations do not represent a weakness in the control and is of the view that no immediate remediation is required."

Issue Title: R38 - Nature and Purpose of the Business Relationship
Issue Description:
The Skilled Person’s review of SEL’s UK Seller KYC Standards note that the procedure documents a CDD requirements checklist for onboarding the different legal entity types. These requirements include:
 ● Identifying and verifying the business name and/or company registration number;
 ● Identifying and verifying the registered address of the business;
 ● Identifying and verifying the ownership structure of the entity;
 ● Verifying the status of the company (e.g. active, inactive, struck off);
 ● Identify the nature of the business and MCC. The Skilled Person identified that the Firm conducts ongoing monitoring of the client through the transaction monitoring process as well as the ongoing review process documented under the KYC Standard procedure. As part of the Firm’s ongoing review process, which is documented within the KYC Standard, a review includes, but is not limited to, analysing the following factors, ensuring that they do not give rise to concerns and align with the expected profile of the seller:
 ● The purpose of the account
 ● The transaction activity to date
 ● Business information
 ● Ownership information
 The Standard states that the information reviewed is available through Regulator, online, or through other third-party information providers such as LinkedIn, Facebook, Instagram, Etsy shop, etc.
 Further to the above, the Skilled Person’s control testing identified that there were limitations in both SEL’s ability to fully understand the Seller it was establishing a business relationship with at the point of onboarding, as well as the quality of the information obtained and retained on file. The control testing identified the following:
 ● In 19/20 CDD files reviewed, it was noted by SEL in the respective Checklists provided to the Skilled person for each sample under “Nature and intended purpose of the account”, that “SEL has assessed the nature of its products through its dedicated product assessments and business wide risk assessments and all sellers undergo a stringent customer risk assessment process. Furthermore, sellers are required to provide information about the nature of their business and expected revenue at onboarding. In a case-by-case basis, SEL may request furthermore detailed information to better understand its clients’ businesses and nature.” ● The Skilled Person observed that the above commentary did not differ across the files, regardless of level of risk of the Merchant, and did not provide specific information or assessments for each sample.
 ● In 1/20 files, it was noted in the Checklist by SEL under “Nature and Intended Purpose”, “We collect the type of business and expected revenue”.
 ● In 20/20 of the CDD files reviewed, the respective Checklists outlined for “Closedd merchant application form”, that “The Square application Screens were provided as part of the evidence for item 4.2. We record the responses in our account and case management system.”. This refers to general application screens as per SEL’s “UK IDV Process Flow”, however this process did not evidence the application form that was Closedd and provided by a Merchant as part of the on-boarding process. ● In 11/20 CDD files reviewed, the business type was noted as “Individual/Sole trader’ without any further documentary evidence of investigation or business verification checks being Closedd to validate the Merchant’s business model / activity, similar to how Companies House is utilised to verify companies’ activities. Such evidence could include a copy of an income tax self-assessment and VAT records
 ● In one case it was identified by the Skilled Person that the name and company number on file was incorrect, as well as the MCC, with the account frozen by SEL. Our review found that this may be a result of the heavy reliance being placed on the automation of the process and the system utilised to pull data from Companies House, without a formalised QA process of this in place. The Skilled Person noted that this also raises issues in relation to record keeping on file to evidence a sufficient audit trail for each Seller.
 While our review noted that there was “Expected Revenue” stated within all of the 20 CDD files as per the Business Details screenshots, this was all that was documented in relation to the expected transactional activity of the Seller, with no time frame outlined. There was no note for example, of estimated average transaction value or estimated volume of transactions.
 Finally, the expected revenue figures noted on all CDD files reviewed did not provide sufficient consideration for the specific Seller, with the amount being selected by the Seller using ranges of values, of which the same set of ranges appeared throughout the files, for example £0-83,000, £500,000 -£1,000,000.
Issue Rating Justification:
"The Firm has implemented the recommendations from the December 2022 S166 Skilled Persons Report related to enhancing the element of the firm's onboarding process related to the seller's nature of business.
 

 The firm collects expected yearly revenue at onboarding and the Firm's CRR system evaluates expectations based on the nature, size and location of the seller. Furthermore, sellers are continuously compared to others of the same business type to risk assess for expected vs. actual activity. The requirement to understand nature and purpose is to 'assess, and where appropriate obtain information on, the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship'. SEL considers that its current processes are sufficient to meet this baseline, and as such considers that the finding is a recommendation and not a material finding.
 

 The firm is not aware of a requirement or recommendation in legislation or JMLSG that requires that income tax self-assessment forms or VAT records be collected as standard for sole trader customers.  
 

 The firm will continue to assess the appropriateness of its onboarding process annually and will adjust and enhance its approach where required and is of the view that no immediate remediation is required"
Issue Action Plans: R38 - Nature and Purpose of the Business Relationship: MAP 1/1 (1138058)

Issue Title: R39 - Enhanced Due Diligence
Issue Description:
The Skilled Person noted that, in line with SEL’s AML / CTF Program document, an EDD review will be triggered if an account meets one or more of the following factors: 
 ● Accounts with confirmed enforcement hits;
 ● Accounts with confirmed PEP/RCA hits;
 ● Accounts with a high risk MCC; or
 ● Merchants identified as high risk through the aCRR & CCRR.
 One of the key drivers that determines if a Merchant is high-risk and requires EDD is the Customer Risk Rating (CRR). When a seller is onboarded, the Firm determines their risk level through the aCRR Model, which uses the seller’s onboarding details such as the business type, product and services sold, estimated revenue etc, to compute a risk scoring which will then determine the initial risk rating. Once the seller has been onboarded the cCRR will be applied, where the seller’s risk rating will be subject to further changes following any ongoing monitoring activities such as high Gross Payment Value (“GPV”). As documented in R36 above, the CRR process is conducted at both onboarding and on an ongoing basis where the risk rating is derived from various customer variables such as:
 ● Seller’s jurisdiction of incorporation 
 ● Jurisdiction of associated persons (e.g. beneficial owners, directors etc)
 ● Type of business
 ● Volume of transactions – assessed within the first 90 days of opening the account
 ● Screening results (e.g. PEP)
 The CRR model will then in turn determine what risk rating is applied at onboarding and at an ongoing basis. The Firm have in place intrinsic risk factors that override the risk rating where deemed a high risk. The only Intrinsic risk factor currently considered by SEL is PEP risk.
 Further to the above, the EDD Procedure outlines that periodic reviews will be Closedd on an annual or on a trigger event basis where the cCRR has picked up changes that would influence the client risk scoring, this review includes the following:
 ● performing a web search on individual names, business name, email address and phone number. These results are then used to determine if the merchant’s web presences is sufficient to understand the business. Further information such as the business website, online reviews on google and using google maps to review the payment locations to ensure that it is line with the expected business activity location.
 ● confirming the MCC aligns to activity on the accounts, products etc. – This is done through aligning the MCC code with the business activity. E.g. a coffee and tea shop would have an MCC number of 5814. The MCC codes used by Square are listed by the regulators VISA and are also checked against the regulators MCC codes to provide the most accurate MCC code for the seller.
 ● performing additional negative news and/or criminal history search for potential risks related to the type of business, reputational risk, or ML/TF (This includes the use of Google string search)
 ● review of transactions: reviewing the payment graphs, item descriptions and timestamps and comparing with the information held on file to assess whether it is in line with the stated purpose and intended nature of the relationship with SEL.
 Additionally, in the Stage One review, it was recommended that the Firm calibrates its aCRR model to ensure that the volume of payments of its customers aligns with the expected volume of payments for the Seller’s MCC, and the score percentile threshold is lowered to capture a higher percentage of high-risk Seller’s. However, it is noted the machine learning tool does not assess the risks attributed to transactional volumes at onboarding. Instead, the Firm is ensuring that payments are in line with expectations through the Continuous CRR model, where payment volumes, sizes, and timing is compared against other sellers in their location and business type and therefore the recommendation from the previous review is no longer applicable..
 When conducting an EDD review, the AML/CTF Policy states that an analyst will review all information available and, on completing their review, will escalate all EDD cases where enforcement hits or PEP hits are confirmed and all EDD cases where suspicious activity has been detected to the MLRO for approval. Where there is a change in beneficial owner or director, the Firm will request additional information from the seller to verify these changes. The MLRO’s decision to approve or decline the customer is then made, along with the documented rationale.
 Additionally, through discussions held with the MLRO, BDO noted that SEL primarily relies on its automated onboarding controls to identify a merchant’s Source of Wealth (“SoW”) information. However, in scenarios where information is not publicly available, the MLRO opined that the Firm will request specific SoW documentation to support its view. Notwithstanding the above, the Skilled Person noted that there is no mention of the Firm’s approach to identifying and verifying a Merchant’s SoW within the AML/CTF Programme, the KYC Standard Procedure or the UK IDV Process Flow. This was also evident in the control testing where there were insufficient levels of SoW documented for high-risk clients. The Skilled Person observed that the Firm’s intrinsic risk factors that overrides the overall risk rating only covers PEP as a high-risk factor and does not consider other high risk factors. This is discussed in detail above in R36.
 The Skilled Person’s review also found that SEL has not addressed the previous R39.3 to remove the GPV as a trigger threshold for the application of EDD or documented a clear rationale as to why the Firm have accepted this risk and not implemented this recommendation. During our discussions with the Firm we understand that they have chosen to only review sellers after they have taken a certain amount of transactions because this allows agents during manual review to have sufficient information to evaluate and make a solid judgement.
 As part of our control testing review, gaps were found in respect to a distinct lack of SoW identification and verification specific to each Seller:
 ● For 2/20 CDD files reviewed, as per the Checklists, SoW is noted simply as “N/A” with no further information noted on file.
 Our control testing identified one file as being a different Seller/company to that noted on file, highlighting the potential inconsistency in the automated process. It should be noted that once this was identified by SEL the account was frozen by SEL.
 ● In 1/20 CDD Files reviewed, there was a lack of clarity around the BO’s within the business, with SEL stating as per the Checklist, “SEL is aware company directors and UBOs are out of date. A case was queued (case token F659F14ACA in the 'SCA-438' file) to review and update. However, this service is currently in the testing phase as we work to further finetune the results.
Issue Rating Justification:
"The Firm has implemented the recommendations from the December 2022 S166 Skilled Persons Report related to enhanced due diligence.
 

 The firm has considered the recommendation about broadening the intrinsic risk factors. The CRR already uses hundreds of data points that consider the merchant type, activity fraud risk, and SEL's fraud systems and rules additionally monitor this.
 

 The firm collects SOW information for its PEP customers, which is collected initially as an SSP (request for information) from the customer. The case is then escalated and reviewed by the MLRO, who decides whether further measures are required by the situation. Therefore, there is a clear and consistent approach at the operational level for the treatment of PEPs as regards SOW. Beyond that, 2nd line Compliance makes a case-by-case decision on additional enhanced measures. To this end, the firm's procedures in guiding correct action on the treatment of PEPs and collection of SOW are clear and consistent.
 

 All information and documentation, including SOW, provided by SEL's merchants is retained in its Case Management System (Regulator) and evidence of this has been provided.
 

 The firm will continue to assess the appropriateness of its EDD process annually and will adjust and enhance its approach where required and is of the view that no immediate remediation is required."
Issue Action Plans: R39 - Enhanced Due Diligence - EDD Process Anual Assessment: MAP 1/1 (1138060)
Closure Request Justification: Due to system limitation, Issue Due Date = Issue Identified Date

Issue Title: R42 - Escalation and Sign-off Channels
Issue Description:
While the Skilled Person has established that SEL’s MLRO is responsible for approving the commencement and continuation of PEP relationships, no other relationships that represent higher risk factors such as high-risk MCC or as a result of SEL’s aCRR or cCRR require approval of any kind, whether by the MLRO or Head of the KYC and Sanctions team.
 The Skilled Person’s review identified that the Firm's approval process is automated and therefore is not a documented procedure within the KYC standards document. The Firm only conduct manual review where there is flag on the account on a high-risk client where additional information is required and therefore the manual review team will review and approve the file accordingly. The Firm do not have in place a formalised QA process which reviews the automated reviews and ensures that they are satisfied with the automated approval outcome.
 As part of the control testing, the Skilled Person reviewed a sample of 20 CDD files. As detailed above, approvals of the customer onboarding process were only noted as per the ‘Business Details’ screenshots, noting an automated ‘Approved’, with no further evidence of an approval process.
 It is also to note that 1/20 CDD files reviewed had PEP hits, although there was clear rationale around why the PEP hit was cleared and discounted, there was a lack of evidence of any communication, escalation or approvals undertaken, including names of any individuals involved in the process.
Issue Rating Justification:
"SEL has a well-established process ensuring higher risk relationships are approved by the MLRO, which are relationships involving PEPs, customers with complex ownership structure and potential sanctions-related concerns. This is evidenced by the UK Compliance Tracker previously provided to the Skilled Person, which contains an audit trail of all cases escalated to the MLRO for sign-off.
 

 The firm will continue to assess the effectiveness of this approval process annually and will adjust and enhance its approach where required but is of the view that no immediate remediation is required as this does not represent a 'substantial weakness in the control'."
Issue Action Plans: R42 - Escalation and Sign-off Channels - Responsabilities and Escalation Procedures: MAP 1/1 (1138062)

Issue Title: R43 - Transaction Risk Assessment
Issue Rating Justification:
"Square utilises an external consultancy firm (Guidehouse) to conduct the coverage assessment. The assessment work includes reference to the types of products and services offered within a given market and the associated product risk. Compliance reviews each of the items and evaluates the risk of a gap in coverage, focusing on themes and allocating a risk rating to each to determine priority for remediation. The Firm’s Head of Compliance is consulted during the assessment process and is involved in drafting and approving any remediation.
 

 The Firm is of the view that the TMRA and BWRA are aligned. It is evident from the BWRA that through remediation efforts via the CRR, the Firm is comfortable with the TM Program Controls. 
 

 As part of the 2024 TMRA exercise, the Firm will continue to add further detail to the rationale underlying the Firm’s conclusions in order to further support its reasoning."

Issue Title: R44 - Transaction Monitoring Rules and Scenarios
Issue Description:
As outlined in our previous review, SEL has implemented a bespoke TM rules and scenarios system which is aligned to Group standards. The Firm’s TM controls consist of a “queue” system that capture significant numbers of alerts related to customer activity. The three UK specific queues are:
 1. “gb_intl”;
 2. “gb_prepaid”; and
 3. “gb_pan_concentration”.
 The above queues are set at a global level but adapted for the UK market and run daily with a lookback over transactions from the preceding seven days. The first queue concentrates on customers in the UK with international transactions and the second on the use of prepaid cards (issued by other card providers). The third queue aims to detect repeat round currency card payments where most of the GPV is concentrated on a set of repeating Primary Account Numbers (“PAN”). This queue generates the largest proportion of customer alerts, which stems from the way in which it is structured to capture a range of factors. SEL has defined specific thresholds within each queue, such as:
 GB_Intl Thresholds
 ● Total Amount = £2,370;
 ● International Amount Percentage = 75%;
 ● Card Not Present (“CNP”) Country Percentage = 50%; and
 ● High Risk Country Count = 1.
 GB_PrePaid
 ● Total Amount = £2,150;
 ● Total Count = 18; and
 ● Prepaid Amount Percentage = 50%.
 2
 GB_PAN_Concentration
 ● Total Amount Per User = £2,055;
 ● Total Count Per User = 10; and
 ● Per PAN Amount Percentage = 50%. During the 12 months of TM alerts provided by the Firm, alerts from this queue accounted for 87% of total alerts. In contrast, the prepaid cards queue accounted for 8.5% and international card transactions only 4.5% of the alerts generated.
 As noted within our assessment of the Firm’s cCRR model, SEL has designed a CRA matrix which uses machine learning technology that is informed by data from its merchants. Through discussions held with SEL’s data teams responsible for its cCRR, we observed that SEL is able to identify thematic trends based on its merchant’s line of business. Thus, SEL can track typical payment activity for merchant’s, such as barber shops, to build an “average model” of merchant behaviour.
 However, the Skilled Person noted in our current review that SEL has not taken steps to incorporate the information which it holds regarding the Merchants Line of Business within its TM rules and scenarios. The Skilled Person acknowledges that, at times, during our TM sample review the commentary recorded by analysts may note that the activity is in line with the merchant’s type of business, we found no evidence that SEL has incorporated Merchant Line of Business data into its specific TM scenarios.
Issue Rating Justification:
"The Firm acknowledges the comments from the Skilled person. In the first skilled person report the firm was asked to ""...configure its systems to assess and compare the behaviour of customer types like taxi firms, jewellers, and hairdressers, for example, on a risk rated basis."" The Firm has explored ways to assess and compare the behavior of different customer business types that could allow us to track deviations from expected behavior.
 

 The Firm has formed the view that this can best be achieved through the use of the cCRR model. 
 

 The customer risk assessment system (CRR) assesses customer risk at onboarding and on an ongoing basis. This system takes into account the merchant's line of business, and also compares each merchant's activity to other merchants of the same type to monitor for anomalies. Where anomalies are detected, the merchant risk level is increased and a case is queued for manual review.
 

 The Firm is committed to having appropriate ongoing monitoring in place that is suitable given the size and complexity of the Square product, customer base and market we operate in, and will continue to assess and enhance ongoing monitoring but is of the view that no immediate remediation is required as this does not represent a 'substantial weakness in the control'."
Issue Action Plans: R44 - Transaction Monitoring Rules and Scenarios - Deviation Rule: MAP 1/1 (1138070)

Issue Title: R45 - TM Coverage Gap
Issue Description:
The Skilled Person’s previous assessment of the configuration of SEL’s TM controls identified that it did not track the deviation of expected or actual customer activity. Further, we also noted that SEL should conduct a review of the seven-day lookback component of its TM queues and consider introducing rules that assess activity over extended timelines. SEL’s seven-day lookback approach consists of the Firm reviewing the previous seven days’ worth of transaction data to identify an average payment activity for the merchant. The Firm will then consider and review any payments which are deemed to either be “Below the Line” or “Above the Line” so that it captures payments that are deemed to be unusual.
 As noted in R44 above, SEL has implemented TM systems and controls which are based on three “queues”.
 The ‘Square TM Rulebook” describes that the “Total Amount” trigger relates to the total minimum aggregate value within the seven-day window which will prompt an alert for investigation. The “Total Count” trigger relates to the minimum number of transactions which will prompt an alert. Specific definitions relating to the “International Amount Percentage”, “Prepaid Amount Percentage”, and “PAN Amount Percentage” are detailed in R44.
 The Skilled Person’s current review of the coverage of the Firm’s TM scenarios identified that it has made amendments to the specific thresholds which would trigger an alert. For example, the ‘Square Transaction Monitoring Rulebook’ notes that its ‘GB_Intl’ queue has been amended whereby the “total amount” value to flag an alert had increased from £2,000 to £2,370 and the “International Amount Percentage” scenario has been reduced from 75% to 60%. While the Skilled Person notes that SEL has demonstrated good practice to review, and where necessary revise, its TM scenarios, it has not provided an audit trail to evidence how or why it has arrived at its conclusions. Additionally, while amendments have been made to SEL’s queue-based TM scenarios, we noted that the Firm has not updated its controls to identify whether a merchant has deviated from its usual or expected activity as previously recommended.
 Further, the Skilled Person found that the Firm has performed a review of its seven-day lookback component within its TM queue system to identify whether this timeframe is appropriate. Through discussions held with the Firm, the Skilled Person was informed that the Firm’s Data Analytics Team performed a review of SEL’s seven-day lookback component in Q2 2023. The Firm provided extracts of the Data Analytic Teams review which concluded that the “number of users flagged was more or less steady when a 7 or 14 day lookback window was applied, but doubled when a 30 day lookback window was used. Nearly 50% of the users flagged in the 30 day lookback window (42) were flagged on the first day of the test period. Effectively, all the units who met the criteria over the past 30 days but had a case opened more than 14 days were flagged on the first day of the test period”. As a result of this, SEL determined that while expanding the queue logic lookback period to 30
 days would yield a higher number of alerts; the majority of those alerts flagged on the first day of the test
 period and would have been captured by the existing 7 day lookback.
 Therefore, the Skilled Person’s review found that the Firm has demonstrated that it has tested the appropriateness of the design of its seven-day lookback system whereby the results have informed the design of its TM systems and controls.
Issue Rating Justification:
"The Firm acknowledges the comments from the Skilled Person. The Firm has explored ways to assess and compare the behavior of different customer business types that could allow us to track deviations from expected behavior.
 

 The Firm has formed the view that this can best be achieved through the use of the cCRR model. 
 

 The finding relates to the ongoing monitoring of customer activity to compare to expectations. SEL scrutinises transactions to ensure that they are consistent with its knowledge of the customer, the customer's business and risk profile through a multitude of control mechanisms, which include its fraud, transaction monitoring and customer risk assessment (CRR) systems. The Firm's CRR system evaluates expectations based on the MCC code of the seller. Furthermore, sellers are continuously compared to others of the same business type to risk assess for expected vs. actual activity.
 

 The Firm is committed to having appropriate ongoing monitoring in place that is suitable given the size and complexity of the Square product, customer base and market we operate in, and will continue to assess and enhance ongoing monitoring annually but is of the view that no immediate remediation is required."
Issue Action Plans: R45 - TM Coverage Gap - Deviation Rule: MAP 1/1 (1138072)

Issue Title: R47 - TM Alert and QC Training
Issue Description:
SEL’s 2023 AML & CTF Program outlines that the Firm maintains a Quality Control (“QC”) function. The AML & CTF Program states that: “...quality control process exists as a process to review the quality of investigative research, actions, documentation, and the associated outcomes”.
 

 Further, the purpose of the QC function is to review actions taken by the Transaction Monitoring team for consistency with established procedures and to provide reporting for management and senior leadership teams and the MLRO on the results of the reviews. In addition, the Skilled Person noted that the results of QC reviews are used to identify opportunities for training and development, as well as continuous improvement for the local compliance team and the AML/CTF Program. As outlined in our Stage One report, SEL documents its TM QC approach within the ‘Square Compliance QC (UK) Quality Control Program United Kingdom’. This includes the objectives, responsibility, scope, road map, review process and outcomes of its quality control programme. We identified that SEL has amended its QC program. For example. SEL has revised its QC sample size selection from 1-4% to 3-4% of all cases generated for each TM queue.
 

 As noted above in R30, SEL has enhanced its financial crime MI. We observed that the MI produced for the OOC includes QC rates of controls sampled within the defined period. Further, our review of the ‘SEL Outsourcing Committee Pack - October 2023’ identified that 319 TM alerts were reviewed by the QC team and that 94% of alerts reviewed had passed the review. However, the Skilled Person’s review of SEL’s QC framework identified that whilst the Firm has designed controls to enable SEL’s QC team to perform tests of its TM controls, our assessment identified that there is no evidence of how the QC function undertakes this review in practice.
 

 Further, the Skilled Person notes that SEL provides specific QC related training to staff responsible for performing assurance reviews of the Firm’s operational controls. Our review of the ‘QC Training Plan’ identified that SEL provides the following QC related training: • New Hire QC Overview; • New Hire Training; • Training Schedule Creation; • Scheduling of Training/Shadows; • Skills Matrix Maintenance; • System Access Setup for New Hires; and • Attending Ops Team Meetings to Present QC Feedback on Observation. 
 

 However, as noted in R48, the Skilled Person identified inconsistencies with the quality of rationales used by analysts within their dispositions of TM alerts. BDO observed that in 8/20 cases we identified limitations with the quality of the narratives recorded by analysts which did not clearly illustrate the risks identified with the alert as well as the steps taken to reach their conclusions. The Skilled Person notes that the lack of a clear audit trail which outlines the full details of the investigation adversely impacts senior management oversight as the Firm is unable to determine whether all of the risk factors associated with an alert haves been appropriately considered within the investigations.
 

 Therefore, although SEL’s QC Team has received training related to their role, the results of our TM sample review reflects limitations with the embeddedness of such training.
Issue Rating Justification:
"The Firm confirms that staff performing TM alert reviews and performing QC functions are fully trained in relation to the adequacy of appropriate investigation and rationale for closing alerts. 
 

 The Firm notes there is a suggested gap in QC documentation to provide evidence of how the QC function undertakes this review in practice. This is contained within checklists that the QC team use to perform their review.
 

 The above notwithstanding, the Firm accepts BDO's suggestion that all risk factors associated with an alert be documented in the case comments to show that appropriate consideration has been given to these factors during the investigation and will enhance the procedures and comments to reflect this."
Issue Action Plans: R47 - TM Alert and QC Training - Deliverying Training: MAP 1/1 (1138074)

Issue Title: R48 - TM Alert Review
Issue Rating Justification:
"In December 2023, the Square TM (UK) Guidance: Best Practices for Case Comments document was retired and replaced with the Square Europe Ltd. (UK) Reference: Best Practices for Case Comments | Square Transaction Monitoring document as part of the 2023 Square Financial Crimes procedure enhancement project. 
 

 Please note that the Firm’s Financial Crimes Transaction Monitoring program provides initial assessments of alerted or referred merchant activity, and serves as a first level triage function within the Firm’s Financial Crimes program. The Transaction Monitoring analysts determine if the alerted or referred activity warrants an investigation for potentially unusual activity. TM work is subject to formal QA and regular feedback is provided to the team.
 

 The Firm already includes this in the TM alert reviews but will document this more clearly in the procedures.
 

 In light of the above, SEL requests that the Skilled Person reviews and either lowers the impact of the finding to Low. "

Issue Title: R49 - Sanction Ops Procedural Documents Update
Issue Description:
Since the Stage 1 review, SEL have made some enhancements to its policies and procedures to include greater detail on how customer screening analysts and compliance staff review, escalate and disposition alerts and the expectations of the analysts. In particular, Section 3 of the SEL's ‘Sanctions Compliance — Square Standard Operating Procedures’ outlines the investigation processes and workflows for actioning screening alerts generated from watchlist screening,transaction screening, invoice keyword screening and bill pay keyword screening. This includes the "RoboCop Analyst Decision Matrix" which provides clarity on individuals or entities and the criteria they are required to meet before the analyst may either clear or escalate the alert. For example, should an entity have a match on name and location (country and/or city) then the alert is required to be escalated a SanctionOps Lead for further review.
 

 The Procedure also cites additional considerations that can be taken into account when assessing potential matches that may allow for a manager to discount the match based on the following criteria:
 • Entity or Vessel vs. Individual, clear based on Type mismatch.
 • Date SDN added to the watchlist is not possible to be the same person; age improbability.
 • News reports or government confirmation that SDN was killed or incarcerated.
 • Visual comparison of customer’s photo ID and photo of SDN confirms they are not the same person.
 

 The Procedure clearly outlines the necessary steps to be taken for an alert to be cleared, to be escalated or to request further information from the customer in cases where the analyst is unsure. All true positive matches must be escalated to the Head of Sanctions and the Chief Compliance Officer is consulted for guidance. Further, as indicated within the Firm's remediation tracker, a section outlining the QA process has now been added to the Procedure. Random samples of discounted alerts are pulled daily by the QC Team with an expected accuracy score of 85% or more. Should this target not be met, the team and/or individual are referred to the Global Sanctions Lead and additional training may be recommended.
 

 However, it was noted that the Firm’s Sanctions Procedure does not set out an expected SLA for the amount of time a client is given to provide additional identity documents or information when a possible screening hit cannot immediately be discounted. As part of the Skilled Person’s review, control testing was conducted on a sample of 15 customer screening alerts and in 13 of these samples the rationale to explain SEL’s actions was found to be unclear and either did not explain why an alert had been generated or provide sufficient detail as to why it had been discounted. For example, for alert FB118346C3 the discounting narrative only states that; “Investigation shows name mismatch to denied party. Watchlist case cleared using provided Drivers Licence.” This does not provide an adequate level of detail as it does not outline the specific information pertaining to both the merchant details and the potential sanction hit that allowed the Firm to discount the possible match. It is noted within the Sanctions procedural document that there is no section dedicated to rationale expectations and therefore the level of expected detail for notes left by the analyst is not set out which may cause the notes to be of an inadequate standard.
 

 As per the Firm's AML/CTF Policy "Customers are screened at onboarding and monthly thereafter. It is worth noting also that sanction screening of staff occurs at onboarding and on a quarterly basis thereafter." During the Stage 1 review, the Skilled Person recommended that the Firm conduct an annual review of the 30-day period between customer screening reviews. While it is acknowledged that this was a low risk finding, there is no documented evidence of this process being considered and therefore the risk remains that the Firm could process payments for a sanctioned entity during the 30-day period between screening. Following discussions with the Firm on this point, the Skilled Person had been informed that the Firm will be switching to daily customer screening which would remove this risk.
 

 At the time of writing, however, the screening remains at 30-day intervals. In the Stage 1 review, it was also noted that the Firm's BWRA set the inherent sanctions risk as high which did not appear to be commensurate with the 30-day period set between customer screening. The Skilled Person notes that, as of the 2023 AML/CTF and Sanctions Risk Assessment, the inherent sanctions risk has now been calculated to the be low and therefore aligns more accurately with the 30-day time frame between customer screening for sanctions, PEPs and adverse media.
 

 Additionally, in the Stage 1 review the Skilled Person found that the Firm’s policies and procedures were lacking sufficient detail to confirm the sanctions regimes covered by its screening programme. At the time of writing, it is noted that the Firm’s ‘Global Sanctions Compliance Programme’ was last updated in July 2022 and still only refers to OFAC, HM Treasury, and the Department of Foreign Trade and Affairs. It is acknowledged, however, the Sanctions section of the ‘Anti-Money Laundering, Counter Terrorist Financing & Financial Sanctions Program’, published in April 2023, notes that: “SEL screens all customers against the Office of Financial Sanctions Implementation (OFSI) list as well as the financial sanctions imposed by the United Nations, European Union Consolidated Sanctions List and the US Department of the Treasury- Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) Sanctions List.” As such, the Skilled Person considers this part of the Stage One recommendation sufficiently addressed.
 

 The Firm continues to use a system called ‘Bad Actors’ to manage the process of consolidating watchlists and generating alerts for review in ‘Regulator’. Potential matches against watchlists or adverse media results are tuned into cases in Regulator for analysts to review. Our Stage 1 review recommended that the tuning analysis was reviewed and updated if deemed necessary. As part of the Stage 2 Review it is noted that the 'Watchlist Screening - Tuning Methodology' document has not been updated since January 2021. However, the LexisNexis Configuration document was most recently reviewed in August 2023, covering the tuning of the sanctions and PEP customer screening controls, while the CSI or 'Bad Actors' system configuration document provided was last updated in July 2023 and this covers adverse media screening via Dow Jones. However, while these documents provide a clear overview of the lists being screened against and the parameters set to generate alerts (this remains at 94% minimum for sanctions, PEPs and adverse media), these documents do not make it clear how the Firm are testing these limits to ensure that they are calibrated appropriately. By contrast, the now outdated Watchlist Screening Tuning Methodology contains sections dedicated to configurations testing and sensitivity analysis as well as historical productivity analysis. Following our closing meeting with the Firm, they advised that a new Testing and Validation Procedure was issued at the end of January 2024, however this was not provided to or reviewed by the Skilled Person.
Issue Rating Justification:
"The Firm's sanctions operations procedural documents contain clear decision matrices and expectations for discounting/ escalations. This point notwithstanding, the Firm will further enhance its Sanctions Procedures to include discounting/ escalation rationale best practices. 
 

 As discussed during the walkthroughs, the Firm is moving towards daily rescreening. The project is underway and the Firm will have moved to daily rescreening by the end of June 2024.
 

 The Lexis Nexis and CSI configuration documents are not intended to contain the testing. The Watchlist Screening - Tuning Methodology document provided to BDO in December 2023 contains a section that details the approach to the ""Configurations Testing & Sensitivity Analysis"".  
 

 As part of our ongoing commitment to enhancing our governance, a new Testing and Validation Procedure (for Lexis Nexis and CSI) was published at the end of January 2024. 
 

 In light of this the firm is of the view that no further remediation is required."

Issue Title: R50 - Screening Specific Training
Issue Description:
The Stage 1 review recommended that the Firm ensures that all those responsible for reviewing customer screening alerts are aware of other factors that may require the need for escalation/concern. In response to this recommendation, it was stated in the SEL’s Remediation Plan that the Firm will further enhance existing procedures and training for analysts to identify and escalate other potential (non-sanctions) red flags. 
 

 As noted above in R49, the Firm’s procedures now include a number of additional considerations for analysts to contemplate when dispositioning alerts such as age improbability, open-source research confirming the designated individual to be deceased, incarcerated or visibility distinguishable from the client. As such the Firm is drawing analysts’ attention to a broader range of factors when making a decision on possible screening hits.
 

 The Firm’s ‘Compliance Training Program & Annual Plan’ notes that there is a Sanction Training course provided to new joiners and annually thereafter, but it is specified that this is provided to Verse, a Spanish payment app acquired by Square, rather than SEL. In the general AML training, there are brief mentions of sanctions, PEPs however, there is no mention of adverse media. Furthermore, this training is only general training provided companywide and is not targeted to those in roles with screening responsibilities and as such the level of detail provided in this training is not particularly in depth. However, it is acknowledged that this training does include a section on common red flags outside of the sanctions as stated on the Firm’s remediation plan. Additionally, we also acknowledge, that the Firm conducts ad-hoc training regarding screening, however this is not recorded in the Compliance Training Program.
Issue Rating Justification:
"The Firm has implemented the recommendations from the December 2022 S166 Skilled Persons Report related to enhancing our training program.
 

 All analysts receive direct training related to their assigned queues during their initial onboarding period and at all points thereafter when there are changes to the procedures or new queue assignments. 
 

 Training includes reviews of relevant standard operating procedures, user guides, terms of service, etc., as well as side-by-side shadow sessions, live training sessions, and ongoing coaching and oversight by their manager and Quality Control teams. 
 

 The Firm will undertake to enhance its screening specific training for analysts and will include ad-hoc training throughout the year."
Issue Action Plans: R50 - Screening Specific Training: MAP 1/1 (1138086)

Issue Title: R51 - Training Log Update
Issue Description:
The Squareup Europe Limited Anti-Money Laundering, Counter Terrorist Financing & Financial Sanctions Program states that:
 • all new employees receive AML/CTF training through an online based interactive training course. The online based interactive training has a comprehensive assessment at the end of the course and must be Closedd within 30 days of joining SEL.
 • all employees are required to take refresher training on an annual basis and is designed to enable employees to understand:
 o What activities amount to ML and TF and how they should be reported;
 o Key elements of the AML/CTF laws including coverage of:
 ▪ developing and maintaining an AML/CTF Program and the key features of such;
 ▪ SEL’s KYC obligations;
 ▪ SEL’s reporting obligations; and
 ▪ record keeping and retention obligations.
 o The type of ML/TF risks that the Firm might face and how employees are able to spot these risks.
 o The consequences of non-compliance with the AML/CTF laws, on SEL and its employees.
 o The effect on society of the failure to implement good AML/CTF programs.
 • targeted and more in-depth AML training is provided to
 o Local Customer Success teams
 o Sales and Account Executive team
 o Board of Directors, in person/via video conference (on annual basis)
 o Compliance personnel whereby a training program is tailored to their compliance role and regulatory issues and risk specific to their region.
 

 While the Program outlines the training requirements for all employees of SEL, it does not outline the requirements for maintaining a training log and the information to be recorded on the training log, the document states that employee training records will be maintained for at least five years from the end of the business relationship.
 

 The Compliance Training Program and Annual Plan is owned by Block and is applicable to all Block business lines, subsidiaries and jurisdictions globally. The document outlines that regional leads own their training content and are responsible for sharing their training content with the Compliance Training Program Manager.
 

 Additionally, International Compliance Leads are responsible for advising and providing input on relevant training requirements, local content for board, delivers the training to the directors, and records attendance for training. Additionally, the Plan states that:
 

 “Training records are maintained and stored in order to demonstrate the implementation of the training program. Training records include:
 • Training Content and Materials, including versions of training materials;
 • Records of Training Completion; and 
 • Where applicable, records of training assessment.”.
 

 The Firm have provided the Skilled Person with a copy of the Learning Assignment Overall Report which documents the training Closedd by employees of the
 Firm which includes: 
 • the learning content; 
 • the date assigned; 
 • the due date; 
 • the number of days completion is overdue by; and 
 • the completion date.
 

 Our review found that whilst the Firm has made some efforts to improve the information recorded on the training log, there are still gaps that need to be addressed by the Firm – namely:
 • The only training module recorded on the log provided to the Skilled Person is the Global AML/CTF training, which is provided by Block.
 • There is no evidence of UK specific training being provided to SEL employees recorded on the log.
 • There is no evidence of AML specific training being provided to the Board of Directors, as outlined in the Program document.
 • There is no evidence of UK specific training being provided to the Block employees who focus specifically on providing outsourced services to SEL.
 • The training log does not record the result of the end of module test and the number of attempts it took to pass the test at the end of the module.
 • The training log has recorded the completion date of the assigned module for a number of the employees before the date that the training was s recorded as being assigned to the individual.
 

 The Firm utilises Computer Based Training (“CBT”) to deliver training to all employees. Under the current process, Block utilises Thomson Reuters content to create the training. The UK specific AML training was prepared by SEL. Our review of the UK AML Training found that: 
 • It considers the UK regulatory landscape including the Money Laundering Regulations, POCA, the Criminal Finances Act, the JMLSG and the FCA handbook.
 • The content is specific to SEL and the products and services offered to clients. For example the training material includes examples of red flags that are specific to Merchants.
 • The assessment at the end of the training module includes SEL specific questions and examples.
 

 However, as noted above the training log does not include any evidence of this training being delivered to relevant SEL and Block employees.
Issue Rating Justification:
"The Firm has implemented the recommendations from the December 2022 S166 Skilled Persons Report related to enhancing our training program.
 

 As discussed during the W3 walkthroughs, the Firm undertakes an annual training needs analysis for its employees and other relevant members of staff who perform outsourced services and creates an annual training schedule on foot of that analysis. 
 

 Training is evaluated throughout the year and new training is created and delivered where needed. This training may take the form of a Workday course, third party training or face-to-face training. Training information is maintained by the training team in Workday. Workday training includes, as standard, the modules tested and the pass/grade obtained. The Scores are recorded within Workday.
 

 The Firm will update its training log so that it includes number of attempts and recorded scores."
Issue Action Plans: R51 - Training Log Update - Track LMS Scores: MAP 1/1 (1138088)

Issue Title: R52 - Sharing Training Log Documents with Board
Issue Description:
The Compliance Training Program and Annual Plan is owned by Block and is applicable to all Block business lines, subsidiaries and jurisdictions globally. The Plan outlines regional specific training which includes UK AML which SEL provides to all new hires and on an annual basis. The Plan also outlines that all new compliance hires will receive enhances new hire training, but the Plan does not outline that this specifically includes AML specific training. 
  
  The Squareup Europe Limited Anti-Money Laundering, Counter Terrorist Financing & Financial Sanctions Program states that targeted and more in-depth AML training is provided to: 
  • Local Customer Success teams
 • Sales and Account Executive team
  • Board of Directors, in person/via video conference (on annual basis)
  • Compliance personnel whereby a training program is tailored to their compliance role and regulatory issues and risk specific to their region. 
  
 Our review found that whilst the Firm has made some efforts to improve the systems and controls in respect of training, there are still gaps that need to be addressed by the Firm: 
  
 The Learning Assignment Overall Report which documents the training Closedd by employees does not include evidence of any additional training, including face-to-face training, webinars, and conferences, are recorded on employee training records to further evidence additional role specific training that the Firm provides, including training provided to the Board. Following our closing meeting with the Firm, they advised that training provided to the Board is recorded by the Corporate Counsel Team, however evidence of this was not provided to the Skilled Person.
Issue Rating Justification:
"The Firm has implemented the recommendations from the December 2022 S166 Skilled Persons Report related to enhancing our training program.
 

 The Skilled Person was provided with the Compliance Training Program & Annual Plan in December 2023. At the Governance & Risk Management walkthrough on 12 December 2023 and during a second CDD/TM/ Screening walkthrough, training was discussed. During these walkthroughs, the Firm discussed the use of annual training needs analysis and its formal training plan for all relevant employees, including the Firm’s employee-wide training and workflow-based training. The 2024 training needs analysis and training plan were under review in December but have since been Closedd and have been provided to BDO. 
 

 Board training is minuted and recorded by the corporate counsel team as part of their tracking of all Board matters. Compliance keeps a log of external training, webinars and conferences which is recorded by the Training Team. All Compliance employees have an annual requirement to Closed their prescribed number of CPD hours.
 

 The Firm is committed to having appropriate training in place that is suitable given the size and complexity of the Square business and will continue to assess training needs but is of the view that no immediate remediation is required as this does not represent a 'substantial weakness in the control'."
Issue Action Plans: R52 - Sharing Training Log Documents with Board: MAP 1/1 (1138090)
Closure Request Justification:
Continuous Education Training Log

Issue Title: 1.1 Internal Procedures: Grouping AML Procedures
Issue Description:
SIL has prepared an Annex (in English) to the AML/CTF SIL´s Manual describing the Spanish AML/CTF regulations/obligations, as well as its internal procedures. However, the Annex does not meet either SEPBLAC´s expectation or best practices. Specifically:
 

 a. The Annex refers, in turn, to different additional procedures that document the controls/processes in place to support compliance with these regulations. This structure, however, is not aligned with SEPBLAC´s expectations and makes it difficult for the reader to correctly understand in totality SIL’s model in place for the prevention of money laundering and financing of terrorism.
 

 Recommendation
 SIL should considers, in continuation of its efforts to improve the Closedness and unification of its control procedures in the area of the prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing, the possibility of grouping all the procedures in this area and translate them into Spanish, in order to meet supervisor ́s expectations.

Issue Title: 2.1 Internal Control Bodies and Board of Directors: External Expert's Report Submission
Issue Description:
SIL did not submit its previous External Expert Report to its Board of Directors within three months of the report’s issuance as required by Article 28 of Law 10/2010. SIL submitted the report on September 26, 2023, however the report was dated March 24, 2023. According to Article 28 of Law 10/2010 “No later than three months from its issue date the report shall be submitted to the Board of Directors or, where appropriate, to the management board or top management body of the obliged entity, which shall take the necessary steps to remedy the detected deficiencies.”
 

 Recommendation 
 As established in Article 28 of Law 10/2010, SIL should ensure that it submits the external expert's report to its administrative bodies within the legally established period (within three months from the issuance of the report).
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: 2.1 Internal Control Bodies and Board of Directors: External Expert's Report Submission: MAP 1/1 (1138094)

Issue Title: 2.2 Internal Control Bodies and Board of Directors: Resolving Outstanding Recommendations From External Expert's Report
Issue Description:
Since 6 out of 14 deficiencies identified by the external expert in its previous annual report have been identified again in this follow-up report, it has been detected that SIL has exceeded the period established for its resolution of one calendar year by Article 38 of Royal Decree Law 304/2014.
 

 Article 38 of Royal Decree Law 304/2014 states that “The Board of Directors of SIL shall take the necessary measures, without delay, to remedy all the deficiencies identified in the External Expert Report. The entity is required to define and implement an action plan to address the deficiencies identified, including both recommendations for rectification and recommendations for improvement. In the case of deficiencies that cannot be resolved immediately, the Board of Directors of SIL shall expressly adopt an action plan, which establishes a precise timetable for the implementation of the corrective measures. This timetable may not exceed one calendar year from the issuance of the report”.
 

 Recommendation
 SIL's board of directors, in accordance with the provisions of Article 38 of Royal Decree 304/2014, should immediately proceed to resolve the outstanding recommendations from the prior External Expert report.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A

Issue Title: 3.1a Know Your Customer: IDV Process Update and Remediation Requirement
Issue Description:
SIL applies the same due diligence measures to all the merchants and has neither defined nor applied specific due diligence measures based on merchant AML/CFT risk typology. Therefore, SIL does not perform enhanced due diligence to customers identified as high risk, as required by Article 20 of Royal Decree 304/2014.
 

 Article 33.c of Royal Decree 304/2014 states that the AML/CFT Manual of a regulated entity must contain a structured procedure for the application of due diligence measures to customers depending on the AML/CFT risk. Further, Article 20 of Royal Decree 304/2014 states that regulated entities must apply enhanced due diligence to high-risk customers.
 

 Recommendation
 SIL should define and apply specific due diligence measures to customers based on their customer typology/AML/CFT risks, which specifies the list of the documents to be obtained in application of such measures, as provided in Articles 20 and 33 c) of Royal Decree 304/2014. Once the specific due diligence measures have been defined, SIL should review its entire Spanish client database and ensure that the due diligence measures applied aligns with its procedures.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: 3.1a Know Your Customer: IDV Process Update and Remediation Requirement: MAP 1/4 (1138098)
Closure Request Justification: Due to system limitation, Issue Due Date = Issue Identified Date

Issue Title: 3.1b Know Your Customer: Applying Due Diligence Measures Before Onboarding
Issue Description:
SIL performs sanctions and PEP screenings within 24 hours after a customer’s account is activated, which means that the customer can fully operate and be settled. This is not in line with Spanish regulatory requirements for due diligence on potential customers to be Closedd prior to onboarding. As established in Article 7.3 of Law 10/2010, regulated entities should not start a business relationship until the due diligence measures have been Closedd. As SIL does not perform sanctions and PEP screenings until after the customer’s account is activated
 

 Recommendation
 SIL should define and apply all due diligence measures before starting a business relationship in alignment with article 7.3 of Law 10/2010.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: 3.1b Know Your Customer: Applying Due Diligence Measures Before Onboarding: MAP 2/4 (1138100)
Closure Request Justification: Due to system limitation, Issue Due Date = Issue Identified Date

Issue Title: 3.1c Know Your Customer: Verifying CDD Information At Onboarding
Issue Description:
SIL does not have a control in place to validate, prior to the activation of the account, the information provided by potential customers on the nature of their business. As established in Article 5 of Law 10/2010 and Article 10 of Royal Decree 304/2014, regulated entities, as part of the due diligence applied to potential customers, should verify the activity or nature of the potential customer’s business through documentary evidence provided by the potential customers or other sources of information.
 It should be noted that during our review of a sample of 100 SIL merchants, we identified 15 merchants whose actual activity was not aligned with the type of business declared by the merchant and 5 merchants were onboarded despite having business types not permitted by SIL.
 

 Recommendation
 SIL should make the necessary efforts to verify the accuracy of the information provided at the time of registration by customers in relation to their professional or business activity, as provided for in Article 5 of Law 10/2010 and Article 10 of Royal Decree 304/2014.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: 3.1c Know Your Customer: Verifying CDD Information At Onboarding: MAP 3/4 (1138102)

Issue Title: 3.1d Know Your Customer: Assigning Risk Rating at Onboarding
Issue Description:
15 out of the 100 files for new merchants sampled do not have an AML/CTF Risk Scoring assigned. Each of these merchants have yet to conduct a transaction and procedurally SIL does not assign an AML/CTF Risk Scoring until there is activity in the account. This however is not aligned with Article 7.1 of Law 10/2010 which requires SIL to provide a Risk Scoring to all registered merchants: “obligated subjects must be able to demonstrate to the competent authorities that due diligence measures adopted have the appropriate scope in view of the risk of money laundering or terrorist financing through a prior risk analysis perform to customers that in any case must be recorded in writing”
 

 Recommendation
 In accordance with Article 7.1 of Law 10/2010, SIL should assign a level of risk in terms of money laundering and terrorist financing to all its merchants at the time of registration.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: 3.1d Know Your Customer: Assigning Risk Rating at Onboarding: MAP 4/4 (1138104)
Closure Request Justification: Due to system limitation, Issue Due Date = Issue Identified Date

Issue Title: 3.2a Know Your Customer: Collecting CDD Information at Onboarding
Issue Description:
SIL´s analysts do not always collect information accrediting the web presence of merchant’s business during onboarding, as required in the Automated Onboarding section of the KYC Procedure (page 5). (Although there is a field to collect this information from merchants, it is not required to move an application forward). It should be noted that during our review of a sample of 100 SIL merchants, we identified 30 merchants that did not have a website or physical presence that could verify the activity declared.
 

 Recommendation
 SIL should establish and apply the necessary controls to ensure that it collects all the information related to the identification and know your client process established in its internal policies.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A

SIL will collect web presence information (1138106)

Issue Title: 3.3 Know Your Customer: Identifying and Rejecting Previously Deactivated Merchants
Issue Description:
SIL does not have in place controls to reject the onboarding of merchants previously frozen or deactivated. It should be noted that 9 out of 100 new merchants sampled had alerted for fraudulent or suspicious transactions and were able to repeatedly open new accounts without any restriction.
 

 Recommendation
 SIL should establish the necessary controls that allow it to identify and reject previously frozen/deactivated merchants during onboarding.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: 3.3 Know Your Customer: Identifying and Rejecting Previously Deactivated Merchants: MAP 1/1 (1138114)
Closure Request Justification: Due to system limitation, Issue Due Date = Issue Identified Date

Issue Title: 3.4 Know Your Customer: Creating Controls to Monitor and Update CDD Information and Remediation Requirement
Issue Description:
SIL does not totally meet regulatory requirement regarding ongoing due diligence. Specifically:
 

 • SIL has not put in place controls for validating and, if applicable, updating customer information and documentation based on AML/CTF risk as part of its ongoing due diligence process, as established in Article 11 of Royal Decree 304/2014, where it states that regulated entities must perform periodic reviews in order to ensure that customer documents, data and information obtained are up to date. For high-risk customers this review must be at least annually as per regulatory requirement.
 It should be noted that in 10 out of the 100 files for new merchants sampled, the identification documents of the account holder and/or related persons were expired.
 

 • SIL only has defined in its internal regulation the frequency for performing the review of high-risk merchants (annually). However, SIL has not established a frequency for medium and low risk merchants. Best practice is to perform it every three years (medium risk) and every five years (low risk).
 

 It should be noted that SIL has not provided the evidence of the list of merchants to whom it had to be applied this due diligence review during the analyzed period.
 

 Recommenation 
 SIL should establish and apply controls for updating merchant documents and information based on AML/CTF risk as part of the ongoing due diligence in accordance with Article 11 of Royal Decree 304/2014. 
 

 Additionally, SIL should review its entire Spanish client database and ensure that the information/documentation maintained is up to date.
 

 SIL should establish controls to monitor the expiration of customer identification documents.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: 3.4 Know Your Customer: Creating Controls to Monitor and Update CDD Information and Remediation Requirement: MAP 1/1 (1138116)

Issue Description:
SIL runs queries against the Commercial Registry to formally identify Spanish legal entities at onboarding but SIL´s analysts does not maintain evidence of these queries for none of the merchants sampled (i.e., a certificate from Commercial Registry).
 

 Recommendation
 SIL should retain evidence (i.e., a certificate) of the checks made in the Commercial Registry in order to be able to justify the formal identification of the legal entities.
