Issue Title: Absence of timely PEP and Sanction screening at onboarding
Issue Description:
At the inception of the Square product in Ireland, SIL did not ensure local PEP and Sanctions screening requirements were included, and adopted a global PEP and Sanctions screening framework that did not fully align with local regulations. This discrepancy was not identified during any periodic reviews of the SIL AML/CTF Program until it was highlighted by an independent audit conducted in March 2023. SIL Compliance informed Internal Audit that it has already developed an action plan to address this risk and it is expected to be implemented in Q3 2024.
 

 The specific misalignment relates to the current SIL EEA Seller KYC procedure, which does not require PEP and sanctions screening to be conducted before establishing a business relationship. SIL initiates both screenings within 24 hours of account activation and during this period, SIL’s sellers are able to fully operate (including withdraw funds) without being validated on PEP and Sanctions screening.
 

 Additionally, since PEP customers (high risk) are identified after onboarding, SIL is not completing customer Enhanced Due Diligence before allowing them to transact. This practice also does not align with local regulations and expectations.
 

 Applicable regulations/guidelines (further detail on appendix 1):
 - Section 37 of the CJA ML/TF Act 2010
 - Section 10.3 of the CBI AML/CTF guidelines
Issue Rating Justification:
"● Adopting a global framework that does not align with local regulations may put SIL at risk of violating AML/CTF laws and facing penalties from regulatory authorities.
 

 ● Onboarding unapproved PEPs or sanctioned customers may lead to non-compliance with the regulatory requirements and guidance resulting in legal actions, fines, penalties and reputational damage."
Issue Action Plans: Adjusting PEP and Sanction Screening Process During Onboarding (1138711)

Issue Title: Timeline for the review and approval of PEP and sanction alerts
Issue Description:
"SIL has not defined the timelines for completing the review and approval for PEP and sanction alerts. IA reviewed the screening procedures and noted that there is no defined timeline for the closure or escalation of alerts by compliance analysts at onboarding and/or ongoing, nor is there a specified timeline for the review and approval of cases escalated to the MLRO, as required by the CBI guidelines. There is no such requirement for sanctions but it is an industry practice.
 

 In 1 out of the 15 PEP, sanctions and adverse media alerts reviewed by IA, it took 68 days from the date of the alert to the approval by the MLRO, which does not meet expected efficiency standards. As per IA’s review, this was an isolated case and the average timeframe for reviewing the sampled PEPs was not excessive (average of 12 days from the PEP alert date to the completion of EDD).
 

 Applicable regulations/guidelines (further detail on appendix 1):
 - Section 5.6.2 ‘Senior Management Approval of PEPs’ of CBI AML/CTF Guidelines"
Issue Rating Justification:
"● Non-compliance with the regulatory guidance may lead to legal actions resulting in fines, penalties and reputational damage.
 

 ● Without clear timelines, there is a risk of delays in addressing alerts and escalating cases, potentially resulting in missed opportunities to mitigate risks promptly."
Issue Action Plans: Updating SIL Screening Procedure (1142672)

Issue Title: There is no designated qualified individual to be responsible for coordinating and monitoring compliance (Regulatory Change Management) with state and federal laws, rules and regulations.
Issue Description:
GCMT reviewed Louisiana Senate Bill 185's state-specific virtual currency requirements for its residents. During walkthroughs with the Legal and Compliance teams, GCMT identified the absence of designated individual(s) and a regulatory change management process for new bills, rules, regulations and their ongoing updates and amendments and therefore were unable to evidence compliance with every aspect set forth by LA SB185 requirements.
 

 These include:
 - Identifying & interpreting applicability of new requirements (i.e., a proactive legal and regulatory analysis intake process)
 - Mapping of requirements to corresponding controls and processes for the identification of gaps
 - Implementing newly established or updated processes or controls (i.e., coordinating compliance)
 - Providing Quality Assurance to confirm that the controls and processes are working as planned (i.e., monitoring compliance)
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

RCA Description: Control Does Not Exist
Issue Rating Justification:
Presents a significant risk to the operations of Block, such as exposure to fraud, material loss, error or misstatement. Overall exposure (existing or potential) is severe and requires an immediate corrective action plan with high priority.
 

 Includes any Issues that may represent violations of requirements set forth "explicitly" in law or regulatory guidance, which could result in an enforcement action via a regulator and/or a material fine.
Issue Action Plans: LA SB185 - Establish a Regulatory Change Management Process (1139392)

 Recent discussions between GCMT and relevant counterparties/stakeholders have led GCMT to conclude that i) the regulatory requirement of designating a Qualified Individual (QI) was satisfied (link to Sarah and team's analysis), and ii) Regulatory Change Management requirements are not prescribed in the Bill's provisions, and therefore should be stricken from the record/issue.

Issue Title: Lack of documented Control for the following Sub-Process: Review Self Reported Claim(s)/Review ATO Closed Account Processing (ACAP) Queue.
Issue Description:
During a Risk and Control Matrix (RCM) exercise for Account Takeover (ATO), the Risk Management Team identified a control gap for the following Sub-Process: Review Self Reported Claim(s)/Review ATO Closed Account Processing (ACAP) Queue. There is no control to mitigate the risk (R.74).

Issue Title: Cash Card Disputes - Compliance with Visa Rules
Issue Description: Currently, in the Cash Card Dispute process, a misalignment exists between Cash App's filing of disputes and Visa's mandated timeframes, particularly concerning Goods & Services Not Received/as Described cases. While Visa requires a 15-day waiting period before disputes can be filed, Cash App permits customers to submit disputes within this window, resulting in subsequent denials. This inconsistency raises regulatory concerns as it may lead to the inadvertent decline of disputes, highlighting the necessity for adherence to required timeframes. Additionally, the lack of an established procedure for Visa bi-annual updates intake necessitates the development of a formalized process. Specifically, there is a need to establish a process for obtaining updates from Visa to Marqeta (disputes processing partner) to Cash Disputes and integrating them into the questionnaire for dispute intake.

Issue Title: Lack of document management process for Knowledge Articles and Past Due Issue for procedure enhancements identified during 2022 testing by Global Compliance Monitoring and Testing (GCMT)
Issue Description:
On September 19, 2022, the Global Compliance Monitoring and Testing (GCMT) Team created an issue in Protecht assigned to Risk Operations (#1025144) associated with gaps procedures identified during testing. In addition to the issue, action plans were established and were due to be completed by September 30, 2023 (~1 year after issue was stood up in Protecht). As of Q1 2024, the action plan for #1025144 remains open is more than 180 days past due.
 

  
 

 Details of issue (#1025144), including established action plan(s), are as follows:
 

 Issue Title: 
 

 Inadequate Error Resolution Procedures (Reg E)
 

 Issue Description: 
 

 Error Resolution Procedures
 

 Requirement:
 

 While Regulation E § 1005.11 is too large to paste here, it generally, defines the term error and describes the steps the consumer must take when asserting an error in order to receive the protection of the EFTA and 12 CFR 1005.11, and the procedures that a financial institution must follow to resolve an alleged error under this section. 
 

 Testing Results:
 

 ITM reviewed all 21 Procedures/SOPs provided by the business that relate to Regulation E. The results are broken down to 1) general procedure enhancements: which encompases all 21 reviewed; 2) individual procedure enhancements: which contains one off procedural gaps; and 3) other procedure enhancements: which speaks to procedures that do not directly apply to resolving errors but could benefit from additional content.
 

 General procedure enhancements:
 

 Procedures all presume that a dispute/error will be resolved within a SLA of 10 days. There is guidance within a knowledge article that generally references time limits for an investigation in the instance a case is not resolved within the SLA, however it is not detailed with steps agents must take should the investigation take up to 45 days. 
 

 No strong guidance on a reassertion of error. The procedures maintain a step in which the agent checks for duplicate cases, however, it is unclear if this process is meant to clarify the lack of responsibility to investigate in the case where a customer makes a reassertion of the same error. Additionally there is no mention of the fact that the financial institution has no further error resolution responsibilities if the consumer voluntarily withdraws the notice alleging an error. A consumer who has withdrawn an allegation of error has the right to reassert the allegation unless the financial institution had already complied with all of the error resolution requirements before the allegation was withdrawn. The consumer must do so, however, within the original 60-day period.
 

 Many procedures do not contain an option for the agent to deny a claim if they determines no error or different error occurred.
 

 Individual procedure enhancements:
 

 > Internal Disputes - Shazam Writeoff Guidelines: lacks guidance/steps for an agent to state that no or a different error occurred.
 

 > Internal Disputes - Account Funding Transactions: This procedure outlines the steps for fraudulent account funding. This would be considered an ‘unauthorized electronic funds transfer’ and there lacks any guidance around meeting Regulation E requirements.
 

 Other procedure enhancements:
 

 While beneficial to include all regulatory requirements like other knowledge articles, there is no direct requirement for each procedure to include every aspect of the rule. In the following cases, the procedures listed apply to advocates that do not directly investigate a Reg E claim, however, this is part of the intake process and procedure enhancements should be considered to include Regulation E basics at minimum:
 

 > Voice - Cash Card Disputes IRF 
 

 > Email and Messaging - Cash Card Disputes - IRF
 

 > P2P - Disputes and Unauthorized Charges
 

 Risk Rating
 

  Medium
 

 Action Plan(s)
 

 Implement semi-annual review of applicable knowledge articles to ensure information is current and accurate.
 

 Expand/define verbiage in workflow steps that clearly correlate steps taken to comply with regulatory guidance (i.e SLA timelines, reassertion).
 

 As of Q12024, the action plan for issue 1025144 is over 180 days past due. To call attention to this overdue item and to ensure that the action plan is completed, the CCO Operational Excellence (OE) Issue Management (IM) Team has opened this secondary issue to drive completion of the original action plan.
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory examination finding, or the substantive potential for the issue to result in regulatory finding if left unmitigated
Issue Action Plans: Lack of document management process for Knowledge Articles and Past Due Issue for procedure enhancements identified during 2022 testing by Global Compliance Monitoring and Testing (GCMT) (1141342)

Issue Title: Retro and SUP Autopay Terms refers to a voluntary repayment schedule that is not disclosed in loan disclosures.
Issue Description:
Requirement: 
 Regulation E - Electronic Fund Transfer Act 12 CFR §1005.10(d)(2) Notice of transfers varying in amount - Range
 The designated payee or the institution shall inform the consumer of the right to receive notice of all varying transfers, but may give the consumer the option of receiving notice only when a transfer falls outside a specified range of amounts or only when a transfer differs from the most recent transfer by more than an agreed-upon amount
 

 Testing Results: 
 The Retro and SUP Autopay Terms, 'Payment Account for Voluntary Prepayments' section states, "If a Voluntary Prepayment will vary from the amount of the previous transfer, then you have the right to receive notice of such varying transfer ten days before the transfer. However, by voluntarily authorizing us to initiate voluntary automatic payments under the Voluntary Prepayment option, you acknowledge and request that you will receive such notice of the transfer only if a transfer will vary from the amount of the previous transfer by more than three times the repayment amount listed for a voluntary payment in your Truth in Lending Act Disclosure." 
 However, the Retro and SUP Truth in Lending Act Disclosure does not include the voluntary payment amounts as stated by the Retro and SUP Autopay Terms.

Issue Title: SUP disputes reasons not aligning with Regulation E error definitions, and SUP improvements concerning written procedures and dispute templates.
Issue Description:
Requirement: 
 Regulation E - Electronic Fund Transfer Act 12 CFR §1005.11 Procedures for resolving errors. (See regulation for full information such as time limits and extent of investigation, procedures if financial institution determines no error or different error occurred, etc.)
 

 12 CFR §1005.11(a)(1) Definition of error. Types of transfers or inquiries covered.
 The term "error" means:
  (i) An unauthorized electronic fund transfer;
  (ii) An incorrect electronic fund transfer to or from the consumer's account;
  (iii) The omission of an electronic fund transfer from a periodic statement;
  (iv) A computational or bookkeeping error made by the financial institution relating to an electronic fund transfer;
  (v) The consumer's receipt of an incorrect amount of money from an electronic terminal;
  (vi) An electronic fund transfer not identified in accordance with § 1005.9 or § 1005.10(a); or
  (vii) The consumer's request for documentation required by § 1005.9 or § 1005.10(a) or for additional information or clarification concerning an electronic fund transfer, including a request the consumer makes to determine whether an error exists under paragraphs (a)(1)(i) through (vi) of this section. 
 

 Testing Results: 
  1. Based on a population of four SUP disputes, all four cases did not meet the Regulation E definition of an "error" but were processed from a Regulation E perspective. However, the cases met the Regulation Z definition of an error. Regulation Z sets error resolution rules for open-end credit. Errors include “goods or services not accepted or delivered as agreed,” and “the creditor’s failure to credit properly a payment or other credit issued to the consumer’s account.” (12 CFR §1026.13(a) Definition of billing error)
  
 The dispute reasons were -
 I never received the goods or services I paid for (2 of 4),
 I was supposed to receive a refund. Note, the refund was due to a return (1 of 4), and
 The goods or services weren't as described (1 of 4).
 

  2. The Notion Workflow- Disputes & ATO l SUP process does not include Regulation E timeframes for investigation, result notification, and error correction along with provisional credit requirements.
 

  3. The Cash App {POT - SUP - Disputes - Fraud - Claim Denied}:
  a. Lacks a written explanation of its findings as it broadly states that, "Based on the details of the transaction, we were unable to process your dispute claim.” There is no supporting information as to why the dispute could not be processed. 
  b. Is missing language regarding the consumer's right to request the documents used in making the determination.
  c. Does not not consider the scenario where an error occurred in a different manner or different amount.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: Retro uplifts concerning Knowledge Articles (KAs), a dispute response template, and written procedures.
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 Regulation E - Electronic Fund Transfer Act 
 a. §12 CFR 1005.11 Procedures for resolving errors. (See regulation for full information such as types of transfers or inquiries covered, time limits and extent of investigation, etc.)
 

 b. §12 CFR 1005.11(d) Procedures if financial institution determines no error or different error occurred. 
  (1) Written explanation. The institution's report of the results of its investigation shall include a written explanation of the institution's findings and shall note the consumer's right to request the documents that the institution relied on in making its determination. Upon request, the institution shall promptly provide copies of the documents.
  (2) Debiting provisional credit. Upon debiting a provisionally credited amount, the financial institution shall: 
  (i) Notify the consumer of the date and amount of the debiting; 
  (ii) Notify the consumer that the institution will honor checks, drafts, or similar instruments payable to third parties and preauthorized transfers from the consumer's account (without charge to the consumer as a result of an overdraft) for five business days after the notification. The institution shall honor items as specified in the notice, but need honor only items that it would have paid if the provisionally credited funds had not been debited.
 

 Finding Description:

 b. Lending Operations created ‘ATO Information’ written procedures. However, the processes described surrounding their involvement in Retro dispute investigations, such as actioning and customer communication, are either outdated or have not been entirely defined.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

 Regulation E - Electronic Fund Transfer Act 12 CFR §1005.11(c) Time limits and extent of investigation
 (1) Ten-day period. A financial institution shall investigate promptly and, except as otherwise provided in this paragraph (c), shall determine whether an error occurred within 10 business days of receiving a notice of error. The institution shall report the results to the consumer within three business days after completing its investigation. The institution shall correct the error within one business day after determining that an error occurred.

Issue Title: Regulation E Policy update for provisional credit debiting
Issue Description:
Requirement: 
 Regulation E - Electronic Fund Transfer Act 12 CFR §1005.11(d)(2) Debiting provisional credit.  
 Upon debiting a provisionally credited amount, the financial institution shall:
  (i) Notify the consumer of the date and amount of the debiting;
  (ii) Notify the consumer that the institution will honor checks, drafts, or similar instruments payable to third parties and preauthorized transfers from the consumer's account (without charge to the consumer as a result of an overdraft) for five business days after the notification. The institution shall honor items as specified in the notice, but need honor only items that it would have paid if the provisionally credited funds had not been debited.
 

 Official Interpretation 11(d)(2) Debiting Provisional Credit
 Alternative procedure for debiting of credited funds. The financial institution may comply with the requirements of this section by notifying the consumer that the consumer's account will be debited five business days from the transmittal of the notification, specifying the calendar date on which the debiting will occur.
 

 Testing Results: 
 The Cash App Regulation E Policy, Page 10 - Error Resolution states, “Cash App will seek to resolve Notices of Error within ten (10) business days but, where circumstances so require, may take up to forty-five (45) calendar days to do so, provided that in such circumstances Cash App will:
  ● Provisionally credit the funds (including interest, where applicable) to the consumer’s account within ten (10) business days;
  ● Advise the consumer within two (2) business days of the Provisional Credit; and
  ● Give the consumer full use of the funds during the investigation."
 

 However, there is no additional information regarding provisional credit debiting requirements when it is determined that no error/different error occurred. In those instances, consumer notification must state that the provisional credit will be debited five business days from the notification and must specify the calendar date when debiting will occur.
 

 Note: The Borrow and SUP disputes sample investigations were all completed within 10 business days; therefore, no provisional credit was required to be debited from the customer's account.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Q2 2024 EFTA/Regulation E Monitoring - Cash App Lending (Borrow, Retro, SUP) (1023964)
Issue Rating Justification: Present a low risk to the operations of Block, or represents a limited weakness in Block’s processes or controls. . Corrective action to address the Issue(s) identified is required.

October 23, 2024: The revised Electronic Fund Transfers Act (Regulation E Policy) was approved and published in the Compliance Policy library (go/policy) on September 24, 2024. The Policy was updated to include the notification requirement in the event that no error or a different error occurred. In these instances, Cash App will notify the customer of the provisional credit that will be debited within five business days and specify the calendar date; refer to Section 4.3 Error Resolution.
 

 This management action plan is complete.

Issue Title: Square IDV Matching Logic with vendor Lexis Nexis.
Issue Description:
Square onboarded a seller with an owner name that is likely fictitious (“John Doe”), prompting a review of Square Seller’s onboarding configuration for LexisNexis (LN). Square identified the following gaps: (1) Square’s use of LN’s data and resources is incomplete and outdated, and (2) Square’s process for combining results from LN and its other identification vendor is improper. 
 

 One of the LN data points Square is not using is the “CVI” or confidence score. CVI can range from 0 (very low confidence) to 50 (high confidence) and is recommended by LN to inform certain addition actions where verification confidence is low.
 

 Square will combine results from its identification vendors in order to verify a seller’s identity. This at times means a seller’s information is not completely verified by one vendor alone and may result in Square considering a seller’s information verified despite its vendors being unable to separately confirm the accuracy of the seller’s information.
 

 The result of this gap is that Square may be onboarding sellers without completing its IDV processes in a way that permits Square to know who its customer is and represents a risk to Square’s ability to uphold its AML obligations.
 

 Approximately 3.8% of Sellers over the last two years have been able to complete onboarding with CVI scores of 0 or 10.
RCA Description: The root cause of this issue is a failure to keep and maintain proper configurations with Square’s identity verification vendors so that Square is properly using the resources made available to it by its identity vendors to ensure Square can verify the identity of its customers.
Issue Rating Justification: Issue presents a potential gap in IDV processes that may result in sellers onboarded with substandard identity verification. One seller observed with identity information that is believed to be false / fictitious ("John Doe") and no other known issues attributed to this Lexis issue.
Issue Action Plans: Adjust configuration and data utilization with Lexis, discontinue practice of blending vendor verification results between identification vendors. (1143984)

Issue Title: Use of Wi-Fi and Cabled Network Connection and Remote Access
Issue Description:
Internal Requirement: 
 CAI WSPs; Section: Use of Wi-Fi and Cabled Network Connection: 
 ● Associated persons are not permitted to use Wi-Fi or other cabled network connections to access 
  customer account information, unless:
  • the associated person is working on Firm premises and Firm premises have installed appropriate
  protections; [...] or uses a Block or Firm provided device with appropriate protections, such as digital 
  certificates, or VPN.
 

 ● Remote Access to Customer Accounts: Some associated persons may be authorized to work at home
  or while traveling, during which CAI’s and Block’s network will be accessed. Associated persons must
  ensure they are following all policies and procedures and that they are utilizing the company’s VPN or
  digital certificates to the extent possible. Associated persons are prohibited from accessing any 
  sensitive or confidential information without utilizing the company’s VPN or digital certificates while 
  using public networks (such as Wi-Fi).
 

 CAI Cybersecurity Policy section Remote Access: CAI relies on Block to maintain access controls to ensure remote work is performed in a secure manner. Remote access to CAI assets:
 ● Must require Multi-factor Authentication (MFA). Must authenticate devices attempting a connection
  with device-level attestation, including a client certificate, or require VPN connections for access to
  resources that do not support device-level attestation checks.
 

 Testing Results: Not all third-party vendor systems are able to integrate with single sign-on (SSO) or Multi-factor authentication (MFA) which can allow access from non-Block issued workstations. For those systems in which there were no restrictions, CAI employees are able to use their private devices to access CAI customer data, which increases the risk of unauthorized access to sensitive customer information and data leaks outside of CAI and Block.
 

 During testing, it was confirmed that CAI associated persons were able to access DriveWealth's DriveHub to view sensitive customer information without being on a Block/Firm controlled network or using a Block/Firm device.

Issue Title: GCMT identified that four former employees in 2023 and five in 2024 did not have certain third-party system access revoked upon leaving the company and were no longer with CAI.
Issue Description:
Requirement: SEC Regulations S-P and S-ID 17C.F.R. 248.30 Procedures to safeguard customer records and information; disposal of consumer report information.
 (a) Every broker, dealer, [...] must adopt written policies and procedures that address administrative, technical, and physical safeguards for the protection of customer records and information. These written policies and procedures must be reasonably designed to: (1) Insure the security and confidentiality of customer records and information; (2) Protect against any anticipated threats or hazards to the security or integrity of customer records and information; and (3) Protect against unauthorized access to or use of customer records or information that could result in substantial harm or inconvenience to any customer.
 

 Internal Requirement: CAI WSPs section Protection of Customer Information and Security includes CAI personnel with access to customer information through the use of a unique user ID and password. The Designated Supervisory Principal or their designee is responsible for creating and distributing user IDs to applicable personnel and, upon termination, disabling internal systems and third-party vendor access. 
 

 Testing Results: GCMT identified that four former employees in 2023 and five in 2024 did not have certain third-party system access revoked upon leaving the company and were no longer with CAI. Whilst access controls are managed in a manual process, CAI does not have a documented process in place for access reviews.
 

 GCMT identified that access to third-party vendors were managed using a manual user management process for onboarding, offboarding, or contract renewal instead of registry as single sign-on (SSO) integration is not possible for some vendors as it is not supported. Multi-factor Authentication (MFA) has not been enabled for all vendors.  
 

 Therefore, offboarding procedures are not fully effective CAI has relied on an employee offboarding list to determine which systems to terminate user accounts when an employee leaves. This approach only covers employees who are part of the CAI team, not contractors, account or internal transfers within CAI teams or amongst other Block teams. 
 

 Recommendation: CAI should explore possibilities of automating user management tasks, such as automated user onboarding/ offboarding (preferably via SSO) or automated alerts when CAI employees are no longer working at Block. If automation is not possible access management processes must be formalized and access reviews performed an a regular basis, such as at least quarterly. Also, include review of non-CAI collaborators' continued need for access.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: GCMT evaluated CAI’s adherence to internal policies regarding appropriate and secure data handling when sharing data with third-party vendors.
Issue Description:
Internal Requirements: The Block Data Classification & Handling Policy and the Block Third Party Security Policy Supplement is used to determine if a particular environment, system, or vendor implements the appropriate security controls to store and process sensitive data requiring that particular level.
 

 Testing Results: GCMT evaluated CAI’s adherence to internal policies regarding appropriate and secure data handling when sharing data with third-party vendors. During the evaluation, GCMT identified three vendors that did not satisfy the requirements stated in the Block Third Security Party Policy Supplement, which requires SSO integration for data classified as DSL 2, 3, and 4. 
 

 Similar conclusions were drawn in the BISO/Compliance review, where a more in-depth DSL evaluation was performed. The evaluation was used to determine if the minimum cybersecurity requirements were in place for the vendors, given the sensitivity (i.e., the DSL rating) of the data they were processing and storing. The review concluded that it was unclear whether data retention and disposal controls were sufficient to satisfy CAI’s needs and DSL requirements.
 

 GCMT established that the findings from the BISO/Compliance review had not been shared with all relevant stakeholders, leading to individual DRIs not being aware of the findings and preventing them from remediating the issues. The findings were not registered in an issue management tool, which has led to all issues remaining open.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: Based on current system configurations and functionality offered by vendors, the Detection And Response Team (DART) does not have sufficient visibility into activities happening within CAI third-party vendors, such as Box and DriveWealth.
Issue Description:
Internal Requirement: Block Data Safety Guidelines; Section: Auditability
 ● Auditable Permissions: An auditable record of changes to access permissions exists that specifies the 
  time of change, the affected entity, the affected privileges, and the justification for the change.
 ● Auditable Data Access: A structured audit trail of all access to a system that processes this data, exists,
  can be reasonably queried during incidents and audits, and is retained for at least one year.
 ● Auditable Data Access: The structured audit trail of all access includes, for each access event, the 
  identity* accessing the data, the system being accessed, the type of event, a timestamp, an indication of 
  success or failure, and where the event originated (e.g. IP address).
 ● Audit trails must record the identity of the user principal (either user or workload identity) whose 
  credentials were used to access the data store. In cases where a workload transitively facilitates 
  human access such as through an application interface, the audit trail must record the identity of the 
  human’s user identity.
 ● Auditable Data Access: The structured audit trail of all access includes, for each access event, 
  information about the specific data that was accessed.
 

 Testing Results: Based on current system configurations and functionality offered by vendors, the Detection And Response Team (DART) does not have sufficient visibility into activities happening within CAI third-party vendors, such as Box and DriveWealth. Due to the lack of visibility, DART case investigations do not contain proper reviews of audit trails and supporting evidence in case of a security incident.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: In Box there are two CAI Books and Records data retention folders: one covering 12 years retention and one for lifetime retention.
Issue Description:

 ● Retention: Data is not retained forever or indefinitely. 
 ● Disposal: When the purpose for collecting this data is no longer valid or the stated retention requirements 
  expire, the data is destroyed within an appropriate time frame.
 

 Testing Results: In Box there are two CAI Books and Records data retention folders: one covering 12 years retention and one for lifetime retention. By exceeding record retention requirements, Block and CAI increase risk exposure caused by a security breach.

RCA Description: Control Design Issue
Issue Rating Justification: Per the Issues Management Program (April 2024), meets the definition of Low: May be used when (ii) a "one-off exception" is identified that does not appear indicative of a broken process / control.
Issue Action Plans: CAI will map required retention periods for different types of data. (1144657)
Closure Request Justification:
A presentation by Bjorn Hoffman - CAI Compliance on 12/30/2024, with Donna Adams - GCMT Compliance in attendance, covered the following in response to the recommendations of this action plan (#1144657):
 

 1) How CAI organizes and assigns DRIs for books and records line items via the Books and Records Inventory spreadsheet. This inventory also maps FINRA-required retention duration(s) for each line item, and also maps newly modified internal deletion schedules:
 

 https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1dO2HxSwW_L9lrv__EU_pRWprIZDFazH71fgn6iSXuhE/edit?gid=1454193476#gid=1454193476
 

 2) How the design of the new system in Box to fulfill retention requirements will operate. The following training fully describes the new system and was provided on the week of Oct 23, with 100% attendance, to all CAI employees who are DRI for a line item on the Books and Records Inventory:
 

 https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1Ocp7t35xO055mnl3DCC0V9178X0RiV4SKxwy3-izNHc/edit#slide=id.g15db49e6373_2_444
 

 3) How the newly applied Box "Retention Policies" in Box satisfy the requirement to avoid infinite retention where possible by limiting exposure to infinite retention to only the few files specified to be retained indefinitely on the Books and Records Inventory;
 

 4) How all other books and records items are now applied Box "Retention Policies" with appropriate retention duration(s) and a clear path forward toward deletion for files that have met their retention requirements;
 

 and 5) How all DRIs for Books and Records line items will, on a quarterly basis, review the files for which they are responsible and ensure they are in the correct location(s) before certifying the following quarterly attestation in Compliance Alpha:
 

 "Certification that all records described herein are managed by the assigned DRI(s) and that the records are stored in a compliant location, as well as retained for the required retention period. Any records that have not been finalized must be identified in the questionnaire portion of this attestation, with the expected date of completion. Questions about this attestation may be directed to the Cash App Investing Chief Compliance Officer."
 

 This presentation aimed to demonstrate that CAI's policy changes successfully avoid infinite retention in Box where possible and that CAI's rooks and records retention policies now comply with the DSL Framework (as documented in the Block Data Safety Guidelines).

Issue Title: Paper Money Disputes - Reg E Alignment
Issue Description:
During Control Testing, CTT identified that Banking Investigations Paper Money Disputes is not aligned to Regulation E in the RCM and the risks identified with this process are not encompassing of the Regulation E risks. 
 

 

 When completing the TOD for Paper Money Disputes SLA, CTT identified an instance that showcased the need to migrate this process into a system like Disputron. The item had a valid notice of error as it contained the transaction date, amount, and reason for dispute. Additional questions were sent to the customer, which they never responded to and then the case was closed without any further communication to the customer. However, since there was a legitimate NOE, the Reg E process should have been followed, which would have included a response indicating the outcome of the investigation within 10 business days. Additionally, all paper money disputes with valid NOEs require that all proper Reg E communications be sent to the customer.
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure resulting in an impact to regulatory obligations
Issue Action Plans: Paper Money Disputes - Reg E Alignment (1145313)
Closure Request Justification: Issue Management Lead reviewed evidence/artifacts to support closure. No concerns noted. - Charlotte Vasi

Issue Title: Cash - Sanctioned Jurisdiction Top Level Domain (TLD) in Email Addresses
Issue Description:
Cash App does not have existing controls in place to identify or prevent the use of email addresses based on suffixes, or Top Level Domains (TLDs), associated with sanctioned jurisdictions (Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Syria, Russia, Ukraine, Afghanistan, Belarus) at onboarding or alias linking. The root cause of this issue is that TLD detection or blocking controls were designed or built into processes related to onboarding or account maintenance throughout a customer’s relationship. TLDs related to sanctioned jurisdictions may be indicative of a party residing in that jurisdiction. The Office of Foreign Assets Control (“OFAC”) has an expectation that companies incorporate appropriate controls related to information they collect, including email addresses, to ensure services are not provided or payments are not processed in violation of sanctions regulations.  
 

 This issue was identified after Sanctions set out to create an email control after OFAC enforcement actions in 2022 and 2023 against companies with an absence of controls preventing customer email addresses with a TLD originating from a sanctioned jurisdiction. The control was drafted in January 2024. On April 1, 2024, Sanctions requested a volume estimate to size the population of customers currently on the platform with a sanctioned TLD. On May 9, 202,4 CAX provided the original estimate, which resulted in a request for onboard and linking controls, and a remediation of the current population with TLDs from sanctioned jurisdictions.
 

 This issue is being raised because failing to meet regulatory expectations increases our risk exposure to facilitating financial activity in sanctioned jurisdictions and could subject Cash App and Block to regulatory fines.
Hyperlinks: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1-GeWuWr6YwdiDcUQ82ZPxejVkZz8_nvOLhzVzx-iL4Q/edit?tab=t.0|1116225- Cash Sanctioned TLD Action Plan Support for Action Plans 1-8
RCA Description: Process Design: Controls to identify and prevent the use of email addresses based on TLDs from sanctioned jurisdictions were not built in to prevent these types of email addresses at seller onboarding or throughout the seller’s relationship.
Issue Rating Justification: Email addresses based on suffixes, or Top Level Domains (TLDs) associated with comprehensively sanctioned jurisdictions may be indicative of a customer’s location or permanent residence, and under OFAC rules and regulations, Cash App is prohibited from providing services to persons ordinarily resident in comprehensively sanctioned jurisdictions (this includes individuals/entities not currently on any OFAC-administered sanctions lists). The observed exposure of email TLDs, associated with comprehensively sanctioned jurisdictions, across Cash App may represent violations of the OFAC requirements/regulations, which could result in regulatory enforcement and fines. OFAC enforcement actions as recently as 2023 have also cited lack of TLD controls as an "aggravating factor" because the business knew or had reason to know that the email address suffixes were associated with sanctioned jurisdictions but did not incorporate this information into its compliance program or controls.
Issue Action Plans:
Block email domains from Sanctioned & High-Risk Juristicions (1173023)
 Placeholder customers are prevented from creating a full account (1173025)
 Remediate existing accounts with comprehensively sanction TLD (1173028)
 Incorporate Block Attempts into Customer Risk Rating (1173034)
 Toolbox Requirements - Flagged by CRR (1173038)
 COM Monitoring (1173045)
 Email Control Monitoring - Signup & Alias Linking (1173058)
 Sustainability (1270717)
Closure Request Justification: All action plans and supporting docs have been reviewed and approved. The issue may be closed as primary risk factors and exposure has been mitigated. Certain lower-risk aspects of action plans 3 and 5 are pending but remain on track to be completed by end of Q2 2025. This issue may be re-opened if that work is not complete and delayed beyond the end of Q2 2025.

Issue Title: Fraudulent Card Ordering Resulting in Cash App Cards Issued to Non-Customers
Issue Description:
Bad actors are using the information of non-customers to order Cash App Cards. The non-customer only becomes aware that they are the victim of fraud when they receive the physical Cash App Card in the mail. 
 

 

 Note: The solution being implemented for Issue #1118213 (opened by the Product Compliance Team) has been determined to address the concerns raised in INTAKEFORM-136. This intake was submitted by the Cash Complaints Insights team regarding consumer complaints stemming from insufficient address validation in Cash App Card orders. Through comprehensive discussions with FLRM Issue Management, Product Compliance, and relevant business stakeholders, it has been confirmed that the address validation enhancements outlined in the action plan for issue #1118213 will effectively mitigate the customer impact documented in INTAKEFORM-136. This alignment eliminates the need for a separate issue documented, as the planned solution will close both gaps through a single, comprehensive implementation. This approach ensures the solution addresses both compliance requirements and customer experience improvements.
RCA Description: Weaknesses in controls for card ordering and address management allow bad actors to take advantage of the virtual card.
Issue Rating Justification: Presents a significant risk to the operations of Block/Cash App, such as exposure to fraud, material loss, error or misstatement. Overall exposure is severe and requires an immediate corrective action plan with high priority.
Issue Progress Updates: Due date extension request CCO approved. Approval evidence attached.
Issue Action Plans: Enhancements to card enrollment and policy changes for card ownership (1182284)
Closure Request Justification: All action plan items have been addressed, with proper evidence attached.

Issue Title: P2P Dispute - Slack Channel Escalation
Issue Description: Concentrix, our Business Process Outsourcing (BPO) team based in Nicaragua, established a Slack channel, staffing it with subject matter expert agents. However, during Nicaragua's exit from the Cash App account, the channel was left unmonitored. This Slack channel was used for high-count transactions (50+ cases attached to one claim) and was specifically for the legacy dispute flow, where disputes exceeded the 10-business day timeframe. As a result, some P2P disputes did not have decisions made within the required 10 business days, as mandated by Reg E. The impact of this oversight is that the cases were actioned outside of the Service Level Agreement (SLA) and now need remediation. Disputes leadership discovered this issue when they were notified by our BPO partner in late Q1, covering the impact dates from 09/2023 to 3/2024.
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure resulting in an impact to regulatory obligations
Issue Action Plans: P2P Dispute - Slack Channel Escalation (1148602)
Closure Request Justification: Issue validation executed 11/13/2024 with zero findings identified. Internal QA completed by Stephen Green and issue is now ready for closure.

Issue Title: CIRT - Complaint Record Form Error Reviews Process
Issue Description:
During the risk and control documentation effort for the Cash Complaint Operations Team (CCOT), the following was noted by the CCOT Cash Complaint Operations Internal Response Team (CIRT) Lead for the Complaint Record Form Error Reviews process:
 

 This is a temporary process (not formalized). There is no official documentation for this process that has been done for correcting Complaint Record Errors. This work is likely to be irrelevant come the end of June. This work was originally started to correct:
 

 Any required Fields on the Complaint Record that are ""Null"" when the complaint is in a ""Closed"" or ""Archived"" status.
 

 There is a tooling improvement that should be deploying this month that will prevent the Complaint Record from saving in a ""Closed"" or ""Archived"" status if the required Fields are not completely filled in. We will have to run a couple more queries to identify remaining complaints with these Null Fields to correct any prior to the deploy but then this work will conclude.
 

 Any complaint where the ""Resulting Action"" on the complaint record is ""Escalated Complaint"". This was never meant to be a final resulting action and was only to be used for when a complaint is escalated to CCOT-CIRT.
 

 This will also be corrected with the tooling improvement above that should be deploying this month and we will do a final query to correct any remaining complaints. TLs to get documentation created for historical purposes.
 

 CIRT Lead has also noted that she would like Team Leads to get documentation created for historical purposes
 

 Based off gaps/inefficiencies provided by business, OE recommendation is to not move forward with a review and documentation of the process. Instead, we may want to document an issue here. This issue would serve as evidence that this process will be retired and the work will be corrected with the tooling improvement.
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: The issue relates to an internal control or process that is associated with low-risk activity (includes instances in which controls or other safeguards are noted to be absent).
Issue Action Plans: CIRT - Complaint Record Form Error Reviews Process (1149260)

Issue Title: Lack of Monitoring / Reviews for Changes to Customer Information
Issue Description:
Block has not established sufficient Ongoing Customer Due Diligence (OCDD) systems and controls in line with Regulatory Expectations to keep KYC information, customer risk ratings, and Enhanced Customer Due Diligence (ECDD) records up to date.
 

 While Block has established processes to review and update KYC information during the investigation of ECDD and Transaction Monitoring alerts, there are currently no mechanisms in place to (i) identify changes made by customers to KYC information (e.g., merchant type / business name / beneficial ownership for Square merchants, full name / address for Cash App users) directly, (ii) re-validate such information, and (iii) apply such changes to customer risk ratings / ECDD requirements timely. 
 

 The existing control structure creates opportunities for Square merchants or Cash App users to change KYC data for a nefarious purpose undetected (e.g., changing business name to make card payments appear as if they were made at a well-known, reputable business).
 

 While law / regulation does not specify how Block should be maintaining CIP / KYC information over time, published regulatory guidance indicates that this is a critical consideration for the AML program as a whole. The FFIEC BSA/AML Examination Manual states that a financial institution’s CDD program must include risk-based procedures for performing ongoing monitoring of the customer relationship, on a risk basis, to maintain and update customer information, including beneficial ownership information of legal entity customerrs and that The requirement to update customer information is event-driven and occurs as a result of normal monitoring. Should the institution become aware as a result of its ongoing monitoring that customer information, including beneficial ownership information, has materially changed, it should update the customer information accordingly. Additionally, if this customer information is material and relevant to assessing the risk of a customer relationship, then the institution should reassess the customer risk profile/rating and follow established policies, procedures, and processes for maintaining or changing the customer risk profile/rating.
Interested Parties:
Adrian Gardner
 Cindy Williams
 Jackie Kim
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation
Issue Action Plans:
KYB Procedure/Controls Roll-out (1112488)
 Product Flows - Business Entity Type (1112490)

Issue Title: Ongoing Customer Due Diligence to Maintain KYC Information (Repeat Finding)
Issue Description:
AAPL has not established sufficient Ongoing Customer Due Diligence (OCDD) systems and controls in line with Regulatory Expectations to review and update the KYC information it holds about customers on a regular basis.
 

 Reasonable measures must be established to keep, update and review documents, data or information collected by applicable customer identification procedures in accordance with the AML/CTF Rules 15.3.
 

 Currently, AAPL has manual processes to (i) review KYC information during the investigation of ECDD and OCDD cases, and (ii) update and reverify KYC information when a customer notifies AAPL of new KYC information proactively. However, a combination of periodic and trigger-based procedures are required to meet Regulatory Expectations to manage and maintain KYC information in order to identify, assess, and manage ML/TF risk effectively.
 

 There is an open management action plan (action number 1001593) from AAPL’s independent review performed in 2021 to review the adequacy of its KYC Refresh processes; this MAP was initially due 31 December 2021. Although the due date was subsequently extended to 30 June 2022, Management confirmed that work on the implementation plan has been delayed to late 2023 as part of AAPL’s product roadmap and incorporation of the “Pay Monthly” service.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation
Issue Action Plans:
Assessment to Determine Design and Implementation Requirements for Afterpay KYC Refresh (1112418)
 Development of In-App Refresh of Consumer Information (1112420)
 Deployment of Frequency-Based KYC Refresh (1112422)

Issue Title: Instances of non-compliance with Standard Operating Procedures for the handling and management of inbound and outbound calls.
Issue Description:
Testing Results:
 GCMT tested compliance of Customer Service Advocate compliance with Standard Operating Procedures for the handling and management of inbound and outbound calls. The below results indicate areas where an effectiveness rate of less than 85% was observed. 
 

 1 - Adequacy of CSA responses and resolutions – Inbound and Outbound Calls 
 GCMT tested a random sample of 70 inbound and 20 outbound calls and identified the following: 
 • Inbound calls: 84% effectiveness rate consisting of a 16% exception rate where the enquiry was only partially resolved (10%) or not resolved in full (6%) 
 • Outbound calls: 65% effectiveness rate consisting of a 35% exception rate where the enquiry was only partially resolved (30%) or not resolved in full (5%) 
 

 2 - Compliance with Regulatory Requirements (PII verification, call recording disclaimer and greeting, Third Party Consent, Email Regulatory Disclaimer, Vulnerable Customers) – Inbound and Outbound Calls 
 GCMT tested a random sample of 70 inbound and 20 outbound calls and identified the following: 
 • Inbound Calls: 50% effectiveness rate consisting of a 50% exception rate where one or more of the above regulatory requirements were not met when applicable to the call. These exceptions were further broken down into the following root causes (note some samples had multiple exceptions which have been individually counted below): 
 

 o  19 exceptions where the Regulatory Disclaimer was not included in outbound email sent as part of the call resolution. (Per finding 4 this has been remediated by management) 
 o 2 exceptions where PII was not correctly completed by the CSA 
 o 21 exceptions where the CSA did not recite the required greeting in full or only partially at the start of the call. 
 

 • Outbound Calls: 40% effectiveness rate consisting of a 60% exception rate where one or more of the above regulatory requirements were not met when applicable to the call. These exceptions were further broken down into the following root causes (note some samples had multiple exceptions which have been individually counted below): 
 

 o 9 exceptions where the call recording language was not provided at the start of the call 
 o 6 exceptions where the Regulatory Disclaimer was not included in the outbound email sent as part of the call resolution. (Per finding 4 this has been remediated by management) 
 

 3 - CSA compliance with administrative requirement to leave adequate notes on the Salesforce Case – Inbound and Outbound Calls 
 GCMT tested a random sample of 70 inbound calls and 20 outbound calls and identified the following: 
 

 Inbound Calls: 75% effectiveness rate consisting of a 25% exception rate where the CSA failed to leave a note on Salesforce (7%), the CSA left a note on Salesforce however it did not adequately reflect the interaction (5%) and where a Salesforce Case and/or note could not be located based on the amazon call connect ID, no personal details were obtained by the caller (13%). 
 

 Outbound Calls: 65% effectiveness rate consisting of a 35% exception rate where the CSA left a note on Salesforce however it did not adequately reflect the interation (6%) and where a Salesforce Case and/or note could not be located based on the amazon call connect ID, no personal details were obtained by the caller. (29%)

Issue Title: There is no control in place to prevent sending Commercial Electronic Messages to merchants upon account / contract termination in order to comply with the Canada Anti-Spam Legislation.
Issue Description:
As per the Canada Anti-Spam Legislation (CASL) Section 10(9), Afterpay Canada operates under an implied consent model for merchants to receive commercial electronic messages (CEMs). An existing business relationship between the merchant and Afterpay is established through the Afterpay Merchant Agreement. 
 As per CASL Section 10(10)(d), when a merchant’s account / contract is terminated with Afterpay, Afterpay is unable to rely on the implied consent to send CEMs after a two-year period.
 

 Afterpay is currently sending electronic messages to merchants, however there is no process or mechanism in place to:
 - Prevent sending CEMs to merchants after they have had their account or contract terminated with Afterpay; or
 - Track the two-year period expiry date post contract termination.
 The Afterpay B2B Marketing team has confirmed that CEMs sent to merchants through Marketo (Afterpay’s B2B marketing system / email sending platform) are based on the merchant’s subscription status. Marketo does not distinguish between the merchant’s “active” or “terminated” status.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Processes, Products, or Services
Issue Rating Justification: There is no control to comply with regulation requirements
Issue Action Plans:
REF 1A - Enhance tooling to prevent sending CEMs to merchants after contract/account termination (1152591)
 REF 1B - Enhance tooling to prevent sending CEMs to merchants after contract/account termination (1152596)
Closure Request Justification: Closure completed - See all action plans for details.

Issue Title: There is no record of a merchants’ request to re opt-in to receive marketing communications after having opted-out
Issue Description:
GCMT tested 100 Merchants (20 samples per region) who opted-in to receive marketing communications during the review period and identified the 14 exceptions (14%) where there was no records or evidence in any form of the merchants’ request to re-opt-in after they had previously opted-out. Of the 14 exceptions: 
 - 12 exceptions was due to the change of subscription status in Marketo that resulted from a modification in Salesforce. Salesforce indicated that the Email Opt-out field was altered from “unsubscribed” to “subscribed” by Preference Sync Robot.
  Note: Merchant data including the marketing email subscription status is recorded in Marketo and synced with Salesforce (Afterpay's CRM system), and data from Salesforce is likewise synced back to Marketo.  
 - 2 exceptions where the change of subscription status of the merchant resulted from a sync with a duplicated merchant account in Marketo. However, there is no evidence of the merchant’s request to opt-in in either of the merchant accounts. 
 

 Notwithstanding that we have no record of the re opt in from the merchant, we also can see that there were 8 instances within the 14 exceptions whereby Afterpay are sending a marketing email post the respective regional regulatory timeframes to process an opt-out request. 
 - NZ: 3 out of 5 merchants received emails past the 5 business day timeframe after the merchant had opted out
 - AU: 2 out of 3 merchants received emails past the 5 business day timeframe after the merchant had opted out
 - US: 1 out of 1 merchant received email past the 10 business day timeframe after the merchant had opted out
 - CA: 2 out of 3 merchants received emails past the 10 business day timeframe after the merchant had opted out
 - UK: 0 out of 2 merchants receievd emails past the 30 day timeframe after the merchant had opted out
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description: Technology
Impacted Countries:
Canada
 United Kingdom
 Australia
 United States
 New Zealand
Impacted Legal Entities:
Afterpay Canada Ltd
 Clearpay Finance Limited
 Afterpay Australia Pty Ltd
 Afterpay US Services
 Afterpay NZ Limited
Issue Rating Justification: No record of opt-in consent from merchants may lead to non-compliance with regulations
Issue Action Plans:
Investigate the data source of Preference Sync Robot on the email opt-out field (1153220)
 REF 2B - Enhance tooling to ensure that opt-out status are synced across platforms to prevent marketing emails to be sent after a merchant has opted out. (1171701)
Closure Request Justification: Closure completed - See action plan for details.

Issue Title: Lack of a formal, documented process for maintaining and tracking Knowledge Articles in Risk Operations
Issue Description:
Knowledge Articles (KA) at Cash are critical documents that provide detailed information on a specific topic and are used by employees to help understand processes, solve customer problems and learn about various services and/or products. KAs typically include step-by-step instructions, FAQs, tips or other relevant information to help employees find accurate and effective solutions when working directly with customers.
 

 Based on a recently completed risk and controls documentation exercise as well as feedback directly from the lines of business and Quality Control (QC) reviews, there is no consistent process in place for maintaining KAs in Risk Operations (RO) to ensure that they are routinely reviewed, optimized, updated timely and trackable by version. Multiple examples of outdated, obsolete or inaccurate KAs were flagged.
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: Operational or process Gap resulting in an update to internal policies, processes, or procedures
Issue Action Plans: Lack of a formal, documented process for maintaining and tracking Knowledge Articles in Risk Operations (1153227)
Closure Request Justification: IM Lead has reviewed all supporting evidence and artifacts in support of the overall action plan. No concerns/gaps noted. - Charlotte Vasi

Issue Title: Missing Deposits Procedures - Reg E and UDAAP
Issue Description: During control testing and review of the Missing Deposit procedures (Knowledge Articles) and documented controls, CTT identified a Reg E and possible UDAAP concern. In this KA, it references that providing supporting documents is optional but the workflow states that if they do not provide supporting documents then the agent is to deny the claim. Stating that the documents are optional within one document but then advising to deny all claims unless they provide supporting evidence within another document is inconsistent and could potentially lead to agents unfairly denying a customer's claim or providing inaccurate information to a customer. Both the procedure and workflow should be updated to align with the Reg E requirements for providing supporting documentation for NOE cases. It's also recommended that a review of all KAs and workflow documents be reviewed to ensure proper alignment internally as well as to the regulatory requirements.
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure resulting in an impact to regulatory obligations
Issue Action Plans: Missing Deposits Procedures - Reg E and UDAAP (1155219)

Issue Title: Lack of Ongoing Screening and Alert Reviews for Weebly Customer Sanctions Screening
Issue Description:
In May 2024, Square ("SQ") Compliance Ops Management initiated a review of the Weebly's sanctions screening and controls following the Square International Product team's identification of Weebly customers from jurisdictions (Russia, Ukraine, Belarus, and Venezuela) that pose heightened sanctions risk due to their broad scope of sanctions. Weebly is a website and eCommerce service acquired by Square in May 2018 and is a Block, Inc. subsidiary; the product is available globally. On May 23, 2024, the Square Sanctions PM and Global Sanction Compliance ("GSC") met with CSI, the third party service provider of the sanctions screening system (CSI Watchdog Elite) used to screen Weebly customers, and discovered that Weebly customers were only subjected to sanctions screening on an initial bases and were not subjected to ongoing sanctions screening. This issue appears to be due to an incomplete/inaccurate design of Weebly's sanctions screening process. Lack of ongoing customer screening is inconsistent with regulatory expectations and Block's existing customer sanctions screening practices and also inhibits Square's ability to ensure it is not providing services to sanctioned parties. 
 

 Additionally in July 2024, SQ Compliance Ops Management discovered that all alert review and disposition work of Weebly customer sanctions screening had been suspended since Q3 2022 due to a decision made by the Sanctions Ops Lead. This decision was not made under the guidance of the Sanctions Ops Lead's management or Global Sanction Compliance ("GSC"), and the Sanctions Ops Lead did not communicate this decision to management or GSC. As such, this issue appears due to human performance/inaction to follow procedures On July 18, 2024, SQ Sanctions informed GSC that they discovered approximately 1,000 open alerts in CSI Watchdog pending investigation. This significant backlog presents a risk and gap in Weebly Sanctions Controls as the lack of alert reviews inhibits Square's ability to ensure it is not providing services to sanctioned parties and is inconsistent with the Weebly Sanctions Compliance Standard Operating Procedures.
Hyperlinks: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1MhwypAzNXkBRwGxl1hxBD6goNhb4m001uR0NctJ0q34/edit?tab=t.vky4f2kt3m5y|1126123- Weebly Sanctions Issue Action Plan Support & Evidence Document
RCA Description:
M3
 

 People: In Q3 2022, the SQ Sanctions Ops lead decided to pause all alert-clearing work on CSI Watchdog Elite regarding the Weebly customer-triggered alerts. This decision was not approved by GSC or SQ Compliance Operations Management and did not adhere to existing procedures and management expectations. Additionally, insufficient oversight and Quality Control Testing by GSC and/or SQ Compliance Operations Management allows the issue to remain undetected for almost two years. 
 

 Process: Weebly Sanctions Screening processes were not initially designed to account for ongoing screening of Weebly customers when Square acquired Weebly in 2018.
Issue Rating Justification: Lack of ongoing customer sanctions screening and lack of sanctions screening alert reviews are inconsistent with regulatory expectations and Block's customer sanctions screening practices, which necessitate a corrective action plan with priority.
Issue Action Plans:
Discovery of Necessary Information (1301722)
 AP2: Turn off CDM and Close CSI Account for Weebly-related activities (1301726)
 AP 3: Cease Sending Weebly Data Files to CSI (1301729)

Issue Title: Merchants not screened in accordance with requirements [Square - SEL]
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 The Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG) Guidance, Part 3, 4: Compliance with the UK financial sanctions regime, Screening of customers and transactions, Timing of screening
 4.80 All customers should be screened during the establishment of a business relationship or as soon as possible after the business relationship has commenced. Firms should be aware of the risks associated with screening customers after a business relationship has been established and/or services have been provided i.e., that they may transact with a sanctioned party in breach of sanctions prohibitions. Firms must be aware of the absolute restrictions embedded in the financial sanctions regime. Where there is any delay in screening, firms face a risk of breaching the legislation.
 

 Testing Resuts:
 GCMT noted in its Adverse Media , Sanctions, and PEP Screening Q3 2023 report issued in Dec 2023 that a total of 279 Square UK merchants onboarded between July 1, 2022 and June 30, 2023 were not screened at the onboarding. 
 On Feb 2024, the SQ Data Analytics (DA) team undertook a comprehensive review of all UK & EU merchants previously overlooked in screenings due to the "activate" button issue. This examination revealed over 1,600 merchants in need of immediate action. This group included test accounts, deactivated accounts, and accounts inactive for the past two years. 
 

 More information here:

Issue Title: Merchants not screened in accordance with requirements [Square - SIL]
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement:
 Section 10 of International Financial Sanctions (10.3.3 Screening Customers against Sanctions Lists) in the Anti-Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism Guidelines for the Financial Sector (Revised 23 June 2021) states that: 
 Firms should have effective screening systems appropriate to the nature, size and risk of their business. Screening new and existing customers and payments against the relevant and up to date EU and UN lists helps ensure that Firms will not breach the sanctions regulations. Customer screening should take place at the time of customer take-on and at regular intervals thereafter.  
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT noted in its Adverse Media , Sanctions, and PEP Screening Q3 2023 report issued in Dec 2023 that a total of 9 Square EU merchants onboarded between July 1, 2022 and June 30, 2023 were not screened at the onboarding. 
 On Feb 2024, the SQ Data Analytics (DA) team undertook a comprehensive review of all UK & EU merchants previously overlooked in screenings due to the "activate" button issue. This examination revealed over 1,600 merchants in need of immediate action. This group included test accounts, deactivated accounts, and accounts inactive for the past two years. 
 

 More information here:

Issue Title: REF 3 - SEL Customer Onboarding Q2 2024 - Discrepancies identified in the Manual and Automated flow (i.e., identity verification processes, gaps in stored customer information, procedures not reflecting current practices)
Issue Description:
MANUAL ONBOARDING
 Requirement:
 The Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017.
 30.—(2) Subject to paragraph (3) or (4), a relevant person must comply with the requirement to verify the identity of the customer, any person purporting to act on behalf of the customer and any beneficial owner of the customer before the establishment of a business relationship or the carrying out of the transaction.
 (3) Provided that the verification is completed as soon as practicable after contact is first established, the verification of the customer, any person purporting to act on behalf of the customer and the customer’s beneficial owner, may be completed during the establishment of a business relationship if—
 (a) this is necessary not to interrupt the normal conduct of business; and
 (b) there is little risk of money laundering and terrorist financing.
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT reviewed the UK Seller KYC Standard Operating Procedure (Master) and identified the following issues: 
 ● No procedure documented for Unincorporated Associations and Intergovernmental Organizations (IGOs).
 ● No SLA in place for the GB KYC Escalations queue.
 ● Compliance Operations manually review the accounts escalated by CS or Sales, however this process is not documented (i.e., Slack channels).
 ● Some employees outside the Compliance Operations function have editing rights to the UK Seller KYC Standard Operating Procedure (Master).
 

 GCMT performed a transactional data testing of 1,315 UK customers manually onboarded in Q1 2024 (i.e., 152 Sole Traders, 1,151 Limited Companies, 6 Partnerships, 1 Unincorporated Association, 5 Unknown) and identified the following issues:
 ● 4 charities onboarded as sole trader. GCMT also noted two trustees missing for 10 charities (i.e., 9 Limited Companies, 1 Partnership) and Compliance Operations stated that the review only occurs at the customer risk rating (CRR) process, which occurs after the onboarding process. Additionally, GCMT noted missing names or classes of beneficiaries for 2 charities (1 Limited Company and 1 Partnership), and beneficial ownership and charity registration number missing for 1 charity. 
 ● Missing directors for 5 Partnerships.
 ● Missing beneficial ownership for 28 Limited Companies.
 ● Missing/incomplete address for 518 beneficial owners related to Limited Companies.
 ● Missing company registration number for 1 Limited Company.
 

 GCMT identified some accounts where a quality check was performed with missing/incorrect KYC information recorded in Regulator (see Quality Control finding - row above). The Quality Control function stated that 'the current UK Seller KYC Standard Operating Procedure (Master) does not currently require operations to update beneficial ownership information via the legal entity tool'.
 

 GCMT reviewed 1,657 GB alerts related to accounts manually activated in Q1 2024 and identified the following issues:
 ● 37 GB alerts reviewed after the 30 days SLA (i.e., between 31 and 256 days).
 The analysis is available here.
 

 AUTOMATIC ONBOARDING
 Requirement:
 The Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017.
 30.—(2) Subject to paragraph (3) or (4), a relevant person must comply with the requirement to verify the identity of the customer, any person purporting to act on behalf of the customer and any beneficial owner of the customer before the establishment of a business relationship or the carrying out of the transaction.
 (3) Provided that the verification is completed as soon as practicable after contact is first established, the verification of the customer, any person purporting to act on behalf of the customer and the customer’s beneficial owner, may be completed during the establishment of a business relationship if—
 (a) this is necessary not to interrupt the normal conduct of business; and
 (b) there is little risk of money laundering and terrorist financing.
 

 The Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG) Guidance (2023 Revised Version), Part 1.
 5.3.73 If documentary evidence of an individual’s identity is to provide a high level of confidence, it will typically have been issued by a government department or agency, or by a court or local authority that has checked the existence and characteristics of the persons concerned. In cases where such documentary evidence of identity may not be available to an individual, other evidence of identity may give the firm reasonable confidence in the customer’s identity, although the firm should weigh these against the risks involved.
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT performed a transactional data testing of 26,374 UK customers automatically onboarded in Q1 2024 (i.e., 21,257 Sole Traders, 4,391 Limited Companies, and 459 Partnerships, 2 Unincorporated Association, and 265 Unknown) and identified the following issues:
 ● The ID type and ID expiry date are not recorded for the UK customers at the onboarding. Although the ID type and ID expiry date are not explicitly stated in the in the requirements listed above, SEL is expected to obtain acceptable KYC documentation in line with the manual IDV section 5.3 of the UK Seller KYC Standard Operating Procedure (Master).
 

 ● 189 accounts (i.e., 1 Sole Trader, 167 Limited Companies, 21 Partnerships) automatically onboarded and 70 accounts manually onboarded were activated prior to completing the identity verification process. Additionally, GCMT also reviewed 1,657 GB alerts related to the accounts activated in Q1 2024 and noted that 79 alerts were still in pending status (i.e., still needed to complete the KYC process), however these accounts were automatically actived by the system prior to the completion of the KYC process (the analysis is available here).
 

 ● For the ultimate beneficial owner information, it was noted that SEL failed to identify and verify the:
 - 1 account activated prior to completing the identity verification process.
 - Date of birth for 38 accounts.
 - Residential address for 44 accounts.
 

 ● No partners identified for all the 459 partnerships sampled, and directors were not identified and verified for 195 partnerships. Additionally, GCMT noted that partners were missing for 5 accounts manually onboarded and Compliance Operations stated that this appears to be a labelling issue as partners are being pulled in and labeled as beneficial owners as part of the automated process.
 

 ● No process in place for the automatic customer onboarding of Charities and Sports Club, Club or Society in line with the UK Seller KYC Standard Operating Procedure (Master). For context, GCMT noted that 109 accounts were onboarded as 'charitable_org' merchant category and GCMT noted that SEL did not identify and verify the trustees, names and classes of beneficiaries. GCMT also noted that the charity registration number was missing for 105 accounts.
 

 ● 265 customers automatically onboarded as 'Unknown' business type, however this is not an appropriate business typology.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: Presents a moderate risk to the operations of Block. Overall exposure requires a corrective action plan with priority.
