Issue Title: REF 2 - SIL Customer Onboarding Q2 2024 - Discrepancies identified in the Manual and Automated flow (i.e., identity verification processes, gaps in stored customer information, procedures not reflecting current practices)
Issue Description:
MANUAL ONBOARDING
 Requirement:
 Anti-Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism Guidelines for the Financial Sector (Revised 23 June 2021).
 33.— (1) A designated person shall apply the measures specified in F43[subsection (2)], in relation to a customer of the designated person—
 (a) prior to establishing a business relationship with the customer.
 (2) The measures that shall be applied F45[, in accordance with section 30B,] by a designated person under subsection (1) are as follows:
 (a) identifying the customer, and verifying the customer’s identity on the basis of documents (whether or not in electronic form), or information, that the designated person has reasonable grounds to believe can be relied upon to confirm the identity of the customer.
 (b) identifying any beneficial owner connected with the customer or service concerned, and taking measures reasonably warranted by the risk of money laundering or terrorist financing.
 F45[(2A) When applying the measures specified in subsection (2), a designated person shall verify that any person purporting to act on behalf of the customer is so authorised and identify and verify the identity of that person in accordance with subsection (2).]
 (5) Notwithstanding subsection (1)(a), a designated person may verify the identity of a customer or beneficial owner, in accordance with F43[subsection (2)], during the establishment of a business relationship with the customer if the designated person has reasonable grounds to believe that—
 (a) verifying the identity of the customer or beneficial owner (as the case may be) prior to the establishment of the relationship would interrupt the normal conduct of business, and
 (b) there is no real risk that the customer is involved in, or the service sought by the customer is for the purpose of, money laundering or terrorist financing, but the designated person shall take reasonable steps to verify the identity of the customer or beneficial owner, in accordance with F43[subsection (2)], as soon as practicable.
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT reviewed the EEA KYC Procedures and identified the following issues:
 ● Compliance Operations manually review the accounts escalated by Customer Support or Sales, however this process is not documented (i.e., Slack channels).
 ● Some employees outside the Compliance Operations function have editing rights to the EEA KYC Procedures.
 ● No documented SLAs for the manual onboarding queues.
 - 198 merchants had 214 alerts in the gb_ad_hoc originating queue (IE-27, ES-68, FR-118) and 207 of them (IE-27, ES-65, FR-115) were reviewed between 0 to 40 days after the alert was generated, and 6 of them (ES-3, FR-3) were reviewed between 74 and 341 days.
 - 59 merchants did not contain the gb_ad_hoc alert noted in the EEA KYC Procedures, therefore it's not clear how these merchants were activated.
 The analysis is available here. 
 

 GCMT performed a transactional data testing of 217 EU customers (IE-34, ES-74, FR-109) manually onboarded in Q1 2024 and noted that the IDV and Underwriting section in Regulator is incomplete/inaccurate for the following accounts:
 IE:
 ● Missing date of birth for 2 accounts.
 ● Missing residential address for 1 account.
 

 FR:
 ● Incomplete NAF codes for 4 accounts.
 ● Missing beneficial ownership for 3 accounts (2 Limited Company, 1 Partnership).
 ● Incorrect date of birth for 1 account
 ● Missing date of birth for 1 account.
 ● Missing residential address for 1 account (the company name is showing as director).
 ● Missing residential address for two directors related to 1 account.
 ● 1 merchant onboarded as partnership, however the merchant is an association based on the information found on their website. This FR account was found as part of the Quality Control samples related to Q1 2024.
 

 ES:
 ● Missing date of birth for 5 accounts.
 ● Missing residential address for 4 accounts (the missing addresses relate to duplicate associated individuals).
 ● Missing beneficial ownership for 9 accounts.
 

 GCMT identified 2 accounts where a quality check was performed but no beneficial ownership information recorded in Regulator (see Quality Control finding - row above). The Quality Control function stated that 'the current EEA KYC Procedure does not currently require operations to update beneficial ownership information via the legal entity tool'.
 

 AUTOMATIC ONBOARDING
 Requirement:
 Anti-Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism Guidelines for the Financial Sector (Revised 23 June 2021).
 33.— (1) A designated person shall apply the measures specified in F43[subsection (2)], in relation to a customer of the designated person—
 (a) prior to establishing a business relationship with the customer.
 (2) The measures that shall be applied F45[, in accordance with section 30B,] by a designated person under subsection (1) are as follows:
 (a) identifying the customer, and verifying the customer’s identity on the basis of documents (whether or not in electronic form), or information, that the designated person has reasonable grounds to believe can be relied upon to confirm the identity of the customer.
 (b) identifying any beneficial owner connected with the customer or service concerned, and taking measures reasonably warranted by the risk of money laundering or terrorist financing.
 F45[(2A) When applying the measures specified in subsection (2), a designated person shall verify that any person purporting to act on behalf of the customer is so authorised and identify and verify the identity of that person in accordance with subsection (2).]
 (5) Notwithstanding subsection (1)(a), a designated person may verify the identity of a customer or beneficial owner, in accordance with F43[subsection (2)], during the establishment of a business relationship with the customer if the designated person has reasonable grounds to believe that—
 (a) verifying the identity of the customer or beneficial owner (as the case may be) prior to the establishment of the relationship would interrupt the normal conduct of business, and
 (b) there is no real risk that the customer is involved in, or the service sought by the customer is for the purpose of, money laundering or terrorist financing, but the designated person shall take reasonable steps to verify the identity of the customer or beneficial owner, in accordance with F43[subsection (2)], as soon as practicable.
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT performed a transactional data testing of 2,845 EU customers automatically onboarded in Q1 2024 (i.e., IE-749, FR-970, ES-1,126) and identified the following issues:
 ● 98 accounts (i.e., 41 FR & 53 ES Limited Companies and 3 FR & 1 ES Partnerships) with no beneficial ownership. GCMT noted in the review that the beneficial ownership information provided by sellers is not checked via reliable sources (e.g., IE/FR/ES companies registration websites, third party vendors). The Payment Onboarding function noted that SIL relies on the seller to provide the beneficial ownership information at the onboarding, and if the seller does not provide this information, the assumption is that they don't have a beneficial owner. 
 

 ● 16 accounts (i.e., 4 IE & 1 ES Sole Traders, and 4 IE, 3 FR, 4 ES Limited Companies) activated prior to completing the IDV process.
 

 ● Missing date of birth for 1 FR account.
 

 ● No process in place for the automatic customer onboarding of ES & FR Associations and IE GAA Clubs in line with the EEA KYC Procedures requirements. It was also noted that the option to select these business typologies are not available for the manual onboarding flow.
 

 ● The Automated Onboarding / IDV section of the EEA KYC Procedures states that Square will gather and verify the ID type and ID expiry date from sellers, however Regulator doesn't have a field to add the ID type and ID expiry date for the manual onboarding. GCMT noted that the ID type was missing for 24 accounts (FR-18, ES-6) and the ID expiry date was missing for 12 accounts (FR-6, ES-6) manually onboarded.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: Presents a moderate risk to the operations of Block. Overall exposure requires a corrective action plan with priority.
Issue Action Plans:
REF 2 - SIL Customer Onboarding Q2 2024 (Manual Onboarding) (1158504)
 REF 2 - SIL Customer Onboarding Q2 2024 (Automatic Onboarding - root cause and remediation of accounts completed prior to completing KYC process and missing date of birth for FR account) (1158507)
 REF 2 - SIL Customer Onboarding Q2 2024 (Automatic Onboarding - FR & ES Associations and IE GAA Clubs) (1158513)

Issue Title: TM Assessment for Inadequate Risk Assessment Methodology & Product Coverage
Issue Description:
IA reviewed Square Canadas 2022 AML and CTF Sanctions Risk Assessment results and noted that the inherent risk questionnaire did not include the following risk categories as required by FINTRAC:
 

 - Other relevant factors including risk factors such as staffing, turnover, and regulatory changes and
 - New developments and technologies supporting the business operations and risk assessment, for example new TM systems, onboarding tools, and new mobile payment services
 

 Additionally, Square Canada did not include any inherent risk factors specific to its fundraising / donations platform service in the aforementioned Risk Assessment, nor has the business completed an assessment to determine whether transaction monitoring rules specific to fundraising are needed to detect potentially suspicious activity.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Requirement

Issue Title: KYC processes relating to the Onboarding of Australian 'Association' Legal Entity types not performing as expected
Issue Description:
The Square AU Compliance team identified a population of circa 296 cases relating to onboarding that may have been activated without undergoing a manual KYC review. 
 

 It appears that these cases were automatically passed for KYC without being subjected to a manual review (the expected outcome). Given the current KYC construct and operational design that necessitates the need for KYC manual review of 'Association' entity type cases, this issue presents increases the Financial Crime, Regulatory & Reputational risk exposure to Square AU. 
 

 Issue Implication:
 

 Missing a small number of Onboarding Cases under the AML/CTF Act can still have significant consequences for a regulated entity. These may include regulatory scrutiny from AUSTRAC, potential fines, and reputational damage. Even minor oversights can lead to increased compliance costs, such as the need for enhanced monitoring systems and additional staff training. Given the nature of these we would suggest the implication of regulatory risk is low but this action still does result in manual work and re-work for the Compliance Team.
RCA Description: The root cause of the missing Know Your Customer (KYC) association cases was due to a system design. The coding error likely resulted in certain association cases being overlooked or incorrectly flagged, leading to gaps in compliance with AML/CTF regulations. This analysis as of todays investigation suggest the issue was back to its original inception and was not picked up in the roll out project through testing or reviewing the roll out.
Issue Rating Justification: Low volume of customers, Small transactional volumes, Customer impact Low, reputation risk low.
Issue Action Plans:
Perform a review and investigation of the impacted KYC cases to determine whether activation was performed in line with legacy & current procedures. (1161179)
 Perform a Manual KYC Review on 20 of the 296 impacted cases (1161802)
 Implement a periodic KYC manual control to capture net new Association KYC exception cases that may require manual review (1161805)
 Inform Audit and Regional AU Team's of Issue and intended action plans (1161809)

Issue Title: Existing Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC) activities in Risk Operations (RO) need to be strengthened and where gaps in coverage are noted, new QA and QC needs to be implemented
Issue Description:
Currently, all QA and QC (herein referred to as “QA/QC”) activities for RO fall under the scope of the broader Quality Management Program within RO. QA/QC activities are currently applied to multiple processes within RO; however, it’s been identified that some material processes considered higher risk may not have QA/QC in place.
 

 Additionally, there is an opportunity to bolster and/or strengthen existing QA/QC activities to ensure that current QA activities are optimized and that there is consistent application of QA/QC methodologies.
 

 These gaps were identified through feedback from the line of business (RO) and from general observations and other risk activities performed by Operational Excellence (OE).
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: The issue is systemic or has applicability across product lines.
Issue Progress Updates: 11/13/24 - After extensive conversation via Slack with QM and IM leadership, this issue due date will be extended from 11/15/2024 to 2/28/2025. This extension request is to accommodate the finalization of Quality Management's Standard document. Once it's published, the QM team will progress with execution of Action #3: Update all corresponding QA/QC procedures to ensure alignment and consistency with overall methodologies outlined in the Quality Management Program and Framework document.
Issue Action Plans: Existing Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC) activities in Risk Operations (RO) need to be strengthened and where gaps in coverage are noted, new QA and QC needs to be implemented (1166420)
Closure Request Justification: Reviewed evidence to support closure of the issue. - Charlotte Vasi

Issue Title: Exceptions File Manual Upload
Issue Description:
During control testing, CTT identified a new un documented risk and control associated with the manual upload of the exceptions file. The risk associated with this process is that the files are not uploaded completely or accurately, which would cause the Missing Deposits team to inappropriately deny disputes and causing customer harm.
 

 In order to mitigate the risk, the upload of the file should be moved to an automated process rather than manual. In the interim, it is suggested that a new control and risk be documented to ensure oversight and accuracy of the manually uploaded file.
RCA Description: Technology
Issue Rating Justification: An issue in interpretation and execution of CCO policy which has material impact on the process or risk assessment.
Issue Action Plans: Exceptions File Manual Upload (1166429)

Issue Title: Lack of Customer Risk Rating Model
Issue Description:
AAPL has not established a Customer Risk Rating Model (CRR model) to systematically calculate a customer risk rating for each customer (e.g. low, medium, high). IA believes there is a regulatory expectation of reporting entities of the nature and size of AAPL that a CRR model is utilized in order to identify and manage customer risk consistently. This is further supported by (i) the risk-based design of AAPL’s AML program controls, in which higher-risk customers are subject to more frequent monitoring, (ii) the potential for transactional data/indicators to inform customer risk assessments given the inherent design of AAPL’s products (i.e., funding small purchases on an AUD value basis), and (iii) AAPL’s use of specific workflows and registers for managing identification and oversight of ML/TF risk across its customer base (e.g., PEPs, SMRs, ECDD).
 

 Currently, AAPL has manual triggers and procedures to increase the risk rating of a customer from Low
 to High (e.g., a customer is a foreign PEP). All customers are considered Low risk at onboarding by
 default.
 

 Management explained that the proof of concept undertaken in 2021 for a customer risk assessment
 model (RAM) and risk scoring was developed to determine how many data sources are appropriate for
 eIDV. AAPL did not proceed with the implementation.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation
Issue Action Plans:
Migration of AP Data Models into Block Systems (1112424)
 Development of CRR Model to Generate Continuous Risk Ratings (1112426)

Issue Title: Incomplete AML/CTF Procedures
Issue Description:
"Some of SIL’s procedures do not accurately reflect the existing processes in place. Consequently, they do not align with regulatory expectations, which require regulated entities to maintain documented procedures that accurately reflect operational practices. Specifically:
 

 KYC Procedures
 The below processes are in place and in line with the regulatory requirements and expectations; however, they are not documented, or accurately documented, in any SIL’s procedures:
 - SIL EEA Seller KYC procedure mistakenly states that Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) is required for customers identified with a high-risk Activation Customer Risk Rating (aCRR) score. SIL’s CCO and Square Compliance ML Lead confirmed the typo in the procedure and that the current process in place only requires to conduct EDD after a customer has transacted and being identified as High in the Continuous Activation Customer Risk Rating (cCRR) or as a PEP customer. This is correctly outlined into the Square Global Activation Customer Risk Rating system designed procedure.
 - The processes for the identification and approval of customers where the ownership structure of the company appears unusual or excessively complex given the nature of the company’s business are not formally documented.
 - The process for identifying and preventing deactivated sellers from creating new accounts with SIL, which was implemented in January 2024 is not formally documented.
 

 Suspicious Transaction Report (“STR”) Procedures:
 -SIL’s MLRO confirmed that a MLRO designee is authorized to approve STRs but this is not explicitly outlined in the SIL EEA STR procedures. In addition, there is no documented evidence of the authorization of the MLRO designee.
 

 Applicable regulations/guidelines (further detail on appendix 1):
 - Section 6.7 of CBI AML/CTF Guidelines"
Issue Rating Justification:
"● Non-compliance with the regulatory guidance may lead to legal actions resulting in fines, penalties and reputational damage.
 

 ● If policy and procedures are not comprehensive and detailed, this may lead to inconsistent application and interpretation of regulatory requirements."
Issue Action Plans: Updating STR and KYC Procedures (1142675)

Issue Title: UAR alert clearing SLA definition discrepancy
Issue Description:
Requirement:
 Pay Monthly Transaction Monitoring SOP (02/20/2024)
 1.4 Service Level Agreements (SLA) Monitoring
 TM maintains oversight of operational compliance service level agreements through regular review of alert volumes, Key Risk Indicator / Key Performance Indicator (KRI / KPI) trends, and escalation rates (further details contained in the WFM for Block Compliance document). TM has a commitment to review and disposition alerts within 30 days of the creation date. Additionally, The Operational Compliance Insights Committee (OCIC) meets monthly to review key trends and metrics with Senior Leadership to help inform and proactively assess any compliance queues that exceed, or are trending toward exceeding, SLA or that present the risk of entering a backlog, including a review of actions taken.
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT determined that two automated transaction monitoring alerts were not cleared within 30 calendar days from the date of the alert (32 and 33 days). At the time of this review business practice tracked the SLA based off of business days (22 and 23 days due to holidays and weekends), rather than calendar, so the discrepancy is based off of the lack of clarity defining what a 'day' is. 
 

 As of 8/22/2024 the procedures have been updated and the process will be to count calendar days when determining the SLA.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: July US Afterpay GCMT Monitoring - Unusual Activity Reports/Referrals (1027268)
Issue Rating Justification: Issue best fits within the moderate category: Presents a moderate risk to the operations of Block. Overall exposure (existing or potential) requires a corrective action plan with priority.
Issue Action Plans: Process and Procedure update for US transaction monitoring alert processing (1167082)
Closure Request Justification:
Closed prior to report issuance. 
 

 No additional action required as the process and procedures have been updated to reflect the 30 calendar day SLA.

Issue Title: Bitcoin Conversion NY Restriction Monitoring
Issue Description:
New York Department of Financial Services (NYDFS) requires Square to have a bitlicense to offer the Square Bitcoin Conversion product to Sellers based in New York, which Square does not currently have. 
 

 Square must monitor Sellers to identify those that enroll in Square Invest/Bitcoin Conversion outside New York, then change location and relocate into New York (To date there have been two sellers that have flagged for this action/activity). Square must:
 

 Deactivate these Sellers in Regulator from Square Invest/Bitcoin Conversion.
 Sweep back any funds in flight to the merchant account at the time of deactivating the seller from the Square Invest/Bitcoin Conversion product
 Develop a macro that Square+Cash Compliance Ops can use to notify the seller that they have been unenrolled from the Square Invest/Bitcoin Conversion product due to their business no longer being located in an eligible state.
 

 Additionally, Product is requesting the implementation of a manual review of sellers enrolled in Square Invest/Bitcoin Conversion to ensure no active sellers are operating in New York.
RCA Description: No issue as the current system in place has captured two sellers who enrolled in the Bitcoin Conversion product then relocated to NY.
Issue Rating Justification: Utilization of Bitcoin in NY without Bit License is a regulatory concern
Issue Action Plans:
Eng Team Developed Control For BTC Conversion NY Exclusion (1175011)
 Temporary Manual Control Process (1175015)
Closure Request Justification: Square Product (BTC Conversions) began rolling out the automated disabling feature starting March 1, 2025. Please reference attached closure memo for details.

Issue Title: Inadequate RegTech configuration assessment and lack of performance monitoring
Issue Description:
"SIL does not have (i) a formal review and approval process to ensure Identity Verification (“IDV”) requirements defined by SIL Compliance in its policies and procedures are accurately identified and applied in the RegTech tool nor (ii) a periodic mapping between SIL policies and procedures and the RegTech configuration to ensure current policy expectations are completely and accurately reflected in the tool. This practice does not align with local regulatory requirements and guidance. 
 

 IA identified a case where a seller was onboarded using a non-EU passport, which is not accepted under the SIL EEA Seller KYC Procedure, and a valid proof of ""right to remain"" or residence permit was neither requested nor obtained, as mandated by the same SIL procedure. The root cause of this issue was that the IDV requirements specified in the SIL KYC Procedures were not accurately reflected in the Compliance Requirements Document (CRD), which was used to configure the RegTech vendor's platform at the inception of the SIL product. This discrepancy went unnoticed due to (i) an inadequate review and approval process before the requirements were applied and (ii) the absence of periodic mapping between SIL policies and procedures and the RegTech configuration, leading to the inaccurate coding of the requirements into the RegTech vendor, Veriff in this case.
 

 In addition, SIL does not have a periodic review process of the RegTech vendors’ performance that could potentially identify inconsistencies, such as the one described above, between the identity documents accepted by the RegTech solution during customer onboarding and the list of acceptable ID documents outlined in SIL's AML/CTF policies and procedures.
 

 Applicable regulations/guidelines (further detail on appendix 1):
 - Section 5.2.6 of CBI AML/CTF Guidelines
 - Section 33(1) of the CJA 2010"
Interested Parties:
Danielle Franklin
 Esther Scott
 Elizabeth Henry
Issue Rating Justification:
"● Customer onboarded without required identity verification exposes SIL to increased risk of fraudulent activities, such as money laundering, or other financial crimes.
 

 ● Onboarding incorrectly identified customers may lead to non-compliance with the regulatory requirements and guidance resulting in legal actions, fines, penalties and reputational damage.
 

 ● RegTech solutions not supporting compliance with the regulatory requirements and guidance resulting in legal actions, fines, penalties and reputational damage."
Issue Action Plans:
Conducting Reg Tech Requirement Assessment (1138133)
 Validating Vendor's Performance (1138137)
 Documenting Procedure Creating and Changing Configurations with Reg Tech vendors (1138704)
 Lookback on Customer Base With Invalid IDs (1138707)
 Correct Veriff settings (1146633)

Issue Title: Absence of Timely PEP and Sanction Screening at Onboarding
Issue Description:
At the inception of the Square product in the UK, SEL did not ensure local PEP and Sanctions screening requirements were included, and adopted a global PEP and Sanctions screening framework that did not fully align with local regulations. This discrepancy was not identified during any periodic reviews of the SEL AML/CTF Program until it was highlighted by an independent audit conducted in another group entity in March 2023. SEL Compliance informed Internal Audit that it has already developed action plans to address this risk, which will be implemented in Q3 2024. 
 

 The specific findings identified are: 
 

 2.1: Sanctions Screening 
 SEL’s ‘UK Seller KYC Operating Procedure' does not align with UK AML/CTF regulatory guidance regarding sanctions screening, as these screening checks are determined 24-48 hours after the seller’s account activation. 
 The Financial Crime Authority (“FCA”) handbook provides some flexibility for retrospective screening if “good reasons” justify the risk posed by such an approach. However, SEL does not have a formal documented rationale in its AML Policy or Risk Appetite statement to support and rationalise the current process. 
 

 These requirements (PEPs and sanctions screening) are currently not met at SEL, allowing seller’s accounts to be activated and fully operate (including withdrawing funds) without completing an appropriate risk assessment. "
 

 2.2: PEPs Screening 
 With respect to PEPs, the MLRs 2017 require senior management approval to be obtained before establishing such a relationship, though this does not take place at SEL as PEP customers (high-risk) are identified after onboarding. Additionally, SEL is not completing Enhanced Due Diligence (“EDD”) on its PEP customer prior to allowing them to transact. This practice does not align with local regulations and expectations. 
 

 These requirements (PEPs and sanctions screening) are currently not met at SEL, allowing seller’s accounts to be activated and fully operate (including withdrawing funds) without completing an appropriate risk assessment.
Issue Rating Justification:
● Adopting a global framework that does not align with local regulations may put SEL at risk of violating AML/CFT laws and facing penalties from regulatory authorities. 
 

 ● The absence of sanctions screening prior to customer activation may result in facilitation of payments to or from sanctioned people and organizations. 
 

 ● The acceptance of this risk by management without appropriate documentation within the risk appetite statement may indicate a lack of transparency and accountability in risk management practices, potentially resulting in regulatory scrutiny. 
 

 ● The absence of PEP screening prior to customer activation may result in inadequate identification and mitigation of risks associated with PEPs. 
 

 ● Failure to conduct a complete EDD review and obtain senior management approval before establishing business relationships with PEP customers is in violation of MLRs 2017, Regulation 35, and could result in fines or penalties. 
 

 ● Allowing customers to transact and operate fully before conducting a full CDD review may increase the risk of potential TF activities going undetected during the initial period.
Issue Action Plans:
Adjusting PEP and Screening Process (1130134)
 PEP Procedure Update (1130785)

Issue Title: Lack of Sanctions Screening for Square Checking U.S. Primary Account Holders
Issue Description: As part of a CIP remediation exercise, the Square Bank Accounts Product Manager and Square Partner Risk & Compliance team identified a potential gap in Square Checking’s sanctions screening process. Further investigation confirmed that some Square Checking account holder names are not subjected to initial or ongoing sanctions screening. Square Checking, which is an add-on product offered to existing sellers who have been onboarded for the Managed Payments product, allows U.S. users to enter a different name on the Square Checking application for Account Holder than the name of the Seller or Activator submitted during Managed Payments onboarding, which leads to a screening gap as the applicant/account holder name is not subjected to sanctions screening if the account holder is not the same name as the seller business or activator who initially onboarded as a Square customer via the Managed Payments product. This issue is the result of a process design. When Square Checking was launched, it relied on the existing screening processes for Managed Payments customers but did not account for the potential differences in account holder names.
RCA Description: PROCESS DESIGN: Inadequate process design is the reason for this gap. Specifically, there is no separate screening process associated with Square Checking signup. Square Checking relies entirely on the screening completed as part of Payments onboarded; however, Square Checking's application allows applicants to enter a different name than what was submitted to Payments onboarding. As a result, there are Square Checking account holders who have not been subjected to initial or ongoing sanctions screening because they are not the same individual that signed up for the Square Payments account.
Issue Rating Justification: The result of this issue is that Square is providing products, specifically banking products, to individuals without having performed sanctions watchlist screening on these individuals. Initial and ongoing sanctions screening of customers is a regulatory expectation, and the failure to screen these individuals presents heightened regulatory risk as it prevents us from confirming that we are not providing products to sanctioned parties.
Issue Action Plans:
One-time Remediation Screen (1210658)
 One-Time Remediation Screen Case Review (1211304)
 Create New Product Intents to Support Square Checking Screening (1211308)
 Implementation of Screening for Square Checking and Backfilling Existing Population (1211314)
 Sustainability (1211318)
Closure Request Justification: Reviewed and approved supporting documents uploaded.

Issue Title: Square - Sanctioned Jurisdiction Top Level Domain (TLD) in Email Addresses
Issue Description:
Square does not have existing controls in place to identify or prevent the use of email addresses based on suffixes, or Top Level Domains (TLDs), associated with sanctioned jurisdictions. The root cause of this issue is that TLD detection or blocking controls were not designed or built into processes related to onboarding or account maintenance throughout a customer’s relationship. TLDs related to sanctioned jurisdictions may be indicative of a party residing in that jurisdiction. The Office of Foreign Assets Control (“OFAC”) has an expectation that companies incorporate appropriate controls related to information they collect, including email addresses, to ensure services are not provided or payments are not processed in violation of sanctions regulations.
 

 Belarus - .by, .бел
 Afghanistan - .af
 Venezuela- .ve
 *Cuba - .cu
 *Iran - .ir, ایران.
 *North Korea - .kp
 Russia - .ru, su, .pф (representing ‘rf’ in Cyrillic)
 *Syria - .sy
 *Ukraine (Crimea, Donetsk, Luhansk) - .ua, .укр, .bg
 

 This issue was identified following the identification of a similar issue by Cash App. After becoming aware of OFAC enforcement actions from 2022 and 2023 against companies with an absence of controls for emails with sanctions-related TLD, Cash App decided to implement a control and identify their customer population with these types of emails. Following Cash App’s review, Global Sanctions Compliance (“GSC”) initiated a request for Square Compliance Analytics (“SCA”) to pull data identifying Square’s customer population with such emails. Data was pulled on 8/8/24 to determine how many users on our platform currently have these types of email suffixes - results here.
 

 Observed exposure identified (thus far): 9,459 customers; 24,437 associated merchants; 150,170 associated users with email TLDs related to Cuba, North Korea, Iran and Syria, and many more to the other jurisdictions. There is a heightened risk with the “customer count” in column B in the Customer Email Summary Tab, as these are Square sellers. There is a lesser, but still, a risk with the “associated merchants” and “associated users” as data was pulled for all email addresses that are recorded within Square. 
 

 This issue is being raised because failing to meet regulatory expectations increases our risk exposure to facilitating financial activity in sanctioned jurisdictions and could subject Square and Block to regulatory fines.
RCA Description: Process Design: Controls to identify and prevent the use of email addresses based on TLDs from sanctioned jurisdictions were not built in to prevent these types of email addresses at seller onboarding or throughout the seller’s relationship.
Impacted Countries:
Canada
 United Kingdom
 France
 Ireland
 Spain
 Japan
 Australia
 United States
Impacted Legal Entities:
Square Canada, Inc.
 Squareup Europe Ltd.
 Squareup International Ltd.
 Square K.K.
 Square AU Pty. Ltd.
 Block, Inc.
Issue Rating Justification:
High - Email addresses based on suffixes, or Top Level Domains (TLDs) associated with comprehensively sanctioned jurisdictions may be indicative of a customer’s location or permanent residence and under OFAC rules and regulations, Square is prohibited from providing services to persons ordinarily resident in comprehensively sanctioned jurisdictions (this includes individuals/entities not currently on any OFAC-administered sanctions lists). A few examples of the relevant regulations:
 Cuba - § 515.201 Transactions involving designated foreign countries or their nationals; effective date. The definition of a Cuban national includes persons domiciled in or a permanent resident of Cuba.
 North Korea - § 510.206 Prohibited exportation or re-exportation of goods, services, or technology to North Korea.
 Iran - Prohibited exportation, reexportation, sale, or supply of goods, technology, or services to Iran.
 The observed exposure of email TLDs, associated with comprehensively sanctioned jurisdictions, across SQ may represent violations of the OFAC requirements/regulations mentioned above and have resulted in enforcement action as recently as 2023. In this case, OFAC called the lack of TLD controls an "aggravating factor" because the business knew or had reason to know that the email address suffixes were associated with sanctioned jurisdictions "but did not incorporate this information into its compliance program or controls.
Issue Action Plans:
Onboarding Control (1193482)
 Post-Onboarding Control (1193484)
 Create a requirements document (1374330)
 Go Forward Control - Account Owners (1374333)
 Go Forward Control - KYB Roles (1374335)
 Remediation- Identify Remediation Population (1374980)
 Remediation Plan and SSP Review Process (1374983)
 Remediation by Sanctions Compliance Ops- Account Owner and KYB Roles (1374986)
 Remediation by Engineering Product Design (EPD)- Account Owner and KYB Role (1374989)
 Remediation- Send Communications to Applicable Cohorts (Non-SSP Comms) (1374992)
 Restricted TLD Changes and Ongoing Maintenance (1374994)
 Go Forward Control - Account Owners (1380985)
  Go Forward Control - KYB Roles (1380988)
 Sustainability (1380991)
Closure Request Justification:
Submitting for closure on behalf of Issue Approver Faisal Sohail. Please see action plans for supporting documentation and closure comments.

Issue Title: Afterpay plus not blocking Zip Pay - which has resulted in successful Zip Pay transactions
Issue Description:
In April 2023 after the launch of Afterpay Plus payments to Zip Pay were blocked through the product merchant blocking function - which was in place blocking Zip Pay transactions between April 2023 - March 2024. 
 

 In March 2024, Afterpay implemented a new merchant blocking mechanism as the prior blocking function implemented in April 2023 was deemed to not scalable and had gaps. This blocking implementation was conducted, however, Zip Pay transactions were not blocked. 
 

 In August 2024 the Afterpay Complaints team notified the the ANZ Compliance team about an IDR complaint from a customer which related to successful Afterpay Plus transactions to Zip Pay, which identified the issue relating to the merchant blocking implementation. Compliance made product aware of the issue and immediately Product applied a block which stopped all transactions at Zip Pay.
 

 In addition to the Zip Pay block, Product additionally have implemented a monthly monitoring process for Afterpay Plus card transactions in August 2024 to identify if there are any transactions to Zip Pay conducted. 
 

 Compliance has reviewed this issue and confirmed that it does not constitute as a breach under the Incident and Breach Management Policy.
 

 NB: 
 If there are any changes to the facts and circumstances compliance to be notified for an assessment.
Issue Rating Justification: issue represents "non-key elements" of internal policies and procedures.
Issue Action Plans: Block the Zip Pay code (1185613)
Closure Request Justification:
Actions complete - ZipPay transactions have been blocked from Aug 2024 - document attached.
 

 Closed Date noted as 10/03/2024 (October 3rd)

Issue Title: A limited partnership was on boarded which is not permitted by the Afterpay New Zealand AML/CFT Programme Requirements
Issue Description:
The Afterpay New Zealand AML/CFT Programme specifies that Afterpay NZ does not provide services to Limited Partnerships. 
 

 GCMT identified one from 397 active merchants onboarded during the review period was operating as a Limited Partnership entity which is outside the Afterpay New Zealand AML/CFT programme requirements. 
 GCMT acknowledges that this onboard met the requirement of Regulation 11(2)(e) regarding the additional requirement to obtain and verifying information about the existence and name of any nominee general partner and that the Compliance team was consulted for the onboarding to occur.. 
 However, GCMT identified a Beneficial Owner for this Limited Partnership on the NZ Company's register which was not identified and verified as part of this onboarding. As the Afterpay New Zealand AML/CFT programme does not permit the onboarding of Limited partnerships, it therefore does not provide guidance for the verification of beneficial ownership within limited partnerships.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Afterpay NZ AML Merchant Due Diligence Post Implementation Review (1027275)
RCA Description: Process: The onboarding for the one accepted Limited Partnership commenced in May 2024 and was completed in June 2024 post implementation of the updated Programme. The merchant was in flight before the policy was updated.
Issue Rating Justification: No other Limited Partnerships were onboarded during the testing period - GCMT only identified one instance.
Issue Action Plans: Risk acceptance (1175019)
Closure Request Justification:
The risk is accepted on the basis that:
 From 1 June 2024 the AML/CFT Programme does not permit the onboarding of Limited Partnerships. The onboarding for the one accepted Limited Partnership commenced in May 2024 and was completed in June 2024 post implementation of the updated Programme. The merchant was in flight before the policy was updated. 
 System preventative controls have been implemented in the Merchant Onboarding Portal to stop Limited Partnerships from being onboarded.
 There will be no exceptions created for the onboarding of limited partnerships in the future.
 The risk rating is low.

Issue Title: Instances where Afterpay New Zealand Merchant Vetting Instructions/Regulation 11 requirements were not completed for verification of company constitutions and addresses.
Issue Description:
Merchant Vetting Instructions (New Zealand)/ Regulation 11 Requirements
 Per the Merchant Vetting Instructions (New Zealand) and consistent with Regulation 11 requirements to verify merchant information, the Onboarding Officer should ensure that the:
 - The Registered Office or Address for Service Address on the NZ Companies Register/NZBN Look up websites matches the address details provided by the Merchant in the application
 - If the merchant is a private company, the existence of a Constitution document on the NZ Companies Register matches the answer provided by the Merchant in the application.
 

 Finding
 Of 42 samples active merchants onboarded, GCMT identified the Merchant Onboarding team did not accurately verify the information received from the merchant required by the regulation and the policy:
 2 samples where the merchant has answered that they have a company constitution through the Compliance Verification Form, but the NZ Companies Register specifies that the merchants does not have a constitution (Regulation 11 requirement) 
 2 samples where the merchant's business address did not match to the NZ Companies Register (pre-existing KYB requirement of the AML/CFT Regulations)
 

 Note; Separately, GCMT is conscious that the Merchant Onboarding Team's Quality Assurance process has been put on hold since June 2024 to allow the team to address a significant backlog due to the Afterpay Salesforce system integrations.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Afterpay NZ AML Merchant Due Diligence Post Implementation Review (1027275)
RCA Description: People: Human performance (action or inaction).
Issue Rating Justification: Small percentage of transactions that had exceptions. Additionally, the Merchant Onboarding Team already has a Quality Assurance process in place but has been put on hold since June 2024 to allow the team to address a significant backlog due to the Afterpay Salesforce system integrations.
Issue Action Plans: Training Refresher (1175664)
Closure Request Justification:
Training refresher has been provided to the Merchant Onboarding team on the Afterpay New Zealand Merchant Vetting Instructions/Regulation 11 requirements for verification of company constitutions and addresses. 
 Refer to action item.

Issue Title: Potential Violation of Mastercard Rule 5.1.1 - Verify Bona Fide Business Operation
Issue Description:
Square Merchant accounts are enabled for card acceptance before the following Screening and Due Diligence is completed: PEP Screening, MATCH Screening, Sanctions screening, Customer Due Diligence (CDD) and Customer Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD). 
 

 SQUARE stated that although the account was activated prior to the completion of Screening / CDD / EDD, new merchants were only allowed to accept transactions and that payments to the merchants were ‘held’ until such time as all screening and due diligence had been completed.
 

 Mastercard has reminded SQUARE that this process is non-compliant with Mastercard Rule 5.1.1, and that SQUARE should review the current process flow to ensure that ‘account activation’ is not allowed until such time as screening and due diligence requirements are being completed”.
Hyperlinks:
https://drive.google.com/drive/u/0/folders/1Lh27vqhu8ZiCssOQnTr1bs7QIlvyochu|Sanctions Screening prior to Activation project status 
 https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1GtaA6Wi2sW_XdCVjVsh5YP9Qvhgq2Vi6/edit?gid=2036561922#gid=2036561922|Mastercard FMP Update tracker
RCA Description:
Square failed to provide adequate regard and consideration for Mastercard Rule 5.1.1 which states the following: 
 

 Before entering into, extending, or renewing a Merchant Agreement, an Acquirer must inquire and verify whether that Merchant from which it intends to acquire Transactions is a Government Controlled Merchant. An Acquirer must not onboard, or maintain a Merchant-related relationship with, any Government Controlled Merchant whose home country government is subject to sanctions laws and regulations enacted by United States sanctions authorities (including, OFAC and the United States Department of State), as well as applicable local sanctions regulations where the Activity is taking place. Before entering into, extending, or renewing a Merchant Agreement or ATM Owner Agreement, an Acquirer must verify that the Merchant or ATM owner from which it intends to acquire Transactions is a bona fide business, has sufficient safeguards in place to protect Account data from unauthorized disclosure or use, and complies with applicable laws, and that each POS Transaction will reflect bona fide business between the Merchant or Sponsored Merchant and a Cardholder. Procedures for verifying that a Merchant or ATM owner is a bona fide business are set forth in Chapter 7 of the Security Rules and Procedures manual.

 Australia
 United States

 Squareup Europe Ltd.
 Squareup International Ltd.

Issue Rating Justification: Mastercard Methodology
Issue Action Plans: Enhance Due Diligence Procedures to ensure Sanction screening is completed prior to Merchant Account Activation (1175672)

Issue Title: Square Canada - Crowdfunding Controls.
Issue Description:
Square Canada is registered as a Money Services Business (MSB) in its capacity as both a payment service provider and provider of crowdfunding platform services. Because Square enables its merchants to accept donations using its software and related applications, Square must maintain controls in order to ensure that its requirements with respect to crowdfunding platform services are met.
 

 Square’s Compliance Team identified that current controls may not be robust enough to provide a high degree of confidence that Square’s obligations specific to crowdfunding platform services are met. Square should review its controls with respect to identity verification and capturing the purpose of donation to ensure, with a high degree of confidence, that its regulatory obligations are met.
RCA Description: Identified controls are not robust enough to maintain high confidence that Square Canada's crowdfunding obligations are met.
Issue Rating Justification: SQ does maintain controls in this space that are reasonably designed to the applicable regulations and SQ's risk. If sellers respond to these controls in the intended way, they will operate effectively. However, they can be circumvented under certain circumstances, thus motivating a medium and not high risk.

Issue Title: Fraudulent 1099-K Tax Forms
Issue Description:
There is no clear path to resolution for Cash App customers and non-customers who report receiving inaccurate 1099-K tax forms as a result of fraudulent accounts created using their personal information. When stolen identities are used for Cash App accounts with activity of large sums of money, tax notices are sent to the victim of ID Theft stating that they did not report these earnings with the IRS. When this occurs, current workflows make it difficult for the victims to contact Cash App Support for help, and the roadblocks persist when a complaint is made.
 

 Additionally, internal operations give a lack of clarity and an abundance of obstacles to the teams who handle these cases and accompanying complaints. At this time, these complaints typically are escalated to four teams: 1) CCOT - receives/responds to the complaint, 2) ATO or ID Theft - provides the evaluation that the account was either taken over or created fraudulently, 3) Civil Process and Tax Operations - helps justify/locate an account and files the Justice JIRA, and 4) Justice Engineering - takes action as requested in the JIRA and processes the request to $0 the forms. Due to the complicated setup of this process, when it comes to Regulatory complaints, CCOT rarely is able to provide a resolution to the customer by its deadline.
 

 What Should Have Happened?
 

 Support should be more accessible and responsive to Cash App customers and non-customers who are victims of ID Theft related to these fraudulent tax forms. Additionally, the workflow for operating these cases and complaints should be a simpler, more effective process to assist the team(s) working on them, specifically allowing CCO to assist these customers and non-customers through traditional support, which would enable CCOT to meet its deadlines with Regulatory complaints.
 

 What is the Impact?
 

 The lack of a clear path to resolution on this issue is causing consumer harm to both customers and non-customers who suffer real penalties (up to $100,000 in some cases). This also puts Cash App and Block at risk when a complainant opts for small claims court due to not receiving help after filing a complaint. In more general terms, this issue is a compromise on ""Trust,"" which Cash App Senior Leadership has identified as one of our current overarching objectives.
 

 Who Identified the Issue and How?
 

 This issue was identified by CCOT’s Complaint External Response Team (CERT) on Monday, May 6, 2024 when an audit was conducted on Regulatory complaints claiming ID Theft with 1099-K tax forms."
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: An issue in interpretation and execution of CCO policy which has material impact on the process or risk assessment.
Issue Action Plans: Fraudulent 1099-K Tax Forms (1176331)
Closure Request Justification: Evidence package reviewed to support closure artifacts. As noted in the evidence package, the scope of the issue when it was opened was tied to a specific process within the overall larger 1099-K process. After the issue was opened (with the specific limited scope), additional workstreams were identified that were targeting other improvements overall to the 1099-K process. While those workstreams were outside of the scope of this issue, we do reference some of that additional work here in the evidence package. While those additional items were not a condition for closure of this issue, they've been included for the overall evidence record for this issue for transparency.

Issue Title: Unknown / Unrecognized P2P Chargeback Fraud Pattern
Issue Description:
What Happened?
 Customers are receiving payments from unknown or unrecognized senders who intentionally use a fraud tactic. The fraudsters first send a payment to the customer and then immediately request a return of funds via a separate transaction. When the customer reaches out to Cash Support to ask what they should do, we inform them that they should refund the payment, but we don’t specify that they should do so within the original payment that was sent to them, instead of accepting the fraudster's request. Once the customer complies and returns the funds, the fraudster files a chargeback with their bank on the original payment. This leads to the funds being clawed back from the complainant’s balance, despite the complainant already returning the money as instructed. This scheme exploits Cash App’s current policies, leaving customers at a financial loss.
 

 

 What Should Have Happened?
 Cash App needs to update its Customer Support verbiage to better protect our customers. Specifically, we should update the Cash App {Unknown/Unrecognized Payment - Recipient} quicktext so that it informs customers to:
 Ignore additional requests for funds from unknown senders
 Reimburse the initial payment directly, as outlined in the Cash App {P2P - Refund Payment} quicktext
 This approach will help prevent chargebacks from occurring and protect customers from losing their funds.
 

 Note: Cash App may want to also implement tooling which blocks the fraudsters from sending a follow-up refund request based on specific heuristics. 
 

 What is the Impact?
 Cash App is currently not providing optimal protection for customers due to generic instructions that advise them to refund the sender without clarifying that this should be done through the original payment. As a result, customers may suffer financial losses when fraudsters exploit this process. The lack of specific guidance on how to handle these situations fails to prevent chargebacks and undermines customer trust in Cash App’s ability to manage P2P transactions securely.
 

 Who Identified the Issue and How?
 The CCOT team first identified the issue on July 24, 2024, while handling regulatory complaint CCN-2790174. They filed a ticket on July 25 with the Content team to update customer communications, advising customers to ignore additional fund requests from unknown senders and to refund only the initial payment. However, Content denied the request, calling it an edge case. Both CCOT and CCPM Insights have since confirmed this pattern through further investigation, and investigation by Richard Lu (MLM) revealed a trend in similar fraud cases.
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: An issue in interpretation and execution of CCO policy which has material impact on the process or risk assessment.
Issue Action Plans: Unknown / Unrecognized P2P Chargeback Fraud Pattern (1176334)
Closure Request Justification:
QA completed by Corey Griggs on 11/20/24, validation executed, issue is ready to proceed with closure process.
 

 Issue Management Lead reviewed and validated all supporting evidence for closure on 11/20/24.

Issue Title: Pro-rata refund for Plus Card subscription fees for customers who have established a Payment Arrangement within a short period of time from the monthly payment being charged.
Issue Description:
Background 
 The current Plus Card membership subscription fee of $AU9.99 is charged on a monthly basis to the customer’s nominated card on file. 
 For Australian customers who have an active Plus Card membership at the time a payment arrangement is established, the Plus Card membership will be automatically cancelled by the system. The customer is advised of this in the ‘Request for More information for Payment Arrangement’ macro. 
 Finding
 GCMT noted that Plus Card subscription fees are not currently refunded on a pro-rata basis, in situations where the customer has paid a recent Plus Card subscription fee and has subsequently established a payment arrangement within a short period of time. 
 

 Whilst this is not a regulatory or code requirement, for customers who have their membership cancelled within a few days of ‘monthly renewal’ this would mean that no or little benefit is gained from the payment. 
 

 GCMT tested 11 samples where the customer had an active subscription at the time they contacted Afterpay for financial hardship assistance. Of the 11 samples, 5 customers had renewed/paid the monthly subscription fee for the coming month and due to the establishment of a payment arrangement only realised half or less of the benefit. 
 

 Date Subscription Fee Paid  Date Subscription cancelled (PA established date)  Days of membership used 
 21 March 2024  24 March 2024   3 days out of 31 days paid 
 27 April 2024   13 May 2024   16 days out of 31 days paid 
 21 May 2024   3 June 2024   13 days out of 31 days paid 
 5 June 2024   14 June 2024   9 days out of 30 days paid 
 10 June 2024   11 June 204   1 day out of 30 days paid
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: The ‘Payment Arrangement: Escalated to Collections team’ SOP requires updates to reflect current practices and regional requirements
Issue Description:
Background 
 The current ‘Payment Arrangement: Escalated to Collections team’ is the only SOP which is used by Collection and Operations Agents when reviewing and actioning on escalated payment arrangement and/or hardship requests.
 

 Finding
 Management should consider updating the current SOP for the following: 
 ● The current SOP should be updated to include clear requirements for each region. For example, in Step 1 it is noted that if it is not the customer’s 5th or more request that the agent should give the customer a call. GCMT notes that this process of conducting an outbound call is only operational in Australia and New Zealand at the current time. For the US, CA and the UK an outbound email is sent instead of a call.  
 o As noted in the above themes for the UK, there are notable variances in the current processes and practices employed in the UK in comparison to ANZ and NA for escalated payment arrangement tickets. 
 o At the time of testing, the SOP was only visible/accessible on the ANZ HelpCentre with no publication in the US, CA or UK. 
 ● ‘Step 1’ notes that ‘If this is the customer's 5th or more request to adjust their payment arrangement’ that the agent should ‘Refer the account to the Operations Support Team Leader for review’. In practice, GCMT notes that this is not conducted as the first step, with the case escalated to the Operations Support Team Leader as the ‘final’ step for approval prior to the processing of the Payment Arrangement. 
 ● In the ‘Actioning Account: What can we approve?’ section it is noted that if a request is for a ‘Payment arrangement that doesn’t start for 21 days and is more/less than 5 years’ that this cannot be approved by the operations team and a Moratorium should be provided instead (if eligible/applicable). In practice, this is not the case, with payment arrangements which have a start date greater than 21 days being approved regularly.
 

 Additionally, GCMT noted during testing that some frontline agents who are escalating the case to the Collections and Operations team are utilising a template escalation note which states that the frontline agent has attempted to ‘negotiate with the customer’. This is not the case in practice, with the CSA escalating straight away after confirming that it is the 4th or more request to amend the payment arrangement and/or the payment arrangement requested is over 5 years.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: Banking Missing Deposits and Paper Money Dispute process lacks the ability to provide provisional credit to comply with regulatory guidance
Issue Description:
During Control Testing, CTT identified an out of scope gap and regulatory risk associated with the Banking Missing
 Deposits and Paper Money Dispute process. Currently, the teams do not have the ability to provide provisional credits
 during an investigation in the event the investigation needs to extend past the original 10 day SLA. There are instances in
 which the missing deposits team does not receive a response from our banking partners prior to the 10 day SLA (RO6)
 resulting in the claim being declined and an inaccurate resolution of the customers reported claim/error. As the current
 process stands, there is a regulatory concern in not being able to extend the investigation period past the 10 day SLA and
 provide the customer with the required provisional credit as advised within Reg E guidelines.
 

 Due to the lack of ability to extend the investigation period and provide the provisional credit, the Missing Deposits and
 Paper Money Dispute teams were advised in the event of untimely responses from banking partners, the claim was to be
 denied, closed and communication be sent to the customer. This process however does not align with Reg E
 requirements and exposes the business to not being compliant with those requirements.
RCA Description: Technology
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure resulting in an impact to regulatory obligations
Issue Action Plans: Banking Missing Deposits and Paper Money Dispute process lacks the ability to provide provisional credit to comply with regulatory guidance (1178300)
Closure Request Justification: Supporting evidence for all action items was reviewed by IM Lead. No exceptions noted. - Charlotte Vasi

Issue Title: REF 2 - SIL Adverse Media, Sanctions, and PEP Screening Q3 2024 - Compliance Operations and Foundational Compliance Engineering processes uplift
Issue Description:
COMPLIANCE OPERATIONS
 Regulatory Requirement:
 Anti-Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism Guidelines for the Financial Sector (Revised 23 June 2021)
 4.4.2. Risk factors that Firms should consider, where appropriate, when assessing the risks associated with a customer’s or their beneficial owner’s reputation include, for example whether:
 ● There are adverse media reports or other relevant information sources about the customer or its beneficial owner.
 5.3. Screening undertaken of all customers to identify new and on-going PEP relationships. The frequency of such screening should to be determined by the Firm, commensurate with the Firm’s Business Risk Assessment.
 

 Policy Requirement:
 SIL Square EEA PEP Screening Procedure (pg 4)
 PEP Queues: 
 [Country] PEP PLATFORM INITIAL SCREEN (1 Day Queue SLA)
 [Country] PEP PLATFORM RESCREEN (30 Day Queue SLA)
 

 Testing Results:
 ● A sample of 12 alerts (Adverse Media - 8, and PEP - 4), generated in April 2024, were reviewed and it was noted that 8 alerts (Adverse Media - 4, and PEP - 4) were reviewed after the 1 day SLA. The review occurred between 2 and 5 days after the alert was generated. 
 The list of alerts reviewed after the 1 day SLA is available here.
 

 ● No procedure in place for the SIL Adverse Media review of alerts.
 

 ● The UK/EU Watchlist Screening (AMLRS/Square) - Adverse Media Standard Operating Procedures don't include the Adverse Media SLAs.
 

 ● GCMT noted that the SLAs for the Adverse Media review of alerts are the same as the SLAs for the PEP review of alerts (see policy requirement above), however the SLAs for Adverse Media are not documented.
 

 ● There are no documented SLAs for Adverse Media & PEP Square Secure Profile (SSP) alerts.
 

 FOUNDATIONAL COMPLIANCE ENGINEERING
 Regulatory Requirement:
 Anti-Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism Guidelines for the Financial Sector (Revised 23 June 2021)
 10.3.3. Firms should have effective screening systems appropriate to the nature, size and risk of their business. Screening new and existing customers and payments against the relevant and up to date EU and UN lists helps ensure that Firms will not breach the sanctions regulations. Customer screening should take place at the time of customer take-on and at regular intervals thereafter. 
 

 Testing Results:
 A sample of 100 accounts screened in Q2 2024 was reviewed and GCMT identified that the 1st rescreen for Sanctions occurred after 30 days SLA for 6 accounts. GCMT raised this issue via Jira ticket CAER-306. GCMT noted that the new SLA for rescreening is dailiy that was udpated in the Global Sanctions documented on 30 August 2024.
 

 The Foundational Compliance Engineering function noted that the missed rescreens were due to an issue related to the third party vendor (i.e., LexisNexis), therefore a mechanism control will be implemented to ensure that alerts are created if this issue occurs. The Jira ticket FCFT-3056 was raised to address this issue and this will apply to updated daily rescreen process.

Issue Description:
Requirement:

Issue Title: Cash App's Military Lending Act oral disclosure does not describe the Retro and SUP payment obligations.
Issue Description:
Military Lending Act Requirement: 
 

 32 CFR 232.6 Mandatory loan disclosures
 32 CFR 232.6(d)(2)(i) In general. The creditor also shall orally provide the information required by paragraphs (a)(1) and (3) of this section.
  (a)(3) Required information. With respect to any extension of consumer credit (including any consumer credit originated or extended through the internet) to a covered borrower, a creditor shall provide to the covered borrower the following information before or at the time the borrower becomes obligated on the transaction or establishes an account for the consumer credit:
  (1) A statement of the MAPR applicable to the extension of consumer credit;
  (3) A clear description of the payment obligation of the covered borrower, as applicable. A payment schedule (in the case of closed-end credit) or account-opening disclosure (in the case of open-end credit) provided pursuant to paragraph (a)(2) of this section satisfies this requirement.
 

 Department of Defense Interpretive Rule-
  - https://www.federalregister.gov/d/2016-20486/p-37: A creditor may also orally provide a clear description of the payment obligation of the covered borrower by providing a general description of how the payment obligation is calculated or a description of what the borrower's payment obligation would be based on an estimate of the amount the borrower may borrow. 
  - https://www.federalregister.gov/d/2016-20486/p-38: A generic oral description of the payment obligation may be provided, even though the disclosure is the same for borrowers with a variety of consumer credit transactions or accounts.
 

 Issue Description:
 The MAPR statement oral disclosure is available through the toll-free number, (855) 949-7782. The oral disclosure does not describe the Retro loan term of up to 4 weeks and the SUP loan term of up to 6 weeks. However, the disclosure provided appears to reflect the Borrow loan term of 26-102 days.

 Refer to the action plan for supporting documentation.
 

 This management action plan is complete.

Issue Title: SCRA Policy was not aligned with SCRA refunding process

Any excess funds due to the Customer must only be:
 1. Applied as a credit to the loan’s linked bank account. If there is no linked bank account, or the account is not available to the Customer, a new linked bank account is permitted to be attached to the loan by the Customer following existing Bank procedures; or

Issue Title: The 314(a) record search process should be uplifted to ensure the completeness of record search in accordance with the regulatory requirements.
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement: 31 CFR 1010.520(b)(3) Obligations of a financial institution receiving an information request —Record search.  
 

 Upon receiving an information request from FinCEN under this section, a financial institution shall expeditiously search its records to determine whether it maintains or has maintained any account for, or has engaged in any transaction with, each individual, entity, or organization named in FinCEN's request. Except as otherwise provided in the information request, a financial institution shall only be required to search its records for:
 (A) Any current account maintained for a named suspect;
 (B) Any account maintained for a named suspect during the preceding twelve months; and
 (C) Any transaction, as defined by § 1010.505(d), conducted by or on behalf of a named suspect, or any transmittal of funds conducted in which a named suspect was either the transmittor or the recipient, during the preceding six months that is required under law or regulation to be recorded by the financial institution or is recorded and maintained electronically by the institution.
 Issue Description: GCMT testing concludes the current design of the 314(a) record search does not meet the aforementioned regulatory requirements based on the following results: 
 The program does not search against any Cash for Business (C4B) accounts for any entity or organizational level identifiable information which leads to an incomplete record search. 
 Background - During the initial implementation of the 314(a) program in March 2022, the program was designed to exclude C4B accounts with the exception of Bitcoin enabled accounts. The program obtained FinCEN’s out of scope approval under the condition that the excluded accounts were only serviced to receive payments related to goods and services, analogous to Square point of sale (POS) activity, and do not constitute money transmission. 
 However, based on the C4B account activities in 2024, the exclusion condition is no longer applicable as it allows money transmission (e.g. Check Deposit, Cash App Pay, etc.). Given the development of products and services, Cash Compliance should continue to monitor the exclusion conditions to ensure compliance with the regulations. 
 

 The program does not include any transactional records that might be subject to the requirement. 
 Per discussion with Cash Legal and Compliance in July 2024, GCMT confirmed there was no formal analysis performed to review all transaction types transmitted at Cash App in order to determine their applicability to the 314(a) record search against transactional records. 
 In 2022, while certain types of transactions (i.e. P2P transfers from an external debit card to third parties, etc.) were identified to be subject to record search, this design logic was not implemented in the code and therefore these transaction types are not being searched since the program's implementation.
 

 For periods with incomplete record searches identified, there was no remediation assessment performed to determine whether a retroactive search should be conducted. 
 In early 2024, Legal and Compliance Operations became aware that the record search logic used since inception from 2022 was not sufficient. The screening included only accounts that had transactional activity in the last 12 months, while the regulatory requirement is to search any ‘current account maintained’ and ‘closed accounts maintained during the preceding twelve months’. 
 The logic was updated to meet the regulatory requirement in March, 2024. However, no remediation was performed for the out of compliance period from 2022- March 2024. 
 GCMT noted that FinCEN FAQs related to the 314(a) Process issued to network participants, states “if a financial institution fails to perform or complete a search on one or more 314(a) requests received in the previous 12 months, it must immediately notify the 314 Program Office and perform a retroactive search of its records”. 
 

 GCMT suggests that the business should give consideration to perform a remediation assessment to showcase the risk-based decision of whether to conduct a retrospective search, for the time periods where the record search was deemed incomplete and the program was out of compliance. 
 

 The condition approved for excluding unverified customers from 314(a) is not being periodically re-assessed. 
 The program obtained FinCEN’s out of scope approval under the condition that Tier 1 Cash App accounts are subject to significantly lower transaction limits, are not participants in a prepaid program, and make up a very small percentage of Block’s SAR filings. Per discussion with Cash App Legal and Compliance, there was no continuing assessment established since inception to confirm the out of scope condition for Tier 1 accounts.

Issue Title: Unmatched customer Social Security Numbers (SSN) identified in the comparison flow are not investigated in accordance with the 31 CFR 1020.220 - Customer identification program (CIP) requirements
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement: 31 CFR 1020.220 - Customer identification program (CIP) requirements for banks.
 (a)(3)(i)(D) At a minimum, the CIP record must include - a description of the resolution of any substantive discrepancy discovered when verifying the identifying information obtained.
 (a)(2)The CIP procedures must enable the bank to form a reasonable belief that it knows the true identity of each customer.
 31 CFR 1022.210(d)(1)(iv) - A money services business that is a provider or seller of prepaid access must establish procedures to verify the identity of a person who obtains prepaid access under a prepaid program and obtain identifying information concerning such a person, including name, date of birth, address, and identification number.
 

 Issue Description: This is an issue identified outside of the scope of this review.
 Cash App utilizes a SSN Comparison Flow to collect the 9-digit-SSN from verified customers who later attempted to order a Cash App Card or originate a Borrow loan. The bank CIP rules require discrepancies identified in key CIP data that are used to form a reasonable belief of the customer identities to be investigated and resolved as part of the CIP program. However, GCMT noted that for the following use case where the comparison flow could conclude an unmatched result, there is no resolution actions performed to investigate the discrepancies- 
 Customer has verified with last 4-digit SSN and the newly collected full 9-digit SSN is not the same as the full 9-digit SSN returned from vendors via prior eIDV flows. Cash App will retain the original vendor returned SSN on file and not change the customers IDV status. Customer is unblocked and no further investigation or other actions will be performed. 
 Based on analysis performed at launching, the unmatched rate was estimated to be 10%.

Issue Title: Records relied upon to make the Suspicious Activity Reporting (SAR) determination are not retained in accordance with the regulatory guidance.
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement: 31 CFR 1022.320(c) Retention of records. A money services business shall maintain a copy of any SAR filed and the original or business record equivalent of any supporting documentation for a period of five years from the date of filing the SAR. 
 Supporting documentation shall be identified as such and maintained by the money services business, and shall be deemed to have been filed with the SAR. A money services business shall make all supporting documentation available to FinCEN or any Federal, State, or local law enforcement agency, or any Federal regulatory authority that examines the money services business for compliance with the Bank Secrecy Act, or any State regulatory authority administering a State law that requires the money services business to comply with the Bank Secrecy Act or otherwise authorizes the State authority to ensure that the money services business complies with the Bank Secrecy Act.  
 FinCEN Guidance (FIN-2007-G003)-SAR Supporting Documentation. "Supporting documentation" refers to all documents or records that assisted in making the determination that certain activity required a SAR filing. 
 Issue Description: Cash App utilizes a variety of methods and tools in Unusual Activity Report (UAR) investigations to determine whether the alerted customer and/or transaction should result in a SAR filing to FinCEN. 
 Per review, GCMT noted that not all critical documents and records relied on to make the Suspicious Activity Reporting (SAR) determination are retained to meet the regulatory expectation. 
 For 60 recommended SAR cases reviewed, GCMT identified - 
 a. There is no retention of the results returned from the Cluster Query used to identify accounts first degree connected by SSN/Bank Account/Payment Card connections. 
 b. Transactions reviewed are retained only for cases with a SAR filed. The CSV file is not retained for cases investigated and resulted in a No-SAR decision. 
 c. Open Source Searches (i.e.Negative News, Web Presence, Criminal History) are not retained.
 As advised in the FFIEC BSA/AML Manual, maintaining records for the SAR decision-making process is critical to an effective suspicious activity monitoring program. 
 For investigations resulting in SAR filings, the Money Service Business is required to retain all supporting documentation for a period of five years from the date of filing. The institution is also required to provide the supporting documents and records to FinCEN or an appropriate law enforcement or federal banking agency upon Request. 
 For investigations resulting in a No-SAR decision, there is no regulatory requirement for the format of record retention. However, various FinCEN guidance and BSA/AML Exam Procedures had provided regulatory expectations on maintaining comprehensive records to ensure the decisions can be explained during examinations. As a result, Cash App may experience risks of not meeting regulatory expectations for record retention.

Issue Title: Some tools relied upon for investigating unusual activities and applying adverse actions are not effective.
Issue Description:
Procedure Requirement: The Cash App Transaction Monitoring and Suspicious Activity Reporting Denylist Procedure details Cash App’s criteria on when to close a customer relationship after a determination of involvement in harmful, fraudulent or illegal activities. The procedure described victims of Account Take Over (ATO) should be exempted from the Social Security Number (SSN) Denylist control.
 Issue Description: GCMT noted the Cluster Query used to identify connected accounts of an alerted account and the system control to prevent Social Security Number (SSN) denylist on victims of Account Take Over (ATO) are not effective. Specifically -  
 a. For 9 out of 18 cases where GCMT performed the Cluster Query re-run, the results returned significant variance from the case comments (i.e. 30 connected tokens returned during testing while investigation comment indicated 0 was returned). As a result, GCMT determined the query re-run is not reliable to re-create the records reviewed during investigation for the testing period. The Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) indicated the difference in records may arise from a Cluster Query update where the logic for SSN collection was modified and returned undesired results for the purpose of Unusual Activity Report (UAR) review.
 b. For 20 initial Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) determination cases reviewed, GCMT noted that for 1 (5%) case the guardrail control did not react to the NHB (No Hard Block) hashtag added to the account by the investigator, and failed to prevent the SSN Denylist from being implemented on an ATO victim. 
 There appears to be no monitoring controls with system/tool owners to ensure their proper development, update and maintenance for where key system/tool dependencies exist for the purpose of managing an effective UAR program.
