Issue Title: The Cash App Transactions Monitoring and Suspicious Activity Reporting Procedures need to be uplifted for the evaluation and disposition of unusual activity.
Issue Description:
Regulatory Guidance: FFIEC BSA/AML Manual - Managing Alerts. The institution should have policies, procedures, and processes in place for referring unusual activity from all areas of the bank or business lines to the personnel or department responsible for evaluating unusual activity. Within those procedures, management should establish a clear and defined escalation process from the point of initial detection to disposition of the investigation.
 FFIEC BSA/AML Manual - SAR Filing on Continuing Activity. Management is required to implement SAR escalation policy and procedures that address senior management and legal staff review, the criteria for when an analysis of the overall customer relationship is necessary, the criteria for whether, and if so,how to close an account, and criteria for when to notify law enforcement, if appropriate.
 

 Issue Description: Per review of 75 Transaction Monitoring Alerts and 60 recommended SAR cases, GCMT determined additional procedure and guidance are needed to address key investigation scope and methods in managing alerts and evaluating unusual activities.
 a. For Transaction Monitoring Alerts, the Cash App Transactions Monitoring procedure does not sufficiently describe the required lookback periods as determined by associated queues. GCMT noted the alerts are generated from 23 rule-based queues which at least 5 queues (i.e. Wires, RCD, etc.) require their specific lookback/frequency periods that differ from the standard review period of 30 days prior to the alert generation date. While the Transaction Monitoring Procedure described the analyst should also review timeframes outside of the 30 day window as determined by associated queues, it failed to disclose any details on such queues and their required lookback period or method respectively. The lack of relevant guidance could lead to undesired and inconsistent investigation results. For 1 out of 5 alerts reviewed from the Remote Check Deposit queue, GCMT noted the analyst closed the alert without performing any investigation due to incorrectly assigning the lookback period.  
 b. For recommended SAR Investigations, GCMT concluded that the investigation scope for continuing suspicious activity monitoring (90 day look-back) should be enhanced. For 90 day look-back reviews, the investigation scope is limited to hard-connected tokens only. The team described hard-connection as connected by SSN and Bank Account, and soft-connection for all other asset connection types (i.e. Payment Card, Email, Phone). However, GCMT noted that the definition of hard versus soft connection, as well as the the exclusion of reviewing soft-connected tokens are not documented in the Suspicious Activity Reporting Procedure for tracked management approval. 
 c. For Transaction Monitoring and SAR Denylisting, GCMT noted that there is no guidance on the exclusion of SSN Denylist requirements for sponsored/unsponsored minor accounts and Green Accounts. The Cash App Transaction Monitoring and Suspicious Activity Reporting Denylist Procedure stated that "all accounts added to the SAR form along with all accounts connected by SSN are denylisted. Deviations from the guidance should be noted in the investigator’s case notes”. However, for all SAR associated customer tokens under the aforementioned account types, GCMT noted there was no SSN Denylist action performed or documented comment to address the deviation from the procedures. While the business accepted the common practice of excluding such account types from the SSN Denylist requirement, the relevant procedure should be updated to accurately capture the expectation and if applicable, any compensating control(s) when the SSN Denylist action can not be applied (i.e. apply Instrument DL).

Issue Title: The Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) case documentation to support the disposition of a no filing decision during continuing activity monitoring (90 day look back) requires improvement
Issue Description:
Procedural Requirement: Cash App Suspicious Activity Reporting Procedure - No SAR Case Comments states - “in instances when the case review conducted results in a determination not to file a SAR, the Compliance Analyst is required to include documentation in Regulator Supporting the decision not to file a SAR."
 

 Issue Description: 
 For 9 out of the 22 (40%) of continuing activity cases reviewed with a No-SAR decision, the case documentation requires improvement, specifically GCMT identified that;  
 For 7 of 9 cases, the case comment concluded “The alerted Cash account and all associated Cash accounts were reviewed with no continuing activity found” which is insufficient in supporting the investigation actions performed to reach the SAR determination. The analyst failed to disclose any details on the exact accounts reviewed, the type of associations that they have with the SAR subjects under the look-back, as well as the source identifying such associations. 
 For 2 of 9 cases, the case comment failed to address the accurate reason for the No-SAR decision. The case concluded that “No SSN and Bank Accounts connection resulted from the cluster run. Therefore, no SAR 90 is required.” However, the actual reason for no further action needed was due to the Denylist status applied in prior SAR(s) which prevented any account activity during the 90-day lookback period. 
 For 1 of 9 cases, the analyst referred to suspicious activities identified during the lookback of the sampled case but failed to note the activities were reported in a separate SAR which resulted in inconsistent documentation in the non-SAR decision with the note details.

Issue Title: The case investigation and documentation to support the disposition of Sanctions Watchlist Alerts requires improvement.
Issue Description:
Regulatory Guidance: FFIEC BSA/AML Manual - Office of Foreign Assets Control. The institution’s policies, procedures, and processes should address how it will identify and review transactions and accounts for possible OFAC violations, whether conducted manually, through interdiction software, or a combination of both. 
 For screening purposes, the institution should clearly define its criteria for comparing names provided on the OFAC list with the names in its files or on transactions and for identifying transactions or accounts involving sanctioned countries. The policies, procedures, and processes should also address how it will determine whether an initial OFAC hit is a valid match or a false hit.
 Procedural Requirements: The Watchlist Analyst User Guide provided detailed requirements on how PII comparison should be performed to reach a false positive or potential match escalation for Sanctions Watchlist alerts.
 Issue Description: GCMT noted that 6 out of 60 (10%) Sanctions Watchlist cases reviewed did not meet the investigation requirements as defined in the Watchlist Screening Investigation User Guide. Specifically:  
 For 2 cases, GCMT noted inconsistent case dispositions of hits triggered on the same customer from the same Sanctions Watchlist without changes to the customer’s profile. GCMT observed that multiple alerts were generated in different screening queues on the same name hit against the same Watchlist (i.e. SDN List) without any changes in the customer’s PII. For both cases, the first alert was dispositioned incorrectly as a name match and resulted in the customer being added to Denylist erroneously; the second alert was worked correctly and the case was concluded as F+ given mismatch of other PIIs.  
 For 4 cases, GCMT noted that while the case conclusions were accurate, the cases consisted of other manual errors such as incorrect comments regarding L2 escalations, missed required messages regarding customer appeal and mistakenly escalated the case despite a F+ decision in L1 review.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Rating Justification:

Issue Action Plans: Sanctions specialists to receive relevant training to improve case review accuracy (1185588)
Closure Request Justification:
Sanctions Overview training was provided to COM Sanctions and QC/QA teams from 11/18/24 to 12/20/24. Training material included an overview of Sanctions, Block’s Global Sanctions Compliance Policy, General and Product-specific Red Flags, OFAC fines and penalties, and best practices.
 

 CashApp specific red flags reviewed include lack of transparency for securities transactions and cross-border relationships for CashApp Invest, out of pattern regions and gifted securities for P2P transactions, and obfuscated network transfers for bitcoin transactions. Example case studies of companies charged with OFAC violations and settlements (Binance, Payoneer, and multiple virtual currency companies) were also discussed.
 

 Per review of the attendance records, relevant COM Sanctions and QC/QA team members completed the training. 
 

 Sanctions Queue-Specific training was provided to COM Sanctions from 11/18/24 to 12/29/24. Training material included OFAC internal processes and investigation steps, workflow review, and actions to take for positive matches. 
 The workflow review section specifically goes over the Sanctions Alert Decision Matrix to determine if the case can be cleared as a false positive or escalated for an additional review. This Matrix defines the required review elements for Block and Watchlist types including Name, Date of Birth, Citizenship, Country of Birth, Nationality, and Location (Country). Specialists are instructed to add comments when clearing the case that indicate the reason for clearing, as well as providing information on what resource was used to determine the False Positive.
 

 Per review of the attendance records, relevant COM Sanctions team members completed the training. 
 

 This management action plan is complete. Refer to attachments in Action ID - 1185588 for supporting documentation reviewed.

Issue Title: Out of SLA tracking for US Sanctions Watchlist Screening requires improvement.
Issue Description:
Procedural Requirement: 
 Watchlist Screening Investigation User Guide. Prior to May 2024, the internal SLA requirement was 24 hour SLA for Initial Screening Queues and 30 day SLA for Rescreen Queues. Starting May 2024, the SLA for both initial and rescreening became 24 hours from case creation. 
 

 Finding Description: For 13 out of 60 (22%) US Sanctions Watchlist alerts reviewed, the cases failed to meet their internal SLA of 24 hours for initial screening for the period of November 2023 to March 2024. Among the exceptions, GCMT noted 12 initial screenings from the Green queues were closed between 5 to 43 days from alert creation, and 1 initial screening from the Cash queue closed on day 7. The Sanctions and OFAC Investigation team indicated that the out of SLA cases are monitored through Tableau reports by management. 
 However, GCMT noted that the Tableau report does not accurately reflect the within 24 hours from case creation SLAs for Watchlist alerts as all queues are tracked against 31 days from case creation.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Q3 2024 AML/CFT and OFAC Sanctions (Subpart A) (1029256)
Issue Rating Justification:
Present a low risk to the operations of Block, or represents a limited weakness in Block’s processes or controls. . Corrective action to address the Issue(s) identified is required.
 

 May be used when (i) a process / control is designed appropriately and operating effectively in practice but not described accurately in program documentation (put otherwise, "pure documentation Issues") or (ii) a "one-off exception" is identified that does not appear indicative of a broken process / control.
Issue Action Plans: Sanctions and CAX will develop accurate SLA tracking for alerts (1185593)
Closure Request Justification:
Action ID - 1185593
 A requirements document states that the report is built to enable daily monitoring of a 24 business hour from case creation SLA for Sanctions Watchlist alerts. Specifically, the alert data in the Tableau report is refreshed every 6 hours and excludes weekends and Block-wide holidays to ensure the SLA period is accurate and reflects the operational working hours. Data inputs to the report include Cash App initial and rescreening of sanctions alerts.
 

 Review of the Tableau Report (live link & screenshot) indicates that the data inputs (queues) are appropriate. Additionally, the report is easily filterable, allowing for quick identification of cases that exceed the 24-hour SLA and those in the backlog. 
 

 Out of SLA and backlogged alerts are reviewed daily and tracked and managed in Cash App Customer Onboarding & Management’s (COM) existing tracker. The tracker highlights significant backlogs, remediation actions (if applicable), DRI responsible for remediation, and estimated remediation date.
 

 This management action plan is complete. Refer to attachments in Action ID - 1185593 for supporting documentation reviewed.

Issue Title: Training of frontline staff to identify and report potential systemic issues
Issue Description:
Afterpay ANZ has an obligation to prevent, identify and manage systemic issues and significant dealings that have the potential to impact a single class, or classes of customers and that issue has the potential to cause consumer harm. 
 

 Customer complaints can be a key risk indicator for systemic issues within a financial firm. To identify systemic issues, financial firms must encourage and enable staff to escalate any possible systemic issues they encounter in individual complaints.
 

 Systemic issues and Significant Dealings may have serious contraventions reportable to regulatory bodies across Australia and New Zealand or external Dispute Resolution or Regulatory Bodies identifying and issuing potential systemic investigations.
 

 Examples of systemic issues across ANZ include:
 - Serious contravention of any law
 - Unconscionable conduct
 - Misleading or deceptive conduct
 - Contraventions of credit activity
 - Poor disclosure or communication processes
 - Flaws in the design of financial products or other financial services; or 
 - Breaches of the terms and conditions or inaccurate interpretation of standard terms and conditions.
 

 A potential weakness is the training of front line staff to identify and report.

Issue Title: GCMT Q3 2024 - Square Banking - Credit Card: Servicing (Ref. #1)
Issue Description:
GCMT notes payment related inconsistencies in the terminology, or lack of defined terms in documentation provided to the customer that could lead to a fundamental misunderstanding of how the Credit Card product functions. 
 There are three terms and descriptions used in customer communication to describe the same feature. The "repayment rate" feature within the loan agreement Cardholder Terms for the credit card product describes debiting loan payments by pulling directly from customer sales in order to meet the minimum payment. Upon reviewing statements the term 'repayment rate’ is not used at all and instead appears to be replaced with the description 'Payment from your daily sales on Square' in the payments section and then as the term ‘hold rate’ in the definitions section of the legal terms. 
 

 There is no definition within the account statement that helps the customer understand how their available balance is calculated. The current method of calculation includes pending charges that do not appear on the customer's statement which does not allow the customer to easily verify the accuracy of their available credit. While pending charges are often left off of customer statements as industry standard, a definition of how the available balance is calculated would increase transparency and reduce any potential customer harm.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: Lack of Sanctions Real-Time Interdiction for P2P Fiat Payments
Issue Description:
Cash App currently lacks in-flight, also known as real-time, sanctions interdiction of its Peer-to-Peer (“P2P”) Fiat payments. When Cash App deployed sanctions screening of P2P Fiat payments in October 2018, the process was designed to subject P2P Fiat payments’ ‘Initiator Notes’ field to post-transactional sanctions screening against an internal ‘sanctions keywords’ list primarily made up terms related to jurisdictions subject to comprehensive sanctions by OFAC. If the Initiator Notes field contains a word that matches a word on the sanctions keywords list, an alert/case will generate for review by Cash App Sanctions Compliance personnel in Regulator, a case management system/platform. Once the alert/case is created, Cash App’s Sanctions team can manually freeze the customer’s stored balance, begin an investigation, and reach out to customers to gather additional information in an attempt to resolve the open investigation. The post-transactional nature of this screening poses elevated risk from a sanctions regulatory perspective as the current state allows the payment and associated funds to credit the intended recipient’s account and be available for withdrawal prior to the creation of the associated sanctions keyword alert/case, which is usually created and reviewed 24 hours after the payment has been processed. 
 

 This gap was formally raised by the Global Sanctions Team in July 2021 with real-world examples to illustrate the elevated risk of potential sanctions violations given the delayed nature of sanctions screening of P2P fiat transactions. 
 

 To ensure compliance with regulatory requirements, ensure adherence to Block policy, improve monitoring, and provide a smoother customer experience, Cash App replace the existing screening process with one where P2P Fiat payments’ Initiator Notes fields will be screened in real time with interdiction that will enable a review of the payment by sanctions compliance personnel before the payment is completed.
RCA Description: Process: Post-transaction sanctions screening was deemed sufficient by compliance at the time Cash App designed the process to subject P2P fiat payments’ 'Initiator Notes' field to sanctions screening against an internal sanctions keywords list.
Issue Rating Justification: The lack of real-time/in-flight sanctions interdiction controls for Cash App P2P Fiat payments increases the risk of potential violations of OFAC sanctions regulations, which could result in regulatory fines and penalties as well as reputational consequences.
Issue Action Plans:
Action Plan 1: Engineering Interdiction Requirements Development (1198106)
 Action Plan 2: Engineering Implementation- Interdiction of Otherwise Successful P2P Fiat Payments (1198111)
 Action Plan 3: Compliance Operational Readiness (1198764)
 Action Plan 4: Sustainability- Post-Validation Testing (1198777)
Closure Request Justification: Supporting documents uploaded, reviewed and approved on 1/31/2025.

Issue Title: SIL - Sales and Account Management do not have a process for flagging Tier 3 Complaints.
Issue Description:
Program Requirement; 
 As part of the Block Complaints Program, all business units must adhere to the global complaint governance requirement including the flagging of complaints. 
 

 Issue Description: 
 The “Global Complaints - Escalation Process for AMs” states that for Tier 1 and Tier 2 complaints, SAM fills out their respective complaints forms. There is no requirement for this form to be completed for Tier 1 complaints. This omission is in contradiction to the Global Complaints Program requirements for all business units to flag complaints.  
 

 GCMT tested a random sample of 100 SIL Sales and Account Management calls from Ireland, France and Spain for the period 1st April 2024 to 1st July 2024 to determine if a Seller expressed dissatisfaction and if so, if the complaint was flagged appropriately on Salesforce per the requirements of the Standard Operating Procedure. 
 

 Sales
 Ireland: GCMT identified 1 case from 20 calls where a seller expressed dissatisfaction and a complaint was not raised.
 Account Management
 Ireland: GCMT identified 2 cases from 20 calls where a seller expressed dissatisfaction and a complaint was not raised (100% exception rate)
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: This issue has been rated as moderate as it is a regulatory expectation to have a process for documenting expressions of dissatisfaction for all customers, and this includes tier 3 complaints.
Issue Action Plans: The ‘Complaints Escalation Process’ and ‘How to File a Complaint’ documentation will be updated with the required elements and socialized with SAM representatives. (1189547)

Issue Title: SEL - Square Customer Service practices for adherence to complaints Standard Operating Procedures can be improved to increase the effectiveness of complaints identification/flagging, handling and resolution.
Issue Description:
GCMT tested Customer Success compliance with Standard Operating Procedures for the handling and management of Tier 1, Tier 2, Tier 3 and Tier 3 - Escalated complaints. 
 

 A random sample of 40 complaints were tested for the period 1st April 2024 to 1st July 2024. The below results indicate areas where an effectiveness rate of less than 85% was observed. 
 

 1 - Adequacy of Complaint Responses and Resolutions – Customer Service Advocate 
 GCMT identified a 76% effectiveness rate consisting of a 24% exception rate where the complaint was only partially resolved (8%) or not resolved in full (16%).
 

 2 - Complaint Reporting Fields – Customer Service Advocate 
 GCMT identified a 76% effectiveness rate consisting of a 24% exception was identified where the CSA did not include all required and/or correct details in the ‘Flag a Complaint Form’ when entering a complaint on behalf of the customer. 
 

 3 - Complaint Reporting Fields – Customer Service Advocate 
 GCMT identified a 82% effectiveness rate consisting of a 18% exception rate where the CSA selected the incorrect Tier for the Complaint.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: The risk of this becoming a material issue is low. Areas of non compliance with Standard Operating Procedures were not less than 76%.
Issue Action Plans: In line with previous findings, all CSAs, internal and BPO, will redo the complaints training to be better equipped to handle complaints and drive down human error rates. (1189552)
Closure Request Justification: GCMT reviewed evidence which shows BPO and CSA have completed the Complaints Management training again.

Issue Title: TSV File Manual Upload Process - Control and Process Update
Issue Description:
During control testing, CTT identified that the control as designed does not completely capture the entirety of the risks scope, as the control does not cover cases not being uploaded completely and accurately. The current process no longer meets the operational needs or technological standards required for optimal performance. Banking Investigation Leads escalate TSV file errors to Marqeta for rectification and Marqeta in turn will correct the identified errors and provide a revised TSV file in the designated Slack channel upon review. The manual nature of this process does not adequately mitigate the intended risk and can increase the likelihood of inefficiencies and increased error rates potentially leading to operational disruptions. It is recommended that the control be redesigned and the risk description be updated to capture the entire scope of exposure. The revised control should include validation separate from the process performer to ensure segregation of duties and also include a practical review cadence to ensure the frequency of review matches the associated risk.
 

 Automation of the process (uploading of the TSV File) will minimize the risk completely. Banking Investigations advised that there was previously an open issue to have this automated, CTT recommends following up with the ticket to retrieve updated status and push the process forward.
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: An issue in interpretation and execution of CCO policy which has material impact on the process or risk assessment.
Issue Action Plans: TSV File Manual Upload Process - Control and Process Update (1192832)
Closure Request Justification:
QA completed by Corey Griggs on 11/20/24, validation executed, issue is ready to proceed with closure process.
 

 IM Lead reviewed evidence/artifacts for closure on 11/21/24.

Issue Title: PII Present in Banking Investigations Documentation
Issue Description: During Control Testing, CTT identified that Banking Investigations documentation contained customer PII in an unsecured location. Further review into the reason for this shows that it is due to the fact that the ACH Recall Requests are not automatically or systemically loaded into a system of record. The cases are worked off of a Google Sheet and housed in a Google Drive. Reviewing the Google Sheet shows that there are Full Names, Addresses, and Account numbers for Customers. This is a shared sheet with Sutton where the banking partner (Sutton) inputs where ACH return requests are added. This poses a risk to customers as their PII is living in an unsecured area. This data should be protected, redacted, and/or there should be controls in place to ensure that the data is safeguarded against people that should not have business purposes to access the data. One of the ways this can be done is to also automate the intake of this case type from the partner bank, Sutton, so that the information is safeguarded in a system of record where only those needing to access cases will have this information.
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure resulting in an impact to regulatory obligations
Issue Action Plans: PII Present in Banking Investigations Documentation (1211349)
Closure Request Justification: Supporting evidence reviewed by Issue Management Lead. Issue was reviewed and approved for closure on 11/20/24.

Issue Title: Lack of Ongoing Screening and Case Creation for U.S. Hardware Installment Loan (HWI) Applicants
Issue Description:
Global Sanctions Compliance (GSC) identified a screening gap where U.S. Hardware Installment Loan (HWI) applicants/loan customers are not being screened on a daily basis or generating rescreening alerts for review by sanctions compliance personnel if the name on the HWI loan application differs from the payments profile seller subjected to daily screening. Square Product Compliance initiated a retroactive Compliance Requirement Document (CRD) for U.S. HWI in April 2024. Through GSC's review, it was identified that the name on the HWI application is not limited to the main seller or activator associated with the seller's payments profile. U.S. HWI was designed so that customers can input an HWI applicant name that differs from the customer's/seller's payment profiles as the applications use a free-text field. Sellers/activators are subjected to sanctions screening via product intent #5 (platform US full_verification), which screens on a daily cadence and generates a case for review in Regulator by Square Sanctions Operations if a potential match is identified through LexisNexis, Block's main sanctions screening tool. Due to the design of the U.S. HWI product, HWI loan applicants whose names differ from the main seller's/activator's profile are only screened against product intent #47, which screens the applicant prior to issuing the loan. Product intent #47 is not set up to create cases if a potential match is identified; as such, an HWI loan application is declined if a potential match is identified. Additionally, product intent #47 is set up to rescreen every 30 days, but because it is not set up to create cases, the HWI applicants are rescreened but Square Sanctions Operations is not notified of potential matches as cases are not created. Product Intent #47 was initially set up to not create cases under the belief that HWI applicants would be screened through product intent #5; however, this belief was mistaken given the design of U.S. HWI that allows for parties other than the main seller/activator to be a loan applicant. Furthermore, the original 30 day rescreen cadence is inconsistent with daily rescreening requirements established in 2024. It was also discovered that HWI applicants' tokens are not set up like merchant or unit tokens but are instead tokens associated with loan applications. To generate cases with sufficient information in Regulator for agents (i.e., sanctions compliance analysts) to review, the engineering teams will need to map C-tokens back to their corresponding merchant/unit tokens and display them on the alert pages.
 

 Lack of ongoing screening and case generation of U.S. HWI applicants whose names differ from the name on the HWI loan application differs from the payments profile seller subjected to daily screening is inconsistent industry best practices to screen customers daily and review potential match cases, and it can also lead to Square inadvertently maintaining an HWI for a sanctioned person. While the risk of a U.S. HWI applicant being a sanctioned party is low given that it is a U.S. product, this gap is a sanctions regulatory gap and also inconsistent with Block's practice to screen customers daily. As such, the risk is Moderate. 
 

 To remediate this issue, Square will need to explore either (1) limiting the names on HWI applications to the payments main seller or activator (which is the case for HWI outside the U.S.) or (2) implement daily screening with case generation through the U.S. HWI product intent.
RCA Description:
Process Design: The U.S. HWI product was designed so that customers can input an HWI applicant name that differs from the customer's/seller's payment profile as the applications use a free-text field. Additionally, the product intent designed to screen U.S. HWI applicants (#47) was not created to generate cases when applicants are screened initially or rescreened at the original 30-day rescreen cadence under the incorrect belief that the applicants would be covered by product intent #5. Square’s current overall change management process involves Product Requirements Documents (“PRD”) and Compliance Requirements Document (“CRD”) processes as well as Risk and Compliance Council meetings. Product intent creations are currently discussed through these change management processes, which were not as mature in 2017 when US HWI was launched. 
 

 Furthermore, the 30-day screening cadence is inconsistent with daily rescreening requirements established for Square customers in 2024, which is due to a change in Block policy and was not necessarily a gap when the product intent was created.
Issue Rating Justification: This issue poses a moderate risk as the lack of ongoing screening and case creation for U.S. HWI applicants are inconsistent with industry regulatory expectations and Block’s existing customer sanctions screening practices and as it may lead to Square inadvertently maintaining a customer who is a sanctioned party, which necessitates a corrective action plan with priority. A High rating is not recommended as these HWI applicants are subjected to initial screening, which declines the application if a potential match is generated. Additionally, the identified gap only impacts the U.S. HWI, which poses lower risk from a sanctions perspective as the likelihood of a U.S. applicant being a sanctioned person is low.
Issue Action Plans:
Assess Association of U.S. HWI Loans with SFS or Banking Partners (1198771)
 Implement Temporary Batch Rescreen of Existing Non-Seller U.S. HWI Applicants (1277973)
 Create File and Process to Generate File for Temporary Batch Rescreen (1277977)
 Review Initial Temporary Batch U.S. HWI Applicants Rescreen Results (1278620)
 Launch product feature preventing US-Based merchants from editing name fields on HWI loan applications (1306342)
 Sustainability—Demonstrate Ongoing Compliance (1306344)
Closure Request Justification:
All supporting evidence for action plans uploaded. 
 

 08/28/2025 - all supporting evidence for actions reviewed and approved. Overall issue approved for closure.

 escalated by the Attorney General (AG), State (e.g.,
 California Department of Financial Protection and
 Innovation) or Executive Escalation complaints, while
 the complaints were being triaged and validated.
 

 This is due to a lack of clarity in ownership over the
 responsibility to apply the collections exclusion, as the
 Compliance team is currently handling AG, State and
 Executive Escalation complaints and transitioning the
 responsibility to the External Dispute Resolution
 (“EDR”) team.
 

 Risk
 This may violate the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act
 (FDCPA) and Regulation F, exposing the organization
 to significant legal risks, including potential
 enforcement actions by the Consumer Financial
 Protection Bureau (CFPB). See applicable regulatory
 requirements, internal policies and procedures in
 Appendix I.
 

 Mitigating Circumstances
 The overall volume of AG, State and Executive
 Escalation complaints is low (# of Complaints in Q2’24:
 AG - 7, State - 3, Executive Escalation - 11). This limited
 scope reduces the potential impact of the issue on the
 broader collection activities.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans:
EDR Refresher (1199434)
 Management transition handling to EDR team (1199438)
 2023 AG, State and Executive Complaints Lookback (1200081)
Closure Request Justification: Both actions have been closed out.

Issue Title: Inadequate Oversight for Goodwill Adjustments and Refund Processing
Issue Description:
The Admin portal used for processing Afterpay refunds
 and goodwill adjustments is not configured to prevent
 complaints agents from processing amounts
 exceeding their delegated authority. Currently, the
 system allows agents to issue refunds up to the full
 value of an order without requiring additional review or
 approval.
 

 Zendesk is used to manually log goodwill amounts
 processed by agents; however, there is no monitoring
 to reconcile refunds processed in the Admin portal
 with what’s entered in Zendesk.
 

 Management relies on the "Resolution Cost
 Monitoring" Tableau report as a detective control to
 retrospectively review larger amounts and ensure they
 are within the agent's delegated authority or have
 received proper approval. However, this control is not
 designed effectively as it depends on agents accurately
 logging goodwill amounts into Zendesk, and gaps have
 been identified in Observation #3, where some agents
 failed to consistently log resolution costs.
 

 Risk
 ● Operational: Complaints agents may process
 refunds exceeding the necessary amount.
 ● Financial: This exposes the company to financial
 losses.
 ● Fraud: There is heightened risk of fraud that the
 agent or others with access to the Admin portal 
 could issue unauthorized payouts to
 complainants who may be friends or family
 members.
 

 Mitigating Circumstances
 1. The Admin portal is configured to prevent
 issuance of goodwill adjustment or refunds that
 exceed the order amount. In addition, goodwill
 adjustments or refunds cannot be processed for
 orders greater than 120 days.
 2. The ability to issue refunds or goodwill
 adjustments as part of complaint resolution is
 limited to 20 employees within the Afterpay
 Complaints Program.
 3. The total amount of actual refunds or goodwill
 adjustments issued as part of complaint
 resolution for 2024 year-to-date is $36K.
 4. The Afterpay Operations QA Framework
 performs sample-based review to identify
 refunds and goodwill adjustments that exceed
 the agent's delegated authority and
 non-adherence to the standard operating
 procedure.
 5. While a control design gap was identified, IA did
 not find any instances of fraud or unauthorized
 payments during the audit that could result in
 consumer harm, unfair practices, or abuse (e.g.,
 customers receiving more or less than they
 should).
Interested Parties:
Corey Chamberlain
 Mario Castillo
 Miles Baker
Issue Rating Justification: N/A

Due to reorgs and various people on leave, there was not an additional approval on the closure memo. Messages are out to various parties, but the only people who have oversight of AP QA data are ooo.
Issue Action Plans:
Leadership SOP update (1200084)

Issue Title: Incomplete and Inaccurate Complaint Log Entries
Issue Description:
When a complaint is identified and / or resolved, there
 are a number of fields that need to be filled out in the
 complaint log. However, IA noted missing or
 inappropriate values for fields like “Tier”, “Product Type,”
 “Root Cause,” “Resulting Action,” and “Legal Tag.”
 “Resolution Cost” was not consistently logged to
 account for various types of refunds/fees waived
 incurred to resolve a complaint.
 

 This is due to:
 1. QA efforts have primarily focused on complaint
 acknowledgment SLAs as they are one of the
 Global Complaints Policy requirements and
 have direct impact on consumer experience.
 Inaccurate or incomplete data entries have
 been treated as opportunities for process
 improvement to better align with a particular
 SOP, rather than critical compliance issues.
 2. Most of the inaccurate or incomplete data
 entries are attributed to new data elements
 (e.g., Product Type, Root Cause, Resulting
 Action, Legal Tag) or updated definition (e.g.,
 Tier) introduced as part of Afterpay alignment to
 the Block Global Complaint Policy and Program
 effective April 1, 2024. The process for updating
 these fields is highly manual and subjective,
 relying on individual judgment.
 3. The refund SOP currently still has the legacy
 process which states to only update the field if
 the complaint agent working the ticket has
 executed the refund vs. disputes/ATO teams
 execute the refund. The team is in the process of
 updating the SOP to clarify what scenarios
 require updating the amount in the resolution
 cost.
 

 Risk
 Inaccurate or incomplete data entries in complaint logs
 undermine the effectiveness of complaint tracking and
 reporting, and more importantly, hinder effective root
 cause analysis, making it difficult to identify and
 address underlying issues.
 ● Certain data elements such as “Legal Tag” are
 introduced to raise legal issues involving potential
 consumer harm from unfair treatment or
 discrimination, fraud/ID theft, or other regulatory
 compliance issues.
 ● Inconsistent use of the “Resolution Cost” field could
 lead to an understatement of the true costs associated
 with resolving complaints.
 See applicable internal policies and procedures in
 Appendix I.
 

 Mitigating Circumstance
 The exception rate for inaccurate or incomplete data
 entries is low. The errors identified were primarily
 technical in nature and did not lead to substantive
 consumer harm. Management noted a similar
 observation in their April’ 2024 reporting pack and
 communicated that the reporting of these required
 data elements and the insights derived from them will
 evolve. Ongoing review and calibration with team
 leaders and complaint servicing staff are in place to
 refine SOPs.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans:
System Automation Capabilities Review (1200093)
 Compliance Breaches Category Evaluation (1200096)

Issue Title: Square Checking Reconciliation Issue
Issue Description: Square Checking has a check deposit feature that launched in July 2021. Since June 2023, check deposits made through this feature are sent to an account with Wells Fargo using the shared Gambit mobile check deposit platform, and are supposed to be pushed to Sutton Bank for our SQ Checking Customers. From July 2021 to June 2023, funds were deposited into a Cash owned Wells Fargo account (x2076), and not the Square account (x4580), using the predecessor platform to Gambit, Checkmate. From the launch of the feature in 2021, across the two Wells Fargo accounts, approximately $147M in funds from Square Checking mobile check deposits have not been moved to Sutton Bank where Square Checking accounts are held; customers were credited, but the underlying funds were not moved. Approximately $99m of SQ Checking Deposits are in the Cash (x2076) Wells Fargo account, and can be moved to Sutton once discussed with Sutton. However, we expected Sutton to be short by approximately this amount with some variation for funds in transit, but they are not showing this discrepancy. However, approximately $44 million is not accounted for in the Wells Fargo account (x4580). While a plan is in place to move the missing funds to Sutton, this does not solve the issue on a go forward basis, and does not address the discrepancy from reoccurring, or identify why funds are missing. Full investigation of the root cause is needed before contacting Sutton, and transferring the funds.
RCA Description: Initial root cause of this issue was a failure to implement automated reconciliation and rebalancing of funds deposited through the Square Checking mobile check deposit feature when it was launched and associated lack of procedures and oversight to ensure this was taking place.
Issue Rating Justification: This issue is rated high on the basis that a substantial amount in customer funds have not been being transferred to the correct account, creating potential for loss, reputational harm and regulatory scrutiny.
Issue Progress Updates:
Root cause investigation is occurring and daily updates are placed in the following slack channel:
 inc-square-3747-discrepancy-between-virtual-square-checking-and-sutton-phys
Issue Action Plans:
Go-Forward Rebalancing Enablement (1221862)
 Square Checking Account Backfill (1242335)
 Long-Term Controls (1242338)
Closure Request Justification: Submitting for closure as all associated actions have been completed.

Issue Title: Lack of UK Frozen Asset Account and Process Documentation
Issue Description:
The UK Compliance Lead identified an issue where Squareup Europe Ltd does not have a formal process in place to handle the freezing of assets identified as prohibited by UK sanctions regulations. While Squareup Europe Ltd can currently freeze funds belonging to a sanctioned person, the process with a financial institution to move the frozen funds to a segregated account is not yet finalized or documented in a policy or procedure. Squareup Europe Ltd is expected to freeze assets belonging to sanctioned persons per UK regulatory requirements.
 

 The issue was originally identified in 2022 when Global Sanctions discovered that Squareup Europe Ltd was holding funds linked to Russia that were potentially in scope of UK sanctions requiring asset freezing. The initiative to formalize the asset freezing process was left unfinished when Global Sanctions determined that the aforementioned held funds were not in scope of UK sanctions freezing requirements.
 

 The root cause of this issue is a process design weakness, as a formal process was not built into the sanctions compliance program. This oversight occurred primarily due to the lack of any incidents requiring asset freezing per UK sanctions regulations to date.
 

 Remediation of this issue consists of development of a process and creation of frozen asset accounts under UK Sanctions Regulations.
RCA Description: Process Design Weakness: The root cause of this issue is a delay in account creation and weakness in process design as a formal procedure was not integrated into the sanctions compliance program. This oversight primarily resulted from the absence of any incidents necessitating asset freezing under UK sanctions regulations to date.
Issue Rating Justification: Currently, there are no UK frozen assets in the possession of Squareup Europe Ltd (Block, Inc.), and this situation is rated as low risk. This indicates minimal immediate risk, but addressing the process design weakness is essential for future compliance with UK sanctions regulations.
Issue Progress Updates: Met with Fernando Barroso on November 13, 2024 to review requirements for a UK frozen assets account. He informed me that a UK Safeguard account has already been created to freeze assets for sanctions, fraud, etc. This is considered a commingled account. Reviewing UK requirements and meeting with OC to get their input on a commingled UK account vs a segregated account as well as need to be interest baring like the US OFAC blocked accounts. This current account undergoes a daily reconciliation.
Issue Action Plans:
Creation of account parameters and process documentation (1204746)
 External Bank Outreach and Account Creation (1204749)
Closure Request Justification:
Issue approved to be closed. Action plan supporting documentation reviewed and approved 03/31/2025.
 

 Two fast-follows that must be completed by 06/30/2025
 

 (1) Updates to the procedures owned by the team overseeing the UK Safeguard account to ensure funds specifically related to sanctions are tagged appropriately and any debits to that account which involve funds related to sanctions are subject to review and approval by GSC.
 

 (2) Development of a user guide which explains steps taken by GSC to account for and reconcile sanctions-related funds in the UK Safeguard Account.

Issue Title: Lack of Canada Frozen Asset Account and Process Documentation
Issue Description:
The Canada Compliance Lead identified an issue where Square Canada, Inc. does not have a formal process in place to handle the freezing of assets identified as prohibited by Canadian sanctions regulations. While Squareup Canada Ltd is currently able to freeze funds subject to Canadian sanctions regulations, a process to segregate funds with a financial institution is not yet finalized or documented in a policy or procedure. Squareup Canada Ltd is expected to freeze assets belonging pursuant to Canadian regulatory requirements.
 

 The issue was originally identified in 2022 when Global Sanctions discovered that Squareup Canada Ltd has assets that are in scope of Canadian sanctions requiring asset freezing. It’s important to note that the funds are currently frozen in a Square omnibus account but not segregated in a frozen Canadian Assets account. The funds have also been reported. The initiative to formalize the asset freezing process was left unfinished when Global Sanctions uncovered at time of funds movement that the financial institution was labeling the funds transfer as an OFAC blocked account pursuant to OFAC Russian sanctions.
 

 The root cause of this issue is a process design weakness, as a formal process was not built into the sanctions compliance program.
 

 Remediation of this issue would consist of continuous creation of frozen asset accounts under Canadian Sanctions Regulations. Prior accounts have been created; however, they must be verified that the accounts were not opened and titled as “OFAC Blocked Account”.
RCA Description: Process Design Weakness: The root cause of this issue is a delay in account creation and weakness in process design as a formal procedure was not integrated into the sanctions compliance program. This oversight primarily resulted from the absence of any incidents necessitating asset freezing under Canadian sanctions regulations to date.
Issue Rating Justification: Currently, there are Canadian frozen assets in the possession of Squareup Canada Ltd (Block, Inc.) but these are not segregated in a frozen Canadian Assets account, which indicates moderate immediate risk. Addressing the process design weakness is essential for future compliance with Canadian sanctions regulations.
Issue Progress Updates: External outreach to JPMC occurred and a meeting was held to review the previously created frozen asset accounts at JPMC in 2022. The accounts were incorrectly created under the OFAC account type and cannot be used for CA frozen assets. Fortunately funds were never moved to these accounts and remain frozen in a Square omnibus accounts. As of 12/18/2024 JPMC is working internally with their CA contacts to align the next steps in creating CA frozen assets accounts in CAD.
Issue Action Plans:
Validation of account parameters and creation of process documentation (1206030)
 External Bank Outreach and Account Creation (1206038)
Closure Request Justification:
All AP supporting documentation and evidence uploaded and approved for closure.
 

 Overall issue approved for closure on 08/25/2025

Issue Title: BIT RCM Enhancements needed to allow for proper Control Testing
Issue Description:
During control testing, CTT identified controls within the BIT Chargeback RCM and BIT Access & Safeguards RCM are designed in a manner which prevents proper testing execution and evidence retention. The following controls are in need of enhancements as well as a new risk and control that should be documented within the RCM. These enhancements are needed to
 

 Incoming Request Monitoring
 

 The current verbiage of the control should be updated to ensure that the proper evidence is able to be retained. Additionally, this process and control execution should be documented within the published procedure/document. 
 

 The verbiage of the control is outdated due to the BIT team no longer receiving requests via SLACK or Google Sheets. 
 

 C.06 Weekly Meetings: 
 

 Current control verbiage states health check ins are performed with key stakeholders however evidence of these health check ins are not available and not realistically available at this time based on the work that is being performed within the BIT space. Updated verbiage is recommended to better capture the true nature of how the business communicates weekly with key stakeholders. It’s recommended that all components of this control be reviewed and enhanced to ensure that proper evidence can be retained. 
 

 New Risk and Control 
 

 Risk: When improperly handled cases are identified through Secondary Review, updates are not made
 

 If during secondary reviews errors are identified, the data should be updated to properly reflect the correct labeling and information needed by the ML team.  
 

 Control: A control should be in place to ensure that these updates are being made and the corrected information is properly being distributed to key stakeholders. 
 

 Additionally, it is recommended that the RCMS be consolidated into one single BIT RCM and structured by process and subprocess rather than separating them out.

Issue Title: Instant Transfer Fees do not strictly stay within the ranges specified in Cash App's Terms of Service in all cases
Issue Description:
GCMT ran a query provided by the instant transfers engineering team for the review’s covered scope period, January 1 - June 30, 2024, to detect whether instant transfer fees stayed within the ranges specified by the Terms of Service, and identified instances where instant transfer fees exceeded the upper limit percentage of 1.75% (within the range of .005 to .02 cents). 
 

 GCMT completed transactional testing of 35 samples of instant transfer fee exceptions and the summary of the results are below. 
 

 For samples 1-26: The transfer amount was between $1 to $10 and could be charged the applicable minimum fee of .25, however each sample was charged $.26.
 For samples 27 and 28, the transfer amounts are $25.00 each. A 1.75% fee for $25.00 is $.4375, however the transfer fee charged was $.45 in each instance.
 For samples 29 and 30, the transfer amounts are $20.00 each. A 1.75% fee for $20.00 is $.35, however the transfer fee charged was $.36 in both instances. 
 For sample 31, the transfer amount was $15.00. A 1.75% fee for $15.00 is $.2625, however the transfer fee charged was $.27. 
 For samples 32 & 34, the transfer amount was $18.00. A 1.75% fee for $18.00 is $.315, however the transfer fee charged was $.32.
 For sample 33, the transfer amount was $14.62. A 1.75% fee for $14.62 is $.25585, however the transfer fee charged was $.26.
 For sample 35, the transfer amount was $16.30. A 1.75% fee for $16.30 is $.28525, however the transfer fee charged was $.29.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: External Dispute Resolution Agents are not providing a Final Response Letter to customers and AFCA/FDRS per the “2 - AFCA - IDR Referral” standard operating procedure.
Issue Description:
Background
 Per the "2 - AFCA - IDR Referral" Standard Operating Procedure the following requirements are noted to be conducted by Agents: 
 

 1 - Investigate: Ensure you save all documentation used during your investigation and provided to AFCA to the AFCA ticket in ZD, and in the Cases folder in the AFCA shared drive, so it can be referred to later.
 11 - Final Response: Compose a letter to the complainant. The purpose of this letter is to detail Afterpay’s final response on the matter.Ensure you retain evidence that you have sent the letter to the complainant via the SendSafely process to avoid claims that the response was not received.
 

 This letter should include:
 Our understanding of the complaint as told by the complainant
 The facts of our investigation
 Any of Afterpay’s Terms that are relevant to the matter
 Afterpay’s position on the matter (e.g. that we’ve acted within our Terms, no errors made, etc.)
 Any offers that we’ve made, noting where the complainant declined to accept the offer(s)
 Any new offers we’re making now to attempt to resolve the matter
 

 Finding
 GCMT identified that there is an inconsistency when a final response letter is being provided to a Customer and AFCA/FDRS as part of the complaint resolution and closure. 
 

 Testing identified 12 instances out of 30 complaints reviewed when a final response letter was not provided to the Customer and AFCA/FDRS as required per the Standard Operating Procedure, with the agent instead requesting confirmation from the customer that the complaint had been resolved via email or providing only a Resolution Agreement. This practice is not currently documented as an approved communication/confirmation method in the Standard Operating Procedure and each method does not contain all of the minimum information noted above. A further breakdown of these instances are below 
 

 AFCA - 8 instances where a final response letter was not provided 
 ● 6 instances when a final response letter was not provided with only a Resolution Agreement provided to the customer 
 ● 2 instances when no final response letter or resolution agreement was provided. 
 

 FDRS - 4 instances where a final response letter was not provided 
 ● 4 instances when a final response letter was not provided with only a Resolution Agreement provided to the customer 
 

 Separately to the above exceptions, GCMT noted instances where an agent would follow up multiple times with a customer to confirm if the complaint had been resolved via email and after multiple attempts with no response, would issue a final response letter. This practice including the number of follow ups required is not documented in the SOP. 
 

 GCMT note per conversations with ANZ EDR Management that in practice, agents may have discretion and/or allowances for the required provision of a final response letter to the Customer and AFCA/FDRS. This discretion and process is not currently reflected in the SOP.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Impacted Legal Entities:
Afterpay Australia Pty Ltd
 Afterpay NZ Limited
Issue Rating Justification: Issue rated in accordance with the Block Issue Management Program and Policy
Issue Action Plans: Complete a User Guide to accompany the AFCA file that outlines when the final resolution agreement is required to be attached and which elements are critical or administrative. (1208663)
Closure Request Justification: All actions have been closed - issue able to be closed as at 30/04/2025

Issue Title: Denied Dispute Claims - Written Explanations [Cash App - US]
Issue Description:
Requirement: 
 Regulation E — Electronic Fund Transfer Act §1005.11(d) Procedure When an Error Did Not Occur or Did Not Occur as Alleged
 Procedures if a financial institution determines no error or different error occurred. In addition to following the procedures specified in paragraph (c) of this section, the financial institution shall follow the procedures set forth in this paragraph (d) if it determines that no error occurred or that an error occurred in a manner or amount different from that described by the consumer: 
 

 (1) Written explanation. The institution's report of the results of its investigation shall include a written explanation of the institution's findings and shall note the consumer's right to request the documents that the institution relied on in making its determination. Upon request, the institution shall promptly provide copies of the documents.
 

 Official interpretation of 11(d)(1) Written Explanation
 1. Request for documentation. When a consumer requests copies of documents, the financial institution must provide the copies in an understandable form. If an institution relied on magnetic tape, it must convert the applicable data into readable form, for example, by printing it and explaining any codes.
 

 Testing Results: 
 The testing concludes the written explanation on Reg E dispute case decisions provided to customers are insufficient:
  1. GCMT identified that 96 of 295 (32.5%) Reg E dispute cases reviewed did not provide sufficient written explanation to the customer where Cash App declined their dispute claim(s). GCMT noted the explanation is exceedingly lacking with the current denial template for denial reasons are more complex and/or driven by specific user/third party behaviors i.e. PIN_USAGE_P2P, USER_ERROR_P2P, ATM_NON_RECEIPT, and MERCHANT_REFUND.
 

  2. 54 out of 62 (87%) loss claim cases did not properly disclose the details of case investigations. The loss claim population represents the claims where the customers requested the supporting documentation used for investigation, after receiving a case decision on the original dispute case. The current templated response only includes the date, status, amount, type and statement description of the transaction claimed, and does not provide sufficient details and explanation to the customer on most of the denial reasons. GCMT noted multiple cases where the customers responded to the documentation provided, claiming it did not provide any additional details other than what they already knew when submitting the claims.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: Processing of Breathing Space requests by Collection Agents in accordance with the Breathing Space (Collections Only) Standard Operating Procedure requires improvement.
Issue Description:
Background 
 The “Breathing Space (Collections Only) SOP details the current requirements and expectations for the processing of DRS Breathing Space Requests. 
 

 The below are pertinent extracts from the SOP: 
 ➢ Written off and Suspended Accounts 
 When an account is written off, you do not need to apply the ‘hardship’ flag as the account is already on hold and will not trigger any payment requests or referral to a collections agency. However, all other steps will apply.
 ➢ Accounts with a debt collection agency who are subject to Breathing Space (Debt Respite ONLY)
 Clearpay Receives a notification a customer is on Debt respite breathing space
 ● Collections team update the account on Admin to apply breathing space hold
 ● Reach out to debt collection agency on the same day to advise they need to close the account on their records and return to Clearpay due to breathing space hold
 ● Update Admin notes as being from the 'Insolvency Service' so agents can tell the difference between standard and debt respite
 ➢ How to apply a Breathing Space hold on Admin Portal (Steps)
 If the request has been received via the 'Insolvency portal' send confirmation to the third party via the portal and update notes on Admin to confirm this has occurred
 

 Issue Description: 
 GCMT tested 50 DRS Standard Breathing Space Requests to determine collection agents compliance with all requirements in the Breathing Space (Collections Only) SOP and identified a 20% or higher non-compliance rate for the following areas/steps. 
 

 1. Written off or Suspended Accounts – For 10 Breathing Space Requests where the customer had a written off or suspended account, GCMT identified 3 of 10 (30%) instances where a Hardship Flag was processed on the customer’s account which is contrary to the requirement of the SOP not to apply the Hardship Flag.
 2. Accounts with a debt collections agency – For 9 Breathing Space Requests where per Admin Portal, the customer’s account was with a Debt Collection Agency (DCA) with no evidence of the account being returned to Clearpay, GCMT noted 3 instances (33%) where no contact was made to the DCA to request the account to be closed, which is contrary to the requirement to contact the DCA on the same day with a request to recall the customer’s Clearpay account. Additionally for 4 (44%) instances, evidence of request for account closure was confirmed by Management via the internal DCA portal, however no note was left on the account to confirm this was actioned by the agent. 
 

 Additionally, there is no current requirement and/or expectation for the DCA to confirm to Clearpay Collections that the request to close the account has been actioned.
 

 3. Confirmation to third party via portal and update of notes - Due to system limitations within the Insolvency Portal at the time of testing, GCMT were not able to confirm if a third-party confirmation was sent by the agent at the time of processing. 
 ● For 50 out of 50 Breathing Space requests reviewed, GCMT noted 0 instances where a note was left on Admin Portal to confirm that the third-party confirmation had been sent by the agent via the Insolvency Portal which is contrary to the requirement of the SOP to document this being completed. In practice GCMT noted per Management confirmation that agents are instead applying the following note to confirm all steps have been undertaken on the portal “Worked on BS Portal”.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Monitoring: 2024 Q4; Afterpay Global; Clearpay - Breathing Space (1031883)
Issue Rating Justification: Issue rated in accordance with Block Issue Management Program and Policy
Issue Action Plans: SOP update and refresher training/team feedback (1209334)

Issue Title: The “Breathing Space (Collections Only) SOP requires updates to reflect current practices to ensure that customer accounts are not referred to a DCA during a breathing space hold.
Issue Description:
Background 
 The “Breathing Space (Collections Only) SOP details the current requirements and expectations for the processing of DRS Breathing Space Requests. 
 

 ➢ Written off and Suspended Accounts 
 When an account is written off, you do not need to apply the ‘hardship’ flag as the account is already on hold and will not trigger any payment requests or referral to a collections agency. However, all other steps will apply.
 

 Issue Description: 
 A) GCMT tested 50 DRS Standard Breathing Space Requests and noted the following practices which is not documented in the current SOP: 
 ● A hardship flag was not applied to 2 customer accounts due to the customer having no balance with Clearpay at the date the Breathing Space was requested. For one of the two accounts, multiple purchases were made with Clearpay within the requested period for Breathing Space per the Insolvency Portal. The potential flow on effect is that should the customer fail to meet payment obligations, that the customer would be sent communications relating to payment or referred to a debt collection agency during the Breathing Space period which would be a breach of the Breathing Space regulation. 
 ● A hardship flag was not applied to 1 customer account due to the account being frozen and excluded from collections indefinitely. The status for this account was noted as ‘Hardship’ at the time the Breathing Space was received instead of written off or suspended. 
 

 B) GCMT observed through review of customer accounts, three instances where the customer’s account was written off on Admin Portal, however the account was still outsourced to the Debt Collection Agency (‘DCA’) as part of a “Bulk Action – Account selected for settlement campaign” within the next 6 months. 
 The referral of a written off account to the DCA contradicts the current wording and exemption in the SOP which does not require a hardship flag to be processed for the account on the basis that there is no risk of referral to a Debt Collection Agency.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Monitoring: 2024 Q4; Afterpay Global; Clearpay - Breathing Space (1031883)
Issue Rating Justification: Issue rated in accordance with the Block Issue Management Program and Policy
Issue Action Plans: SOP Update, System Limitation investigation and amendment to Settlement Selection Criteria for Bulk Actions (1209337)

Issue Title: The Merchant Authorization for the PAD Agreement is not being retained at onboarding and/or for a minimum of 12 months following the last PAD processed, in some instances.
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement - Canadian Payments Association; Rule H1 - Pre-Authorized Debits (PADS): 
 Appendix II - Payor’s PAD Agreement - Mandatory and Optional Elements
 A statement by the Payor that must be duly Authorized in accordance with its account agreement with its Processing Member, clearly and unambiguously authorizing the Payee to debit an account specified by the Payor.
 

 Subsection V - PAD Processing
 The Payee shall ensure that an audit trail, including the Payor’s PAD Agreement and evidence of Authorization, where applicable, and all information needed to retrieve or trace a PAD is maintained for a minimum of 12 months following the last PAD processed in accordance with that Payor’s PAD Agreement.
 

 Background 
 Merchants are presented the PAD for review during the Merchant Onboarding Application at either the “Add your bank account manually” or “Link your bank to” page. 
 

 Merchant Authorization and Agreement to the PAD, is provided by the Merchant clicking one of the above links with the following disclaimers provided: 
 Add your bank account manually: “By submitting these details, I confirm I agree to the PAD authorization and authorize Afterpay to debit and credit this account pursuant to the merchant agreement”. 
 Link your bank to: “By selecting ‘Link Bank’, I confirm I agree to the PAD Authorization and authorize Afterpay to debit and credit my account pursuant to the merchant agreement”. 
 

 Issue Description:
 GCMT tested a sample of 665 Merchants onboarded for the review period 1 January 2024 - 30 June 2024 to confirm that the Merchant Authorization and Agreement was obtained at onboarding and retained for record keeping purposes. 
 GCMT were unable to obtain 18 of 665 (3%) Authorization/Agreement for Merchants.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Monitoring: 2024 Q3; Afterpay Canada; Pre- Authorized Debits (PAD) (1032557)
Issue Rating Justification: Based on GCMT's Issue Management Program
Issue Action Plans: Merchant PAD Authorization (1209994)
Closure Request Justification:
GCMT obtained and sighted the investigation into the missing PAD timestamps. The root cause was due to various critical systems that support onboarding which went through database migration (Salesforce & HerokuConnect). This had caused pulling data challenging. 
 

 Of the exceptions identified, the business was able to manually provide the timestamps.

Issue Title: The practices for Defense Manpower Data Center screening, customer response case expiration timeframes and sending quick text messages to a borrower were not completed in some cases.
Issue Description:
Procedure Requirement: 
 Per the Lending Operations SCRA Procedures: 
 1. Page 4, Receiving Notification: Operations specialist will request a copy of the service notification if it hasn’t been received yet. Send Cash App {Borrow - SCRA Notification Request} quick text, requesting:
  a. The DOJ letter calling for active duty/military service
  b. A letter on official letterhead from the office of the Service Member’s superior certifying the call to active duty status and the dates that it applies.
 

 2. Page 5, Receiving Notification:
  a. After receiving notification, the Operations specialist will send acknowledgement to the borrower that we’ve received the notification and it’s under review. Use Cash App {Borrow - SCRA Notification Received} quick text.
 

  b. If the borrower does not send notification documents within 30 days of initial communication, Operations specialist will follow up by doing the following:
  i. Searching the DMDC website to independently verify active duty/Military Service
  1. If Military Service is confirmed, move to the Confirmed Military Service Step
  a. Comment on the regulator account that Military Service was confirmed via DMDC
  b. Any documents confirming the Military Service should be screenshot and added to the SCRA Google Drive folder, in a folder labeled with the plan token
  2. If unable to confirm, comment on buyer’s account that Military Service was not confirmed via DMDC and leave SC case pending
  a. Operations will create a calendar alert to check back in on the case on the 30th day after receiving the notification.
 

 3. Page 6, Expired Requests: If the borrower does not send notification documents within 30 days of initial communication, the benefit request will be considered expired.
  a. Operations specialist will send Cash App {Borrow - SCRA Follow Up}
 

 Per the Notion SCRA Procedures:
 Receiving Notification: If the borrower does not send notification documents within 14 days of initial communication, Operations specialist will follow up doing the following: Searching the DMDC website to independently verify active duty/Military Service. If Military Service is confirmed, move to the Confirmed Military Service Step, comment on the regulator account that Military Service was confirmed via DMDC. Any documents confirming the Military Service should be screenshot and added to the SCRA Google Drive folder, in a folder labeled with the plan token. If unable to confirm, comment on buyer’s account that Military Service was not confirmed via DMDC and leave SC case pending. Operations will create a calendar alert to check back in on the case on the 30th day after receiving the notification.
 

 Issue Description:
 GCMT identified procedure deviations and inconsistent timeframes when requesting military orders.
 

 1. 1 of 2 SCRA customers did not respond to the business with their military service documents, and the business did not implement its formalized procedures for follow-up:
  a. A Defense Manpower Data Center (DMDC) search was not performed. Note, the DMDC search does not provide the servicemember's active duty start date but rather the active duty status based on the screening date. Therefore, this process does not provide the required information needed to apply SCRA benefits. Additionally, the SCRA requirement is for the servicemember to provide creditors of written notice to qualify for SCRA benefits. SCRA does not require creditors to conduct active duty screening (this is instead an Military Lending Act, MLA, requirement).
  b. The case was closed after 14 days per the Notion SCRA Procedures and not 30 days per the Lending Operations SCRA Procedures which is the correct procedure to follow. 
  c. The QT explaining case expiration due to the lack of customer response was not sent to the customer per the Borrow - {SCRA Follow Up} QT. Instead, the case was closed without further communication.
 

 2. 1 of 2 SCRA cases was not sent the acknowledgment QT, Cash App {Borrow - SCRA Notification Received}, which details Cash Lending's SLA for reviewing the military orders submitted by the Borrower. Since acknowledgment was not provided, the customer contacted Cash App 10 calendar days after submission to follow up on their case.
 

 3. The timeframe to request military orders were inconsistent and did not follow the general 48-hour customer service response SLA. Note, per the business the delay was due to an overall increased wait at the time and was not particular to SCRA. 
  a. 151162260 - Case was transferred on August 8, 2024, and the business request for military orders was sent on August 14, 2024. (4 business days)
  b. 150629605 - Case was transferred on August 4, 2024, and the business request for military orders was sent on August 14, 2024. (8 business days)
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
