Issue Title: Dishonored debit wording in Afterpay’s Canada’s PAD agreement.
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement - Canadian Payments Association; Rule H1 - Pre-Authorized
 Debits (PADS): Subsection VI
 A PAD that has been dishonored may only be re-presented in accordance with the applicable
 provisions of Rule F1 or F4. In addition, a PAD shall only be re-presented for the same
 amount as the original PAD (i.e. shall not include additional charges).
 

 Issue Description:
 Afterpay’s PAD agreement currently states Afterpay will “issue another debit in substitution
 for the dishonored debit until the debit is honored”, suggesting Afterpay may re-attempt a
 failed debit multiple times until we receive a successful debit.
 GCMT note per conversations with Afterpay Finance Operations, that the current practices
 and processes are aligned to the regulatory requirement of Subsection VI, whereby a failed
 debit occurs, an Afterpay Finance Operations Analyst will only re-attempt a debit on a
 one-time only basis for the original amount dishonored.
 However, there is no manual or system based control and/or documentation to prevent
 multiple re-attempts occurring.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Monitoring: 2024 Q3; Afterpay Canada; Pre- Authorized Debits (PAD) (1032557)
Issue Rating Justification: Based on GCMT's Issue Management Program
Closure Request Justification:
Based on a meeting that was attended by - 
 GCMT (David Nguyen, Shannon Foo and Keith Roche) and Product Counsel (Judy Liao and Lucy Farmer) on November 1st 2024.
 

 Afterpay Product Counsel have acknowledged the discrepancy with the regulation however are relying on the review undertaken by outside counsel including the template PAD, which occured at the time Afterpay Canada was established. Counsel are accepting the risk and are not proposing to complete any actions.

Issue Title: Borrow loan repayments that are received in the form of a check are not credited directly to the Borrow loan when the Cash App stored balance is negative; rather they are applied to the negative balance first.
Issue Description:
Guidance Requirement: 
 CFPB UDAAP Examination Manual,
  1. Page 1: The standard for unfairness in the Dodd-Frank Act is that an act or practice is unfair when: (1) It causes or is likely to cause substantial injury to consumers;
 

  2. Page 5: A representation, omission, actor practice is deceptive when (1) The representation, omission, act, or practice misleads or is likely to mislead the consumer;
 

 Agreement Requirement:  
 Borrow Loan Agreement, 
  1. Section 10. How Your Payments Are Applied: "Except as prohibited by law, payments will be applied to Your loan as Bank determines in its sole discretion."
 

  2. Section 12(d) Payment by Mail. "You may send Checks payable to “First Electronic Bank c/o Square Capital” to the following address: First Electronic Bank c/o Square Capital, LLC \ Attn: Cash App Lending Program Operations \ Lockbox Services 208731 \ Dallas, TX 75320-8731
 

 Your Check must specify Your Cashtag, original Borrowed Amount, and original borrow date. Your payment must be made in U.S. dollars from a valid U.S. source in a form acceptable to Bank. Conforming payments can be made in the following ways: A Check must be physically received at the above address by 4:00 PM CT on a Business Day in order to be credited to Your account on the same day."
 

 Policy Requirement: 
 Collections Policy, Page 6, Post-Dated Checks, "There are a number of prohibitions concerning a debt collector’s use of post-dated checks. The Lending Team does not honor post-dated check requests. Checks will be credited as of the date in which they are received."
 

 Issue Description: 
 GCMT identified that Borrow Loan repayments from customers which are received in the form of a check are not credited directly to the Borrow loan in instances where the Cash App stored balance is negative. 
 

 GCMT identified 1 of 12 checks where the customer requested for their check to be applied to their overdue Borrow Loan. However this check was applied to their Cash App account which was overdrawn. The business practice is to apply any Borrow incoming funds to the overdrawn Cash App balance first in order to bring the Cash App account balance to a positive state. Once a Cash App account has a positive balance, the customer agreed auto payments will resume to the Borrow loan.  
 

 GCMT notes that the Cash App Borrow Loan Agreement section 10 permits payments to be applied at the Bank's discretion and section 12(d) explains how a customer can make a cheque payment; however, by applying the customers borrow loan repayment to the Cash App negative balance and not the Cash App overdue Borrow loan, it is arguable that this practice could be considered unfair and deceptive, as the overdue balance fee and/or interest will be accruing at a higher cost to the customer than the "free overdraft coverage" as stated in the Cash App Terms of Service.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2024 Q3; Cash App Borrow; Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices (UDAAP) - Complaints, Training, Servicing and Collections (1033209)
Issue Rating Justification: Includes any Issues that may represent violations of requirements set forth "explicitly" in law or regulatory guidance.
Issue Action Plans: Borrow loan repayments received in the form of a check are not credited directly to the Borrow loan when the Cash App stored balance is negative; rather they are applied to the negative balance first. (1211330)
Closure Request Justification:
June 4, 2025: The business implemented a Toolbox button permitting payments to a customer’s outstanding loan even though the stored value balance is negative. The “Allow Payment from Negative SVB (Stored Value Balance)” button is selected by staff to ensure that a payment will not fail. Additionally, the Lending - Borrow Physical Check Tracker was updated on April 8, 2025 with a negative SVB indicator. 
 

 GCMT conducted validation testing of physical checks received for the period February 11 - May 5, 2025. Testing identified one case where the customer had a negative SVB. GCMT determined that the check payment for the outstanding loan was successfully applied in this negative balance scenario.
 

 Refer to the action plan for supporting documentation. This management action plan is complete.

Issue Title: There is no written procedure describing the collection activities, nor is there an inventory of all Cash Borrow collections notices and the situations in which they would be used.
Issue Description:
Policy Requirement: 
 The Cash App Lending Collections Policy requires:
  1. Page 4, Communication with the Borrower: 
  a. Collectors conduct collection activities only during approved hours (8:00 a.m. to 9:00 p.m. customer’s local time) and via calls to the borrower’s home or place of work, unless specifically requested otherwise. 
  b. The Lending Team limits written communication to 1 communication every 7 days for debt collection. 
 

  2. Pages 5-6: Miranda and Mini-Miranda: Where the debt is being collected is deemed to be consumer debt, the Collectors must state the following Maxi and Mini Miranda warnings (or other language approved by the Legal and Cash App Compliance Teams) stating:
  a. Initial Communication: “This communication is from a debt collector and any information obtained will be used for that purpose.”
  b. Subsequent Communication: “This communication is from a debt collector.”
 

 Issue Description: 
 There is no inventory (source of truth) of all the Cash Lending-specific collections notices and the action/cadence that triggers the notices. Push notifications were provided but it is unclear whether this was the complete set of active emails or quick texts used to collect on overdue loans as there is a larger inventory of notifications housed in Toolbox. GCMT, however, was not given access by Cash Customer Operations to the repository and there is no written procedures to holistically describe Cash App Lending's collection activities. The absence of a defined process and which messages the business considers as collection communications means that communication times and Miranda language could not be reviewed during testing.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2024 Q3; Cash App Borrow; Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices (UDAAP) - Complaints, Training, Servicing and Collections (1033209)
Issue Rating Justification: Presents a moderate risk to the operations of Block.
Issue Action Plans: There is no written procedure describing the collection activities, nor is there an inventory of all Cash Borrow collections notices and the situations in which they would be used. (1211333)
Closure Request Justification:
The business created the Lending Operations Collections Procedure for the Cash Lending Retro product. Lending Operations utilizes various tools to collect on past due amounts including in-app notifications, text messaging, and emails. The procedures also contain two exhibit sections that include relevant screenshot template copies that align with actual practices. 
 

 This management action plan is complete. No further action is required.

Issue Title: There were process deviations identified with the Better Business Bureau complaint handling which resulted in missed response due dates, incorrect response channels, and unreported complaints to the Bank Partner.
Issue Description:
Program Requirement: 
 The Global Complaint Program requires, "All business units must aim to close complaints in 15 business days or less. Business units should view the 15 business day or less requirement as the baseline for complaint resolution. The business unit must adhere to tighter deadlines than those set by the Global Complaint Program if the complaint: 
  - Is submitted by a consumer or complaint authority with its own deadline;" 
 

 BBB Requirement: 
 Per the BBB letter sent to business, 
  1. Section: How do I take care of this? Your BBB is requesting a written response to this complaint within the next 10 calendar days. Your prompt and professional response greatly enhances the chance for a successful resolution and will further your reputation as a member of the BBB. 
 

  2. Section: What should I include in my response? Please provide an explanation of the events from your perspective and/or a concrete plan for how to work toward a resolution. Remember that the more factual information we have about the case, the easier it is for all parties to develop a full understanding of the situation.
 

 Procedure Requirement: 
 Lending Operations Complaint Procedure, 
  1. Page 21, Escalation Process for Various Teams - Cash Executive Relations Team,
  - iii, "CERT will respond to the customer via the BBB or CFPB portal."
 

  2. Page 19, Duties Owed to Bank Partners:
  - "Specifically, the scenarios in which Block owes a duty to notify the Bank Partner associated with the loan regarding a complaint includes: Complaints received from the Better Business Bureau;"
  - "Where Block is required under one of these scenarios to share information regarding a complaint, the information that must be shared includes: A copy of the complaint; and A proposed resolution and response."
 

 Issue Description: 
 GCMT identified process deviations in BBB complaint handling, specifically:
  1. 3 of 3 BBB complaints reviewed were not resolved within the 10 calendar days timeframe as required by the BBB. For all three complaints, an acknowledgment of the complaint and that the case was being reviewed was communicated on their due dates. The response to address the complaints were provided 10, 12, and 3 calendar days after their respective due date dates. 
 

  2. CERT responds to the customers via direct email to the customer as evidenced in CF1, not the BBB portal as described in the Lending Operations Complaint Procedure.

Issue Title: Invest Search Disclosures
Issue Description: The Cash App Investing ("CAI") Disclosures website (https://cash.app/legal/us/en-us/disclosures-in-app) does not contain some disclosures related to the "Invest Search" product feature. The Invest Search feature provides additional information about assets available on the CAI platform by utilizing screening logics to surface certain stocks that fit the screening criteria. Screening logics used include: (i) most traded stocks, (ii) most traded ETFs, and (iii) stocks that paid the highest dividend yields. The disclosures website does not identify "Invest Search" as a feature, and does not contain a description of the screening logics for “Most Traded Stocks,” “Most Traded ETFs,” and “Stocks that Paid Dividends” categories. The issue was identified via CAI's annual testing process. CAI's public facing content is subject to regulatory content standards, which must be based on principles of fair dealing and good faith, must be fair and balanced, and must provide a sound basis for evaluating the facts in regard to any particular security or type of security, industry, or service. The potential regulatory impact is that not having sufficient disclosures could be seen as not in compliance with regulatory content standards.
Issue Rating Justification: The potential regulatory impact is that not having sufficient disclosures could be seen as not in compliance with regulatory content standards.
Issue Action Plans: Update Cash App Investing Disclosures website (1211972)

Issue Title: Refund Processing Issues Identified: Failed Transactions for Peer to Peer (P2P) Payments Over 365 Days and Acquirer Changes
Issue Description: Cash App has identified a group of customers who were informed they would receive a refund to their linked instrument, but the refund attempt was unsuccessful. Normally, Cash App’s peer-to-peer (P2P) refund logic attempts to refund transactions to the original payment method, as long as the transaction is within 365 calendar days. However, two issues have recently been discovered that prevent refunds from being processed as expected. First, a longstanding issue with Esperanto’s code fails any refund attempts for transactions older than 380 days, meaning customers will not receive their refund. Second, due to a business decision to discontinue refunds through the old acquirer, Wells Fargo, any refunds for payments made on or after May 13, 2024, will be automatically redirected to SVB, instead of being processed back to the original linked instrument. These failures highlight discrepancies between Cash App's intended refund process and the actual outcomes for some customers.

Issue Title: Cash App Pay Refund Failures Result in Missing Automated Customer Communications and Uninformed Customers
Issue Description:
Currently, when an agent hits the File button on a Cash App Pay claim, we expect it to always result in a refund to the customer. Previously, agents would hit the File button and email the customer via Salesforce to let them know that they have been refunded.
 With the launch of $Pay Phase 1 in Disputron, we introduced the capability to send automated comms to customers when they receive a refund. This eliminated the need for agents to send manual emails, and also enabled us to better track the data around it. On inspecting the data around these comms, we noticed that some customers never received emails. This led us to discover that sometimes, the customer refund fails in the backend, and this failure doesn’t get surfaced to the agent handling the claim.
 It turns out, this issue had been occurring for a while, but the introduction of email comms via Disputron and the reporting around it had helped us uncover it.

Issue Title: Advocates lack a clear process to handle suspicious customer requests
Issue Description:
Issue Overview:
 Advocates currently lack a clear path for escalating cases or a defined workflow when identifying customers contacting support with malicious intent.
 

 Recent Incident What happened? (to the customer):
 A recurring issue has been noted where customers use identical verbiage such as “I need all devices logged out from support” and “Where is advocate” in the same chat. Examples include:
 

 [Case 1](https://cf1.lightning.force.com/lightning/r/5005w00002QiqzpAAB/view)
 [Case 2](https://cf1.lightning.force.com/lightning/r/5005w00002Qi76BAAR/view)
 [Case 3](https://cf1.lightning.force.com/lightning/r/5005w00002QQ9nFAAT/view)
 [Case 4](https://cf1.lightning.force.com/lightning/r/5005w00002QmcABAAZ/view)
 [Case 5](https://cf1.lightning.force.com/lightning/r/5005w00002QmISgAAN/view)
 

 These cases, dating back to 2023, illustrate a potential pattern of account takeover (ATO) attempts. Without a clear escalation path or workflow, advocates must follow standard procedures, which could inadvertently aid these malicious attempts.
 

 Proposed Solution What should have happened? (in a perfect world):
 Implement a process allowing advocates to tag and escalate cases when these inquiries arise. Possible solutions include:
 - Integrating a Personally Identifiable Information (PII) verification step for messaging when these specific requests are made.
 - Initiating a process for customers to verify PII via a voice call before logging out all devices.
 

 Impact on Customer and Business What is the impact? (to the customer/business):
 This process will build trust with our customers by enabling customer support to identify and mitigate bad actors early. It will decrease the risk of ATOs, ultimately improving business impact and customer account safety.
 

 Issue Discovery Who discovered the issue and how? :
 Advocates have been identifying these trends and escalating them to CSOLs. However, there is no standardized process for next steps when malicious intent is detected. Establishing a clear workflow will ensure consistent and effective mitigation of these risks.
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure resulting in an impact to regulatory obligations
Issue Action Plans: Advocates lack a clear process to handle suspicious customer requests (1216590)

Per IM Lead Charlotte Vasi this issue will be treated as a doc & close as the work completed on CCOPGM-250 satisfies the requirements needed to close the original gap submitted on INTAKEFORM-44.

Issue Title: Gambling ads shown to Clearpay UK customers without Compliance approval
Issue Description:
The problem that has been identified
 ROKT ads after purchase has gone live with categories that have not been approved by Clearpay UK compliance. A US approval was given and this has been taken as a global approval.
 Gambling adverts where shown to clearpay opted into marketing customers which poses clearpay uk significant risk.
 

 What was expected to occur but did not
 Region specific approvals for categories which can be shown as ads post purchase.
 

 Why the Issue is of concern and the risk it pose to the business eg the potential
 incidents (events) that may occur .
 The UK Advertising Standards Authority (ASA) is the independent regulator of advertising across all media in the United Kingdom. Advertising gambling immediately after a "buy now, pay later" (BNPL) purchase can be considered socially irresponsible under the ASA CAP Code rule(16.1) for several reasons:
 Exploitation of Financial Vulnerability: The CAP Code emphasizes that gambling advertisements should not exploit the financial vulnerabilities of consumers. BNPL schemes are often used by individuals who may not have immediate funds available, indicating a potential financial strain. Promoting gambling in this context could be seen as exploiting individuals who might be financially vulnerable.
 Irresponsible Financial Behavior: The CAP Code requires that gambling ads must not portray gambling as a solution to financial problems or as a way to achieve financial security. Linking gambling opportunities with a BNPL transaction could imply that gambling is an acceptable or viable method to manage or alleviate financial commitments, which is misleading and irresponsible.
 Social Responsibility: The Code mandates that all gambling advertising must be socially responsible. Associating gambling with BNPL purchases could encourage impulsive behavior and poor financial decision-making, which is contrary to the principles of social responsibility.
 Potential for Harm: The ASA's rules are designed to prevent gambling-related harm. By targeting individuals who have just made a purchase on credit, there is a heightened risk of encouraging gambling behavior in people who may already be in a precarious financial situation, potentially leading to further financial harm.
 Vulnerability to Gambling Addiction: The CAP Code advises against targeting individuals who are susceptible to gambling-related harm. People using BNPL services might be more vulnerable to gambling addiction due to financial pressures, making such advertising particularly inappropriate.
 

 Clearpay UK is currently awaiting the finalization of rules and timelines before submitting an application to the FCA to continue offering our credit service. The regulator's expectations are high, as they expect Clearpay to ensure that our customers experience good outcomes at every stage of the product lifecycle, including post-purchase, and to comply with the Consumer Duty.
 Promoting gambling immediately after a BNPL purchase could risk breaching this Duty. Additionally, if Clearpay were to receive an adverse ruling from the ASA leading up to our application, it could place us in a precarious position, potentially impacting the regulator's perception of our firm and raising questions about our controls and our ability to evaluate good customer outcomes.
 

 The pervasiveness of the issue (i.e. number of samples affected / total number of
 samples tested)
 Unsure of number of customers who have seen these adverts, however this has been live for up to a year.
 

 How the Issue was identified
 Issue was flagged to Clearpay UK by a supplier.
 

 Clearly describe root cause of the Issue
 Appropriate approvals not sought for launch of ROKT ads in the UK.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
RCA Description:
3.3 Untimely / Inadequately Executed Technology Solutions
 Failure to obtain appropriate approvals
Issue Rating Justification:
Risk of breaching Advertising Standards Authority rules (1.3) Social responsibility 
 Risk of this issue impacting our ability to be granted a licence by the FCA to continue offering our credit product.
 Risk of customer detriment and complaints.

Issue Title: Missing the download and print feature on customer agreements in Afterpay app
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirements: E-Sign Act - The Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act 
 

 Retention by Financial Institutions
 Under the E-Sign Act, if a financial institution is legally required to maintain copies of a contract or other records of a transaction, the institution may rely on an electronic record of the information that accurately reflects the information in the contract or other record, and that remains accessible to all persons who are legally entitled to access the information in a form that can later be reproduced. 15 U.S.C. §7001(d)(1)
 

 Retainable Form for Consumers 
 The Board stated in the Federal Register preamble to the November 2007 final rule that financial institutions satisfy the requirement to provide electronic disclosures in a form that the consumer can retain if the disclosures are provided in a standard electronic format that can be downloaded and saved or printed on a typical home personal computer. 72 Fed. Reg. 63471
 

 Compliance Gap: Both parties must be able to access and save the signed document. Electronic records must be kept in a format that accurately reproduces the original information and complies with other laws. Currently in Afterpay app, customers can only open the agreements on screen, but cannot download as PDF nor has a print option.
Issue Rating Justification: It is a legal and regulatory requirement that we want to be proactive to ensure we are complying, it has not been brought up (yet) by a regulator.
Issue Action Plans: Making all customer agreements in-app downloadable/open into PDF as well as printable (1222524)
Closure Request Justification: On behalf of Matt Elison, Issue Owner: See closure details in the action plan 1222524 closure information and attachments.

Issue Title: Observation 1: Lack of Controls enabling the Timely Destruction of Personal Data
Issue Description:
SEL has not established a formal process or mechanism to determine whether terminated customers should have their personal data deleted, as legally required, upon completion of the regulatory retention period (typically five years but this could be up to a maximum of 10 years for transaction records). Through an ad hoc analysis, SEL Management identified 404 terminated accounts that were closed over five years ago as of October 21, 2024. SEL’s program documentation also fails to explicitly acknowledge (i) the requirement to delete terminated customer records within the aforementioned timeframes and (ii) the criteria that permit extended data retention beyond the initial five-year period (e.g., ongoing legal proceedings or investigations).
 

 Potential Impact:
 Failing to adhere to the data retention requirements outlined in the MLRs 2017 could lead to regulatory sanctions and legal penalties.
 Retaining personal data beyond the required period unnecessarily increases the risk of data breaches and unauthorized access, potentially impacting individuals' privacy rights.
Issue Rating Justification:
Potential Impact:
 Failing to adhere to the data retention requirements outlined in the MLRs 2017 could lead to regulatory sanctions and legal penalties.
 Retaining personal data beyond the required period unnecessarily increases the risk of data breaches and unauthorized access, potentially impacting individuals' privacy rights.
Issue Action Plans:
MAP 1 - Analysis of Customer Data (1224503)
 MAP 2 - Deletion of Customer Data (1224507)
 MAP 3 - Ongoing Analysis of Closed Account Customer Data (1224512)
 MAP 4 - Documentation of ongoing customer data retention and deletion requirements (1224515)

Issue Title: Lack of systematic control in the Disputes Reimbursement Process allows agents to refund amounts >$2,500 without the required approval from a SeniorLead Dispute Specialist, leading to potential errors and non-compliance with the Reimbursement Policy.
Issue Description:
During the execution of control testing and review of QC’s P2P Disputron Report Summary, CTT identified that there is currently no systemic approval process or control in place to ensure that any refund amount in excess of $2,500 for a dispute claim is approved by a Senior or Lead Dispute Specialist. Currently, the process as stated within the reimbursement policy is that an agent must obtain approval to refund any amount over $2,500 however, the system DOES allow for the agent to reimburse any amount regardless of an approval being obtain due to the lack of systemic controls in place. The items are manually tracked within a Google sheet which also leaves room for agent error. The manual nature of this escalation process and lack of systemic controls in place for the approvals not only leaves room for agent error but also lack of adherence to the Reimbursement Policy.
 

 

 It is recommended that a systemic control and approval process be implemented within CF1 to ensure agents must receive approval within the system before systemically being allowed to refund a customer any amount in excess of $2,500.
RCA Description: Technology
Issue Rating Justification: An issue in interpretation and execution of CCO policy which has material impact on the process or risk assessment.
Issue Action Plans: Lack of systematic control in the Disputes Reimbursement Process (1223859)
Closure Request Justification: Evidence/artifacts to support close have been reviewed by the IM Lead. - Charlotte

Issue Title: Subset of Cash Card Disputes are wrongfully denied for debited transactions using their Personal Identification Number (PIN) when the customer did not use their PIN
Issue Description: In 2024, 2.29% of Cash Card disputes were denied due to policies that incorrectly flag PIN usage based on the PINDEBIT transaction attribute. Both manual and automated processes rely on this logic to identify PIN transactions. However, Disputes recently discovered that some disputes were wrongly denied, assuming PIN usage when, in fact, the customer did not use their PIN. As a result, a small subset of customers will require remediation identified by this gap. The issue, which began in March 2022, continues to affect a subset of disputes, where transactions are wrongly denied as PIN-based when no PIN was actually used. This problem will persist until the necessary logic enhancements are implemented.
RCA Description: Technology
Issue Rating Justification: An issue in interpretation and execution of CCO policy which has material impact on the process or risk assessment.
Issue Action Plans: Subset of Cash Card Disputes are wrongfully denied for debited transactions using their Personal Identification Number (PIN) when the customer did not use their PIN (1225169)
Closure Request Justification: Evidence package reviewed by IM Lead on 5/7/25. - Charlotte Vasi

Issue Title: Failure to Complete Escalated Case Investigations Timely
Issue Description:
Square Australia has not adhered to the case investigation SLA set forth in the AU Suspicious Matter
 Reporting Procedure consistently. The procedure defines a 28 day window within which compliance
 must conclude whether or not the activity associated with an escalated alert is suspicious; if compliance
 determines there are reasonable grounds for suspicion, the team has to file a Suspicious Matter Report
 (“SMR”) with AUSTRAC (i) within 24 hours for activity related to terrorism financing or (ii) within three
 business days for activity related to fraud or money laundering, in line with regulatory requirements.
 IA reviewed alerts that were escalated for SMR review due to their association with a fraud vector that
 remained open / active throughout the covered period; as compliance expected to identify reportable
 activity over an extended timeframe (i.e., until the completion / remediation of the fraud attack), the
 team decided to complete case investigations and file related SMRs in batches every six months. This
 review and reporting cadence is not aligned with the 28 day investigation SLA defined in the procedure,
 which does not specify permissible exceptions. Compliance subsequently failed to execute the
 bi-annual review timely and did not file corresponding SMRs with AUSTRAC.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Requirement
Issue Action Plans: MAP 1: Remediate late SMRs (1225162)

Issue Title: Customer Onboarding Control Gaps - Trusts & Associations
Issue Description:
Square Australia has not established sufficient controls to ensure that the business onboards
 customers that are Associations and Trusts in accordance with the requirements set forth in the
 Customer Identification and Verification Procedures. At onboarding, potential customers who are not
 sole-traders must provide their Australian Business Number (“ABN”) on a digital form, which prompts
 Square Australia’s system to retrieve the entity type (e.g., association, partnership, trust, etc.) from the
 Australian Business Register (“ABR”). The entity type returned triggers a subset of customers, including
 Associations, to populate in a queue for manual compliance review, as certain verification procedures
 are performed manually. For Associations classified as “Other Incorporated Entities” or “Other
 Unincorporated Entities” in the ABR, Square Australia’s system may apply a value of “unknown” as the
 entity type erroneously, thereby preventing the customer from populating in the queue for manual
 compliance review. Compliance identified 20 Association accounts that bypassed required verification
 procedures as a result of this system bug. With respect to Trusts, Square Australia’s onboarding
 procedures do not provide clear instructions regarding the steps required to verify intermediate legal
 entities in the ownership structure.
 Although Trusts and Associations comprise a relatively low percentage of Square Australia’s customer
 base, the business must ensure that automated and manual processes supporting / enabling its
 onboarding procedures are operating effectively.
Issue Rating Justification: Policies & Procedures
Issue Action Plans:
Onboarding Flow Bypassed (1225170)
 MAP 2: Missing Association Case Monitoring (1225173)
 MAP 3: Procedure Update - Entity Node Tool Screening (1225179)

Issue Title: Absence of An Ongoing Monitoring / Transaction Monitoring Program
Issue Description:
Afterpay Canada has not yet implemented transaction monitoring procedures/controls to identify unusual activity and red flags indicative of ML/TF. While Afterpay Canada does conduct Transaction Monitoring for fraud, there is no Transaction Monitoring to detect potentially suspicious activity or transactions indicative of ML/TF. Transaction Monitoring is a pillar of an effective AML Program as it is necessary to review and monitor activities to detect potentially suspicious transactions.
 

 Potential Impact:
 The absence of a Transaction Monitoring Program hinders Afterpay Canada's ability to detect potentially suspicious activity and file voluntary STRs with FINTRAC or refer suspicious activity to its partner bank.
Interested Parties:
Bridget Hovancsek
 Brian Kearns
 Pascalle Castagne
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation; Policies & Procedures
Issue Action Plans:
Development and Implementation of TM Program (1225222)
 Development and Implementation of TM Program (1314268)
Closure Request Justification: Closing as IA has validated the action plan (AP closed by Kaitlin Pipitone on 11/18).

Issue Title: Lack of Clear KYC Refresh Procedures: Multi-location Sellers
Issue Description:
Square Australia’s Enhanced Customer Due Diligence (“ECDD”) Review Procedure does not specify
 the appropriate customer record required to execute the KYC refresh assessment for merchants
 operating multiple locations. As part of the ECDD review, higher risk customers / beneficial owners
 must be subject to identity verification procedures every 24 months. If a customer has Square profiles
 for multiple locations, compliance analysts need to confirm the original date of verification at the
 merchant level (rather than the location level, as subsequent locations were likely onboarded at
 different times).
 Compliance has never provided training to cover this scenario, though Internal Audit acknowledges
 that multi-location merchants comprise a relatively low portion of Square Australia’s customer base.
Issue Rating Justification: Policies & Procedures
Issue Action Plans:
MAP 1: Onboarding Case Remediation (1225185)
 MAP 2: Team Training on KYC Refresh Date (1225189)
Closure Request Justification: Both action plans validated by IA 7/9/25

Issue Title: Incomplete Methodology for Reviewing PEP Alerts - Sibling Relationships
Issue Description:
In January 2024, the Square Australia compliance team decided to exclude sibling and sibling-in-law
 relationships from the PEP screening configuration without performing a detailed risk assessment or
 obtaining legal advice. As a result, the system no longer generates alerts when customers match the
 names of siblings or siblings-in-law of officials on monitored watchlists. Regulators expect the business
 to consider PEP exposure through sibling and / or sibling-in-law relationships in situations posing
 heightened ML / TF risk (e.g., the
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectations
Issue Action Plans:
MAP 1: Risk Assessment on siblings/siblings in law of PEP (1225196)
 MAP 2: PEP Screening Criteria Update - If Applicable (1225199)

Issue Title: Failure to Follow AML/CTF Training Distribution and Training Completion Monitoring Procedures
Issue Description:
Throughout the covered period, Square Australia did not have sufficient controls in place to ensure that
 employees transferred to high-risk roles complete Enhanced Compliance Training within the 60 day
 timeframe defined in the Employee Due Diligence Program. Prior to April 2024, compliance’s process
 to identify transfers, distribute training materials, and monitor training completion status was entirely
 manual. There was no notification to the compliance team regarding any transfers to high-risk roles and
 the person responsible for managing the training was out on leave without replacement; as a result,
 one individual who transferred into a high-risk role as of March 2023 did not complete the required
 training until November 2023.
 As of April 2024, Square Australia automated the training process such that when a transfer happens
 and the employee’s team information gets changed in Workday, training will be automatically sent out.
 

 Reminders of training are set up and if the employee fails to finish the training within the required
 timeframe, escalation emails will be sent to the supervisor.
Issue Rating Justification: Policies & Procedures
Issue Action Plans:
MAP 1: Enhanced Compliance Training (1225206)
 MAP 2: Automatic Enhanced Compliance Training (1225218)

Issue Title: Lack of Documentation Evidencing Management Review of new AUSTRAC Guidance
Issue Description:
Square Australia has not established sufficient control to make sure all assessments addressing new
 AUSTRAC guidance are being documented and approved. The Square Australia compliance team
 maintains a register to track the assessments performed over new AUSTRAC guidance and to ensure
 timely review and approval of the assessments and incorporation of the guidance into the AML
 program. However, there is no control in place for ensuring the completeness of the register. As a
 result, Internal Audit noted that the assessments for two new AUSTRAC guidance issued during the
 cover period, (i) “Data breaches and AML/CTF considerations” and (ii) “Working together to prevent
 the sexual exploitation of children”, were not included in the register and thus were not reviewed and
 approved timely by the Compliance Lead. The Square Australia Compliance team has concluded in
 the assessments that there is no impact and no program update is needed for the new guidance and
 they added these two assessments in the register and obtained the Compliance Lead’s approval after
 Internal Audit raised the observation.
Issue Rating Justification: Policies & Procedures
Issue Action Plans:
MAP 1: AUSTRAC Regulation Assessments (1225228)
 MAP 2: AUSTRAC Register Annual Review (1225232)

Issue Title: Absence of Consumer Identity Verification
Issue Description:
Afterpay Canada does not perform identity verification procedures on Consumers. While Afterpay Canada collects certain information from Consumers at onboarding, including full name, address, date of birth, email, phone number and linked debit/credit card, it does not have procedures in place to use this information to verify the Consumer’s identity. In addition, there are no procedures to validate this information, which can result in inaccurate KYC records.
 

 Potential Impact:
 Without verifying the identities of customers, Afterpay Canada may unknowingly onboard consumers with higher ML/TF risks or whose true identity is unknown.
Interested Parties:
Pascalle Castagne
 Safiya Adenekan
 David Yu
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation

Natalie Katz will still be the issue owner with oversight from Deep Bedi. @deep is not in the Protecht system at this time, but will discuss with the AP CA team about whether we need to update this issue accordingly.
 MAP 2: Noting that the Risk Acceptance memo has been updated per Internal Audit's request and validation evidence has been provided to IA and attached in Protecht.
Issue Action Plans:
Implementation of TM (1225233)
 Implementation of IDV mechanism (1225239)
 IDV Lookback (1225243)

Issue Title: Inappropriate Alert Review SLAs
Issue Description:
The initial alert investigation SLAs defined in Square Australia’s procedures are not aligned with
 Management’s assessment of ML / TF / Sanctions risk exposure. For example, the Transaction
 Monitoring and Ongoing Customer Due Diligence queues have 24 hour closure SLAs that the
 compliance team considers overly aggressive given the nature of the company’s customer base. The
 aforementioned procedural requirements and a temporary holiday staffing shortage contributed to the
 following exceptions identified through Internal Audit’s sample testing:
 ● 3 out of 25 Transaction Monitoring case samples were cleared after the 24 hour SLA;
 ● 8 out of 25 Ongoing Customer Due Diligence case samples were cleared after the 24 hour
 SLA;
 ● 10 out of 25 Watchlist case samples were cleared after the 24 hour SLA; and
 ● 1 out of 25 Enhanced Customer Due Diligence case samples was cleared after the 7 day SLA.
Issue Rating Justification: Policies & Procedures
Issue Action Plans: SLA Adequacy Review (1225247)

Issue Title: Inadequate Consumer Risk Rating Methodology
Issue Description:
Afterpay Canada's Consumer Risk Rating (CRR) model/methodology is not designed to comprehensively risk-rate the full population of consumers based on relevant ML/TF attributes and red flags. Currently, all Afterpay Canada consumers are considered Low risk at onboarding by default; higher risk ratings are assigned only as a result of manual due diligence reviews triggered by Sanctions screening. Afterpay Canada should develop a comprehensive CRR model/methodology that considers relevant risk attributes based on management’s risk assessment(e.g., results of PEP, Adverse Media, Transaction, and Identity Verification screenings), as such methodologies are applied by other Afterpay and Block entities. 
 

 In addition, the existing CRR model/methodology documentation references specific EDD triggers such as PEP Screening, Adverse Media Screening, KYC Discrepancy, and Transaction Monitoring;in practice, controls to monitor for these triggers do not exist. 
 

 Potential Impact:
 The risk ratings assigned to consumers may be inaccurate and fail to identify higher risk customers. Higher-risk consumers are/should be subject to more frequent and robust monitoring; the existing CRR model/methodology increases the likelihood that higher risk consumers are not being subject to the appropriate level of due diligence.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation, Policies & Procedures
Issue Progress Updates: 11/12KP: validated by IA
Issue Action Plans:
Implementation of Consumer Risk Rating model (1225251)
 CRR Model/Methodology Documentation Review (1225254)
Closure Request Justification: 11/12KP: Validated by IA

Issue Title: Lack of Clear Guidance on Escalating Business Name Change Cases to Square Australia Compliance Team
Issue Description:
There was no clear guidance to the Global Compliance team regarding when to escalate business
 name change cases to Square Australia compliance team for SMR filing. The Global Compliance Team
 is responsible for monitoring business name change activities and performing an initial review of
 potentially suspicious changes. For any cases that they deemed to be suspicious, they should escalate
 the cases to the Square Australia compliance team to determine if SMR is needed. Under AU
 regulation, there is no dollar amount threshold for SMR; however, the Global Compliance team agent
 was not familiar with AU regulations and thought that similar to US, dollar amount threshold is applied
 to SMR filing. As a result, Internal Audit identified one out of 10 sample cases that were not escalated
 to the local team for SMR determination.
Issue Rating Justification: Policies & Procedures
Issue Action Plans:
MAP 1: Suspicious Merchant Cases Lookback (1225257)
 MAP 2: Case Escalation Procedure Update (1225261)
 MAP 3: Enhanced Compliance Training for Agents Working on AU Cases (1225271)

Issue Title: Inadequate Merchant Risk Rating Methodology
Issue Description:
Afterpay Canada's Merchant Risk Rating model/methodology is not designed to comprehensively risk-rate the full population of merchants based on relevant ML/TF attributes and red flags.. Currently, all Afterpay Canada Merchants are considered Low risk at onboarding by default; higher risk ratings are assigned only as a result of manual due diligence reviews triggered by Sanctions screening, Adverse Media, or Prohibited Merchant Verticals. Afterpay Canada should develop a comprehensive Merchant Risk Rating model/methodology that considers relevant risk attributes based on management’s risk assessment. For example, relevant risk attributes for merchants could include PEP Screening, High-Risk Merchant Verticals, KYC Discrepancy, and Transaction Monitoring, which are attributes commonly considered due to regulatory expectations and industry practices and are also considered by other Afterpay and Block entities.
 

 In addition, Internal Audit noted that although the Merchant Risk Rating model/methodology lists additional EDD triggers such as PEP Screening, High-Risk Merchant Verticals, KYC Discrepancy, and Transaction Monitoring, in practice none of these triggers are being monitored/screened by implemented controls. 
 

 Potential Impact:
 The risk ratings assigned to Merchants may be inaccurate and fail to identify higher risk Merchants. Higher-risk Merchants are subject to more frequent and robust monitoring, the existing Merchant risk rating model/methodology increases the likelihood that higher risk Merchants are not being subject to the appropriate level of due diligence.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation, Policies & Procedures
Issue Progress Updates: 5/27KP: Both MAPs tracking toward 6/30 due date per slack: https://square.enterprise.slack.com/archives/C08H6T9QBFE
Issue Action Plans:
Implementation of Merchant Risk Rating model (1225262)
 MRR Documentation Review (1225265)
Closure Request Justification: 11/11/2025KP: MAP 2 validated by internal audit; changed status to closed.

Issue Title: Inaccurate Risk Assessment Result
Issue Description:
The 2023 Afterpay Canada AML/CTF and Sanctions Risk Assessment Control Effectiveness ratings appears to be overstated and residual risk therefore understated, due to incorrect answers in the Risk Assessment Questionnaire. Specifically, Afterpay Canada input "Satisfactory" or "Adequate" scores for the following questions in the Control Effectiveness section of the BNPL Risk Assessment questionnaire even though Afterpay Canada does not in fact have controls for these areas: 
 Process to Identify PEPs (Question 20), 
 Customer Identification Verification Processes (Question 21), 
 Process to Validate a Customer's Ownership/Control of a Payment Instrument, 
 Transaction Monitoring Scenarios (Question 92), and 
 Documentation of Models, Logic, and Thresholds for TM systems (Question 73). 
 

 Further, the Risk Assessment process does not include adequate quality checks and challenge processes which should have identified the incorrect answers in the Risk Assessment Questionnaire. The answers in the Risk Assessment Questionnaire were reviewed and approved by the Afterpay Canada AML Officer, but the incorrect answers were not identified.
 Potential Impact:
 

 An inadequate quality check and challenge process for the Risk Assessment Questionnaires could result in inaccurate responses being input in the Afterpay Canada Risk Assessment Questionnaires, resulting in miscalculations in the Risk Assessment and therefore inaccurate reporting to Afterpay Canada's Board of Directors and Management as to risk assessment results. This may result in Management not having a true understanding of Afterpay Canada’s AML/CTF and Sanctions risk profile and quality of controls.
Interested Parties:
Pascalle Castagne
 Safiya Adenekan
 Amit Bhushan
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation, Policies & Procedures
Issue Progress Updates:
6/13KP: Reached out to @miread for update.
 

 4/3KP: Extension request approved on 4/2/2025 by John Alfano. See extension request evidence in attachments. 
 

 4/2KP: Followed up about John Alfano's approval of extension request on 3/26, 3/27, 3/28, and 3/31. On 4/1, John was tagged for review.
Issue Action Plans: Enhanced Risk Assessment Process (1225272)
Closure Request Justification: 6/26KP: uploaded evidence + closure memo at both action and issue level. submitting issue and action for closure.

Issue Title: Inadequate Training Content
Issue Description:
Training Content
 The Afterpay Canada AML Training content is not tailored to Afterpay Canada's business model and AML Program. Specifically, it does not include the following key aspects:
 

 1. A description of Afterpay Canada's KYC requirements for Consumers and Merchants
 2. Instructions/contacts for escalating AML or Sanctions issues
 3. Descriptions of the types of potential Sanctions violations or issues that should be escalated to Afterpay Canada's Sanctions Team
 4. Descriptions of the specific AML red flags applicable to the Buy Now Pay Later product
 

 Training Knowledge Tests
 While there is a testing component for the Global AML training, Afterpay Canada does not have a testing component for the Afterpay Canada AML Training Program. It is common industry practice that AML training includes a testing component to ensure the recipient understands the importance of AML, regulatory requirements, company's policies and procedures and how they can support mitigating the AML risk.
 

 Potential Impact:
 Afterpay Canada employees may not be fully aware of the risks associated with the Afterpay product or may not understand their roles and responsibilities with respect to executing the Afterpay Canada AML Program. 
 Afterpay Canada employees do not have the requisite knowledge / skills to execute the AML program properly
Issue Rating Justification: Policies & Procedures
Issue Action Plans: Development and Implementation of Canada-specific AML Training (1225278)

Issue Title: Lack of System Controls and Oversight in Customer Reimbursement Processes
Issue Description:
Customer reimbursements are issued through Regulator or Toolbox to a customer's Cash App account when applicable. However, Toolbox and Regulator are not configured to prevent Customer Success (CS) or Cash Complaint Operations Team (CCOT) staff from issuing reimbursements exceeding their delegated authority. Currently, the systems allow staff to issue reimbursements up to the full value of a transaction. Some workflows even allow staff to input arbitrary reimbursement amounts. While staff are expected to escalate for approval via Slack channels, this is not enforced systematically.
 

 Additionally, there are thousands of users, including 933 full timers and nearly 6,000 BPO or contractors, who can issue reimbursements on any customer account without limitation by assigned cases.
 

 Risk
 ● Operational: Complaints staff may issue reimbursements exceeding the necessary amount.
 ● Financial: This exposes the company to financial losses.
 ● Fraud: There is heightened risk of fraud that the staff with access to issue customer reimbursements could issue unauthorized payouts to complainants who may be friends or family members.
Issue Rating Justification: Policies and Procedures
Issue Action Plans:
Development of Customer Reimbursement Policy (1225290)
 Develop Reimbursement Report and Establish Recurring Distribution (1225295)

Issue Title: Complaint Procedure Violations and Inaccurate Complaint Log Entries
Issue Description:
IA noted exceptions where complaints were not
 adequately addressed or were only partially addressed by
 Customer Success (CS) or Cash Complaint Operations
 Team (CCOT). Key themes included:
 ● Agent behavior violations such as failure to perform
 investigation and provide formal complaint
 response (FCR), failure to flag a high-risk complaint
 as Tier 1, and failure to escalate after customers
 requested to speak to a manager two or more
 times.
 ● Past due Attorney General (AG) response due to
 missed automated response and follow-up.
 ● Instances where support teams either
 misunderstood the complaints, were noticeably
 confused by the information provided, lacked the
 knowledge to address customer concerns, or
 provided incorrect information to the customer,
 which was not identified until the customer filed a
 CFPB investigation with related complaints.
 When a complaint is identified and / or resolved, several
 fields must be completed in the complaint log. However,
 IA noted incorrect designation as "Non-Complaints" and
 inaccurate values for fields such as “Resulting Action”,
 "Tier" and “Legal Tag” and "Redress Paid"
 This is due to:
 1. The introduction of new requirements (e.g., updated
 complaint categories, escalation criteria, and response
 protocols) as part of Cash App’s alignment with the Block
 Global Complaint Policy and Program, effective April 1,
 2024. The process for managing and resolving complaints
 is highly manual and often subjective, requiring CS to
 make judgment calls without standardized or automated systems to guide them. This reliance on individual
 discretion increases the likelihood of inconsistencies and
 errors, particularly when handling complex or high-priority
 complaints.
 2. Lack of clear instructions in current documentation,
 such as Knowledge Articles and procedures, for managing
 complex or ambiguous scenarios and ensuring accurate
 responses.
 3. Limited review in the current Cash Customer Operations
 (CCO) Quality Assurance (QA) process. Only a limited
 number of random samples per staff member are
 reviewed each month, which may not adequately capture
 all errors given the volume and complexity of complaints.
 Risk
 Failure to ensure all complaints are identified and resolved
 in an effective manner, as required by Block's policy, may
 lead to unsatisfactory customer experience and
 non-compliance with regulatory expectations.
 Inaccurate data entries in complaint logs undermine the
 effectiveness of complaint tracking and reporting, and
 more importantly, hinder effective root cause analysis,
 making it difficult to identify and address underlying
 issues.
 See applicable regulatory requirements, internal policies and procedures in Appendix I.
 

 Mitigating Circumstances
 1. Most cases have minimal or no impact on the overall
 customer experience.
 2. The identified issues are isolated and do not indicate a
 systemic failure in the complaints handling process.
 3. These themes are encompassed within the areas being
 evaluated in the CCO QA process at the staff level and have
 been recognized by the Quality Control (QC) team as
 trends for improvement.
 4. The CCO Quality Management (QM) team conducts
 Monthly Inquiry Reviews. This baseline review specifically
 focuses on inquiries (CF1 cases) that were not identified as
 containing complaint language and do not have a
 complaint record logged in relation to the customer
 interaction. Additionally, the QM team conducts ad-hoc
 reviews, with the most recent review assessing the
 accuracy of Primary Issue Tags applied to complaints.
 5. As of 10/1/2024, Team Leads perform a Complaint Quality
 review for each agent on their team per month.
Issue Rating Justification:
Regulatory Expectation
 Policies and Procedures
Issue Action Plans:
Attorney General (AG) Complaints (1225298)
 Partnerships and Training (1250904)
 Knowledge and Training Enhancement (1250907)
 Quality Assurance Improvement (1250910)

Issue Title: Certificates of Compliance are not being provided to PCF and CF employees in line with CBI Fitness and Probity guidance/standards and SIL Standard Operating Procedures.
Issue Description:
Issue Description: 
 GCMT conducted testing over 57 SIL PCF's and CF's for the period June 1, 2023 to September 30, 2024 to confirm if a Certificate of Compliance was issued prior to their appointment and by the end of September 2024. 
 

 New hire
 GCMT identified that for 9 CFs out of 11 SIL PCF's and CF's (81% exception rate) a Certificate of Compliance was not issued prior to appointment. (Note for the new hire sample tested, we identified that the due diligence checks were completed and reviewed by HRBP as required in the F&P Policy and Procedure).
 

 Existing hires
 GCMT identified that for 45 out of 47 SIL PCF's and CF's (96% exception rate) a Certificate of Compliance was not issued by the end September 2024, for existing CFs and PCFs per requirements of the F&P Policy.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: This issue is low due to all the remaining F&P checks being completed.
Issue Action Plans: A retrospective Certificate of Compliance will be completed for each new and existing CF and PCF. The annual Certificate of Compliance was completed and reported to the board in December 2024. (1226490)

Issue Title: The CF and PCF register is not being maintained/updated in accordance with the CBI it and Proper (F&P) Standards and the register is not being circulated to business leaders for approval on a quarterly basis in accordance with the SIL F&P Procedure. As a result, the board was not provided with annual confirmation that the F&P checks were completed and all PCFs and CFs were compliant with the standards.
Issue Description:
Issue Description: 
 1- GCMT noted the following observations relating to the requirement to ensure the CF and PCF register is accurate and up to date:  
 

 Blank/Non-Updated Fields
 For 54 out of 58 current SIL PCFs and CFs (93%) the “Adverse Media Findings field” has not been updated (i.e left blank). (noting that for our sample testing of adverse media, we identified that these checks were completed).
 For 21 out of 58 current SIL PCFs and CF (90%) the “Outsourced Roles Fields” has not been updated and left blank.
 For 10 out of 58 current SIL PCFs and CFs (17%), the 'Start Date with Square' and 'Start Date in Regulated Role' has not been updated and marked as "TBC". 
 Inaccurate Fields Updated
 CBI Approval date was not updated for Eoin Shields whereby CBI approval was obtained on 29th July 2024. In addition, the short term and long-term successor field has not been updated in the register. 
 Celine Singleton's annual recertification has been marked as complete for '2022' and '2023', but this is not correct as the PCF was not at the firm during this period.
 

 2 - GCMT identified that the SIL F&P procedural requirement to circulate the CF and PCF Register to business leads of CFs and PCFs on a quarterly basis for review and approval is not being completed.
 

 3. The SIL F&P procedural requirement to provide an annual confirmation to the SIL Board that the F&P checks are complete and all PCFs and CFs are compliant with the standards was not provided. 
 

 GCMT reviewed the SIL Board Minutes from December 2023 and confirmed that an update was provided to the Board on the progress of the F&P checks. However, GCMT noted that there was no subsequent update provided to the SIL Board Q1 2024 meeting to confirm F&P checks had been completed and all PCFs and CFs were compliant. As the checks in the register were not completed, the board could not be provided with an annual confirmation that all F&P checks were completed and PCF’s and CF’s were compliant because the status was incomplete and not known in December or in Q1 2024.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

RCA Description: People
Issue Rating Justification: Low. Internal Process
Issue Action Plans: GCMT identified that the QA Policy details an inaccurate void QA form process (1227143)
Closure Request Justification:
Comment by Donna Adams: Action Owner provided the responses to the Actin Plan for Ref #1. Also, review of updated QA Forms and QA process will be conducted during the upcoming Q2 2025 Square Banking - Credit Card: Complaints review for validation.  
 1. The Square CM team has addressed this issue, which originally stemmed from an initial ramp-up period after the new 
  program launched and the design and update of a QA form to fit the updated program requirements. 
  --Response by Reuben Malicoat, Action Owner: The nuance and subject matter requirements for this role result in an 
  approximately 6 month period for new team members to achieve a moderate level of understanding and autonomy. 
  For this reason, the team does not QA new team members for the first 90 days, and instead they work with existing 
  team members and have much more oversight. It would be counterproductive, given the ramp up period required, to 
  subject new team members to QA - which they likely would not pass - only to have to performance manage them out 
  due to failed QA scores. 
   
  In the instance noted for AU on this action item, the CM team hired and onboarded 2 new CMs for that market, and 
  they were subject to the policy described above. 
 

 2. CM leadership will monitor these AIs and increase review of completed QA forms to ensure areas of opportunity 
  highlighted are addressed. 
  --Response from Reuben Malicoat: The CM team has removed the ambiguity around this action item, which did not have 
  a specific set date for QA completion. This was addressed during the investigation/audit period, where it was 
  determined that QA for the preceding month would be completed no later than the 24th day of the following month. 
  This ensures QA is completed consistently. 
 

  Additionally, the CM team will look to enhance the QA form further by including the suggested additions on or by the 
  action implementation date.

Issue Title: The Anti-Bribery and Corruption Risk Assessment has not been reviewed and kept current. [Square and Cash App - UK]
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement / Best Practice Guidance;
 There is an expectation for firms to have an updated Risk Assessment in place, which is periodic, informed, documented and kept up to date. The below requirements were tested.
 

 FCA Handbook, Guidance and Principles, Risk Assessment - FCG 2.2.4, FGC 6.2.2. 
 FCA Handbook, Guidance and Principles, Dealing with third parties - FCG 6.2.4
 The Bribery Act 2010, Principle 3 - Risk Assessment
 The Bribery Act 2010, Principle 4 - Due Diligence 
 

 Specifically, the Bribery Act 2010 - Principle 3, Risk Assessment states that the commercial organization assesses the nature and extent of its exposure to potential external and internal risks of bribery on its behalf by persons associated with it through performing a risk assessment. The assessment is periodic, informed and documented. 
 

 Finding Description:
 The most recent Global Anti-Bribery and Corruption Risk Assessment was completed in 2021. The Assessment rated inherent and residual risks as mostly high/medium and included recommendations to reduce the residual risk level. However, the Risk Assessment has not been kept current and the original recommendations have not been actively followed up to inform ABC activities, controls and ultimately risk levels. At the time that the Risk Assessment was completed, there were no formal requirements and timelines in place for the business to remediate the risks and recommendations that were identified in the assessment. 
 

 As an example, the risk identified for Third Party Invoicing with critical recommendations has not been closed off. This risk has linkages to the Financial Conduct Authority guidelines section 6.2.4 Dealing with Third Parties whereby "We (the FCA) expect firms to take adequate and risk-sensitive measures to address the risk that a third party acting on behalf of the firm may engage in corruption".
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Anti-Bribery and Corruption / Gifts and Entertainment Review (1013400)
Issue Rating Justification: The issue has been rated as moderate, in line with the Result Rating matrix. The moderate rating in the matrix applies to non compliance with procedures and policies and is for an issue which cannot be corrected in the ordinary case of business. As an updated ethics assessment needs to be performed, with coverage of anti-bribery and corruption, therefore the moderate rating is appropriate as this is a regulatory requirement (Per the UK Bribery Act 2010).
Issue Progress Updates:
1) Perform a Risk Assessment for Anti-Bribery and Corruption on an periodic basis as expected by the regulatory obligations listed in the The Bribery Act 2010 (Principle 3 - Risk Assessment). 
 2) Implement a process to track the risk items which are identified during the Risk Assessment and assign designated risk owners to each item to ensure there is a plan to remediate the risks.
Issue Action Plans: Anti Bribery and Corruption / Gifts and Entertainment 2023 Review - Ethics Risk Assessment for 2024 (1058180)

Issue Title: Afterpay Canada does not have a formalized process to respect Sole Proprietor’s rights or for us to inform and serve them in French.
Issue Description:
Background

 1. Individuals purchasing goods for speculation purposes,
 2. Individuals entering into contracts relating to the management of a residential building,
 3. Professionals practicing as sole proprietors, and
 4. Skilled tradesmen/artisans.
 

 Issue Description:
 Merchant onboarding has a process in place to flag Quebec Sole Proprietors (with a
 compliance note) during business verification. These Sole Proprietors are emailed as a follow
 up to be asked;

 2. Have they no or only a few employees that are family members?
 These clarifying questions point to Category 4 - Skilled Tradesman/Artisans but not all sole proprietor categories outlined in the background above. Irrespective of the response received from the Sole Proprietor, there is no control or formalized process to action these responses in any way.

Issue Title: Square Australia Screening Gap Due to Improper Bridger Settings
Issue Description:
On December 5, 2024, Global Sanctions Compliance (“GSC”) identified an error in the configurations associated with the AURW predefined search in Bridger. This predefined search is used to screen Square Australia merchants against relevant Australian sanctions watchlists. The error was identified when a GSC Program Manager (“GSC PM”) was preparing amendments to the AURW predefined search in line with recommendations made as part of the Global Sanctions Annual List Review.
 

 The GSC PM identified that the WorldCompliance Sanctions & Enforcement list within the predefined search had “Australia” selected under the Country option. Because of the way the list is configured (including the use of “AND” as the Top Group Operator), the “Australia” selection means that Block is currently only screening against parties on the selected watchlists if those watchlist parties have an address in Australia. This was confirmed with LexisNexis via email on Dec 4, 2024.
 

 The root cause of the issue appears to be knowledge/training deficiencies regarding LexisNexis setup and configurations. The predefined search was initially established in June 2019 and did not include the Country Selection, but when it was updated in April 2020 the Country selection was enabled. . As a result of this issue, Square Australia is not fulfilling its regulatory expectation to perform sanctions screening against the entire Australia Consolidated Sanctions List. While the risk is somewhat mitigated due to some level of overlap between the Australian Sanctions List and the UN Sanctions List (which Square Australia merchants are screened against), the potential for an Australian sanctions violation still presents a heightened risk to Square Australia.
RCA Description: People- The root cause of this issue can be attributed to People. Specifically, it appears there were knowledge/training deficiencies when the predefined search was configured. Due to these knowledge/training deficiencies in how to appropriately configure Bridger, there is an existing gap in sanctions screening of Australia merchants.
Issue Rating Justification: This issue is rated High because sanctions watchlist screening is a regulatory expectation. As a result of this issue, Square Australia has failed to fulfill its regulatory expectation to perform sanctions screening against the entire Australia Consolidated Sanctions List. This presents a risk that Square Australia has onboarded or maintained a customer that is prohibited under Australian sanctions law. Violations of Australian sanctions laws and regulations can result in penalties including imprisonment for individuals and substantial fines for individuals and entities.
Issue Progress Updates: The GSC PM informed the Square Sanctions Ops Lead, Square Australia Ops Lead, and Square Compliance Country Lead of this issue on Dec 16, 2024. Additional discussion occurred async.
Issue Action Plans:
Capacity Planning (1233745)
 Amendment of AURW Predefined Search (1233748)
 Training (1233752)
 Predefined Search Review (1233755)
 Testing (1233758)
Closure Request Justification:
Supporting documentation related to all action plans has been reviewed and approved. All action plans closed.
 

 Overall issue approved to be closed on May 6, 2025

Issue Title: Control 4 – Documentation of Back-up Contacts and process documentation to support the completion of tasks by non-DRI’s Needs Improvement in Operation to meet control objective
Issue Description:
GCMT noted that the current control Needs Improvement to meet the stated control objective “To ensure in the case of unexpected leave of key employees, that a back-up plan is available to ensure PayUK obligations, requirements and tasks are duly and timely met.”
 

 Part A – Back-Up Contacts
 GCMT reviewed the “FPS Contact Data – Square Up Europe” Spreadsheet which was established with the purpose of recording a minimum of 2 Square contacts per contact area, for PayUK personnel utilization GCMT noted a 78% compliance rate (7 of the 9 contact areas) whereby a minimum of 2 Square contacts were documented.

 1. Inter FPS Payment Queries
 a. CBR+POO Truncated Data – No Square contact listed
 b. FPS Profanity Contacts – Only one Square contact listed
 2. KEK and Working Keys Management – Only one Square contact listed
 

 Part B – Procedures and Process Documents for Back-Up Personnel
 GCMT reviewed the PayUK Player sheet to document the tasks where a procedure/policy or standard operating procedure would be expected to be required for another individual within the same team/same skill set to complete the documented task and to determine the following:
 1. Is there currently documentation attached/noted to the activity/task in the PayUK player sheet?
 2. Is the documentation currently noted in the PayUK player sheet aligned to the activity and sufficient?
 

 Of the 16 items (activity/task/requirements) currently documented within the PayUK Player Sheet, GCMT noted that it would be reasonable to expect a policy, procedure, SOP, or additional information to be provided for 11 of these items.
 

 GCMT noted the following: 
 3 items within the General Category with no documentation or information provided. (27%)
 4 items which had attached documentation can be enhanced to ensure all information is documented in the event a Backup person would be required to complete the activity (36%)

Issue Title: The Id Theft - Frozen Balance process lacks clear instructions, quick texts, and proper case routing tools, preventing effective QC and QA oversight.
Issue Description:
The QC team identified several blockers in the Id Theft - Frozen Balance process:
 There are no detailed work instructions for handling cases once they are transferred from Customer Service to the Frozen Balance queue, with current practices relying on informal knowledge sharing.
 There is no established process for managing Frozen Balance claims when the source of the freeze cannot be identified by the ID Theft team or stakeholders in the relevant Slack channel.
 There are no official Quicktexts available for consistent communication with customers.
 Tooling limitations on the Customer Service side prevent proper case routing, as they lack access to necessary information in Toolbox to direct cases appropriately once the ID Theft team identifies the source of the freeze.
 There are no internal SLA expectations for the Law Enforcement and Investigation teams to provide guidance and next steps to the identity theft agent working frozen balance cases.
 These issues collectively prevent QC and QA from providing effective oversight.
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: The issue relates to an internal control or process that is associated with low-risk activity (includes instances in which controls or other safeguards are noted to be absent).
Issue Progress Updates: 8/14/25 - evidence package and all supporting artifacts reviewed. - Charlotte Vasi
Issue Action Plans: The Id Theft - Frozen Balance process lacks clear instructions, quick texts, and proper case routing tools, preventing effective QC and QA oversight. (1235076)

Issue Title: REF 3 - SEL Transaction Monitoring, KYC Refresh, Enhanced Due Diligence, Suspicious Activity Reporting Q4 2024 - No SSP request sent for some accounts as per KYC Refresh process, and no formalised process in place for the approved PEPs without enhanced monitoring.
Issue Description:
Guidance Requirements:
 Section 5.5.1 of The Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG) Guidance (2023 Revised Version), Part 1, states that a firm must apply EDD measures on a risk-sensitive basis in any situation which by its nature can present a higher risk of money laundering or terrorist financing. As part of this, a firm may conclude, under its risk-based approach, that the information it has collected as part of the customer due diligence process (see section 5.3) is insufficient in relation to the money laundering or terrorist financing risk, and that it must obtain additional information about a particular customer, the customer’s beneficial owner, where applicable, and the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship.
 

 Section 5.5.13 of the The Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG) Guidance (2023 Revised Version), Part 1, states that individuals who have, or have had, a high political profile, or hold, or have held, public office, can pose a higher money laundering risk to firms as their position may make them vulnerable to corruption. This risk also extends to members of their immediate families and to known close associates. PEP status itself does not, of course, incriminate individuals or entities. It does, however, put the customer, or the beneficial owner, into a higher risk category. The level of risk associated with any PEP, family member or close associate (and the extent of EDD measures to be applied) must be considered on a case-by-case basis. 
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT reviewed a sample of alerts (period January through August 2024) created for the below queues and noted the following:
 ➤ KYC Refresh
 → compl_square_eu_uk_pos_ccrr_ongoing_v1_a: No Square Secure Profile (SSP) sent for 3 out of 40 alerts reviewed.

 https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1o9NzW1neP77LJKLrEPG55PfTGjVqULrF/edit?gid=1223055687#gid=1223055687
 

 ➤ SEL Seller PEP & AM Screening Procedure 
 GCMT noted that the PEP Enhanced Monitoring section of the procedure states that PEPs can be approved with or without enhanced monitoring and approved PEPs will be subject to ongoing enhanced monitoring. However, the procedure does not include the process for approved PEPs without enhanced monitoring.

Issue Title: REF 3 - SIL Transaction Monitoring, KYC Refresh, Enhanced Due Diligence, Suspicious Activity Reporting Q4 2024 - No SSP sent to some customer as per KYC Refresh Process. No formalised process for the approved PEPs without enhanced monitoring. Gaps in the IE, FR, ES PEPs tracker
Issue Description:
Guidelines Requirement:
 Section 5.5 of the Anti-Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism Guidelines for the Financial Sector (Revised 23 June 2021) states that Sections 37 to 39 of the CJA 2010 prescribes a number of circumstances in which Firms are required to apply EDD measures, including the following:
 ● Where the customer, or the customer’s beneficial owner, is a politically exposed person (PEP);
 ● Where a Firm enters into a correspondent relationship with a respondent institution from a non-EEA state;
 ● Where a Firm deals with a customer (whether a natural person or legal entity) established in high-risk third countries; and
 ● To a business relationship or transaction that they have identified as presenting a higher degree of risk.
 

 Section 5.6.2 of the Anti-Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism Guidelines for the Financial Sector (Revised 23 June 2021) states that Firms should put appropriate policies and procedures in place clearly setting out the timelines for obtaining senior management sign-off.
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT reviewed a sample of alerts (period January through August 2024) created for the below queues and noted the following:
 ➤ KYC Refresh
 → compl_square_eu_uk_pos_ccrr_ongoing_v1_a: No Square Secure Profile (SSP) sent to 5 out of 15 accounts.
 The transactional data testing is available here:
 https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1WHO7Si-ttEUzLsSnbw_xVnus4LDzJWVP/edit?gid=1223055687#gid=1223055687
 

 ➤ SIL Square EEA PEP Screening Procedure
 The PEP Enhanced Monitoring section of the SIL Square EEA PEP Screening Procedure states that a PEP can be approved with or without enhanced monitoring, however the procedure does not state the difference between the PEP approval with monitoring and without monitoring. In addition, the EU/UK Compliance Tracker (IE, FR, and ES Seller SAN & PEP Watchlist tabs) does not include the escalation outcome (i.e., PEP approved with or without monitoring).
 

 ➤ Approved PEPs Tracker
 GCMT also noted that the EU/UK Compliance Tracker (IE and ES Seller SAN & PEP Watchlist tabs) do not include the PEP approver name, therefore it's not possible to determine who approves the PEPs. Further, the EU/UK Compliance Tracker (IE, FR and ES Seller SAN & PEP Watchlist tabs) do not include the PEP approval date, therefore it's not possible to determine if a PEP is approved within the 7 day SLA as per the MLRO Escalation Process document.
