Issue Title: 1. Square Complaint Management's current practices for the closure of escalated complaints does not meet regulatory requirements which impacts the timely provision of a formal IDR response.
Issue Description:
The Flag a Complaint form on Salesforce is currently set up to track the resolution time of a complaint from the date when Complaint Management acknowledges the complaint to complaint closure. However, this does not align with RG271.35 where the IDR process begins from when the complaint is identified at frontline, not when the complaint is escalated to Complaints Management. 
 

 The Complaints Management team is using the "Closed Date" field on the Flag A Complaint form to record the date they reviewed the complaint. However this is misaligned to definition of complaint closure as outlined in RG 271.73 and Block GCP that states that a complaint is closed only when the complaint has been resolved to a complainant’s satisfaction, a final response has been provided or when the business determines that the complaint issue has been addressed and no new action will be taken. 
 

 Additionally, GCMT notes that the AU Complaints Management Runbook does not provide a clear definition of the "Closed Date" or specify when a complaint is considered resolved as per ASIC RG 271.73 or the Block GCP. 
 

 GCMT tested 40 samples of complaints flagged and noted 11 instances where the Closed Date was recorded on the same date Complaint Management reviewed the complaint case:
 - For 7 instances, the complaint was recorded as closed following confirmation that the complaint had been resolved by the CSA/SAS teams at an earlier date 
 - For 4 instances, the complaint was recorded as closed despite the complaint still being managed by SAS/CSA with ongoing Seller communication
 

 As a result, the dates currently recorded for acknowledgment and closure are only reflective of Complaint Management and does not account for any prior interactions between Customer Success and/or SAS as part of the customer's Complaint Journey with Square Australia as required by RG 271.35. As ASIC calculates the time to resolve as Date Closed - Date Open, the current practices applied by Complaint Management for the "Open (Acknowledgement)" and "Closed Date" may be impacting the accuracy of Square's complaint resolution times for escalated complaints, reported to ASIC. 
 

 Early closure impacts the provision of formal IDR response - 5 business day rule
 Of the 40 complaints tested, GCMT also identified 4 instances (out of 5 samples where an IDR response was required past 5-business days) where Complaints Management failed to provide an IDR response when the complaint was closed after the 5-business-day timeframe had passed as required by RG271.56 and 271.71. This was due to the complaint being closed by Complaint Management before the complaint was actually resolved. 
 As the Square Complaints Program follows a multi-tier IDR process as described in RG 271.103, the IDR timeframes still apply to all steps in the multi-tier process (RG271.102), including the initial frontline resolution, and not just when the complaint reaches the Complaints Management team.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2024 Q4 Square Australia Complaints Management Program (1031889)
RCA Description: Process - Misaligned process to regulatory requirements and internal standards
Issue Rating Justification: Includes any Issues representing violations of regulatory expectations
Issue Action Plans:
REF 1 - Complaints Management will update the AU Complaints Management Runbook to include the definition of “Closed Date” (1243642)
 REF 1 - Complaints Management will manually monitor complaint timelines (5-business days) (1243645)
 REF 1 - Complaints Management will submit a feature request to Engineering, to enhance tooling (1243649)
Closure Request Justification: See action plans for details

Issue Title: 3. Complaints received from complaints-au@ email address are not being flagged and responded through Salesforce
Issue Description:
In the Complaints Management Review Q4 2023, GCMT identified issues with the intake flow, and manual and undocumented processes for handling complaint handling through complaint-au@ email address. In response to the finding, Complaints Management documented the procedures for responding to non-agency complaint emails received via Salesforce in the AU Complaints Management Runbook, which were implemented in August 2024. 
 As per the updated AU Complaints Management Runbook, Complaint Managers are required to create an email case on Salesforce to respond to Non-Agency Complaint emails and flag the corresponding complaint. 
 

 Through sample testing of 20 complaints received through complaints-au@ email address, GCMT identified:
 - 10 instances (50%) where cases were not being responded to via Salesforce
 - 11 instances (55%) where complaints were not being flagged 
 

 Shortly thereafter implementing creating a case in Salesforce process, Complaints Management discontinued this process due to inefficiencies caused by email threads from various complainants to be bundled up into a single thread on Google groups, making it difficult to respond and flag the complaints effectively. Complaints Management has requested tooling enhancements where future inbound emails are automatically received into Salesforce, which would streamline the flagging process and address challenges related to the retention of documentation and responses to complaints.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2024 Q4 Square Australia Complaints Management Program (1031889)
RCA Description: Process - Initial process implemented was not suitable after trial
Issue Rating Justification: Issue is around documentation. Present a low risk to the operations of Block, or represents a limited weakness in Block’s processes or controls.
Issue Action Plans:
REF 3 - Complaints Management will ensure that data is transcribed into the Flag a Complaint form (1243652)
 REF 3 - Complaints Management to enhance tooling to capture inbound complaints (1243655)
Closure Request Justification: Refer to Action Plans for closure details

Issue Title: 4. Complaints Management are not completing Quality Assurance Reviews on Complaint Managers workflows
Issue Description:
The Complaints Quality Assurance (QA) Policy states that Complaint Programme Specialists are required to complete QA of Complaint Managers on a monthly cadence, with each month’s QA process completed before the 24th day of each month.
 

 The Complaints Management QA template specifies that "a minimum of two QA reviews per person per month" should be conducted.
 

 GCMT observed that no QA reviews were performed within the Complaints Management team during the review period. This indicates that the QA quota set by the function is not being met, rendering the Complaints Management QA control ineffective in identifying and addressing any discrepancies in a timely manner.

Issue Title: 5. Practices for data completeness and accuracy for External Dispute Resolution (EDR) / Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) Complaints can be improved
Issue Description:
Complaints Management processes External Dispute Resolution (EDR) or Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) Complaints through the Global Regulatory Intake and Triage (GRIT) board within the JIRA system. GRIT allows the Complaint Managers to record the required data points of the complaint from the receipt of the complaint until closure, store documents such as responses from AFCA or Square, and communication between stakeholders. EDR / AFCA Complaints data reporting for the SQAU Board Reports are sourced from GRIT. 
 

 EDR / AFCA complaints are also required to be recorded on Salesforce. 
 

 GCMT conducted testing on a random sample of 20 AFCA complaints to determine if the complaint was processed in accordance with the AU Complaints Management Runbook and reviewed Complaint Management alignment and consistency between complaint recording on GRIT board and Salesforce. 
 

 Of the 20 AFCA complaints tested, GCMT identified the following themes: 
 

 1. Date of Initial Acknowledgement between Salesforce and GRIT were misaligned 
 GCMT identified 5 instances from 20 AFCA complaints (25%) where the date of initial acknowledgement on Salesforce was recorded on a later date compared to the date of initial acknowledgement on GRIT. GCMT noted in practice that Complaint Managers were logging cases in Salesforce post-investigation and upon closure, resulting in the misaligned dates. 
 The inaccurate recording of the complaint acknowledgement dates on Salesforce gives the impression that we failed to meet the regulatory and Block GCP requirements to acknowledge complaint within 24 hours. However in reality, Square was compliant and adhered to the requirement.
 This impacts the calculation of the resolution times of the complaint (Closed Date minus Initial Acknowledgement Date) which Complaint Management uses for internal Service Level Agreements (SLAs) as the data is reliant on Salesforce. This makes verification of the SLAs unreliable due to the data misalignment on Salesforce.
 GCMT notes that there is no impact on the Square AU Board Report on AFCA numbers/SLAs since we obtain the data from GRIT. 
 

 Additionally, the AU Complaints Management Runbook does not define the fields that are required to be filled such as "Date of Initial Acknowledgement" or "Closed Date". 
 

 2. Resolution Date was not updated on GRIT
 GCMT identified 6 instances from 20 samples (30%) where the Resolution Date was not updated on GRIT but was updated on Salesforce. 
 This may result in inaccurate data and inefficiency in obtaining the data for complaints reporting for the SQAU Board Reports as the reports are dependent on GRIT. 
 

 GCMT noted that the issue of misalignment of data was previously raised in the Complaints Management Review in Q4 2023. The recurrence of this observation suggests that the underlying cause may be the lack of QA reviews performed on Complaint Managers since the implementation of the new Complaints Program.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2024 Q4 Square Australia Complaints Management Program (1031889)
RCA Description: Process - Lack of QA reviews performed on employees and clarity on procedural documents
Issue Rating Justification: Misalignment of data making verification of internal SLAs unreliable. Present a low risk to the operations of Block, or represents a limited weakness in Block’s processes or controls.
Issue Action Plans: REF 5 - Complaints Management will update the AU Complaints Management Runbook & QA requirement (1243658)
Closure Request Justification: Closure completed - See all action plans for details.

Issue Title: 7. The Square Digital Engagement Team has not fully implemented the Square Complaints Management Program
Issue Description:
During the review period, there were no established / documented procedures on how to flag complaints identified from social media channels or accounts owned or controlled by Square Australia such as Facebook, TikTok and Square Seller Community. 
 GCMT noted that the Digital Engagement Team (DET) employees have completed the Square Complaints Management Training (2024), however noted that complaints were not flagged in transactional testing. This will result in underreporting complaints volumes.
 

 GCMT tested 20 general social media posts/comments across SQ’s social media channels and Square Seller Community to determine DET's compliance for identifying complaints in accordance with the Square Complaints Management Program. 
 GCMT identified 8 instances (40%) where the complaint was not flagged by DET
 Of those 8 instances, 5 complaints were acknowledged by the DET team member and attempted resolution or where DET has reached out to the complainant to provide further information via Direct Messaging if the complaint was sensitive or account-specific. 
 Additionally, DET has an established Quality Assurance Program to measure the quality of service Digital Engagement is providing to Square Sellers across multiple platforms. However the DET Auditing/QA Rubric currently does not have any grading criteria to ensure that complaints are being flagged and handled.
 

 In December 2024, DET developed a document, DET Process - Sprinklr Playbook detailing procedures to respond to different types of cases ingested from social media platforms, review websites, and the Seller Community platform. The document contains a section on flagging complaints. GCMT noted the Playbook can be further enhanced to reflect the ASIC RG271, Block Global Complaints Program and Square Complaints Management Program requirements as follows, but not limited to:
 The Playbook specifies that DET "is responsible for filing complaints for cases received that fall under Tier 1". There is no requirement to flag Tiers 2, 3-escalated, and 3 complaints as required by the Square Complaints Management Program. 
 Definition of a complaint can be included into the Playbook to align with the Square Complaints Program and Block Global Complaints Program
 Additionally, for Australia specifically, the RG271.32 details that posts where the author is both identifiable and contactable are considered complaints
 The requirement to acknowledge the complaint within 24 hours as per RG271.51 & .52.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2024 Q4 Square Australia Complaints Management Program (1031889)
RCA Description: Process - lack of procedural documentation and QA program
Issue Rating Justification: Processes were established at the end of the review. Present a low risk to the operations of Block, or represents a limited weakness in Block’s processes or controls.
Issue Action Plans:
REF 7 - Update the DET Process - Sprinklr Playbook (1239030)
 REF 7 - QA Grading Criteria will be updated (1239032)
Closure Request Justification: Closed - See action plans for details

Issue Title: 8. Sales, Accounts Management and Square Loans team members have not been assigned or completed the updated Square Complaints Program training
Issue Description:
Program Requirements
 The Square Complaints Management Program Guide requires all seller-facing Square employees receive training during their initial onboarding, as well as annual recertification. Within the first 30 days of their start date new hires of the Square are assigned and required to complete a Complaint Program training delivered by the Learning and Development Team. 
 

 Issue Description 
 Square Loans 
 GCMT identified that Square Loan team members last completed the Complaints training in 2022 and have not been assigned the updated Square Complaints Program training. 
 There is currently no process to ensure that recertification of the complaints training is completed annually. 
 Sales and Accounts Management 
 SAM has established the Mastering Seller Complaints - Sales and Account Management Training module that reflects the Block Global Complaints Program and includes a procedure on how to flag complaints through go/fac form. 
 

 GCMT has identified out of 59 SAM employees:
 16 team members have not been enrolled into the course
 Of the 16 team members, 12 were new employees that commenced between July to September 2024 that were meant to complete within 30 days of their start date. 
 12 team members did not complete the course when it was assigned to them
 20 team members last completed the training in 2023 (17 team members) or 2022 (3 team members) and were not assigned for recertification.
 

 There is currently no process to ensure that recertification of the complaints training is completed annually. This observation was also raised in the Q3 2024 SIL/SEL Complaints Program Review.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2024 Q4 Square Australia Complaints Management Program (1031889)
RCA Description: Process - Lack of process to ensure annual recertification of training
Issue Rating Justification: Training related. Present a low risk to the operations of Block, or represents a limited weakness in Block’s processes or controls.
Issue Action Plans:
REF 8 - SQ Loans team to complete Complaints Training (1239039)
 REF 8 - SAM Complaints training will be scheduled annually (1239043)
Closure Request Justification: Closed - See Action Plan for details

Issue Title: 10. The Square Loans/MCA Complaints Framework, Procedures and Frequently Asked questions are not updated to be aligned with the Square Complaints Management Program
Issue Description:
Background
 Square has updated its Complaints Program to align with Block's new Global Complaints Program, which has been in effect since April 2024.
 

 Issue Description 
 GCMT identified that the following documents have not been updated to reflect and align with the revised Square Complaints Program:
 1. INTL Square Loans / MCA Complaints Framework 
 Areas requiring updates include, but are not limited to: 
 Purpose Section: The section linking various complaint program documents must be updated to reflect the Square Complaint Management Program and Block Global Complaints Program documents. 
 Global Complaint DRIs Section: This section requires to be revised to include the current DRIs 
 Training Section: Updates are required to incorporate the current complaints training module and content. 
 Escalation Channels Section: Banking Ops:Global Loans/MCA Complaint Management SOP  
  
 2. Global Loans/MCA Complaint Management SOP
 Areas requiring updates include, but are not limited to: 
 Definitions Section: Updating the definition of a complaint and removing "complaint taxonomy" definition
 Related Documents Section: The section linking various complaint program documents must be updated to reflect Square Complaint Management Program and Block Global Complaints Program documents.
 

 3. Seller FAQ document on go/fac page 
 The go/fac form used by non-CS teams such as SAM and DET to flag complaints, includes a linked Seller FAQ document detailing how to complete the complaint form. As this document is readily accessible on the page, it is more likely to be referenced than individual team SOPs or training materials on how to file complaints.  
 

 GCMT identified that the Seller FAQ document is outdated and requires to be enhanced to reflect the new complaints program requirements include, but are not limited to:
 Updating the link to the current Square Complaints Management Program 
 Include the requirement to file Tier 3 complaints 
 Updating the definitions and how to fill out the provided fields such as:
 Removing Global Taxonomy field as this requirement has been deprecated 
 Including definitions to the Severity Tier, Primary Complaint Issue, Primary Complaint Subcategory and Product fields
 Clarifying what should be included in the Description of the Complaint (example: summary of the complaint, any actions/offers made to address the complainant's concerns)
 Update the additional resources section with the updated complaint program documents to reflect the current program
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2024 Q4 Square Australia Complaints Management Program (1031889)
RCA Description: Process - Lack of review of program documentation
Issue Rating Justification: Documentation issue. Present a low risk to the operations of Block, or represents a limited weakness in Block’s processes or controls.
Issue Action Plans:
REF 10 - Square Loans will update the INTL Square Loans / MCA Complaints Framework and the Global Loans /MCA Complaint Management SOP (1238378)
 REF 10 - The Complaints Management team will update the Seller FAQ document (1239021)
Closure Request Justification: Closure completed - See all action plans for details.

Issue Title: The practices for appropriate delegation in accordance with the CBI guidance and Conduct Standards Procedure is not being followed.
Issue Description:
The practices for appropriate delegation in the CBI guidance and Conduct Standards Procedure are not being followed. For 2 PCF's tested by GCMT the following was noted: 
 

 ○ For 2 of 2 PCF's Delegation forms were not in place; and
 ○ For 1 of 2 PCF's there was no roles matrix documented to highlight the scope of their delegation. 
 

 Separately, GCMT identified that the HRBP does not have a process for obtaining a copy of the delegation form and delegation is not monitored by the HRBP via the Regulatory Governance Committee.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: This is identified as a low rated issue as the risk of non compliance is low.
Issue Action Plans: The Conduct Standards Procedure will be updated with regard to delegation and the HRBP will distribute delegation forms to all PCFs and CF1s for comepltion. (1240357)
Closure Request Justification:
The Conduct Standards Delegation process has changed whereby the relevant PCF/CF is now responsible for maintaining the Delegation Form "As part of the Conduct Standards annual notification to PCFs/CF1s, they will be reminded to complete a Delegation Form." GCMT reviewed evidence of the HRBP sending PCFs/CF1s an email to complete a Delegation Form, with a due date of the 11th April. The onus is on the PCF/CF1 to retain, maintain and update their Delegation Form where necessary, which is documented in the Conduct Standards Procedure. The HRBP will be responsible on this occasion for following up on the Delegation Forms by the deadline. Going forward, it will be the PCFs/CF1s responsibility. The annual recertification process is another control in place to trigger an update on if the PCF/CF1 has had any changes to their delegation. 
 

 Additionally, aside from the annual recertification being a trigger to update PCF/CF1s delegation forms, other controls that can be pointed to are the Conduct Standards policy that sets out the requirements around delegation, training provided to PCFs/CF1's which covers delegation, the Conduct Standards procedure and the PCF/CF1 are informed of their responsibilities around delegation via the Conduct Standard notification.

Issue Title: Monthly performance reviews and notification/follow-up of personnel changes for Pre-Approval Controlled Function and Controlled Function employees by the Human Resources Business Partner is not being conducted per the Conduct Standards Procedure.
Issue Description:
Issue:
 ○ The procedural requirement for the HRBP to meet with Leads that have direct reports in PCF/CF roles on a monthly cadence to review the performance of the PCFs/CFs in their team is not being completed. 
 ○ The procedural requirement for the HRBP to follow up a personnel change with leads does not occur.
 ○ The procedural steps that indicates that the HRBP will be notified of Personnel changes does not occur as part of the PCF CF's notification/acknowledgement as there is no facility on the form to provide this information as part of the notification/acknowledgment. We understand this is a separate process by email.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: This issue is rated as low due to limited risk of non compliance with the CBI Guidance on IAF.
Issue Action Plans: The Conduct Standards Procedure will be updated with a revised process for the quarterly confirmation from leads of PCFs and CFs on performance/conduct issues and if there are any personal changes. (1241000)
Closure Request Justification: The Action Owner confirmed with the GCMT that the remedial action for this MAP has been changed from what was initially agreed but the new proposed action fulfils the closure of the MAP. The Action Owner confirmed a discussion with HR Compliance has taken place, given the Action Owner’s role change post functionalization. Issue Title: QC identified a segment of complaints that were incorrectly downgraded to an inquiry instead of to either a Tier 2 or 3 complaint resulting in the need to reclassify those complaints and update processes and procedures.
Issue Description:
During the review of the Inquiry, Not a Complaint process, Quality Control (QC) identified several instances where complaints were incorrectly downgraded to an inquiry when in fact, true complaint language was present and should have been downgraded to a tier 2 or 3 instead.
 

 The review consisted of a sample size of 208 in which 33% (69 samples) were incorrectly downgraded to an inquiry but did in fact have Complaint language present. Currently, the documented procedures do not contain instructions to reclassify a complaint from Tier 1 to either Tier 2 or 3, but rather indicate that an agent marks the complaint as an inquiry ultimately resulting in improperly recording and tracking the complaints.
 

 Per CFPB guidance, all complaints must be captured, properly classified and tracked for reporting and trending purposes. 
 

 As the process currently stands, these specific complaints are not properly being tracked and classified.
 

 It is recommended that the process and procedures be updated to include instructions for reclassifying Complaints in order to allow for proper tracking and recording of complaints.
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: The issue relates to an internal control or process that is associated with low-risk activity (includes instances in which controls or other safeguards are noted to be absent).
Issue Progress Updates: Reviewed evidence for closure. - Charlotte Vasi (12/19/25)
Issue Action Plans: QC identified a segment of complaints that were incorrectly downgraded to an inquiry instead of to either a Tier 2 or 3 complaint resulting in the need to reclassify those complaints and update... (1242327)

Issue Title: 6. The Customer Success practices for the handling and processing of complaints can be improved to align with standard operating procedures and regulatory requirements.
Issue Description:
GCMT tested 40 complaint cases to assess CS's adherence to the SQ Wise Process Complaints [Global] Article and regulatory requirements and identified the following themes:
 Refer to Appendix A for a summary of transactional testing results by key process area. 
 

 Regulatory requirement exception
 1 instance where the CSA did not acknowledge the complaint within 24 hours. This is a non-compliance to RG271.51which states "A financial firm should acknowledge receipt of each complaint promptly. We expect that firms will acknowledge the complaint within 24 hours". 
 

 SOP requirement exceptions
 1. CSAs did not include a comprehensive summary of issue and/or any actions taken or offers made in the "Description of Complaint" field on the Flag a Complaint form (14 instances - 35%)
 As per the SQ Wise Process Complaints [Global] Article, the "Description of Complaint" field requires "detailed description of the complaint, following these guidelines:
 - Ensure it's at least 25 characters long.
 - Include the reason for selecting the specific severity tier.
 - Detail any actions or offers made to address the complainant's concerns."
 This process enables the Complaint Management team to efficiently review the cases without the need to listen into the calls or read through emails and messages. 
 

 This requirement to provide a detailed description of a complaint is a grading requirement in the CSQA Rubric. 
 Out of 40 CSQA graded tickets GCMT tested, 2 cases were flagged as a complaint by the CSA. In both cases, the CSQA Grader did not identify and address the insufficient description provided in the field as required by the rubric. 
 Additionally, GCMT noted the "How to Flag a Complaint" section within the Square Complaints Management Training (2024), does not specifically state to "detail any actions or offers made to address the complainant's concerns" in the "Description of Complaint" field.  
 

 2. Incorrect Line of Business used in the Salesforce Flag a Complaint form (5 instances - 13%)
 As detailed per the Square Complaints Handling Guide, Frontline and seller-facing teams should utilize the “Line of Business” field to select the complaint receiver’s general team. 
 Additionally, the Square Wise Process Complaints [Global] Article specifies to "Select Customer Success - Frontline from the dropdown menu" for the "Line of Business" field.
 GCMT noted five instances where CSAs were utilising the "Line of Business" field to indicate either the team associated with the complaint or the subject matter of the complaint. 
 

 3. Incorrect Complaint Tiers chosen (7 instances - 18%)
 The SQ Wise Process Complaints [Global] Article categorises complaints that present a reputational risk as Tier 1. 
 Reputational Risk is defined as "The potential harm to a company's reputation refers to the adverse impact that may occur when its actions, decisions, or the behavior of its employees or products are perceived negatively by the public, customers, or stakeholders. This type of risk goes beyond isolated incidents of negative feedback on social media or unfavourable reviews. It encompasses significant major controversies, scandals, media exposure, and any significant event that brings adverse attention to the company. Such risks can erode trust, diminish customer loyalty and lead to significant losses in business opportunities, impacting the company's long-term success and sustainability". 
 GCMT identified 5 instances where CSAs incorrectly classified the complaints involving threats to post bad reviews on social media or threats to take legal action as Tier 1 which do not align with the definition of reputational risk provided in the SOP. These complaints were subsequently corrected to Tier 3 by Complaint Managers upon review of Tier 1 complaints. 
 However, Complaint Managers are documenting the reasons for complaint tier changes in the "Investigator Notes" on Salesforce but are not consistently providing direct feedback to advocates on the incorrect tier flagging, despite the AU Complaints Management Runbook requiring feedback to be shared via the #cs-feedbackloop Slack channel or to directly with the individual who flagged the complaint. This does not effectively support CSAs in improving tiering practices. 
 GCMT notes that the #cs-feedbackloop Slack channel has been deprecated. CS has developed a CS Feedback Bot workflow as a new channel to provide feedback. 
 

 4. Other minor exceptions noted
 - 2 instances where the CSA incorrectly flagged the complaint when Square Advanced Support (SAS) accepted a Manager Escalation workflow upon the customer's request. This is a non-compliance to the SQ Wise Process Complaints [Global] Article. 
 - 1 instance where the incorrect initial complaint date was recorded. This is a non-compliance to the SQ Wise Process Complaints [Global] Article and impacts accurate reporting of IDR data to ASIC.
 

 Inconsistent CS practice to provide the complainant with the complaints-au@ email
 The Square Wise Process Complaints [Global] Article outlines that, when a complainant wishes to submit a complaint in writing, the CSA (for Australian cases only) must provide the complainant with the relevant complaints policy, which includes both the complaints email address and a physical address for submitting written complaints.
 However, in practice, the advocates are only providing the complaints-au@ email address to the complainant if they are not satisfied with the resolution as there was no further action/information that CS could have provided further. GCMT identified 5 instances from 20 where this process occurred for complaints received through the complaints email.
 Additionally, in 3 out of those 5 instances, the CSA did not flag the complaint, as required. 
 The Square Wise Process Complaints [Global] Article does not specifically emphasize that the pre-complaint macro (which includes the provision of the Square AU Complaints Policy, complaints-au email and physical address) should always be issued in conjunction with filing a formal complaint, not as a substitute for it.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2024 Q4 Square Australia Complaints Management Program (1031889)
RCA Description: Process
Issue Rating Justification: Non-key process exceptions. Present a low risk to the operations of Block, or represents a limited weakness in Block’s processes or controls.
Issue Action Plans:
REF 6 - CSQA will provide supplemental refresh/training materials for graders regarding AU complaints scoring (1243661)
 REF 6 - Customer Success will update the SQ Wise Process Complaints Article. (1243664)
 REF 6 - Complaints Management to use "snippet" for description field and provide feedback on Salesforce cases (1243667)
Closure Request Justification: Refer to closed action plans for details

Issue Title: 9. Sales and Accounts Management team members are not flagging complaints in accordance with the Square and Block Complaints Management Programs.
Issue Description:
The SAM Quality Assurance (QA) team established a process to monitor SAM general interactions for complaints in September 2024. The SAM QA Complaints Playbook requires the SAM QA team to identify and flag the complaints on behalf of SAM team members to report complaint numbers to the SQ Complaints Management team. 
 

 This approach does not align with the Square Complaints Management Program, Block's Global Complaints Program or the Mastering Seller Complaints - Sales and Account Management Training, which assigns responsibility for flagging complaints to the Sales and Account Management team members. The QA role should be limited to acting as a monitoring control to ensure employees adhere to the complaints process and providing feedback where there are policy violations. 
 

 Additionally, the QA program is only focused on reviewing phone call interactions. Other channels utilised by SAM team members such as emails or text messages are currently not being reviewed for complaints. Management has advised that the QA team is unable to monitor all SAM emails and text messages as they are not being automatically captured in Salesforce or Gong, Square’s revenue software for recording customer interactions. 
 

 GCMT performed Grade-the-Grader testing of 2 complaints that were identified by SAM QA during the review period and identified:
 - 1 complaint was identified by the grader but was not flagged through the go/fac form. 
 Grader was unable to locate the regulator ID to file a complaint using the form due to the complaint received by a previous/deactivated Seller. GCMT was able to locate the previous/deactivated Seller’s account. 
 - 1 complaint where there was a difference in grading. 
 Whilst the Grader flagged the complaint, GCMT determined that the interaction did not meet the definition of a complaint. The Mastering Seller Complaints | SAM Training, and SQ Complaints Management: Complaint Guide specifies that for a complaint to be considered it must contain at least one of the following elements:
 1. How the problem has negatively affected them (the complainant), 
 2, How the problem has led to a negative perception of our company or what it offers,
 3. A belief that our company has breached their expectation of treatment.
 

 GCMT notes that the definition of a complaint in the QA Complaints Playbook can be updated to reflect the definition outlined in The Mastering Seller Complaints | SAM Training and SQ Complaints Management: Complaint Guide.
 Note: As the QA process was only established in September 2024, GCMT was only able to test 2 complaints that were identified by SAM QA. 
 

 GCMT also tested 40 general interactions (20 Accounts Management and 20 Sales) to determine SAM team member’s compliance for identifying complaints in accordance with the Square Complaints Management Program. 
 GCMT identified 8 out of 20 Account Management (AM) general interactions where the complaints were not flagged by the AM team members but noted that the AM team members did acknowledge the complaints and attempted resolution. GCMT did not note any exceptions for the 20 Sales general interactions. 
 The non-compliance for identifying and flagging the complaints may be attributed to the deficiency in complaints training.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2024 Q4 Square Australia Complaints Management Program (1031889)
RCA Description: Process and People
Issue Rating Justification: QA process has been established. Present a low risk to the operations of Block, or represents a limited weakness in Block’s processes or controls.
Issue Progress Updates: 11/11/25 - removed Matt Lamb as Issue Owner and Senior Business Lead (Matt is no longer with the company) and replaced him with Colin Birney. Alanna Ong endorsed this update as well.
Issue Action Plans:
REF 9 - The SAM QA team will update the existing process to focus more on a feedback loop with the SAM team members (1243672)
 REF 9 - AU ENBL team to organise Complaints Training for Sales & AM (1243675)
Closure Request Justification: Refer to Closed Action Plan for details

Issue Title: Sephora Canada Customers Promo Code ACA-83
Issue Description: A promo code for 25% off (to be applied while checking out with Afterpay for customers that made a purchase with the merchant Sephora) was sent to a certain number of Canada customers while the promotion was supposed to be U.S. only. The issue was identified through a Better Business Bureau complaint (but has also been identified in the CS space) by the U.S. Compliance team; 81 customers were impacted by the issue. Despite the Afterpay Marketing team mentioning that it was an U.S.-Only promotion, the merchant Sephora displayed the promotion banner over their Canadian App. Risk generated by this type of issue could lead to a breach of contract as the merchant did not respect the terms and conditions that were approved and submitted to them by Afterpay. In terms of preventing this in the future , Afterpay Marketing team has already worked with the Sephora team to ensure they do not launch marketing/promotions without approval ever again.
RCA Description: The staff of a 3rd party merchant inadvertently provided the promo code to all customers, instead of the approved subset of customers.
Issue Rating Justification: Direct customer impact and possible consumer harm
Issue Progress Updates: 9800+ Sephora CAN customers received the promotion
Closure Request Justification: Analysis of customers who should not have received notice, but took advantage of the promotion was conducted. Eighty-one (81) were discovered. All accounts were remediated.

Issue Title: A Factoring Training assessment has not been implemented and some employees have not completed the training to comply with the Online Factoring Association Voluntary Guidelines.
Issue Description:
OFA Factoring Voluntary Guidelines Main Rules, Article 8 states Association Members must prepare in-house rules to comply with laws and regulations concerning the factoring transaction business activity, disseminate the in-house rules to users and other employees engaged in the business of factoring transaction business activity, and implement necessary training. 
 

 Training is specified as implementation of regular training sessions, and implementation of testing to assess understanding of in-house rules. 
 

 GCMT assessed Training for conformance with OFA membership guidelines and noted the following deficiencies:
 1) Testing has not been developed for Factoring training to assess employees' understanding of training content. 
 2) Per review of Training records, 39 employees that management identified as having access to the Japan MCA product did not complete training. GCMT noted that the current employment status for 23 of those individuals is terminated and according to management, training is not required for 4 of the 39 individuals based on their role. Training records should be updated and kept current to reflect training requirements for relevant employees. 
 

 Separately, GCMT identified 3 employees with access to the Japan MCA Product that were not included in the Training audience and did not complete training. Management should ensure all employees with access to the Japan MCA product complete training as recommended by the OFA.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2024 Q4; Square Japan; Merchant Cash Advance (MCA) (1031895)
RCA Description: Root cause stems from People. Recommendations for implementing training for Japan Merchant Cash Advance were not followed.
Issue Rating Justification: OFA guidelines are not explicit regulatory guidance or law; however, not following guidance can pose moderate risk to Operations in Japan.
Issue Action Plans: Training practices will be uplifted to conform with Online Factoring Association (1245646)
Closure Request Justification: Refer to related action plan (ID 1245646) record for closure details and support documentation.

Issue Title: There are no local standard operating procedures for Japan Complaints Management and Complaint Management reviews are being completed outside of the Square Complaints Management Guide Program Service Levels.
Issue Description:
During the review period, Tier 1 and Tier 2 Complaints were either not reviewed or untimely reviewed by Complaints Management. Delays in review ranged between 20 days and 71 days. The Global SLA for Complaints Management to review a flagged complaint is 1-2 business days. According to management, a Complaint Manager was not assigned to Japan during the time period when the Complaints were flagged.
 4 out of 10 Tier 1 and Tier 2 complaints were not reviewed by Complaints Management. 
 For 2 out of 10 Tier 1 and Tier 2 complaints reviewed, an explanation describing how the case was resolved was not documented. 
 For 9 out of 10 Tier 2 complaints reviewed, root cause details were not documented. 
 

 Tier 3 Complaint details are inconsistently documented in case records. According to management, Tier 3 cases are not reviewed by Complaints Management to ensure accuracy and resolution. 
 For 24 out of 30 Tier 3 complaints reviewed, an explanation describing how the case was resolved was not documented. 
 For 28 out of 30 Tier 3 complaints reviewed, root cause details were not documented. 
 

 Incomplete or missing complaint details could have downstream impact on root cause analysis and reporting. Defining procedures for Japan seller complaints will help to ensure consistency, and accuracy in complaint handling and documentation.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2024 Q4; Square Japan; Merchant Cash Advance (MCA) (1031895)
RCA Description: Root cause stems from People and Process. Expectations for documenting complaint records were not clearly understood, and standards for managing complaints for Japan are not defined and documented.
Issue Rating Justification: There is regulatory guidance for identifying, investigating and resolving complaints; however, there are no explicit laws. Failing to comply with regulatory guidance for managing complaints could pose moderate risk to Block.
Issue Action Plans:
Framework for Square Japan Complaints will be developed and Implemented. (1245653)
 Management will create training plan for frontline teammates to ensure compliance with Global Complaints Program Standards (1247605)
Closure Request Justification: Refer to the related action record (ID 1235453) for closure details and support documentation.

Issue Title: Loan Processing practices could be improved to better align with Standard Operating Procedures.
Issue Description:
A) Manually Reviewed Approved Applications
 1) Non-Seasonal Payment Processing Gaps Procedure
 If the seller has not processed for 7 or more consecutive days prior to receiving the application, send an SSP Payment Slowdown for explanation. If the seller indicates processing will resume prior to the 14 day threshold, skip the case until processing has resumed or if the seller indicates processing won't resume within the 14 day threshold, then decline. 
 

 If the seller displays material (i.e., >14 days), non seasonal payment processing gaps in the last 60 days leading up to the application, SSP for an explanation and confirm that the seller acknowledges meeting the minimum payment requirement. 
 

 For 1 out of 60 samples, a seller indicated processing will not resume greater than the 14 day threshold, and the agent moved forward with approving the application. 
 

 For 4 out of 60 samples, the seller displayed material processing gaps; however, an SSP was not sent for an explanation. Management indicated that the processing gap observed for one seller was during a holiday period and exceptions are sometimes granted. The SOP states any exceptions must be escalated to and approved by the Banking Program Managers and/or Program Manager Lead. GCMT did not observe a documented approval for the exception. 
 

 B) Manually Reviewed Declined Applications 
 1) Compliance and Seller Risk Requirements - Tax Seizure
 Applications received for accounts with a tax seizure notice from a government body should be declined for 180 days. 
 

 For 11 out of 40 samples, the application was declined for 90 days instead of 180 days. The business indicated the decline timeframe in the SOP changed from 90 days to 180 days during the review period. GCMT did not observe a change log in the SOP to demonstrate procedure changes.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2024 Q4; Square Japan; Merchant Cash Advance (MCA) (1031895)
RCA Description: Root cause stems from People. Agents responsible for the process did not follow standard operating procedures.
Issue Rating Justification: Low error rate observed presents lower risk to Block. There are no explicit laws associated to the process.
Issue Action Plans: Management will perform minor uplifts to Loan Processing practices (1245656)
Closure Request Justification: Refer to the related Action record for closure comments and remediation attachments.

Issue Title: Standard Operating Procedures could be uplifted to align with Operational and QA processes.
Issue Description:
GCMT identified opportunities to uplift procedures to align with Japan MCA Operational and QA processes: 
 

 1) For 1 out of 60 samples, a separate case was created by Japan Compliance due to a high risk of buyer claims against the seller while the MCA application was in review by Banking Ops. The agent proceeded with the review and approved the application. The merchant account was deactivated due to chargeback risk, 13 days after the application was approved. GCMT confirmed, funding was cancelled. Procedures do not currently describe expectations for agents to review accounts for open Compliance cases and escalate to the Compliance team for approval to proceed with the application review.
 

 2) For 2 out of 60 samples, the MCA application was declined by Banking Ops for terms of services violations related to the seller's splitting payments and mixing business. Procedures do not currently describe expectations for reviewing applications for terms of services violations such as splitting payments and mixing business purposes.
 

 3) Procedures should be uplifted for alignment with the Quality Assurance Grading rubric as agents are graded on the following attributes that are not described in the Standard Operating Procedure: 
 Attribute 1.3 - Did the Analyst confirm that business onboarding (where applicable) and IDV has been completed?
 Attribute 5.2 - If applicable, did the Analyst follow the correct revised offer procedures?
 

 4) GCMT tested 4 samples where the funding application was canceled and the merchant opened a new Square account. Emails confirming cancellation were sent for 2 applications, but were not sent for the other 2 applications. Business standards for cancelling applications and communicating with merchants should be clearly defined to ensure consistent execution of the process.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2024 Q4; Square Japan; Merchant Cash Advance (MCA) (1031895)
RCA Description: Root cause stems from Process. Procedures do not fully align with operational and QA processes.
Issue Rating Justification: Minor uplifts needed to operating procedures which poses lower risk to Block. There are no explicit laws associated with this process.
Issue Action Plans:
Management will uplift Standard Operating Procedures. (1245659)
 Management will update Standard Operating Procedures (1247611)
Closure Request Justification: Refer to the related action plan record ID 1247611 for validation details.

Issue Title: Lack of documented procedure in place for handling disputes related to unauthorized cash withdrawals that occur due to a lost or stolen device (explicit to Missing Deposits with a non-Disputron claim type).
Issue Description:
There is no established workflow for Customer Service (CS) to address (or Risk Operations to resolve) claims of Unauthorized Cash Outs when customers report a device as lost or stolen.  Note: This is not a CCO-wide issue, but rather tied to this specific workflow with minimal total claims.

Issue Title: Inaccurate leap year date reference found in Cash Disputes - Regulatory Timeframes for First Contact and Investigations (Reg E) Knowledge Article
Issue Description: The ‘Identifying the Dispute Claim Window’ table within the Cash Disputes - Regulatory Timeframes for First Contact and Investigations (Reg E) Knowledge Article (KA) (Version 5-7) doesn’t account for the leap year. And while Disputes has plans to depreciate this KA in 2026 once all Disputes vertices have successfully transferred to Disputron until that transfer is complete, an update to the existing KA is required.

Issue Title: SSNs and PANs in Addresses
Issue Description:
SSN and PAN-like values were documented as Addresses in Cash App Invest (“CAI”) customer records. 
 

 On December 5, 2024 DriveWealth (A U.S.-based, SEC-registered, FINRA member, and carrying broker-dealer that provides custody and brokerage services for CAI-introduced accounts) alerted CAI that records for four CAI accounts had what appeared to be social security numbers in the address field in data reported to the Customer and Account Information System (“CAIS”).  
 

 CAI requested an incident be logged with DART. A day later, CAI received a phone call from the FINRA CAT-CAIS personnel alerting it to the fact that DriveWealth reported address information containing potential social security numbers to the CAIS for the same four accounts. FINRA CAT-CAIS personnel requested that CAI remediate the addresses and submit a CAIS form disclosing how potential PII got into reported data.  
 

 Additionally, a related issue was self-identification of PAN-like values entering address fields.
 

 Following investigation, a total of 3,320 c_tokens were identified as having an SSN-like value in an address field, and 1,028 c_tokens were identified as having a PAN-like value in an address field.
RCA Description:
Prior to this issue being remediated, Cash App would suggest a list of valid and normalized addresses (using Google Maps) but not require the customer to choose or submit what is suggested. Therefore, the customer can proceed with submitting a non-valid and normalized address.
 

 While some basic checks are performed to ensure an address is satisfactory (i.e., it is not a PO box, not a general delivery address, that there are no critical blank fields, and that the state and postal code are valid) there were previously no checks beyond these that would validate customer-inputted addresses. This means that number sequences (e.g. SSNs/PANs/or otherwise) could be entered into the address fields as well as nonsensical addresses (ranging from gibberish to cross streets) .
 

 The lack of address validation controls are what allowed this issue to occur in our address collection flows.
Interested Parties:
Langston Walker
 Wesley Chow
 Sruthi Prabhu
Impacted Legal Entities:
Block, Inc.
 Cash App Investing, LLC
Issue Rating Justification: High risk issues represent violations of requirements set forth "explicitly" in law or regulatory guidance, which could result in an enforcement action via a regulator and/or a material fine. Setting this issue as High given the scrutiny from FINRA.
Issue Action Plans: PAN-like data in address spaces (1342659)
Closure Request Justification: On December 13, 2024 the engineering team introduced a system adjustment that prohibited the entry of numbers, greater than 9 digits, into the "address 2" field, and city and state fields, as the "zip code" section was already limited to 5 digits. In January 2025, the impacted addresses were corrected internally by removing SSN or PAN-like data without need for intervention by users. The data rectification was confirmed as complete on January 31, 2025. The address normalization and validation control was uplifted and activated for both Borrow and Retro on 6/5/25.

Issue Title: Incorrect claim documents were sent to customers who uploaded evidence via the Voice channel due to systematic error in CF1 (Salesforce).
Issue Description:
Agents were mistakenly sending incorrect claim documents to Cash App customers who contacted via the Voice Channel and uploaded evidence, which potentially compromised the credibility of investigations. This issue arose when the Voice Evidence Upload feature was introduced to collect evidence for disputes submitted through the voice channel but failed to accurately record the additional evidence upload question and response.
 

 Claim documents sent between May 28, 2024 (the date that the copy claims feature was introduced) and January 6, 2025 (the date the fix was deployed) were affected. These documents were manually requested through Support (CF1), and Dispute Claim IDs were not consistently linked to the support cases. Additionally, the Copy Claim Docs button on the Disputes page in Toolbox falsely indicated that customers had not uploaded any files, even though they had. This led to a situation where the evidence customers provided was not properly reflected in the claims process.
RCA Description: Technology
Issue Rating Justification: The issue relates to an internal control or process that is associated with low-risk activity (includes instances in which controls or other safeguards are noted to be absent).
Issue Action Plans: Incorrect claim documents were sent to customers who uploaded evidence via the Voice channel due to systematic error in CF1 (Salesforce). (1251572)
Closure Request Justification: Evidence reviewed by IM Lead. No concerns noted. - Charlotte Vasi

 Preventative Control - On a daily basis, the Banking Investigation Leads monitor the workload and the completion status of pending Wire Recall Request reviews using Wells Fargo Secure Message Center and Google Sheets to check if the cases are resolved in a timely manner and within the due date established by Wells Fargo. For cases pending review and approaching due date, the Banking Investigation Leads confirm the case is considered as a priority and reviewed before the due date.

Issue Title: Board and Management Oversight #1 - Enterprise-Wide Risk Assessment - Enhance and Conduct Comprehensive ERA
Issue Description:
Finding: The adequacy of the 2022 enterprise-wide risk assessment could not be fully evaluated as most identified risks, control mitigants and residual risks have been redacted from the document provided. Based on the limited review that could be conducted, it appears that the assessment is not sufficiently granular to link and assess the effectiveness of identified inherent risks with mitigating controls. There are no ratings assigned to control mitigants or residual risks. There is no evidence of the levels of identified risks and the supporting rationales. There is also no evidence that internal policies and procedures were evaluated as part of the assessment. The robustness of the assessment could not be evaluated as there appears to be no evidence of quantitative data, or trends, supporting assigned risks in visible areas. In addition, a risk assessment methodology was not provided.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to conduct an enterprise-wide risk assessment to ensure that policies, procedures, and processes are commensurate with the institution’s risk profile, and appropriate oversight is being provided to areas of higher risk. The risk assessment process should be robust, incorporating quantitative data and trends, and periodically updated to reflect changes in the institution’s risk profile as necessary. Management is reminded that a comprehensive risk assessment is a key ingredient to developing and maintaining a BSA/AML compliance program that is appropriate for the institution, and examiners need access to the entire document in order to evaluate the adequacy of the assessment.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Review current ERA methodology and processes (1255526)

Issue Title: Consumer Compliance #1 - Consumer Disclosures - Align Consumer Disclosures with NYDFS VC Disclosure Requirements
Issue Description:
Finding: Compliance with consumer protection was determined through a review of Block Inc’s., Cash App Terms of Service (2/8/2022) and receipts that are provided upon completion of any transaction. Although the delivery of the Cash App Terms of Service is timely and included most of the disclosures mandated by 23 NYCRR Part 200.19(a) & (b), it was noted that the required disclosures were disseminated between pages 40 and 79 of a 121- page document. This fragmented approach to required disclosures does not meet the clear and conspicuous requirement of the regulation. In addition, the Cash App Terms of Service do not include the following required disclosures, that must be provided as part of establishing a relationship with the customer, and prior to entering into an initial transaction:
 o Account and value balances are not subject to FDIC or SPIC protection, in violation of 23NYCRR 200.19(a)(1)
 o The nature of VC may lead to an increased risk of fraud or cyber-attack, in violation of 23 NYCRR 200.19(a)(8)
 o The nature of Virtual Currency means that any technological difficulties experienced by the Licensee may prevent the access or use of a customer’s Virtual Currency, in violation of 23 NYCRR 200.19(a)(9)
 The disclosure of general terms and conditions did not include:
 o The customer’s right to stop payment of a preauthorized VC transfer and procedure to initiate such a stop payment order, in violation of 23 NYCRR 200.19(b)(2)
 o Under what circumstances the Licensee will, absent court or government order, disclose information concerning the customer’s account to third parties, in violation of 23 NYCRR 200.19(b)(3)
 o The consumer’s right to receive periodic account statements and valuation from the Licensee, in violation of 23 NYCRR 200.19(b)(5).
 Management was unable to provide evidence that additional required transaction disclosures, specifically the disclosures of terms of transactions and acknowledgment of disclosures, were being provided.
 1. Disclosure of Terms of Transaction must be provided to customers prior to each transaction in Virtual Currency and must include:
 a) the amount of the transaction (pursuant to 23 NYCRR 200.19(c)(1))
 b) any fees, expenses, and charges borne by the customer, including applicable exchange rates (pursuant to 23 NYCRR 200.19(c)(2))
 c) the type and nature of the Virtual Currency transaction (pursuant to 23 NYCRR 200.19(c)(3))
 d) a warning that once executed the transaction may not be undone, if applicable; (pursuant to 23 NYCRR 200.19(c)4)) and
 e) such other disclosures as are customarily given in connection with a transaction of this nature (pursuant to 23 NYCRR 200.19(c)5)).
 2. Acknowledgment of disclosures. A process is not in place to ensure that all disclosures required in this Section are acknowledged as received by customers (pursuant to 23 NYCRR 200.19(d)).
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to implement a noted industry best practice of grouping required disclosures of material risks and general terms and conditions to meet the clear, conspicuous and reasonably understandable form requirement of the regulation. Management is also required to further revise the Cash App Terms of Service to include all applicable disclosures mandated by the regulation. A process to ensure delivery of disclosures of the terms of transactions for applicable transactions and acknowledgment of receipts of disclosures must be implemented.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Revise Cash App’s onboarding screens to link to and have customers acknowledge the disclosures. (1255532)

Issue Title: Legal and Compliance #1 - Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) (Russian-Linked Accounts) - Enhance Controls to Mitigate Risks Associated with Criminal Netowrks Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: As part of an internal investigation of potential sanction evasion related to Russian Asset Flight, Block self-identified more than 8,000 accounts assessed to be under the control of a Russian-linked criminal network that was operating on the Cash App platform. This significant finding has been referred to Block’s Special Investigations Units to follow up with reporting obligations and account closure. As this appeared to be a developing situation as of the review date, examiners were not able to assess the scope of this finding or the adequacy of the firm’s remediation efforts.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to develop a documented project plan with actionable timelines including clearly delineated steps that will be taken, controls to measure progress, and personnel responsible to ensure the successful review, closure and reporting of these identified accounts. Management must ensure that controls are developed to identify and mitigate the significant risks associated with onboarding these types of criminal networks. The identified risk(s) associated with Russian-linked network transactions must be factored into the risk assessment. Management is required to provide the Agency with quarterly updates of the firm’s remediation efforts and filing obligations.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Implement sanctions screening for all restricted accounts at onboarding and conduct daily sanctions screening for all accounts. (1255536)

Issue Title: Legal and Compliance #10 - Alert Review Backlog - Ensure Adequate Resources are Available to Prevent Alert Backlog
Issue Description:

The analysis should also assess the impact of this protracted backlog on internal controls.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Document the forecasting and capacity planning process (1256180)

Issue Title: Legal and Compliance #11 - Training - Ensure BSA/AML Training is Completed Timely Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: Based on the documentation provided, it appears that training is not tailored to employees’ specific responsibilities and lines of business. There is no evidence of crypto-specific training, although Block is considered to be primarily a cryptocurrency institution. In addition, a review of the training record tracker disclosed that 305 employees did not complete required training by the assigned due dates, and as of examination date, eleven employees continued to have past due training status. Forty-six new hires also did not complete required New Employee Training within the time assigned. The new employee training policy does not clearly state a specific time when the required training module must be completed.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to assess employees’ BSA/AML needs and provide training that is appropriate and timely. New employee training should clearly state when required training must be completed. Management is also required to ensure that employees, whose duties require knowledge of BSA and virtual currency specific risks, comply with the firm’s training requirements and timeline for the completion of assigned training modules.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Develop and rollout bitcoin risk awareness training (1256182)

Issue Title: Legal and Compliance #2 - Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) (Blocked and Rejected Transactions) - Comply with OFAC Filing Timing Requirements
Issue Description:
Finding: An adequate process is in place for the handling of blocked and rejected transactions under the various sanction programs. Although procedures specify required reporting of identified prohibited transactions within 10 business days of transactions being blocked or rejected, transactions testing of judgmental samples, revealed that there is a pattern and practice of late filings in violation of 31 C.F.R. 501.604(b)(1)(i) & 604(c) respectively. Six of sixteen blocked cash transactions and all fifteen rejected BTC identified in 2021 were filed more than 10 business days after the transactions were blocked, in violation of 31 C.F.R. 501.604(b)(1)(i) and 501.604(c) respectively. OFAC reports were filed between 39 and 94 days after the prohibited transactions were identified.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to ensure that filing obligations are conducted within the requisite timeframe stipulated in the regulation. It was noted that all blocked and rejected transactions identified in 2022 were filed on a timely basis.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Automate metrics related to OFAC reporting to identify reporting trends and to ensure applicable reporting deadlines are being followed. (1256192)

Issue Title: Legal and Compliance #3 - Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) (Green Accounts) - Conduct Sanctions Screening on Restricted Accounts
Issue Description:
Finding: The Control Environment Questionnaire that is the source of the Block’s OFAC risk assessment, identified transactions that are being conducted on the firm’s platform by customers that are unverified and not fully onboarded AKA Green Accounts, as having weak or non-existent controls to prevent businesses from transacting with Specially Designated Nationals (“SDN”) and other prohibited persons. This is a significant identified inherent risk that could potentially expose the institution to the assessment of civil money penalties for conducting business with sanctioned persons and businesses, if left unchecked. This practice violates 23 NYCRR 200.15(h)(1) that requires all customers to be screened against OFAC SDN Listing.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to establish policies and procedures to mitigate this significant identified risk, by implementing a process to filter all customers against current sanction listings. Management is reminded of the prohibition of conducting business with prohibited persons and businesses. Management is also reminded that OFAC violation is strictly punitive.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Implement sanctions screening for restricted accounts at onboarding and conduct daily sanctions screening for all accounts. (1256194)

Issue Title: Legal and Compliance #4 - Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) (Quality Assurance Reviews) - Conduct Sanctions Screening on All Block Personnel and Vendors Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: A quality assurance review process is not in place to confirm periodic updates are made to the sanction listings in a timely manner, nor is there a review process in place to ensure that the entire database is being filtered against updated sanction listings. In addition, discussions with management revealed that service providers and employees are screened against sanction listings as part of the initial onboarding process; however, onboarded employees and service providers are not subject to periodic filtering against updated sanction listings.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to develop review processes to confirm periodic updates to OFAC filters and provide assurance that the institution’s entire database is being scrubbed against updated sanction lists. Management is also required to enhance the periodic filter process to include vendors who conduct business with the institution and current employees, to provide assurance that business is not being conducted with sanctioned individuals or entities who have been added to updated listings. Management is reminded that exceptions to OFAC requirements are strictly punitive.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Implement ongoing monthly sanctions screening for Block’s vendors. (1256196)
Closure Request Justification: Closing this Issue (in the absence of Kathryn Hemlock) to align with the "Work Status" in the MTL Exam Remediations sheet.

Issue Title: Legal and Compliance #5 - Customer Due Diligence & Enhanced Due Diligence (Green Accounts) - Conduct Sufficient KYC and Minimize Risk of Restricted Accounts Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: Due diligence is not conducted for customers who transact below a certain threshold and there are no policy or procedures in place for the monitoring and oversight of these account holders. Several of the subjects of the SARs reviewed were customers that were holders of multiple green accounts, transacting below the threshold established for due diligence. Specifically, this examination identified Green Account customers that were subjects of SAR filings for having multiple accounts, conducting extensive gambling and other unacceptable activities, and being residents of penitentiaries. Although Green account transactions are limited to the sending and receiving of $1,000 in a rolling 30-day period, a SAR was filed for $1.6MM with 91 subjects that were holders of 16,811 accounts with 19,518 transactions. Green accounts are also allowed to withdraw up to $25,000 per week without triggering due diligence requirements. Examiners also noted that there is an ongoing concern where de-risked green customers have reestablished accounts on the platform. The failure to establish policies and procedures to mitigate the significant risks associated with green accounts is a cause for supervisory concern. There is also a concern that sanctioned individuals may also be green account holders as the only information needed to establish an account is a phone number or email. Absent appropriate oversight and tailored controls to such populations, Block may have significant exposure to potential illicit activity.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to establish policies and procedures for the effective oversight of these accounts. The significant inherent risks associated with these accounts must be incorporated in the institution’s risk assessment and management must develop policies, procedures and processes to mitigate this significant risk. Management is reminded of the mandate to know your customer and the requirement that enough information should be collected as part of the account opening process to form a reasonable belief that you know the true identity of each customer. Management is also reminded of the prohibition of conducting business with sanctioned parties.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Implement sanctions screening for all restricted accounts at onboarding and conduct daily sanctions screening for all accounts. (1256202)

Issue Title: Legal and Compliance #5 - Customer Due Diligence & Enhanced Due Diligence (Green Accounts) - Conduct Sufficient KYC and Minimize Risk of Restricted Accounts Part 2
Issue Description:
Finding: Due diligence is not conducted for customers who transact below a certain threshold and there are no policy or procedures in place for the monitoring and oversight of these account holders. Several of the subjects of the SARs reviewed were customers that were holders of multiple green accounts, transacting below the threshold established for due diligence. Specifically, this examination identified Green Account customers that were subjects of SAR filings for having multiple accounts, conducting extensive gambling and other unacceptable activities, and being residents of penitentiaries. Although Green account transactions are limited to the sending and receiving of $1,000 in a rolling 30-day period, a SAR was filed for $1.6MM with 91 subjects that were holders of 16,811 accounts with 19,518 transactions. Green accounts are also allowed to withdraw up to $25,000 per week without triggering due diligence requirements. Examiners also noted that there is an ongoing concern where de-risked green customers have reestablished accounts on the platform. The failure to establish policies and procedures to mitigate the significant risks associated with green accounts is a cause for supervisory concern. There is also a concern that sanctioned individuals may also be green account holders as the only information needed to establish an account is a phone number or email. Absent appropriate oversight and tailored controls to such populations, Block may have significant exposure to potential illicit activity.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to establish policies and procedures for the effective oversight of these accounts. The significant inherent risks associated with these accounts must be incorporated in the institution’s risk assessment and management must develop policies, procedures and processes to mitigate this significant risk. Management is reminded of the mandate to know your customer and the requirement that enough information should be collected as part of the account opening process to form a reasonable belief that you know the true identity of each customer. Management is also reminded of the prohibition of conducting business with sanctioned parties.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Conclude implementation of aggregation of transaction limits across restricted accounts using device fingerprints for the final portion of accounts not currently covered. (1256204)

Issue Title: Legal and Compliance #6 - Customer Due Diligence & Enhanced Due Diligence (Customer Identification) - Collect Sufficient Information and Minimize Risk of Restricted Accounts Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: The failure to collect minimum customer information is a cause for significant concern as this practice does not align with the supervisory expectations of FinCEN Guidance (FIN-2019-G001) for business models involving convertible virtual currencies and is in violation of 23 NYCRR 200.15(h)(1).
 

 Limiting initial customer information to an email or phone number, for green accounts, does not meet FinCEN’s obligations of MSBs, to determine both the identity and profile of its customers and know enough about their customers to be able to determine the risk levels they represent to the institution. There are no policies or procedures in place for the oversight of Green Accounts. To compensate for the lack of customer identification, these customers are limited to sending or receiving $1,000 in a rolling 30-day period. However, this lowered transaction amount is not an effective control, as the institution has identified several instances of customers establishing multiple accounts and conducting extensive gambling and unacceptable use activities utilizing Block’s platform. The institution has also identified instances of de-risked customers who have reestablished accounts and continued illegal activities on the licensee’s platform.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to establish policies, procedures, and processes for the collection of identifying information, to form a reasonable belief as to the true identity of customers. Management must also collect sufficient information at account opening to develop an understanding of the customer’s normal and expected activities to be able to determine the risk they represent to the institution. Management is also required to establish policies and procedures to mitigate the significant risks inherent in Green Accounts.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Implement sanctions screening for all restricted accounts at onboarding and conduct daily sanctions screening for all accounts. (1256210)

Issue Title: Legal and Compliance #7 - Customer Due Diligence & Enhanced Due Diligence (Customer Risk Rating) - Risk Rate All Customers Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: As of examination date, a reliable and rigorous customer due diligence process is not in place to consistently obtain and analyze sufficient information to develop a meaningful understanding of the normal and expected activity of the institution’s customer base. The initial due diligence information should be used to assign risk ratings to customers to provide assurance that appropriate risk-based oversight is being provided to customers that pose high money laundering or terrorist financing risks. This initial due diligence information should be updated periodically for the duration of the customer’s relationship with the institution. Discussions with management disclosed that a customer risk rating process was being implemented. However, examiners noted that key information such as anticipated transactions and source of funds is not collected as part of the account opening process. In addition, a process is not in place to periodically refresh customer due diligence (See periodic customer due diligence of existing customers).
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to develop comprehensive policies, procedures and processes for risk rating all customers. At account opening, enough information should be collected to develop an understanding of the normal and expected activity for customers. Based on the collected information, all customers should be risk rated to allow for risk-based monitoring. Lower-risk customers and account should be monitored through suspicious activity monitoring and customer due diligence process, and high-risk customers should be subject to robust periodic Enhanced Due Diligence reviews.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Enhance EDD workflow to generate alerts based on real time aCRR model. (1256216)
Closure Request Justification: This issue was risk accepted.
