Issue Title: Legal and Compliance #8 - Periodic Customer Due Diligence – Existing Customers - Develop Ongoing Reassessment of CRR
Issue Description:
Finding: There is no formalized process or structure in place to consistently ensure the updating of initial customer due diligence information. Outdated information may affect a customer’s risk profile and impact the institution’s AML risk rating assigned to the customer. As oversight is risk-based, the failure to assign correct (updated) ratings, pose significant legal and reputational risks to the institution.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to develop policies, procedures, and processes regarding periodic customer due diligence reviews. The policies and procedures should include processes designed to reassess the adequacy of the customer’s risk rating based on the results of the periodic customer due diligence reviews, as warranted.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Begin implementation of the KYC refresh process (1256220)

Issue Title: Legal and Compliance #9 - Customer Due Diligence & Enhanced Due Diligence (LInked Accounts) - Verify Ownership of Linked Accounts Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: Although linked accounts are referenced in the Block’s procedures, there is no documentation of any due diligence review that is conducted for linked bank/payment accounts to ensure that these accounts are owned by the onboarded customers. Linked accounts are used to send or receive funds for Cash App customers. A noted industry best practice is to conduct some type of due diligence confirmation to ensure that linked accounts that are being used to process payments are owned and controlled by customers. Examiners noted several instances where analysts conducting SAR investigations could not confirm the ownership of linked accounts that were used to process suspicious activities. Since there is no process to verify the ownership of linked accounts, there is also the risk that prohibited transactions may be processed through these accounts.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to ensure that a documented process for verifying the ownership of linked accounts is established. The risk associated with conducting business with potential noncustomer linked accounts, should be factored in the firm’s risk assessment.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Update Compliance procedures and other review materials, and establish training, to better facilitate identification of red flags associated with linked financial instruments. (1256222)

Issue Title: Legal and Compliance #9 - Customer Due Diligence & Enhanced Due Diligence (LInked Accounts) - Verify Ownership of Linked Accounts Part 2

Issue Rating Justification: N/A

Closure Request Justification: This issue was risk accepted.

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #1 - Comprehensive IT General Controls Audit - Conduct Audit of GLBA Compliance Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: The current IT Audit plan focuses on the review of IT, and IT controls as a subcategory of financial reporting controls (Sarbanes-Oxley “SOX” IT general controls) testing. There is no comprehensive audit of IT and IT controls in place. While the 2022 Audit Plan included IT-related audits, the Plan was not supported by an IT audit risk assessment. An IT Audit Plan based on IT risk assessment results should be developed to ensure that IT audit coverage at Block is comprehensive and risk-based and not limited to financial reporting controls.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to conduct a comprehensive IT Audit Risk Assessment and use the results to develop an IT risk-based audit plan and program. Management is also required to expand audit review coverage to include business continuity management and compliance with the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (GLBA). 
 

 This is a repeat finding from the 2020 Multi-state Report of Examination.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: In its next plan (the 2024 Internal Audit plan), Internal Audit will include GLBA compliance. (1256226)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #2 - IT Audit Report Formality - Create IT Audit Report Formality Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: There is no evidence that management is provided with written IT Audit reports. IT Audit findings are reported through the use of a “gap log” and presentation of the issues at Audit & Risk Committee meetings. Examiners were unable to fully assess the reporting phase as the IT audits were in progress and there were no reports to evidence the required written report process. Examiners also noted that an audit rating system is not in place, which is a noted industry best practice pursuant to the FFIEC guidance to convey the risk posed by the function/area being audited, in a concise and consistent manner.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to establish procedures for written audit reports that are presented to the board or delegated committee of the board. Management is also required to implement a rating system in order to facilitate a consistent and concise assessment of the risk posed by the area or function being audited.
 

 This is a repeat finding from the 2020 Multi-state Report of Examination.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Adopt a uniform rating system across audit types or a crosswalk between rating systems. (1256228)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #3 - IT Audit Program - Align IA with FFIEC Guidance Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: Although an Internal Audit Department charter is in place, written audit policies, procedures and standards have not been established. Pursuant to FFIEC guidance, elements of an effective internal audit program should include risk assessment methodology, a system for rating audits, standards for audit workpapers, reporting guidelines, follow-up procedures and retention policies. These components are necessary in order for the board or delegated committee to provide appropriate oversight and effectively evaluate the levels of risk exposure and control issues affecting the institution.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to develop Internal Audit policy, standards, and procedures, that conform to the audit program elements prescribed by the FFIEC Guidance.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Adopt an Internal Audit policy, procedures, and standards, taking into account, as appropriate, FFIEC guidance and other regulatory and industry guidance. (1256230)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #4 - Third Party Service Provider Policy & Program - Align TPRM with Part 500.11 Requirements - Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: The institution’s Third-Party Service Provider policies and procedures do not include all the elements required by 23 NYCRR 500.11. Current program, policies, standards, procedures are not sufficiently detailed as to:
 - the identification and assessment risks, in violation of 23 NYCRR 500.11(a)(1) & (3), and
 - periodic risk-based assessments of all providers, in violation of 23 NYCRR 500.11(a)(4),
 - due diligence guidelines/contractual protections pertaining to cybersecurity event notifications, in violation of 23 NYCRR 500.11(b)(3), and
 - cybersecurity related representations and warranties, in violation of 23 NYCRR 500.11(b)(4).
 

 Management was unable to provide the examiner with a complete list of its vendors, and approximately a third of the vendors identified as critical did not have an assigned risk rating or the date that the last due diligence review was conducted. Examiners also noted that a process is not in place to periodically verify the vendor population, to provide assurance that ongoing and effective oversight is being provided to all vendors.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to enhance policies and procedures to bring them into compliance with the applicable sections of 23 NYCRR 500.11. All vendors must be risk rated and subjected to periodic re-evaluations, based on the assigned risks. Management is also required to implement a reconciliation process to ensure that oversight is being provided to all third-party service providers.
 

 This is a partial repeat finding from the 2020 Multi-state Report of Examination.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block will review its TPRM policies and procedures (1256232)

Issue Rating Justification: N/A

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #5 - Business Continuity Management - Align BCPs with Part 200.17 - Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: The Licensee’s Business Continuity Plan does not comply with 23 NYCRR 200, as it is limited to the firm’s Pandemic Response and Disaster recovery procedures for its BitBank process and does not address all the applicable sections of 23 NYCRR 200.17(a). In addition, there is no evidence of independent testing or an audit review of the firm’s business continuity plan, in violation of 23 NYCRR 200.17(e).
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to enhance the firm’s business continuity plans to address all applicable sections of the regulation and ensure that required annual testing is conducted. Management is also required to conduct a full-failover test of Cash App, observed by independent personnel. Policies and procedures should be further enhanced to address notification requirements to NYSDFS.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Create Cash App product-level BCPs addressing all critical business processes. (1256236)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #5 - Business Continuity Management - Align BCPs with Part 200.17 - Part 2
Issue Description:
Finding: The Licensee’s Business Continuity Plan does not comply with 23 NYCRR 200, as it is limited to the firm’s Pandemic Response and Disaster recovery procedures for its BitBank process and does not address all the applicable sections of 23 NYCRR 200.17(a). In addition, there is no evidence of independent testing or an audit review of the firm’s business continuity plan, in violation of 23 NYCRR 200.17(e).
 

 These issues may hinder the Licensee’s ability to recover effectively from a business continuity event. Insufficient independent testing and management reporting to the Board of Directors may limit the Board’s ability to provide effective oversight of the Licensee’s business continuity management program.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to enhance the firm’s business continuity plans to address all applicable sections of the regulation and ensure that required annual testing is conducted. Management is also required to conduct a full-failover test of Cash App, observed by independent personnel. Policies and procedures should be further enhanced to address notification requirements to NYSDFS.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Create a comprehensive high-level Cash App BCP that satisfies all the Department’s requirements in 23 N.Y.C.R.R. 200.17. (1256238)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #6 - Cybersecurity Governance - Enhance Cybersecurity Governance Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: The Information Security Policy and other policies that compose the firm’s cyber security policy are not subjected to annual board review and approval, in violation of 23 NYCRR 200.16(b). In addition, the cyber security policy does not address capacity and performance planning, in violation of 23 NYCRR 200.16(b)(5). The Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) has a dual reporting structure to the Audit and Risk Committee as well as the Head of Foundational Engineering. Since Foundational Engineering includes operational responsibilities, this reporting line gives the appearance of a potential conflict of interest that could cause IT security decisions and funding to be influenced by operational ease or budgetary constraints.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to ensure that the cyber security policy is enhanced to fully comply with the requirements of the regulation and that required annual reviews and approvals are conducted. Management is also required to ensure that the CISO’s reporting line is independent of IT Operations.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Obtain Board review and approval of revised Information Security Policy. (1256244)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #7 - Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) Reports - Submit CISO Reports to NYDFS
Issue Description:
Finding: The redacted board minutes did not provide evidence that annual status reports on the institution’s Cybersecurity Program were presented by the CISO, in violation of 23 NYCRR 200.16(d) and 23 NYCRR 500.04(b). The Licensee asserted attorney-client privilege for not providing the CISO’s written reports. Thus, examiners did not assess the adequacy of the CISO’s written report at this review.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to ensure that the CISO’s annual written report on the status of the institution’s Cybersecurity Program and related material risks is reviewed by the board and documented in the minutes. Management is also required to submit this annual written report to NYSDFS to comply with the regulation.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Document in a procedure the requirement to submit CISO annual reports to the Department according to Department regulations. (1256246)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #8 - Cyber Event Notification Procedures - Develop Procedures for Reporting Cybersecurity Events Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: The Licensee does not maintain procedures for reporting cyber events to the NYSDFS, in violation of 23 NYCRR 500.17(a). In addition, the Incident Response Plan does not reference the required timeframes or reporting process. The lack of cyber event reporting procedures may hinder management’s ability to consistently identify reportable events and submit the timely reports to the NYSDFS.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to establish procedures for the timely reporting cyberevents to NYSDFS. The NYSDFS Cybersecurity Industry Guidance should beutilized in developing the procedures.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Revise incident response plan to specifically address reporting of cyber events to the Department. (1256248)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #9 - Audit Log Retention Standard - Align Audit Log Retention Standard with Part 200.16 - Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: The Audit Logs, Alerts, and Monitoring Standard requires retention of system audit logs for at least one year, which does not conform with the minimum retention requirement prescribed by 23 NYCRR 200.16(e)(2)(v). Examiners noted that actual retention practices were counter to stated policy and conformed with regulatory requirements.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to revise internal policy to align with the required seven-year retention period.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Revised Audit, Logs and Alerts Standard Policy to align with seven-year retention requirement. (1256250)

Issue Title: Legal and Compliance #1 - OFAC Russian-Linked Accounts - Enhance Controls to Mitigate Risks Associated with Criminal Netowrks Part 3
Issue Description:
Finding: As part of an internal investigation of potential sanction evasion related to Russian Asset Flight, Block self-identified more than 8,000 accounts assessed to be under the control of a Russian-linked criminal network that was operating on the Cash App platform. This significant finding has been referred to Block’s Special Investigations Units to follow up with reporting obligations and account closure. As this appeared to be a developing situation as of the review date, examiners were not able to assess the scope of this finding or the adequacy of the firm’s remediation efforts.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to develop a documented project plan with actionable timelines including clearly delineated steps that will be taken, controls to measure progress, and personnel responsible to ensure the successful review, closure and reporting of these identified accounts. Management must ensure that controls are developed to identify and mitigate the significant risks associated with onboarding these types of criminal networks. The identified risk(s) associated with Russian-linked network transactions must be factored into the risk assessment. Management is required to provide the Agency with quarterly updates of the firm’s remediation efforts and filing obligations.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block has implemented the following enhancements (1256252)

Issue Title: Legal and Compliance #2 - Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) (Blocked and Rejected Transactions) - Comply with OFAC Filing Timing Requirements Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: An adequate process is in place for the handling of blocked and rejected transactions under the various sanction programs. Although procedures specify required reporting of identified prohibited transactions within 10 business days of transactions being blocked or rejected, transactions testing of judgmental samples, revealed that there is a pattern and practice of late filings in violation of 31 C.F.R. 501.604(b)(1)(i) & 604(c) respectively. Six of sixteen blocked cash transactions and all fifteen rejected BTC identified in 2021 were filed more than 10 business days after the transactions were blocked, in violation of 31 C.F.R. 501.604(b)(1)(i) and 501.604(c) respectively. OFAC reports were filed between 39 and 94 days after the prohibited transactions were identified.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to ensure that filing obligations are conducted within the requisite timeframe stipulated in the regulation. It was noted that all blocked and rejected transactions identified in 2022 were filed on a timely basis.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block implemented two principal remedial steps in March 2022 and January 2023, respectively, which it has continued to refine over time (1256254)

Issue Title: Legal and Compliance #5 - Customer Due Diligence & Enhanced Due Diligence (Green Accounts) - Conduct Sufficient KYC and Minimize Risk of Restricted Accounts Part 5
Issue Description:
Finding: Due diligence is not conducted for customers who transact below a certain threshold and there are no policy or procedures in place for the monitoring and oversight of these account holders. Several of the subjects of the SARs reviewed were customers that were holders of multiple green accounts, transacting below the threshold established for due diligence. Specifically, this examination identified Green Account customers that were subjects of SAR filings for having multiple accounts, conducting extensive gambling and other unacceptable activities, and being residents of penitentiaries. Although Green account transactions are limited to the sending and receiving of $1,000 in a rolling 30-day period, a SAR was filed for $1.6MM with 91 subjects that were holders of 16,811 accounts with 19,518 transactions. Green accounts are also allowed to withdraw up to $25,000 per week without triggering due diligence requirements. Examiners also noted that there is an ongoing concern where de-risked green customers have reestablished accounts on the platform. The failure to establish policies and procedures to mitigate the significant risks associated with green accounts is a cause for supervisory concern. There is also a concern that sanctioned individuals may also be green account holders as the only information needed to establish an account is a phone number or email. Absent appropriate oversight and tailored controls to such populations, Block may have significant exposure to potential illicit activity.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to establish policies and procedures for the effective oversight of these accounts. The significant inherent risks associated with these accounts must be incorporated in the institution’s risk assessment and management must develop policies, procedures and processes to mitigate this significant risk. Management is reminded of the mandate to know your customer and the requirement that enough information should be collected as part of the account opening process to form a reasonable belief that you know the true identity of each customer. Management is also reminded of the prohibition of conducting business with sanctioned parties.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block has recently undertaken significant enhancements to controls applicable to restricted accounts (1256258)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #6 - Cybersecurity Governance - Enhance Cybersecurity Governance Part 2
Issue Description:
Finding: The Information Security Policy and other policies that compose the firm’s cyber security policy are not subjected to annual board review and approval, in violation of 23 NYCRR 200.16(b). In addition, thencyber security policy does not address capacity and performace planning, in violation of 23 NYCRR 200.16(b)(5). The Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) has a dual reporting structure to the Audit and Risk Committee as well as the Head of Foundational Engineering. Since Foundational Engineering includes operational responsibilities, this reporting line gives the appearance of a potential conflict of interest that could cause IT security decisions and funding to be influenced by operational ease or budgetary constraints.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to ensure that the cyber security policy is enhanced to fully comply with the requirements of the regulation and that required annual reviews and approvals are conducted. Management is also required to ensure that the CISO’s reporting line is independent of IT Operations.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block has revised its Information Security Policy as part of a yearly update process to address capacity and performance planning, which Block conducts as a matter of course. (1256260)

Issue Title: CA Violation #1 - Non-Compliance with Conditions of Approval (“COA”) - Ensure COA Compliance Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: Item 11 of the COA letter dated October 9, 2012, requires the monthly submission of financial statements and average daily transmission liability (ADTL) reports within 21 days after the end of each month. Examiners noted that the February 2022 reports were submitted three days late, and the March 2022 reports were submitted one day late. 
 

 Item 15 of the COA requires 60 days’ advance notice of the launch of any new products with material changes. The Licensee failed to provide advance notice to the DFPI of the Bill Pay services, which occurred in February 2023.
 

 Management Required Action: Ensure that monthly financial statements, including ADTL reports, and material changes are submitted timely as required. The Licensee maintained the required TSE; therefore, no further action is required.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Enhance report calendaring system to ensure that responsible staff have better visibility of deadlines. (1256264)

Issue Title: CA Violation #2 - Failure to Issue Customer Receipt (Repeat Violation) - Contemporaneous Receipt
Issue Description:
Finding: Based on testing of the Cash App, the Licensee does not provide the official receipt via email delivery when a customer conducts a send money transaction, but merely a confirmation that a payment is sent. To access the official receipts that contain the California right to refund language, Block’s customers need to go to their transaction history and click on a link for “web receipt” after the transaction is completed. Block should ensure the official receipt is given to the customer at the time of the transaction. Because of this deficiency, Block also violates item 1 of its October 19, 2021, Board Resolution, adopted due to the joint May 2021 examination findings.
 

 Management Required Action: Ensure customers receive a receipt via email, which contains California’s right to refund language for each transaction.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Ensure that California customers receive a contemporaneous receipt. (1256268)

Issue Title: CA Violation #3 - Use of Unfiled Receipt (Repeat Violation) - Develop Process for Obtaining DFPI Approval of Receipts
Issue Description:
Finding: It was noted at the 2021 examination that Cash App receiving money for transmission receipts did not include the sender’s name and that the receipt version used was not filed with the DFPI. The Licensee’s domestic receipts have since been updated to include the sender’s name on the receipt and were filed with DFPI on April 6, 2023. 
 

 However, international receipts have not been filed with the DFPI. Per the Licensee, it is finalizing the new format to submit for approval.
 

 Management stated at the joint May 2021 examination that they would develop a process for filing new receipts with the DFPI to ensure compliance with the CFC. This commitment was memorialized via Item 2 of the October 19, 2021, Board Resolution adopted after the 2021 examination. However, it appears that receipts are still not being filed on time to comply with requirements.
 

 Management Required Action: Ensure transaction receipts are filed with DFPI before their use going forward. Management must develop a process for filing new receipts with DFPI to ensure compliance with the requirements under CFC.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Enhance and enforce process for DFPI receipt filing. (1256270)

Issue Title: California Other Findings/Recommendations #1 - For the Benefit of (“FBO”) Bank Account - Submit Use of FBO Account as Eligible Security for Approval to DFPI
Issue Description:
Finding: Examiners noted that the Licensee is applying the Wells Fargo -5212 bank account balance as eligible securities. This Wells Fargo account is titled under the name of Square, Inc. for the benefit of its customers (“FBO” account). CFC 2084 states that the inclusion of an FBO account as eligible securities must first be reviewed by the DFPI. Management should submit a formal request to the DFPI if they plan to use this or any other FBO accounts as eligible securities. It was noted that without considering this FBO account, the Licensee still maintains adequate eligible securities.
 

 Management Required Action: Ensure FBO accounts or any eligible securities are formally submitted to DFPI for review/approval. As mentioned in the finding above, management should update this account title with the current name.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: No MAP - The Wells Fargo FBO account ending in 5212 has been updated (1256272)

Issue Title: Federal Violations #2 - Consumer Financial Protection Bureau - Ensure Reg E Compliance for International Remittances
Issue Description:
Finding: Foreign Remittance Receipts - Date Available
 The Licensee is in violation of Title 12 CFR Section 1005.31(b)(2)(ii) for failure to issue a receipt for remittance transfers that discloses to the sender the date in the foreign country on which the funds will be available to the designated recipient, using the term “Date Available” or a substantially similar term. Customer receipts issued by the Licensee state that funds will be available in the recipient’s Cash App immediately. As the Remittance Rule requires, the receipt does not give an exact date when the funds will be available. Per the CFPB guidance, the Licensee may disclose the latest date on which the funds will be available. The Licensee may also disclose that funds “may be available sooner” or use a substantially similar term to inform senders that funds may be available to the designated recipient on a date earlier than the date disclosed.
 

 Management Required Action: Update the international remittance receipt to comply with Title 12 CFR Section 1005.31(b) requirements. Develop policies and procedures for error resolution as required under Title 12 CFR Section 1005.33(g). Furthermore, inform the participating states of any extraordinary events occurring from the aforementioned CFPB investigation.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: None: Block appreciates the examiner’s recognition of Cash App’s policies and procedures (1256302)

Issue Title: Federal Violations #3 - Office of Foreign Assets Control - Ensure Timely OFAC Reporting Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: A review of the 2022 audit report revealed that the Licensee failed to timely report 21 transactions (18 Cash App and three Square managed payments) rejected for sanctions match to OFAC within the ten-day reporting deadline. Twenty of the transactions were rejected timely, but reported to OFAC outside the ten-day window. In contrast, the remaining transaction was initiated by a Cash App user 72 days before its rejection and reported to OFAC one day post-rejection. All rejected transactions were reported to OFAC between 16 - 97 days after the initial transaction date. 
 

 Additionally, examiners provided a list of four names to test the effectiveness of the Licensee’s transaction screening against sanctioned lists. Two of the names provided were slightly altered, with the remaining names being recently removed from the Specially Designated Nationals and Blocked Persons List (SDN); however, none were flagged within the Licensee’s sanctions screening. The two names that should have been flagged for review received a fuzzy logic score of 92 or above, according to the OFAC’s website.
 

 Management Required Action: Ensure blocked and rejected transactions are reported to OFAC timely as required under 31 CFR 501.603 and 501.604. Failure to timely report blocked and rejected transactions also expose the Licensee to fines and penalties. Furthermore, review the OFAC screening software to understand why these names were not flagged and take corrective actions to ensure all sanctioned names are identified correctly. The Licensee should also determine the acceptable risk level for OFAC screening within its AML Program.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Automate metrics related to OFAC reporting to identify reporting trends and to ensure applicable reporting deadlines are being followed. (1256304)

Issue Title: Joint Other Findings/Recommendations #1 - Funds Transfer Rules - Comply with Travel Rule for BTC Withdrawals
Issue Description:
Finding: The Licensee has not developed policies or procedures for collecting the required information from its customers to comply with the Funds Travel Rule 31 CFR 1010.410(e) & (f) for transmitting VC equivalent to $3,000 or more. To mitigate the risk of non-compliance, the Licensee implemented a $2,000 transaction limit for BTC withdrawals. However, as previously discussed, customers can create multiple accounts. Additionally, the Licensee does not have policies and procedures to identify whether the sender owns and controls the external wallet address.
 

 Management Required Action: Develop policies, procedures, and internal controls to prevent customers from circumventing the BTC withdrawal limits, including the required recordkeeping information related to the Funds Transfer and Funds Travel Rule under 31 CFR 1010.410(e) & (f). Management must also detail how the Company plans to comply with this requirement.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Review existing practices for complying with the Funds Travel Rule and develop any additional policies, procedures, or controls that may be appropriate. (1256308)

Issue Title: Joint Other Findings/Recommendations #2 - Verification of Digital Assets - Ensure Digital Assets Go Through Financial Audit
Issue Description:
Finding: The notes to the audited financial statements briefly describe the digital assets held on customers’ behalf, totaling $427.2 million and $726.4 million on December 31, 2022, and March 31, 2023, respectively. Furthermore, the Licensee provides its external auditors, Ernst & Young (EY), quarterly de minimis test reports to demonstrate its control of, and custody over, the BTC stored in their wallets. However, the results of these tests were excluded from EY’s financial audit report in 2022. Additionally, no documentation of EY’s review and subsequent verification of Block’s BTC holdings was provided during the examination. EY’s review of the Licensee’s digital assets is not included in the scope of the auditor’s engagement letter. Notably, wallet balances were reviewed during the examination. Additionally, examiners reviewed the custody tests provided to EY to evidence control/ownership of the private keys.
 

 Management Required Action: Ensure financial audits or other verifications by a qualified and credentialed third-party firm are conducted on all digital assets, including detailed formal reporting of all totals, as well as verification of the methodologies used. The Licensee is also advised to review the aforementioned HB 1666 to ensure compliance with Texas digital asset service provider requirements.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: No MAP - Ernst and Young, Block’s financial auditor, provides an audit opinion covering Block’s financial statements (1256310)

Issue Title: Joint Other Findings/Recommendations #3 - Bank Account Title - Ensure Bank Accounts are Titled in the Name of Block, Inc.
Issue Description:
Finding: On December 31, 2021, Block changed to its current name from Square, Inc. Examiners sampled four bank accounts for ownership verification and noted that the bank statements of two Wells Fargo bank accounts are still titled under the name of Square, Inc. Per management, the signature cards of the two accounts had not been updated to reflect the name change. In addition, examiners noted the investment account with Clearwater is also titled under Square, Inc.
 

 Management Required Action: Review and update all bank and investment accounts and ensure the current name is consistently applied to all accounts.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: No MAP - Block has reviewed all relevant Wells Fargo bank accounts and has updated the accounts to be titled under the name Block, Inc. (1256312)

Issue Title: Joint Other Findings/Recommendations #4 - Management Action Plans - Track and Manage MAPs
Issue Description:
Finding: Examiners’ review of the management action plan (MAP) revealed several findings from the 2021 joint examination that appeared to be in progress. Management communicated that the findings have all been remediated in some form, but the MAP has not been updated to reflect the corrective actions that have been implemented.
 

 Management Required Action: Ensure corrective actions are noted and updated within the MAP tracker. Keeping an up-to-date MAP ensures that findings and remediation are not being overlooked.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Expand current issues management processes to more comprehensively track corrective actions in response to regulatory exam findings to conclusion. (1256314)

Issue Title: Joint Violation #1 - Failed to Timely Report Director Changes - Report Timely Officer/Director Changes Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: A review of the NMLS revealed that the Licensee failed to timely report changes in officers and directors to the participating states.
 

 Management Required Action: Ensure changes to officers and directors are reported timely to the participating states.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Update and formalize Block’s money transmission licensing reporting procedures with specific steps for notification of officer and director changes in NMLS and outside NMLS. (1256316)

Issue Title: Joint Violation #1 - Failed to Timely Report Director Changes - Report Timely Officer/Director Changes Part 2
Issue Description:
Finding: A review of the NMLS revealed that the Licensee failed to timely report changes in officers and directors to the participating states.
 

 Management Required Action: Ensure changes to officers and directors are reported timely to the participating states.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Update procedures based on 50-state survey of notification requirements. (1256318)

Issue Title: MA Violation #1 - Failed to Maintain Adequate Bond Coverage - Mantain Adequate Bond Coverage for MA
Issue Description:
Finding: The Massachusetts Division of Banks (MADOB)’s regulation 209 CMR 45.03(3)(a) requires each foreign transmittal licensee to maintain a bond or security equal to at least twice the weekly average amount of transactions, or a minimum of $50,000, whichever is greater. Further, as advised in the MADOB’s Annual Report (AR), licensees must periodically review remittance activity and bond coverage throughout the year and increase the bond amount (if necessary) to ensure adequate coverage.
 

 Management Required Action: Review its Massachusetts remittance activity regularly, and at least quarterly, to ensure the minimum required bond coverage is maintained.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: No MAP - Block is committed to preventing future bonding issues and accordingly, Block has updated its procedures to review our volume against the bond requirements on a quarterly basis. (1256320)

Issue Title: MA Violation #2 - Failed to File Accurate Annual Reports - File Accurate Annual Report for MA
Issue Description:
Finding: The MADOB’s regulation 209 CMR 45.12(4) requires foreign transmittal agencies to file an AR with the Commissioner, on or before March 31st of each year, for the preceding calendar year. A review of the Licensee’s 2021 AR revealed discrepancies related to the total amount received for remittance abroad and the required bond amount. Specifically, the total amount received for transmission abroad, as reported on its 2021 AR, differed from transmission figures reported on MSB Call Reports (MSBCRs) and Schedule B submitted to the MADOB.
 

 Management Required Action: Going forward, ensure all information reported on the ARs is accurate.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: No MAP - Block is committed to accuracy in its reporting and has updated its procedures, adding additional instruction to confirm that both Schedules of the MADOB annual report and the MSBCRs align (1256322)

Issue Title: MA Violation #3 - Earnings Performance - Net Losses - Submit Profit Plan to MA
Issue Description:
Finding: The MADOB’s regulation 209 CMR 45.03(2)(a) requires licensed foreign transmittal agencies to demonstrate financial responsibility. The MADOB considers a Licensee’s earnings performance as a measure of its financial responsibility. Block’s earnings performance is considered unsatisfactory. As noted above, Block reported significant comprehensive losses in 2022 and Q1 2023. Block reported comprehensive losses of $1.1 Billion for its 2022 fiscal year, and $68.8 million for the three-month interim period ending on March 31, 2023. The Licensee’s reported losses call into question whether the Licensee is operating in a financially responsible manner.
 

 Management Required Action: Block must submit a formal and measurable profit plan to the MADOB, together with an affirmation of its commitment to the continued maintenance of adequate capital levels, as a demonstration of the financial responsibility required by the MADOB’s regulation 209 CMR 45.03(2)(a). An acceptable profit plan should document the goals and strategies for improved profitability and the enhancement of capital growth and maintenance.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: No MAP - Block will formally submit a profit plan to the MADOB for review and affirms that it is a financially responsible licensee and is committed to continued maintenance of adequate capital levels (1256324)

Issue Title: New Hampshire Other Findings/Recommendations - Trade Name - File Cash App as a Trade Name in NH
Issue Description:
Finding: NH RSA 399-G:9, I. states that no licensee shall engage in the business of money transmission under a trade or other name that is different from the name stated in its principal office license...
 

 The Licensee is conducting activity under the name Cash App, and it is not easily identifiable that Block, Inc. is the provider of this service. The trade name has not been registered with the New Hampshire Secretary of State.
 

 Management Required Action: Register the Cash App trade name with the NH Secretary of State as required.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: No MAP - Block is committed to maintaining identifiable trade names with the states (1256326)

Issue Title: NH Violation - Inaccurate Average Daily Outstanding Money Transmission Liability Calculation - Report NH ADTL Accurately
Issue Description:
Finding: The 2022 submission sent on March 31, 2023, only included New Hampshire’s outstanding money transmission liability and did not include transmission liability in all jurisdictions. A corrected submission for 2022 was provided via NMLS during this examination.
 

 Management Required Action: No immediate action is required; however, the Licensee should ensure that future submissions include all jurisdictions to avoid further fines and violations.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: ensure that future submissions include all jurisdictions (1256328)

Issue Title: Ohio Other Findings/Recommendations - Virtual Currency Volume Reporting – Schedule ST - Report BTC P2P Transactions as ST240 and ST250 on Call Report
Issue Description:
Finding: For the Q1 2023 MSBCR submission, the Licensee reported Ohio P2P VC transactions within the Licensee’s platform on line items ST320 and ST330 of the NMLS call report. However, these P2P transactions should be reported on line items ST240 and ST250.
 

 Management Required Action: Going forward, management should report all Ohio P2P virtual currency transactions on line items ST240 and ST250.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: No MAP - Block’s Bitcoin P2P feature does not fit within the definition for ST240 and ST250. (1256330)

Issue Title: WA Violation - Failed to File Accurate Annual Assessment Reports - File Accurate Assessment Reports
Issue Description:
Finding: The Licensee failed to file accurate Annual Assessment Reports (AARs) for 2020 and 2021, in violation of RCW 19.230.110(2) and WAC 208-690-090. Review of the quarterly MSBCRs filed in the NMLS revealed that virtual currency (VC) transactions were omitted from the annual report filings; therefore, the total volume in AARs were under-reported. The under-reporting of activity did not impact the annual assessment.
 

 Management Required Action: Ensure AARs are filed accurately by including all reportable activity in the annual report filing.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: No MAP - Block has updated its procedures to clarify that the Washington Annual Assessment Report should include virtual currency transactions. (1256332)

Issue Title: Washington Other Findings/Recommendations - Virtual Currency Disclosures - Make VC Disclosures Clear and Conspicouous
Issue Description:
Finding: The Licensee provided all required VC disclosures within the terms of service agreement; however, the disclosures must be provided separately from all other required information as required under RCW 19.230.370 and WAC 208-690-205(3).
 

 Management Required Action: Ensure VC disclosures are provided clearly and conspicuously. The disclosures under RCW 19.230.370 and WAC 208-690-205(a-e) may be disclosed together, but must be disclosed together, but separately from any other information required.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: No MAP - On October 23, 2023, Block updated the Cash App bitcoin disclosures page to separately provide the disclosures required under RCW 19.230.370 and WAC 208-690-205(a-e). (1256334)

Issue Title: Internal Controls and Auditing #1 - Enterprise-Wide Risk Assessment - Conduct ERA Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: The adequacy of the 2022 enterprise-wide risk assessment could not be fully evaluated as the licensee provided a heavily redacted version of the Enterprise-Wide Risk Assessment in which most identified risks, control mitigants and residual risks were obscured from the document. 
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to conduct an enterprise-wide risk assessment to ensure that policies, procedures, and processes are commensurate with the institution’s risk profile, and appropriate oversight is being provided to areas of higher risk. The risk assessment process should be robust, incorporating quantitative data and trends, and periodically updated to reflect changes in the institution’s risk profile.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: We acknowledge that the redactions made to the Enterprise Risk Assessments ("ERA") did not enable the examination team to fully evaluate the adequacy of the 2022 ERA. (1256336)

Issue Title: Internal Controls and Auditing #1 - Enterprise-Wide Risk Assessment - Conduct ERA Part 2
Issue Description:
Finding: The adequacy of the 2022 enterprise-wide risk assessment could not be fully evaluated as the licensee provided a heavily redacted version of the Enterprise-Wide Risk Assessment in which most identified risks, control mitigants and residual risks were obscured from the document. 
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to conduct an enterprise-wide risk assessment to ensure that policies, procedures, and processes are commensurate with the institution’s risk profile, and appropriate oversight is being provided to areas of higher risk. The risk assessment process should be robust, incorporating quantitative data and trends, and periodically updated to reflect changes in the institution’s risk profile.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block will review its current ERA methodology (1256338)

Issue Title: Legal and Regulatory Compliance #2 - Compliance Training - Ensure BSA/AML Training is Completed by All Employees
Issue Description:
Finding: The licensee’s AML/CTF manual includes a policy that requires new staff members to complete AML/CTF training within a reasonable time frame following their start date and each calendar year thereafter. While established AML training procedures are documented, they are not properly monitored as training records showed that nearly 24 percent of the licensee’s personnel, including new hires, did not complete the training.
 

 Management Required Action: Management should strengthen its compliance monitoring activities to ensure adherence to the training policy and procedures. In addition, the policy should be revised to remove the ambiguity of “a reasonable time frame” and specify when training is required for new hires. As a sound practice, training for new hires should be completed not longer than 30 days of the start date.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block acknowledges that some employees did not complete AML training (1256340)

Issue Title: Legal and Regulatory Compliance #3 - NYS Unclaimed Property – Late Filings - Implement Procedures for Monitoring Abandoned Property
Issue Description:
Finding: A review of the 2022 list of funds escheated to the Office of Unclaimed Funds (“OUF”) showed that nearly all the items on the list exceeded the dormancy reporting requirement period of five years. For example, 1,368 items totaling $84.2 thousand were dormant between years ended 2013 and 2016. In addition, the 2021 escheatment list showed that 61 or 2 percent of reported items totaling $15.8 thousand were reported five years after the dormant period which occurred between the years ended 2013 and 2015.
 

 Management Required Action: Management must implement appropriate procedures to ensure that abandoned property is adequately monitored, and funds are escheated to the OUF in accordance with that agency’s reporting requirements.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block believes the NYDFS MTL Examiner miscalculated Block's overdue unclaimed property reports. (1256342)

Issue Title: Legal and Regulatory Compliance #4 - NYS Transaction Receipts – Disclosures - Ensure Receipts Comply with NY SR406.3(f)
Issue Description:
Finding: NYS receipt disclosures require specific information on receipts for every licensee engaged in money transmission. A sample of receipts for Cash App payment transactions funded by customers’ stored balance accounts disclosed that the licensee’s refund policy as well as a statement of liability for non-delivery or delayed delivery were not included on the receipt. As a result, a violation of the Superintendent's Regulations (SR) 406.3(f) is cited.
 

 Management maintains that SR 406.3(f) is not applicable to Cash App stored balance, a prepaid access transaction where the customer is accessing funds paid in advance. Management also believes that S.R. 406.3(f) is not applicable where funds have been accepted for transfer at the direction of the customer. Management further stated that the receipts sampled were funded from the sender's and therefore exempt from New York receipt disclosure requirements. 
 

 The Cash App stored balance is money that a sender has deposited in their cash app or wallet account and payments made by Block to recipients are funded by withdrawing from senders’ Cash App Stored balance which are also based on instructions from senders. According to S.R.406.3(f) “A receipt, or other evidence of acceptance of funds shall be given to every person who utilizes a licensee to transfer funds. The receipt issued by the licensee shall contain ...:
 (1) a statement of the liability of the licensee for non-delivery or delayed delivery. (2) a statement of the refund policy of the licensee; ...
 

 Management Required Action: The licensee should amend its receipts to comply with SR 406.3(f).
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block updated its Cash App payment receipts to include the company's refund policy (1256344)

Issue Title: Legal and Regulatory Compliance #5 - Identity Verification (IDV) Vendor Testing – Periodic Quality Assurance - Test IDV Vendors Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: Block uses multiple vendors to perform IDV for customers accessing their cash or payroll applications. It was noted during this examination that the IDV testing results received from these vendors to either provide or deny customer use of these applications is not reviewed or tested periodically to validate the results received.
 

 IDV is a key control used to determine compliance with BSA\AML obligations and internal policies. Periodic verification of this control point is prudent.
 

 Management Required Action: The licensee must implement a process to periodically test the results that the IDV vendors provide for Block’s use. The IDV vendors may have had independent SOC reviews performed that can provide assurance that IDV processes performed are accurate. The licensee’s review of these reports, and evaluation of controls, could be accepted as a verification process.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block will require all IDV vendors to undergo annual due diligence and will request a SOC report from such vendors as applicable. (1256346)

Issue Description:
Finding: Block uses multiple vendors to perform IDV for customers accessing their cash or payroll applications. Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block will amend existing governing documents (e.g. policies and procedures) (1256348)

Issue Title: Legal and Regulatory Compliance #5 - Identity Verification (IDV) Vendor Testing – Periodic Quality Assurance - Test IDV Vendors Part 3
Issue Description:
Finding: Block uses multiple vendors to perform IDV for customers accessing their cash or payroll applications. The licensee’s review of these reports, and evaluation of controls, could be accepted as a verification process.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A

Issue Title: Legal and Regulatory Compliance #5 - Identity Verification (IDV) Vendor Testing – Periodic Quality Assurance - Test IDV Vendors Part 4
Issue Description:
Finding: Block uses multiple vendors to perform IDV for customers accessing their cash or payroll applications. It was noted during this examination that the IDV testing results received from these vendors to either provide or deny customer use of these applications is not reviewed or tested periodically to validate the results received.
 

 IDV is a key control used to determine compliance with BSA\AML obligations and internal policies. Periodic verification of this control point is prudent.
 

 Management Required Action: The licensee must implement a process to periodically test the results that the IDV vendors provide for Block’s use. The IDV vendors may have had independent SOC reviews performed that can provide assurance that IDV processes performed are accurate. The licensee’s review of these reports, and evaluation of controls, could be accepted as a verification process.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block will conduct regular IDV vendor testing according to the governing documents, and testing process as stated above. (1256352)

Issue Title: Management #6 - Heavily Redacted Reports and Meeting Minutes - Reduce Redactions of Board Materials
Issue Description:
Finding: The licensee provided copies of the quarterly meeting minutes and Pre-Read Materials (“documents”) for the Audit and Risk Committee of Block, Inc. While the minutes disclosed the meeting dates, time, place, names of members and directors who were present and absent, the documents were so heavily redacted, at times reaching several continuous pages, that examiners were unable to determine the Board’s and management’s respective roles and ability to identify, measure, and monitor the risks of the licensee’s activities. Management must be mindful of New York Banking Law Section 646 as well as the New York Superintendent’s Regulations Part 406.7 Examinations and make available full and complete documents to examiners to allow for appropriate review.
 The superintendent shall have the power to examine any licensee at any time when in the judgment of the superintendent such examination is necessary or advisable to determine:
 (a) the financial condition of the licensee.
 (b) the safety and soundness of the conduct of its business.
 (c) the policies of its management.
 (d) whether the requirements of law have been complied with in the administration of its affairs. (e) whether the licensee has policies and procedures sufficient to control the activities of any authorized agents; and
 (f) such other matters, as the superintendent may determine, including but not limited to any activities of the licensee outside this State if in the opinion of the superintendent such activities may affect the licensee's money transmission business in this State.
 

 Management Required Action:
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: We acknowledge that the redactions made to the Pre-Read Materials for the Audit and Risk Committee (1256354)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #7 - Comprehensive Information Technology (“IT”) General Controls Audit – REPEAT FINDING - Conduct Audit of GLBA Compliance Part 2
Issue Description:
Finding: The current IT Audit plan focuses on the review of IT and related controls as a subcategory of financial reporting controls (Sarbanes-Oxley “SOX” IT general controls) testing. There is no comprehensive audit of IT or existing IT controls. While the 2022 Audit Plan included IT-related audits, the Plan was not supported by an IT audit risk assessment. An IT Audit Plan based on IT risk assessment results should be developed to ensure that IT audit coverage at Block is comprehensive and risk-based and not limited to financial reporting controls.
 

 This is a repeat finding from the 2020 Multi-state Report of Examination.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to conduct a comprehensive IT Audit Risk Assessment and use the results to develop an IT risk-based audit plan and program. Management is also required to expand audit review coverage to include business continuity management and compliance with the Gramm- Leach-Bliley Act (“GLBA”).
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Please refer to Block's response to Systems and Technology Finding #1 from Block's NYDFS Virtual Currency Report of Examination. (1256356)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #8 - IT Audit Report Formality (Repeat) - Create IT Audit Report Formality Part 2
Issue Description:
Finding: There is no evidence that management is provided with written IT Audit reports. IT Audit findings are reported through the use of a “gap log” and presentation of the issues at Audit & Risk Committee meetings. Examiners were unable to fully assess the reporting phase as the IT audits were in progress and there were no reports to evidence the required written report process. Examiners also found no audit rating system in place. A rating system can inform management of the degree of risk, severity, and necessity for timely action associated with audit findings. In addition, a rating system is an industry sound practice and recommendation under the FFIEC guidance.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to establish procedures for written audit reports that are presented to the board or delegated committee of the board. Management is also required to implement a rating system in order to facilitate a consistent and concise assessment of the risk posed by the area or function being audited.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Please refer to Block's response to Systems and Technology Finding #2 from Block's NYDFS Virtual Currency Report of Examination. (1256358)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #9 - IT Audit Program - Align IA with FFIEC Guidance Part 2
Issue Description:
Finding: Although an Internal Audit Department charter is in place, written audit policies, procedures and standards have not been established. Pursuant to FFIEC guidance, elements of an effective internal audit program should include risk assessment methodology, a system for rating audits, standards for audit workpapers, reporting guidelines, follow-up procedures and retention policies. These components are necessary in order for the board or delegated committee to provide appropriate oversight and effectively evaluate the levels of risk exposure and control issues affecting the institution.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to develop an Internal Audit policy, standards, and procedures, that conform to the audit program elements prescribed by FFIEC Guidance.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block will develop and adopt an Internal Audit policy, procedures, and standards, taking into account, as appropriate, FFIEC guidance and other regulatory and industry guidance. (1256360)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #10 - Third Party Service Provider Policy & Program – REPEAT FINDING – PARTIALLY RESOLVED - Align TPRM with Part 500.11 Requirements Part 3
Issue Description:
Finding: The institution’s Third-Party Service Provider policies and procedures do not include all the elements required by 23 NYCRR 500.11. Current program, policies, standards, and procedures are not sufficiently detailed as to:
 • the identification and assessment risks, in violation of 23 NYCRR 500.11(a)(1) & (3), and • periodic risk-based assessments of all providers, in violation of 23 NYCRR 500.11(a)(4), • due diligence guidelines/contractual protections pertaining to cybersecurity event
 notifications, in violation of 23 NYCRR 500.11(b)(3), and
 • cybersecurity related representations and warranties, in violation of 23 NYCRR 500.11(b)(4).
 

 Management was unable to provide the examiner with a complete list of its vendors, and approximately a third of the vendors identified as critical did not have an assigned risk rating or the date that the most recent due diligence review was conducted. Examiners also noted that a process is not in place to periodically verify the vendor population, to provide assurance that ongoing and effective oversight is being provided to all vendors.
 

 This is a partially repeat finding from the 2020 Multi-state Report of Examination.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to enhance policies and procedures to bring them into compliance with the applicable sections of 23 NYCRR 500.11. All vendors must be risk rated and subjected to periodic re-evaluations, based upon the assigned risks. Management is also required to implement a reconciliation process to ensure that oversight is being provided to all third-party service providers.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block has reviewed and updated its TPRM policies and procedures (1256362)

All vendors must be risk rated and subjected to periodic re-evaluations, based upon the assigned risks. Management is also required to implement a reconciliation process to ensure that oversight is being provided to all third-party service providers.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block has revised its process going forward to identify third-party service providers pertaining to money transmission activity (1256364)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #11 - CISO Reports - Submit CISO Report to DFS Part 1
Issue Description:
Finding: The redacted board minutes did not provide evidence that annual status reports on the institution’s Cybersecurity Program were presented by the CISO, in violation of 23 NYCRR 200.16(d) and 23 NYCRR 500.04(b). The Licensee asserted attorney-client privilege for not providing the CISO’s written reports. Thus, examiners did not assess the adequacy of the CISO’s written report at this review.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to ensure that the CISO’s annual written report on the status of the institution’s Cybersecurity Program and related material risks is reviewed by the board and documented in the minutes. Management is also required to submit this annual written report to NYSDFS to comply with the regulation.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block has reassessed its approach to redaction (1256366)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #11 - CISO Reports - Submit CISO Report to DFS Part 2
Issue Description:
Finding: The redacted board minutes did not provide evidence that annual status reports on the institution’s Cybersecurity Program were presented by the CISO, in violation of 23 NYCRR 200.16(d) and 23 NYCRR 500.04(b). The Licensee asserted attorney-client privilege for not providing the CISO’s written reports. Thus, examiners did not assess the adequacy of the CISO’s written report at this review.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to ensure that the CISO’s annual written report on the status of the institution’s Cybersecurity Program and related material risks is reviewed by the board and documented in the minutes. Management is also required to submit this annual written report to NYSDFS to comply with the regulation.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block has also updated its Information Security Policy to reflect the requirement that the CISO submit their annual written report to the Board and to NYDFS. (1256368)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #12 - Cyber Event Notification Procedures - Develop Procedures for Reporting Cybersecurity Events Part 2
Issue Description:
Finding: The Licensee does not maintain procedures for reporting cyber events to the NYSDFS, in violation of 23 NYCRR 500.17(a). In addition, the Incident Response Plan does not reference the required timeframes or reporting process. The lack of cyber event reporting procedures may hinder management’s ability to consistently identify reportable events and submit the timely reports to the NYSDFS.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to establish procedures for the timely reporting cyber events to NYSDFS. The NYSDFS Cybersecurity Industry Guidance should be utilized in developing the procedures.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block has developed an incident response plan to specifically address reporting of cyber events to the Department. See Exhibit F. (1256370)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #13 - Audit Log Retention Standard - Align Audit Log Retention Standard with Part 200.16 - Part 2
Issue Description:
Finding: The Audit logs, Alerts, and Monitoring standard requires retention of system audit logs for at least one year, which does not conform with the three-year minimum retention requirement prescribed by 23 NYCRR 500.06(b) (Fiat). Examiners noted that actual retention practices were counter to stated policy and conformed with regulatory requirements.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to revise its internal policy to align with current practice and the required three-year retention period.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block has revised its Audit, Logs and Alerts Standard Policy to comply with 23 NYCRR 500.06(b) (Fiat). (1256372)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #14 - Business Continuity Management - Align BCPs with Part 200.17 Part 5
Issue Description:
Finding: The licensee’s Business Continuity Plan is not comprehensive, as it does not address each of the components recommended in the FFIEC Business Continuity Management IT Handbook and was limited to the firm’s Pandemic Response and Disaster recovery procedures for its BitBank process. In addition, there is no evidence of independent testing or an audit review of the firm’s business continuity plan, though such testing is also recommended by FFIEC Guidance.
 

 These issues may hinder the licensee’s ability to recover effectively from a business continuity event. Insufficient independent testing and management reporting to the board may limit the board’s ability to provide effective oversight of the licensee’s business continuity management program.
 

 Management Required Action: Management is required to enhance Block’s business continuity plans to include the components recommended in the FFIEC Business Continuity Management IT Handbook and ensure that independent testing is conducted. Management is also required to conduct a full-failover test of Cash App, observed by independent personnel.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block will create comprehensive, high-level Business Continuity Plans (BCPs) that align with FFIEC Guidance. (1256374)

Issue Title: Systems and Technology #15 - Cybersecurity Governance - Enhance Cybersecurity Governance Part 4
Issue Description:
Finding: The Information Security Policy and other policies that compose the licensee’s cyber security policy is not subjected to annual board review and approval. Additionally, the CISO has a dual reporting structure to the Audit and Risk Committee as well as the Head of Foundational Engineering. Since Foundational Engineering includes operational responsibilities, this reporting line gives the appearance of a potential conflict of interest, that could cause IT security decisions and funding to be influenced by operational ease or budgetary constraints.
 

 Management Required Action: The board is ultimately responsible for governance and oversight of the licensee’s information security program. Therefore, the licensee’s board, or designated board committee, should approve the licensee’s written information security program, and affirm responsibilities for the development, implementation, and maintenance of the program at least annually. Evidence of policy approvals by board and/or management committees should be memorialized in the meeting minutes. Management is also required to ensure that the CISO’s reporting line is independent of IT Operations.
Issue Rating Justification: N/A
Issue Action Plans: Block's Information Security Policy has been updated to include a requirement for annual Board review. (1256380)

Issue Title: FirstSource PIF Paid Last Four of Account
Issue Description: In November of 2021, we made a change to our PIF paid email due to avoid potential customer confusion if the balance became $0 due to a balance adjustment and to avoid confusion regarding the last payment amount if the customer made multiple payments to Afterpay. The solution was to create a new template that would state the balance was $0. This email was created separately and did not use any of the existing master templates. When it was created in a test environment, the last four of the account was hardcoded to display a value. When it was moved to production, the variable was never added to replace the variable.
RCA Description: A new template was created to send to customers when their balance was 0. The test email was created with the variable for the last four of the account number hardcoded. This was not changed when the email was pushed to production.
Issue Rating Justification: No consumers filed a complaint with Afterpay or FirstSource.
Closure Request Justification: The issue has been resolved as of 1/14/2025. Please refer to Incident Report.

Issue Title: Lending and Consumer ProtectionTraining course content requires an uplift to align with the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau and Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council guidance to be tailored to help implement internal policies and procedures.
Issue Description:
Regulatory Guidance:
 The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) and Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council (FFIEC) both express in guidance the importance of relevant, targeted compliance training for staff as summarized below by the Compliance Management Review Exam Manual of the CFPB:
 'Management and staff should receive specific, comprehensive training that reinforces and helps implement written policies and procedures. Requirements for compliance with Federal consumer financial laws, including prohibitions against unlawful discrimination and unfair, deceptive, and abusive acts and practices, should be incorporated into training for all relevant officers and employees.'
 

 Testing Results:
 The 17 courses provided in the US Afterpay training program, '2024 Lending and Consumer Protection Training', requires tailoring to meet regulatory expectations for specificity and reinforcement of company policies and procedures. Each course provides high level regulatory training that is not tailored to the Afterpay program and would benefit from uplift. 
 

 Design of the training course content could consider:
 - connecting role specific content to applicable aspects of the rule
 - adding specific examples of customer communications, disclosures, loan agreements, and statements
 - reducing the amount of inapplicable content (i.e. mortgage content), including test questions 
 - linking to company policies and/or procedures
 - removing duplicate course work (e.g. courses 'Red Flags of Identity Theft' and 'The Red Flags Rule' share similar themes and rules that could be combined)

Issue Title: Retro and Single Use Payment (SUP) Loan Agreements refer to early repayment refunds in accordance with state laws but Retro (First Electronic Bank originated) do not offer early repayment refunds, and Retro (Square Capital Originated) and SUP (Cash App Over Time) have a standard refund process irrespective of the state the loan originated.
Issue Description:
Agreement Requirement:
 Retro Loan Agreement (FEB Originated), Effective September 4, 2024 - Present, Section 8.c Repayments 
 Upon the early prepayment of your loan, Bank will refund any unearned amount of the Finance Charge in accordance with the law of your state of residence when you entered into this Agreement.
 

 Retro Loan Agreement (Square Capital Originated) , Effective November 12, 2024 - Present, Section 6.c Prepayment Options
 Upon the early prepayment of your loan, we will refund any unearned amount of the Finance Charge in accordance with the law of your state of residence when you entered into this Agreement, but in no event in a manner less favorable than the actuarial method assuming payments are made when scheduled.
 

 SUP (Cash App Pay Over Time) Loan Agreement, Effective April 2, 2024 - Present, Section 6.c Prepayment Options
 You may prepay your loan in full or in part at any time. There is no fee or charge for early payment of all or any part of your loan. Upon the early prepayment of your loan, we will refund any unearned amount of the Finance Charge in accordance with the law of your state of residence when you entered into this Agreement, but in no event in a manner less favorable than the actuarial method assuming payments are made when scheduled.
 

 Issue Description:
 The Retro Loan Agreements (First Electronic Bank (FEB) Originated, Section 8.c and Square Capital Originated, Section 6.c) and SUP Loan Agreement state that early repayments are based on state law, "...will refund any unearned amount of the Finance Charge in accordance with the law of your state of residence when you entered into this Agreement." However, business practices do not consider state laws since:
 Early repayment refunds are not applied to FEB originated Retro loans, and
 Retro Square Capital originated and SUP loans early repayment refunds are the same for all customers, regardless of state. Customers receive an early repayment refund that is proportionate to their setup fee and repayment period.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: Truth in Lending Act (TILA) disclosure content requirements (12 CFR 1026.18(c)(d)(p) - itemization of amount, finance charge and contract reference were not included or were inconsistently applied in the Borrow and Retro TILA Agreements.
Issue Description:
Regulation Requirement:
 12 CFR 1026.18 Content of disclosures For each transaction other than a mortgage transaction, the creditor shall disclose the following information as applicable: 
  (c) Itemization of amount financed.(1) Except as provided in paragraphs (c)(2) - (Right to request statement) and (c)(3) - (NA. RESPA) of this section, a separate written itemization of the amount financed, including:
  (iv) The prepaid finance charge.
  Official Interpretation: 
 Paragraph 18(c)(1)(iv) 1. Prepaid finance charge. Prepaid finance charges that are deducted under §1026.18(b)(3) must be disclosed under this section. 
 Paragraph 18(b)(3) Amount Financed
 (1)(ii): In some instances, as when loan fees are financed by the creditor, finance charges are incorporated in the face amount of the note...When the finance charges are included in the principal loan amount, they should be deducted as prepaid finance charges under §1026.18(b)(3)
  (d) Finance charge. The finance charge, using that term, and a brief description such as “the dollar amount the credit will cost you.”
  (p) Contract reference. A statement that the consumer should refer to the appropriate contract document for information about nonpayment, default, the right to accelerate the maturity of the obligation, and prepayment rebates and penalties. .
 

 Issue Description:
 The Borrow and Retro TILA Agreements did not include required regulatory disclosure content:
  1. Borrow and Retro: The term "finance charge" was not used for the Borrow one-month, Borrow two-month, and Retro TILA agreements. Instead, the business used the term, "finance fee."
  2. Retro: 
  a. Retro did not include the finance charge in the itemization of amount financed but the Borrow TILA Agreements disclosed the finance charge.
  i. Retro Example: "Itemization of amount financed, 100% of the $25.94 Amount Financed is distributed directly to your Cash balance."
  ii. Borrow Example: "Itemization of amount financed, 100% of the $80 amount financed is distributed directly to your Cash balance. The $4 is a prepaid finance charge."
  b. The Retro Loan Agreement is linked for disclosure acceptance purposes ("By tapping Confirm, you agree to the: E-Sign Consent, Privacy Notices, and Autopay Terms & Loan Agreement".); however, it does not explicitly state that the customer may refer to it for additional loan information. Note, the Borrow TILA Agreement explicitly states, "For more details about nonpayment, default, prepayment refunds, and other terms, see the Loan Agreement."
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: Borrow and Retro marketing materials using triggering terms did not disclose the Truth in Lending Act (TILA) additional required advertising terms (12 CFR 1026.24(d)(2) - downpayment, terms of repayment, and annual percentage rate).
Issue Description:
Regulation Requirement: 12 CFR 1026.24(d) Advertisement of terms that require additional disclosures - 
 (1) Triggering terms. If any of the following terms is set forth in an advertisement, the advertisement shall meet the requirements of paragraph (d)(2) of this section:
  (i) The amount or percentage of any downpayment.
  (ii) The number of payments or period of repayment.
  (iii) The amount of any payment.
  (iv) The amount of any finance charge.
 

 Official Interpretation: 
 Paragraph 24(d)
  1. General rule. These provisions apply even if the triggering term is not stated explicitly but may be readily determined from the advertisement.
 4. Finance charge. 
  i. The dollar amount of the finance charge or any portion of it includes statements such as:
  A. “$500 total cost of credit.”
  B. “$2 monthly carrying charge.”
  C. “$50,000 mortgages, 2 points to the borrower.”
  ii. In the last example, the $1,000 prepaid finance charge can be readily determined from the information given.
 

 (2) Additional terms. An advertisement stating any of the terms in paragraph (d)(1) of this section shall state the following terms, as applicable (an example of one or more typical extensions of credit with a statement of all the terms applicable to each may be used):
  (i) The amount or percentage of the downpayment.
  (ii) The terms of repayment, which reflect the repayment obligations over the full term of the loan, including any balloon payment.
  (iii) The “annual percentage rate,” using that term, and, if the rate may be increased after consummation, that fact.
  
 Issue Description:
 The Borrow Marketing Emails and Notifications and Retro Notifications link to the product flow screens below that contain triggering terms but do not disclose the required additional terms i.e. downpayment, terms of repayment, and the annual percentage rate using that term.
  a. The Borrow Flow Triggering Terms are:
  i. 1026.24(d)(1)(ii) - Number of payments or period of repayment (all at once or in 4 installments)
  ii. 1026.24(d)(1)(iv) - Amount of any finance charge (5% flat fee. Note, the finance charge amount can be determined since the advertise included the language, 'up to $200')
 

  b. The Retro Flow Triggering Terms are:
  i. 1026.24(d)(1)(ii) - Number of payments or period of repayment (More time to pay: Get 4 weeks to pay it back)
  ii. 1026.24(d)(1)(iv) - (Simple fee and no credit check: Pay a flat X% fee)
