Issue Title: Early repayment refund calculations did not use the TILA Agreement origination date due to system programming which adjusts when a customer moves to a new US timezone after loan origination.
Issue Description:
Policy Requirement: 
 Truth in Lending Act Policy, Cash App (U.S.) l Lending Product, January 2025
 Page 7, Purpose: TILA was adopted to ensure that credit terms are disclosed in a meaningful way so consumers can knowledgeable and easily compare credit terms before entering into a credit transaction.
 

 Page 23, Closed-End Credit, General Disclosure Requirements: Cash App will provide the disclosures to the Consumer in a reasonably understandable form.
 

 Issue Description: 
 Refund calculation for 2 of 7,334 Borrow customers (<1%) used the incorrect origination dates during computation due to a system programming that adjusts origination and due date times when customers move to new timezones. Both customers moved to a timezone that is three hours ahead. Based on their loan origination time (November 1, 2024, 11:30 pm PST and October 1, 2024, 11:39 pm PST), the timezone move resulted in changed origination dates for the refund calculation (November 2, 2024, 2:39 am EST and October 2, 2024, 2:39 am). Note, in both instances the due date times were adjusted based on the new timezone but remained on the same day.
 There was no consumer harm since the refunds were in the customers' favor by $0.12 cents and $0.01 cent; however, the system origination date did not reflect the TILA Agreement date for the calculation.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Monitoring: 2024 Q4; Cash App Lending - Borrow, Retro, Single Use Payment (SUP); Truth in Lending Act (TILA) (1030573)
Interested Parties:
Vivek Tatineni
 Zhipeng Ouyang
 Mia Bockus
Issue Rating Justification: Presents a moderate risk to the operations of Block. Overall exposure (existing or potential) requires a corrective action plan with priority.
Issue Action Plans:
Early repayment refund calculations did not use the TILA Agreement origination date due to system programming which adjusts when a customer moves to a new US timezone after loan origination. (1258836)
 Early repayment refund calculations did not use the TILA Agreement origination date due to system programming which adjusts when a customer moves to a new US timezone after loan origination. (1259480)
 Product Compliance will develop a policy to address refund thresholds. This new policy will determine the remediation action for early repayment refunds impacted by the US timezone changes. (1283248)
Closure Request Justification:
UPDATE: 
 ENG has adjusted the timezone to reflect the origination location, rather than the current state of the customer. 
 

 GCMT validated the updated code pulls the timezone from the data based on loan origination date based on the updated string of code. No independent transactional testing was conducted as the data required to test would require there to be a loan repaid early by a customer who moved from one state to a state with a different timezone. To locate this data GCMT could potentially wait months. Rather, GCMT will include this testing element during the upcoming November 2025 monitoring review which focuses on ECOA, FCRA, and TILA.
 

 No further action required.
 

 

 

 

 Action #1258836
 April 3, 2025: On February 13, 2025, the business implemented the correction for early repayment refunds to align with TILA agreement dates by utilizing the loan origination timezone. This update ensures that both origination and due dates are calculated based on the borrower's timezone at the time of loan initiation. 
 

 Validation testing on 43 loans with timezone changes and early repayments during February 14 - March 27, 2025 confirmed accuracy in all cases. 4 of the 43 loans had address relocations that could have potentially affected timing calculations, and the review confirmed that calculations remained accurate and unimpacted. 
 

 Refer to the action plan for supporting documentation. 
 

 This management action plan is complete.

Issue Title: Inadequate Analysis of Selection of Thresholds for Cash App Customer Source of Wealth Collection, Analysis and Verification
Issue Description:
During the covered period, Block implemented additional controls to collect, analyze, and verify Source of Wealth (“SoW”) information from Cash App customers whose transaction volume exceeds defined thresholds. When (i) high risk customers receive inflows exceeding $2,000 per week and (ii) moderate or low risk customers receive inflows exceeding $15,000 per week, the account owners are sent a questionnaire to provide their employment status, income sources, and income range. Based on the responses, certain customers (e.g., those paid $250,000 or more per year by an employer) are required to upload supporting documentation (e.g., pay stub, W-2, bank statement).
 The aforementioned thresholds triggering the collection, analysis and verification of SoW information do not appear reasonably designed in that:
 1) They do not consider the impact of outflow transactions (e.g., cashing out stored balance to a linked external bank account);
 2) The rationale for selection of the $15,000 per week collection threshold applied to Moderate and Low risk customers is not documented within and is not clearly aligned with Management’s underlying analysis of threshold selection; and
 3) The verification thresholds do not consider the extent to which a customer’s reported employment status and income align with transactional activity observed on the platform, nor whether reported income aligns with occupation.
 With respect to item 1, unusual outflow transactions may be an indication that a customer is engaging in suspicious activity; for example, users cashing out a material portion of their reported annual income over a period of days or weeks should be further investigated. With respect to item 2, Management shared an analysis of platform activity throughout July 2023 demonstrating that average inflows per user over a 30-day period range from $1,300 - $4,000, which is not aligned with the $15,000 collection threshold selected for Moderate and Low risk customers. Further, the analysis provided did not indicate why the $15,000 collection threshold was selected. With respect to item 3, the current approach to SoW verification does not allow Cash App to fully investigate red flags associated with customers transacting a material portion of reported annual income in all scenarios (e.g., a customer reporting annual income of $35,000 - $74,999 from an employer who cashes in between $17,500 - $37,500 over a period of days or weeks is not required to verify SoW).
 

 Risk:
 Block fails to identify suspicious Cash App activity due to its lack of ability to determine expected / baseline activity per customer based on SoW information.
 Block has not adequately assessed the ML / TF risk of its Cash App customers based on SoW information.
 

 Mitigating Circumstances:
 Cash App’s Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) and Transaction Monitoring programs / procedures provide sufficient flexibility for compliance analysts to request SoW information from users on a discretionary basis in response to ML / TF red flags and transaction activity that triggers the queue, including inflows and outflows. The EDD and Transaction Monitoring procedures were in effect prior to and throughout the covered period.
 Cash App has Transaction Monitoring rules in place that consider outflow transactions to generate alerts for potentially suspicious or unacceptable activity.
Issue Rating Justification:
Process and/or control gaps that may result in:
 Significant deficiency that is less severe than a material weakness but still important enough to merit attention 
 Significant decrease in operational efficiency
 Non-compliance with regulatory expectations, laws or policy that may result in moderate penalties or correction actions 
 Negative short-term regional coverage and perception of Block’s (and/or entities) 
 Potential for fraud to occur
 Significant security vulnerabilities, moderate data breaches and partial system compromises
Issue Action Plans:
MAP 1. SOW Threshold Analysis (1263442)
 MAP 2. SOW Procedure Update (1263446)

Issue Title: Inadequate Governance over Risk Assessment Results / Conclusions
Issue Description:
The 2023 Block AML Risk Assessment executed during the covered period was performed using the “Ready Assess” tool. The tool is a third party tool which is managed / administered by a dedicated Block Compliance team responsible for entering risk assessment questionnaire responses (provided by each business unit) completely and accurately to calculate residual risk.
 

 Internal Audit identified 752 instances (~16%) where the source data input into Ready Assess differed from the corresponding BSA/AML, Sanctions, and Control Effectiveness risk assessment questionnaire responses that serve as the source data for the risk assessment process. Documentation rationalizing or substantiating the changes that were made was not provided by Compliance and the process for approving overrides was not documented in the risk assessment policies and procedures.
 

 In establishing the risk assessment workflow using Ready Assess, the Compliance team indicated that a process to verify the accuracy of data entered into the tool was in place. However, because documented policies and procedures governing the review, approval, and documentation of the rationale for changes and overrides to the original responses from the questionnaires do not exist, and no persuasive evidence could be provided by Compliance to support that this process occurred and to what extent, IA was unable to validate that the review procedures occurred as communicated by Compliance leadership.
 

 Risk:
 Block may struggle to demonstrate to regulators how risk assessment conclusions are supported by its methodology.
 Risk assessment results offer limited or unreliable value / insight to Block Management due to data integrity issues.
 

 Mitigating Circumstances:
 Internal Audit reviewed the Block, Inc. risk assessment results presented to and approved by the Board of Directors and did not identify any residual risk scores that appear materially unreasonable or inaccurate.

 Elizabeth Miller

Issue Title: Excessive Length of Time to Complete IDV for Sponsored Users Attempting to Graduate to their own Cash App Investing Account
Issue Description:
Cash App Investing’s graduation process for sponsored
 (i.e., underage) users engaging through the Families
 product requires that sponsored users (underage minors)
 attempt identity verification (IDV, i.e., CIP) upon turning
 18.5 years of age in order to “graduate” to an independent
 Cash App Investing account. However, the process in
 place to comply with this requirement is not designed
 appropriately in that graduating sponsored users who fail
 identity verification due to having a thin credit file have
 approximately one and a half years to pass IDV and open a
 new Cash App Investing account, even if they repeatedly
 fail IDV. This approximately one and a half year period is
 an excessive amount of time to allow sponsored users to
 attempt to open a Cash App Investing account and
 complete IDV. Internal Audit notes that 31 CFR §
 1023.220(a)(2)(ii), Customer Identification Programs for
 Broker-Dealers: Customer Verification, states that “the CIP
 must contain procedures for verifying the identity of each
 customer... within a reasonable time before or after the
 customer’s account is opened.” Internal Audit’s
 understanding of regulatory expectation is that 1.5 years
 for the sponsored user to attempt to complete IDV to
 open an account does not appear to constitute
 “reasonable time before the customer’s account is
 opened.”
 Subsequent to fieldwork, Internal Audit was informed by
 CAI and Block that the Cash App Families team is working
 on a proposal to require the sponsored users to complete
 (i.e. successfully pass) the IDV process at 18.5 years of age
 and not just require them to attempt to IDV. In the plans,
 if the sponsored user does not complete (i.e. does not
 pass) IDV by age 18.5, the money movement will be
 restricted. This work is expected to be completed in Q2.
 Risk
 ● Cash App Investing may not know the true identity
 of adult sponsored users, thereby limiting the
 company’s ability to investigate potentially
 suspicious activity and provide meaningful insights
 to law enforcement.
 Mitigating Circumstances
 1. Cash App Investing’s CIP, requiring that the adult
 sponsors of underage users pass identity
 verification prior to establishing the sponsored
 account, is designed appropriately and operating
 effectively. Therefore, the company is capable of
 mapping graduated accounts that fail identity
 verification to a verified identity on the platform.
 2. When Cash App Investing denylists accounts tied
 to adult sponsors, the connected sponsored
 accounts are restricted to balance withdrawal
 transactions only.
 3. Sponsored accounts that fail identity verification at
 the point of graduation remain subject to Cash
 App Investing’s Transaction Monitoring program,
 thereby allowing Cash App Investing to identify
 potentially suspicious activity.
 4. Cash App Investing will close the accounts of
 sponsorees who have not successfully completed
 IDV by the age 20.
 5. Sponsorees who do not successfully complete IDV
 are not permitted to open a graduated account
 until such time as they pass IDV.
 6. Sponsored users may have difficulties completing
 CIP due to a thin credit file and lack of documents
 necessary for successful IDV.
 7. CAI and Block have processes whereby the EDD
 Team will review failed IDV attempts by Sponsored
 Users and consider whether suspicious
 behavior/activity may have occurred and if an
 escalation to the SAR Team is necessary.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Expectation
Issue Progress Updates: 10/28KP: updated issue owner. Waiting on approval of extended due date/rationale.
Issue Action Plans: Update Graduation Process for Sponsored Accounts (1263459)

Issue Title: Inadequate Keyword Analysis - Cash App Sanctions Screening
Issue Description:
Block has not performed a sufficient analysis to demonstrate that the keywords configured in Cash App’s model designed to screen payment messages for potential sanctions hits are appropriate / reasonable.
 Throughout the covered period, the payment message screening model was configured with the following Russia-related keywords only: “Putin”, “Ukraine”, “Crimea”, “Donetsk”, “Luhansk”, “DNR”, LNR”, “Doneck”, “Lugansk”, and “Donets Basin”. In response to Internal Audit’s request for the analysis justifying the limited keyword selection, Management provided a dataset indicating that the terms “Russia”, “Putin”, and “Ukraine” would not generate productive sanctions alerts over the sample period.
 The aforementioned analysis appears flawed in that it did not consider:
 Keyword combinations containing the names of specific industries, companies, and individuals (ex. Putin) associated with Russia-related sanctions (e.g., “Russia” + “Oil & Gas” or “Energy”, “Russia” + “Gazprom”, “Russia” + “Peskov”, etc.); or
 Keywords associated with government sanctions programs unrelated to Russia (e.g., Iran, Syria).
 

 Risk:
 Block may process transactions involving sanctioned individuals, entities or sectors in violation of OFAC regulations. 
 Block may fail to block or reject transactions subject to OFAC sanctions and may fail to report such transactions to OFAC as required by OFAC regulations.
 

 Mitigating Circumstances:
 Cash App’s processes / tools used to screen the names of customers, employees, and vendors for potential sanctions matches are not impacted by this observation (as they are separate and distinct processes).
 Cash App P2P is a closed-loop payments product that is currently only available in the US. As a closed-loop product, it is only available to customers of Cash App. All Cash App customers are subject to sanctions screening. Given the domestic and closed-loop nature of the product and that all Cash App customers are subject to sanctions screening, the product presents a low sanctions risk.
Issue Rating Justification:
Process and/or control gaps that may result in:
 Deficiency that is unlikely to have a significant impact on financial reporting or operations 
 Decrease in operational efficiency 
 Minor non-compliance with regulatory expectations, laws or policy and/or insufficient documentation
 Unfavorable short-term local coverage and perception of Block’s (and/or entities) 
 Minor security issues, minor data breaches and isolated system issues
Issue Action Plans:
Sanctions Keyword Analysis (1264111)
 Potential Sanctions Keyword List Update (1264114)

Issue Title: Excessive Length of Time to Complete IDV for Sponsored Users Attempting to Graduate to their own Cash App Account
Issue Description:
Block’s graduation process for sponsored (i.e., underage) users engaging through the Families product requires that sponsored users (underage minors) attempt identity verification (IDV, i.e., CIP) upon turning 18.5 years of age in order to graduate to an independent Cash App account. However, the process in place to comply with this requirement is not designed appropriately in that graduating sponsored users who fail identity verification due to having a thin credit file have approximately one and a half years to pass IDV and open a new Cash App account, even if they repeatedly fail IDV. This approximately one and a half year period is an excessive amount of time to allow sponsored users to attempt to open a Cash App account and complete IDV. 
 

 Subsequent to fieldwork, Internal Audit was informed by Block that the Cash App Families team is working on a proposal to require the sponsored users to complete (i.e. successfully pass) the IDV process at 18.5 years of age and not just require them to attempt to IDV. Issue Title: KYC Reviews and Alerts Not Completed According to SLA
Issue Description:
Internal Audit noted a high exception rate (approximately 29.6%) of KYC Alerts/Reviews closed after the 30-day SLA. Compliance indicated that the reason for the late Alert/Review closures were due to capacity constraints as well as an influx of alerts/cases in Q1 2024 for CashApp.
  
 Across a Sample of 125 KYC Alerts/Reviews tested by Internal Audit, Internal Audit identified 37 KYC Alerts/Reviews that were not reviewed and dispositioned by the relevant Compliance Operations teams within the 30-day SLA required by Block's internal procedures (an exception rate of ~29.6%). The alerts/reviews completed outside of SLA are as follows:
 Square Merchant: 14 out of 25 alerts sampled were completed outside of SLA;
 Payroll: 7 out of 25 alerts sampled were completed outside of SLA;
 KYC Information Change Reviews: 16 out of 25 reviews sampled were completed outside of SLA
  
 The Compliance Analytics Team built a Capacity Model in 2022 to forecast staffing needs to appropriately manage and disposition alert reviews timely to prevent backlogs. Compliance informed Internal Audit that the Capacity Model identified the need for resources, however Block was unable to hire additional staff in the necessary time. Block stated that as a result, they pivoted the resourcing strategy to leverage outsourcing at a greater volume, which allowed time to hire additional resources for agents working alerts and for the quality team to oversee performance of work by outsourcing and internal staff. 
 Internal Audit noted that Compliance provided evidence that there were no backlogs as of 11/4/2024.
 

 

 This is a repeat finding from the 2023 Block Audit Report which also noted KYC Reviews/Alerts completed outside of SLA.
 

 

 Internal Audit recommends that Compliance continue to monitor staffing needs through the Compliance Analytics Capacity Model and assess and escalate staffing needs to Senior Management.
 

 Risk:
 Customers are not subject to timely due diligence and as a result Block may not understand the true money laundering risk of the customer
 

 Mitigating Circumstances:
 No backlogs existed at the end of the Covered Period
 Compliance is utilizing a staffing model to monitor staffing needs and escalates identified staffing needs to Senior Management.

 Decrease in operational efficiency

 Minor security issues, minor data breaches and isolated system issues

Issue Description: Weaknesses were observed in Incident and breach reporting systems and procedures as documentation of processes were not identified, specifically as it relates to warning and breach levels of AML and Sanctions systems, alert management, and KYC case management. Failures of this control could lead to non-compliance with laws, rules, and regulations.
Issue Rating Justification: The Compliance Team lacks a centralized standard documenting how AML/CFT incidents and breaches are reported, which could leave to non-compliance with laws, rules, and regulations.
Issue Action Plans: Implementation of Compliance Incident / Breach Reporting Standard (1264779)
Closure Request Justification: Following the issuance of this self-identified finding, Block’s Compliance organization developed and published a Compliance Incident Reporting Standard on go/policy. This new Standard describes the framework that Compliance follows to respond to, contain, remediate, and resolve Incidents involving Compliance-related processes, systems, tools, or data. It also describes the structure, roles, and responsibilities of key personnel involved in responding to and escalating such Incidents. This framework aligns with Block’s enterprise level Incident Management framework.

Issue Title: Lack of Retrospective Criminal Background Checks in Employee Vetting
Issue Description:
Per Section 57 of the AML/CFT Act 2009, Afterpay NZ is required to
 have procedures to perform vetting (i.e. due diligence screening)
 on all employees “engaged in AML/CFT related duties.”
 Internal Audit’s interpretation based on regulatory expectation is
 that criminal background checks should be included in the
 employee vetting program for employees engaged in AML/CFT
 related duties (i.e. higher risk roles).
 Between February 2022 and October 2022, Afterpay NZ’s
 employee due diligence process did not include criminal
 background checks for employees engaged in AML/CFT related
 duties (i.e. higher risk roles). Following Block’s acquisition of
 Afterpay NZ in February 2022, Afterpay NZ’s existing employee due
 diligence process that included criminal background checks, was
 deprecated and integrated into Block’s global employee screening
 procedure, without consultation with Afterpay ANZ Compliance
 team. The Afterpay ANZ Compliance team identified the gap and
 implemented a risk based employee due diligence process in
 October 2022, which includes a criminal background check for
 employees in higher risk roles.
 Employee vetting is a minimum required component for AML/CFT
 Programs in New Zealand, and as a result firms that lack such
 sufficient risk-based procedures are at-risk for regulatory scrutiny
 and financial penalty. In addition, there is an increased risk that
 Afterpay NZ may employ individuals that have ties to criminal
 and/or terrorist activities, which would compromise the
 effectiveness of Afterpay NZ’s AML/CFT Program.

Issue Title: DOB not captured at Onboard for Web Sign-in
Issue Description:
Met on 12/10/24 with PM Adrienne Wong to learn the details about a pilot initiative to test customer onboarding via web. Also in attendance was Alana Joyce, Courtney Smith, and Eric Nistad. We were informed that testing pilots took place from Feb to Oct 2024 led by the Engineering team. No PM was assigned and neither Compliance nor Legal were engaged prior to launching the testing pilot. 50K accounts were created as part of the pilot via the web. In the meeting it was identified that no DOB at onboarding was collected. This collection became a requirement for the App in Oct 2024. The immediate concern was a) did we allow teens to create green accounts and b) did we sanction screen these accounts.
 The PM was given the instruction to discontinue any pilots and not launch any new ones, until further notice.
RCA Description: Legal & Compliance not engaged
Interested Parties:
Courtney Smith
 Lauren Butters
 Alana Joyce
Issue Rating Justification: Issue was found on a limited feature testing pilot. Pilot was no longer live when the issue was discovered.
Issue Action Plans: Remediate DOB capture when onboarding via web (1267416)
Closure Request Justification:
Pilots were conducted between February to October 2024. Trust did not start collecting and storing DOB for users 18+ in onboard until Q1 2025. Therefore, no remediation of DOB is necessary as DOB will be collected and verified if/when the customer attempts identity verification. Further, 95% of Cash App customers are verified, and Cash App has additional controls to further it's verified population, such as a lifetime monetary limit on unverified accounts, and one unverified account per device. 
 

 Additional concerns outlined and mitigated included: (a) Sanctions screening, which was confirmed on all accounts that had legal names, there was a population of ~6k that had no screening record, but those accounts did not appear to have completed onboarding (e.g. dropped from flor after providing alias); and (b) Families model; ML confirmed that the model that looks for teens in green accounts captures all accounts regardless if opened via app or web. 
 

 One final item included an analysis completed by Emerging Risk which highlighted concerns with accounts onboarded during the pilot, include: (1) IP address, SIM, and device information availability and accuracy; (2) multiple accounts using the same device or web browser; (3) time zone information in non-US countries; (4) potential emulator use (e.g. devices included iPhone, galaxy, moto G, and octopus apps; and (5) established accounts with lack of money movement. These signals are not specific to the pilot cohort but are applicable toweb onboarding generally. As such, this analysis should be addressed as part of ongoing risk oversight for web onboarding, if warranted, rather than within this pilot-specific issue.

Issue Title: Undisclosed Mandatory Fees for Mastercard-Only Cash Out Transactions
Issue Description: Cash App does not disclose that customers who have linked a MasterCard to their account and who are unable to link a bank account (possibly due to not having a bank account) are required to use the “instant cash-out” feature (and cannot use the standard, free feature), and thus, that they are required to pay a fee to cash-out their funds. There is considered to be no reasonable alternative methods to move their funds off the platform. All debit card transactions are instant, but they cost more than ACH. The only reason we accept Visa debit rails for standard transactions is because Visa offers us a lower price in situations where we artificially delay the transaction when a customer selects the standard speed and MasterCard does not. This is not expected to be a pervasive issue and it only impacts a small population of customers that do not have a bank account or alternative debit card. It is not economic to support MasterCard standard transfers. No metrics to provide at this time. This issue was uncovered in early January 2025.
Issue Rating Justification: Issue presents some regulatory and operational risk to Cash App. Most customers do not fit the criteria of only using a Mastercard debit with no bank account so the risk is not considered High.
Closure Request Justification:
"The following steps will further mitigate this risk:
 

 (1) Revising the TOS to include a disclosure re the fact that you may only withdraw funds via instant deposit with a linked Mastercard debit card. Advised by Product Counsel that the ToS changes have been completed on June 13th.
 

 (2) Transfers is actively working with FinOps to explore offering standard transfers via Mastercard. [Caveat there is no guarantee negotiations will lead to a solution; comeptitive rates are an issue]. Transfers Product has advised on June 12th that there has been no concrete progress regarding the functionality or rates.
 

 (3) Transfers is also working on enabling multi-linking, which would allow customers to link more than debit card (today, limited to 1 bank account,1 debit card, and 1 credit card)." Transfers Product has advised on June 12th that development has started and they are aiming for a mid/late-Q3 launch.
 

 Given the ToS has been updated. Closing this issue out.

Issue Title: No Quality Assurance of Merchant Onboarding cases is performed.
Issue Description:
The Merchant Onboarding Line 1 Quality Assurance program that included regular peer review of Merchant Onboarding cases, ceased in December 2023. Per confirmation with Merchant Onboarding Management, it was agreed with the Clearpay Compliance Lead that Quality Assurance should be conducted by AML Operations’ Quality Assurance team. 
 

 However, GCMT noted that since December 2023 there have been no reviews of Merchant Onboarding cases by the AML Operations’ Quality Assurance team to ensure ongoing compliance with Standard Operating Procedures for existing team members. 
 

 The Merchant Onboarding Lead currently reviews cases for new hires only as part of Onboarding and Training.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: Issue rate in accordance with the Issue Management Program rating methodology.
Issue Action Plans: Implement and launch Quality Assurance procedure and process to review Merchant Onboarding cases. (1272696)
Closure Request Justification: Clearpay UK have provided appropriate evidence and addressed the issue.

Issue Title: The identification and verification of a Merchant’s beneficial owner was missed for one onboarded Merchant.
Issue Description:
Regulatory requirement: The Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 requires that 'where the customer is beneficially owned by another person, the relevant person must—
 identify the beneficial owner;
 take reasonable measures to verify the identity of the beneficial owner so that the relevant person is satisfied that it knows who the beneficial owner is.
 Policy requirement: The “UK Risk Onboarding Playbook”, page 18 requires ‘Under UK anti-money laundering legislation, Clearpay is required to verify the Ultimate Beneficial Owners “UBO(s)” of any applying business. A UBO is determined as any Individual owning 25% or more of the applying business.’
 

 Issue: GCMT tested a sample of 76 merchants onboarded with UBO’s for the review period 1 July 2024 - 31 December 2024 to confirm appropriate measures were taken to verify the identity of Ultimate Beneficial Owners. 
 

 GCMT noted for one Merchant onboarded, that the analyst failed to correctly identify and verify all beneficial owners. This resulted in only one out of two Ultimate Beneficial Owners being screened by the Clearpay AML team. 
 

 Since that merchant was onboarded in July 2024, the second UBO was identified and verified at the end of February 2025 as part of checks required due to reverify the UBO’s as part of an entity novation i.e. a change of entity name.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: Issue rate in accordance with the Issue Management Program rating methodology.
Issue Action Plans: Training to be provided to analyst to ensure the process is followed. (1272699)
Closure Request Justification: Onboarding Lead was the analyst for this review. Noted and already fully trained.

Issue Title: Not all required supporting evidence or documentation was attached to Salesforce Onboarding cases, for a sample of Merchants.
Issue Description:
Program: Clearpay UK Merchant Due Diligence Program v1.3 (Aug-2024) states 'All reports and other information relied upon to identify the beneficial owners of the merchant should be saved as attachments to the salesforce entity.
 Any additional controls or checks imposed by the Money Laundering Reporting Officer “MLRO” upon review should be recorded by the MLRO in an auditable format in the Salesforce risk case or opportunity.'
 

 Issue: GCMT tested a sample of 76 merchants onboarded and 15 periodic review cases ( a check and rescreen of a merchant’s information every 5 years) for the review period 1 July 2024 - 31 December 2024 to confirm Analyst adherence with the “UK Risk Onboarding Playbook” and the “Clearpay UK Merchant Periodic Review Procedure”. As part of the review, GCMT assessed the completeness of each Merchant Onboarding file on Salesforce including the retention of documents relied upon and/or required approvals. 
 

 GCMT identified six merchants (7%) noted below, where appropriate record retention was not met: 
 One of six Periodic Review samples (17%) had a modified onboarding process exempting the beneficial owner from screening, however written approvals could not be sighted. Management have confirmed individuals responsible have left Clearpay and GCMT are unable to verify the exemptions.
 Four of six merchant onboarding samples (67%) did not have the Creditsafe report attached to the Salesforce case (3 Onboarding cases and 1 Periodic Review). Upon request, management were able to provide the report used at the time of onboarding. 
 One of six Enterprise merchant samples (17%) did not have transaction fee approval that could be sighted. The onboarding of this merchant occurred prior to the JIRA process being established. Prior to JIRA, records for approvals were stored in individual’s emails, and could not be retrieved at the time of the review.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: Issue rate in accordance with the Issue Management Program rating methodology.
Issue Action Plans: Training to be provided to Risk team members. (1273342)
Closure Request Justification: GCMT obtained evidence of onsite that occurred Tues, 25th March 2-3pm where training was provided.

Issue Title: There is no current requirement for Merchant Onboarding to confirm that written permissions and confirmation of required licenses/memberships have been obtained prior to onboarding, as required by the Clearpay Terms and Conditions. There is no current requirement for Merchant Onboarding to confirm that written permissions and confirmation of required licenses/memberships have been obtained prior to onboarding, as required by the Clearpay Terms and Conditions.
Issue Description:
Issues: 
 Merchants agree to Terms and Conditions in their agreement with Clearpay at the time of application which restricts them in dealing in certain goods and services. 
 

 These state "you will not, without the prior written permission from Clearpay, allow the Services to be used to purchase Restricted Goods or Services as outlined in Schedule 2 to these General Terms via your Website and/or in-Store (if applicable)" 
 and "you hold all required licenses, approvals and consents necessary to provide the Goods offered by you".
 

 GCMT noted that the ‘UK Risk Onboarding Playbook’ does not currently include requirements/expectations for checks to be completed that written permission and confirmation of required licenses/memberships have been obtained by Sales, Reputational Risk or other relevant teams prior to the onboarding of the merchant. 
 

 GCMT noted during our testing of 76 merchant onboarding cases, six merchants (8%) provided "beauty or cosmetic treatments that are physically invasive;" from the Restricted Goods and Services list. For these 6 merchants, GCMT were not able to sight written permission and confirmation of licences as required by the merchant T&Cs.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: Issue rate in accordance with the Issue Management Program rating methodology.
Issue Action Plans:
Implement Compliance GMCP restricted category in consultation with internal stakeholders (1273345)
 Training to be provided to Sales team. (1273348)

Issue Title: Merchant Onboarding analysts’ process adherence to the UK Risk Onboarding Playbook can be uplifted.
Issue Description:

 GCMT noted 18 exceptions (23%) for newly onboarded merchants detailed below:

Issue Title: Clawback reversals on chargeback cancellations
Issue Description:
As part of Cash Apps chargeback handling, Cash App attempts to claw back funds from customers both when a new chargeback is opened and again if the chargeback is ultimately lost. However, a “lost” chargeback isn’t always final—customers or their bank may cancel the chargeback. When this happens after a successful clawback, Cash ends up with more money than it should: both the clawed-back amount and the refunded chargeback.
 

 In these cases, Cash App should programmatically reverse the original clawback to ensure funds are returned appropriately and avoid over-collection.
 

 Currently, this is only addressed manually when a customer contacts Cash App. Prior analysis shows most customers don’t reach out—or their cases aren't correctly routed—resulting in poor user experiences. Automating this process is essential for improving customer trust and ensuring fair outcomes. 
 

 Similarly, Cash Apps lack of established processes has resulted in ~$8.4m in over collected funds since 2018. Of that, approximately ~$4 still remains with Cash App that should of been returned to customers. Please note, these numbers are approximate as the team has had difficulty in determining what this actually is.
RCA Description: Issue occurred due to programmatic procedural lapses
Issue Rating Justification:
Presents a moderate risk to the operations of Block. Overall exposure approximately ~$4mil requires a corrective action plan with priority and proactive customer remediation. 
 

 Issues does not represent violations of express regulatory obligations, but does present an implied risk by holding funds that are not owned by Cash App. Also presents risk related to inaccuracies in accounting and ledgering.
Issue Action Plans: Clawback reimbursements (1347922)
Closure Request Justification: P2P reimbursements have rolled out to 100% as of August 18, 2025. Please see https://www.notion.so/cashappcash/Centralized-Clawbacks-Reimbursements-Launch-Plan-14d54e8e103181bca544c564b430f4ac

Issue Title: Cash App Terms of Service Definition of Business Day
Issue Description:
Policy / Program or Procedure Requirement: 
 Cash App's "Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices" (UDAAP) policy outlines the company's commitment to consumer protection and compliance with federal and state laws, ensuring transparency and fairness throughout its operations. 
 

 Result:
 This placement and inconsistency of the definition for the term ‘business day’ has the potential to mislead customers, as it is referred to multiple times in the terms of service for Automated Clearing House (ACH) network transfers, funds availability, and arbitration, but it is inconsistently defined within two discrete subsections.
 Specifically, "business day" appears on page 62 of the 105-page document in section XII.2 (Transaction Information and Disputes), defined as "Monday through Friday, excluding holidays." However, a subsequent section XVII.7 (Funds Availability for Mobile Check Deposit) states "Every day is a business day except Saturdays, Sundays, and federal holidays." (emphasis added)
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: UDAAP related with potential for customer harm.
Issue Action Plans: Cash App Terms of Service Updates regarding the term "business day" (1276652)
Closure Request Justification:
Legal updated and published to ToS today, prominently defining the term "business day" in the second, standalone body paragraph of the terms, as follows: "As used throughout these terms, the phrase “Business Day” means all days of the week except Saturdays, Sundays, and federal holidays."
 

 https://cash.app/legal/us/en-us/tos#:~:text=As%20used%20throughout%20these%20terms%2C%20the%20phrase%20%E2%80%9CBusiness%20Day%E2%80%9D%20means%20all%20days%20of%20the%20week%20except%20Saturdays%2C%20Sundays%2C%20and%20federal%20holidays.

Issue Title: Cash App's website for Savings (and Savings Yield) omits details around IRS form eW9, which is an “eligibility requirement” to earn interest on Savings accounts as is stated in the Cash App Savings Terms of Service (Section 4. Savings Yield)
Issue Description:
Regulation/Act/Guidance Requirement: 
 The Dodd-Frank Act prohibits conduct that constitutes a deceptive act or Practice. 
 Per the Act:
 

 An act or practice is deceptive when:
 (1) The act or practice misleads or is likely to mislead the consumer;
 (2) The consumer’s interpretation is reasonable under the circumstances; and
 (3) The misleading act or practice is material.
 

 Per the Federal Trade Commission (“FTC”) guidance, the "Commission will find deception if there is a representation, omission or practice that is likely to mislead the consumer acting reasonably in the circumstances, to the consumer's detriment."
 

 Policy/Program or Procedure Requirement: 
 Cash App's "Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices" (UDAAP) policy outlines the company's commitment to consumer protection and compliance with federal and state laws, ensuring transparency and fairness throughout its operations. Specifically, Section 4.3 of Cash App’s UDAAP Policy states:
 

 Any unfair, deceptive, or abusive act or practice can result in consumer harm. Consumer harm is any actual or potential loss or injury to a consumer, whether financial or non-financial, that may be caused by the violation of federal or state consumer protection laws or regulations, or by weaknesses in the Compliance Management System (CMS). The company seeks to offer its products and services in a clear, transparent, and accurate manner and in accordance with applicable state and federal consumer protection laws and regulations to avoid instances of consumer harm.
 

 Further, Cash App's guidelines emphasize the importance of clear, prominent, and accurate descriptions of all material limitations, conditions, and restrictions on offers. Compliance reviews are mandatory prior to the use of advertisements or promotional materials to ensure they meet these standards and avoid UDAAP breaches.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: Cash App; Peer-to-Peer (P2P), Cash Card, & Savings - UDAAP (1040471)
Issue Rating Justification: Legal provided their documented acceptance, and GCMT will treat as risk-accepted.
Issue Progress Updates:
Cash App's website for Savings (and Savings Yield) omits details around IRS form eW9, which is an “eligibility requirement” to earn interest on Savings accounts as is disclosed in the Cash App Savings Terms of Service (Section 4. Savings Yield). While the Interest bearing (yield) feature is prominently displayed on the promotional webpage, the associated footnote for Savings Yield (excerpted below) is silent regarding the eW9 requirement.
 

 To earn interest on your Cash App savings balance, you need to be 18 or older and have a Cash App Card. To earn the highest interest rate Cash App offers, you also need to direct deposit at least $300 monthly into Cash App. Sponsored Accounts are not eligible to earn interest. Other exceptions may also apply. Savings yield rate is subject to change.
 

 Omitting this eligibility detail in promotional materials may mislead or misinform consumers about their ability to earn interest on the Cash App Savings Yield product.

Issue Title: The Engineering Team implemented a 12 month limit for approving new sponsorships as the Product Review Document did not specifically require a 12-month limit for active sponsorships.
Issue Description:
GCMT Control Description
 Sponsors are limited to a maximum of four (4) active sponsorships per account, six (6) active sponsorships per SSN over a trailing 12-month period, and 10 total sponsorships per SSN over a 10-year period (including graduated and suspended sponsorships). When a new sponsorship request is made by a teen, Aegis automatically checks the number of sponsorships associated with the sponsor and denies the request if the limits have already been met. This activity is performed to ensure no individual sponsors beyond the control's tolerable limits.
 

 Issue:
 GCMT noted that the Product Requirements Document (PRD) defined the SSN-based limit as: "Allow a maximum of 6 sponsorships per SSN per trailing 12 months. This is inclusive of large families where some teens may be graduating while others may be reaching the age of 13." However, the PRD failed to specify whether this 12-month limit applied to active sponsorships at a point in time or new sponsorship approvals within that period. This lack of clarity resulted in inconsistent interpretations across teams.
 The Engineering team implemented the control based on the number of new sponsorships approved within a trailing 12-month window, allowing a sponsor to exceed six active sponsorships if earlier ones remain open. In contrast, Product Compliance and Product Data Science (PDS) interpreted the requirement as limiting sponsors to no more than six active sponsorships at any given time within a rolling 12-month period—aligning with the control’s intent to cap ongoing risk exposure. Following internal review, the Cash for Families Product team, Product Compliance, and Engineering aligned that the current implementation should be updated to reflect active sponsorships. 
 The control should be clarified to eliminate ambiguity and clearly define the metric being enforced. Ambiguous language in future PRDs should be avoided to ensure consistent implementation aligned with risk and product intent.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: The Cash for Families Alpha & Beta (Distance and Mutuals Requirements) Controls Lack Auditability Due to the Limitation of Retained Customer Location Data
Issue Description:
GCMT Control Description
 Control α (alpha): Verified adult (unrestricted) accounts can only send money to a teen if a) the adult lives within 20 miles and shares a mutual friend with either the teen or their sponsor; OR b) the adult shares 3 mutual friends with the teen or their sponsor.
 

 Control β (beta): Teens can only send more >$50 in their first week with a new peer if a) that peer lives within 20 miles and shares a mutual friend with either the teen or their sponsor; OR b) that peer shares 3 mutual friends with the teen or their sponsor.
 

 Issue:
 The controls rely on the Cash Customer Location Model v2 to estimate customers’ geographical locations for distance calculations. However, this model is not auditable, creating significant challenges in verifying its accuracy. The lack of auditability is further complicated by the following factors:
 

 - For past transactions, only the distance between parties is stored, without details of the calculations or model signals (such as mailing postal codes, IP addresses, etc.) used to determine this distance.
 - Model outages may lead to inaccurate location calculations without affecting the transaction itself, potentially causing control failures.
 - Inconsistencies in data sources across different Snowflake tables can lead to unreliable or incomplete information being used for control checks.
 

 Due to these model limitations, GCMT cannot verify the accuracy of location and mutual connection calculations at the time of the transaction for the review period. This prevents GCMT from definitively identifying true control exceptions, determining accurate failure rates, or verifying historical control effectiveness with certainty. This situation highlights the need for robust model risk management practices, in line with Block's internal Compliance Engineering and Technology (CET) Model Risk Management Policy. This policy requires model outputs to be both validatable, ensuring alignment with expected results, and replicable, allowing for comparison with production data to investigate discrepancies.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: The Legacy Process in IDV Flow Allowed Re-Sponsorship of 7 Graduated Accounts
Issue Description:
GCMT Control Description
 When a customer first signs up for Cash App, their account is classified as restricted until they verify their identity or become sponsored. Unverified adult accounts must complete Identity Verification (IDV) before engaging in any transactions with teen accounts. 
 

 Exception Results from All Eligible Control Events:
 IDV did not occur: 0.0004% of the population of 84,178,399 
 IDV occurred after the transaction by more than one day: 0.0181% of the population of 84,178,399
 

 Manual Sample Review Results: 
 GCMT noted that out 2 of 30 (7%) sponsored accounts reviewed, the customer graduated (i.e., turned 18 and were no longer eligible for sponsorship) and were subsequently re-sponsored by a different adult sponsor. The Engineering and Data Science teams conducted a population review and identified additional currently active sponsored accounts that had previously graduated successfully from another sponsor.
 

 Issue:
 Currently, sponsored accounts cannot complete IDV until they graduate, at which point all graduated teens must undergo IDV. Previously, sponsored accounts could complete identity verification (IDV) because there was no comprehensive rule preventing teens from doing so. These early sponsored accounts successfully graduated with IDV already completed. Their IDV was later revoked, allowing them to become eligible for sponsorship again. As a result, individuals who underwent the legacy IDV process and are no longer teens can fraudulently extend their sponsorship status, creating misleading customer profiles. As a result, adults can bypass critical program controls and requirements specifically designed for independent adult accounts.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: Unassociated Aliases Allow for IDV Bypass in Adult-Teen Transactions
Issue Description:
GCMT Control Description
 When a customer first signs up for Cash App, their account is classified as restricted until they verify their identity or become sponsored. Unverified adult accounts must complete Identity Verification (IDV) before engaging in any transactions with teen accounts.
 

 Exception Results from All Eligible Control Events:
 IDV did not occur: 0.0004% of the population of 84,178,399 
 IDV occurred after the transaction by more than one day: 0.0181% of the population of 84,178,399 
 

 Manual Sample Review Results: 
 GCMT noted that 22 out of 30 (73%) adult-teen transactions reviewed, the required plasma flow (IDV prompt) failed to execute as designed at the time of transaction. Specifically, these transactions were processed either a) without the adult completing IDV; or b) >24 hours before the adult completed IDV.
 

 Issue:
 This control bypass can occur when a transaction is initiated using an unassociated/unclaimed alias (such as a phone number or email address) that has not yet been linked to the teen's account. This gap in control allows transactions to proceed without satisfying the Identity Verification (IDV) requirements. In this case, when an unverified adult sends a peer-to-peer (P2P) payment to an unassociated/unclaimed alias, the counterparty eligibility check is performed on the sender's side and passes the transaction as the alias is not yet associated with a teen. Once the alias is claimed by the teen and the counterparty check is run on the recipient side, the transaction has already progressed, as the system flow does not return to perform an eligibility check on the sender. This may lead to unauthorized transactions and/or inappropriate activity with teens, as it enables unverified adults to send payments to teens without proper checks.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: The unverified adult IDV Flow Allows for Control Alpha & Beta (Distance and Mutuals Requirements) Bypass in Adult-Teen Transactions
Issue Description:
GCMT Control Description
 Control α (alpha): Verified adult (unrestricted) accounts can only send money to a teen if a) the adult lives within 20 miles and shares a mutual friend with either the teen or their sponsor; OR b) the adult shares 3 mutual friends with the teen or their sponsor.
 

 Control β (beta): Teens can only send more >$50 in their first week with a new peer if a) that peer lives within 20 miles and shares a mutual friend with either the teen or their sponsor; OR b) that peer shares 3 mutual friends with the teen or their sponsor.
 

 In order to be verified, the adult must also complete Identity Verification (IDV) prior to the transaction.
 

 Exception Results from All Eligible Control Events:
 Control α (alpha) Auto-Fail triggered but payment still succeeded: 0.00095% of the population of 40,478,956 
 Control α (alpha) Auto-Fail not triggered but should have: 0.80791% of the population of 40,478,956 
 Control β (beta) Auto-Fail triggered but payment still succeeded: 0.0081% of the population of 546,249
 Control β (beta) Auto-Fail not triggered but should have: 2.68% of the population of 546,249
 

 Manual Sample Review Results: 
 GCMT noted that 2 out of 30 (7%) adult-teen transactions reviewed, the transactions were not automatically blocked despite not meeting the distance or mutual friend constraints. In these cases, the adult counterparty completed IDV on the same day as the transaction.
 

 Issue:
 When a transaction is attempted between a teen and an unverified adult, the adult is prompted to complete identity verification (IDV) to proceed. However, after IDV is completed, the transaction does not consistently undergo the necessary checks for distance and mutual friends between the adult and the teen before the payment is processed. This may lead to unauthorized transactions and/or inappropriate activity with teens, as it enables unverified adults to send payments to teens without proper checks.

Issue Description:
** ISSUE HAS BEEN RISK ACCEPTED. SEE ATTACHED RISK ACCEPTANCE REQUEST FOR MORE DETAILS**
 

 GCMT Control Description
 When a sponsored account receives a denylist adverse action, Cash App applies a "strike" (SponsorshipStrikeAdversity) to the sponsor's account with a warning. If a second sponsored account for the same sponsor receives a denylist adverse action, the sponsor is not able to sponsor new accounts with their SSN (SponsorshipCreationAdversity).

 A sponsored account was closed and the sponsorship canceled before an automated check could determine if the dependent was sponsored and a corresponding strike (SponsorshipStrikeAdversity or SponsorshipCreationAdversity) applied to the sponsor. This oversight could allow sponsors associated with teen accounts involved in prohibited activities to continue sponsoring new accounts without restriction if the sponsor account re-opens.

Issue Title: Risk Ops Banking: RDFI Debit Returns
Issue Description:
What happened? (to the customer)
 ACH Debits that went from Cash App to the Receiving Depository Financial Institution (RDFI), but were rejected by the RDFI, were not credited directly back to the customer. Instead, the RDFI credits the funds back to Sutton Bank instead and Sutton credits the Cash App operating account. Cash App is expected to reimburse the customer on Sutton’s word that the operating account was credited. 
 

 A total of ~180 customers were determined to be impacted, roughly 150k in funds not returned to customers. 
 

 These are cases do not involve a monetary loss to Cash App. 
 

 What should have happened? (in a perfect world)
 

 The funds that are rejected by the RDFI should be automatically credited back to the Customer’s account directly. 
 

 What is the impact? (to the customer/business)
 

 Customer
 

 The customer has delayed access to their funds after they are rejected to the RDFI. In many cases, these are ACH Debited from the account to cover security purchases or other large purchases (currently no limits on ACH debit).
 

 Creates a bad customer experience and makes us appear untrustworthy. 
 

 The Business
 

 Lowers customer trust especially with higher gpv customers using us as their main bank and using secondary or tertiary Banks/Financial Institutions to invest, buy crypto, or to conduct other transactions.
 

 Who discovered the issue and how? 
 

 This was initially discovered by CCOT involving a complaint, Missing Deposits on RO Banking began to field these until it was discovered that these were escalated through an existing process done through RO: Banking Fraud called ACH Recalls, but was on a tab that was not accessed.
RCA Description: Technology
Issue Rating Justification: An issue in interpretation and execution of CCO policy which has material impact on the process or risk assessment
Issue Action Plans: Risk Ops Banking: RDFI Debit Returns (1280602)
Closure Request Justification:
IM Analyst reviewed and validated to support action plan closure. 
 

 IM Lead reviewed artifacts on 4/29/25 and no concerns were noted. - Charlotte Vasi (4/30/25).

Issue Title: Ensuring Compliance with OFAC Record Retention Requirements
Issue Description:
The Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) has updated its record-keeping requirements, extending the retention period for certain transaction records from five (5) to ten (10) years (“The New Record Retention Requirement”). This change aligns with the 21st Century Peace Through Strength Act, signed into law on April 24, 2024, which extended the statute of limitations for civil and criminal violations of OFAC sanctions under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) and the Trading with the Enemy Act (TWEA) from five (5) to ten (10) years.
 

 As a result, OFAC's final rule, which went into effect March 12, 2025, requires organizations to maintain comprehensive records of all transactions subject to OFAC sanctions for ten years. This update pertains to records including but not limited to reports of blocked or rejected transactions and specific licenses for permissible transactions and unblocked property. Notably, the ten-year statute of limitations applies to violations occurring after April 24, 2019.
 

 Due to competing priorities, a comprehensive assessment of Block’s record retention practices has not been completed to ensure compliance with the 10 year record keeping requirement that went into effect on March 12, 2025. Under The New Record Retention Requirement, Block must maintain detailed records for a minimum of 10 years for the following:
 All transactions subject to OFAC provisions
 Blocked property and fund transfers
 KYC, KYB and other customer-related records for customers who Block confirmed are sanctioned by OFAC
 Third Party Vendors (i.e. LexisNexis, Chainalysis, etc.)
 Note: LexisNexis has confirmed their retention period has been updated to the required 10 year obligation
 

 This issue has been assigned a moderate risk rating. Block has existing policies and procedures for recording and retaining records relevant to the Global Sanctions Program. This issue has been opened to complete a necessary assessment to determine to what extent we may need to amend existing record keeping practices.
Hyperlinks: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1msldyM2AOgUNwZs2ZnsnimB03Y6Vd_2zEM0KGyctZZI/edit?tab=t.0|Record Retention Issue Action Plan Intake Form
RCA Description:
Processes, Products, or Services: Process weaknesses, process or product/service design, or monitoring/management of processes resulting in errors, control breakdowns, customer complaints, or operational losses. 
 

 OFAC provided an interim final rule in September 2024 which provided guidance and opportunity for feedback of the upcoming changes to record retention from 5 years to 10 years. On Mar 12, 2025 the Final Rule went into place effectively implementing the record retention changes. Validation of the record retention has not occurred due to competing priorities.

Issue Title: Square Japan Compliance analysts are not documenting sufficient rationale to support their case decisions and are not reviewing cases within the 24-hour service level agreement (SLA) per the established Transactions Monitoring Alert Queue procedures
Issue Description:
GMCT identified that 14 out of 40 cases (or 35%) reviewed by Square Japan analysts did not follow the procedural guidance defined in the TM Alert Queue Procedures. The following issues was identified:
 

 For 9 of 40 TM cases, analysts did not provide sufficient rationale to support the final conclusion of the case review. Insufficient case documentation may lead to the potential mishandling of suspicious activity. 
 - 3 cases did not provide reasonableness for the lack of SSP submission to verify the details of an unusual transaction
 - 2 cases did not contain sufficient rationale to support the observed transaction activity (i.e. peer deviation comparison) 
 - 2 cases did not contain sufficient rationale to support the lack of SSP submission as reliance was placed on a prior SPP; in addition, the reasonableness used to support the verification of an unusual transaction was not documented in the case conclusion
 - 1 case did not provide reasonableness for acceptance of an unusual transaction
 - 1 case did not provide rationale to support the reliance on a prior SSP to explain and verify a recent transaction
 

 For 5 of 40 TM cases, 3 jp_compliance_pan_concentration and 2 jp_compliance_intl, an analyst did not claim or review the cases within the 24-hour SLA timeframe established in the JP International Card Queue and JP Pan Concentration Queue procedure documents. This delay in case management could lead to operational inefficiencies.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2025 Q1; Square Japan - AML Operational Testing (1041129)
RCA Description: Root cause stems from People and Process. Analysts performing case reviews are not providing sufficient details to support case review conclusions. Additionally, analysts responsible for reviewing cases did not follow procedural guidance.
Issue Rating Justification: There are no Japan specific laws associated with the related processes; however, the error rate observed presents medium/moderate risk to Block and Square Japan.
Issue Action Plans: Transaction Monitoring case review training will be conducted and relevant SOPs will be updated (1281935)
Closure Request Justification: Refer to the related action plan records for attachments and closure details.

Issue Title: There is no established or formalized Square Japan Anti-Money Laundering (AML) & Countering the Financing of Terrorism (CFT) Policy/Program
Issue Description: A Program document detailing the Square Japan AML and CTF Program and its alignment with local Japan regulations has not been established. While some operational AML controls exist, there is no formalized AML/CTF or Compliance Program document, nor a legal assessment completed to govern compliance with any of Japan’s AML laws. This represents a gap in meeting Block’s policy expectations for documented, jurisdiction-specific programs and controls and may expose Block Inc and its subsidiaries to increased regulatory, financial and operational risks.

Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2025 Q1; Square Japan - AML Operational Testing (1041129)

Issue Action Plans: Square KK's AML/CFT & Sanction Program will be drafted and implemented (1282584)
Closure Request Justification: Refer to the related action plan records for attachments and closure details.

Issue Title: PAN data in address fields
Issue Description: It was discovered that some customers had input information that contain strings of numbers consistent with SSN or PAN-like (primary account number) data in sections meant for address entry. These discrepancies were undetected during the address collection process due to a gap in Cash App’s Address Validation controls. Both Borrow and Retro lending customers, totaling 110 individuals, were identified as having this type of data in their address fields.
RCA Description: Customers are able to enter addresses that are not properly formatted because the only validation in place was to ensure that fields are not left blank, and to validate state and zip code, only. On December 13, 2024, after discovery of this matter, a control was put in place to limit the amount of numbers, greater than 9-digits, to be placed in the “address 2” field.
Interested Parties:
Corey Chamberlain
 Lauren Butters
 Langston Walker
Issue Rating Justification: Though this data point is not accessible to the public, the fact that PII is exposed in a data field not related to that type of data, and not related to the data requested (personal/mailing address), needs to be addressed.
Issue Progress Updates: The matter was initially discovered December 5, 2024. A detailed review of the impacted addresses showed the SSN and PAN-like data resided in the “address 2” field and could be safely removed without invalidating the address or requiring follow-up action by users. The Tier 1 change was first applied to test data in the staging environment and the behavior of the change was confirmed to be correct. Prior to this issue being identified, we only had basic validation in place to ensure that address fields are not blank and that the postal code and state are valid. After the Tier 1 change was applied in the production environment an audit of a small subset of changed addresses confirmed the change. The matter as initially identified by a 3rd party, Cash App Invest (“CAI”) partner, which impacted over 4000 CAI users (see Protecht entry 1202036).
Issue Action Plans: PAN-like data in address spaces (1342659)
Closure Request Justification: On December 13, 2024 the engineering team introduced a system adjustment that prohibited the entry of numbers, greater than 9 digits, into the "address 2" field, and city and state fields, as the "zip code" section was already limited to 5 digits. In January 2025, the impacted addresses were corrected internally by removing SSN or PAN-like data without need for intervention by users. The data rectification was confirmed as complete on January 31, 2025. The address normalization and validation control was uplifted and activated for both Borrow and Retro on 6/5/25.

Issue Title: Process deficiency in responding to non-customer identity fraud requests
Issue Description:
An instance was identified where Block failed to provide requested account transaction details within the mandated 30-day timeframe according to FCRA 609(e) to a non-customer reporting identity theft on a fraudulently created account. This instance stemmed from inadequate procedures for handling information requests for non-customers who were the victims of identity theft. This led to delays in authenticating the claim and providing the requested information. 
 Independent of FCRA requirements, a standardized process is required to ensure all victims of identity theft, whether customers or non-customers, are supported consistently and appropriately. The corrective actions associated with this issue reflects the business’ commitment to doing the right thing and providing a reliable experience for anyone impacted by identity theft.
 Based on guidance from Compliance, the risk has been assessed as medium due to this being an isolated incident. This event highlighted inadequacies in managing these rare scenarios where non-customers require support for identity theft claims.
 Lastly, it is important to highlight that in the isolated instance referenced above, the individual (a non-customer) received all necessary information, and no further corrective actions are needed. The action plan will have multiple owners across different teams (CS, RO, Legal, Compliance) as this process spans across the entire organization.
 This workflow is expected to involve multiple handoffs between teams, potentially spanning the entire end-to-end process and requiring coordination among various action owners
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: An issue in interpretation and execution of CCO policy which has material impact on the process or risk assessment. One isolated (and now resolved)instance with confirmed regulatory impact.
Issue Action Plans: Process deficiency in responding to non-customer identity fraud requests (1282593)
Closure Request Justification: Evidence/supporting documentation reviewed by IM Lead, Charlotte Vasi 9/30/25.

Issue Title: CIP MVP missed AANs
Issue Description:
On 8/2/2024, it was discovered that in CIP MVP/V1 implementation, Customers who had not provided their full, 9-digit SSN before (i.e. they were already verified by last-4) were asked to provide their full SSN. This was then matched against their previous vendor-provided full SSN.
 

 Problem 1:
 Some Borrow customers seemed to be able to draw loans even after failing the 2 SSN matches.
 

 Problem 2 (SSN failure blocker):
 Upon exiting the SSN failure blocker–either via clicking the “Got it” button at the bottom or via the “X” dismiss button at the top–the IDV subflow ended, resulting in the main borrow flow ending and Loanstar never getting a sync failure response back from IDV, which is what triggers the AAN generation.
 

 Problem 3 (Missed AANs and incorrect AAN copy): 
 The AAN copy for CIP MVP failures today states the reason as We weren’t able to verify the identity document(s) you submitted. This needs to be updated to reflect the fact that no docs are provided anymore as dIDV fallback is gone.
 The AAN copy for CIP MVP failures today states the reason as, “we weren’t able to verify the identity document(s) you submitted.” This needs to be updated to reflect the fact that no docs are provided anymore as dIDV fallback is gone.
RCA Description: The root cause was identified as this edge case scenario where the full 9 match failing Customers later went on to pass dIDV elsewhere; then when they came to Borrow, IDV flow let them pass because they had failed SSN attempts and a subsequent passed dIDV.
Interested Parties:
Mia Bockus
 Corey Chamberlain
 Langston Walker
Issue Rating Justification: Potential Customers, who were unable to complete the full-9 SSN match were ultimately able to pass dIDV despite this fact. This resulted in some customers able to obtain loans OR not correctly receive AANs OR did receive AANs, but with incorrect information. ECOA and FCRA concerns
Issue Progress Updates: Typically, if it doesn’t match, then the user gets 1 more attempt to retry. If they fail to match both times, they were taken to dIDV fallback. As of Aug-Oct last year, IDV team has removed the dIDV fallback. So now if a Customer fails a full-9 match two times, they are considered as having failed CIP, therefore should be unable to draw a loan. Problem 1 was resolved 1/15/25. Problem 2 was resolved on 3/19/25. Problem 3 is still ongoing, but has had fixes pushed out on 4/1/25, 4/3/25 and 4/8/25.
Closure Request Justification:
- Mar 19th IDV team fixed the “got it” button on ssn failure screen elegantly failing the flow, resulting in AAN sends. [slack](https://cash.slack.com/archives/C088VDDU2KC/p1742342510120999?thread_ts=1741708216.233049&cid=C088VDDU2KC) ([deploy link](https://cash.slack.com/archives/D0280BDUNUV/p1743710518912789)).
 

 - April 1st, 2025 Lending pushed a fix ([deploy link](https://cash.slack.com/archives/C01ND9YATFA/p1743536803699489)) for dismissals getting tracked elegantly and sending AANs.
 

 - Apr 3rd, 2025 Lending pushed the AAN copy fix (for 2b) to Prod ([deploy link](https://cash.slack.com/archives/C01ND9YATFA/p1743700589374079))
 

 - April 8th, 2025 Backfill runs ([1](https://backfila.sqprod.co/backfills/28688) and [2](https://backfila.sqprod.co/backfills/28690)) completed to send retroactive AANs.

Issue Title: Security Vulnerabilities in CF1 Workflows Allowing Unauthorized Customer Token Changes
Issue Description:
An Advocate Escalations Team (AET) Specialist (former Voice Customer Support) has identified a security vulnerability in CF1 workflows that compromises customer account integrity through unrestricted C-token handling. The current implementation allows Scaled Support Voice Agents to modify customer tokens through the CF1 side panel's 'customer actions' feature without completing mandatory PII verification protocols.
 

 This bypassing of established security controls has resulted in a series of operational vulnerabilities:
 Unauthorized account modifications (including account closures, card terminations, and sponsorship dissolutions)
 Compromised account security frameworks
 Absence of a comprehensive audit trail for token manipulations in both CF1 and Looker analytics
 

 The observed pattern fundamentally contradicts established security protocols, which mandate that customer escalations proceed only after successful PII verification through one of three authorized channels:
 Manual verification processes
 Tap-to-confirm authentication
 Paycheck Deposit Status Actives (PDSA) callback protocols
 

 In instances where PII verification cannot be completed, operational guidelines require that agents must restrict their support to general information dissemination or redirect customers to in-chat support channels. This security vulnerability is especially severe in cases involving sponsored accounts not utilizing PDSA, where the potential for unauthorized account access and manipulation is heightened due to the complex relationship structure between primary and sponsored accounts.
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: An issue in interpretation and execution of CCO policy which has material impact on the process or risk assessment
Issue Progress Updates: Evidence packet reviewed by IM Lead, Charlotte Vasi on 11/7/25.
Issue Action Plans: Security Vulnerabilities in CF1 Allowing Unauthorized Customer Token Changes (1283244)
