Issue Title: Customer Account Records Standardization
Issue Description: Upon review of the sample of CAI customer accounts, the record of principal approver was recorded prior to the CAI account opening. However, CAI Compliance noted two observations regarding the record of principal approver record: (1) the principal approver record could be recorded at a date that is significantly different than the CAI account opening date, and (2) that the record of principal approver could represent CAI staff who are no longer employed with CAI at the time of the CAI account opening or represent CAI staff that no longer typically approve accounts at the time of the CAI account opening due to moving to a different CAI function. In these cases, the principal approver record was recorded at a customer event during the CAI onboarding process. The customer then did not move forward and complete all steps required to open a CAI account and returned to the CAI account opening workflow at a later date. The previously recorded principal approver information is maintained in these cases.
Issue Rating Justification: CAI Compliance recommends that Brokerage Operations implement changes to principal approver records so that there is a principal approver record that is within a reasonable expected timeframe and that represents the appropriate CAI staff at the time of CAI account opening.
Issue Action Plans: Assessment of Principal Account Approver Record (1283903)
Closure Request Justification:
Brokerage Operations in collaboration with CAI Engineering has created a design draft to reduce the time delta between the recorded broker review approval and actual brokerage account opening. The implementation is expected to be completed in 2026.
 Link to Design Draft: https://www.notion.so/cashappcash/Broker-Review-Account-Opening-Continuity-26b54e8e10318043a3d0fc0a1f4dfd46

Issue Title: Trusted Contact Records
Issue Description: Upon a sample review of customers where it was identified that a trusted contact was provided, CAI Compliance identified three (3) accounts where the trusted contact information could not be located in the customer’s internal account record viewable in Toolbox. Toolbox is an internal Block system to view customer accounts.
Issue Rating Justification: CAI Compliance recommends that Brokerage Operations perform a review to determine if trusted contact information is being appropriately reflected in expected internal fields when reviewing customer account information.
Issue Action Plans: Assessment of Trusted Contact Records (1283906)
Closure Request Justification: Upon review, Brokerage Operations determined that the issue is that the query used tracked whether users interacted with the trusted contact agreement prompt, not whether they actually completed the process and submitted valid trusted contact information. A 'Yes' value in trusted_contact_agreement simply indicates a user engaged with the agreement flow, but doesn't confirm they finished entering their contact details, successfully submitted the information, or didn't abandon the process midway. The designated storage location for trusted contact information is in the Brokerage Operations AWS table, trusted_contacts. Upon review of the records from testing that did not contain trusted contact information, the trusted_contacts table also showed no information recorded as expected.

Issue Title: Update CAI Identity Theft Prevention Program
Issue Description:
CAI Compliance noted the following observations with respect to CAI’s ITPP:
 -References in the ITPP to the Cash App Investing Red Flag Identification and Detection Grid (“Grid”), CAI CIP, and CAI AML programs did not appear to be tailored to what CAI does in practice. As a wholly owned subsidiary of Block, CAI relies on Block’s infrastructure in the areas of cybersecurity, vendor oversight, and AML, among others. Many practices noted in the ITPP are done by Block and not directly by CAI though CAI provides oversight of those functions. 
 -The ITPP stated that CAI has a process to confirm that its clearing firm and other service providers have reasonable policies and procedures designed to detect, prevent, and mitigate identity theft. The process appeared to refer to CAI’s vendor due diligence of its clearing firm and its reliance on Block for overall vendor management but the ITPP did not state as such.
Issue Rating Justification:
CAI Compliance recommends that Brokerage Operations update the ITPP to:
 -Clearly outline CAI’s practices versus Block’s practices and to update any vague or broad language that is not tailored to CAI’s practices. 
 -Identify how, when, and where CAI can escalate potential identity theft issues to the appropriate Block teams. 
 -Update the ITPP to ensure adherence for the upcoming amendments to Regulation S-P if necessary.
Issue Action Plans: Update CAI ITPP (1283909)

Issue Title: SEV-5293: Cash App Processing Error Triggered Unauthorized Bank Debits and Customer Overdraft Complaints
Issue Description: Between September 24, 2024 and October 11, 2024, Cash App experienced a technical malfunction (SEV-5293) that affected customer payment processing. When customers attempted to make payments using their Cash App balance, the system incorrectly withdrew funds from their linked bank account instead of using their available Cash App balance as intended. This error resulted in direct financial harm to customers, as some were charged overdraft fees by their banks due to these unexpected withdrawals. The issue came to light after multiple customers filed complaints through customer service channels, with the situation escalating to include a Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) complaint and discussions on social media platforms like Reddit. The Complaint Insights team's analysis revealed the scope of the problem through both first-line customer service complaints and regulatory channels.
RCA Description: Technology
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure resulting in an Impact to regulatory obligations
Issue Action Plans: SEV-5293: Cash App Processing Error Triggered Unauthorized Bank Debits and Customer Overdraft Complaints (1284567)
Closure Request Justification: Evidence package reviewed by IM Lead Charlotte Vasi on 8/5/25.

Issue Title: SIL Spain has not updated its Merchants KYC processes for full alignment with local regulatory requirements.
Issue Description:
Deficiency type: Rectification

 As noted in last year´s External Expert Report, SIL has not updated its Merchants Know Your Customer processes for full alignment with regulatory requirements in Spain. 
 It should be noted that during our review of a sample of 100 SIL merchants, we identified 9 merchants whose actual activity was not aligned with the type of business declared by the merchant, causing in 6 out of the 9 cases that merchants could register and operate before being deactivated.

Issue Title: Complaint tier severity was not consistently assigned and complaint responses failed to reach recipients due to email address errors.
Issue Description:
Program Requirement:
 Lending Operations Complaint Program
  1. Page 3, Cash Lending Complaints Program
 Cash App Lending Operations is a first line business function, supporting and growing the Cash App Lending business every day. We have a unique opportunity to identify, investigate and resolve complaints that we discover in our day-to-day operations.
 

  2. Page 5, Severity Tiers - Complaint Severity Tiering is a method used to rank complaints based on the inherent risk presented within each communication. This helps us understand the seriousness of a complaint and determine the level of escalated or specialized review necessary.
  - Tier 1 (High Risk)
  > Complaint presents a high risk or highly complex issue requiring specialized review
  > Complaint presents a Tier 1 Global or business level taxonomy
  > Tier 2 complaint requiring escalation to Tier 1 for further support
 

  - Tier 2 (Medium Risk)
  > Complaint requires additional customer support or escalation due to complexity, customer request, or employee discretion
  > Complaints in which a Tier 2 Global or business-level taxonomy is identified
 

  - Tier 3 (Low Risk)
  > Complaints expressing dissatisfaction with a Block product, process, service, or interaction that does not require escalation or specialized support to resolve or respond to the individual.
 

 Issue Description:
 1. Borrow 
  a. Tiering was inconsistent for complaint cases that had the same theme concerning automatic withdrawal of funds to make loan payments. The basis for selecting the Tier was unclear since Tiers 1, 2, and 3 were all used for similar events. 
  i.  Tier 1 examples - 173347634, 173468156, 173711576
  ii. Tier 2 examples - 171094516, 171949623, 172246812, 172918162
  iii. Tier 3 examples - 171184492, 173637995, 173311733
  
  b. 2 of 30 (7%) cases did not respond to the customer complaint within the 5-business day SLA for the initial responses (173468156 - 12 business days, 173711576 - 8 business days). Despite the initial response delays, both cases were resolved within the 14 business day SLA resolution for Tier 1 complaints. Note, the business experienced an influx of complaint volume due to a new approach implemented requiring complaints with a 'UDAAP' Legal tag to be escalated as a Tier 1 complaint. Due to this increase, Cash Lending has assigned a Complaint Handler to focus on Tier 1 complaints in order to meet SLAs going forward.
 

  c. 1 of 30 (3%) complaint cases was partially addressed by the advocate or agent as the provided response to the customer did not address all concerns (173859465). Note, the business has since flagged this case with leads for review. 
 

 2. Retro: 
  a. Tiering was inconsistent for complaint cases that had the same theme concerning automatic withdrawal of funds to make loan payments. The basis for selecting the Tier was unclear since Tiers 1, 2, and 3 were all used for similar events. 
  i.  Tier 1 examples - 172636013, 173245256
  ii. Tier 2 examples - 170635493. 173496722, 172773436, 171383124, 171906451
  iii. Tier 3 example - 171181102
 

  b. 4 of 30 (13%) cases did not respond to the customer complaint within the 5-business day initial response SLA (173336074 - 11 business days, 172854364 - 14 business days, 172999882 and 173664050 - 10 business days). Despite the initial response delays, all cases were resolved within their respective 14 business day and 30 business day SLA resolutions for Tier 1 and 3 complaints. Note, the business experienced an influx of complaint volume due to a new approach implemented requiring complaints with a 'UDAAP' Legal tag to be escalated as a Tier 1 complaint. Due to this increase, Cash Lending has assigned a Complaint Handler to focus on Tier 1 complaints in order to meet SLAs going forward.
 

 3. Undeliverable Email Address Errors: 2 of 60 (3%) complaint responses were undeliverable due to email address errors:
  a. 173711576: The delivery failed due to the incorrect email domain spelling provided by the customer and later used by the agent ('gmial.com' instead of gmail.com). The complaint was eventually resolved but only due to the customer following up less than 10 minutes later.
 

  b. 172999882: The Advocate overlooked one letter in the customer's email address; thus, the message was never delivered and remained unresolved.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

The training presentation included the definition of a complaint; examples of Tiers 1, 2, 3 and non-complaints; and keywords and phrases that qualified complaints as Tier 1. The presentation was also shared with staff to reference as needed to assist with complaint tiering.

Issue Title: Lack of Comprehensive, Sanctions-only Freeze
Issue Description:
Prior to February 20, 2024, Square Sanctions Operations applied the “Freeze” action in Regulator while conducting sanctions investigations in order to prevent a merchant from settling (or “cashing out”) their stored balance to an externally linked bank account. However, after a freeze was erroneously lifted by a Block employee who did not work within the Sanctions Compliance function, Square Sanctions Operations transitioned to using the “Freeze (under investigation)” action, which is available to a smaller number of employees across Block (primarily the Law Enforcement Response team).
 

 In March 2024 while investigating the capabilities of the “Freeze (under investigation)” action, Square Sanctions identified that both freezes only prevent the movement of funds from the merchant’s stored balance to their linked bank account and do not have any impact on other funds movement (such as chargebacks, refunds, etc.). 
 

 The root cause of this gap is due to service design. The Freeze action and Freeze (under investigation) actions were not originally created or designed for a sanctions investigation use case and were already in existence before sanctions teams adopted their use. From a sanctions perspective, this represents a gap as it does not comprehensively restrict money movement, which was previously unknown to the Square sanctions team when they initially adopted the Freeze action and later the Freeze (under investigation) action.  
 

 On March 12, 2024, Global Sanctions Compliance (“GSC”) raised a request with Compliance Engineering to create a new “sanctions-only” freeze which, among other things, would only be available for use by members of Square Sanctions or GSC and would prevent refund and chargebacks in addition to preventing the movement of funds from the merchant’s stored balance to any internal or external account. Due to the upcoming transition from Regulator to Notary, it was understood that the control would be built as part of the migration to Notary. However, the migration of Square Sanctions cases to Notary has been delayed multiple times, and there is currently no confirmed date for migration.
 

 The delay in implementing the control recently resulted in an apparent sanctions violation. In March 2025, after confirming prohibited sanctions exposure, GSC instructed Square Sanctions to initiate the funds blocking process in connection with a merchant’s transactions. However, before the funds were blocked, the merchant initiated refunds of the subject transactions. As a result, Block was unable to block and report the transactions as mandated by OFAC regulations. As this issue reduces Block’s ability to comply with OFAC regulations, this issue is rated High.
RCA Description: Processes, Product, or Services: The root cause of this issue is service design. The freeze control as it exists today was not specifically designed for the sanctions investigation use case. While it was designed to prohibit certain money movement, it was not designed to serve as a comprehensive money movement restriction, which is a design limitation for a sanctions-specific use case. Additionally, users outside of Block Sanctions can place and lift the freeze, which is another design limitation that results from the freeze control not being specifically designed for a sanctions-specific use case.
Interested Parties:

Issue Rating Justification: This is considered a High risk issue because it exposes Block to regulatory enforcement risk and reduces Block’s ability to comply with regulatory requirements, including requirements to block funds and report to various sanctions regulatory authorities (including but not limited to OFAC). As noted above, GSC recently identified one instance where Block was unable to fulfill its blocking and reporting requirements due to gaps involving the existing freeze control—specifically regarding refunds.
Issue Progress Updates:

 While the initial request for the comprehensive, sanctions-only freeze was raised in March 2024, a subsequent proposal was raised in September 2024 using the intake form that recently had been implemented by Compliance Engineering & Technology (CET).
 

 Although the initial request for the control enhancement was raised in March 2024, an issue was not formally raised in Protecht at that time. This is because Block was in the early stages of the launch of the Issue Management Program, and the Sanctions function was prioritizing other items that presented a more real and tangible risk. It was not until March 2025 that the hypothetical risk associated with refunds turned into a tangible risk. That, coupled with the delay in building out control, prompted the formal creation of this issue.

Issue Title: Vendor thresholds
Issue Description: Block utilized information from two vendors to block and alert transactions with exposure to terrorism associated wallets. The Department’s investigation revealed that, with respect to one of the vendors, Block’s system did not generate alerts on Bitcoin transactions until the recipient’s wallet had more than 1% exposure to terrorism-connected wallets, and Block did not automatically block transactions to wallets with exposure to terrorism-connected wallets until the exposure exceeded 10%. Any amount of funds transferred to terrorism-connected wallets is illegal and setting threshold alerts above 0% without a risk-based analysis supporting that decision, falls short of the regulatory requirement that licensees implement risk-based policies, procedures, and practices to ensure compliance with BSA and OFAC regulations.

Issue Title: Lack of Real-Time Sanctions Interdiction for Bitcoin (BTC) withdrawals
Issue Description:
Prior to May 2024, Cash App bitcoin withdrawals were not subjected to real-time interdiction. While all bitcoin withdrawals were screened in real-time using third party blockchain analytics providers, (Chainalysis and Elliptic), there was no accompanying real-time interdiction. When the screening tools flagged a potential sanctions concern, the system would automatically decline the withdrawal and return the Bitcoin to the customer's Cash App balance. This would trigger the creation of a case for review by a Cash Sanctions Operations Analyst (“Analyst”). However, by the time an Analyst reviewed a flagged case, the customer might have already withdrawn their Bitcoin through a separate transaction or series of transactions. In such situations, even if the analysis revealed that the original transaction violated sanctions laws and regulations, Cash App had no way to block or recover the funds. This meant that Cash App could find itself unable to fulfill its regulatory obligations under U.S. sanctions laws. Given this, the issue rating is High.
 

 The root cause of this issue is process design. When bitcoin withdrawal functionality and screening was initially rolled out, it was not designed to contain the ability to interdict transactions in real-time. The bitcoin withdrawal screening was in place since at least 2018; however, the screening queue did not transition to a sanctions team until late 2021, which may have factored into the design limitation.
 

 This gap was formally raised by the Cash Sanctions Team in December 2022 with real-world examples to illustrate the elevated risk of potential sanctions violations given the lack of real-time interdiction of BTC withdrawals.
Hyperlinks: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1m6d4btYwpSLUsQqqGZhWsVIXyBurjEJx-WiU8u5-KAE/edit?tab=t.0|Action Plan Intake Form (with Support/Evidence Linked under Deliverable)
RCA Description: Process design: When bitcoin withdrawal functionality and screening was initially rolled out, it did not contain the ability to interdict transactions in real-time.
Issue Rating Justification: The lack of real-time sanctions interdiction controls for Cash App BTC withdrawal transactions increases the risk of potential violations of OFAC sanctions regulations, which could result in regulatory fines and penalties as well as reputational consequences.
Issue Progress Updates:
This issue was fully remediated as of May 2024. Remediation was done in phases, starting with a 1% roll-out on January 17, 2024.
 

 While this issue was identified in 2022 and remediated in May 2024, an issue was not formally raised in Protecht at that time. This is because Block was in the early stages of the launch of the Issue Management Program, and this issue, which was on track for closure, was a known issue well before the Issue Management Program launched.
Issue Action Plans:
Identify Compliance Requirements (1287871)
 Identify Technical Requirements (1287874)
 Implement phased roll-out of real-time interdiction control (1287876)
Closure Request Justification: This issue was added for record-keeping purposes only. The concerns associated with this issue were mitigated with the implementation of BTC Real-Time Sanctions and Interdiction Screening in May 2024. The action plans uploaded this to issue explain critical steps taken to implement the control.

Issue Title: Process Deficiency in Risk Operations - Banking : Manual SSN Validation Workflow
Issue Description:
As part of the Cash App Risk Operations Banking CCS workflow, Marqeta's (vendor supporting a portion of the workflow) daily Social Security Number (SSN) validation process was suspending Cash App customers for invalid SSNs. To resolve these suspensions, Cash App Risk agents were then engaged to:
 

  1. Request SSN cards from customers via SendSafely (CF1), and
  2. Manually enter SSN data into Marqeta's dashboard. 
 

 This manual workflow on suspensions presented the following risks which could have led to regulatory complaints and/or brand damage:
  - Customer Trust: Email-based SSN documentation requests created security concerns
  - Security Risk: Manual handling of sensitive Personal Identifiable Information (PII) by agents increased exposure
 

 This gap was identified during an internal leadership review of SSN Suspension workflows. Root cause is believed to be upstream, within the Identity Eng/IDV Vendor space, though unconfirmed. This issue documents the mitigation actions taken by Risk Operations Banking to address the known downstream impacts only.
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: Operational or process Gap resulting in an update to internal policies, processes, or procedures
Issue Action Plans: Process Deficiency in Risk Operations - Banking : Manual SSN Validation Workflow (1287863)
Closure Request Justification: 4/22/25 - IM Lead reviewed evidence package; no exceptions noted. - Charlotte Vasi

Issue Title: A bug affecting Android app versions 4.2 - 4.72 (July 2023 - November 2024) limited Dispute submissions to single-file uploads despite allowing multiple file selection.
Issue Description:
A bug in Android app versions 4.2-4.72 (July 3, 2023 - November 15, 2024) prevented customers from uploading multiple files during dispute submissions. While customers could select additional files through the system file picker, only the first file would successfully upload and attach to the dispute. All subsequent file selections appeared to work but silently failed - the files wouldn't appear in the upload screen or attach to the submission.
 

 Since dispute flows often require multiple pieces of evidence across different steps (e.g., receipts, communication records, product photos), this limitation may have led to incomplete submissions. Customers likely believed they had successfully uploaded all their evidence, when in fact only their first file was included. This missing documentation could have directly impacted dispute outcomes, potentially resulting in denials that might have been approved with complete evidence.
 

 Key Dates:
 

 Issue Detected: October 30, 2024
 

 SEV Initiated: October 31, 2024
 

 Fix Rollout: November 11-15, 2024
 

 Fix Completion: November 15, 2024
 

 The bug affected all dispute types where multiple file uploads were needed, potentially leaving customers without proper representation in their dispute cases.
RCA Description: Technology
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure resulting in an impact to regulatory obligations
Issue Progress Updates: Internal QA completed by IM Analyst Stephen Green - No exceptions found, clear to proceed with closure.
Issue Action Plans: A bug affecting Android app versions 4.2 - 4.72 (July 2023 - November 2024) limited dispute submissions to single-file uploads despite allowing multiple file selection. (1287867)
Closure Request Justification: Evidence packet reviewed by Issue Management Lead (Charlotte Vasi) on 6/27/25.

Issue Title: BIN denylist eligibility data (ineligible cards) does not properly flow to downstream systems to block an attempt to link a denylisted BIN that has expired or changed.
Issue Description:
GCMT reviewed a sample of 60 linking attempts (30 that were successful, and 30 that failed) for cards that are denylisted to verify that the control appropriately blocked attempts to link the BIN to an account. 8 out of 30 (26.67%) successfully linked BINs were on the denylist. 
 

 The business performed a root cause analysis noting that when a customer attempts to link a denylisted BIN, Instrument Store performs a real time account update verification check with the bank issuing the card. If the denylisted BIN has expired or changed, the bank provides the replacement card information. The replacement card data is flagged as ineligible for linking; however, the eligibility data does not flow to downstream systems allowing the card to be linked to the Cash App account. This gap creates a circumvention in the control that could allow bad actors associated with denylisted cards to perform bad activity on their Cash App account.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2025 Q1; Cash App - NYDFS Post Implementation Phase 4 (1041793)
RCA Description: Root cause stems from the control design. The control design does not fully capture downstream impact of upstream data updates.
Issue Rating Justification: Linking Denylisted BINs to customer account may result in regulatory penalties. Engineering updates will be required (and should be prioritized) to eliminate all downstream impact when the data has been updated.
Issue Action Plans: Cash App DenyListed BIN Eligibility Code Update (1289853)
Closure Request Justification:
GCMT reviewed the implementation support and found no exceptions. Please refer to the closure document for details on the testing performed.

Issue Title: Square Australia’s current programs, policies and procedures do not comply with all Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre guidance requirements for the Outsourcing of Anti-Money Laundering and Counter Terrorism Financing functions
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement 
 Due to the size, volume and scale of the guidance, It is not practical to include all the specific guidance (excerpts) from the Austrac Outsourcing Guidance in this report. We have provided links below so you can reference the relevant section of the Austral Guidance when reviewing our individual observations.
 https://www.austrac.gov.au/business/core-guidance/using-outsourcing-help-meet-your-amlctf-obligations 
 

 Issue Description 
 GCMT performed a GAP analysis between the guidance requirements and relevant Square AU programs, policies and procedures to determine if Square Australia's practices were currently designed to align with the new guidance or if an uplift is required to comply in full. The GAPS identified below have been documented by the Guidance Section and Title. 
 Section 1 - Identify the risks that may arise through Outsourcing 
 a) Individual Risk Assessment for Outsourced Service Providers
 There is no current expectation (documented or in place) in practice, where an individual risk assessment is required to be performed and documented when outsourcing AML/CTF functions.
 Based on AUSTRAC's definition in this section relating to the assessment of risk factors, the risk assessment should be performed by the Risk SME following the review of the initial completed "Compliance Due Diligence" Questionnaire. The answers to the due diligence questions should in part, drive the final outcome of the risk assessment and in determining if mitigating controls are required to be put in place, agreed and documented with the Service Provider, prior to the engagement commencing with Square AU. 
 b) Outsourcing Risk in Square Australia's Annual ML/TF Risk Assessment 
 Outsourcing risk is not separately considered as part of the annual ML/TF risk assessment with the exception of a brief notation within "Distribution Channels" that the likelihood of ML/TF risk is unlikely from the use of third parties (IDV and PEP reviews) with low business risk. This statement is not supported by any further analysis or risk assessment as GCMT noted in "Observation A". 
 c) Transaction Monitoring 
 The current AML or Outsourcing Programs, Procedures and Policies do not prescribe requirements for “a thorough ML/TF risk assessment to be undertaken” where transaction monitoring is being proposed to be outsourced. GCMT notes that Square AU is not currently,or historically outsourcing transaction monitoring activities.
 Section 2 - Conduct Due Diligence on Outsourced Service Providers 
 Compliance Due Diligence processes and oversight do not ensure an assessment of suitability & expertise in all cases
 The completion of due diligence is currently triggered by the Think Smart system, whereby due diligence will be required to be completed and reviewed by Block/Square Subject Matter Experts. The Due Diligence is facilitated by “Due Diligence Questionnaires” which are sent to the Outsourced Service Provider to complete with the responsibility for review and approval by the assigned SME. This process is currently focused on questions to assess and evaluate the provider from a compliance, financial, legal and/or regulatory risk perspective. 
 The “Compliance Due Diligence Questionnaire” currently consists of 38 questions which are focused on key elements such as Privacy/Data, Record Retention, Due Diligence, personnel and monitoring and quality control practices. 
 The current compliance process for review and oversight does not have individual due diligence and/or toll gates that require an assessment to be undertaken and documented over the provider’s suitability and skills to perform the proposed function and/or activities. 
 GCMT note that within the Go/Buy process there is an option for the individual submitting the ticket to select the Request for Proposal "RFP" option which will then be forwarded through to the Block Sourcing team who will work with the individual to assess the proposed Vendor and determine if additional service providers may need to be considered. As part of this process the Sourcing team may send out a questionnaire to the proposed service provider which is focused on business level information such as expertise and experience. The responses and documentation received is retained in a separate system called Scout. 
 However, as the individual submitting the Go/Buy ticket may not be the Square AU Risk SME, confirmation to if an RFP has been completed and if yes, access to the responses/information received including Sourcing's final assessment would be reliant on the Square AU SME to request during the compliance due diligence review. 
 Additionally, GCMT notes per confirmation with Third Party Risk Management that an RFP is not required for all Outsourced Service Providers and is driven by the expected dollar amount of the engagement. 
 In the event that an RFP is not triggered or requested/reviewed by the Risk SME, an assessment of suitability in accordance with the guidance requirements cannot be guaranteed. In these situations, consideration should be given to if additional questions relating to these attributes should be included in the Compliance Due Diligence Questionnaire. 
 For AML Outsourced Service Providers, this would also be required to factor into the overall AML/CTF Risk Assessment conducted for the provider (link to GCMT observations in Section 1 above). 
 GCMT notes that the “Square AU Outsourcing Policy - Planning and Assessing Risk” states that Square AU will choose third parties using the following criteria "the reputation, creditworthiness and performance of each third party and Square AU's understanding of the operations and capabilities of each third party. Additionally, the policy notes that in each case Square AU will need to satisfy itself that the third party is competent, experienced and qualified, has the required resources, is familiar with Square AU's business and obligations as an Australian Financial Services License holder and of good fame and character.
 Section 3 - Understand legal restrictions on sharing information with Outsourced Service Providers No differentiation between Due Diligence, Legal Contracts and/or Onboarding for Outsourcing of Suspicious Matter Reports (SMR), AUSTRAC correspondence or Section 49/49B of the AML/CTF Act. 
 The current programs, practices and controls for due diligence, legal contracts and onboarding of Outsourced Service Providers do not differentiate between an Outsourced Service Provider who is providing general AML services and one who will be providing services relating to SMR, AUSTRAC and/or Sections 49/49B of the AML/CTF Act. Per the guidance this includes: 
 ● Additional terms and/or clauses in contractual agreements relating to the "tipping off" restriction which may result in criminal penalties 
 ● Controls and checks to ensure third parties do not have unauthorised access to data, correspondence or files containing information relating to SMR, AUSTRAC or 49/49B Notices. 
 GCMT notes that Square AU is not currently or historically outsourcing any activities noted above. 
 Section 4 - Use a written agreement for Outsourcing 
 GCMT obtained a copy of the current Square AU “BPO Outsourced Service Provider Agreement” template to determine if all information required by the guidance was included. 
 GCMT noted the following information which is partially or not currently included as a pre-existing clause in the template. However, per confirmation with Commercial Counsel, additional information and clauses can be included in all contracts at the discretion of the Subject Matter Experts. 
 1. Minimum Requirements: Services and performance targets, oversight mechanisms and mechanisms to manage compliance risks if the AML/CTF functions are not carried out properly. 
 2. General Details, Performance Targets and Verification of Performance: oversight, monitoring and review provisions, expected service standards including reporting arrangements and quality assurance and details of how you and your outsourced service provider will implement any outcomes of any independent reviews. 
 3. Breaches of the Agreement: The current template contains references to Breaches however there is no detailed information relating to expectations around Breaches as required by the Guidance. 
 Additionally, GCMT notes that the Square AU Outsourcing Policy states that “agreements should contain the obligations of vendors, service standards, dispute resolution and other relevant matters. However, the current Outsourcing Policy does not reference the responsibility of the Risk SME to ensure that where required, template agreements are amended to include all additional clauses and/or information noted above. 
 The current Due Diligence process via ThinkSmart/VER does not have an additional “Tollgate” or checkpoint for Compliance review/sign-off prior to agreement finalization. However, GCMT notes that the Risk SME has access to view the progress of the due diligence process from start to finish including relevant team stages with functionality to comment or raise concerns in ThinkSmart to Commercial Counsel. Additionally, a copy of the contract may also be uploaded/visible in the ticket but in some cases might not be the final version. 
 Section 5 - Monitor and review Outsourcing Arrangements 
 The current policies do not document an expectation for ongoing monitoring to include confirmation and review that the AML/CTF obligations are being met whilst using the outsourcing arrangement and for the arrangement to be adjusted in light of any changes to the AML/CTF risks the business is exposed to. 
 Additionally, as there are currently no expectation for an individual risk assessment to be undertaken for Service Providers as noted above, the following guidance recommendation cannot be met:
 "As with your due diligence, AUSTRAC recommends that the processes you use to monitor the outsourced service provider are proportionate to the level of AML/CTF compliance risks and ML/TF risks you have identified with the outsourcing arrangements." 
 Section 6 – Document procedures for managing Outsourcing Arrangements in your AML/CTF program
 The Square AU AML/CTF Program does not document how the Board will have oversight over outsourced arrangements and linkages with ML/TF risks. 
 The Square Outsourcing Policy documents controls in place for maintaining Board oversight however this is limited by the following factors: 
 1. No mention of ML/TF risks 
 2. Currently only Trulioo and Veriff are noted as outsourced service providers which are monitored under the policy 
 3. The outsourcing policy is not referenced in the AML/CTF Program 
 The Third Party Risk Management policy and processes do not require board approval for an Outsourced Service Provider to be accepted/finalized. The current responsibility and decision to obtain board and/or committee approval prior to the engagement of an AML/CTF provider is held by the regional Compliance Lead/MLRO. 
 Additionally, consideration should be given to Intragroup Outsourcing of AML/CTF functions/activities within Block given the recent structural changes and any future changes. Square AU's positioning on Intra-Group Outsourcing should then drive, if these functions/activities will be subject to the requirements of this AUSTRAC guidance with appropriate documentation.

Issue Title: Square Australia's current processes, controls and contracts in place for AML Rights Source do not comply with all Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre guidance requirements for the Outsourcing of Anti-Money Laundering and Counter Terrorism Financing functions
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement 
 Due to the size, volume and scale of the guidance, It is not practical to include all the specific guidance (excerpts) from the Austrac Outsourcing Guidance in this report. We have provided links below so you can reference the relevant section of the Austral Guidance when reviewing our individual observations.
 https://www.austrac.gov.au/business/core-guidance/using-outsourcing-help-meet-your-amlctf-obligations 
 

 Issue Description 
 GCMT performed a GAP analysis between the guidance requirements and the processes and practices in place for AML Rights Source ('AMLRS'). The GAPS identified below have been documented by the Guidance Section and Title. 
 Section 1 – Identify the risks that may arise through Outsourcing 
 No individual risk assessment has been conducted for AMLRS 
 In reviewing Square AU's engagement with AMLRS since initial engagement in 2020, GCMT noted that the scope of the work performed from case reviews of beneficial ownership has changed to case reviews of PEP & Sanction alerts at the date of testing in 2025. 
 During this time period GCMT noted that there have been changes to AMLRS which may affect a risk assessment if conducted individually, such as a change in the operations of AMLRS from a staffing and offshoring perspective. 
 ● i.e. when AMLRS was engaged in 2020 all operations and staffing were within North America. At today’s date, staffing has now been outsourced outside of North America with resourcing for PEP and Sanction cases in India. GCMT noted that this was an advised future strategy at the date of engagement. 
 Section 4 – Use a written agreement for Outsourcing 
 Following review of Master Service Agreements ('MSA') and Statement of Work ('SOW') between Square Australia and AMLRS, GCMT noted the following information/clauses required by the Guidance which were only partially included or wholly absent from the current and historic agreements. 
 1. Minimum Requirements: Oversight mechanisms are not specifically detailed within the SOW or MSA with the exception of Service Level Agreements and Remediation requirements including Penalties in Amendment agreements. Oversight mechanisms to manage compliance risks if the relevant AML/CTF functions are not carried out properly are also not included with the exception of Block's right to terminate the SOW in the event that AMLRS fails to meet performance standards or breaches one of the contractual terms 
 2. General Details, Performance Targets and Verification of Performance: Oversight, monitoring and review provisions are not included in the SOW/MSA with respect to detail pertaining to current processes and controls agreed between AMLRS and Square AU. "Details of how you and your outsourced service provider will implement the outcomes of any independent reviews" is not currently documented. GCMT notes that an amendment was made in 2021 to include a section for "Audit" which requires the Vendor to permit Block appointment of auditors and inspectors however does not include the implementation of any outcomes or findings for these reviews. 
 3. Breaches of the Agreement: The "maximum number of breaches allowed before a review of the agreement is initiated" and "record keeping targets" are not currently included in the SOW/MSA. Other specific requirements relating to breaches of the agreement include "requirements for the outsourced service provider to remedy any breach of the agreement within a specified timeframe", "suspension of the agreement until identified deficiencies are addressed". 
 Additionally, the guidance states "In accordance with your AML/CTF program and the level of AML/CTF compliance risk or ML/TF risk you assess in relation to the breach, you may decide to escalate breaches by the Outsourced Service Provider to your board or senior management for action. GCMT noted per confirmation with the Outsourced Oversight Manager that no breaches had occurred during our scope period 1 February 2024 - 1 February 2025. However, GCMT noted no documented practices or processes in place for communication and/or notification to the Square AU Compliance Lead to ensure oversight and reporting to the Board, if deemed required following review. 
 Section 5 - Monitor and review Outsourcing Arrangements 
 As noted in Section1 – Risk Assessment above, no individual risk assessment is conducted for AMLRS. As a result, the below guidance recommendations cannot be fully met; 
 ● confirm that your business is meeting its AML/CTF obligations while using the outsourcing arrangement; and
 ● adjust the arrangement in light of any changes to the ML/TF risks your business is likely to be exposed to.
 Section 6 – Document procedures for managing outsourcing arrangements in your AML/CTF Program 
 GCMT notes that there is no reporting which is provided to the Board relating to oversight, performance and management of AMLRS. 
 Per the “Q4 2024 Square AU Board Pre Read”, reporting for quarterly PEP numbers are noted on page 12 however this is confined to the number of alerts which were raised between Q4 23 and Q3 24. 
 The Square AU Outsourcing Policy was submitted for review and approval in Q4 24 with the major changes noted on page 16 with minor updates to reflect changes in the program identified through annual review of AFSL policies. (Update Square title, new template, removal of operational information) Per the December 2024 Square AU Outsourcing Policy, there are controls in place which document the expectations of reporting against Service Providers to the Board on an annual basis to ensure ongoing monitoring and oversight. However, GCMT note that per policy, AMLRS is not listed in Annexure 1 as an Outsourced Service Provider/outsourcing Arrangement which is subject to ongoing monitoring and reporting to the Board 
 Additionally, the following considerations were noted: 
 1. From review of prior Square AU AML/CTF Programs (from 2020 and 2021) GCMT note the existence of a section where Outsourced Service Providers were documented including their main scope of work/outsourced services. GCMT notes that the current AML Program in 2024 does not contain any references or information relating to Outsourced Service Providers or AMLRS. It should also be noted that the above inclusion of Outsourced Service Providers in the annual AML/CTF Programs were the main and sole source of review and approval from the Board for AMLRS at initial engagement and where changes occurred to the scope of work. 
 2. The current controls in place for the ongoing monitoring and management of AMLRS by Outsourced Service Oversight Management, Square Quality Assurance and Square Compliance are not currently documented in a centralized location/procedure.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: Inaccurate Denial Messaging for Account Funding Transactions - Related to Goods and Services Not Received and Goods and Services Not as Described
Issue Description:
The Disputes team has identified a gap with how Cash App communicates Account Funding Transaction (AFT) claim denials to customers. This gap occurs when customers dispute purchases made on other platforms (like Apple Pay) using money transferred from Cash App.
 

 When customers file these claims as goods and services not received or not as described, agents deny them using the ""Unrelated Account"" indicator and send a standard denial message. This message incorrectly states, “our investigation has led us to determine that the merchant fulfilled their obligations for the goods/services associated with this transaction”. However, Cash App cannot investigate these transactions because they occur on other platforms.
 

 The current denial message implies an investigation that Cash App cannot perform, which could be considered misleading under UDAAP regulations. Customers should be provided clear information that explains Cash App cannot investigate purchases made on other platforms. The message should direct customers to file their claim with the platform where they made the purchase and clarify that Cash App's investigation ability is limited to the initial money transfer.
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: The issue relates to an internal control or process that is associated with low-risk activity (includes instances or other safeguards are noted to be absent)
Issue Action Plans: Inaccurate Denial Messaging for Account Funding Transactions - Related to Goods and Services Not Received and Goods and Services Not as Described (1291160)

Issue Title: The questionnaire that customers must submit when disputing ‘cancelled recurring subscriptions’, 'paid by other means', 'atm withdrawal error', 'goods and services not as described', and 'goods and services not received' does not include the necessary questions to accurately assess and resolve the claim
Issue Description:
As part of the disputes process and based on specific rules outlined by Visa, customers are required to provide certain information in order to properly decision a claim. The customer questionnaire used to collect this information must reflect the requirements as outlined by Visa to ensure the business obtains all of the necessary information to decision the claim.
 

 Currently, the questionnaire is lacking the following information the following categories:
 - Canceled Recurring Subscription (date merchant was notified that the account was closed, details used to contact the merchant including email address, telephone number, or physical address)
 - Paid by Other Means, Duplicate Transaction (details about Merchant response, supporting documents)
 - ATM Withdrawal Error (details about partial funds and supporting documents)
 - Goods & Services Not as Described (details about the merchandise/service, merchant response, attempt to return and canceled service)
 - Goods & Services Not Received (details about merchant response and expected goods/service)
 

 As a result of the omission, claims could potentially be denied in error. This gap was identified by the Quality Management (QM) review process when evaluating the ‘Canceled Recurring Subscription workflow’. While no specific failures have been confirmed, the data provided by QM suggests that a large number of these claims were denied in 2024.
 

 It’s important to highlight that a tangential issue was opened in 2024 (Protecht ID 1108967) to develop and document a process for reviewing and implementing recommended changes from Visa. That issue was resolved and closed in November 2024. This current issue represents gaps that existed prior to the new process implemented in 2024 with respect to issue ID 1108967.
 

 Between January 1, 2024, and November 6, 2024, a significant 91.25% (66,812 out of 73,212) of Canceled Recurring Subscription claims were denied, with only 0.05% (38 out of 73,212) successfully filed. This indicates that only <1% of these claims are both disputable and meet the necessary filing requirements. Such a low filling percentage further suggests that the questionnaire fails to guide customers in providing the essential information needed for successful claims.
RCA Description: Product
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure resulting in an Impact to regulatory obligations
Issue Progress Updates: IM Lead reviewed and approved the evidence package on 10/15/25. - Charlotte Vasi
Issue Action Plans: The questionnaire that customers must submit when disputing ‘cancelled recurring subscriptions’ does not include the necessary questions to accurately assess and resolve the claim (1291165)

Issue Title: AAN detection control for Retro
Issue Description: This issue relates to the Retro product. Currently, Retro does not have a control in place which reviews whether or not users, who should have received an adverse action notice (AAN), did in fact receive their AAN. In other words, a control to detect AAN receipt. A similar control currently exists for Borrow and should be mirrored for the Retro product.
RCA Description: Control needs to mirror the current control in place for Borrow, but ensure the AAN reasons specific to Retro are put in place.
Interested Parties:
Mia Bockus
 Corey Chamberlain
 Langston Walker
Issue Rating Justification: Given the regulatory risk related to ECOA and likelihood of increased AAN use-cases. Additionally, we would like to have the control in place before the upcoming exam in Q225.
Issue Progress Updates:
1). Currently, Retro only sends AANs if the customer fails to pass the CIP V1 flow. They receive an AAN in the case that they: a) Fail to IDV; or, b) exhaust all allowed attempts.
 2). This control is not meant to determine whether or not the information contained within the AAN is accurate, but rather that AANs are correctly received.
 3). We should document reasons for existing use cases for sending AANs for Retro users; and how different/similar to Borrow; outline differences. Additionally, we should note what are the new requirements and use cases for sending an AAN (i.e. if someone overdue on Borrow loan, they cannot take Retro, but no AAN needs to be sent).
 4). Once implemented for Retro, reporting will cover controls for both Borrow and Retro via Anomolo and monitored on #lending-situation.
 a). 10 day SOP, after such an investigation would be opened.
Closure Request Justification: The control went live on May 13. According to Cash Engineering, the control will only "look forward," and if it detects an AAN is not sent, it will flag the user to have an engineer to manual send the AAN.

Issue Title: Cash App Complaints Workflow and Technology Limitations Caused Complaints Case Backlog, Leading to Missed SLAs and Regulatory Risks
Issue Description:
Technology and workflow limitations have impacted the Cash App Complaints Team's ability to efficiently resolve cases, which fall into two categories: 
  - Planned: Identified through internal regular cadence reviews (e.g. Team Lead reviews, QA reviews, QC reviews, Compliance reviews, etc.)
  - Unplanned: Reactionary events in response to internal incidents or external parties
 The technology supporting the process workflow has suffered from two critical deficiencies: the absence of systemic case monitoring and detection capabilities, and no mechanism for post-closure case adjustments, which left the team unable to flag, reopen, or reroute closed cases that required additional resolution steps. These constraints resulted in a growing backlog of unresolved complaints, putting the team at risk of missing service level agreements (SLAs) for customer response times. Beyond the immediate impact on customer service, this situation raised the organization's exposure to regulatory risk, as the team lacked the essential tools needed to ensure thorough and timely complaint resolution within 30 days of the case opening.
RCA Description: Technology
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure resulting in an Impact to regulatory obligations
Issue Action Plans: Cash App Complaints Workflow and Technology Limitations Caused Complaints Case Backlog, Leading to Missed SLAs and Regulatory Risks (1292495)

Issue Title: Unauthorized Issuance of Cash Cards to Non-Customers
Issue Description:
Instances were identified where Cash Cards were issued to individuals who were not legitimate customers of our platform (e.g., non-customers). A preliminary investigation determined that the root cause stemmed from deficiencies in the identity verification process, resulting in failures to properly authenticate and verify users prior to card issuance. Complaint data further corroborated these findings. This gap introduced fraud risk and regulatory exposure.
 

 In late April 2025, it was confirmed that a recently completed large-scale project effectively mitigated the risk to a level within the organization's risk tolerance. Accordingly, this issue is being registered in Protecht, along with documentation of the corrective actions completed between February and March 2025.
 

 The issue is scheduled to close in May 2025 to provide the Issue Management Team adequate time to evaluate the supporting evidence and artifacts associated with the remediation efforts.

Despite the implementation of targeted corrective actions, the potential for fraudulent activity cannot be entirely eliminated. Issue Title: Early Repayment Refunds
Issue Description:
The Retro Loan Agreements (First Electronic Bank (FEB) Originated, Section 8.c states that early repayments are based on state law, "...will refund any unearned amount of the Finance Charge in accordance with the law of your state of residence when you entered into this Agreement." 
 

 However, business practices do not consider state laws since: Early repayment refunds are not applied to FEB originated Retro loans, and repayment refunds are the same for all customers, regardless of state. Product Counsel will revise the language in this agreement to align with our current practice (more specifically: the Bank will refund pro rata any portion of the Finance Charge that exceeds 5% of the Amount Financed, proportional to the number of days remaining until the Due Date etc.).
 

 Note that issue description previously specified the following, however after further review, the issue scope was broadened:
 UT law states that if a borrower pays off a loan before the end of the agreed term, the company must refund any portion of a prepaid finance charge over 5%, proportional to the remaining loan term. A control already exists for Borrow users, regardless of location, which meets the requirements of the UT law. Several departments are reviewing the matter to understand if a similar control must be uplifted for Retro users.
RCA Description: The need for a control to identify qualifying loans, and subsequently refund amount if needed.
Interested Parties:
Langston Walker
 Isaiah Boswell
 Corey Chamberlain
Issue Rating Justification: Given that the control already exists for Borrow, mirroring it for Retro should not be difficult. However, because a remediation will need to be conducted for Retro users, this increases the rating.
Issue Progress Updates:
1. Multiple departments, outside counsel, Product and Lend Reg, worked together to provide three options to the business. They decided to accept the one that mirrored Borrow.
 2. The control for the Borrow product already detects and refunds the user if appropriate. This satisfies the requirements of the UT law; and in essence, all Borrow and Retro loans, regardless of location, will meet the UT law standards.
 3. It would be preferable to imbed the automation of the refund of fees in the control, as it is for Borrow. However, if manual procedures were included, after qualifying loans are identified, this would still be in compliance with the law.
 4. A regular term for review and refund, if a loan qualifies, should also be put in place
 5. Testing and monitoring rules should also be set up.
 6. A manual remediation and refund of Retro users who qualify will be conducted.
Issue Action Plans:
APR Calculation (1344627)
 Loan Agreement Update (1361135)
 Refund Effected Population (1391542)
Closure Request Justification:
This issue is ready for closure, all steps below have been taken:
 

 1. APR Calculation Validation
 2. Customer Remediation (Refunds)
 3. Loan Agreement Updates

Issue Title: Technical Gap Enabled Continued Use of Discontinued Risk Queue Despite Process Updates
Issue Description:
Between June 2024 and January 2025, a significant workflow breakdown occurred in the appeals case management process. Despite Project Burgundy's updated Knowledge Articles (KAs) explicitly instructing against its use, frontline advocates retained the functionality/capability to transfer cases to the discontinued "Risk Denylisted queue" in CF1. The objective of Project Burgundy was to uplift the current denylist process and to provide an initial pathway for customers to appeal those decisions. 
 As a result of this breakdown (the functionality/capability not being removed), advocates were able to continue routing cases to an abandoned queue, resulting in unresolved cases, extended processing delays, and increased regulatory risk.
RCA Description: Technology
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure resulting in an Impact to regulatory obligations
Issue Action Plans: Technical Gap Enabled Continued Use of Discontinued Risk Queue Despite Process Updates (1293804)

Issue Title: Customer Success Case Transfer Process Gap Resulted in ID Theft and ATO Queue Cases Going Unprocessed
Issue Description: A significant workflow vulnerability has been discovered in Cash App's case management system, specifically affecting the ID(Identity) Theft and ATO(Account Take Over) Brokerage and Lending queues. The Quality Control team first identified this issue in the ID Theft queue during August 2024, with subsequent discovery in the ATO Brokerage and Lending queue in January 2025. The fundamental problem arose from Customer Success Advocates' improper case transfer practices, which rendered cases invisible to specialized processing teams. These incorrect transfers occurred in two ways: either cases were transferred while already in "Closed" status, or they were transferred as "Open" but subsequently closed after the transfer. The situation was further exacerbated by CF1's (Salesforce case management workflow tool) Support Bot, which automatically closes cases after 7 days of inactivity, as per its intended functionality. This automated closure created an inaccessible backlog of unprocessed cases. The problem persisted due to two critical control gaps: the lack of clear documentation in Knowledge Articles (KAs) regarding proper transfer procedures, and the absence of technological safeguards to prevent closed-case transfers. Downstream effects include: unaddressed identity theft claims remained unresolved, potentially extending customers' exposure to fraud, while missed service level agreements created additional regulatory risk.
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure resulting in an Impact to regulatory obligations

Issue Title: Extract, Transform and Load (ETL) Design Flaw Disrupted Denylist Appeals Processing which Led to Cases Not Being Routed Properly
Issue Description:
A critical system failure in the Denylist Appeals (formerly known as Compliance Denylisted) Queue process was identified by the Complaint Insights team, affecting over 60,000 customer cases since July 2024. The root issue was identified as a fundamental design flaw in the ETL process, categorized by Complaints Insight Team as "Technology / Poor System Selection and or Design." The standard workflow requires customer appeals to be processed through CF1 (Salesforce platform for customer service), where they enter the Denylist Appeals Queue. From there, an ETL process should evaluate each case's eligibility and either create a case in Notary for manual review if eligible, or mark cases as "ineligible" for resolution through a target resolve process designed to inform customers that their case is ineligible for appeal. 
 

 It appears that incremental logic changes were introduced over time, which inadvertently modified the original case flow. This led to unintended handling behaviors or errors. The high case volume amplified the impact of these issues. These limitations prevented proper tagging of ineligible cases, disrupting the resolution process and causing cases to remain unresolved well beyond the committed 30-day response window. 
 

 The consequences of this system failure have resulted in extensive customer service delays, increased volume of customer complaints, erosion of customer trust, and elevated regulatory risk exposure. The investigation revealed that the ETL system's fundamental design lacked both scalability and sustainability for managing the current case volumes, highlighting a critical need for architectural redesign.
RCA Description: Technology
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure resulting in an Impact to regulatory obligations
Issue Action Plans: Extract, Transform and Load (ETL) Design Flaw Disrupted Denylist Appeals Processing which Led to Cases Not Being Routed Properly (1295142)
Closure Request Justification: Evidence package reviewed by IM Lead on 7/16/25. - Charlotte Vasi

Issue Title: Missing evidence of screening for a subset of Square Merchants
Issue Description:
This Sanctions Issue Intake Form is being submitted to clarify and re-define the issue scope. Note that the original submission did not follow the Sanctions Issue Intake Review Process as outlined in the form instructions: a Root Cause Analysis was not completed, nor were draft Action Plans submitted by the identifying party. This updated Issue Description outlines the relevant background of how this issue was initially identified as well as remedial activity that has occurred since. 
 

 Background
 In March 2025, SQ Global Risk Operations (GRO) and Compliance Analytics (CAX) first identified indications of this issue as part of a review of UK/EU SQ merchants who were manually activated by Customer Support (CS) and might not have been sanctions screened. Through April 2025, the scope of GRO and CAX’s review was broadened to verify sanctions, PEP, and adverse media screening completion for all manually onboarded SQ merchants, and finally all system onboarded SQ merchants. Apparent missing evidence of screening was identified across all phases of review, with differing potential root causes suggested for each population. Again, a formal RCA was not initially completed, with the original submission of this form noting the Root Cause as “unknown.”  
 

 Phase 1 Review 
 Phase 1 consisted of a test of manually onboarded SQ merchants and identified 28,700 merchants (out of a total population of 994,000) without evidence of screening. Of those, 8,888 merchants (with 3,796 remaining active at the time of review) had transactions on record despite a lack of screening evidence. Multiple root causes were suggested and differed based on the underlying reason for manual onboarding. 
 -- Specific to SQ merchants undergoing EIN onboarding, previous weaknesses in the manual activation process were identified as the likely cause. Specifically, Regulator previously allowed for certain Activation action/combos that overrode all activation requirements, reversed all deactivations, and blindly turned on card processing for a unit. However, as of March 31, 2025, new action workflows were launched in Regulator and new SQ merchants cannot be payment-activated without a sanctions screening and ongoing monitoring.
 -- For SQ merchants onboarding in the Falklands Islands, a manual onboarding and initial screening workflow was previously leveraged due to issues with address verification that prevented use of the standard onboarding flow which would create a Legal Entity node and trigger BadActors daily re-screening. This gap has been resolved on a going forward basis as of April 25, 2025. However, the gap remains for existing Falkland Islands SQ merchants which are still re-screened manually. Global Sanctions Compliance approved a three-month policy exception in May 2025. As the matter has not yet been resolved, it has been confirmed that an issue is to be raised in Protecht. Manual onboarding processes that result in lack of a Legal Entity node also impacts Weebly and email TLD screening. These are being addressed via a CET Proposal (CET-461) that remains pending due to its complex scope. 
 

 Phase 2 Review
 Phase 2 consisted of a review of all system-onboarded (i.e. not manually) SQ merchants and identified 1.07 million (out of a total population of 34.8 million) without evidence of screening. Of those, 663,273 merchants (with c. 17,300 currently active) had transactions on record despite a lack of screening evidence. Specific to this population, it is suggested that a BadActors migration may have resulted in the lack of screening evidence on record. In response to a separate/unrelated audit request this matter was discussed and it was recently confirmed that “[t]he actively transacting sellers (17,342) were sent for screening on 5/7/25, which resulted in a small number of cases across various list concerns (less than 70 cases) and provided screening records for the actively transacting population. No concerns were found in the resulting cases and the incident was closed.”
 

 Risk Identification & Remediation 
 While screening and retaining evidence of screening are not explicitly required per OFAC, Block utilizes these mechanisms to meet its sanctions compliance obligations. Failure to provide documentary evidence of completed sanction screens for merchants who have transacted within our ecosystem may be viewed as a significant control failure and point to weaknesses in our Sanctions compliance program.
 

 As outlined above, the overall population of SQ merchants without evidence of screening appears to be the result of multiple root causes. These have already been subject to remediation via a variety of pending (CET Proposal related to legal entity node impacting manual onboards; Protecht issue specific to Falklands Island onboarding workflows) and completed (Regulator Activation changes in March 2025, and ad hoc screening for the sub-population of active system onboarded merchants in May 2025) actions. Given the large number of SQ merchants impacted here and the various potential root cases attributable across that population, from a risk perspective, the remaining gaps are (1) the remediation of any SQ merchants without evidence of completed screening, and (2) the lack of systematic monitoring to detect and escalate any such screening gaps on a going forward basis. 
 

 Accordingly, it is suggested that proposed Actions Plans cover the following: 
 (1) Refreshed CAX request for the current population of SQ merchants that lack evidence of completed screening to identify the remaining population of merchants requiring any gap screening. 
  (a) Review and verification of those results by GSC
  (b) Engage with Sanc Ops to complete gap screening for any active/transacting merchants
  (c) Document plan for remaining lower risk customer cohorts including inactive merchants with historical transactions, and merchants with no transactional activity. This could include additional ad hoc gap screening, deactivation of non-transacting customers, etc.  
 (2) CAX request for implementation of a continuous monitoring dashboard and escalation pathway for any subsequent exceptions, as well as any procedure updates required as a result.
 (3) Sustainability efforts to test that the continuous monitoring is effective
RCA Description: The original submission of this Sanctions Issue Intake Form did not indicate a Root Cause. On reassessment, multiple apparent causes were identified for the various sub-populations of SQ merchants with missing evidence of screening. While remedial action was taken in response to the identified causes thus far, manual workarounds/onboarding flows and system migrations will re-occur as part of BAU. The lack of ongoing monitoring to confirm that evidence of screening is in place for all SQ merchants is a process gap and the ultimate root cause of this issue. Remediating this will ensure that the existing screening gaps are resolved and any future gaps, for whatever reason, are identified and resolved in a timely manner.

Issue Title: Borrower Access to Loan Agreements and Auto Pay Authorization
Issue Description: Users of Borrow (Nick Lomanto) and Retro (Lucy Liu) products are not able to access the copies of the versions of loan agreements in place at the time of their loan’s approval. Lend Counsel and Compliance want to enable the ability to provide the user a copy of the version of the loan document they agreed to at the time they took out the loan; specific loan docs do not need to be provided. The autopay agreement should also be included in this exercise.
RCA Description: It has been determined that we should provide users access to the version of the loan and autopay agreements that they agreed to, as updates are not reflected in the link to the document on the Cash App application.
Interested Parties:
Mia Bockus
 Nicholas Lomanto
 Langston Walker
Issue Rating Justification: There is a regulatory risk related to providing TILA loan docs to borrowers that they agreed to at the time of agreeing to the loan. However, borrowers are provided these docs at the time of origination, but can only access the most recent version thereafter.
Issue Progress Updates:
1). Users can access the copies at the time of origination. However, it's after the point of origination that they may only see the latest version.
 2). Counsel already keeps track of the versions, the work to do here is make sure it is properly linked in the PDF copy of the TILA accessed via ‘borrow history’ or ‘activity.’
 3). Frequency of changes to the loan docs/auto pay agreements should be minimum. If multiple changes do need to be made, it should be made in as few updates as possible.
 4). Individual loan docs should not be stored, however, links to specific, unique versions should be made available, if a borrower (or regulators) asks.
 5). Links to E-sign and Privacy agreements should remain static.
 6). Once a fix is put in place, it will only impact users going forward.
 7). Currently, a link to standard most recent loan documents, via Cash App website, is provided to borrowers.
Closure Request Justification: Product Engineering has confirmed that users are provided links to the loan docs active at the time they originated the loan, and that users can continue to access these documents throughout the life of the loan. Currently, there are two loan agreements in circulation, "February 16, 2021-April 6, 2025" and "April 7, 2025-present."  All updated versions, regardless of the changes (e.g. punctuation vs. accommodations of new features), are stored in Contentful and provided differentiating version numbers. When Product Counsel deems a change as meeting the definition of "material changes," such as those which change the functionality of the disclosures, they subsequently notify Product Engineering to update the URL link for future corresponding loans.

Issue Title: IRS B-Notice Requirement: Cash App needs to update b-notice communications to reflect the date that yield will be disable
Issue Description:
In Q4 2024, when Block received the list of b-noticed customers, those who were enrolled in savings yield received communication in late October that informed them that they needed to correct their personal information on file with Cash App by November 29 or they would no longer be able to earn yield on savings. Yield for these customers was disabled on November 1 to avoid yield accrual by b-noticed customers. At this point, customer-facing surfaces within the app reflected that the customer was not earning yield. (The APY that appears within the Savings applet for those earning yield was removed, as was the estimated interest row that appears in the Activity feed.)
 Gap: While the app surfaces were updated when yield was disabled, we did not proactively notify customers that their yield was disabled on November 1.
Issue Rating Justification: Aligned between Legal, Compliance and Product
Issue Action Plans: Send B-Notice comms (1355184)

Issue Title: 3. The Letter Of Authority Standard Operating Procedure can be enhanced to align with current practice for Customer Service Agents receiving contact from customers that have authorised financial counsellor on file.
Issue Description:
Letter Of Authority Standard Operating Procedures Requirements
 Where the customer wants to take action on the account and where there is an authorised financial counsellor or debt mediator on the customer’s account, the LOA SOP requires the agent to ask the customer if the authorised party is still acting on the customer’s behalf. If the customer confirms ongoing representation, the agent should direct the customers to communicate through their authorized third party.
 

 Issue Description
 GCMT observed that agents are bypassing the question of asking if the authorised party is still acting on the customer's behalf and immediately redirecting customers to their authorized representatives when they determine that the customer wants to take action on the account. This currently does not align with the established procedures. GCMT understands that the question is asked to process LOA removals as it is within the procedure to obtain confirmation from the Account Holder to remove an authorised LOA.
