Issue Title: Previous loan activity appearing on customers’ profiles
Issue Description: During data migration in anticipation of SFS roll-out, a bug was detected which caused consumer loans previously closed to appear as more recent loans on borrowers’ profiles. This caused confusion and multiple complaints before the matter was resolved.
RCA Description: A bug, which appeared during data migration, caused time stamps on historical data to be labeled as more recent. Instead of loan activity being listed behind 2 years of data, the receipts for these items appeared at or near the top of their activity feeds.
Interested Parties:
Mia Bockus
 Corey Chamberlain
 Langston Walker
Issue Rating Justification: Customer confusion due to incorrect data labeling caused numerous complaints.
Issue Progress Updates:
1. No PII data was exposed (i.e. borrowers were only able to view their own loan data and payment history)
 2. The bug impacted a 3rd-party servicer, Loanstar, and only loans prior to June 2023 appeared on users’ profiles.
 3. A backfill was deployed to the system which resolved the issue. The impact was only about 1 day.
 4. The bug was determined to be a 1-time issue and non-systematic.
 5. An approved communication was provided to users while the fix was put in place and after it went live.
Closure Request Justification: Once the issue was identified, a backfill was uploaded in order to resolve the issue; which lasted no more than 24 hours.

Issue Title: Due to differences in current incident handling approaches, there is a need to establish common practices for consistent outcomes.
Issue Description:
Today, teams responding to incidents operate with significant flexibility, choosing from a variety of optional processes. While this approach offers adaptability, it has led to fragmented and inconsistent practices across the organization. Without a more consistent approach, we risk delays, miscommunications, and potentially uneven incident resolution quality. To enable potentially faster, more predictable outcomes and improve enterprise-wide coordination, standardized methodologies are required.
 

 Key concerns are as follows:
 

 1. Unclear Legal and Compliance escalation Criteria:
 

 - Business units lack clarity on when and how to engage Legal, Compliance, or other risk-related functions during and after incident response.
 

 - This ambiguity may result in missed obligations for reporting, risk mitigation, or regulatory compliance.
 

 2. Inconsistent Impact Analysis Across Business Units:
 

 - No standardized methodology exists for assessing customer and/or business impact during incidents.
 

 - Business units apply different criteria for determining when corrective actions are needed beyond correcting for the incident, which may result in misalignment and delays in cross-functional coordination.
 

 3. Lack of formalized workflows for execution of corrective action:
 

 - There is no enterprise-level workflow or playbook guiding when and how to perform analysis to determine customer impacts and/or operational impacts, how corrective action should be planned, executed, and tracked following an incident.
 

 - The absence of structured timelines, ownership assignments, and follow-up checkpoints is likely to contribute to delays and incomplete resolutions and risk exposure, both regulatory and reputational.
 

 4. Varying Roles and Responsibilities:
 

 - Incident management approaches vary and are not clearly defined across the organization with respect to who is required to perform impact analysis and creation and execution of potential action plans to support corrective action.
 

 - This can lead to confusion during incident response and could impact accountability for remediation and follow-up actions.
RCA Description: Processes
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure resulting in an impact to regulatory obligations
Issue Progress Updates:
12/13/2025 - Artifacts and summary package reviewed by IM Lead. - Charlotte Vasi
 10/3/25 - Due date extension request has been approved by Tyler Hand. Please note that the pre-9/30/25 due date extensions process was leveraged as the request was in progress prior to the launch of the new ERM extensions process that went live on 9/30/25. A copy of the approved due date extension request has been added to the issue record. - Charlotte Vasi (FLRM IM Lead)
Issue Action Plans: Due to differences in current incident handling approaches, there is a need to establish common practices for consistent outcomes. (1300401)

Issue Title: The Manual ID team are approving customer identification documents which are not in compliance with the “Manual ID: ANZ Process” and the “Manual ID NZ ONLY: Using Frankie to verify ID” Standard Operating Procedures
Issue Description:
Background
 

 Where Afterpay New Zealand (NZ) is unable to verify a customer electronically using their NZ Drivers Licence, NZ Birth Certificate or NZ Passport, the customer has the opportunity to provide the same or alternative ID using the Afterpay NZ Document Based Verification process.
 

 Procedural Requirement
 

 Per the “Manual ID: ANZ Process” and the “Manual ID NZ ONLY: Using Frankie to verify ID” Standard Operating Procedures, Manual ID team members can only accept documents (E.g. Passport and Driver’s License) that have been certified as true and correct by an authorised trusted referee (E.g. Justice of the Peace, Member of Police or Lawyer) within the last 3 months. The minimum requirements for certification are- 
 Full name of Authorised Trustee
 Title of Authorised Trustee
 Certification of true copy of original document
 Signature
 Date of certification
 

 Issue Description 
 

 GCMT conducted testing for 60 Manual Identity Verification cases that were ‘approved’ and identified 11 cases (18%) where the Manual ID team did not follow all requirements in the “Manual ID: ANZ Process” and the “Manual ID NZ ONLY: Using Frankie to verify ID” Standard Operating Procedures. 
 

 These exceptions related to: 
 6 cases where non-certified documents were accepted; 
 - 1 Primary Photographic Identification Document - Passport 
 - 6 Proof of Address documents - Proof of Bank Account
 

 5 cases where documents were not certified in accordance with the minimum requirements;
 - 3 documents did not include the full name of the Authorised Trustee 
 - 2 documents with certification older than 3 months
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Afterpay New Zealand - Anti Money Laundering - Document Based Verification (1042443)
Issue Rating Justification: Issue rate in accordance with the Issue Management Program rating methodology.
Issue Action Plans: Management to reiterate SOP requirements for Document Based Verification to Manual ID team (1300405)
Closure Request Justification: The business provided notes from a team meeting including topics discussed and attendee list. Additionally, a spreadsheet was shared that included QA Maestro feedback notes for each of the test samples that GCMT noted as observations.

Issue Title: Retro offers not expiring after 7-days
Issue Description: About 6mil expired Retro offers remained on the ‘Activity’ logs of about ~2.2mil customers, which resulted in ~37K customers, who attempted to access financing, were not able to take out loans for desired transactions. Additionally, this error was not detected for almost 2 weeks.
RCA Description: Initial bug was undetected in the product code. Further, there is no control in place to detect number of expired offers
Issue Rating Justification: Though there was no regulatory impact, due to the number of impacted customers and overall negative customer experience, is the reason for a medium rating.
Issue Progress Updates:
1). The incident began on 5/1/25, but was not detected until 5/13/25.
 2). The previously undetected bug manifested itself after an experiment was run to allow a sample of users to access offers for 30-days (standard access remains 7-days).
 3). Users who attempted to access expired offers received a message, “can’t offer financing,” and thus, were not able to initiate the flow to originate a loan.
 4). A temporary fix was put in place on 5/13/25 and a permanent fix was put in place by 5/16/25.
 5). A control to be uplifted to Retro product to monitor expired transactions.
Closure Request Justification: Both controls to detect unexpected spikes in users seeing the "can't offer financing" screen; and, 2) detect for volume of expired transactions increasing were uplifted and activated on 5/22/25. Testing has not been completed as of this entry, but both have not detected any issues.

Issue Title: Customer information is missing or incomplete for automatic onboarded customers for the SEL entity which is in non compliance wit the SEL KYC procedure.
Issue Description:
GCMT performed a transactional data testing of 13,825 UK customers automatically onboarded during 1st - 14th October-December (first 2 weeks of each month) in Q4 2024 (i.e., 11,582 Sole Traders, 1893 Companies, and 207 Partnerships, 1 Government Body, 1 Unincorporated Association, 1 incorporated Association and 140 Unknown) and identified the following issues:
 

 ● For Companies (including Limited), it was noted that SEL failed to identify and verify the Ultimate Beneficial Owner (UBO) information below: 
 - 1 Merchants without a Day documented for Date of Birth Data field.

 The following was noted for Directors:
 - 26 Merchants without a Date of Birth documented 
 - 1 Merchant account where there are two directors without an Address documented
 

 GCMT noted 156 Companies (including Limited) without a Beneficial Owner or Two Directors in lieu of a Beneficial Owner, which is in non compliance with the requirements in the SEL KYC Procedures. 
 

 ● For Partnerships it was noted that SEL failed to verify and identify the following:
 - 64 Merchants without two Directors or Partners assigned to the Account in lieu of a Beneficial Owner. 
 - 38 Merchant Accounts with no Business role or ownership.
 - 80 Accounts without a business (charity) registration number.
 - 1 Merchant with no Business Name identified 
 

 ● 46 Merchant Accounts with no Verification type assigned. GCMT were unable to identify if this verification was Automatic or Manual. The Accounts were from the following business structures:
 - Companies 
 - Sole Trader 
 - Partnership 
 - Unknown

Issue Title: REF 1 - Results Matrix - SEL Customer Communications and Marketing Q1 2025 - Theme One - Freezes placed on Seller accounts (i.e., Risk: Credit Risk and Fraud, Compliance Operations, Sanctions, Transaction Monitoring)
Issue Description:
General Observation:
 The Seller Journey for freeze and deactivations requires improvement to ensure fair and transparent communication. GCMT have noted Themes documented in the "Seller Journey - Freeze and Deactivations Memo" which are contributing to an increased level of Seller dissatisfaction and friction. 
 

 Theme One
 By design, Freezes can be placed on Seller accounts by multiple teams for a variety of reasons resulting in instances where a Seller can be frozen-unfrozen multiple times in a short period of time.
 

 Freezes on Seller accounts per testing, are predominantly placed on accounts by the following teams 
 1- Risk: Credit Risk and Fraud 
 2- Compliance Operations 
 3- Sanctions 
 4- Transaction Monitoring 
 

 GCMT also observed at times automatic freezes which are placed by the system and require IDV to be provided by the Seller via SSP. GCMT observed that in practice, it is not uncommon for a Seller to undergo multiple Freeze-Unfreeze actions in a short period of time by different teams outlined above. However, in some instances GCMT identified where this has occurred within the same team e.g. Credit Risk and Fraud Risk.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: SEL Customer Communications and Marketing Q1 2025 (1043100)
Issue Rating Justification: Presents a moderate risk to the operations of Block. Overall exposure requires a corrective action plan with priority.
Issue Action Plans: REF 1 - Theme One - Compliance Operations and Transaction Monitoring SSPs (1303704)
Closure Request Justification:
Action plan reviewed and validated. Documented evidence available below.
 https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1i4cutVmQpLnApMPl7J7v-a1tTGxPyCrM

Issue Title: REF 2 - SEL Customer Communications and Marketing Q1 2025 - Themes 2 to 4 - Seller journey and SEL communications/responses uplift
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement:
 Provision 2A.5.3(2) of the ""Consumer Duty: retail customer outcome on consumer understanding"", states that a firm must communicate information to retail customers in a way which is clear, fair and not misleading.
 

 General Observation:
 The Seller Journey for freeze and deactivations requires improvement to ensure fair and transparent communication. GCMT have noted Themes documented in the ""Seller Journey - Freeze and Deactivations Memo"" which are contributing to an increased level of Seller dissatisfaction and friction. 
 

 Theme Two 
 Communications and information provided to the Seller about why a review is being conducted or the reason for deactivation is limited. 
 

 - Due to the limited detail provided in Square communications at the start of the review and at review completion, Sellers do not understand when/why multiple reviews are triggered in a short period of time. From the Seller’s perspective an assumption is made that an “account review” is completed in its entirety and not in part. 
 - Upon a Seller receiving a deactivation email, GCMT observed that when a Seller contacts Customer Service for more information about why their account has been deactivated, the approved CSA response is to advise the Seller to refer to the deactivation email as no further information can be provided or is visible to the CSA. This creates further friction for the seller as the email does not provide sufficient rationale to the seller as to ‘WHY” they have been deactivated i.e it is a doom loop.
 - GCMT noted instances where automatic communications advise the Seller to contact the Support Centre if they believe that Square has made a mistake with the deactivation decision. This is not aligned with the current processes and practices for agents in the Support Centre. 
 

 Theme Three 
 “Guarantees” and/or inaccurate information provided by Square Customer Success Agents causing increased Seller aggravation. GCMT noted through sample-testing of calls and emails made by Sellers to Square Customer Success the following behaviours and errors made by Square Customer Service Agents which are causing increased Seller dissatisfaction and an increase in the amount of contact made by a Seller to Square during the review/freeze period.  
 

 1. Cases where CSAs provide “guarantees” to Sellers when the Seller has called CS and is unhappy with their funds being held and requesting for the review to be expedited.
 - In some instances, the CSA will provide a timeframe to the Seller for when the review will be completed with a promise of internal escalation. In majority of these cases, especially where the timeframe promised is unrealistic such as 2-3 hours, this timeframe is not met which results in higher Seller dissatisfaction. 
 - In some instances, the CSA in addition to committing to a time-frame as noted in A above will also verbally confirm that the Sellers funds will be released. This should not be communicated as following the initial review undertaken by “Account Services” there are two other scenarios which may occur; 
  a) More information is required for the review which would result in the funds being held until this is provided and a satisfactory review result is obtained 
  b) The account is deactivated following review which would result in the funds being held for 90 days (standard). 
 2. Where CSAs are providing inaccurate or incomplete information to the Seller about a Freeze/Deactivation. From sample testing the common root cause of this, appears to be incomplete review of the account prior to responding to the Seller. 
 

 3. Cases where CSAs commit to provide the Seller an update via callback or email after a promise to escalate the case for a quicker review or outcome, however this action does not occur resulting in the Seller calling back Square after the committed time. 
 

 Theme Four 
 Automatic Communication/Notification to Sellers are not being sent for all Freeze types. GCMT observed that there is a cohort of freezes which are placed on Seller accounts which do not trigger or provide any automatic communication to the Seller to advise them that their account has been frozen, and their funds have been held/transfers stopped. Additionally, GCMT noted potential GAPS where additional communication should be considered. 
 

 Freeze Actions where no communication is being sent 
 24-48 hour freeze - Account Flagged for review (risk)
 Currently, in cases where an account is flagged for review by risk there are no communications sent out to the Seller. This is to avoid Seller frustration and the large percentage of Sellers which would be flagged on a routine basis. Whilst the majority of Sellers will not be aware that there is a hold on their account if the account is reviewed and cleared within the SLA, this may impact Sellers who have signed up for instant transfer who would be more aware of a hold on their account. 
 Potential ATO (Potential Enhancement Opportunity) 
 Currently, in cases where the Seller is flagged and reviewed for a potential ATO there are no communications sent out to the Seller. 
 GCMT noted one sample in the review where the agent clicked the “unfreeze and close action” following confirmation of a false positive; however due to a system issue the freeze was not cleared by the system and remained in a frozen state until the Seller contacted Square Customer Success. 
 

 Communication GAPS in Seller Journey 
 Automatic Identity Verification Requests (Veriff)
 For Automatic IDV requests which are triggered by the system there are no reminders sent between the date of the initial request and the date the account is deactivated due to non -response.
 Incomplete SSP
 GCMT noted in testing it is common for a Seller to only partially complete an SSP request i.e. 10 out of 15 questions. In these instances, the Seller will not receive a “information received email” as a review alert is not triggered for the requesting team until all questions have been responded to in full. There is no communication in place to remind or alert the Seller that the SSP is incomplete and that the review will not be commenced until each question has been answered. 
 Sellers deactivated by Sanctions (Potential Enhancement Opportunity) 
 All deactivation emails currently include wording to advise the Seller that where their funds are being held by Square for a period of time (usually 9 days) that the Seller is able to process refunds for their Customers to allow earlier access to funds being held. Consideration should be given to if this wording should be removed for Sanction deactivations and/or other high risk AML cases.
 Release of funds - Sellers with no or negative balance at date of communicated release (Potential Enhancement Opportunity) 
 Following deactivation of a Seller, in most cases the funds will be held by Square for a period of 90 days to safeguard against chargebacks/disputes which may be raised by the Seller’s customers. Currently, where the Seller has received more chargebacks then their balance at the date of deactivation, no communication is sent on the communicated date of release to advise them that no funds would be released. Whilst not required, consideration should be given to if a communication should be sent to confirm this including a summary of the balance minus chargebacks. 
 

 Automatic Communications which are being triggered which should not be sent/no longer relevant 
 GCMT noted in testing three samples where an auto-communication has been sent to a Seller, following an action already been completed or the Seller deactivated.
 A Seller received a request to complete IDV by Postbox after the Seller had already been successfully verified and received communication to confirm this.
 Per confirmation with Risk Management, this is due to the system having two separate flows, with no current requirement and/or functionality for Risk to cancel a pending veriff (IDV) request, in the event that the risk SSP has also requested IDV. 
 The Seller was already deactivated however received a request for more information the next day. 
 Per confirmation with Risk Management, the deactivation was processed by the Transaction Monitoring Team, with the request for more information sent by the Risk Team for a separate alert/review. Currently Risk cannot stop auto generated emails from being sent to a Seller which has been deactivated by another team. Auto-generated emails once processed in the system by a “triggering action” will be sent regardless of whatever action is taken by another team. 
 The Seller was already deactivated however received a request to complete Identify Verification, 3 days post the deactivation email being sent to the Seller. This email was triggered by “Postbox” Additionally, prior to the deactivation the Seller had already received a request for IDV which was successfully completed and reviewed by Square. 
 Investigation still pending about why this email was triggered.
 

 Compliance Operations Program Management - 9 exceptions noted 
 1. GCMT noted four exceptions where the Seller did not recieve an automatic email notifying them that the review has been completed and that their funds had been unfrozen. Addiitonally, GCMT noted one exceptions where the Seller did not recieve an automatic email notifying them that their account had been deactivated. Per investigation by Compliance Operations Program Management, this was due to the agent unfreezing the Seller outside of case view, which will not trigger any communications as required 
 2. GCMT noted one exception where no automatic communications relating to the freeze was sent to the Seller. Per investigation by Compliance Operations Program Management, this was due to the agent utilizing the "Sanctions SSP request" which should not have been utilized, as the SSP request was never correctly built out, resulting in a case for the freeze failing to be created. 
 3. GCMT noted two samples where an automatic communication was displayed in Regulator as being sent to the Seller, however the email was not visible on the Salesforce link. 
 4. GCMT noted one exception out of the 70 samples tested, where a Seller was sent both a "you're back up and running email" (which is the standard email sent after a review and freeze has been lifted) and another email to note that a review had been completed. The content provided in the "review completed" email contains more detailed information in comparison to the standard email.
 5. GCMT noted one exception where no deactivation email was sent to the Seller. Per Compliance Operations Management investigaiton this was due to the linked deactivation action "Compliance"BadactorsHit"Deactivate" not having an email tied to it.
 

 GCMT noted one exception where there were no automatic communications sent to the Seller. Additionally, for the Seller there was no audit trial on Regulator to confirm why the Seller was frozen.

Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement:
 Provision 8.14 of the FG22/5 Final non-Handbook Guidance for firms on the Consumer Duty states that there are a range of legislative and regulatory disclosure requirements that apply to providers of retail financial products and services. These were introduced to ensure that consumers are provided with certain information to help them make effective decisions at key points in the customer journey. Firms should therefore continue to comply with these requirements (see Annex 3).
 

 Further, provision 2A.5.5 of the "Consumer Duty: retail customer outcome on consumer understanding", states that, with regard to ■ PRIN 2A.5.3R(1)(c), for a firm to provide information on a timely basis, it must communicate in good time for retail customers to make effective decisions, including:

Issue Title: REF 5 - SEL Customer Communications and Marketing Q1 2025 - Record Retention
Issue Description:
Policy Requirement:
 The Record Retention section of the SEL Consumer Protection Policy states that SEL must ensure compliance with record retention requirements. SEL’s policy is to retain all necessary customer records for at least six years from the date SEL ceases to provide any product or service to that customer. In the event that any record is to be retained for a timescale greater than the minimum requirement (i.e., in the event of a regulatory request), such request must be documented in accordance with relevant recordkeeping requirements.
 

 Types of records required to be kept include: 
 

 • Copies of all original documents submitted by the consumer in support of an application for the provision of a service or product;
 • Copy of all documents required for consumer identification;
 • Details of products and services provided to the consumer;
 • All correspondence with the consumer;
 • All documents completed or signed by the consumer; and
 • All other relevant information and documentation concerning the consumer.

Issue Title: REF 1 - SIL Customer Journey Q1 2025 - Theme One - Freezes placed on Seller accounts (i.e., Risk: Credit Risk and Fraud, Compliance Operations, Sanctions, Transaction Monitoring)
Issue Description:
General Observation:
 The Seller Journey for freeze and deactivations requires improvement to ensure fair and transparent communication. GCMT have noted Themes documented in the "Seller Journey - Freeze and Deactivations Memo" which are contributing to an increased level of Seller dissatisfaction and friction. 
 

 Theme One
 By design, Freezes can be placed on Seller accounts by multiple teams for a variety of reasons resulting in instances where a Seller can be frozen-unfrozen multiple times in a short period of time.
 

 Freezes on Seller accounts per testing, are predominantly placed on accounts by the following teams 
 1- Risk: Credit Risk and Fraud 
 2- Compliance Operations 
 3- Sanctions 
 4- Transaction Monitoring 
 

 GCMT also observed at times automatic freezes which are placed by the system and require IDV to be provided by the Seller via SSP. GCMT observed that in practice, it is not uncommon for a Seller to undergo multiple Freeze-Unfreeze actions in a short period of time by different teams outlined above. However, in some instances GCMT identified where this has occurred within the same team e.g. Credit Risk and Fraud Risk.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: REF 2 - SIL Customer Journey Q1 2025 - Themes 2 to 4 - Seller journey and SEL communications/responses uplift
Issue Description:
General Observation:
 The Seller Journey for freeze and deactivations requires improvement to ensure fair and transparent communication. GCMT have noted themes documented in the "Seller Journey - Freeze and Deactivations Memo" which are contributing to an increased level of Seller dissatisfaction and friction.
 

 Theme Two 
 Communications and information provided to the Seller about why a review is being conducted or the reason for deactivation is limited. 
 

 - Due to the limited detail provided in Square communications at the start of the review and at review completion, Sellers do not understand when/why multiple reviews are triggered in a short period of time. From the Seller’s perspective an assumption is made that an “account review” is completed in its entirety and not in part. 
 - Upon a Seller receiving a deactivation email, GCMT observed that when a Seller contacts Customer Service for more information about why their account has been deactivated, the approved CSA response is to advise the Seller to refer to the deactivation email as no further information can be provided or is visible to the CSA. This creates further friction for the seller as the email does not provide sufficient rationale to the seller as to ‘WHY” they have been deactivated i.e it is a doom loop.
 - GCMT noted instances where automatic communications advise the Seller to contact the Support Centre if they believe that Square has made a mistake with the deactivation decision. This is not aligned with the current processes and practices for agents in the Support Centre. 
 

 Theme Three 
 “Guarantees” and/or inaccurate information provided by Square Customer Success Agents causing increased Seller aggravation. GCMT noted through sample-testing of calls and emails made by Sellers to Square Customer Success the following behaviours and errors made by Square Customer Service Agents which are causing increased Seller dissatisfaction and an increase in the amount of contact made by a Seller to Square during the review/freeze period.  
 

 1. Cases where CSAs provide “guarantees” to Sellers when the Seller has called CS and is unhappy with their funds being held and requesting for the review to be expedited.
 - In some instances, the CSA will provide a timeframe to the Seller for when the review will be completed with a promise of internal escalation. In majority of these cases, especially where the timeframe promised is unrealistic such as 2-3 hours, this timeframe is not met which results in higher Seller dissatisfaction. 
 - In some instances, the CSA in addition to committing to a time-frame as noted in A above will also verbally confirm that the Sellers funds will be released. This should not be communicated as following the initial review undertaken by “Account Services” there are two other scenarios which may occur; 
  a) More information is required for the review which would result in the funds being held until this is provided and a satisfactory review result is obtained 
  b) The account is deactivated following review which would result in the funds being held for 90 days (standard). 
 2. Where CSAs are providing inaccurate or incomplete information to the Seller about a Freeze/Deactivation. From sample testing the common root cause of this, appears to be incomplete review of the account prior to responding to the Seller. 
 

 3. Cases where CSAs commit to provide the Seller an update via callback or email after a promise to escalate the case for a quicker review or outcome, however this action does not occur resulting in the Seller calling back Square after the committed time. 
 

 Theme Four 
 Automatic Communication/Notification to Sellers are not being sent for all Freeze types. GCMT observed that there is a cohort of freezes which are placed on Seller accounts which do not trigger or provide any automatic communication to the Seller to advise them that their account has been frozen, and their funds have been held/transfers stopped. Additionally, GCMT noted potential GAPS where additional communication should be considered. 
 

 Freeze Actions where no communication is being sent 
 24-48 hour freeze - Account Flagged for review (risk)
 Currently, in cases where an account is flagged for review by risk there are no communications sent out to the Seller. This is to avoid Seller frustration and the large percentage of Sellers which would be flagged on a routine basis. Whilst the majority of Sellers will not be aware that there is a hold on their account if the account is reviewed and cleared within the SLA, this may impact Sellers who have signed up for instant transfer who would be more aware of a hold on their account. 
 Potential ATO (Potential Enhancement Opportunity) 
 Currently, in cases where the Seller is flagged and reviewed for a potential ATO there are no communications sent out to the Seller. 
 GCMT noted one sample in the review where the agent clicked the “unfreeze and close action” following confirmation of a false positive; however due to a system issue the freeze was not cleared by the system and remained in a frozen state until the Seller contacted Square Customer Success. 
 

 Communication GAPS in Seller Journey 
 Automatic Identity Verification Requests (Veriff)
 For Automatic IDV requests which are triggered by the system there are no reminders sent between the date of the initial request and the date the account is deactivated due to non -response.
 Incomplete SSP
 GCMT noted in testing it is common for a Seller to only partially complete an SSP request i.e. 10 out of 15 questions. In these instances, the Seller will not receive a “information received email” as a review alert is not triggered for the requesting team until all questions have been responded to in full. There is no communication in place to remind or alert the Seller that the SSP is incomplete and that the review will not be commenced until each question has been answered. 
 Sellers deactivated by Sanctions (Potential Enhancement Opportunity) 
 All deactivation emails currently include wording to advise the Seller that where their funds are being held by Square for a period of time (usually 9 days) that the Seller is able to process refunds for their Customers to allow earlier access to funds being held. Consideration should be given to if this wording should be removed for Sanction deactivations and/or other high risk AML cases.
 Release of funds - Sellers with no or negative balance at date of communicated release (Potential Enhancement Opportunity) 
 Following deactivation of a Seller, in most cases the funds will be held by Square for a period of 90 days to safeguard against chargebacks/disputes which may be raised by the Seller’s customers. Currently, where the Seller has received more chargebacks then their balance at the date of deactivation, no communication is sent on the communicated date of release to advise them that no funds would be released. Whilst not required, consideration should be given to if a communication should be sent to confirm this including a summary of the balance minus chargebacks. 
 

 Automatic Communications which are being triggered which should not be sent/no longer relevant 
 GCMT noted in testing three samples where an auto-communication has been sent to a Seller, following an action already been completed or the Seller deactivated.
 A Seller received a request to complete IDV by Postbox after the Seller had already been successfully verified and received communication to confirm this.
 Per confirmation with Risk Management, this is due to the system having two separate flows, with no current requirement and/or functionality for Risk to cancel a pending veriff (IDV) request, in the event that the risk SSP has also requested IDV. 
 The Seller was already deactivated however received a request for more information the next day. 
 Per confirmation with Risk Management, the deactivation was processed by the Transaction Monitoring Team, with the request for more information sent by the Risk Team for a separate alert/review. 
 Currently Risk cannot stop auto generated emails from being sent to a Seller which has been deactivated by another team. Auto-generated emails once processed in the system by a “triggering action” will be sent regardless of whatever action is taken by another team. 
 The Seller was already deactivated however received a request to complete Identify Verification, 3 days post the deactivation email being sent to the Seller. This email was triggered by “Postbox” Additionally, prior to the deactivation the Seller had already received a request for IDV which was successfully completed and reviewed by Square. 
 Investigation still pending about why this email was triggered.

Issue Title: Inadequate Product Launch Governance and Approvals
Issue Description: To ensure appropriate reviews and approvals are conducted and captured prior to the deployment of certain development efforts (i.e., net new products/features), Cash App's Compliance team (in conjunction with Product Counsel) operate the Counsel Requirements Document (CRD). The CRD is intended for the respective teams (Compliance and Legal) to identify requirements and controls that are required to be addressed either before or after launch. There have been instances where requisite approvals are not documented in the CRD prior to product release.
RCA Description: Lack of controls to ensure certain products and features that have a CRD get required approvals prior to launch.
Issue Rating Justification: In general, all approvers are engaged and aligned with the proposed features, and compensating controls are deployed prior to release. This issue highlights the lack of formality around approvals and completing documentation prior to launch.

Issue Title: Incomplete Management Action Plan from the Assessment of Donation Specific Transaction Monitoring Rules
Issue Description:
FINTRAC requires
 

 2 the reporting entities to assess the
 risks posed by the combination of the products, services
 and delivery channels. Square Canada conducted an
 assessment in 2024 to determine whether transaction
 monitoring rules specific to fundraising are required;
 however, the action items resulting from the assessment
 have not been fully implemented. As a result, Square
 Canada faces the risk of failing to implement transaction
 monitoring rules commensurate with its risk profile.
 *This is a repeat observation from the 2023 Square
 Canada audit given the management action plan was
 not fully completed at the time of this audit.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Requirement/Expectation
Issue Progress Updates: See also: Action ID 1112320
Issue Action Plans: Development of Transaction Monitoring ML for Flow of Funds (1305681)

Issue Title: Risk Assessment Methodology Excludes Assessment of Domestic High Risk Areas
Issue Description:
FINTRAC requires
 

 4 that reporting entities identify and
 assess risks associated with the geographic (domestic
 and international) location of their business and
 operations, including geographic location of clients, and
 expects the reporting entities to document and include
 all prescribed elements, including geography, in the risk
 assessment. The Square Canada Risk Assessment
 Methodology covers only international high risk areas
 and does not include an assessment of domestic high
 risk areas.
 As a result, if domestic geographic considerations are
 excluded in the the Square Canada Risk Assessment, the
 inherent and residual risk scores may not accurately
 reflect Square Canada's money laundering risk.
 Additionally, FINTRAC’s published guidance indicates
 that the absence of elements within a risk assessment,
 such as an assessment of domestic activities, represents
 partial non-compliance with regulatory requirements.
 Lacking an assessment of domestic activities is
 commonly cited in FINTRAC examination letters, which
 can further lead to regulatory fines and penalties.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Requirements/Expectations
Issue Action Plans: Update Risk Assessment Methodology to Include Domestic Geographical Risk (1305684)
Closure Request Justification: Closing issue as IA has validated all action plans (see closure of linked action plan by Kaitlin Pipitone).

Issue Title: Lack of Adherence to FINTRAC Guidance for Suspicious Transaction Report (STR) Forms Completed
Issue Description:
FINTRAC requires that reporting entities make
 reasonable efforts to obtain information for fields in the
 STR form, which require reasonable measures, provided
 that they are applicable. IA tested a sample of 25 STR
 forms and noted that the following fields in the form
 were not completed even though the information was
 available:
 ● Time of Transaction (25 of 25 STR forms tested)
 ● Indicating in the narrative the relationship
 between a merchant and an individual for whom
 a transaction was conducted on their behalf (1 of
 25 STR forms tested)
 As a result Square Canada may fail to provide useful
 information to regulatory authorities and law
 enforcement. Further, FINTRAC’s published guidance
 indicates STRs with data quality issues impact its ability
 to assess and analyze reports to produce financial
 intelligence, which may lead to regulatory criticism, fines
 and penalties.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Requirements/Expectations
Issue Action Plans: Assessment of Transactional Data for Timestamp Data (1305687)
Closure Request Justification: 12/5KP: IA validated on 12/4. Closing related action; issue ready for closure.

Issue Title: Lack of SLA to Close Transaction Monitoring (TM) and Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) Cases Pending Merchant Responses
Issue Description:
FINTRAC requires
 

 8 that reporting entities submit STRs
 “as soon as practicable”. There is no defined SLA for
 closing TM and EDD cases that are awaiting merchant
 responses via Square Secure Portal (SSP). IA tested a
 sample of 25 EDD cases and 25 TM alert cases. IA noted
 that 13 of the EDD cases and 7 of the TM alert cases
 remain in the pending status, with the longest for 648
 days, with no determination of whether suspicious
 activity occurred or if a STR is required.
 As a result, this increases the risk of failing to timely
 assess suspicious activities and file STRs if required.
 Further, the risk of Square Canada engaging with bad
 actors increases, which may lead to regulatory criticism,
 fines and penalties.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory Requirements/Expectations
Issue Action Plans: Implementation of Additional EDD and TM Alert Controls (1305690)

Issue Title: Multi-state/DFS: Tier 1 (Unverified) Accounts Occasionally Exceed $1K/Day Prepaid Access Threshold
Issue Description:
We reviewed unverified Cash App customer activity in March 2025 to evaluate our risk of non-compliance under FinCEN's Prepaid Access Rule. This rule mandates stricter Bank Secrecy Act requirements for open loop prepaid programs that permit daily access or use of open loop prepaid access products exceeding $1,000 without verifying the user's identity. For unverified Cash App accounts, this would include access to Stored Value Balance and use of said balance to Cash Out to linked financial instruments.
 

 Findings:
 - 99.992% of Tier 1 customers stayed within the $1,000/day cash-out limit.
 - 57 of 740,452 (0.008%) unverified customers exceeded this threshold during the 30 day window ending on March 31, 2025.
 

 Root Causes:
 These exceptions occur when accounts transition into Tier 1 from Verified or Sponsored status (where balances are not limited), through manual actions (e.g., support transfers, or reimbursements), or due to edge cases where the timing of transactions allowed for a user to cash out more than $1,000 over 30 days. See the Root Cause Analysis section for additional detail.
 

 Regulatory Risk:
 While the exception rate is very low, any access by unverified users to >$1,000/day may trigger classification of the product as a covered prepaid program under the Prepaid Access Rule, which would trigger obligations for customer identity collection and verification under the Bank Secrecy Act. Non-compliance in this area could lead to severe financial penalties, reputational damage, and ongoing regulatory oversight.
RCA Description:
The 57 identified exceptions occurred due to the following reasons.
 - Previously Sponsored accounts (15)
 - IDV Revoked (Borrow ReIDV) accounts (14)
 - IDV Revoked (Other) accounts (2)
 - Edge cases with multiple cash outs and P2P receives in between (12)
 - CashApp Support Balance Transfers (3)
 - Data timestamp discrepancies (Not a true exception - incorrect data labeling during analysis) (3)
 - Savings accruals exceeding $1K before cash out (1)
 - P2P receive overage followed by a cash out (1)
 

 Additionally, a separate analysis at the $2k Cash Out threshold identified a single exception caused by conflicting data about IDV status between Franklin and cash-idv.
Interested Parties:
Laura Lennon
 Valerie Chadha
 Stephen Ritter
Issue Rating Justification: The High rating is based on the Issue Management Policy rating matrix. This issue stems from a regulatory requirement that is explicitly stated in law (FinCEN Prepaid Access Rule). It is not a regulatory expectation that is open to interpretation.
Closure Request Justification:
Closing the issue as approved in the issue closure memo

Issue Title: Collection House IT Outage 12/05/2025
Issue Description:
The CLH Team Manager has reported system performance issues affecting Reporting, C5, Telephony, and other CLH applications.
 Impacted users have experienced delays, slower response times, and intermittent disruptions when accessing these systems.
 Key impacts include:
 • Reporting delays affecting campaign loading.
 • Citrix and C5 slowness impacting business processes and overall productivity.
 

 

 AP Question - If your phone system was completely down (which was advised to us initially)?
 CH response - The CLH collections suite and telephony systems were impacted by the incident on 12/5. While there was minimal telephony functionality and service available through manual outbound calling in some sectors of the CLH collection services business, the inbound and automated dialler systems predominantly utilised on the significant volumes of the Afterpay portfolio meant that the standard automated telephony treatments were unable to run as planned.
 

 AP question - That CH had put inbound phone lines into voicemail as part of the BCP?
 CH response - BCP measures were deployed at 8.55am 12/5, phone lines were closed and voicemail on all inbound telephony was activated.
 AP question - How many Afterpay customers called in and went to voicemail?
 CH response - 40 voice mail messages were left by Afterpay customers.
 

 AP question - Whether CH returned all call backs and messages of inbound contacts?
 CH response - All Afterpay customers who left a voice mail message on 12/5 were contacted before 12.30pm on 13/5
 

 AP question - The specific time indicating when the phone system was fully operational again?
 CH response - As per Incident report, all IT server migrations work were completed at 7.30pm 12/5. CLH systems telephony and collections suite were functional and operational, as per standard operating business hours on 13/5.
 

 Business Impact - All CLH services were impacted, preventing all CRM and CRA staff from performing their normal work duties.
RCA Description:
During the planned maintenance window, the migration process took significantly longer than expected, extending into Monday business hours.
 The increased load from treatments, reporting, and users accessing the system simultaneously led to a noticeable drop in performance, affecting all users.
 Despite extensive efforts to halt the migration, there was no safe way to pause or stop the process without risking further complications.
Issue Rating Justification: Third party vendor had IT outage for one day (12/05/2025) and implemented BCP immediately. No material impact to Afterpay customers.

Issue Title: 2. Clearpay Debt Management Companies SOP can be uplifted to align with Afterpay Collections - LOA procedures on verification processes and processing of accounts post LOA removal
Issue Description:
The current Clearpay Debt Management Companies (DMC) Standard Operating Procedure requires alignment with Afterpay Collections Letter of Authority (LOA) procedures in two key areas:
 1) Verification Process Standards 
 - The DMC SOP only requires the Collections agent to verify customer's full name and address, where the Collections LOA SOP requires Collections to verify the customer's full name, address and Date of Birth.
 - For Financial Counsellors / DMC, the LOA SOP has an additional requirement whereby if the account cannot be verified with the information provided, there is additional process to obtain further information, whereas the DMC SOP does not have this process. 
 

 GCMT tested 10 samples of DMCs providing LOAs and noted 2 samples where the agent provided the balance to the authorised financial counsellor when the last names on the LOA did not match the account name. 
 We noted that in both instances, the date of birth, address, email address or Clearpay account number related to the account was correct. 
 

 2) Post-LOA removal account processing 
 - The DMC SOP is not clear on the verification requirements when processing Letter of Authority (LOA) removal. While other regional procedures practice the verification protocols, the DMC SOP does not specify whether similar verification processes should be implemented. 
 - DMC SOP does not include the Collections LOA SOP's requirement to confirm LOA removal with financial counsellors and/or third-party representatives. 
 - DMC SOP does not include the Collections LOA SOP's requirement to notify the customer of the LOA removal 
 - The Collections LOA SOP requires the agent to updating the account status, freeze status and hardship tabs upon LOA removal, but the DMC SOP does not specify any of these requirements.

Issue Title: 1. Letter Of Authority (LOA) verification processes for Financial Counsellors/Financial Advocates are inconsistent and non-compliant to the Afterpay LOA Standard Operating Procedure.
Issue Description:
Letter of Authority SOP Requirements
 The process for verifying Letter Of Authority (LOA) documents from Financial Counsellors or Financial Advocates requires Collection agents to validate the following information including ensuring the LOA is signed and that it is less than 12 months old:
 - Account Holder’s full name
 - Date of Birth
 - Address registered to the Afterpay account
 When discrepancies are identified, agents are instructed to send the “Collections:: LOA - IDV Failed” macro to request further verification. Agents are prohibited from altering the macro to indicate which specific identification point (i.e. full name, date of birth, or address) was mismatched.
 

 Design Testing Result 
 GCMT noted that Collection agents are are currently escalating tickets to Team Leaders for approval when primary identification details (full name, date of birth, and address) on the LOA document do not match with the Account Holder's records, but they are able to match secondary identifiers such as (Afterpay account number, email address, and/or mobile number) when provided. 
 Transactional testing demonstrated that in 12% of sampled cases, the Team Leader approved based on secondary identifier verification in:
 -3 out of 41 samples (7%) of LOA received tickets 
 -4 out of 23 samples (17%) of LOA removal tickets 
 -3 out of 20 samples (15%) of graded Collection tickets (QA) tickets. 
 This escalation process whereby the agents are seeking the Team Leader's approval for mismatched primary identification is currently not documented within the LOA SOP requirements.
 

 Transactional Testing Results on SOP Adherence to Verification Processes
 GCMT tested a total sample of 64 tickets across ANZ and US, relating to LOA received and LOA removals from financial counsellors / financial advocates. GCMT noted the following observations: 
 ANZ LOA Received 
 - 3 out of 40 samples (6%) where the Collections agent failed to verify the key details of the Account Holder against the details provided on the LOA before actioning the account. 
  > Primary identification details such as the full names or address did not match which is non-compliant to the SOP.
  > In 2 of the 3 instances, the agent requested for secondary identifiers such as the account holder’s mobile and email address utilising a different macro, “Collections:: LOA Received - Unable to Locate Account” instead of the “Collections:: LOA - IDV Failed” macro as per the SOP requirement. 
  > In all instances, there was no Team Leader approval for deviating from the process.
 - 1 out of 40 samples (2%) where the “Collections:: LOA - IDV Failed” macro was altered to highlight which ID was different from the initial receipt of information. 
 

 ANZ LOA Removals
 22 samples of LOA removal requests tested fall into two distinct categories:
 1. Indirect removal requests through new LOA submissions, where incoming financial counsellors/advocates, upon submitting new LOAs, determine ongoing representation is unnecessary (such as in cases of zero account balances) and subsequently request removal (7 samples)
 GCMT did not note any exceptions in relation to Account Holder verification in this category. 
 

 2. Direct removal requests from existing authorized financial counsellors/advocates (15 samples)
 - 9 out of 15 samples (60%) where the Collections agent failed to verify the key details of the Account Holder against the details provided on the LOA before actioning the account. 
  > Not all key details (i.e. name, date of birth and address) as required by the SOP was provided. The authorised third party provided either the full name, Afterpay account number, mobile or email address. 
  > The agent did not use the “Collections:: LOA - IDV Failed” macro to request further information for verification.
  > In all instances, there was no approval from the Team Leader on the variation.
 Although these verification exceptions primarily occur in lower-risk scenarios involving direct removal requests from existing authorized representatives, the current Collections LOA SOP lacks specific guidance on verification requirements for LOA removals, resulting in procedural inconsistencies. As noted above, GCMT identified 3 instances (in ANZ) of Direct removal requests from existing authorized representatives that had Team Leader approval on the deviation of the process. 
 

 ANZ QA on Collections 
 GCMT tested 20 samples of the Collections LOA tickets and noted only 1 instance where the grader did not pick up on the agent altering the “Collections:: LOA - IDV Failed” macro to highlight which ID was different from the initial receipt of information.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2025 Q1 Global Afterpay Letter of Authorities and Third-Party Requests (1036515)
Impacted Legal Entities:

 Afterpay NZ Limited

Escalations or emails are no longer being sent to Team Leads for further review and approval of the IDV.

Issue Title: Cashbot Large Language Model (LLM) is Misrouting Cash App Pay Disputes Due to Transaction Type Recognition Failure
Issue Description:
Cashbot's Large Language Model (LLM) cannot recognize Cash App Pay transaction types during dispute-related customer inquiries. The system incorrectly defaults to Cash Card/P2P dispute content, routing customers to an incompatible self-service flow that cannot process Cash App Pay disputes. This results in delayed or prevented dispute filings for Cash App Pay customers.
 

 The issue was discovered during a PGM Support and Disputes impact assessment review on May 16, 2025 (Incident 5967) when a spike in unresolved Cash App Pay disputes was identified. The correct behavior should be transaction-type detection and routing to transaction-specific dispute resolution processes.
Issue Rating Justification: Due to direct customer impact and required remediation
Issue Action Plans: Update LLM and Remediate Customers (1309651)

Issue Title: Cashbot Risk Framework and Quality Assurance Gaps
Issue Description: The Cashbot risk framework currently operates without integrated compliance guidelines in its design, while the established weekly QA review process failed to detect transaction routing errors. This gap was identified during Incident 5967, when PGM Support and Disputes teams observed an increase in unresolved Cash App Pay disputes during their May 16, 2025 impact assessment review. The absence of proper compliance controls creates potential risk for inaccurate or non-compliant dispute resolutions. Following a cross-functional root cause analysis meeting, several corrective actions have been identified: routing Cash App Pay disputes to advocates (now completed), revising the Cashbot Risk Framework with built-in compliance guidelines, enhancing the weekly QA process, and implementing new compliance monitoring capabilities for Cashbot performance.
RCA Description:
Scope of self-service: The in-app dispute flow is only designed for P2P and Cash Card transactions; no automation exists for other transaction types.
 LLM limitation: Cashbot’s support-article LLM cannot reliably distinguish the transaction type when a dispute begins. Lacking that signal, it defaults to the content it does know—Card or P2P dispute articles.
 The QA Script designed didn’t detect this error. 
 The Cashbot Risk Framework didn’t include compliance guardrails. 
 Customer impact: For any other transaction type, the bot serves Card/P2P guidance that simply loops the customer back to the same self-service path, leaving the issue unresolved.
 Current remediation: The AIDA team has disabled LLM responses for all non-Card/P2P transactions. The bot now checks for specific Card or P2P tokens; if they aren’t present (or if a new token type is introduced), the customer is automatically routed to a live support agent.
Issue Rating Justification: Potential for inaccurate and/or non-compliant resolution
Issue Action Plans: Cashbot Risk Framework and QA Uplifts (1309657)
Closure Request Justification:
Cashbot risk framework has been updated to incorporate disputes intake in the risk framework - see change log dated as of 5/22 here- https://docs.google.com/document/d/1jGAJmnsH877IWdI6CR_BJYEUP7ki79Pc3Dwzb8FIS1k/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.g206wgdwtnx6
 

 Following the risk framework updates the following updates were made to the QA requirements 
 1. Increased in sample size - screenshot 1
 2. inclusion of assessment of customer issue in the QA evaluation form- see screenshot 2 
 3. Overall review cashbot llm policy violation against table 1 requirements in cashbot framework - see screenshot 3
 

 All evidence is attached under the attachments section in action plan & status update tab.

Issue Title: Cashbot Customer Complaint Handling Deficiencies and UDAAP Risk
Issue Description: Cashbot currently lacks the capability to properly manage and de-escalate customer complaints, creating a frustrating experience where customers become trapped in repetitive loops without clear escalation paths. This limitation presents significant UDAAP (Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices) risk, particularly when the chatbot provides inaccurate information or promises complaint handling services it cannot deliver. The issue was identified during Incident 5967 on May 16, 2025, when PGM Support and Disputes teams observed an increase in unresolved disputes. In an optimal system, the chatbot should recognize complaint-specific language and automatically direct customers to appropriate complaint filing channels. To address these gaps, corrective actions include implementing new routing processes where Lending Complaints and Tier 1 Complaints will be directed to advocates for handling, while Tier 2 and Tier 3 complaints will be managed through the chatbot with machine learning records serving as documentation for complaint notes.
RCA Description: Block’s Global Complaint Oversight program was not included in Cashbot’s development as it expanded beyond FAQs. This led to an incomplete design that fails to account for complaint detection, escalation, and documentation—raising UDAAP concerns.
Issue Rating Justification: Direct Customer Impact and UDAAP risk
Issue Action Plans: Updates to Cashbot and Customer Complaint Handling Requirements (1310961)
Closure Request Justification:
The closure evidence is contained within this document
 https://docs.google.com/document/d/1lAbgqCsn192R-n6UoGtifUiz9neErEYUh9ekPBUNjaY/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.z97fqtqauuhh
 

 We are now able to quantify T3 handled by Cashbot, along with the Customer Issue Tag (CIT) which allows for trend analysis to be performed. Complaint Insights is now able to use this information for monthly complaint reporting. 
 We have an existing ML model that identifies complaint language in all customer messages, and created a separate model that assigns Customer Issue Tags (CITs) to all customer messages. Using this information, Cashbot LLM will now offer advocate handover if:
 *a Tier-1 complaint is detected.
 *any complaint is detected, 
 *and a lending-related CIT is tagged.
 (We always offer handover if customers appear stuck in a loop, regardless of complaint language. We always offer handover for requests to speak to a person (i.e., Tier-2 complaints).

Issue Title: Unauthorized electronic fund transfer loan payment disputes meet the regulatory definition of an error; however, these disputes do not follow the error resolution workflow.
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement:
 12 CFR 1005.11 Procedures for Resolving Errors
  (a) Definition of error 1) Types of transfers or inquiries covered. The term “error” means:
  (i) An unauthorized electronic fund transfer;
 

  (c) Time limits and extent of investigation. (1) Ten-day period. A financial institution shall investigate promptly and, except as otherwise provided in this paragraph (c), shall determine whether an error occurred within 10 business days of receiving a notice of error. The institution shall report the results to the consumer within three business days after completing its investigation. The institution shall correct the error within one business day after determining that an error occurred
 

  (d) Procedures if financial institution determines no error or different error occurred. (1) Written explanation. The institution's report of the results of its investigation shall include a written explanation of the institution's findings and shall note the consumer's right to request the documents that the institution relied on in making its determination. Upon request, the institution shall promptly provide copies of the documents.
 

 Issue Description:
 Cash Lending error resolution processes do not encompass customer disputes of loan payments (not the loan). Notice of Borrow and Retro unauthorized electronic fund transfer payments meet the EFTA definition of an error; however, these cases follow a complaint workflow rather than an EFTA error resolution workflow. When managed through complaints, the EFTA-qualifying claims may potentially not receive the error resolution investigation time limits (10 business days versus 14/30 business days depending on the complaint Tier, three business days to report the results after completing the investigation, and one business day to correct the error after determining an error occurred) and if no error occurred, notice to the consumer of their right to request the documents Cash Lending relied on when making its determination.

Issue Title: Error resolution cases did not meet timeframe requirements and written procedures require updates to reflect newly implemented workflow processes and EFTA timeframes.
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement:
 12 CFR 1005.11 Procedures for Resolving Errors
  (c)(1) Ten-day period. A financial institution shall investigate promptly and, except as otherwise provided in this paragraph (c), shall determine whether an error occurred within 10 business days of receiving a notice of error. The institution shall report the results to the consumer within three business days after completing its investigation. The institution shall correct the error within one business day after determining that an error occurred.
  (c)(2) Forty-five day period. If the financial institution is unable to complete its investigation within 10 business days, the institution may take up to 45 days from receipt of a notice of error to investigate and determine whether an error occurred, provided the institution does the following:
  (i) Provisionally credits the consumer's account in the amount of the alleged error (including interest where applicable) within 10 business days of receiving the error notice.
 

 Issue Description:
 1.  3 of 30 (10%) Borrow error resolution cases did not meet regulatory requirements-
  a. 2 of 30 (7%) did not provide the customer with the lending investigation result. (162742757, 165106131)
  b. 1 of 30 (3%) was not resolved within the 10 business day requirement and provisional credit was not provided for the unauthorized $12.88 loan payment. The investigation was completed in 13 business days and the refund was provided at that time. (163161988)
 

 2. The Lending Operations Notion Account Takeover (ATO) Borrow and ATO Retro procedures contain outdated workflow information regarding a Final Resolve process that was retired and lack regulatory timing requirements for error investigations. Procedures should remain current and include key regulatory requirements so that staff can follow the appropriate steps to resolve ATO reported errors and meet timeframe requirements for customer notification and error resolution.

Issue Title: Borrow and Retro loan and autopay terms are inconsistent and/or unclear when describing autopay cancellation practices, including the self-service option to disable autopayments.
Issue Description:
Borrow and Retro Terms of Service Requirement
 Refer to the Results Matrix (see Engagement 1042449) for the agreement information.
 

 Issue Description: 
 When autopayments are disabled, all future automated processing stops. This includes both regular scheduled payments and recovery payments. However, 
 Terms of service agreements do not consistently describe this autopay cancellation practice. Loan Agreements and Retro Autopay Terms indicate ongoing authorization for recovery autopayments regardless of autopay status. However, 
 

 1. Borrow (FEB and SFS) Autopay Agreements state that cancelling weekly/split repayment autopay also cancels authorization for due date outstanding and post-due date payoff collection. Issue Title: Incomplete Adverse Media Screening Due to Missing Bad Actors Records on Highest Risk Account Token
Issue Description:
Background
 Cash’s KYC policy requires adverse media screening for all accounts classified as high risk by the Continuous Customer Risk Rating (cCRR) model, as well as medium- and low-risk accounts exhibiting elevated transactional activity. Screening is executed by LexisNexis and initiated via the Bad Actors service, which is populated with account records based on logic owned by the Trust team.
 Currently, when evaluating whether an account requires adverse media screening, the system identifies the SSN, selects the highest risk customer token associated with it, checks for a Bad Actors record, and—if one exists—submits it to LexisNexis for screening. However, in practice, the logic used to select the highest risk customer token for adverse media screening—prior to submission to the Bad Actors service—may select a denylisted or partially onboarded token. If that token lacks a corresponding record in Bad Actors, the system does not fall back to submit any other associated customer tokens, even if one is fully onboarded and eligible for screening. As a result, adverse media screening is not initiated for that customer via this flow.
 Issue
 As part of an ongoing third-party assessment, we reviewed data for all accounts deemed eligible by compliance for adverse media screening but were unable to locate evidence of a successful screen in LexisNexis. As a result, we identified that ~330k accounts (5% of all accounts identified as eligible for screening according to Compliance criteria) were being sent to the Bad Actors service but were filtered out because they did not have an associated account record in Bad Actors.
 

 Key contributing factors include:
 Use of denylisted or incomplete accounts as the primary screening proxy for a customer
 Misalignment between Trust and Compliance on the definition of when an account is “fully onboarded” for screening purposes
 

 Impact
 This logic gap introduces a material compliance risk by failing to conduct required adverse media screenings on eligible accounts. It exposes the company to potential regulatory findings, and may allow high risk users to operate without proper due diligence.
Engagement Title: 2025 Crowe | Block Inc KYC Remediation Validation (1047721)
Issue Rating Justification: This logic gap introduces a material compliance risk by failing to conduct required adverse media screenings on eligible accounts. It exposes the company to potential regulatory findings, and may allow high risk users to operate without proper due diligence.
Issue Action Plans:
Adverse Media Root Cause Analysis (1399474)
 Adverse Media Screening lookback (1400120)
Closure Request Justification: 12/26 AG - Reviewed the remediation actions taken to address the issue of incomplete adverse media screening. The relevant controls have been updated and remediation has been completed

Issue Title: Lack of Ongoing Sanctions Screening for Square US Debit Card Additional Cardholders
Issue Description:
While providing data in connection with a Model Validation review, the Foundational Compliance Engineering (“FCE”) identified a gap in the rescreening of Square US Debit Card additional cardholders. Specifically, FCE identified that these additional cardholders (also referred to as authorized users) are not currently subjected to ongoing sanctions screening.
 

 In July 2023, Square launched a new feature that allows Square Debit Card/Checking account holders to allow up to four authorized users on their account. As part of the rollout of this feature, a new product intent was created to support sanctions screening of these additional cardholders. The new product intent was configured to include a rescreen cadence; however, because the intent is not configured to create cases, rescreening is not being conducted.
RCA Description: Process Design: Inadequate process design is the reason for this gap. Specifically, the product intent was not configured to create cases, which prevents BadActors from fulfilling the ongoing screening requirements. As a result, Square Debit Card additional cardholders are not being subjected to ongoing sanctions screening.
Issue Rating Justification: This issue represents medium risk as it is inconsistent with industry best practices and fails to adhere to Block's Global Sanctions Policy. A High rating is not recommended as these additional cardholders are subjected to initial screening. Additionally, this feature is currently only available in the US, which poses lower risk from a sanctions perspective as the likelihood of a U.S. cardholder being a sanctioned person is low.
Issue Progress Updates: Although this issue was initially identified in March 2024, an issue was not formally raised in Protecht at that time. This is because Block was in the early stages of the launch of the Issue Management Program, and stakeholders within the business found it challenging to identify the appropriate issue owner as well as DRIs and due dates for certain milestones within the action plan, without which it would be difficult to ascertain whether the issue could be remediated by March 6, 2025 according to ERM guidelines for Medium-rated issues.
Issue Action Plans:
AP 1: Create a New Token Type to Use for Debit Card Authorized Users Screen Requests (1316904)
 AP 2: Enhance Systems to Enable Proper Case Creation in Regulator (1316907)
 AP 3: Amend Square Card Product Intent to Create Cases (1316910)
 AP 4: Initiate screen requests using the New Token Type to screen Debit Card Authorized Users (1316913)
 AP 5: Backfilling Existing Population of Square Card Additional Cardholders (1316916)
 AP 6: Sustainability (1316919)
Closure Request Justification:
Supporting evidence and documentation for all action plans reviewed and approved for closure. 
 

 Overall issue approved for closure on 08/25/2025

Issue Title: Lack of Proper IP Address Blocking for Cash App BTC Lightning Deposits & Withdrawals
Issue Description:
Cash App BTC Lightning Deposits & Withdrawals have been using an outdated, free-trial version of the MaxMind IP Database for IP address blocking of nodes in Lightning payment paths.
 

 In November 2022, Cash App launched support for Lightning Deposits and Withdrawals, a new bitcoin feature. Lightning Network payments are routed through various nodes via interconnected payment channels to reach their destination. Given the decentralized nature of the Lightning Network, Cash App needed to ensure that payments were not being routed through nodes located in sanctioned jurisdictions. To do this, the Block Lightning team began using MaxMind, a geolocation vendor, to block routing nodes within a payment path if their broadcast IP was from a sanction nexus. 
 

 During the transition of Cash App Lightning Repository ("cash-lit") to the Block Lightning Team (BLT) due to Block’s functionalization, an engineer on BLT discovered that cash-lit was not using the standard neteng-iamproxy service for IP blocking that is currently used by the BLT for purposes of IP address blocking of the Block Lightning routing node. The neteng-iamproxy service, also known as the neteng-ipamproxy, is owned by Block’s Foundational Network Engineering (NetEng) teams and its standard function is to provide centralized IP address risk checking via a central source of truth for prefix lists of blocklisted/whitelisted IP addresses other teams can query for their systems/networks, particularly for purposes such as IP blocking, sanctions compliance, and allowlisting for network proxies. Upon investigation, it was determined that cash-lit had been relying on a MaxMind IP database from a free trial account implemented approximately three years ago when the product initially launched. This free trial database has not been updated since implementation, despite having updated IP information readily available because the vendor, MaxMind, updates their IP data weekly. Therefore, IP address blocking was not using the most up to date vendor-maintained list of IP addresses associated with sanctioned jurisdictions.  
 

 This issue was identified by a BLT engineer during the transition process and subsequently verified by D'Khari Quarles. The original engineer who implemented the controls has since left the organization, and the Bitcoin Platform Team (BitPlat) who took over the repository was unaware that the system was relying on outdated geolocation data for sanctions screening.
 

 The issue affects all Lightning Network BTC transactions processed through Cash App since the feature launched, potentially impacting 14 million transactions. Due to logging limitations, transaction IP logs are only retained for two weeks, preventing a comprehensive review of historical transactions to identify potential sanctions exposure.
 

 The root cause is a failure in technology governance and monitoring processes, specifically a lack of formal control documentation, control handover processes, and technological monitoring following product/control launch. When ownership transferred to BitPlat, BitPlat could not find formal documentation of the sanctions geolocation control implementation details. Additionally, it does not appear there were proper control handover processes or technological monitoring in place after product/control launch to (1) assess whether the implemented control was working as intended or (2) ensure the proper version of the vendor system was being used instead of a free trial, resulting in a critical gap in compliance oversight.
 

 This creates a significant regulatory risk under OFAC regulations as transactions from sanctioned jurisdictions may have been processed due to inaccurate or outdated IP geolocation data. The outdated database could fail to properly identify IP addresses that have been reassigned to sanctioned jurisdictions over the past three years.
 

 To remediate this issue, Block needs to: 1) Immediately implement the neteng-iamproxy service for IP blocking of cash-lit consistent with other routing nodes; 2) Establish a formal control monitoring process for new and existing Lightning Network-related products to occur after product/control launch and on a periodic basis thereafter.
RCA Description: Technology and Processes: The root cause is a failure in technology governance and monitoring processes. When the Lightning Network functionality was initially launched, a temporary free trial version of MaxMind was implemented without establishing proper procedures for database updates or verification of data currency post-launch. There were no monitoring controls implemented to ensure regular updates occurred after initial implementation.
Issue Rating Justification: This issue is rated High due to potential violations of sanctions regulations which could result in significant regulatory penalties, enforcement actions, and reputational damage. Using an outdated IP database for sanctions screening creates a material risk of failing to identify and block transactions involving sanctioned jurisdictions directly or indirectly. The severity is elevated due to the age of the database (three years old) and the absence of any updates despite weekly refreshes being available from the vendor.
Issue Progress Updates: Given that logging limitations exist (IP logs are only retained for 2 weeks), a lookback to identify potential violations cannot be completed and, therefore, will not be a required task for issue remediation. This record retention limitation will not be assessed through this issue but rather will be assessed through an existing issue related to record retention: ID #1230402 Ensuring Compliance with OFAC Record Retention Requirements
Issue Action Plans:
AP 1: Implement the neteng-iamproxy service for IP blocking of cash-lit consistent with other routing nodes (1325483)
 AP 2: Establish a formal control monitoring process for new and existing Lightning Network-related products to occur after product/control launch and on a periodic basis thereafter. (1325486)
 AP 3: Formally document Lightning-related IP Blocking Controls (1325491)
Closure Request Justification:
All Supporting Documentation in Action Plan records have been attached and submitted. 
 

 All action plan successfully closed. Root cause (leveraging an old database for IP blocking) was remediated in April 2025 and has been sufficiently documented.
