Issue Title: Debt Collection Guide breach on 16943056
Issue Description:
On August 1, 2024 at 10:47am, Probe received an inbound call from the customer. The outcome of the call resulted in an agreement with the customer to pay the amount due that day. At 3:26pm that same day, an outbound call was made via the predictive dialler, representing contact made whilst an arrangement was in place.
 

 This issue was raised by Afterpay QA team from an annual audit of 2024 with the presentation by the QA team on 18/06/2025
RCA Description:
On August 1, 2024 at 10:47am, Probe received an inbound call from the customer. The outcome of the call resulted in an agreement with the customer to pay the amount due that day. At 3:26pm that same day, an outbound call was made via the predictive dialler, representing contact made whilst an arrangement was in place.
  The agent who made contact with the customer quickly identified that an agreement had been made earlier that day and apologised for the repeated contact. There were no further customer impacts to remediate.
Issue Rating Justification: Only one account has been identified with this issue. Ongoing calls have been audited at 5 random calls per month since then.

Issue Title: Double cash-ins on IOS
Issue Description:
Problem: Starting on or around January 2025, approximately 0.3% of manual cash in requests on iOS are resulting in double cash ins despite a fix in the 5.2.1 and 5.3.1 releases. The issue was caused by a client-side implementation bug that allowed multiple rapid cash-in requests during biometric authentication. This bug was exacerbated by the rollout of the MWTAM experiment, particularly variant A with the ATM picker feature. The issue specifically affected iOS users but not Android due to differences in how the two platforms handled button taps in the UI. The SEV was declared on June 2, 2025.
 

 Impact: Customers experience duplicate charges from their linked accounts which could potentially lead to overdraft fees, affecting over 2,000 cash ins per day. A complaint analysis was performed https://docs.google.com/document/d/1h-xHbDx3CIwpQFEWAsmRKiJUVLRu3W586hu6N8p9i3M/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.4yiyjj4bovuo and "of the 891,302 potentially affected customers, 17,936 (2.01%) reported problems through complaints, with 7,936 customers filing complaints the same day their double cash-in occurred".
 

 Remediation: Deployment of version 5.2.1 on June 4, 2025, was pushed to production to fix the issue.
Issue Rating Justification: given that nearly 1m customers were impacted by this issue which could have caused overdraft fees, a medium rating appears appropriate.
Closure Request Justification: I have been advised to close this ticket and note that the FLRM Team will be creating a ticket for this issue. The FLRM Team has been closer to the issue.

Issue Title: Fidelius token logic is mapped to a unique routing number vs financial institution
Issue Description:
Issue: Out fidelius token logic is mapped to a unique routing number versus a financial institution. More specifically, a unique fidelius token is mapped to a single bank account number and a single routing number. In most cases, institutions have several routing numbers which presents an opportunity to circumvent our Max 2 and Max 5 SSN / instrument controls if you change the routing number. This issue appears to only apply in situations in which a customer manually links their bank account since there's a field to manually enter both a routing and account number. Plaid/Stripe which support ~85-90% of bank account linking traffic, do not provide users with the opportunity to enter/select a unique routing number. Therefore, we need to either change the fidelius token logic or apply new logic to map the token to the institution. If the token is mapped to the institution, there wouldn't be a way to potentially circumvent our Max SSN related controls.
 

 This data is extremely challenging to analyze so we are currently unable to size the potential impact of this issue. The belief is this is likely only impacting a relatively small population as it only applies to manual links and we have begun more closely monitoring unusual bank linking activity.
Issue Rating Justification: Given the possibility to circumvent our SSN related controls which would elevate the risk associated with laundering funds and other illicit activity, a Medium risk seems appropriate.
Issue Action Plans: Incomplete Fidelius Token Mapping (1358498)

Issue Title: Cash App is not consistently providing all information requirements in periodic statements required under Regulation E Regulation E, 12 C.F.R. § 1005.9.
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirements
 Regulation E requires that, "The (periodic) statement shall set forth the following information, as applicable: 
 §1005.9(b)(1)(iv) For a transfer initiated by the consumer at an electronic terminal (except for a deposit of cash or a check, draft, or similar paper instrument), the terminal location described in paragraph (a)(5) of this section"
 terminal location including the city and state and at least one of the following: (a) the street address; (b) a generally accepted name for the specific location; or (c) the name of the owner or operator of the terminal if someone other than the account-holding institution.
 §1005.9(b)(1)(v) The name of any third party to or from whom funds were transferred
 §1005.9(b)(2) The number of the account
 §1005.9(b)(4) The balance in the account at the beginning and at the close of the statement period.
 Issue Description
 Based on the specific sections outlined in the regulatory requirements section above, GCMT identified the following specific statement errors:
 Cash App’s Account Statements do not consistently include the terminal location for consumer-initiated EFTs (excluding deposits of cash, checks, or similar paper instruments)
 Periodic statements do not consistently show the name of third parties involved in the transfer (sender or recipient).
 Account numbers/identifiers are absent from Savings statements. Additionally, Cash App’s General Account Statements do not consistently display the account number.
 GCMT's assessment of statements concluded that the statement details for the balance in the account at the beginning and at the close of the statement period do not account for money movement, and instead only reflect Stored Balances.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

 Add Cashtag as an account identifier on statements (1322199)
 Include a balance disclaimer on statements and clarify passthrough transactions in new designs, consulting Legal for approval. (1322202)

Issue Title: In automated disputes, Cash App provided consumers with inaccurate denial reasons (outcomes) in written explanations of investigations when the consumer's notice of error was not submitted within 60 days.
Issue Description:
Regulatory Requirement
 Regulation E, 1005.11(b)(1)(i), outlines the timeliness of the customer's submission of their Notice of Error as 60 days from the date of the periodic statement. In response to the customer’s submission, Reg E requires that an institution’s report of its error investigation results must include a written explanation of its findings. The report must also inform the consumer of their right to request copies of the documents the institution relied upon in reaching its determination. Upon request, these documents must be provided promptly.
 Issue Description
 GCMT identified Cash App’s dispute automation flow should have automatically denied all Cash Card claims that were submitted by consumers beyond the 60-day Notice of Error period. Instead, Cash App denied these disputes based on the fact that no error was found, rather than denied based on the timing violation. These written explanations were inaccurate and cannot be supported by Cash App’s internal records/information.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

RCA Description: Systems/Technology
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory nexus, fixed in normal course of business.

Issue Title: Terms of Service were not properly displaying for new Android users during the Cash App signup/account creation flow between March 24th through May 29, 2025.
Issue Description:
Regulation E § 1005.7(a) requires that, "A financial institution shall make the disclosures required by this section at the time a consumer contracts for an electronic fund transfer service or before the first electronic fund transfer is made involving the consumer's account."
 Issue Description
 GCMT identified that the Cash App Terms of Service (ToS) hyperlinks were not properly displaying on Android devices utilizing the "arcade" version of the sign-up flow beginning on March 21, 2025 (for Android versions between 4.90 and 5.1). As a result, ~25,000 Android device customers per day were unable to access/view the Terms of Service links during the account onboarding flow, with approximately 1.6 million users affected over 69 calendar days (per Cash App Product Data Science team (PDS)) until the blocker was fixed by Engineering on May 29th, 2025. 
 GCMT notes the following risk mitigating factors:
 When signing up for an account, customers are required to provide their phone number or email address alias, which is verified through a One-Time Passcode (OTP). This alias can only be linked to one account at a time. If customers enter an alias that is already linked to an existing Cash App account, they proceed with the login flow rather than the account creation flow in which disclosures have been previously provided. 
 When signing up via mobile phone, an SMS with terms URL is sent at first signup. This link was confirmed to be functional.
 For email signup, the one-time password (‘OTP’) email includes links to relevant terms, as follows: "By logging into Cash App, you agree to the Terms of Service, E-Sign Consent, and Privacy Policy". This link was confirmed to be functional.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Q2 2025 - Cash App- Regulation E (EFTA) subpart A (P2P & Card) (1045093)
RCA Description: Technology
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory nexus, with risk mitigating factors.
Issue Action Plans: Quality Assurance Engineering has updated its platform rollout testing protocols to include verification checks for onboarding flows across email, phone, and general onboarding processes (1322212)
Closure Request Justification: BU provided evidence of updated processes to ensure required links are functional and directed to the appropriate locations.

Issue Title: Voice Support Customer Experience Uplift
Issue Description: Gaps were identified in voice support customer experiences. Voice support customers are not provided with an expected hold/wait time before connecting with an advocate. Voice support customers that call outside of business hours are not given the option to leave a voicemail message and/or a timeframe in which they can expect to receive a response from an advocate.
RCA Description: Process weaknesses, process or product/service design, or monitoring/management of processes resulting in errors, control breakdowns, customer complaints, or operational losses.
Issue Rating Justification: Gaps identified via NY DFS guidance. Solutions put into place.
Issue Action Plans:
Develop and enhance Voice Strategy for CS (1323517)
 Chat hours of operations expansion and IVR updates (1324163)
Closure Request Justification:
To meet requirements, we implemented a comprehensive upgrade to our customer service framework. This includes the introduction of a 24/7 support system via our Messaging channel, ensuring that customers receive timely assistance at any hour. Additionally, verbiage was added to the IVR to direct folks to chat if voice servicing is unavailable.
 

 IVR messaging after hours includes the following verbiage: Thank you for calling Cash App Support. Our phone lines are currently closed. You can reach us by phone every day from 8 AM to 9:30 PM Eastern Time. Need help now? Chat with us in the app—our team is available 24/7. Just tap your profile icon in the top right corner, then scroll down and tap Support to browse topics or start a live chat.
 

 Estimated wait time is played in the general IVR and details are below.
 

 EWT < 1 Min:
 We play the prompt that they will be connected shortly. But after 60 seconds (in case our EWT was not accurate, we give them the option to leave callback.
 EWT b/w 1 and 5 mins:
 We announce the EWT and give them an immediate option to leave callback or wait in the queue. Then after every 30 seconds we give them the option to stay in queue or leave VM.
 EWT > 5 mins:
 EWT announced and only given option to request callback. This is repeated 3 times giving them opportunities to request callback. After 3 tries we play prompt and hang up.
 

 Knowledge Article is linked in details tab, but below is snippet that advocates tell Bitcoin consumers about call back timeframes.
 

 Voice: Inform them about response timing:
 During hours (12 PM - 7:30 PM ET): Live transfer, or callback when the next specialist is available
 Important: Remind customer they must press 1 when prompted to receive a callback
 After Specialty hours: Callback within the next day
 

 Bitcoin-specific IVR after business hours: You have reached Bitcoin outside our normal business hours of 8am to 4:30pm PST, 7 days a week. You can still request a callback within our next business day by pressing 1 now. In the meantime, you can reach out to us in the app. Just tap the icon in the top right corner and tap support.

Issue Title: Inconsistent Mobile Check Deposit Timeline Messaging Caused Customer Confusion and Escalations
Issue Description:
Some customers have received conflicting information about mobile check deposit processing times across various touchpoints in their journey. When depositing checks, customers have encountered different processing timeframes ranging from 1-5 days, 1-3 days, up to 6 days (sometimes extending to 8 days), four days, or even one day. These varying timeframes appeared in different locations within the app, auto-generated deposit confirmation emails, and our knowledge base articles used by support advocate teams.
 

 This inconsistency in messaging has created significant customer confusion and frustration, leading to an increase in manager escalations. More critically, some customers experienced financial hardship when funds weren't available on the expected date due to these mixed messages. Through analysis of numerous support cases, we identified this as a persistent gap in our customer communication that required immediate attention.
 

 This issue was discovered through a pattern analysis of customer support cases and escalations.
RCA Description: Product
Issue Rating Justification: An issue in interpretation and execution of CCO policy which has material impact on the process or risk assessment.

Issue Title: The SIL Adverse Media and PEP Screening procedure should be updated to reflect current watchlist screening processes.
Issue Description:
1. The "SIL Adverse Media Case Decision Tree Document" noted on pg 16 of the "SIL EEA Seller PEP & Adverse Media Screening Procedure - October 2024" states the following: Has the source (individual or entity) of the adverse media processed any transactions within the last year? (Check if any related accounts are also active) 
 If yes, the decision tree states the following: 
 Has the source (individual or entity) of the adverse media processed any transactions within the last year? (Check if any related accounts are also active)
 • YES→ Review the KYC information
 - GCMT reviewed token # (account) LPFPA72YPMBKK (Associated User Token: L7P7VFFG2D20M) as part of the QA transactional testing and noted a true positive Adverse Media match for Money Laundering and organized crime activity on the account. GCMT noted that the customer transacted €554.65 and Compliance Operations deactivated the account and added a comment on January 24, 2025, stating that the customer has not processed since March 2024. 
 The step taken was to deativate the account as the customer had not processed since March 2022, however this appears to not be in alignment with the decision tree, which requests the agent to check IDV, if customer transacted in the last 12 months. In this case the customer had transacted within 12 months ( it was 10 months prior to the agent check on 24 January 2025). So the decision made to deactivate is not consistent with the decision tree ask and or there is no direction for what other steps may need to be taken after the 12 month/idv check is completed e.g Leave the account open, Deactivate, refer to SAR team, escalate to MLRO.
 

 2) The SIL Seller PEP & Adverse Media Screening Procedure - October 2024 should be updated to reflect the 1 business day initial screen and 30 business day rescreen SLA that is configured on Regulator. Currently, the Procedure does not reference that the SLAs are configured by business days.

Issue Title: KYB Refresh Overdue for High Risk Cash for Business (C4B) Customers
Issue Description:
Cash for Business (C4B) accounts are accounts that Cash App customers can open to manage their business funds, so that funds remain separate from their personal account.  
 

 Know your Business (KYB) Refresh is a periodic process used to update verifying information about existing business customers to ensure compliance with regulatory requirements and to manage potential risks. It involves confirming and updating business information (e.g. legal name, legal structure, registration number, business documentation), ownership and control information/ authorized individuals, understanding the nature of the business, and assessing associated risks. 
 

 In 2024, Cash App made enhancements to its C4B account onboarding flow to collect additional information from businesses at account opening. To date there is no mechanism in place to capture updated information from business accounts. Therefore, it has been identified that there are high risk business customers who onboarded more than a year ago and have not received a full KYB Refresh review. Specifically, there has been no information or documentation refresh. According to the Cash App KYC Policy, KYC Refresh is required annually for high risk customers and includes: 
 (1) information and documentation refresh
 (2) a periodic due diligence review. 
 

 

 While high risk C4B customers may have received a periodic due diligence review, because KYC Refresh requires (1) information and documentation refresh and (2) a periodic due diligence review, KYB Refresh is incomplete for high risk C4B customers who are currently due for KYB Refresh.
RCA Description: Process- There is no process in place to collect business information or documentation requiring refresh.
Issue Rating Justification: The impact of this issue is as follows: (1) the business is not in compliance with applicable internal AML policies, specifically the Cash App KYC Policy, (2) due to the lack of adherence to internal policies, and considering prior audit and/or regulatory feedback specific to KYC Refresh, there is the potential for regulatory exposure, and (3) there is increased risk exposure due to outdated customer business information used to effectively monitor for BSA/AML risk.
Issue Action Plans: KYB Refresh Overdue for High Risk Cash for Business (C4B) Customers Action Plan (1407459)

Issue Title: GCMT identified some Adverse Media and PEP alerts out of SLA and procedural documentation related to screening processes needing enhancement.
Issue Description:
Testing Results:
 Transaction Testing Results
 GCMT has identified the below recurring issue in Q2 2025 when compared to our Q3 2024 Adverse Medium Sanction and PEP screening review. Management did not provide a management action plan on the basis that “Management advised that they have a mechanism in place to track cases out of SLA.”
 

 ▶︎ Missed SLAs - Manual Alert Review: 
 1) GCMT reviewed 100 alerts ( 44 - Adverse Media, 48 - PEPs and 8 - Sanctions - includes initial screening and rescreening cases) generated in December 2024 and noted that the following:
 • PEPs initial screening - 1 out of 48 Alerts reviewed after the 1 day SLA (Merchant ID: MLGJM2YF6BHCZ). 
 • Adverse Media initial screening - 2 out of 44 Alerts were reviewed after the 1 day SLA. The out of SLA timeframe ranges from 1 to 51 days. Compliance Operations confirmed this was due to delays in obtaining MLRO approval for an escalation and a pending SSP not completed by the Merchant (Merchant IDs: MLQWW1T78KC1E & MLSZDK1J8S27B). 
 

 

 Procedure  
 1) The "SEL Adverse Media Case Decision Tree Document" noted on pg 15 of the "SEL Seller PEP & Adverse Media Screening Procedure - October 2024" states the following:
 

 Has the source (individual or entity) of the adverse media processed any transactions within the last year? (Check if any related accounts are also active) 
 If yes, the decision tree states the following: 
 Has the source (individual or entity) of the adverse media processed any transactions within the last year? (Check if any related accounts are also active)
 • YES→ Review the KYC information
 

 GCMT noted that the decision tree is not clear and specific as to how to proceed following review of the KYC information (e.g., leave the account open, Deactivate, refer to the SAR team, escalate to MLRO, etc).
 

 2) The SEL Seller PEP & Adverse Media Screening Procedure - October 2024 should be updated to reflect the 1 business day initial screen and 30 business day rescreen SLA that is configured on Regulator. Currently, the Procedure does not reference that the SLAs are configured by business days on Regulator.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: The issue is rated as low as the procedural documentation requirements enhancement.
Issue Action Plans: Compliance Operations will ensure alerts are reviewed within the expected SLAs and update the SEL procedural documents with the required updates/recommendations. (1332747)

Issue Title: Incorrect logic was applied for a cohort of initial adverse media screening alerts, resulting in the alerts not being reviewed.
Issue Description:
▶︎ Bulk Closure for Adverse Media Alerts 
 Of the 100 Adverse Media Alerts, GCMT noted that for 28 Merchants (36 total alerts - consisting of 4 initial and 32 rescreen alerts) which were “Bulk Closed” as part of the ongoing project. GCMT notes that there are no formalized processes for the ongoing Bulk Closure processes including agent expectations for managing these alerts and or a record on the alert that it has been closed as part of a bulk closure.
 As a result of our inquiries with CAX, they have advised that the logic applied as part of the ongoing bulk closures has been incorrect for initial screening adverse media screening alerts. The logic included an additional cohort of initial screening adverse media alerts which were generated for Sellers who were active but had not yet transacted with Square. The logic should have only included Sellers which were deactivated at the date of the data run. This means that there were 47 bulk case alerts closed for this cohort of customers that should have been reviewed and dispositioned by compliance operations, but were not. Refer to ticket CAX-3242 for further details.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: The issue is rated as low as only a small proportion of the alerts closed are for merchants have not transacted yet.
Issue Action Plans: Compliance Operations will work to remediate the 47 alerts here which should have been reviewed but were incorrectly bulk closed. (1332750)

Issue Title: There is no formalised processes for documentation on Regulator for the ongoing bulk closure processes and the logic applied as part of the ongoing bulk closures has been incorrect for initial screening adverse media screening alerts.
Issue Description:
▶︎ Bulk Closure for Adverse Media Alerts 
 Of the 100 Adverse Media Alerts, GCMT noted that for 28 Merchants (36 total alerts - consisting of 4 initial and 32 rescreen alerts) which were “Bulk Closed” as part of the ongoing project. GCMT notes that there are no formalized processes for the ongoing Bulk Closure processes including agent expectations for managing these alerts and or a record on the alert that it has been closed as part of a bulk closure. As such GCMT identified variances in the processes; 
 

 For 7 of 44 Adverse Media Rescreen and 1 of 48 PEP Rescreen alert cases generated in December 2024, the agent added a comment on Regulator referencing SCA-780 JIRA Ticket as a "Bulk Close" action. 
 Upon review the JIRA Ticket, GCMT note that reference is made to July 2024 Bulk Closures only, however Compliance Analytics and Engineering have confirmed that this ticket is being applied for all ongoing bulk closures as part of this project including the 6 alert cases reviewed by GCMT in December 2024. 
 For 10 of 44 Adverse Media and 12 of 48 PEP Initial and Rescreen alert cases which were bulk closed in December 2024, GCMT noted that no comment was added on Regulator to reference the bulk closure or SCA-780 JIRA ticket. Per confirmation with Compliance Operations there is no expectation for agents to include commentary for alerts which have been bulk closed. 
 

 Additionally, as a result of our inquiries with CAX, they have advised that the logic applied as part of the ongoing bulk closures has been incorrect for initial screening adverse media screening alerts. The logic included an additional cohort of initial screening adverse media alerts which were generated for Sellers who were active but had not yet transacted with Square. The logic should have only included Sellers which were deactivated at the date of the data run. This means that there were 47 bulk case alerts closed for this cohort of customers that should have been reviewed and dispositioned by compliance operations, but were not. Refer to ticket CAX-3242 for further details.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Issue Rating Justification: The issue is rated as low as the logic has already been remediated and a documentation enhancement is required.
Issue Action Plans: Technical controls will be implemented to ensure the ETL logic is operating correctly and the bulk closure procedures will be formalised. (1333402)
Closure Request Justification: A formal Standard Operating Procedure has been developed for all bulk closures and has been shared with relevant stakeholders. The SOP defines the specific criteria, approval workflows, and documentation standards. The SOP will be appended to all relevant code reviews, and reviewers will be required to validate that the automation logic adheres to the procedure before granting approval. The remediation to close the issue involves placing Squarewave Job #37547 (which performs bulk closures for Square Compliance screening alerts) into maintenance mode with strict controls that prohibit adding new functionality or criteria. The job ownership will transition from CAX to CDE (CET Data Engineering) by Q1 2026, with future bulk closure needs addressed through process improvements in upstream systems rather than expanding the automation job.

Issue Title: Afterpay Customer Service-Failed Refunds
Issue Description:
An operational risk has been identified by Risk Management in the handling of Failed Refund cases. Currently, agents use a shared Google Drive folder containing spreadsheets with customer PII (e.g., names, account details, refund data). However, there are no access restrictions in place—any agent with the link or within the workspace can access these files, regardless of business need.
 

 This lack of access governance creates significant exposure to unauthorized access, mishandling, or accidental data sharing, violating principles of least privilege and data minimization. It also poses compliance risks under data privacy regulations (e.g., CCPA, GDPR) and prevents auditability or accountability in data handling. If not addressed, this vulnerability could lead to customer harm, regulatory findings, or reputational damage.
RCA Description: The root cause of this issue is the absence of standardized access controls and data governance procedures for handling sensitive refund case data. The use of shared Google Drives evolved organically to meet operational needs but was not reviewed through a formal risk lens. As a result, folders containing customer PII were created and shared without role-based restrictions, approval workflows, or access monitoring, leading to an environment where data exposure is possible and untraceable.
Impacted Countries:
Canada
 United Kingdom
 France
 Ireland

 Lithuania

 Bermuda
Impacted Legal Entities:

Issue Rating Justification: Given the sensitivity of the data involved and the regulatory requirements surrounding PII, this issue should be rated as High/Critical. Immediate action is needed to restrict access to only those agents who require it to perform their job duties, and to implement appropriate audit and monitoring controls.
Issue Action Plans: Restrict Access (1334075)
Closure Request Justification: Action resolved

Issue Title: Absence of a Structured Settlement Monitoring and Recovery Framework
Issue Description:
There is no formal process to confirm settlement completion, track delivery timing, detect potential delays early, or respond to missed deadlines. Without these operational safeguards, settlement issues may go unnoticed until failure occurs, increasing the risk of delays, financial exposure, and customer impact.
 

 The absence of these mechanisms means that problems with settlements may not be detected until they result in an actual failure or significant delay. This can lead to a range of risks and adverse outcomes, such as:
 -Settlement delays that may disrupt normal business operations
 -Increased financial exposure or losses due to late or failed settlements
 -Damage to reputation and potential loss of customer trust if issues are not proactively managed
 -Additional workload and resources required to manage issues after they escalate
 

 The issue was identified during an operational review/audit/internal assessment by Paul Gallagher, which found no documented process or framework for settlement monitoring and timely recovery actions in case of delays or failures.
RCA Description: The core reason is the lack of a developed and implemented operational framework or set of procedures covering the end-to-end settlement monitoring and recovery. This may be due to historical reliance on manual checks or the assumption that settlements would complete without incident, without considering the need for formal oversight and escalation procedures.
Impacted Countries:
Canada
 Australia
 United States
 New Zealand
Impacted Legal Entities:
Afterpay Canada Ltd
 Afterpay Australia Pty Ltd
 Afterpay US Services
 Afterpay NZ Limited
Issue Rating Justification: Lack of monitoring may result in delayed settlements and customer dissatisfaction, and regulatory harm.
Issue Action Plans: Missing Settlement Monitoring & Recovery Action Plan (1334063)

Issue Title: Lack of Basic Data Protection in Manual Payment File Handling
Issue Description:
Identified Problem:
 There are currently no structured protections in place for sensitive information contained within manual payment files. This means that important personal and financial data is not being properly secured as it should be. Instead, when the process lacks these protections—files containing sensitive data are not encrypted when transmitted, there are no rules being enforced about who can see what data, files are not consistently marked based on their sensitivity, and there are no automatic privacy controls in place to help meet privacy regulations such as GDPR.
 Without these protections, there is a real risk that confidential financial or personal information could be accidentally shared with or accessed by people who are not authorized to see it. This could result in privacy breaches, loss of customer trust, and substantial regulatory fines if customer information is improperly disclosed.
 

 How the Issue Was Identified:
 This control gap was discovered during an internal audit of manual payment handling processes by Paul Gallagher from the FLRM team. 
 

 This gap could cause violations of data protection regulations, particularly the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), which requires strict controls over the handling of personal data.
RCA Description: The organization has not implemented foundational technical controls for protecting sensitive data in manual payment workflows. There are no encryption standards, no access restrictions enforced by technology, and no systematic privacy checks tied to the sensitivity of the information.
Impacted Countries:
Canada
 Australia
 United States
 New Zealand
Impacted Legal Entities:
Afterpay Canada Ltd
 Afterpay Australia Pty Ltd
 Afterpay US Services
 Afterpay NZ Limited
Issue Rating Justification: Critical gap in protection of sensitive payment data with legal compliance exposure.
Issue Action Plans: Absence of Foundational Data Protection Protocols Action Plan (1334724)

Issue Title: Afterpay Merchant Experience Team: Insufficient Guidance and Monitoring to Ensure Appropriate XBT Dispute Decisioning
Issue Description: A compliance risk has been identified where the Afterpay Merchant Experience team does not currently have documented guidance on how to decision on the XBT disputes. Additionally, there is no control (secondary review, quality monitoring, etc.) performed over the process to ensure that the XBT disputes were decisioned appropriately and that the process was followed. The identified documentation and control gap, acknowledged by both Afterpay leadership and Risk Management, creates compliance risk through potential inconsistencies in decisioning outcomes for XBT disputes and potential inaccurate decisioning. Action is needed to comprehensively document the steps and considerations needed to ensure consistent XBT decisioning as well as implement a review control.
RCA Description: There is no documented SOP outlining eligibility criteria or steps for XBT dispute decisioning, and the QA process does not currently include these cases in scope. As a result, frontline team members are relying on informal knowledge and inconsistent judgment when processing these disputes.

Issue Title: Cash App Borrow - Reg Z - APRs Understated
Issue Description:
(10) Four of the seventy APRs tested were
 understated by more than .125%
 using the FFIEC APR calculator. In
 all four cases, the APR disclosed used
 one extra day of interest. In our
 calculations, the date of origination
 and the date the payment was due
 were taken from the credit agreement.
 Our calculations were provided to
 Block management for review.
 Block explanations for the
 inaccuracies were related to rounding.
 It appears the APRs were being
 calculated based on the setup fee
 percentage divided by the term in
 day, multiplied by 365. Due to the
 APR calculations within Regulation Z
 including a present value calculation,
 we do not think this calculation is
 accurate. In addition, Block
 management referenced that the
 FFIEC APR tool should not be used
 to calculate APRs; however, the
 referenced text in the FFIEC
 calculator only states the tool is not
 recommended over other tools.
 Wipfli is in agreement with the
 statement and welcomed other
 verifiable calculations. Remediation
 was provided in 2023 for 3 of the
 inaccurate APRs, none were
 considered reimbursable by the
 FFIEC APR tool. We recommend
 management review the APR tool
 being used to determine if it follows
 the formulas in Appendix J to
 Regulation Z. We also recommend
 continued monitoring with a reputable
 tool be completed so that any
 inaccuracies continue to be identified.
Issue Rating Justification:
Matrix from report for low: 
 Low (15 findings)
  Minimal potential for consumer harm
  Minimal potential for financial loss
  Can be corrected through the normal course of business
  Minimal potential for damage to reputation
  Violation affects a limited number of consumers
Issue Progress Updates:
Block Response: Block disagrees.
 APR Calculation - To calculate
 the APR for Cash App Borrow
 Loans:
 (Setup Fee % / term length) * 365)
 = APR (BPS)
 Example
 Origination Date:12/19/2023
 Maturity Date: 1/19/2024
 Term Length: 32 Days
 Setup Fee: 5% of the loan
 Loan Amount: $100.00
 (500 / 32) * 365
 5703.125 BPS = 57.03125 %
 APR Tool - This was remediated
 Oct 2023. Block identified that
 rounding to the closest integer
 cent was causing APR’s to fall
 outside the .0125% variance. To
 remediate, Block updated the
 bases of the APR calculation on
 the fees charged in the final
 rounded cents amount instead of
 the fees charged in percentage. In
 addition, Block will round down
 on any fee dollar calculation.
 As part of the remediation, Block
 elected to refund consumers
 impacted by the APR calculation
 mismatch. The calculation above
 remains the primary method to
 calculate a Cash App Borrow
 Loan.

Issue Title: Same SSN, Different DOB
Issue Description: Compliance has self-identified a persisting population of accounts on the platform that share an SSN but have different, customer-provided DOBs ("same SSN / Different DOB") indicating that these accounts are potentially owned by two different identities. Cash App Trust Product implemented a control on December 6, 2024 to prevent customers from signing up for a Cash App Card if they attempt to verify their identity with the same SSN but different DOB as an existing verified customer. On February 13, 2025 another control was added to fail identity verification for customers with a neutral SSN signal if they share an SSN with an existing, verified Cash App customer, but have a different DOB. However, both of these controls were only implemented on a go-forward basis and no remediation was conducted on the historic population.

Adrian Gardner
 Ashley Adkins
 Courtney Smith
Impacted Legal Entities:
Block, Inc.
 Cash App Investing, LLC
Issue Rating Justification: This issue is rated high due to potential regulatory non-compliance for KYC.

 Reassess current Same SSN Different DOB Control Criteria (1394181)

Issue Title: Inauthentic Associated - Potential Hidden Teens
Issue Description: Cash App Families introduced a Machine Learning model (assumed 75% precision) in January 2025 that identifies verified adult Cash App accounts potentially owned by teens ("Hidden Teens"). Hidden Teens are defines as an underage individual who used another person's credentials (potentially a family member's) to pass verification and successfully onboard to an adult Cash App account. Since it's inception the model has been identifying these potential Hidden Teen accounts, but if otherwise no bad activity is detected on the account, no adverse action has been taken.

Issue Title: Afterpay Plus - FCRA - AAN Credit Decline
Issue Description:
(20) For all adverse action notices sampled, the
 adverse action notice contained the language
 stating the credit decision was made in whole
 or in part based on information contained in a
 credit report and included the name and
 address of the credit reporting agency (CRA).
 The notices also stated that an affiliate or
 third party provided credit related information
 used in whole or in part in the credit decision.
 The application/transaction was not declined
 due to information from the CRA report or
 from an outside source, they were generally
 declined for not having enough funds to make
 the purchase. We recommend management
 review system settings used to produce
 adverse action notices to ensure the FCRA
 language is only included on the notice when
 the denial is related to credit-related
 information contained in a credit report
 obtained for the transaction or credit-related
 information from an outside source.
Issue Rating Justification:
Rating matrix from Wipfli report: 
 

 Low (15 findings)
 Minimal potential for consumer harm
 Minimal potential for financial loss
 Can be corrected through the normal course of business
 Minimal potential for damage to reputation
 Violation affects a limited number of consumers
Issue Progress Updates:
Block Response: 
 Block respectfully disagrees
 with the finding for the
 following reasons:
 1. Applicable FCRA
 Provisions:
 (a) The FCRA’s scope
 extends beyond traditional
 consumer reports; (b) 15
 USC 1681m(b) specifically
 addresses adverse actions
 based on information from
 sources other than from
 consumer reporting agencies;
 and (c) this includes
 information flows between
 affiliates and third parties that
 bear on creditworthiness
 2. Transaction Structure
 Analysis:
 The transaction information
 flows from Afterpay to FEB
 and FEB is the creditor.
 The information is being
 provided by a party reporting
 only its own consumer
 transaction and experience
 data (Afterpay), such that the
 flow of information is not a
 consumer report.
 Legal Framework:
 Under 15
 USC1681a(d)(2)(A)(i)inform
 ation reported by Afterpay
 about its direct transactions
 with consumers is explicitly
 excluded from the definition
 of a “consumer report.”
 However, 15 USC 1681m(b)
 separately requires specific
 disclosures when credit is
 denied or adversely modified,
 the decision is based on
 information from non-CRA
 sources, and the information
 bears on
 creditworthiness. When
 these conditions are met,
 FCRA mandates the
 following disclosure:
 “Our credit decision was
 based in whole or in part on
 information obtained from an
 affiliate or from an outside
 source other than a consumer
 reporting agency. Under the
 Fair Credit Reporting Act,
 you have the right to make a
 written request, no later than
 60 days after you receive this
 notice, for disclosure of the
 nature of this information.”
 Therefore, the current
 adverse action notice
 language is appropriate and
 compliant with FCRA
 requirements for situations
 where credit decisions are
 based on information
 obtained from non-CRA
 sources.

Issue Title: Afterpay Plus - UDAP - Billing Errors
Issue Description:
(25) Two customer billing errors were
 resolved by applying a credit to
 another order. As the payment due
 dates and payment amounts vary
 between the two purchases, crediting
 the refund to a different purchase may
 result in the customer having to make
 payments sooner or for a different
 amount than would have been the
 case had the credit been applied to the
 correct order. This appeared to meet
 the definition of an unfair practice
 under UDAP. The consumer was
 harmed by making a payment on a
 different date in a different amount
 than required by agreement. The
 consumer could not control the harm,
 and there were no countervailing
 benefits that outweighed the harm.
 We recommend refunds be applied
Issue Rating Justification:
Moderate (8 findings)
 May reflect a system design issue
 Singular or multiple instances of noncompliance with a provision of a law, rule, or regulation, regulatory guideline,
 regulatory expectation, or internal policy and procedure
 Moderate potential for consumer harm
 Moderate potential for financial loss
 Serious in nature and should be corrected in a timely manner
 Potential damage to reputation
 Violation might have persisted over a limited period of time
 Violation may affect a moderate number of consumers
Issue Progress Updates:
Block Response: 
 Block respectfully
 disagrees. There are certain
 scenarios where a refund cannot
 be processed automatically,
 specifically where a refund is
 made by the card rails. In this
 instance, the merchant applied the
 refund directly back to the
 customer's Afterpay Plus Card.
 Because the refund went back via
 card rails and not via the Afterpay
 merchant integration, Afterpay
 follows a refunds matching
 process, known as “orphan
 refunds”. The high level process
 is:
 1. Customer taps the Afterpay
 Plus Card in the merchant’s store
 when returning the goods.
 2. Funds are sent back to
 Afterpay via the card network. At
 this point, Afterpay only
 knows the customer, the amount,
 and the merchant at which the
 refund was processed.
 3. Afterpay attempts to match the
 refund amount back to an order
 using specific
 matching criteria, including the
 network reference identifier,
 customer name, refund
 amount, order amount and the
 merchant name.
 4. If the automated matching is
 unsuccessful, the customer will
 be notified and prompted to open
 the Afterpay App and select an
 order to apply the refund to,
 known as “consumer choice
 refunds”.
 The customer has 3 days to select
 an order
 and several notifications are sent
 with eligible orders for them to
 select from.
 5. If a customer does not select an
 order within the timeframe, the
 unmatched
 refund is sent to a queue for an
 agent to match to a correct order.
 6. If an agent cannot manually
 match to the right order, the
 remaining balance is
 then returned to the customer’s
 preferred card on file.

Issue Title: Afterpay Plus - SCRA - Applicability
Issue Description:
(28) Management stated the Afterpay Plus product
 does not apply to SCRA interest relief. The
 product has a participation fee of $5.99 per
 month for the use of the service and a late fee.
 SCRA clarifies interest as “the term ‘interest’
 includes service charges, renewal charges,
 fees, or any other charges (except bona fide
 insurance) with respect to an obligation or
 liability.”
 Due to the fees assessed on this product, the
 product would have been applicable to the
 relief under the Act. We recommend
 management review the accounts to determine
 if any requests were made and take
 appropriate action.
Issue Rating Justification:
High (3 findings)
 May reflect a system design issue
 Singular or multiple instances of noncompliance with a provision of a law, rule, or regulation, regulatory guideline,
 regulatory expectation, or internal policy and procedure
 Moderate to high potential for significant consumer harm
 Moderate to high potential for significant financial loss
 Significant in nature and needs immediate attention
 Potential damage to reputation
 Violation might have persisted over an extended period of time
 Violation may be widespread and affect numerous consumers
Issue Progress Updates:
Block Response: 
 Block respectfully disagrees
 with this finding. The
 Afterpay Plus product's $5.99
 monthly participation fee does
 not constitute "interest" under
 SCRA for the following
 reasons:
 Product Structure
 - Afterpay Plus is a
 subscription service offering
 enhanced features
 - The monthly fee is for access
 to these features
 - The underlying credit
 product remains interest-free
 - Fee is not tied to any specific
 credit transaction or balance
 SCRA Application
 - SCRA regulates fees that
 function as interest substitutes
 - Participation fees that
 represent service access, rather
 than cost of credit, are not
 covered
 - The fee is fixed regardless of
 transaction volume or balance,
 and not a charge related to
 credit extension

Issue Title: Block-Wide Process Standardization for Revocation and Honoring of Consent for TCPA Compliance
Issue Description:
The TCPA (Telephone Consumer Protection Act) prohibits businesses from initiating text message solicitations to customers registered on the national National Do Not Call (DNC) registry, and requires businesses to allow text message recipients to opt out of receiving future messages “by using any reasonable method.” Similarly, the Telemarketing Sales Rule prohibits businesses from making telemarketing calls to consumers who have listed their phone numbers on the DNC Registry, unless an exception applies.
 

 Cash App and Square platforms lack standardized processes to handle customer requests for opting out of automated communications (calls and SMS), but this may extend to other Block services and products. This process uplift opportunity, identified through legal team analysis of existing support workflows, affects our ability to efficiently manage communication preferences and honor customer opt-out requests for automated outreach. The absence of clear, documented procedures creates risks from both a customer experience and compliance perspective, potentially affecting all Block entities. From the customer experience side, this leads to inconsistent handling of opt-out requests, potential continuation of unwanted communications, extended resolution times due to unclear processes, and customer frustration from having to make multiple contact attempts. Internal legal review of customer support processes uncovered opportunities for uplift in how communication preferences are handled. 
 

 Currently, Square Sales does not verify numbers against the DNC registry before making outbound sales calls or sending text messages to prospects, though work is underway to address this gap. Discovered via Internal legal review of outbound sales practice processes.
 

 These revocations of consent, and honoring of prior do-not-call preference opportunities exist due to missed intent when the processes were built out.
RCA Description: Processes - There was no TCPA policy in place and because of that the requirements were never given to customer service to operationalize them.
Issue Rating Justification: Operational gap or process failure resulting in an impact to regulatory obligations
Issue Action Plans: Uplift of TCPA Consent Revocation Process (1338031)

The lack of evidence and formalization in the review process creates a critical compliance risk, as it impairs the ability to demonstrate proper handling and resolution of customer disputes to regulators. The absence of a structured monitoring control could lead to systemic failures in dispute resolution, creating both immediate and long-term risks to the organization's regulatory standing and customer relationships.

Issue Title: Lack of UK Frozen Asset Account and Process Documentation
Issue Description:
Squareup Europe Ltd does not currently have a formal, documented process in place for freezing assets that are subject to UK sanctions regulations. While the company is capable of restricting access to funds for sanctioned individuals, there is no finalized or documented procedure for transferring and holding such frozen funds in a dedicated, segregated account at a financial institution.
 

 This gap was first identified in 2022, when Global Sanctions observed that Squareup Europe Ltd was holding funds linked to Russia that could potentially fall under UK asset freezing requirements. Efforts were initiated to formalize and document the asset freezing process at that time. However, the process was not completed after it was determined that the specific funds in question were not in scope for UK asset freezing, and no urgent use case was identified.
 

 A prior issue (1161775) was opened to request the creation of a segregated frozen asset account at Barclays Bank, to ensure compliance with UK sanctions regulations and facilitate clear reconciliation. During that process, it was suggested that the existing UK Safeguard Account might serve for this purpose. However, following review, it was noted, particularly by Compliance stakeholders that the Safeguard Account is commingled with other funds and does not support the complete segregation required by sanctions regulations. This lack of segregation raises risks related to auditability, regulatory compliance, and reporting.
 

 The absence of a formalized process for asset freezing is a process design deficiency in Squareup Europe Ltd’s sanctions compliance program. The primary driver of this gap has been the lack of actual incidents requiring asset freezing under UK sanctions to date. While this inherently contributes to a low risk rating, this issue is being rated as Medium Risk given that it is a repeat issue. 
 

 To address this compliance gap, Squareup Europe Ltd must:
 -Open a dedicated, segregated frozen asset account at Barclays Bank (or an equivalent financial institution) for UK Frozen Assets, ensuring that frozen funds are fully segregated from other company funds.
 -Develop procedures for freezing assets in accordance with UK sanctions regulations.
 -Update relevant existing procedures related to asset freezing (Global Sanctions Procedures, Compliance Operations Procedures, etc.)
 -Establish controls and reconciliation mechanisms that support clear audit trails and regulatory reporting.
RCA Description: Process: The root cause of this issue is twofold: a delay in establishing a dedicated segregated account for frozen assets, and a weakness in the underlying process design. A formal procedure for asset freezing was never fully integrated into the sanctions compliance program, largely because no prior incidents had necessitated freezing assets under UK sanctions regulations. Additionally, the initial approach of using the UK Safeguard Account for holding frozen sanctions assets proved unsuitable after further review and stakeholder feedback, it was determined that the Safeguard Account is commingled with other funds and does not meet the strict segregation requirements. As a result, a formalized, fit-for-purpose process and appropriately segregated account are needed to ensure both regulatory compliance and effective auditability.
Issue Rating Justification: Currently, there are no UK frozen assets in the possession of Squareup Europe Ltd (Block, Inc.), and this situation is rated as low risk. This indicates minimal immediate risk, but addressing the process design weakness is essential for future compliance with UK sanctions regulations. However, because this is a repeat issue, the issue rating has been increased to Medium.
Issue Progress Updates: March 2026 has been designated as the due date given the dependency on Barclays (or another financial institution) agreeing to open a frozen assets account for Block (or one of its legal entities). Opening and maintaining blocked or frozen assets accounts inherently carry greater regulatory risks for our financial institution partners which contribute to the delay in account opening. We expect to close this issue well before March 2026 if the opening of an external frozen assets account is expedited.
Issue Action Plans:
Action Plan 1: External Bank Outreach and Account Creation (1339357)
 Action Plan 2: Process/Procedure Documentation and Training (1340000)

Issue Title: Discrepancies between the Product Requirements Document and implemented Logic in the SSN+1 Denylist Propagation Control
Issue Description:
GCMT Control Description:
 This control is designed to automatically denylist accounts that are connected via SSN and one additional asset (from: Email, Phone Number, Cash Device, Payment Card, or Bank Account) to a “seed” account that has been denylisted due to any of the three in-scope risk denylist reasons: Chargebacks, Dispute Abuse, or Scams. The control’s objective is to reduce exposure to coordinated fraud by enforcing denylist actions on high-risk connected accounts within a timely manner—currently defined operationally as within 96 hours.
 

 Testing Results: 
 GCMT testing identified two key areas of concern with this control: 
 (1) differences between the logic described in the Product Requirements Document (PRD) and the control as implemented, and;
 (2) the lack of a documented or risk-reviewed SLA to govern the timing of enforcement actions.
 

 1) Discrepancies Between the PRD and Implementation Logic - Several implementation-level changes were identified that diverged from the PRD without formal documentation, review, or impact evaluation:
 - SSN + Month/Year of Birth Matching Logic: The control implementation requires a match on both SSN and Month/Year of birth, although the PRD defined only an SSN match. This narrowed the population eligible for denylist propagation, reducing it from 128,158 to 126,104 accounts.
 

 - Inclusion of IP Address as a +1 Asset Type: The implemented control includes IP_ADDRESS as a connected token asset type, expanding the propagation criteria beyond the PRD-defined scope.
 

 - Temporary Exclusion of In-Flight Paycheck Deposits: Accounts with pending paycheck deposits are excluded from denylisting until the deposits settle. While this appears to impact SLA rather than logic, it creates temporary risk exposure.
 

 - 30-Day Lookback Window at Launch: The initial version of the control (October 2024) only considered connections formed within 30 days of the seed account’s denylist date. The application of the 30-day lookback window was treated as an incident and was remediated in January 2025, resulting in approximately ~57k accounts being denylisted on a delayed basis—ranging from days to more than a few months after meeting the connection criteria.
 

 2) Lack of Rationale and Documentation for Control SLA
 The control currently uses a 96-hour service level agreement (SLA) to define how quickly denylist actions must propagate from a seed account to connected accounts. However, the PRD does not specify any timing expectation for this execution, nor was the 96-hour SLA reviewed or approved by Compliance or Risk stakeholders.
 Based on analysis of propagation timing and related transaction volumes, $1.57M in activity was observed within the 48–72 hour window following a seed account’s denylist event, during which connected accounts remained active. GCMT noted that there is no documented rationale outlining how the 96-hour timeline was established or what level of risk exposure it is intended to address. In the absence of such justification, it is unclear whether the current SLA is appropriately calibrated to mitigate potential abuse.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: 2025 Cash App FinCrimes Post Implementation Testing - Trust & Identity Controls (1046404)
Issue Rating Justification: Discrepancies between the Product Requirements Document and implemented Logic in the SSN+1 Denylist Propagation Control can result in untimely denylisting of customer account, blocking a customer (connected) account leading to an increase in AML/fraud, regulatory fines/penalties, financial loss to the company, and reputational risk.
Issue Action Plans:
Action Plan # 1: Update to PRD for SSN + 1 Control (1340662)
 Action Plan # 2: Monitor SLAs and potential exceptions using GCMT-Validated Logic (1340669)
 Action Plan # 3: Adjust ETL to Denylist Accounts That Fall Outside the 96-Hour SLA (1340672)

Issue Title: Delays in execution Service Level Agreement and Missed Denylist Actions for SSN+1 Connected Accounts
Issue Description:
Testing Objective: 
 For seed accounts DL'ed from 3 in-scope risk DL (i.e. Chargebacks, Dispute Abuse, Scams), confirm the denylist action was executed timely for the connected token(s) identified by SSN + 1 other asset (i.e. Any of the 6 asset types - "'EMAIL',
 'PHONE_NUMBER', 'CASH_DEVICE', 'PAYMENT_CARD', 'IP_ADDRESS', 'BANK_ACCOUNT'). 
 

 Testing Results: 
 For 137,926 connected accounts evaluated for proper denylist propagation based on SSN+1 matching criteria, GCMT identified the following-
 

 1. Timeliness Exception (SLA > 96 hours)
 A total of 13,755 (9.97%) connected accounts were denylisted after the 96-hour SLA threshold.
 

 This exception includes:
 - 10,303 (7.47%) where SSN+1 match was met before the seed account was denylisted.
 - 3,452 (2.50%) where SSN+1 match occurred after the seed account was denylisted.
 

 2. Failure to Propagate Denylist to SSN+1 Connected Accounts
 A total of 1,934 (1.40%) of accounts met the SSN+1 criteria but were never denylisted despite eligibility.
 

 Per discussion with Data Science and Engineering owners of the control, GCMT concluded that the following root causes contributed to the exceptions:
 

 1. Complex and Timing-Sensitive Data Dependencies
 The SSN+1 control depends on data from approximately 20 different source tables to validate whether two accounts are connected. This reliance introduces both technical complexity and timing sensitivity. For example, the control required both SSN and DOB (month/year) to match before applying a denylist, to reduce false positives from inconsistent vendor data. In some cases, the DOB wasn’t yet available in the system when the seed account was denylisted, so the connected account was missed—even though it would have qualified once the data arrived.
 

 2. Evolving Scam DL Criteria
 The criteria used to classify scam-related denylist actions have changed over time (e.g., addition of RO_DL_DURABLOCK), leading to inconsistencies in tagging. While enforcement for direct scam tagging now exists, historical gaps have affected propagation accuracy.
 

 3. Retroactive Dispute Abuse Tagging
 Dispute abuse DLs have occasionally been applied retroactively, after the seed account was already denylisted. These cases often rely on inferred reasons from regulator comments, which lack timestamps. This has introduced delays and ambiguity in downstream propagation logic.
 

 4. Remediation of 30-Day Lookback Limitation (Delayed SLAs Only)
 A fix merged on January 17 corrected the application of a 30-day lookback window, which had previously excluded eligible connections from timely denylisting. This update led to a noticeable increase in denylist activity between January 18–27 and accounted for approximately 75% of the delayed SLA cases.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: 2025 Cash App FinCrimes Post Implementation Testing - Trust & Identity Controls (1046404)
Issue Rating Justification: Delays in execution Service Level Agreement and Missed Denylist Actions for SSN+1 Connected Accounts can result in untimely denylisting of customer account, blocking a customer (connected) account leading to an increase in AML/fraud, regulatory fines/penalties, financial loss to the company, and reputational risk.
Issue Action Plans: Reevaluate Whether the 96-Hour SLA Is Appropriate (1340679)

Issue Title: Not All Connected Assets Are Propagated During Asset Denylist Execution: Higher Miss Rate Observed for Devices
Issue Description:
Testing Objective: 
 For seed accounts denylisted (DL'd) via the 3 in-scope reasons ((i.e. Chargebacks, Dispute Abuse, and Scams), confirm the 3 connected assets (i.e. device, phone # & instrument) are timely propagated with no new successful linking post the propagation. 
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT reviewed denylist propagation effectiveness across three connected asset types—SMS/phone numbers, payment instruments, and devices. Testing results conclude all exceptions fall under one of the three patterns below: 
 

 Pattern 1: Asset and seed account were both already denylisted, but the asset was still successfully linked to another account.
 

 

 Pattern 2: Asset was not denylisted after the seed account was denylisted, and was later successfully linked to another account.
 

 

 Pattern 3: Asset was not denylisted after the seed account was denylisted, but no relinking occurred.
 

 Across the SMS and instrument asset types, exception rates were statistically insignificant: 
 - SMS / Phone Numbers: Among 452,384 records reviewed, Pattern 1 occurred in 0.003% of cases, Pattern 2 in 0.0002%, and Pattern 3 in 0.003%.
 - Payment Instruments: Among 366,858 records reviewed, Pattern 1 occurred in 0.003%, Pattern 2 in 0.002%, and Pattern 3 in 0.302%. While the majority of exceptions fell under Pattern 3, no relinking was observed.
 

 However, device-level propagation showed an elevated exception rate:
 - Devices: Among 952,940 records reviewed, Pattern 1 occurred in 0.0003% of cases, Pattern 2 in 0.005%, and Pattern 3 in 3.0%. While most exceptions fell under Pattern 3 with no relinking activity, the error rate is notably higher relative to other asset types. The volume of missed device denylist events is directionally significant and may warrant further analysis.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Rating Justification: This issue Presents a low risk to the operations and processes or controls.

Closure Request Justification: Issue closed per risk acceptance due to strategy change. See linked action plan for artifacts submitted by issue owners. Tester reviewed and confirmed that engineering has completed its investigation and provided root cause analysis and impact assessment for this issue. The analysis confirms that the identified control gap falls within the scope of the FTI MAPs (G-06) initiative, which includes a comprehensive reassessment and redesign of asset denylisting controls. Given the low severity of the issue, limited exploitation observed, and the ongoing program-level remediation, it is reasonable to accept the risk at this time rather than implement a standalone fix. This control will be re-evaluated as part of the FTI MAPs assessment and associated policy and control updates.

Issue Title: Technical Limitations in Device-Account Linking Resulting in Discrepancies Between Control Implementation and the Product Requirements Document
Issue Description:
GCMT Control Description:
 This control is designed to prevent unverified customers from transacting on a mobile device if another account (verified or unverified) has previously completed a successful transaction on that same device. The control enforces an Identity Verification (IDV) requirement for the second and any subsequent unverified users on the device.
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT identified that the implemented control does not fully align with the Product Review Document (PRD) description. 
 

 The PRD states:“If an account (unverified or verified) has ever successfully transacted on a mobile device, another unverified account cannot transact using the same mobile device.” 
 

 However, the implemented control uses a broader linking definition: it enforces IDV if the unverified account is connected to any prior transacting token, regardless of which device was used. Additionally, the “first ever successful transaction” reference point is also not device-specific. Currently, an unverified customer may be required to complete IDV even if they are the first—and potentially only—user to transact or log in on that device.
 

 Furthermore, per discussions with the Engineering and Data Science owners of the control, aggregated account-over-device linking data is not directly retained on the platform today. As a result, the control operates by checking whether two tokens have ever been connected via any device, using app token–to–device token connection data stored in device-grip. This introduces technical limitations that prevent the control from enforcing logic strictly on the same device. This architecture appears to be a legacy carryover from the earlier “Max 5 Devices” control.
 

 GCMT cannot conclude whether this implementation meets the intended design of the control, as the PRD provides no guidance on whether the broader model is expected, appropriate, or aligned with compliance or risk objectives.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Rating Justification: This issue presents a low risk to the operations and represents a limited weakness in the process.

Issue Title: Not All Transactions By Unverified Accounts (not first transacted) Are Blocked By the Max 1 Control
Issue Description:
Testing Objective: For in-scope transactions as executed via Cash Limit, ensure the control block the transaction(T1) attempted on an unverified account (C1), if C1 is associated with any other account (C2+ verified or unverified, active or dormant) that successfully transacted prior to T1. Confirm IDV was triggered for C1 and no successful transaction occurred until C1 successfully IDV’ed. 
 

 Testing Results: 
 GCMT identified 192,478 (1.4%) exceptions based on SQL analysis of approximately 13 million total successful records during the review period. In all exception cases, the transacting customer was not the first ever successful transacted token, as required by the control design to bypass the IDV flow.
 

 The review included various scenarios based on IDV attempt timing to confirm exception accuracy and assess impact:
 

 a) IDV Verified ≥ 25 hours post-transaction: 20 of 20 samples reviewed (100%) confirmed as true exceptions
 b) IDV Verified < 25 hours post-transaction: 2 of 2 samples reviewed (100%) confirmed as true exceptions
 c) Customers Never IDV Verified: 10 of 10 samples reviewed (100%) confirmed as true exceptions
 

 Root causes of all 32 samples were investigated in collaboration with the control’s Engineering and Data Science owners, as well as Compliance Analytics, and concluded the following:
 - 22 out of 32 transactions were not blocked, and the customer was not prompted for IDV due to a missed device link event from the upstream service (device-grip).
 - 10 out of 32 experienced a delayed device link event, also from device-grip.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: Cashbot provides incorrect information related to Retro eligibility due to state restrictions.
Issue Description: When customers consult CashBot with questions regarding Afterpay options, CashBot provides information related to the functionality of the Retro product, without mentioning that the customer’s eligibility is initially based on their state of residency. This can cause confusion, due to state restrictions, and the team is looking to better inform the ChatBot to provide more succinct responses.
RCA Description: CashBot does not intake and process signals from customers related to location, which would impact the information provided; specifically if a customer is eligible for the Retro product due to their location.
Interested Parties:
Yishi Garrard
 Langston Walker
 Corey Chamberlain
Issue Rating Justification: Concerns over possible deceptive practices in the inaccurate information provided by ChatBot to users.
Issue Progress Updates: The primary concern is whether or not implying the customer can access Retro is deceptive or misleading if the product is not available in the state. Though the majority of customer interaction with CashBot is related to eligibility or functionality of Afterpay products, the description of the Retro product is provided as an answer in a few instances. Because of the planned expansion of Retro products, it is important to ensure that ChatBot responds to inquiries with increased accuracy; especially in regards state eligibility.

The upgrade allows Cashbot to access a user's lending signals in order to make a more accurate assessment of the user and provide a more accurate and correct response. A user's signal consists of active lending products, outstanding loan balances, and eligibility status. In the case of this issue, Cashbot is able to detect if a user is authorized access to particular products, due to geography restrictions, thus ensuring Cashbot does not suggest the use of products which a user does not have access to.

Issue Title: Thematic Discrepancies Between Requirement Documentation and Production Launch
Issue Description:
GCMT’s testing and post-implementation reviews have consistently identified discrepancies between what is documented in Product Requirement Documents (PRDs) and what is ultimately implemented in production. These gaps stem from the lack of a fully structured documentation, reconciliation, and change governance framework over the compliance product control development cycle. This introduces risk that implementation might not reflect the intended compliance design, proceed without proper approval, or lack a trackable and auditable record of compliance risk decision. Common issues include:
 Engineering-driven changes during implementation (e.g., due to system or data limitations) that are not reflected in the PRD
 Scope or technical design modifications made post-approval without documentation of rationale or stakeholder re-review
 Missing or ambiguous documentation of key control parameters, such as SLAs for end-to-end control execution
 Discrepancies between PRDs and Engineering Design Docs, with critical logic described only in the latter
 Undocumented or unclear rollout scope (e.g., e.g., “GA 1/1/2025” might indicate 50%, 95%, or 100% rollout without clarity or verification)
 

 While the Customer Summary Document (CSD) is intended to replace the PRD under the Trust product development lifecycle, it does not yet fully address these documentation and change management gaps. 
 

 To address the identified gaps, GCMT is of the view that the process should be enhanced to:
 Ensure that requirements documented in the CSD are traceable to the Launch Requirements Document and Legal Memo. 
 Update the CSD template to include a dedicated Change Log and establish a formal process for Product, Engineering, Legal, and Compliance to align on post-approval changes.
 Ensure rollout strategies and any interim constraints are explicitly documented and communicated.
 Define and document SLA expectations for control execution to ensure alignment across stakeholders.
 Introduce a pre-launch reconciliation step to confirm that control requirements are fully documented, implemented, and approved.
 Evaluate whether backfilling documentation is needed for existing controls under the new process.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

 Includes any Issues representing violations of (i) regulatory expectations (i.e items that are not necessarily stated explicitly in law or regulatory guidance, more open to interpretation) or (ii) "non-key elements" of internal policies and procedures.

Create Detailed Action Plans and Assign Owners (1342655)
