Issue Title: Afterpay Customer Service Team: Control Gap to Ensure Deletion of Sensitive Customer Data on Individual Devices
Issue Description: Advocates download dispute documentation/evidence provided by the customer to include in a dispute filing but there is no recorded process for deleting this information once downloaded to their device, potentially leading to non-compliance with privacy laws which would result in legal, financial, or reputational harm. The storage of customer PII on individual devices could lead to noncompliance with financial privacy regulations and result in legal ramifications. Since there is no formally documented process for removing customer documentation from individual devices, it's highly likely that sensitive information is being stored unknowingly. Action is needed to implement a step in the Merchant Disputes process that outlines deleting downloaded customer data after a dispute has been filed.
RCA Description: The root cause of the control gap is the absence of a formally documented process and technical controls requiring the deletion of customer dispute documentation after it is downloaded to advocate devices. This oversight stems from a lack of data retention policies, insufficient training on privacy obligations, and an operational process that prioritizes efficiency over secure data handling, leading to potential non-compliance with privacy regulations.
Impacted Legal Entities:
Afterpay Canada Ltd
 Clearpay Finance Limited
 Afterpay Australia Pty Ltd
 Afterpay US Services
 Afterpay NZ Limited
Issue Rating Justification: As the gap in data retention could result in non-compliance with retention policies and breaches of storing customer PII, there is a high and severely undesirable level of risk to the organization that requires immediate management attention to mitigate the risk adequately. If this situation were to materialize, there could be regulatory breaches due to inadequate controls, and inconsistent removal of sensitive customer data from individual devices.
Issue Action Plans: Establish SOP Guidance for Secure Deletion of Downloaded Customer Dispute Documentation (1343309)
Closure Request Justification:
The SOP has been updated to remind team members to delete any docs that have been downloaded to local PC's, Link to SOP on ZD Helpcentre https://internalsupporthelp.zendesk.com/hc/en-au/articles/46283358744217-Handling-Customer-Documents-SendSafely-Guide-Document-Deletion-Steps - PDF copy attached
 

 This has been distributed via the weekly comms process - page 3 of the doc that is attached, this will be covered off via a huddle with the teams next week

Issue Title: Merchant Misattribution Issue for Square and Cash App Card Merchants
Issue Description: SEV 6195: Initially escalated through an FDIC Complaint Follow up, the incident was related to incorrect parent-child merchant relationships, and highlighted a long-standing issue with unreliable merchant grouping. Historically, the system lacked formal review processes for new regex rules, relied on reactive manual interventions for corrections, and had an incomplete remediation strategy that overlooked low-volume merchants. This resulted in legitimate transactions being erroneously categorized as another vendor (i.e. Shell gas station showing as Shell oyster company) transactions, affecting a possible and potentially estimated 41,000+ Cash App Card customers across 145,000+ transactions (initial estimate was later disproved, but included in a reportable event memo shared with Sutton Bank, our banking partner for the product). The issue has regulatory implications as the FDIC's formal second follow-up indicates heightened scrutiny. Additionally, no formal incident ticket or SEV was initially created for this issue, and no impacted population has yet been solidified in partnership with engineering/technical teams. Additionally, there is potential UDAAP concern due to potential perceived deception in misattributing merchant names on customer receipts/activity, though indications show the statement was indeed correct. Incident is being stabilized and further investigated to understand the true and holistic impact.
RCA Description:
Why did legitimate transactions get misattributed to MarleyLilly?
 Because the transaction categorization rule was too broad, matching incorrect merchants in the activity/receipt field with improper merchant, yet similar in name, in their description.
 

 Why was the categorization rule too broad?
 Because insufficient specificity was built into the rule during development, lacking proper validation criteria, system for merchant classification had 436 blank rules.
 

 Why was insufficient specificity implemented?
 Because the merchant categorization rule development and review process lacked adequate controls and testing protocols
 

 Why did the development process lack adequate controls?
 Because there were gaps in the change management protocols for merchant categorization systems
 

 Why were there gaps in change management protocols?
 Because the organization lacked comprehensive incident management processes and formal tracking systems for merchant categorization changes
 

 Additionally, the engineering team had the following explanation for the SEV: Absence of a Formal Review Process: The creation of new regex rules was not subject to mandatory peer review or the required inclusion of Merchant Category Codes (MCCs). Rules were often implemented based on the subjective judgment of the individual creator.
 Reactive and Manual Intervention: Corrections were not proactive. Instead, we relied on employee reports, which then required manual ungrouping by the on-call engineer.
 Incomplete Remediation Strategy: The protocol for correcting errors was limited, only mandating fixes for merchants with a transaction count exceeding a certain amount in the prior three months. Low-volume merchants with known grouping errors were not required to be fixed.
Interested Parties:
Brittany Kelley
 Samira Afzali
 Joe Petti
Issue Rating Justification: This issue meets the criteria for a Medium risk rating as it presents moderate operational risk wit+ Cash App Card customers and multiple transactions potentially affected across multiple Block products, requiring priority corrective action due to formal FDIC follow-up and upcoming Q3 2025 regulatory review for the banking partner who identified the SEV / incident.
Issue Progress Updates:
FinOps and FLRM are currently awaiting Engineering's estimate of affected Square Card customers to understand the full scope of impact for the Square request
 Code review is scheduled for Q3 2025
 Seven customers who disputed transactions on May 9-10, 2025 have been identified and contacted by Square
 This issue is likely to come up in the Q3 2025 FDIC Complaints Targeted Review
 Some affected customers may still be unnotified, creating ongoing risk of continued impact
Issue Action Plans: Improve merchant grouping through prevention of new incorrect groupings and establishment of a resilient framework (1387593)

Issue Title: Dispute Cases Coming Back are Partial Wins marked as case lost
Issue Description: SEV 6160: During routine dispute case monitoring, discrepancies were identified in how dispute cases marked as "Case Lost" by Marqeta are being processed, resulting in incorrect money movement and customer communications. The system is improperly handling partial reversal amounts from Marqeta webhooks, defaulting to full dispute amount reversals when receiving "Case Lost" status instead of processing the specified partial amounts. This affects two distinct scenarios: Scenario One involves 320 cases YTD where customers are incorrectly debited full amounts ($173K total) instead of partial liability amounts, and Scenario Two involves 6 cases YTD where customers receive incorrect dispute loss communications despite actually winning their disputes. Both scenarios appear to have originated at the Cash App Card program inception, with Marqeta providing limited historical data from 2023-2025 citing data reliability challenges for earlier periods.
RCA Description:
Primary Root Cause: Improper webhook processing logic for "Case Lost" status from Marqeta
 

 5 Whys Analysis:
 

 Why are customers being incorrectly charged/receiving wrong communications?
 Cash App's system is treating all "Case Lost" webhooks as full dispute losses instead of processing partial reversal amounts
 Why is the system defaulting to full reversal amounts?
 The webhook processing logic doesn't properly parse partial reversal amounts when status shows "Case Lost"
 Why wasn't the webhook logic designed to handle partial reversals with "Case Lost" status?
 The original system design didn't account for Marqeta's practice of marking partial wins as "Case Lost" to prevent double crediting
 Why wasn't this business logic requirement captured during initial implementation?
 Insufficient requirements gathering and testing scenarios during Cash App Card program inception didn't cover edge cases of partial dispute resolutions
 Why weren't comprehensive dispute resolution scenarios tested?
 Lack of end-to-end testing protocols that included all possible Marqeta webhook combinations and dispute outcome scenarios during initial program launch.
Interested Parties:
Lyndsey Weiss
 Paige D'Monte
 Naina Jain
Issue Rating Justification:
Scale of Impact: With 41,000+ Cash App Card customers and 145,000+ transactions potentially affected, this presents moderate disruption to Block's payment processing operations
 Cross-Product Impact: The issue spans multiple Block products (Cash App Card and Square Card), creating operational complexity and resource allocation challenges
 Ongoing Customer Impact: Some affected customers remain unnotified, creating continued operational burden through potential additional disputes and customer service inquiries. Team is leaning towards picking up the effort to designing and deploying the ideal solution likely sometime in Q1 2026 and in the interim time ensuring we adjust the status and DO NOT adjust the provisional credit. This approach will need sign off, and a communication has been shared and approved by Sutton Bank as of end of August.
Issue Progress Updates: Awaiting comprehensive historical data from Marqeta beyond the current 2023-2025 dataset (1,180 cases, $350K+ owed). Legal and Compliance review requested from regarding UDAAP concerns for Scenario 2, though compliance assessment indicates lower risk due to minimal direct consumer harm and small population size (6 cases). Marqeta has cited data reliability challenges for pre-2023 periods but we've pushed back requesting full historical scope given issue presence since program inception.

 Although some AP teams may use aliases, other AP agents (i.e. the AP Internal Disputes Response “IDR” team) are currently unable to utilize aliases due to possible system or operational limitations, leaving them more exposed to potential personal threats and emotional stress during their interactions with customers.

 [+https://secure-afterpay.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/39628935+|https://secure-afterpay.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/39628935]
 

 Details:
 

 * Explicit racial slurs, bomb threats, and death threats issued to named identifiable agents, posing serious immediate dangers.

Issue Title: Weaknesses in Sanctions Screening Control Processes for Transactions using Bitkey Block-Owned Third Key
Issue Description:
Weaknesses have been identified in the operating effectiveness of the sanctions screening control for Block’s Bitkey product, specifically for transactions processed using the Block-Owned third key(hereafter referred to as the “Bitkey wallet”). Bitkey is a self-custody bitcoin wallet developed by Block that is designed to make bitcoin ownership accessible, simple, and secure. One of the Bitkey features is that if an owner is missing their private key or public key to access their wallet, Bitkey has a third, Block-owned private key through which the customer can send bitcoin transactions. Because Block has this key, Block can see the transactions conducted that rely on that key and, therefore, can see the wallet addresses involved in transactions. As such, Block has a responsibility to screen those transactions against known sanctioned wallet addresses to ensure a transaction is not processed in violation of applicable sanctions regulations, namely those of the US, UN, UK, EU, and the Netherlands. The weaknesses documented below were identified in a Slack thread (bitkey-compliance-legal). 
 

 Lack of Updates to Designated Wallet Address Screening List
 The sanctioned wallet addresses list used for screening Bitkey wallet co-signed transactions hasn't been updated since February 2024. This is considered an issue that warrants remediation as OFAC and other regulatory bodies (including the EU) have designated additional sanctioned wallet addresses between February 2024 and May 2025 when this issue was identified, which means Block may have missed detecting transactions in the Bitkey wallet involving newly designated addresses. The root cause of this weakness is process design and lack of control monitoring/validation resulting in errors. Bitkey Compliance team has the false assumption that the process used by Bitcoin Compliance Operations to update the designated wallet addresses list for Cash App’s bitcoin transaction screening was also updating the list used for Bikey transaction screening. The Cash App process involved a Bitcoin Compliance Operations Program Manager adding the designated wallet addresses to Guardrails, which is an internal Cash App system that handles the creation and revocation of Adversities (i.e., adverse actions taken against customers) but also wallet addresses. Remediation requires development of automated list updates as well as creation of a process to validate that list updates are being made timely and as expected. 
 

 While implementing direct Toolbox integration, which will automate updates to the list of designated wallet addresses, would only take hours of actual work, the process is significantly delayed (estimated Q4 completion) due to Virtual Private Cloud (“VPC”) networking constraints between Bitkey and Cash App systems, requiring approximately a quarter of lead time to establish the necessary connections. This automated process will focus on strict OFAC/EU/UN sanctions lists and redundancies will be developed so there is no longer a single point of failure for updating this list which will ensure continuous, real time compliance with requirements. As a temporary solution, Bitkey Product Compliance will execute updates manually while working on the longer-term, automated Toolbox integration, with the Bitkey Product Compliance Manager tracking this issue and working to establish a clear process for update frequency.
 

 Insufficient Designated Wallet Address Screening List
 The sanctioned wallets addresses list used for screening Bitkey wallet co-signed transactions currently only consists of specific wallets designated by OFAC, which is inconsistent with the Cash App process that screens bitcoin transactions against supplementary lists of known sanctioned wallet addresses identified by Chainalysis, Block’s bitcoin screening vendor, in addition to OFAC and other regulatory body-designated wallets. This is considered a weakness as it is industry best practice to screen against vendor-owned supplemental known sanctioned wallet address lists because OFAC lists do not typically identify all known wallet addresses tied to a targeted bad actor while vendors’ lists identify more. The root cause of this is process design. 
 

 Lack of Effective Alert Review Process
 From November 2024 through Jan 2025, no team or person was dedicated to reviewing alerts resulting from wallet address screening. This is primarily due to the design of the Bitkey Wallet screening process as a manual process owned by a single DRI (the Compliance Lead for TBD at the time). When the Bitkey team, which was previously under Block’s TBD brand that winded down in November 2024, experienced turnover, the lack of a documented process and insufficient cross training resulted in reviews not being carried out. Additionally, prior to the Bitkey product launch, the Bitkey team committed to hiring additional staff to review screening alerts; however, their staffing request was denied. As such, the overall root cause is poorly defined roles and responsibilities and training deficiencies. Though this creates potential sanctions risks, Emerging Business Compliance has confirmed that no potential match alerts have generated from Bitkey Wallet screening for screening from January 2025 to now. Additionally, as of February 1, 2025, the Bitkey Product Compliance Manager has taken over the review process. However, remediation should ensure a documented process exists and Emerging Business Compliance has a plan in plan for training and continuity for DRI changes.  
 

 Lack of Documented Processes and Procedures
 The Bitkey Wallet sanctions screening processes, including alert reviews and escalations to Global Sanctions are not documented in a procedure or user guide, which is inconsistent with regulatory expectations for sanctions screening processes. Remediation will involve the Bitkey Product Compliance Manager drafting procedures and user guides and training relevant individuals on the process. The root cause of this is weak design and management of a process.
 

 Lack of Control Testing of this Screening Process
 At the end of 2024, Global Sanctions submitted a request to Global Compliance Monitoring and Testing (“GCMT”) to add testing of this process to their roadmap as a P0 request to ensure that the control was working; however, this request did not make it onto GCMT's roadmap for 2025, which contributed to these weaknesses being undetected between February 2024 and May 2025. The root cause of this is limited resources and competing priorities. There are currently only 2 individuals supporting FinCrimes monitoring and testing. Additionally this year’s roadmap focused on high-risk and high-weighted (based on trans/customer volume) products flagged in our AML/Sanctions risk assessments and a few platform-level trust and identity controls with high customer impact.
 

 This overall issue merits a medium risk rating because while it impacts Block’s ability to meet core regulatory obligations under OFAC sanctions compliance, namely prohibiting Bitkey bitcoin transactions associated with known sanctioned wallet addresses, a manual interim solution has been put in place as of May 2025 to update the list used for screening.
RCA Description:
People and Process: The various weaknesses associated with the Bitkey wallet address sanctions screening process have different root causes related to People and Process breakdowns. 
 

 The root cause of the lack of updates to the designated wallet address screening list used in the screening process is process design and lack of control monitoring/validation resulting in errors because validation was not conducted to ensure the bitkey screening list was being updated between February 2024 and May 2025 due to an incorrect belief a Cash App process to update the sanctioned wallet address list for Cash App bitcoin screening was applicable to Bitkey screening.  
 

 The root cause of having an insufficient designated wallet address screening list is process design because the design included OFAC-designated wallets but excluded supplementary lists of known sanctioned wallets offered by Chainalysis, Block’s screening vendor.  
 

 The root cause of an ineffective alert review process began with this being a manual process owned by a single DRI. When the DRI left Block due to turnover, poorly defined roles and responsibilities and training deficiencies resulted in no one taking over the process. 
 

 The root cause behind a lack of documented process and procedures is design and management of a process as the individual responsible for the process did not create documentation when it was designed.
 

 The root cause behind a lack of monitoring/testing of this sanctions screening process is limited resources and competing priorities.
Issue Rating Justification:
This issue would ordinarily warrant a high risk rating as it impacts our ability to meet core regulatory obligations under OFAC sanctions compliance ; however, a manual interim solution has been put in place as of May 2025 to update the list used for screening which lowers the issue risk rating to medium - expanded upon below. While Block has controls in place through co-signing, the effectiveness of these controls depends on having current sanctions data, specifically updates list of sanctioned wallet addresses. The control weakness since February 2024 means Block may have missed screening against newly designated addresses, including those linked to state-level threat actors and money laundering networks.
 

 The mitigating controls/factors below support a medium risk rating. This issue remains a priority instead of an immediate crisis.  
 -A manual interim solution has been put in place as of May 2025 to update the list used for screening
 -The existing screening process has a new DRI reviewing alerts as of May 2025
 -Documentation creation is in process
 -There is a clear path to a long term technical solution and full remediation
Issue Progress Updates:
A manual patch list has been developed (HERE: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1AfZ5nXKYoIaHLBLAze1cFRUGCHj4CnEhIbguMRHxAxc/edit?gid=0#gid=0) which has all the OFAC designated wallet addresses added since Jan of 2024.
 

 A manual patch/update of this list will be added to Bitkeys screening while an automatic path that utilizes Toolbox is being developed.
Issue Action Plans:
Bitkey Bitcoin Wallet Screening List Update (1392212)
 Timely Review of Declined Transactions (1392215)
 Sustainability (1393520)
Closure Request Justification: Supporting documentation for all three action plans uploaded, reviewed and approved to be closed on December 12, 2025.

Issue Title: The English version of the published website Privacy Policy does not contain information relating to deletion and/or anonymization of personal data
Issue Description:
GCMT reviewed the English and French versions of the published website Privacy Policy and identified that the English version does not contain information provided in the French version relating to the treatment of personal information upon expiration of applicable retention periods. 
 

 Section 5 - How long we keep your Personal information
 French: "Une fois les périodes de conservation applicables à vos informations personnelles expirées, nous prendrons autrement des mesures pour supprimer ou anonymiser les données personnelles lorsque nous n'avons plus de base légale pour conserver vos informations personnelles."
 

 English Translation: "Once the applicable retention periods for your personal information have expired, we will otherwise take steps to delete or anonymize personal data when we no longer have a legal basis to retain your personal information."
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: Q3 2025 Afterpay Canada - Privacy Notice - Content Requirements (1047066)
Issue Rating Justification: Rated in accordance with the issue management program
Issue Progress Updates: Issue Owner to be updated - Awaiting entry into Protecht by the ERM team - GCMT team member entered temporarily
Issue Action Plans: Privacy counsel will update the English version of the privacy notice with the missing paragraph to ensure consistency. (1344638)
Closure Request Justification:
14/01/2025
 

 Privacy counsel have removed the wording "Once the applicable retention periods for your personal information have expired, we will otherwise take steps to delete or anonymize personal data when we no longer have a legal basis to retain your personal information." from the French version of the Privacy Policy. External counsel also agreed that omitting this sentence is preferable if there is a chance that it may be misinterpreted. GCMT verified it has been removed from the publicly available policy on-line and now the English and French versions are consistent. 
 

 Additional context- during a global review in 2023, it was flagged that the wording in question could be misinterpreted by customers. The decision was made to remove this from privacy policies globally, but during this exercise it was missed in the French version.

Issue Title: 2: Australian Seller data deletion requests were not processed and some Data Services have not configured Privacy Vault as per the Implementing ForgetEntity Block Dev Guide
Issue Description:
Background
 Block's Privacy Engineering team has built a website/customer-facing service called Privacy Portal. A link to the Portal is located on the Square Australia (AU) Privacy Notice on the Square AU website and was established and designed as a channel for Square to streamline the handling of correction or deletion requests from Sellers and permit a Seller to export their personal information that Square collects. This tool was established in 2018 for General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and Square AU adopted its use and standards in May 2024 as GDPR represented a higher compliance threshold. 
 Following a Seller request for the deletion of their data, Square has a system called Privacy Vault, which is designed to assist with the management of these privacy related data requests by deleting and encrypting data. Privacy Vault is configured to interact with the various Square systems to automatically and or manually complete data deletion. These downstream Square systems have individual data service owners who determine how they connect into (configure) the Privacy Vault.
 When a data deletion request is submitted through the Privacy Portal, the Privacy Vault system automatically raises JIRA tickets for each integrated service.
 
 - For services configured with automated data deletion or vaulting, data service owners are required to implement “Fulfillment” events in the audit logs that document the action taken (e.g., data deleted or vaulted) and clearly identify the type of data affected according to the Implementing ForgetEntity Block Dev Guide. The corresponding JIRA ticket is then closed as “Resolved” to indicate that the task (i.e. deletion or vaulting) has been completed. 
 - For services that require manual data deletion, data service owners must manually acknowledge the JIRA ticket, process the deletion request, and document the action by commenting within the ticket to ensure an adequate audit trail is maintained.
 - If a service holds no relevant data or where the service is not applicable to the Seller, the service owner is required to configure the backend of Privacy Vault to return a status of "Entity-Not-Found" or "Entity-Not-Applicable" respectively as per the Implementing ForgetEntity Block Dev Guide. This ensures the corresponding JIRA ticket is closed with a “Non-applicable” status.
 

 Issue Description
 Since the launch of Privacy Portal in May 2024, Square AU has received two (2) Seller requests to delete their personal data via the portal.
 GCMT tested these 2 samples and identified that these requests to delete personal data were cancelled automatically by the Privacy Vault system due to an incorrect pre-condition not being met, whereby the system could only accept requests from the UK. As these were Australian requests, they were automatically declined. Privacy Engineering has advised that this bug was fixed in October 2024. Upon request by GCMT, the two customer requests to delete their data were completed in June 2025.
 Upon review of the 2 processed deletion requests, each request generated 56 Jira ticket requests (services). Of the 56 services, GCMT identified 11 services with automated configurations that had their tickets marked as “Resolved,” but the corresponding audit logs did not contain the required “Fulfillment” events in the audit logs. The inconsistencies between the audit logs and the actual backend actions performed were attributable to incorrect or incomplete return statuses configurations by the respective service teams:
 - 10 services where the respective teams have not configured to return a status of "Entity Not Found" response when no data exists or associated with the merchant
  > Cards-api (Cardsonfile trust platform team )
  > Instruments (Cardsonfile trust platform & Bank Accounts API teams)
  > Esperanto (Esperanto Engineering team)
  > Web (Bank Accounts API team)
  > Gringotts (Bank Accounts API team)
  > Instruments (Bank Accounts API team)
  > Uhura (Integration Lifecycle Engineering team)
  > Naturalizer (Data Aggregation Services team)
  > Checklist (Onboarding Setup Server team)
  > Pre-cog (Onboarding Setup Server team)
 - 1 service where the service owners had not configured a “Fulfillment” event in the audit log to document the data deleted. 
  > Items (Catalog Foundations team)
 

 Although the respective data service teams have confirmed that data was either deleted or not present for the 2 Sellers who submitted deletion requests, these logging configuration gaps seem to be not limited to the Australian region as GCMT pulse checked a UK example and it appeared to be similar.
 
 These inconsistencies present a risk of incomplete or unverifiable audit trails for the affected automated services, where Square may not be able to adequately demonstrate compliance with the Seller/Customer request. From an audit perspective, the identified exceptions may require manual follow-up processes to verify or fulfill deletion requests, which can limit the scalability and efficiency of the data deletion process, particularly as request volumes increase.
 Furthermore, there is currently no requirement to include “Entity-Not-Applicable” or “Entity-Not-Found” statuses within the audit logs when no relevant data exists or the service is not applicable to the Seller, even though the corresponding JIRA ticket is closed with a “Non-applicable” status. There is an opportunity to enhance transparency and accuracy by capturing these statuses in the audit log to more clearly reflect the actions taken in response to data deletion requests.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2025 Q2; Square Australia; APP 11.2 Record Retention and Data Deletion (1046402)
RCA Description: People and Technology
Issue Rating Justification:
Present a low risk to the operations of Block, or represents a limited weakness in Block’s processes or controls. 
 These inconsistencies present a risk of incomplete or unverifiable audit trails.
Issue Action Plans:
1 - Review and update Privacy Vault Configurations (1346612)
 2 - Adjust audit logging logic (1346616)

Issue Title: REF 1 - Square Australia is currently non-compliant with Australian Privacy Principle (APP) 11.2 and the Block Data Retention standards
Issue Description:
Australian Privacy Principle 11.2 Requirement: 
 If:
 an APP entity holds personal information about an individual; and
 the entity no longer needs the information for any purpose for which the information may be used or disclosed by the entity under this Schedule; and
 the information is not contained in a Commonwealth record; and
 the entity is not required by or under an Australian law, or a court/tribunal order, to retain the information;
 the entity must take such steps as are reasonable in the circumstances to destroy the information or to ensure that the information is de - identified.
 

 Background
 Block published a Data Retention Standard in November 2024 which in part specified periods for retaining various data types for all Block entities. Since then, Privacy Engineering and Data Governance foundations has developed a strategic implementation roadmap for data retention and deletion capabilities (Data Retention Engineering Strategy/Project). The initiative is beginning with offline platforms, specifically Snowflake and Databricks, allowing the team to develop foundational infrastructure and resolve implementation challenges in a controlled environment. This is considered a measured approach to facilitate the development of scalable solutions that will ultimately support regulatory compliance across Block's online systems. 
 

 Issue Description 
 Whilst the Data Retention Engineering Strategy/Project is in progress, Square AU does not appear to have taken steps as are reasonable in the circumstances to destroy the (personal) information or to ensure that the information is de-identified as is required by the Australian Privacy Principle 11.2. Most personal data for Customer Support, Marketing and Analytics, and Customer Records (Financial Services Accounts) which sit on online systems, are being retained ongoing and beyond the periods specified in the Block Data Retention Standards published in November 2024.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities
Engagement Title: GCMT Testing: 2025 Q2; Square Australia; APP 11.2 Record Retention and Data Deletion (1046402)
RCA Description: Process - Strategy is in progress
Issue Rating Justification: Non-compliance to regulation requirements.
Issue Action Plans: 1 - Inner-core risk acceptance (1346619)
Closure Request Justification: See Action Plan for details

Issue Title: KYC Data Inconsistencies
Issue Description:
Data integrity within the context of KYC refers to the accuracy, consistency, and reliability of customer data throughout the customer’s lifecycle. Risks posed by inconsistent personally identifiable information (PII) and KYC information, include regulatory and financial crime exposure.  Common challenges involve inconsistent customer data exacerbated by the use of disparate sources (e..g biometrics, IDV vendors, and manual collection) which can lead to conflicting personal identifying information data such as name misspellings, erroneous date of birth and SSN (due to erroneous vendor data), and dated address information.
 

 Incomplete or inaccurate PII directly weakens CDD and EDD processes. Incomplete PII, including missing or incomplete full legl name, date of birth, or address prevents accurate customer identity verification. Further, inconsistent data (e.g. conflicting name spellings or mismatched dates) can lead to duplicate or fragmented records, making it hard to determine the true identity of the customer. Without accurate PII, we are unable to fully assess customer risk exposure, or accurately assign risk ratings. Not having accurate PII impacts the quality of risk-based controls, transaction monitoring, EDD, Sanctions screening, and other downstream monitoring systems that rely on accurate PII to correlate activity to specific customers and detect unusual behavior. Poor data quality results in false positives/negatives, missed alerts, and failure to generate or support suspicious activity reports (SARs). Further, inadequate PII undermines the ability to detect structuring, layering, or cross-account laundering. Recent trends in enforcement highlight that failures of KYC/CDD/EDD programs, including lapses in identity verification and insufficient risk assessments consistently lead to regulatory scrutiny and fines.
 

 The below case examples highlight the type of behavior typically observed as KYC Risk due to poor data integrity:
 1. C_h6dprpyzf (associated accounts: C_n02pxhyyn and C_fscya1mb0, C_ntdxppmze) - Summary: Verified legal name and IDV information across the three associated accounts belong to Chrissy Robinson, however, the customer’s two SSN connected accounts have PII that indicates the individual in control of the account and the funds associated with the account are Jewilliam Stewart and Chrissy Thomas (likely a prior name).
 2. C_f42da8mww (associated accounts C_hyce48mv0, C_hjenx1m8t, C_nje5w8mbs, C_shpfr0ywy, C_n02rp1mxm, C_fhdcrqyyv) - Summary: The verified legal name and driver’s license provided on C_f42da8mww are different names and are associated with a SHAMYRIA E PARKS and AAZEDEES ANTIONETTE AZEEM PARKS, respectively. Aazedees Parks appears to be in control of funds associated with the account; however, is not the verified account owner.
 3. C_s6dzx8m13 (associated accounts C_fqcxapmhr, C_592z90m45, C_89cdpgy17, C_gxdt9qmz2, C_nqdn2hm2e, C_g0dewhmay, C_hyw0j1yrt, C_f9ce4gym4, C_n6evkqmf2) - Summary: The verified legal name across all nine accounts belong to STACIE S STINEBAUGH, however each account also maintains other PII information/ transactional behavior that indicates that Stacie Stinebaugh is not ultimately in control of the account or the funds associated with the account. At least one of the accounts appears to have been established to support the transfer of funds on behalf of a third party. It is unclear if Stacie’s credentials were intentionally shared with third parties to enable the creation of each distinct account. Connected accounts escalated for money movement.
Issue Rating Justification: Regulatory expectations and obligations.
Issue Action Plans: KYC Data Inconsistencies Action Plan (1403487)

Issue Title: Afterpay Canada is providing limited information about complaints processes to consumers
Issue Description:
Guidance Requirement:
 Guideline on Complaint-Handling Procedures for Banks and Authorized Foreign Banks requires a Bank’s complaint-handling Policies and Procedures for Consumers to include: 
 35.1. how the Consumer can complain to the Bank through different channels
 35.2. the Bank’s process and the action(s) that will follow
 35.3. the Consumer’s right to submit a complaint to the external complaints body if:
 35.3.1. the Bank has exhausted the prescribed time period for dealing with the complaint
 35.3.2. the Consumer is not satisfied with the resolution offered by the Bank’s most senior Designated Employee
 35.4. how to contact the external complaints body
 35.5. how to contact Financial Consumer Agency of Canada (FCAC)
 

 Issue: Information relating to complaints is currently contained within Afterpay Canada's "Terms of Service", "Installment Agreement" pages and “Make a Complaint” Article in HelpCentre. The complaint information is limited to the provision of the email address customers can use if they would like to make a complaint and direction to a complaint form/Afterpay Account with no information relating to the following: 
 ● 35.2: Afterpay Canada’s complaint process and the actions that will follow including complaint time-frames; and 
 ● 35.3 - 35.5: Customer's rights or how to contact and complain to regulatory bodies e.g FCAC should they be dissatisfied with the resolution offered by Afterpay or if Afterpay has exhausted the prescribed time period. Additionally, GCMT noted that communication macros for complaints do not include any reference to regulatory bodies i.e how to contact them..

Issue Title: Customer Service and Complaint Agent assigning of Tiers and applying collections exclusions can be improved
Issue Description:
Procedural Requirements: 
 ● The 'Investigating and resolving the IDR complaint' Standard Operating Procedure requires Customer Service Agents apply a 60 day 'collections exclusion' to a customer account to prevent the account being referred to a Debt Collection Agency, while a complaint is investigated.
 ● The 'Investigating and resolving the IDR complaint' Standard Operating Procedure requires CSAs apply a 60 day 'collections exclusion' to a customer account to prevent the account being referred to a Debt Collection Agency, while a complaint is investigated. 
 ● The 'IDR Complaint response timeframes' and the 'Complaint Triage' Standard Operating Procedure requires CSAs acknowledge each complaint received in 1 business day, or 24 hours regardless of whether it is digital, voice, and regardless of the complaint tier. 
 ● The Resolution cost section of the 'How to Update Complaint Fields' Standard Operating Procedure requires CSAs to update the resolution cost "monetary remediation" to be categorised into severity tiers.
 

 Issue: GCMT tested a sample of 90 complaints (consisting of 16 Tier 1, 4 EDR, 30 Tier 2 and 40 Tier 3) to assess customer success and complaint management agent compliance with the Complaint Standard Operating Procedures. 
 

 GCMT identified a 78% effectiveness rate (70 complaints), consisting of a 22% exception rate (20 complaints) where one or more steps were missed for the complaint case. The 22% exception rate consisted of 90% where a key process step was missed and 10% where a low-risk or administrative error was recorded.  
 

 The overall effectiveness/exception rate is broken down by Tier below: 
 ● Tier 1: 31% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 69% exception rate (11 exceptions) 
 ● Tier 1 (EDR): 75% effectiveness rate, consisting of a 25% exception rate (1 exception) 
 ● Tier 2: 83% effectiveness rate, consisting of 17% exception rate (5 exceptions)  
 ● Tier 3: 93% effectiveness rate, consisting of 7% exceptions rate (3 exceptions)
 

 The exceptions are related to the following:
 ● 8 complaints where the Complaint Severity Tier was not correctly assigned, based on the nature of the complaint. 
 ● 7 complaint cases where the collections exclusions were not applied correctly by the agent.  
 ● 2 complaint cases where the complaint was not acknowledged within the 24 hour SLA. Acknowledgements were sent between Day 2 - 6. 
 ● 2 complaint cases where the general administration of complaint handling could be improved. 
 ○ Response to the customer referenced Clearpay.
 ○ The macro template was not modified before responding to the customer and did not include Admin Portal notes.
 ● One EDR complaint where the resolution cost was not updated in Zendesk.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: Specialized content stored outside the CMS lacks defined governance, oversight, and centralized visibility.
Issue Description:
During routine First Line of Defense risk assessment activities, it was identified that specialized content documentation across the organization is often stored and maintained in locations external to the established content management system. Upon further investigation, the team discovered that there is no formal governance process in place to:
 Enforce centralized content storage within the designated content management system
 Establish criteria for when exceptions to centralized storage may be appropriate
 Provide oversight and approval mechanisms for content stored outside the system
 Monitor compliance with content management requirements
 Ensure consistent access controls and version management across all content locations
 This lack of governance creates potential risks related to content integrity, inconsistent customer experience, regulatory compliance, and operational efficiency.
RCA Description: The root cause is two-fold: first, there is no clearly defined accountable owner for content governance; second, while policies and procedures exist, there are no formal exception processes. This lack of accountability and structured exceptions results in fragmented content management practices across the organization.”
Issue Rating Justification: The content team enabled exceptions to centralized content management on an as needed basis.
Issue Action Plans: Specialized content stored outside the CMS lacks defined governance, oversight, and centralized visibility-Action Plan (1349905)
Closure Request Justification:
Resolution
 

 A new Content Storage and Governance Policy has now been created, reviewed, and approved. The policy establishes:
 

 Centralized storage requirements for all support and operational content.
 

 Clear exception criteria and an approval process for any content that must reside outside the system.
 

 Defined oversight responsibilities and compliance monitoring expectations.
 

 Standardized access controls, version management, and lifecycle management requirements.
 

 Minor final edits have been incorporated and approved.
 

 Effective Date
 

 The policy will go into effect on January 2nd. Implementation guidance will be shared with relevant teams, along with expectations for compliance and ongoing governance.

Issue Title: Systemic Quality Oversight Gaps Across Customer Operations and Risk Operations Post-Jan 2024 Layoffs
Issue Description:
Identification of the Issue:
 

 The issue was identified through a review conducted by leadership following the January 2024 layoffs, which assessed the status of independent quality checks (quality monitoring). This review revealed multiple customer-facing workflows with minimal oversight. . 
 

 Specific indicators included: the suspension of reviews in entire operational areas (e.g., Bitcoin support), the absence of internal quality checks for several Disputes processes, a reduction in "audit-the-auditor" spot checks (from approximately 10 to 6–8 per reviewer), and a narrow focus of complaints reviews on only one phase of the process.
 

 

 Personnel Responsible for Identification:
 

 The issue was identified by the Quality Management leadership for Block Customer Operations, under the direction of Mario Castillo, in collaboration with operations leaders and the teams responsible for managing external vendor reviewers.
 

 Pervasiveness of the Issue:
 

 The identified gaps are extensive and impact numerous high-priority areas:
 Risk and Disputes: Multiple dispute types and loss-document workflows currently exhibit limited internal quality checks.
 Complaints: Reviews are predominantly concentrated on the investigation phase, with other phases (including external customer responses) receiving limited review, and the sample size falling below statistical targets.
 Banking Operations: Several workflows (e.g., transaction monitoring for partner banks, ACH and wire recall requests, check deposit returns, risk reviews, and exceptions) have limited oversight; existing team leads are responsible for an excessive number of domains.
 Scams and Account Integrity: Coverage expansion is required for estates, deceased customer handling, power of attorney, escheatment, and scam-related queues.
 Bitcoin Customer Support: Independent quality checks were suspended in January 2024 and have not been reinstated.
 Second-Line Oversight ("Audit the Auditor"): Understaffing has led to a reduction in calibration and consistency checks across all reviewers.
 Program Effectiveness and Tooling: The Quality Management System (e.g., automated quality checks, contact reason analysis, summarization) is underutilized due to the absence of dedicated owners.
 

 Regulatory Impact:
 

 The potential regulatory impact is significant. The affected areas include:
 Customer complaints and responses (implicating consumer protection and complaint-handling standards; potential regulatory scrutiny).
 Disputes and error resolution (implicating electronic fund transfer and card network rules, including timely and accurate handling).
 Banking operations (ACH and wire recalls, remote check deposits, partner bank monitoring), which must comply with network rules and partner bank expectations.
 Scams and risk monitoring (elements linked to the overall AML/fraud program effectiveness).
 Escheatment/unclaimed property processes.
 Bitcoin/crypto customer support (implicating partner and licensing obligations).
RCA Description:
Primary Cause: Significant headcount reductions in early 2024, while customer volumes and the number of workflows continued to expand.
 Secondary Causes:
 Expansion of work allocated to external review partners without dedicated internal oversight to ensure alignment of standards and prompt issue resolution.
 Fewer "audit-the-auditor" checks and calibrations, resulting in reduced scoring consistency and diminished feedback loops.
 A planned statistical sampling program has not yet been fully implemented; current manual sampling methods are inadequate for reliable results in certain areas.
 An insufficient number of team leads covering an excessive number of domains, leading to bottlenecks.
Issue Rating Justification:
Scope and pervasiveness: Quality control gaps span multiple high‑risk, customer-facing workflows (disputes/Reg E, complaints/UDAAP, banking ops/ACH-wire-RDC, scams/AML, escheatment, Bitcoin support).
 Control breakdown: Core first-line QA and second-line “audit-the-auditor” checks are reduced or suspended; statistical sampling not implemented; vendors lack adequate internal oversight.
 Duration and trend: Gaps have persisted since January 2024 with growing workflow volume—raising likelihood of undetected errors.
 Regulatory exposure: Credible risk of noncompliance with consumer protection, card/network rules, partner bank expectations, AML program effectiveness, and licensing obligations.
 Potential for customer harm and financial loss: Inaccurate or untimely dispute handling, complaints responses, and banking exception processing can drive restitution, fines, and remediation.
 Limited compensating controls: Understaffing, underutilized tooling, and excessive span of control indicate residual risk remains high.
Issue Action Plans: Systemic Quality Oversight Gaps Across Customer Operations Post-Jan 2024 Layoffs-ACTION PLAN (1351887)
Closure Request Justification:
The following information was provided by Issue Owner (Mario Castillo) for issue closure. 
 This issue has been separated into a secondary ticket (Reference: #1011882) per Vanessa Simpson's approval. 
 

 Customer Operations QM Update: 
 Social: 2 resources hired (Indu Merritt and Lora Osborn); QA oversight is now in place, and the QA team is conducting 8 evaluations per advocate per month; based on the amount of QA evals being performed, we are exceeding the statistical sampling requirements of 90% confidence level with a 10% of margin of error. 
 

 Bitcoin: 2 resources hired (Stephanie Brito and Taylor Mills); QA oversight is now in place, and the QA team is conducting 10 evaluations per advocate per month; based on the amount of QA evals being performed, we are exceeding the statistical sampling requirements of 90% confidence level with a 10% of margin of error. 
 

 Complaints: 1 resource hired (Lily Anticouni) and 1 CE (to cover a LOA, Mohsin Lodhi); the QA team is delivering >230 Tier 1 External Complaints per month and >100 Internal Complaints per month; based on the amount of QA evals being performed, we are exceeding the statistical sampling requirements of 90% confidence level with a 10% of margin of error. The Complaints QA team also conducts 4 QA evals per advocate on complaint cases logged for MET, Disputes, Social and BTC. We have a plan in place to transition this volume to the respective QA teams (e.g. QA teams covering MET, Disputes, Social and BTC) during 1H 2026, which will further free up capacity for the Complaints QA team. 
 

 Audit the Auditor (ATA): 1 resource hired (Patrick Winston) and 2 HIDs targeted for onboarding in Nov ‘25 to support incremental growth at partner); target audits per QA Specialists have been shifted from 6 to now 8 per month.

Issue Title: Lack of SLA to Close Transaction Monitoring Cases Pending Merchant Responses
Issue Description: CBI requires1 that the regulated entity to submit Suspicious Transaction Reports (STRs) “as soon as practicable”. There is no defined SLA for closing TM cases that are awaiting merchant responses, and such cases may remain pending indefinitely. Investigations were not closed out as the Compliance team is awaiting the merchant's response. IA tested a sample of 25 TM alert cases and identified one TM alert that was closed after 156 days. In addition, IA noted that two of the TM alert cases remained in pending status greater than 400 days as of June 30, 2025.  As a result, this increases the risk of failing to timely assess unusual or suspicious activities, determine if an STR should be filed, and file STRs, if required. Further, the risk of SIL engaging with bad actors increases, which may lead to regulatory criticism, fines and penalties.

Issue Title: Cashapp’s External (CFBP) Complaints SLA breach
Issue Description:
The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) Consent Order triggered a significant spike in regulatory complaint volumes, resulting in a surge of more than 36,000 complaints in just a few days. For reference, throughout 2024 CCOT would receive around 320 complaints from the CFPB per month.
 

 CCOT’s current handling capacity is insufficient to maintain compliance with the CFPB’s 15 SLA or the extended 60-day SLA and have now encountered an SLA breach as CFPB complaints are not being answered within the allotted amount of time. Previously CCOT could sustainably handle around 320 complaints per month, maintaining the initial 15 day CFPB SLA.
 

 As of 9/4/2025 413 closed complaints and 83 open complaints are out of SLA. There is a backlog with an additional 106 complaints at risk in the next 3 days.
 

 Based on current operational strategy, CCOT should be able to limit potential SLA breaches to around 700 complaints.
 

 The issue is pervasive given the sheer number of complaints, regulator visibility, and SLA breaches. The backlog created by this volume surge exposes Cash App to direct regulatory scrutiny, potential enforcement action, and reputational harm, especially in the context of the issue stemming from the recent CFPB settlement.
 

 Remediation will be demonstrated through the return to SLA compliance and backlog reduction (2033 complaints). CCOT is addressing the issue by (1) increasing resources through surge staffing, and (2) implementing the new “Hold The Line” strategy, which prioritizes complaints that can be resolved within SLA while strategically deferring a small volume into the backlog to prevent widespread SLA breaches. These actions are designed to contain operational risk, improve timeliness, and restore complaint-handling stability.
RCA Description: While the root trigger was outside of Cash App’s control, the internal contributing factor was a process gap in anticipating and managing large-scale, regulator-facing complaint surges. This gap led to insufficient resource capacity and backlog accumulation, ultimately preventing CCOT from resolving complaints within the required 15 day CFPB SLA or the CFBP provided 60-day SLA extension.
Issue Rating Justification: The CFPB has already extended the 15 day SLA given to all financial institutions to 60 days due to the circumstance. Further delays could cause regulatory risk.
Issue Progress Updates:
As agreed by the Global Complaints Oversight Team for Block and the broader Block Consumer Protection Team, there is zero tolerance or risk acceptance for missing the CFPB-defined deadline for processing complaints received through its portal. It jeopardizes our relationship with the regulator and, more importantly, with our customers who have turned to the regulator due to dissatisfaction with dealing with us directly. 
 

 CCOT recently reached the inflection point where they are now responding to more complaints per day than they receive on average so the backlog should not continue to GROW substantially. CCOT anticipates the backlog starting to reduce this month.

Issue Title: 2025 APUS GA Exam Finding - COVERED EMPLOYEE BACKGROUND CHECKS
Issue Description:
Prior to July 1, 2023, O.C.G.A. § 7-3-42(e) stated, in part, every applicant and licensee shall be authorized and required to obtain and maintain the results of criminal history record checks on covered employees. Such checks shall be handled by the Georgia Crime Information Center pursuant to O.C.G.A. § 35-3-34 and the rules and regulations of the Georgia Crime Information Center. An applicant or licensee may only employ an individual whose criminal history has been checked and has been found to be in compliance with all lawful requirements prior to the initial date of hire.
  
 During the course of the examination, it was found that Afterpay did not obtain and retain prior to hire any G.C.I.C. handled background check for its covered employees, placing the licensee in violation of O.C.G.A. § 7-3-42(e) as it previously read.  
  
 Prior to July 1, 2023, Department Rule 80-14-3-.01(10) stated, in part, any licensee that does not obtain a Georgia Crime Information Center ("G.C.I.C.") criminal background check on each covered employee prior to the initial date of hire or retention shall be subject to a fine of one thousand dollars ($1,000) per occurrence. Since it was found that the licensee did not obtain G.C.I.C. handled background checks in accordance with O.C.G.A. § 7-3-42(e) as it read prior to July 1, 2023, a fine of $1,000 is cited.
  
 The licensee is also reminded that, effective July 1, 2023, covered employee background check requirements have been modified in O.C.G.A. § 7-3-42(e), which states, in part, that licensee shall be authorized and required to obtain and maintain the results of criminal history background checks on covered employees. Such criminal background checks shall be commercial background checks. Licensees shall be responsible for any applicable fees charged by the company performing the commercial background check. A licensee may only employ a covered employee whose criminal history has been checked in this manner and has been found to be in compliance with all lawful requirements prior to the initial date of hire.
Issue Rating Justification:
Present a low risk to the operations of Block, or represents a limited weakness in Block’s processes or controls. .
 

 Corrective action to address the Issue(s) identified is required.

Issue Title: Observation #2: CDD Control Effectiveness Discrepancies
Issue Description:
Enhancement CM.02
 

 Five irregularities were identified during throughput testing and backtesting on the Linked Instrument Name Mismatch and Manual IDV PII Mismatch queues, where alert outcomes did not align with the implemented queue logic. Specifically:
 

 Throughput Testing:
 •Three of 25 sampled records (sample record #2, 23, and 24) evaluated as part of Manual IDV PII Mismatch queue throughput testing failed to alert despite all queue logic thresholds being satisfied.
 •One of 25 sampled records (sample record #15) evaluated as part of Linked Instrument Name Mismatch queue throughput testing failed to alert despite all queue logic thresholds being satisfied
 

 Backtesting:
 •One of 25 sampled records (sample record #23) evaluated as part of Linked Instrument Name Mismatch queue backtesting improperly generated an alert despite queue logic criteria not being met.
 

 It was determined that the root cause of the discrepancies was a timing delay between when customer risk scores are generated and when those scores are populated in downstream tables and used within the queue logic. For the impacted records, the expected risk score values were not yet present in the relevant tables at the time the queue logic was executed, resulting in either missed alerts or unintended alert generation.
 

 Block's Business Intelligence team investigated the irregularities and determined that it may take up to three days for generated risk scores to be populated into the downstream tables referenced by the queue logic. To further assess potential impact, additional analysis was performed on the impacted samples to determine whether an alert generated for the customer in the subsequent weeks (i.e., once risk scores were ingested). Block confirmed, via email on 07/15/2025, that no alerts were generated. In those instances, although the customers met the original queue criteria when the alerts should have been generated, by the time the delayed risk score was ingested, the customer's most recent risk rating had changed (i.e., from Medium to Low), resulting in the customer being ineligible for alert generation under the current logic criteria of the Linked Instrument Name Mismatch and Manual IDV PII Mismatch queues. As a result, those customer records were not re-evaluated under the originally valid risk score, and no alerts were created in subsequent runs.
 

 Impact
 The completeness of data directly impacts the quality of the control output and performance. Data latency issues could result in ineffective identification of potentially higher risk customers. Additionally, issues with data completeness may also lead to the inability of the control to fully mitigate BSA/AML risks, exposing the institution to elevated risk levels and increased regulatory scrutiny.
 

 *Recommendation*
 It is recommended that Block assess the prevalence of the timing delays within its workflows and determine whether other monitoring queues may be similarly impacted by risk score ingestion latency. The Institution should also determine, based on the results of the assessment, whether adjustments to the timing of queue executions (e.g., operating on a T-3 delay) are warranted. Additionally, it is recommended that Block document the results of the assessment and any subsequent decisions made, including the acceptance of risk presented by any control limitations, as applicable.

Issue Title: Observation #3: CDD Alert Disposition Control Effectiveness Discrepancies
Issue Description:
Enhancement CM.02
 

 Alert output operational effectiveness testing identified inconsistencies in the documentation of required CDD review elements within alert dispositions (i.e., CASE_COMMENT) for 77 of 78 sampled alerts (98.72%) across the Manual IDV PII Mismatch, Linked Instruments Name Mismatch, Linked Instrument Red Flag, and Zipcode Red Flag queues. While certain customer information (i.e., Case Disposition Decision, IDV and Selfie, Linked Instrument and Payment Sources Review, and SSN Connections) were consistently present, other required elements (i.e., adverse media results, Box.com review, PEP status, Toolbox account review, and web presence) were not specifically addressed within case comments. During the scope period, it was not required that case comments address each required CDD review element. Instead, red flag behavior and appropriate mitigation were required to be addressed within case comments. Block acknowledged, via email communication on 07/15/2025, that the absence of the documented audit trail of the case review was a known gap identified during the Institution's 2024 BSA/AML audit and noted that enhanced disposition templates were implemented in April 2025 as part of the Institution's Management Action Plan (MAP) to remediate the control deficiency. As the corrective action was deployed after our scope period, Crowe could not confirm whether the MAP was effective at promoting consistency and completeness across alert reviews. Additionally, for 44 of 78 sampled alerts (56.41%), the TLO Results field reflected a generic message stating that “TLO results are currently unavailable due to connectivity problems.” Although Block confirmed, via email communication on 07/15/2025, that Compliance Specialists were advised to use Google searches when TLOxp was inaccessible, the usage of a secondary tool to perform public records and negative news searches was not captured within any of the 44 impacted alerts’ alert disposition or TLO Results field to indicate that a secondary solution had been used in place of TLOxp. 
 

 Impact 
 The absence of required CDD review elements within alert dispositions limits the Institution’s ability to demonstrate that CDD reviews were consistently executed in accordance with Block’s CDD Procedures during the period under review and subsequently may be unable to evidence the completeness of its CDD processes. Regulatory agencies emphasize the importance of identifying customers with a higher risk profile within a timely manner for appropriate risk mitigation (i.e., targeted monitoring, periodic reviews, relationship exits). Consistent compliance with alert disposition procedures is critical to support that higher risk customers are evaluated and mitigated within expected timelines and to confirm compliance with regulatory expectations.  
 

 Recommendation 
 It is recommended that the Institution implement enhancements to its CDD documentation procedures to ensure that all required customer information reviewed during the CDD process is consistently documented within the alert disposition. Specifically, the Institution should ensure that the updated disposition templates are fully deployed and utilized across all CDD reviews performed, regardless of the queue that results in the review being performed. Additionally, Compliance Specialists should be required to note the use of alternative sources within the alert disposition (i.e., CASE_COMMENT) or corresponding fields to ensure that the CDD review is fully auditable and aligned with procedural expectations.

Consistent compliance with alert disposition procedures is critical to support that higher risk customers are evaluated and mitigated within expected timelines and to confirm compliance with regulatory expectations.)

Issue Title: Observation #4: CDD/EDD Training Control Effectiveness Discrepancies
Issue Description:
Enhancement CM.05
 

 One of the 25 personnel sampled – despite working CDD/EDD alerts during their time with Block – did not undergo the trainings on the updated EDD guidelines and customer level review expectations as expected. Block indicated that the personnel was trained through the third party vendor, AML RightSource; however, as Block’s Copy of _01.21.25__know_your_customer__kyc__policy___cash_app___cash_app_u.s.__2___3_.pdf indicates that fixed term and contingent workers are considered “staff” and Block further indicated that training is administered to individuals who are new to the CDD/EDD review workflow and/or current staff if uplifts to the original training occur, it was expected that this personnel would also have attested to the training materials.  
 

 Impact 
 Regulatory agencies emphasize the importance of ensuring that alert reviews are conducted by personnel who are both trained and knowledgeable in current procedures and control expectations. When individuals lack training on updated processes or enhancements, the Institution’s ability to validate proper implementation and execution of those controls is significantly impaired. This misalignment can compromise the integrity of the alert review process, diminish the overall effective
 

 Recommendation It is recommended that the Institution ensure all personnel responsible for conducting CDD and EDD alert reviews, inclusive of fixed-term, contingent, and third-party resources, are required to complete Block-administered training on applicable policies, procedures, and recent control enhancements. This training should cover customer-level review expectations, procedural updates introduced through the KYC remediation effort, and any system-specific guidance necessary to perform the review function in accordance with Block’s current CDD Procedures. Completion of training should be documented and retained, and personnel should attest to their understanding of the materials prior to engaging in alert review activities.

Issue Title: Dispute Submission Flow Expiration & Invalid Link Resulted in Denied Claims for Customers (INC-5845)
Issue Description:
A critical service disruption impacting the Step-up IDV flow was identified on April 3rd, 2025, by a Software Engineer for Cash/Trust/Disputes during an investigation of evidence upload flow failures. The incident, which began on January 8th, 2024, affected 413 unique customers who encountered invalid IDV links and flow expiration issues, resulting in 1,155 denied claims across Cash Card and Cash App Pay services. The root cause was traced to customers attempting to use invalid IDV links, failing to initiate the in-app flow, and subsequently being denylisted due to IDV flow expiration. While initial carveouts were in place for mobile users who had successfully started the flow, their removal exposed the full scope of the impact.
 

 The financial exposure totaled ~$125,000, with Cash Card services bearing the majority (314 unique customers, 1,014 claims) and Cash App Pay accounting for the remainder (99 unique customers, 141 claims). The analysis excluded customers who had successfully initiated the in-app step-up IDV flow, passed IDV, or failed the IDV process. The issue persisted until July 2nd, 2025, when final resolution was implemented. This extended duration of approximately 18 months highlights the significance of the impact and the complexity of the resolution process.

Issue Rating Justification: Operational Gap or Process Failure Resulting in an impact to Regulatory Obligations

Issue Title: The system control to cap all Pay Monthly Loans at 35.99% Annual Percentage rate permitted four loans to be charged greater than the 36% Military Annual Percentage Rate and one loan was charged greater than the 35.99% APR cap set by Afterpay.
Issue Description:
Internal Policy Requirement:
 The Military Lending Act Policy, Afterpay US, Inc. June 2024, describes all the requirements set forth by the Act, including the calculation of the Military Annual Percentage Rate (“MAPR”). Furthermore it states that Afterpay will not impose an MAPR greater than 36 percent on a covered transaction to covered borrowers. In practice, no borrower is meant to be charged a rate above 35.99% in order to ensure that no borrower would ever exceed the MAPR calculation.  
 

 Regulatory Requirement - Military Annual Percentage Rate. 
 All transactions covered by the MLA are subject to the Military Annual Percentage Rate (“MAPR”). The MAPR is analogous to the Annual Percentage Rate (“APR”) as defined in other consumer protection regulations in that both are calculated in the same manner. Still, the MAPR differs from APR in that the MAPR calculation must include certain fees and charges regardless of whether other regulations require those fees or charges to be included in the APR calculation. Specifically, the MAPR calculation must include the following charges:
  Credit insurance premium or fees;
 > Debt cancellation or debt suspension fees;
 > Fees for ancillary products sold in connection with the credit transaction;
 > Finance charges; and
 > Application fees.  
 The MLA APR cap is 36%. 
 

 Testing Results:
 GCMT used data analysis to review 415,654 new Pay Monthly loans during the period of 1 May through 31 July 2025 and identified 5 loans that exceeded the 35.99% threshold, of which 4 exceeded 36% by .01 - .02%.
Issue Source: Second Line of Defense (2LoD) Review of First Line of Defense (1LoD) Activities

Issue Title: Observation 5: EDD Control Design Documentation Enhancement
Issue Description:
"Block’s Global Enhanced Due Diligence Procedure (Personal Account) _ Cash App.pdf (“EDD Procedure”) states that high risk restricted accounts are subject to ongoing Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) based on the continuous Customer Risk Rating (cCRR) score. The procedure outlines review expectations specific to restricted accounts within the “IDV Attempts and Connections” and “Connected User Graph” sections. However, the supporting user guide, Enhanced Due Diligence User Guide (Personal Account) _ Cash App.pdf (“EDD User Guide”), does not contain a section describing the review process for the High Risk Unverified Monitoring queue. It also does not include procedures specific to cCRR Restricted Account reviews. Although the EDD User Guide includes guidance for the Cash EDD cCRR queue that references investigative steps applicable to restricted accounts (i.e., use of open-source searches in place of IDV and analysis of connected accounts via the Connection Tab and Customer Relationship Query), these instructions are only applicable to unrestricted customer reviews.
 

 Additionally, it was noted that the implemented queue logic for the High Risk Unverified Monitoring queue, documented within SQL code for EDD high risk.xlsx, includes a filter to exclude denylisted accounts from the eligible population. However, this exclusion criterion is not reflected in the documented queue definition located on page 32 of Cash App [US] Customer Onboarding Management Rulebook.pdf."
Engagement Title: 2025 Crowe | Block Inc KYC Remediation Validation (1047721)
RCA Description: Misalignment and gaps in procedural documentation vs actual system logic and operational practices
Issue Rating Justification: Insufficient or inaccurate documentation supporting the configuration of deployed controls and supporting processes can expose the Institution to increased regulatory scrutiny regarding its ability to demonstrate appropriate program governance.
Issue Progress Updates: 9/25KP: Issue is related to both operational documentation and queue logic documentation; looped in additional stakeholders for viz
Issue Action Plans:
Update Enhanced Due Diligence User Guide (1361783)
 Update Cash App [US] Customer Onboarding Management Rulebook (1361786)
Closure Request Justification: 12/26 AG, Reviewed and validated the completion of the MAP associated with this issue. The user guide has been enhanced to document the review process for high risk restricted accounts. Also, the COM Rulebook has been updated to document the exclusion of denylisted accounts

Issue Title: Observation 6: Adverse Media Screening for Verified Accounts Control Design Documentation Enhancement
Issue Description:
Block maintains multiple design documents outlining the criteria for adverse media screening eligibility; however, inconsistencies were identified across these materials regarding the exclusion of customers with open Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) investigations or recent SAR filings.
 

 Specifically, Negative News Screening – Adyen Acquiring.pdf (“Negative News Design) states that non-high risk customers with an open SAR investigation are ineligible for adverse media screening. In contrast, [US] CashApp cCRR System Design.pdf (“cCRR System Design”) indicates that both high and non-high risk customers with a SAR filing in the past 90 days are ineligible for screening. Meanwhile, Cash App [US] Customer Onboarding Management Rulebook.pdf (“Customer Onboarding Management Rulebook “) does not reference SAR investigations or filings at all within the exclusion criteria for adverse media queues.
 

 Block confirmed during testing that the exclusion logic deployed in production is consistent with the criteria outlined in Negative News Design, meaning that only non-high risk customers with an open SAR investigation are excluded. However, the absence of this exclusion logic in Customer Onboarding Management Rulebook and its inconsistency with other design documentation introduces inconsistency in the documented queue design.
Engagement Title: 2025 Crowe | Block Inc KYC Remediation Validation (1047721)
RCA Description: Absence of a formalized governance process to ensure alignment between design documentation, system rulebooks, and production logic
Issue Rating Justification:
The absence of consistent and comprehensive documentation may limit the Institution’s ability to ensure that
 adverse media screening queues are fully aligned with their intended design.
Issue Progress Updates: 9/25KP: looped in additional stakeholders as the issue + MAP have multiple owners due to root cause related to governance of design documentation, system rulebooks, and production logic.
Issue Action Plans: Update Customer Onboarding Management Rulebook to include Adverse Media Screening Design w SAR based Exclusion Logic (1361789)
Closure Request Justification: 12/26 AG - Reviewed and confirmed the remediation actions completed to address the design documentation enhancement issue. Relevant documents (as noted in the observation) have been updated (or deprecated) and now accurately reflect the adverse media screening processes. This issue is now closed

Issue Title: FirstSource Call Recording Interruption
Issue Description:
On September 8th, 2025 at approximately 9 AM ET, customers using the NA3 U19 environment began experiencing issues with call recordings not appearing in LiveVox Portal (LVP). Support engineers quickly confirmed the issue, and subsequent checks by the LiveVox NOC team verified an impact to the call recording tasks. Recordings generated during the initial impact window are unfortunately not recoverable. Between September 9–10, 2025, additional symptoms emerged for some customers:
 •
 Call recordings displaying incorrect or extended timestamps (e.g., call lengths of “14000”).
 •
 Recordings ended prematurely while
RCA Description:
The initial outage on September 8th was caused by a disruption in the call recording component pool. During the automated recovery, all rebuilt recording tasks passed health checks and were confirmed as functional. However, at some point after the rebuild, two of the tasks degraded to a suboptimal performance state while still appearing healthy. This degradation caused inaccurate call lengths, premature call recording cutoffs, and missing recordings for a subset of customers between September 9–10.
 Further investigation identified performance degradation linked to Hazelcast, which supports the distribution and synchronization of call recording tasks. The corruption in these two tasks prevented full recovery after the initial rebuild.
Issue Rating Justification: There were only 6 Afterpay affected calls during this time.

Issue Title: FirstSource Incorrect Settlement Provided
Issue Description:
Account: 1986790
 For this account, although it was not eligible for settlement, a settlement of $50 was provided because the latest balance was not available for the ML engine to generate the offer.
 The account had a prior payment failure for a $50 transaction on 07/18/2025, with the failure status updated on 07/22/2025 at 7:30 PM EST based on the payment gateway report.
 As a result, the balance changed on 07/23, but this update was not factored into the offer predictions.
RCA Description: When the ML model predicted offer for the said account, latest balance was not factored due to timing issue and hence the settlement was wrongly provided.
Issue Rating Justification: Only 1 account was impacted and the DCA has since paid the balance to reflect Paid in Full.
Closure Request Justification: We received the payment from the DCA on 9/30/2025 which changed the status of the account to Paid in Full.
